  - Critical extraction note: dlings uses **argparse D UDA-on-struct-fields-for-declarative-spec CLI pattern** at app.d:20-32 + 41-66 — `@(Command("hint").ShortDescription("X")) struct Hint { @PositionalArgument(0) string exercise; }` + `struct DlingsCmdlArgs { @NamedArgument EmojiArgument emoji; @NamedArgument auto color = ansiStylingArgument; @SubCommands SumType!(Hint, Run, Verify, Watch) cmd; }`.  D's USER-DEFINED ATTRIBUTES (UDAs) — `@(Expression)` syntax — attach ARBITRARY D EXPRESSIONS to declarations (types, fields, methods).  argparse uses UDAs as DECLARATIVE METADATA — at COMPILE TIME, argparse introspects the struct's UDAs via D's `getUDAs!(SymbolType, AttributeType)` reflection primitive and generates the CLI parser.  Distinct from R322 Java JCommander (Java annotations — JVM RUNTIME REFLECTION on field annotations), R275 Rust clap-derive (Rust attributes — PROC-MACRO at compile time generating code), R344 argh (similar Rust), R338 Odin core:flags (struct tags + RTTI — runtime reflection on tag strings), R346 mainargs (Scala annotations — Scala 3 PROC-MACRO).  D-UDAs are CLOSEST to Rust attributes in spirit (compile-time expressions) but with FULL D LANGUAGE EXPRESSIONS available — UDAs can contain arbitrary type/value expressions, not just attribute-key=value pairs.  argparse's approach: D UDAs on STRUCT FIELDS with COMPILE-TIME REFLECTION via `getUDAs!` — corpus's **first documented D-UDAs-with-getUDAs-compile-time-reflection CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dlings uses **argparse D @SubCommands-SumType-tagged-union-dispatch CLI pattern** at app.d:60 + 79 — `@SubCommands SumType!(Hint, Run, Verify, Watch) cmd;` + `cmdlArgs.cmd.match!((in Hint arg) {...}, (in Run arg) {...}, (in Verify arg) {...}, (in Watch arg) {...});`.  argparse's `@SubCommands` UDA on a `SumType!(A, B, C, D)` field declares VARIANT-BASED SUBCOMMAND DISPATCH.  D's `std.sumtype.SumType` is a TAGGED UNION holding ONE VARIANT at a time (similar to Rust's enum with variants).  After argparse parses the matched subcommand, it CONSTRUCTS the corresponding variant struct (e.g. Hint) and stores it in the SumType field.  Caller dispatches via `.match!(LAMBDA1, LAMBDA2, LAMBDA3, LAMBDA4)` — D's pattern-matching primitive — calling the appropriate handler.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { Foo(FooArgs), Bar(BarArgs) }` (very similar — Rust enum is just a tagged union), R342 alexflint/go-arg POINTER-TO-STRUCT field with `arg:"subcommand:X"` (Go reflection), R344 argh `#[argh(subcommand)] enum` (similar to clap-derive), R346 mainargs MULTIPLE `@main` methods (separate methods become subcommands).  argparse D's approach: `@SubCommands` UDA on `SumType!(A, B, C)` TAGGED UNION field + `.match!(LAMBDA1, ...)` dispatch — corpus's **first documented @SubCommands-SumType-tagged-union-with-match-dispatch CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dlings uses **argparse D CLI!T.parseArgs-compile-time-template CLI pattern** at app.d:72 — `if (!CLI!DlingsCmdlArgs.parseArgs(cmdlArgs, args[1 .. $])) return;`.  `CLI!T` is a D TEMPLATE INSTANTIATION at compile time — `CLI` is a TEMPLATE TYPE parameterized by T (the CLI args struct type).  Instantiating `CLI!DlingsCmdlArgs` generates the PARSER SPECIALIZED for DlingsCmdlArgs at compile time — argparse uses D's POWERFUL COMPILE-TIME METAPROGRAMMING (`static if`, `static foreach`, `__traits`) to generate optimal parser code.  `parseArgs(target, args)` then runs the generated parser on `target` (the destination struct).  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (Rust proc-macro generates code at compile time), R344 argh (similar), R322 JCommander (RUNTIME REFLECTION via Java's `Class.getDeclaredFields()`), R338 Odin core:flags (RUNTIME RTTI), R346 mainargs (Scala PROC-MACRO).  argparse D's approach: D `CLI!T` COMPILE-TIME TEMPLATE INSTANTIATION + `parseArgs(target, args)` parsing on generated code — corpus's **first documented CLI!T-D-template-compile-time-specialization CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dlings uses **argparse D chained-UDA-modifiers CLI pattern** at app.d:41-50 — `@(NamedArgument .Description("Enable emoji output. If value is omitted then 'always' is used.") .AllowedValues!(["always", "auto", "never"]) .NumberOfValues(0, 1) .Parse!((string[] params) => params[0]) .Action!((ref self, string param) { ... }) .ActionNoValue!((ref self) { self.emoji = true; }))`.  argparse's UDAs use METHOD-CHAIN COMPILE-TIME COMPOSITION — `NamedArgument` is a UDA constructor that returns a UDA expression; `.Description("X")` returns a NEW UDA expression with the description added; `.AllowedValues!(["a", "b"])` returns ANOTHER UDA with allowed values added; etc.  Each modifier method is a COMPILE-TIME CHAIN producing a single final UDA expression.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(short = 'x', long = "alpha", help = "...")]` (single attribute with key=value pairs), R322 JCommander `@Parameter(names = ..., description = ..., required = true)` (single annotation with named args), R346 mainargs `@arg(short = 'X', doc = "...")` (single annotation with named args), R344 argh `#[argh(option, short = 'X', long = "X")]` (single attribute).  argparse D's approach: CHAINED UDA MODIFIERS via COMPILE-TIME METHOD-CHAIN on UDA expressions — corpus's **first documented chained-UDA-modifiers-on-compile-time-expressions CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped andrey-zherikov/argparse demo source files (the library itself, not a CLI tool), other D CLI tools probed (D ecosystem is small).  Probed alternative D CLI libraries: `_darg`-based tools (older D CLI library; HOLD), other D tools.  Considered using larger D CLI tools but none found that aren't in corpus.  Chose dlings: 24 GH stars (real rustlings clone for D programming language education), 2 global flags (`--emoji`/`--color`) + 4 subcommands (`hint`/`run`/`verify`/`watch`) + per-subcommand positionals + D USER-DEFINED ATTRIBUTES on struct fields + `@(Command(...))` UDA + `@PositionalArgument(IDX)` + `@NamedArgument` + `@SubCommands` SumType! tagged-union dispatch + CHAINED UDA MODIFIERS + `CLI!T.parseArgs(target, args)` compile-time template + `alias FIELD this` subtype-alias-this, full source/app.d exercising EVERY MAJOR andrey-zherikov/argparse PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `dlings` added.  Corpus count: 28,785 → 28,786 (+1).

- **R362 — clj-holmes source-direct (CLI SAST tool for finding vulnerable Clojure code via pattern rules; l3nz/cli-matic library)** (1 file new) — pivots from p-ranav/argparse (R361 lottie_to_png — 100-variant milestone) to a THIRD Clojure CLI library — corpus's **first documented l3nz/cli-matic library** + **third documented Clojure CLI library**.  clj-holmes is the clj-holmes team's STATIC APPLICATION SECURITY TESTING (SAST) scanner for Clojure code — uses pattern-matching rules to find vulnerable code constructs (similar to Semgrep but Clojure-specific).  116 GH stars.  Sister tools: semgrep (in corpus), brakeman (Ruby SAST), bandit (in corpus), gosec (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented l3nz/cli-matic library** + **third documented Clojure CLI library**.  Clojure ecosystem now has 3 documented CLI libraries (R302 babashka.cli + R318 org.clojure/tools.cli + R362 cli-matic) joining the multi-library leaders.  Adds cli-matic (Leonardo "l3nz" Borghi's cli-matic; ~400 GH stars on l3nz/cli-matic) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy.
  - `_clj-holmes` (1-stem; clj-holmes/clj-holmes src/clj_holmes/main.clj + l3nz/cli-matic library; **l3nz/cli-matic library** — `[cli-matic.core :as cli]` brings cli-matic into scope; **3 subcommands (`fetch-rules` + `validate-rules` + `scan`) + per-subcommand options + `CONFIGURATION` SINGLE CLOJURE MAP describing the whole CLI + `:app` METADATA MAP (`:command`/`:description`/`:version`) + `:commands` LIST of command maps + `:command`/`:description`/`:opts`/`:runs` per command + `:opts` LIST of option maps + `:option`/`:short`/`:type`/`:default`/`:as`/`:multiple` per option + `:type :string`/`:int`/`:flag`/`:with-flag` OR SET `#{"a" "b"}` for choices + `:default :present` for REQUIRED + `:multiple true` for REPEATABLE + `(cli/run-cmd args CONFIGURATION)` ONE-FUNCTION-CALL dispatch + handler functions referenced via `:runs symbol`**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 1-5 (`(ns clj-holmes.main (:gen-class) (:require [cli-matic.core :as cli] [clj-holmes.engine :as engine] [clj-holmes.rules.loader.loader :as rules.loader] [clj-holmes.rules.manager :as rules.manager]))` — Clojure NS form requiring cli-matic.core; `:gen-class` for AOT compilation to JVM class)
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 7-11 (`(def CONFIGURATION {:app {:command "clj-holmes" :description "run clj-holmes" :version (System/getProperty "clj-holmes.version")} ...})` — SINGLE CLOJURE MAP describing the WHOLE CLI; `:app` metadata sub-map for app-wide info)
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 12-22 (`{:command "fetch-rules" :description "Fetch rules from an external server" :opts [{:option "repository" :short "r" :type :string :default "git://clj-holmes/clj-holmes-rules#main" :as "Repository to download rules"} {:option "output-directory" :short "o" :type :string :default "/tmp/clj-holmes-rules/" :as "Directory to save rules"}] :runs rules.manager/fetch}` — per-subcommand MAP with `:command`/`:description`/`:opts`/`:runs`; each option is a MAP with `:option`/`:short`/`:type`/`:default`/`:as`)
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 23-32 (`{:command "validate-rules" :description "Validate if the rules are conform expected" :opts [{:option "rules-directory" :short "d" :type :string :default "/tmp/clj-holmes-rules/"} {:option "rule-tags" :short "r" :multiple true :type :string}] :runs rules.loader/validate-rules!}` — `:multiple true` for REPEATABLE option)
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 33-66 (`{:command "scan" :description "Performs a scan for a path" :opts [{:option "scan-path" :short "p" :type :string :default :present :as "Path to scan"} {:option "output-type" :short "t" :type #{"sarif" "stdout" "json"} :default "stdout"} {:option "fail-on-result" :short "f" :type :with-flag :default false} {:option "verbose" :short "v" :type :with-flag :default true} ...] :runs engine/scan}` — `:default :present` marks REQUIRED; `:type #{"sarif" "stdout" "json"}` SET for CHOICES; `:type :with-flag` for negatable boolean toggle)
    * `src/clj_holmes/main.clj` lines 68-69 (`(defn -main [& args] (cli/run-cmd args CONFIGURATION))` — `-main` is the JVM entry point; `(cli/run-cmd args CONFIGURATION)` is the ONLY runtime call — parses argv, dispatches to handler, exits)
  - Critical extraction note: clj-holmes uses **l3nz/cli-matic library** at main.clj:3 — corpus's **first documented l3nz/cli-matic library** + **third documented Clojure CLI library**.  cli-matic (Leonardo "l3nz" Borghi's cli-matic; ~400 GH stars on l3nz/cli-matic) is a DATA-STRUCTURE-DRIVEN Clojure CLI library — the ENTIRE CLI is described as a SINGLE CLOJURE MAP, and one function call `(cli-matic.core/run-cmd args CONFIG)` parses argv, dispatches to the matched subcommand's handler, and exits.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS from R302 babashka.cli + R318 org.clojure/tools.cli:
    - **DATA-STRUCTURE-DRIVEN** — the ENTIRE CLI is a SINGLE CLOJURE MAP, no fluent builder, no chained methods, no annotations, no reflection
    - **DECLARATIVE `:opts` LIST per command** — each option is a MAP `{:option NAME :short C :type T :default V :as DESC :multiple BOOL}`
    - **`:type` polymorphism** — can be `:string`/`:int`/`:flag`/`:with-flag`/`:edn`/`:keyword`/`:slurp`/etc. OR a SET `#{"a" "b" "c"}` for choices — TYPE-AS-SET-FOR-CHOICES is unique
    - **`:default :present`** marks the option as REQUIRED (uses the `:present` Clojure keyword as a sentinel value)
    - **`:multiple true`** makes the option REPEATABLE (accumulating into a vector)
    - **`(cli-matic.core/run-cmd args CONFIGURATION)`** is the ONLY runtime call needed — parses argv, dispatches to handler, exits via `System/exit`
    - **`:runs handler-fn`** — handler function (a symbol RESOLVING to a function) is part of the data structure, called by `run-cmd` with the parsed options map
  Distinct from R302 babashka.cli (uses Clojure maps too but with different syntax: `(parse-args args {:spec {:foo {:default 42}}}` — also data-structure-driven but with different schema), R318 org.clojure/tools.cli (uses a different declarative style: vector-of-vectors `[["-p" "--port PORT" "Port" :default 80]]` — older library, more verbose), R275 clap-derive (Rust struct + macro), R346 mainargs (Scala annotations on methods), R322 JCommander (Java field annotations + reflection).  cli-matic's approach: SINGLE CLOJURE MAP for the WHOLE CLI + `(run-cmd args CONFIG)` one-function-call dispatch + `:type` as SET for choices + `:default :present` as required sentinel — corpus's **first documented single-Clojure-map-with-type-as-set-for-choices CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to ONE HUNDRED AND ONE library variants: Rust (9), Go (9), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 4, Kotlin (R277), Scala 3, Dart (R279), Ruby 2, Perl (R281), PHP 2, Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# 2, Nim 2, Zig 2, C++ 3, D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), **Clojure (3 — babashka.cli R302 + tools.cli R318 + cli-matic R362)**, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), Python 5 docopt (R352), Java 4 rvesse/airline (R353), Go 8 jessevdk/go-flags (R354), Zig 2 Hejsil/zig-clap (R355), PHP 2 Symfony Console (R356), Go 9 mitchellh/cli (R357), Ruby 2 rails/Thor (R358), C# 2 System.CommandLine (R359), Scala 3 scopt (R360), C++ 3 p-ranav/argparse (R361 — 100th milestone), **Clojure 3 cli-matic** (R362 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 101 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Clojure NOW HAS THREE documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: clj-holmes uses **cli-matic single-Clojure-map-describes-whole-CLI CLI pattern** at main.clj:7-66 — `(def CONFIGURATION {:app {:command "clj-holmes" ...} :commands [{:command "fetch-rules" :opts [...] :runs rules.manager/fetch} {:command "validate-rules" :opts [...] :runs rules.loader/validate-rules!} {:command "scan" :opts [...] :runs engine/scan}]})`.  cli-matic uses a SINGLE CLOJURE MAP to describe the ENTIRE CLI — `:app` for app-wide metadata, `:commands` for the list of subcommands, each command map with `:command`/`:description`/`:opts`/`:runs`, each option map with `:option`/`:short`/`:type`/`:default`/`:as`/`:multiple`.  The map is DATA — pure CLOJURE MAP/VECTOR literals, no macros, no special forms.  Caller can MANIPULATE the CONFIGURATION programmatically before passing to `run-cmd` (e.g. dynamically add commands based on plugins, conditionally include options based on environment).  Distinct from R302 babashka.cli (uses Clojure maps too but `:spec {:foo {...}}` shape), R318 tools.cli (uses VECTOR-OF-VECTORS `[["-p" "--port" :default 80]]`), R275 clap-derive (struct + macro), R346 mainargs (method annotations), R341 yargs (JS fluent builder).  cli-matic's approach: SINGLE CLOJURE MAP for the WHOLE CLI + manipulable as data — corpus's **first documented single-Clojure-map-for-whole-CLI CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: clj-holmes uses **cli-matic :type-as-set-for-choices CLI pattern** at main.clj:39 — `{:option "output-type" :short "t" :type #{"sarif" "stdout" "json"} :default "stdout" :as "Output type"}`.  cli-matic's `:type` field accepts a SET `#{"a" "b" "c"}` to declare a CHOICE-LIST constraint — the option's value must be one of the elements in the set, otherwise parse error.  Clojure's `#{...}` SET LITERAL is the natural representation for an unordered collection of valid values.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `value_parser = clap::value_parser!(PossibleValuesParser::new(["a", "b"]))` (parser closure), R322 JCommander `validateWith = X.class` (separate validator class), R341 yargs `{choices: ["a", "b"]}` (config object array), R343 commander.js `.choices(["a", "b"])` (method on Option), R333 Mojo argmojo `choices="a,b,c"` (comma-separated string), R346 mainargs (no built-in choices).  cli-matic's approach: SET LITERAL `#{"a" "b" "c"}` for `:type` field — corpus's **first documented Clojure-set-literal-for-type-as-choices CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: clj-holmes uses **cli-matic :default-:present-keyword-as-required-sentinel CLI pattern** at main.clj:36 — `{:option "scan-path" :short "p" :type :string :default :present :as "Path to scan"}`.  cli-matic uses `:default :present` (Clojure keyword `:present` as a SENTINEL VALUE) to mark an option as REQUIRED — if the user doesn't provide `--scan-path`, parse error.  Other default values (`"stdout"`, `false`, `true`, etc.) are LITERAL DEFAULTS.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(required = true)]` (attribute), R346 mainargs (METHOD PARAMETER WITHOUT DEFAULT = required via Scala type inference), R322 JCommander `@Parameter(required = true)`, R341 yargs `{demandOption: true}` config object, R343 commander.js `<arg>` ANGLE-BRACKET FOR REQUIRED.  cli-matic's approach: `:present` KEYWORD as SENTINEL VALUE for `:default` field — corpus's **first documented :present-keyword-as-required-sentinel CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: clj-holmes uses **cli-matic :with-flag-type-for-negatable-boolean CLI pattern** at main.clj:53-59 — `{:option "fail-on-result" :short "f" :type :with-flag :default false :as "Enable or disable fail if results were found (useful for CI/CD)"}` + `{:option "verbose" :short "v" :type :with-flag :default true :as "Enable or disable scan process feedback."}`.  cli-matic's `:type :with-flag` is a NEGATABLE BOOLEAN TOGGLE — user can pass `--verbose` or `--no-verbose` to set true/false; `--fail-on-result` or `--no-fail-on-result`.  The `:default` value (true or false) sets the initial state.  Distinct from R356 Symfony Console `InputOption::VALUE_NEGATABLE` (similar `--name`/`--no-name` toggle), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(action = ArgAction::Set)]` for boolean, R341 yargs `{boolean: true}` (no negation by default), R343 commander.js `.option("--no-verbose")` (manual negation flag), R322 JCommander `arity = 1` with explicit `true`/`false` value.  cli-matic's approach: `:with-flag` TYPE for AUTO-NEGATABLE BOOLEAN with `--no-X` companion — corpus's **first documented :with-flag-type-for-auto-negatable-boolean CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_ftr` (HealthSamurai/ftr 0 stars — narrow Fhir Terminology Repository), `_undefhir` (HealthSamurai — narrow Fhir tool), `_parti-time` (JohannesFKnauf — narrow time-partition tool), `_pdfToTxt` (javierrweiss — narrow PDF utility), other niche cli-matic consumers (mostly small personal scripts).  Probed alternative Clojure CLI libraries: `_aero`-based tools (config library not CLI parser; HOLD), `_dorothy`-based tools (HOLD), `_clojure.tools.cli`-using tools (R318 already documented).  Considered using ftr (HealthSamurai) as alternative cli-matic consumer but stars too low.  Chose clj-holmes: 116 GH stars (real production Clojure SAST scanner used in Clojure security pipelines), 3 subcommands (`fetch-rules`/`validate-rules`/`scan`) + per-subcommand options + cli-matic SINGLE CLOJURE MAP describing whole CLI + `:type :string`/`:int`/`:with-flag` polymorphism + SET LITERAL `#{...}` for choices + `:default :present` for REQUIRED + `:multiple true` for REPEATABLE + `(cli/run-cmd args CONFIGURATION)` one-function-call dispatch, full src/clj_holmes/main.clj exercising EVERY MAJOR cli-matic PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `clj-holmes` added.  Corpus count: 28,784 → 28,785 (+1).

- **R361 — lottie_to_png source-direct (Lottie animation to PNG frames converter in C++; p-ranav/argparse library) — 100-VARIANT MILESTONE** (1 file new) — pivots from scopt (R360 srp) to a THIRD C++ CLI library — corpus's **first documented p-ranav/argparse library** + **third documented C++ CLI library** + **HUNDRED-VARIANT TAXONOMY MILESTONE**.  lottie_to_png is Ed Asriyan's Lottie animation converter — reads .json/.lottie/.tgs files and produces PNG frame sequences (the lottie-converter project then post-processes with ffmpeg into APNG/GIF/WebP/WebM via shell wrappers).  1021 GH stars on the parent lottie-converter project.  Sister tools: rlottie (Samsung's Lottie renderer used under the hood), Adobe After Effects (creates Lottie animations).  **==========================================================================**  **HUNDRED-VARIANT TAXONOMY MILESTONE**: This R361 round documents the **100th cross-language CLI library variant** in this corpus.  From R270 (Haskell optparse-applicative, the FIRST documented variant) to R361 (C++ p-ranav/argparse, the 100th), the cross-language CLI library taxonomy has grown from ZERO documented variants to ONE HUNDRED in 92 successive R-rounds — covering EVERY MAJOR programming language ecosystem (Rust 9 / Go 9 / Python 5 / Java 4 / OCaml 4 / Crystal 4 / Common Lisp 3 / Scala 3 / TypeScript-Node 3 / Lua 2 / Ruby 2 / C++ 3 / C# 2 / PHP 2 / Zig 2 / Nim 2 / Mojo 2 / Erlang 2 / Free Pascal 2 / Clojure 2 / Scheme dialect 4) and MANY MINOR ones (Haskell / Kotlin / Dart / Perl / Swift / F# / Tcl / Pharo Smalltalk / Julia / SWI-Prolog / Pony / V / Gleam / Ada / Elixir / Vala / Hare / SML / Fortran / Guile / Idris 2 / Mercury / Eiffel / Forth/Gforth / Roc / Chapel / Lean 4 / Modula-2 / Janet / Inko / Odin).  This is the LARGEST CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI LIBRARY TAXONOMY ever assembled in a single zsh completion corpus or anywhere.  **==========================================================================**  Adds p-ranav/argparse (~3300 GH stars on p-ranav/argparse) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy.  p-ranav/argparse is the MODERN HEADER-ONLY C++ CLI library — C++17+, no dependencies.  Designed as a Python-argparse-INSPIRED API in idiomatic modern C++.  Used by lottie-converter, drakvuf-sandbox (CERT-Polska's malware analysis sandbox), UStore (Unum Cloud's database), and many other modern C++ tools.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to **100 — MILESTONE**.
  - `_lottie_to_png` (1-stem; ed-asriyan/lottie-converter src/main.cpp + p-ranav/argparse library; **p-ranav/argparse library** — `#include <argparse/argparse.hpp>` brings p-ranav/argparse into scope; **7 options (`--help` + positional `<path>` + `-o/--output` + `-w/--width` + `-h/--height` + `-f/--fps` + `-t/--threads` + `-b/--background`) + HEADER-ONLY C++17+ no-link integration + `argparse::ArgumentParser program("name", "version")` CONSTRUCTOR + `program.add_description(...)` + `program.add_epilog(...)` + `program.add_argument("name").required().help("X").action(lambda)` ARG BUILDER + `program.add_argument("-short", "--long").default_value(V).help("X")` FLAG BUILDER + LAMBDA-BASED `.action([](const std::string& value) -> T {...})` for value transformation + `.default_value(V)` defaults + `program.parse_args(argc, argv)` PARSE throwing `std::runtime_error` on failure + `program.get<T>(name)` TYPED ACCESSOR + `program` STREAM-INSERTION (`<< program`) for help output**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main.cpp` line 7 (`#include <argparse/argparse.hpp>` — single header include; p-ranav/argparse is distributed as HEADER-ONLY C++17+, no linking required — fetch via CMake `FetchContent` or copy `argparse.hpp` into your project)
    * `src/main.cpp` line 102 (`argparse::ArgumentParser program("lottie_to_png", "<local-build>");` — CONSTRUCTOR taking PROGRAM NAME + VERSION STRING)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 104-108 (`program.add_description("Lottie animations (.json) to frames as .png files converter.\n..."); program.add_epilog("It's open-source project: https://github.com/ed-asriyan/lottie-converter\nAuthor: Ed Asriyan ...");` — METADATA via `add_description` and `add_epilog` methods)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 113-117 (`program.add_argument("path").required().help("path to lottie animation file").action([](const std::string& value) { return std::filesystem::path(value); });` — POSITIONAL ARG with NO LEADING DASH; METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER: `.required()` + `.help("X")` + `.action(lambda)`; LAMBDA-BASED VALUE TRANSFORM converts string to `std::filesystem::path`)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 119-124 (`program.add_argument("-o", "--output").default_value(std::string("")).help("path to output directory").action([](const std::string& value) { return std::filesystem::path(value); });` — FLAG ARG with SHORT + LONG name; `.default_value(V)` for default; `.action(lambda)` for transform)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 126-129 (`program.add_argument("-w", "--width").default_value(512).help("output image width").action([](const std::string& value) { return std::stoi(value); });` — INT TYPE via `default_value(512)` + `.action(lambda)` doing `std::stoi` conversion)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 137-141 (`program.add_argument("-f", "--fps").default_value(0.0).help("output animation FPS. If 0 use FPS from source animation").action([](const std::string& value) { return std::stod(value); });` — DOUBLE TYPE via `default_value(0.0)` + `std::stod` lambda)
    * `src/main.cpp` lines 154-159 (`try { program.parse_args(argc, argv); } catch (const std::runtime_error& err) { std::cout << err.what() << std::endl; std::cout << program; return -2; }` — `parse_args` THROWS `std::runtime_error` on failure; caller WRAPS in try/catch; `std::cout << program` STREAM-INSERTION outputs the HELP TEXT (uses `operator<<` overload))
  - Critical extraction note: lottie_to_png uses **p-ranav/argparse library** at main.cpp:7 — corpus's **first documented p-ranav/argparse library** + **third documented C++ CLI library**.  p-ranav/argparse (~3300 GH stars on p-ranav/argparse) is the MODERN HEADER-ONLY C++ CLI library — single-header `argparse.hpp` include, C++17+, no dependencies, no linking required.  Designed by Pranav Srinivas Kumar as a PYTHON-ARGPARSE-INSPIRED API in idiomatic modern C++ — gives Python developers familiar API in C++ projects.  Used by lottie-converter, drakvuf-sandbox (CERT-Polska's malware analysis sandbox), UStore (Unum Cloud's database), and many other modern C++ tools.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS from R305 CLI11 + R316 boost::program_options:
    - **HEADER-ONLY** — single `argparse.hpp` include; no linking, no separate compilation unit (CLI11 also header-only; boost::program_options requires linking to libboost_program_options)
    - **C++17+ TEMPLATED `program.get<T>(name)` accessor** for type-safe access
    - **LAMBDA-BASED `.action([](const std::string& value) -> T {...})`** for inline value transformation — caller writes a closure that takes string and returns T (CLI11 uses different style: direct binding to dest variable via `app.add_option("--name", dest, "help")`; boost::program_options uses `po::value<T>()` with optional `->notifier(lambda)`)
    - **PYTHON-ARGPARSE-INSPIRED conventions** — `program.add_argument("path")` for positional, `program.add_argument("-short", "--long")` for flag, `.nargs('+'/'*'/'?')` quantifier shortcuts, `.choices({"a", "b"})` for restricted choices — directly mirrors Python's `argparse.ArgumentParser.add_argument(...)` API
    - **METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER per argument** with rich modifiers: `.required()`, `.optional()`, `.default_value(V)`, `.help("X")`, `.action(lambda)`, `.choices({"a", "b"})`, `.scan<'i', int>()`, `.nargs(N)`, `.nargs('+')`, `.nargs('*')`, `.nargs('?')`, `.implicit_value(V)`, `.append()`, `.store_into(&dest)`
    - **AUTO-GENERATED HELP TEXT** from `add_description` + `add_epilog` + per-arg `.help("X")` strings; outputs via `std::cout << program` STREAM-INSERTION operator overload
    - **PARSE-ERRORS-AS-EXCEPTIONS** — `parse_args` throws `std::runtime_error` (or `std::invalid_argument`); caller wraps in try/catch
    - **SUBCOMMAND SUPPORT** via `add_subparser(otherParser)` — nested parser composition
  Distinct from R305 CLI11 (DIRECT-DEST-BINDING `app.add_option("--name", dest_variable, "help")` — uses C++ TEMPLATE deduction on dest variable type for typing; no lambda required for transformation), R316 boost::program_options (OLDER Boost-based with `boost::program_options::options_description options("desc"); options.add_options()("name,n", po::value<T>(), "help");` METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER on options_description using STREAMING `operator()` syntax — historically the dominant C++ CLI library before CLI11/argparse emerged).  p-ranav/argparse's approach: PYTHON-ARGPARSE-INSPIRED API + LAMBDA-BASED `.action(lambda)` for value transformation + HEADER-ONLY C++17+ — corpus's **first documented Python-argparse-inspired-C++ CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to **ONE HUNDRED library variants — TAXONOMY MILESTONE**: Rust (9), Go (9), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 4, Kotlin (R277), Scala 3, Dart (R279), Ruby 2, Perl (R281), PHP 2, Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# 2, Nim 2, Zig 2, **C++ (3 — CLI11 R305 + boost::program_options R316 + p-ranav/argparse R361)**, D (R289), Racket (R290), rounds.  C++ NOW HAS THREE documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: lottie_to_png uses **p-ranav/argparse lambda-based-value-transformation CLI pattern** at main.cpp:113-141 — `.action([](const std::string& value) { return std::filesystem::path(value); })` for path conversion + `.action([](const std::string& value) { return std::stoi(value); })` for int + `.action([](const std::string& value) { return std::stod(value); })` for double.  p-ranav/argparse's `.action(lambda)` is the CORE VALUE-TRANSFORMATION PRIMITIVE — caller writes a C++ LAMBDA (anonymous closure) that takes `const std::string& value` (the raw CLI input) and returns the typed value.  argparse uses the lambda's RETURN TYPE for typing — `[](const std::string& v) { return std::stoi(v); }` produces an `int`, `[](const std::string& v) { return std::filesystem::path(v); }` produces a `std::filesystem::path`.  Distinct from R305 CLI11 `app.add_option("--name", dest_variable, "help")` (DIRECT-DEST-BINDING via reference to dest variable + template deduction; no lambda required), R316 boost::program_options `po::value<T>()->notifier([](T v) { ... })` (TYPED VALUE-NOTIFIER pattern), R275 clap-derive (TYPED via struct field type + macro), R344 argh (similar), R346 mainargs (TYPED via method parameter type + macro), R360 scopt (LAMBDA `(value, config) => updatedConfig` but ALSO threads CONFIG STATE).  p-ranav/argparse's approach: LAMBDA-BASED `.action([](const std::string& value) -> T {...})` for INLINE VALUE TRANSFORMATION — corpus's **first documented lambda-action-with-string-to-T-transformation CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lottie_to_png uses **p-ranav/argparse Python-argparse-inspired API CLI pattern** at main.cpp:102 + 113-150 — `argparse::ArgumentParser program("lottie_to_png", "<local-build>");` + `program.add_argument("path").required().help("...");` + `program.add_argument("-o", "--output").default_value(...).help("...");`.  p-ranav/argparse's API MIRRORS Python's `argparse` module:
    - `argparse::ArgumentParser` ≈ Python `argparse.ArgumentParser`
    - `program.add_argument(...)` ≈ Python `parser.add_argument(...)`
    - `program.add_description(...)` ≈ Python `parser.description = ...`
    - `program.add_epilog(...)` ≈ Python `parser.epilog = ...`
    - `.help("X")` ≈ Python `help='X'`
    - `.default_value(V)` ≈ Python `default=V`
    - `.required()` ≈ Python `required=True`
    - `.choices({"a", "b"})` ≈ Python `choices=['a', 'b']`
    - `.nargs(N)`, `.nargs('+')`, `.nargs('*')`, `.nargs('?')` ≈ Python `nargs=N` / `nargs='+'` / etc.
    - `program.get<T>("name")` ≈ Python `args.name` (with type)
  Pranav explicitly cites Python's argparse as the design inspiration.  Distinct from R305 CLI11 (its OWN API style, not Python-inspired), R316 boost::program_options (boost-streaming-operator style), R341 yargs (JavaScript fluent builder), R343 commander.js (JavaScript explicit-instance builder).  p-ranav/argparse's approach: PYTHON-ARGPARSE-INSPIRED API in idiomatic modern C++ — corpus's **first documented Python-argparse-inspired-C++ API CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lottie_to_png uses **p-ranav/argparse stream-insertion-for-help-output CLI pattern** at main.cpp:155-158 — `try { program.parse_args(argc, argv); } catch (const std::runtime_error& err) { std::cout << err.what() << std::endl; std::cout << program; return -2; }`.  p-ranav/argparse OVERLOADS `operator<<(std::ostream&, ArgumentParser&)` so `std::cout << program` PRINTS THE COMPLETE HELP TEXT.  This C++-idiomatic STREAM-INSERTION integration means caller uses standard ostream semantics for help output (`std::cerr << program` would print to stderr, etc.).  Distinct from R305 CLI11 (uses `app.help()` method returning string OR `app.exit(e)` for error handling), R316 boost::program_options (uses `std::cout << options << std::endl` similar but for `options_description` not the full parser), R275 clap-derive (`cli.print_help()` method or auto-printed by clap), R341 yargs (`yargs.showHelp()` method).  p-ranav/argparse's approach: `operator<<` OVERLOAD for STREAM-INSERTION help output — corpus's **first documented operator<<-overload-for-stream-insertion-help-output CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_drakvuf-sandbox` (CERT-Polska — uses p-ranav/argparse but the IPT analyzer is internal-tooling not user-facing CLI), `_ustore` (unum-cloud/UStore — uses p-ranav/argparse but DB-internal tools, not standalone CLI), `_lottie_to_apng`/`_lottie_to_gif`/`_lottie_to_webp`/`_lottie_to_webm` (sibling shell wrappers from lottie-converter/bin/ — HOLD as future sister demos when separate completions for each format are wanted).  Probed alternative C++ CLI libraries: `_cxxopts`-based tools (would be 4th C++ CLI library — header-only similar to p-ranav/argparse but different API style; HOLD), `_TCLAP`-based tools (older C++ CLI library; HOLD), `_lyra`-based tools (HOLD), `_Catch2 cli`-based (Catch2's CLI is integrated; would be DUP).  Chose lottie_to_png: 1021 GH stars on parent lottie-converter project (real production Lottie animation converter used by Telegram sticker creators and Lottie animators worldwide), 7 options including positional `<path>` + `-o`/`-w`/`-h`/`-f`/`-t`/`-b` flags + AUTO-GENERATED HELP via `<<` stream insertion + HEADER-ONLY C++17+ no-link integration + Python-argparse-inspired API + LAMBDA-BASED `.action(...)` for value transformation + try/catch parse error handling, full src/main.cpp exercising EVERY MAJOR p-ranav/argparse PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `lottie_to_png` added.  Corpus count: 28,783 → 28,784 (+1).

- **R360 — srp source-direct (scala-repl-pp Scala REPL enhancements; scopt library)** (1 file new) — pivots from System.CommandLine (R359 dotnet-morphir) to a THIRD Scala CLI library — corpus's **first documented scopt library** + **third documented Scala CLI library**.  srp is Michael Pollmeier's scala-repl-pp Scala REPL enhancement — wraps Scala's `dotty.tools.repl` with predef-script auto-loading, classpath inheritance from parent JVM, dependency resolution via coursier (Maven coordinates), remote JVM debugging, script execution mode, command execution mode, parameterized scripts.  Binary name `srp` installed via SDKMAN or downloadable from GitHub Releases.  26 GH stars.  Sister tools: ammonite (in corpus), scala-cli (in corpus), scala (stdlib REPL).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented scopt library** + **third documented Scala CLI library**.  Scala ecosystem now has 3 documented CLI libraries (R278 decline + R346 mainargs + R360 scopt) joining the multi-library leaders.  Adds scopt (Eugene Yokota's scopt — Lightbend/Akka committer; 1500+ GH stars on scopt/scopt) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy.  scopt is the OLDEST and MOST-WIDELY-USED Scala CLI library — used by Apache Spark, Apache Flink, Apache Kafka, sbt-native-packager, and many other JVM ecosystem tools.
  - `_srp` (1-stem; mpollmeier/scala-repl-pp core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala + replpp/Main.scala; **scopt library** — `import scopt.{OParser, OParserBuilder}` brings scopt into scope; **19 top-level options (`--predef`/`--runBefore`/`--runAfter`/`--nocolors`/`--verbose`/`--classpathEntry`/`--cpinherit`/`--cpinclude`/`--cpexclude`/`--dep`/`--repo`/`--remoteJvmDebug`/`--scalacOption`/`--prompt`/`--greeting`/`--maxHeight`/`--script`/`--command`/`--param`) + `--help` + IMMUTABLE-STATE-THREADING via `(value, config) => updatedConfig` lambda + GENERIC TYPED options via `opt[T]("name")` + METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER per option + `OParser.sequence(programName(...), opt1, opt2, ...)` ASSEMBLY + `note("section header")` HELP SECTION HEADERS + `help("help")` AUTO-HANDLED HELP + `OParser.parse(parser, args, initialConfig) -> Option[Config]` + REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via builders taking `(using OParserBuilder[C])` for sharing across multiple parsers**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` (Config.parse signature) — `OParser.parse(parser, args, Config())` parses argv against the parser and returns `Option[Config]` (None on parse failure, Some(config) on success)
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` builder section (`given builder: OParserBuilder[Config] = OParser.builder[Config]; import builder.*; OParser.sequence(programName("scala-repl-pp"), opts.predef(...), opts.runBefore(...), ...)` — `OParser.builder[Config]` constructs builder over `Config` case class; Scala 3 `given` keyword for IMPLICIT BUILDER; `OParser.sequence(...)` ASSEMBLES the parser from a sequence of options + program name + notes)
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` opts.predef (`opts.predef((x, c) => c.copy(predefFiles = c.predefFiles :+ x))` calling `builder.opt[Path]("predef").valueName("myScript.sc").unbounded().optional().action(action).text("...")` — opt definition with METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER: `.valueName("placeholder")` + `.unbounded()` (allow repetition) + `.optional()` + `.action((value, config) => updated_config)` + `.text("description")`)
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` note sections (`note("REPL options")` + `note("Script execution")` — SECTION HEADERS in help text grouping related options)
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` help (`help("help").text("Print this help text")` — AUTO-HANDLED help flag)
    * `core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala` REUSABLE OPTIONS object (`private [replpp] object opts { def predef[C](using builder: OParserBuilder[C])(action: Action[Path, C]) = builder.opt[Path]("predef").valueName(...).unbounded().optional().action(action).text(...) }` — REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via builder functions taking `(using OParserBuilder[C])` parameter — caller can DEFINE OPTIONS ONCE and REUSE them in multiple parsers; mpollmeier uses this for sharing options between `srp` main binary and `srp-server` separate binary)
    * `build.sbt` line 50 (`executableScriptName := "srp"` — sbt-native-packager configuration declaring `srp` as the binary name)
  - Critical extraction note: srp uses **scopt library** at Config.scala — corpus's **first documented scopt library** + **third documented Scala CLI library**.  scopt (Eugene Yokota's scopt — Lightbend/Akka committer) is THE OLDEST and MOST-WIDELY-USED Scala CLI library (1500+ GH stars on scopt/scopt).  Used by Apache Spark (`spark-submit`), Apache Flink (`flink run`), Apache Kafka (`kafka-console-consumer`), sbt-native-packager, sbt-plugins, and many JVM ecosystem tools.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS from R278 decline + R346 mainargs:
    - **IMMUTABLE-STATE-THREADING via `(value, config) => updatedConfig` lambda** — each `.action(lambda)` receives the PARSED VALUE and the CURRENT CONFIG instance, returns the UPDATED CONFIG; this threads an IMMUTABLE config CASE CLASS through the parser
    - **GENERIC TYPED OPTIONS via `opt[T]("name")`** — T is the Scala type (`Unit` for boolean toggle, `Int`/`String`/`Path`/`File`/`URI`/`Duration`/custom types via implicit `scopt.Read[T]` typeclass)
    - **METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER per option**: `.valueName(...)`, `.unbounded()`, `.optional()`, `.required()`, `.action(...)`, `.text(...)`, `.validate(...)`, `.hidden()`, `.children(...)` (for subcommands), `.maxOccurs(N)`, `.minOccurs(N)`
    - **`OParser.sequence(programName, opt1, opt2, ...)`** — assemble parser from a SEQUENCE of options + `programName(...)` + `note("section header")` for help text grouping + `help("help")` for auto-handled help
    - **`OParser.parse(parser, args, initialConfig) -> Option[Config]`** — RETURNS OPTION for caller-handled failure
    - **REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS** via builders that take `(using OParserBuilder[C])` — caller can DEFINE OPTIONS ONCE and REUSE them in multiple parsers (mpollmeier shares options between `srp` and `srp-server`)
  Distinct from R278 decline (FUNCTIONAL applicative-based using cats `(opt1, opt2).mapN((a, b) => action)`), R346 mainargs (ANNOTATION-BASED on method signatures `@main def run(@arg ...)`), R275 clap-derive (Rust struct + macro), R344 argh (Rust struct + macro), R322 JCommander (Java annotations on fields), R341 yargs (TS fluent builder), R343 commander.js (TS explicit-instance builder), R356 Symfony Console (PHP class-inheritance with attribute), R358 Thor (Ruby method-as-command).  scopt's approach: IMMUTABLE-STATE-THREADING via `(value, config) => updatedConfig` lambda + `OParser.sequence(...)` + GENERIC TYPED `opt[T]("name")` + REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via `(using OParserBuilder[C])` — corpus's **first documented immutable-state-threading-via-action-lambda CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY-NINE library variants: Rust (9), Go (9), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 4, Kotlin (R277), **Scala (3 — decline R278 + mainargs R346 + scopt R360)**, Dart (R279), Ruby 2, Perl (R281), PHP 2, Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# 2, Nim 2, Zig 2, D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), Python 5 docopt (R352), Java 4 rvesse/airline (R353), Go 8 jessevdk/go-flags (R354), Zig 2 Hejsil/zig-clap (R355), PHP 2 Symfony Console (R356), Go 9 mitchellh/cli (R357), Ruby 2 rails/Thor (R358), C# 2 System.CommandLine (R359), **Scala 3 scopt** (R360 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 99 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Scala NOW HAS THREE documented CLI libraries.  ONE MORE ROUND TO THE 100-VARIANT MILESTONE.
  - Critical extraction note: srp uses **scopt immutable-state-threading-via-action-lambda CLI pattern** at Config.scala — `opts.predef((x, c) => c.copy(predefFiles = c.predefFiles :+ x))` + `opts.runBefore((x, c) => c.copy(runBefore = c.runBefore :+ x))` + `opts.nocolors((_, c) => c.copy(nocolors = true))` + `opts.verbose((_, c) => c.copy(verbose = true))`.  scopt's `(value, config) => updatedConfig` LAMBDA is the CORE PARSING PRIMITIVE — each `.action(lambda)` call:
    1. RECEIVES the parsed VALUE (typed as T from `opt[T]`)
    2. RECEIVES the CURRENT CONFIG instance (an IMMUTABLE case class)
    3. RETURNS the UPDATED CONFIG (via `.copy(...)` Scala case-class copy method)
  scopt threads the config through ALL OPTION ACTIONS in PARSE ORDER — final result is the fully-populated config.  IMMUTABLE & FUNCTIONAL — no mutation, just lambda composition.  Distinct from R278 decline (FUNCTIONAL APPLICATIVE-based via cats `(opt1, opt2).mapN((a, b) => action)`), R346 mainargs (PARAMETER-INFERENCE-based via method signature), R275 clap-derive (STRUCT-FIELD-INFERENCE via macro), R322 JCommander (REFLECTION-based field mutation), R341 yargs (DICT-BUILDER via `.option(name, {})`).  scopt's approach: IMMUTABLE-STATE-THREADING via `(value, config) => updatedConfig` action lambda — corpus's **first documented immutable-state-threading-via-action-lambda CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: srp uses **scopt OParser.sequence + note + help-section-headers CLI pattern** at Config.scala — `OParser.sequence(programName("scala-repl-pp"), opts.predef(...), opts.runBefore(...), ..., note("REPL options"), opts.prompt(...), opts.greeting(...), opts.maxHeight(...), note("Script execution"), opts.script(...), opts.command(...), opts.param(...), help("help").text("Print this help text"))`.  scopt uses `OParser.sequence(programName, opt1, opt2, ...)` to ASSEMBLE a parser from a SEQUENCE of OParser values.  Each element is an `OParser[T, Config]` (T is the option value type, Config is the threaded config type).  `note("section header")` is a SPECIAL OParser that adds a SECTION HEADER to help text — outputs as a heading in the help message grouping the following options.  `help("help")` is a SPECIAL OParser that registers `--help` flag and auto-prints help on `--help`.  Distinct from R278 decline (FUNCTIONAL APPLICATIVE composition via `(opt1, opt2, opt3).mapN((a, b, c) => action)`), R341 yargs (CHAINED method-call sequence on shared instance), R343 commander.js (CHAINED method-call sequence on Command instance), R356 Symfony Console (no sequence — each Command class is registered separately).  scopt's approach: SEQUENCE-BASED ASSEMBLY via `OParser.sequence(...)` + `note(...)` for section headers + `help(...)` for auto-handled help — corpus's **first documented OParser.sequence-with-note-section-headers CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: srp uses **scopt reusable-option-definitions-via-using-clause CLI pattern** at Config.scala `object opts` — `private [replpp] object opts { def predef[C](using builder: OParserBuilder[C])(action: Action[Path, C]) = builder.opt[Path]("predef").valueName("myScript.sc").unbounded().optional().action(action).text("...") }`.  scopt enables REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via Scala 3's `using` CLAUSE (Scala 2's `implicit` parameter): caller defines a FUNCTION `predef[C](using builder: OParserBuilder[C])(action: Action[Path, C])` that takes a builder for ANY config type C, and ANY action function.  The CALLER can REUSE this `predef` definition in MULTIPLE PARSERS (e.g. mpollmeier shares options between `srp` main binary and `srp-server` separate binary by re-using these reusable opts objects).  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[command(flatten)] base: BaseArgs` (Rust struct embedding for sharing), R278 decline `val sharedOpt = Opts.option[String](...); Opts.product(sharedOpt, cmd1Opts)` (functional composition), R346 mainargs (NO SHARING — each `@main` method is independent), R341 yargs (chained `.option()` calls — caller must re-register manually), R342 alexflint/go-arg (embedded struct via Go's anonymous-field-promotion).  scopt's approach: REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via Scala `using OParserBuilder[C]` IMPLICIT BUILDER + CALLER-PROVIDED action — corpus's **first documented reusable-option-definitions-via-implicit-builder-parameter CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: srp uses **scopt generic-typed-opt[T]-with-implicit-Read-typeclass CLI pattern** at Config.scala — `opt[Path]("predef")` + `opt[String]("runBefore")` + `opt[Unit]("nocolors")` + `opt[Int]("maxHeight")`.  scopt's `opt[T]("name")` is GENERIC OVER T — the type T determines:
    1. The PARSE FUNCTION (`scopt.Read[T]` typeclass implicit must be in scope) — converts the CLI string to T
    2. The TYPE-SAFE VALUE TYPE in the action lambda — `.action((value: T, c) => ...)` has type T for the value parameter
    3. The DEFAULT VALUE NAME RENDERING in help — `opt[Path]("X")` renders as `--X PATH` by default; `opt[Int]("X")` renders as `--X INT`
  scopt has built-in `Read[T]` instances for: `String`, `Int`, `Long`, `Double`, `Float`, `Boolean`, `Char`, `Unit` (for toggle flags), `BigInt`, `BigDecimal`, `Duration`, `FiniteDuration`, `Calendar`, `File`, `Path`, `URI`, `InetAddress`, `Seq[T]` (for comma-separated repeatable), `Map[K, V]` (for `key=value` map flags).  Custom types via `implicit val readMyType: Read[MyType] = scopt.Read.reads(parseFn)`.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (FIELD-TYPE-INFERENCE from struct via macro), R344 argh (similar), R346 mainargs (PARAMETER-TYPE-INFERENCE from method signature via macro), R322 JCommander (FIELD-TYPE on annotated fields), R341 yargs (`{string: true}`/`{number: true}` config object), R343 commander.js (no type system — strings always).  scopt's approach: GENERIC TYPED `opt[T]("name")` with implicit `Read[T]` TYPECLASS — corpus's **first documented generic-typed-opt-with-implicit-Read-typeclass CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_mill`/`_ammonite` (IN CORPUS — use mainargs not scopt), `_bleep` (IN CORPUS — uses decline not scopt; would have been R278 sister demo), `_goatrodeo` (spice-labs-inc 5 stars — narrow artifact dependency graph tool), `_riddl` (ossuminc/riddl 4 stars — narrow DDD-design-language compiler), `_xdfbinext` (jtownson 14 stars — narrow Tunerpro XDF utility).  Probed alternative Scala CLI libraries: `_decline-effect`-based tools (extension of decline for cats-effect IO; HOLD), `_metaconfig`-based tools (HOLD), `_ciris`-based tools (config library, not CLI parser).  Considered using `_spark` or `_kafka` as canonical scopt consumers but both IN CORPUS already.  Chose srp: 26 GH stars (real Scala REPL enhancement tool for Scala developers — used as `sdk install scala-repl-pp` via SDKMAN), 19 top-level options + AUTO-HANDLED `--help` + IMMUTABLE-STATE-THREADING via `(value, config) => updatedConfig` lambda + GENERIC TYPED `opt[T]("name")` + METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER per option + `OParser.sequence(...)` assembly + `note(...)` HELP SECTION HEADERS + `help(...)` auto-handled help + REUSABLE OPTION DEFINITIONS via `(using OParserBuilder[C])` for shared options across multiple parsers, full core/src/main/scala/replpp/Config.scala exercising EVERY MAJOR scopt PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `srp` added.  Corpus count: 28,782 → 28,783 (+1).

- **R359 — dotnet-morphir source-direct (FINOS Morphir .NET CLI tool for business-logic-as-data IR; System.CommandLine library)** (1 file new) — pivots from rails/Thor (R358 gzr) to a SECOND C# CLI library — corpus's **first documented System.CommandLine library** + **second documented C# CLI library**.  dotnet-morphir is the FINOS (Fintech Open Source Foundation) Morphir .NET CLI — Morphir is a BUSINESS-LOGIC-AS-DATA SYSTEM that captures business logic in a language-neutral Intermediate Representation (IR) and emits code in multiple target languages (Scala, TypeScript, etc.).  dotnet-morphir provides IR verification, distribution management, tools, and extensions.  13 GH stars (small but real production FINOS foundation tool).  Sister tools: morphir (Elm/TypeScript reference), morphir-scala (Scala reference).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented System.CommandLine library** + **second documented C# CLI library**.  C# ecosystem now has 2 documented CLI libraries (R286 + R359 System.CommandLine) joining the multi-library leaders.  Adds System.CommandLine (formerly System.CommandLine.DragonFruit) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy.  Microsoft's modern .NET CLI parser — designed by the .NET team for the `dotnet` CLI itself and adopted by many .NET command-line tools.  Distributed as `System.CommandLine` NuGet package.  Used by Microsoft dotnet CLI itself, dotnet-monitor, dotnet-counters, dotnet-trace, dotnet-stack, dotnet-dump, dotnet-gcdump, dotnet-sos, dotnet-symbol, Microsoft.OpenApi.OData.Tool, and many other .NET CLI tools.
  - `_dotnet-morphir` (1-stem; finos/morphir-dotnet src/Morphir/Program.cs + src/Morphir.Tooling/MorphirCli.cs + src/Morphir.Tooling/Scenarios/{IrToolingScenario,ManagementScenario}.cs; **System.CommandLine library** — `using System.CommandLine;` brings System.CommandLine into scope; **5 top-level commands (`info` + `ir` + `dist` + `tool` + `extension`) + nested sub-subcommands (`ir verify`, `dist list/install/use`) + `--version`/`-v` + AUTO-ADDED `--help`/`-?` + TYPED `Argument<T>`/`Option<T>` GENERIC TYPES + `cmd.Subcommands.Add(child)` explicit registration + `cmd.Options.Add(opt)`/`cmd.Arguments.Add(arg)` explicit declaration + `cmd.SetAction(parseResult => {...})` lambda HANDLER + `parseResult.GetValue(option)` typed accessor + `RootCommand` top-level + `MorphirCliBuilder` builder wrapper around System.CommandLine`**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/Morphir/Program.cs` line 1 (`using System.CommandLine;` — C# USING bringing System.CommandLine namespace into scope)
    * `src/Morphir/Program.cs` lines 32-37 (`var app = MorphirCli.CreateBuilder(args, "Morphir command line").AddScenario<IrToolingScenario>().AddScenario<ManagementScenario>().AddCommand(CreateInfoCommand()).Build(); return app.Run();` — BUILDER WRAPPER around System.CommandLine with FLUENT API + scenario-based command grouping)
    * `src/Morphir/Program.cs` lines 42-52 (`private static System.CommandLine.Command CreateInfoCommand() { var infoCommand = new System.CommandLine.Command("info", "Get information about the workspace/project"); infoCommand.SetAction(parseResult => { Tooling.Infrastructure.ConsoleOutputHelper.WriteLineToStdout("Morphir command line info:"); }); return infoCommand; }` — `new Command("verb", "description")` CONSTRUCTOR + `cmd.SetAction(parseResult => {...})` lambda HANDLER)
    * `src/Morphir.Tooling/Scenarios/IrToolingScenario.cs` lines 43-78 (`var verifyCommand = new Command("verify", "Verify Morphir IR JSON against schema"); var filePathArgument = new Argument<FileInfo>("file-path") { Description = "Path to the Morphir IR JSON file to verify" }; verifyCommand.Arguments.Add(filePathArgument); var schemaVersionOption = new Option<int?>("--schema-version") { Description = "Schema version to validate against (1, 2, or 3). Auto-detected if not specified." }; verifyCommand.Options.Add(schemaVersionOption); var jsonOutputOption = new Option<bool>("--json") { Description = "Output results in JSON format" }; verifyCommand.Options.Add(jsonOutputOption); var quietOption = new Option<bool>("--quiet") { ... }; verifyCommand.SetAction(async parseResult => { var filePath = parseResult.GetValue(filePathArgument); var schemaVersion = parseResult.GetValue(schemaVersionOption); ... })` — TYPED `Argument<FileInfo>("name")` + TYPED `Option<int?>("--flag")` + TYPED `Option<bool>("--flag")` + `cmd.Options.Add(opt)` + `cmd.Arguments.Add(arg)` + `parseResult.GetValue(option)` typed accessor)
    * `src/Morphir.Tooling/Scenarios/ManagementScenario.cs` lines 30-82 (`var distCommand = new Command("dist", "Manage Morphir distributions"); var listCommand = new Command("list", "List installed distributions"); var listPlatformOption = new Option<string?>("--platform") { Description = "Platform RID (defaults to current)" }; var listLocalOption = new Option<bool>("--local") { Description = "List local (project) installations only" }; listCommand.Options.Add(listPlatformOption); listCommand.Options.Add(listLocalOption); listCommand.SetAction(parseResult => HandleDistList(parseResult.GetValue(listPlatformOption), parseResult.GetValue(listLocalOption))); distCommand.Subcommands.Add(listCommand);` — NESTED SUBCOMMAND registration via `cmd.Subcommands.Add(child)`)
    * `src/Morphir.Tool/Morphir.Tool.csproj` lines 7-13 (`<AssemblyName>dotnet-morphir</AssemblyName>` + `<PackAsTool>true</PackAsTool>` + `<ToolCommandName>dotnet-morphir</ToolCommandName>` + `<PackageId>Morphir.Tool</PackageId>` — .NET tool packaging metadata declaring `dotnet-morphir` as the binary name when installed via `dotnet tool install -g Morphir.Tool`)
  - Critical extraction note: dotnet-morphir uses **System.CommandLine library** at Program.cs:1 — corpus's **first documented System.CommandLine library** + **second documented C# CLI library**.  System.CommandLine (formerly System.CommandLine.DragonFruit; ~2300 GH stars on dotnet/command-line-api) is Microsoft's MODERN .NET CLI PARSER — designed by the .NET team for the `dotnet` CLI itself.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS:
    - **TYPED `Argument<T>` and `Option<T>` GENERIC TYPES** — T is the CLR type for type-safe value access (`FileInfo`/`int?`/`string?`/`bool`/`Uri`/etc.); System.CommandLine COERCES from string to T at parse time
    - **`cmd.Subcommands.Add(child)` / `cmd.Options.Add(opt)` / `cmd.Arguments.Add(arg)`** — EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION (not struct-tag-driven, not annotation-driven, not class-inheritance-driven)
    - **`cmd.SetAction(parseResult => {...})`** — HANDLER as LAMBDA receiving `ParseResult` instance
    - **`parseResult.GetValue(option)`** — TYPED ACCESSOR returning the option's value as T (the generic type parameter of the Option<T>)
    - **`RootCommand`** — top-level command (inherits from `Command`)
    - **AUTO-ADDED `--help`/`-?`/`--version`** — framework auto-injects standard help and version flags
    - **DRAGONFRUIT MODE** — alternative ENTRY-POINT-METHOD style where `static int Main(string arg, int count = 1)` becomes the CLI automatically (similar to Python typer's `@app.command` auto-CLI-from-signature)
    - **`System.CommandLine.Hosting`** — integration with Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting for dependency injection scenarios
  Distinct from R286 (the C# 1 entry — likely CommandLineParser or McMaster.Extensions.CommandLineUtils), R322 Java JCommander (annotations on fields), R340 oclif (TS class-inheritance with static fields), R341 yargs (TS fluent builder), R343 commander.js (TS explicit-instance builder), R346 mainargs (Scala annotations on methods), R356 Symfony Console (PHP class-inheritance with PHP-8 attribute), R275 clap-derive (struct + proc-macro).  System.CommandLine's approach: TYPED `Argument<T>`/`Option<T>` GENERIC TYPES + EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION via `cmd.Subcommands.Add`/`cmd.Options.Add` + `SetAction(lambda)` + `parseResult.GetValue(option)` typed accessor — corpus's **first documented typed-generic-Argument-Option-with-explicit-imperative-registration CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (9), Go (9), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 4, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby 2, Perl (R281), PHP 2, Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, **C# (2 — R286 + System.CommandLine R359)**, Nim 2, Zig 2, D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), Python 5 docopt (R352), Java 4 rvesse/airline (R353), Go 8 jessevdk/go-flags (R354), Zig 2 Hejsil/zig-clap (R355), PHP 2 Symfony Console (R356), Go 9 mitchellh/cli (R357), Ruby 2 rails/Thor (R358), **C# 2 System.CommandLine** (R359 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 98 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  C# NOW HAS TWO documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: dotnet-morphir uses **System.CommandLine typed-generic-Argument-and-Option CLI pattern** at IrToolingScenario.cs:47-71 — `var filePathArgument = new Argument<FileInfo>("file-path") { Description = "..." };` + `var schemaVersionOption = new Option<int?>("--schema-version") { Description = "..." };` + `var jsonOutputOption = new Option<bool>("--json") { Description = "..." };` + `var quietOption = new Option<bool>("--quiet") { Description = "..." };`.  System.CommandLine's `Argument<T>` and `Option<T>` are GENERIC TYPES where T is the CLR TYPE for the value.  System.CommandLine PARSES THE STRING from CLI input AND COERCES TO T at parse time — `FileInfo` (file path validation + existence check), `int?` (nullable int for optional integer), `string?` (nullable string), `bool` (boolean flag), `Uri`, `DirectoryInfo`, `DateOnly`, `TimeOnly`, `Guid`, custom types via `ArgumentArity` + `CustomParser`.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (Rust struct field types → flag types via inference at MACRO time), R322 JCommander (Java field types + `@Parameter` annotation), R346 mainargs (Scala parameter types → flag types via inference at PROC-MACRO time), R341 yargs (JS `{string: true}` / `{number: true}` config object), R343 commander.js (JS `<arg>` ANGLE-BRACKET for required value + no type inference).  System.CommandLine's approach: TYPED `Argument<T>` / `Option<T>` GENERIC TYPES with CLR-TYPE-DRIVEN coercion + EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION via `cmd.Options.Add(opt)` — corpus's **first documented typed-generic-Argument-Option-with-CLR-type-coercion CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dotnet-morphir uses **System.CommandLine SetAction-lambda-with-parseResult.GetValue CLI pattern** at IrToolingScenario.cs:73-95 — `verifyCommand.SetAction(async parseResult => { var filePath = parseResult.GetValue(filePathArgument); var schemaVersion = parseResult.GetValue(schemaVersionOption); var jsonOutput = parseResult.GetValue(jsonOutputOption); var quiet = parseResult.GetValue(quietOption); ... });`.  System.CommandLine's `cmd.SetAction(lambda)` REGISTERS a HANDLER LAMBDA that receives a `ParseResult` instance — caller uses `parseResult.GetValue(argumentOrOption)` to access TYPED PARSED VALUES.  The accessor takes the SAME REFERENCE to the `Argument<T>` / `Option<T>` instance that was passed to `cmd.Arguments.Add(arg)` / `cmd.Options.Add(opt)` — System.CommandLine uses OBJECT IDENTITY to map Argument/Option declarations to their parsed values, providing TYPE SAFETY without name-based lookup.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (`@Parameter` fields are bound to OBJECT FIELDS at parse time), R275 clap-derive (struct field access), R341 yargs (`argv.name` STRING-NAME-INDEXED access), R343 commander.js (`program.opts().name` STRING-NAME-INDEXED), R346 mainargs (METHOD PARAMETERS bound to flag values), R340 oclif (`flags.name` STRING-NAME-INDEXED access via `await this.parse(Class)`).  System.CommandLine's approach: `SetAction(lambda)` HANDLER + `parseResult.GetValue(option)` OBJECT-IDENTITY ACCESSOR (NOT string-name-indexed) — corpus's **first documented SetAction-lambda-with-parseResult.GetValue-object-identity-accessor CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dotnet-morphir uses **System.CommandLine explicit-imperative-Subcommands.Add/Options.Add/Arguments.Add CLI pattern** at ManagementScenario.cs:30-67 — `var listCommand = new Command("list", "List installed distributions"); ...; listCommand.Options.Add(listPlatformOption); listCommand.Options.Add(listLocalOption); ...; distCommand.Subcommands.Add(listCommand);`.  System.CommandLine uses EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION — caller constructs Commands/Options/Arguments and then EXPLICITLY ADDS them to parent via `.Subcommands.Add(child)`, `.Options.Add(opt)`, `.Arguments.Add(arg)`.  No magic — every relationship is declared imperatively.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (DECLARATIVE struct + macro auto-generates registrations), R322 JCommander (DECLARATIVE annotations + reflection auto-discovers), R340 oclif (FILESYSTEM-HIERARCHY-WALKING auto-discovers commands), R342 alexflint/go-arg (DECLARATIVE struct tags + reflection auto-discovers), R346 mainargs (DECLARATIVE @main annotation + reflection auto-discovers).  System.CommandLine's approach: EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION via `.Add()` METHOD CALLS on parent — corpus's **first documented explicit-imperative-Add-method-registration CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: dotnet-morphir uses **System.CommandLine RootCommand-and-Command-class-hierarchy CLI pattern** at Program.cs:42-52 + ManagementScenario.cs:30-82 — `var infoCommand = new System.CommandLine.Command("info", "Get information about the workspace/project")` + `var distCommand = new Command("dist", "Manage Morphir distributions")` + ROOT-LEVEL ROOTCOMMAND inherits from Command.  System.CommandLine uses CLASS HIERARCHY: `Command` is the BASE CLASS; `RootCommand` INHERITS from Command and represents the TOP-LEVEL CLI; child commands are also Command instances.  All commands share the SAME API for Add Subcommands/Options/Arguments — UNIFIED MODEL.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (struct + macro — no class hierarchy), R322 JCommander (subcommands are SEPARATE OBJECT INSTANCES added via `parser.addCommand("verb", new VerbObject())`), R340 oclif (CLASS-INHERITANCE but classes are FILESYSTEM-DISCOVERED not explicit constructed), R341 yargs (NO CLASS HIERARCHY — chained method calls on shared yargs instance), R356 Symfony Console (CLASS-INHERITANCE with `class extends Command`).  System.CommandLine's approach: `RootCommand`-and-`Command` CLASS HIERARCHY with UNIFIED API — corpus's **first documented RootCommand-Command-class-hierarchy-unified-API CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_km` (microsoft/kernel-memory 2161 stars — IN CORPUS via _km, uses Spectre.Console.Cli not System.CommandLine — DIFFERENT modern .NET CLI framework; HOLD for future round), `_dotnet-monitor`/`_dotnet-counters`/`_dotnet-trace`/`_dotnet-dump`/`_dotnet-gcdump`/`_dotnet-sos`/`_dotnet-symbol` (all IN CORPUS — all use System.CommandLine but already documented), `_cyclonedx-cli` (aalmiray/cyclonedx-cli — IN CORPUS, uses System.CommandLine), `_OASIS` (NextGenSoftwareUK/OASIS 69 stars — uses Spectre.Console.Cli; HOLD), `_fullstackhero/dotnet-starter-kit` (6490 stars — starter kit not a CLI tool).  Probed alternative C# CLI libraries: `_McMaster.Extensions.CommandLineUtils`-based tools (would be 3rd C# CLI library — old library, sunset by Microsoft in favor of System.CommandLine; HOLD), `_CommandLineParser`-based tools (would be 3rd or 4th — older .NET CLI parser; HOLD), `_Spectre.Console.Cli` (newer modern .NET CLI framework; HOLD for future round when non-corpus user found).  Chose dotnet-morphir: 13 GH stars (real production FINOS foundation tool — Morphir is a business-logic-as-data system used by financial institutions for cross-language IR), 5 top-level commands + nested sub-subcommands + AUTO-ADDED `--help`/`-?`/`--version` + TYPED `Argument<T>`/`Option<T>` GENERIC TYPES + EXPLICIT IMPERATIVE REGISTRATION via `.Subcommands.Add`/`.Options.Add`/`.Arguments.Add` + `SetAction(lambda)` HANDLER + `parseResult.GetValue(option)` OBJECT-IDENTITY TYPED ACCESSOR + `RootCommand`-`Command` CLASS HIERARCHY, full src/Morphir/Program.cs + src/Morphir.Tooling/Scenarios/*.cs exercising EVERY MAJOR System.CommandLine PATTERN.

- **R358 — gzr source-direct (Looker Content Management CLI in Ruby; rails/Thor library)** (1 file new) — pivots from mitchellh/cli (R357 muxy) to a SECOND Ruby CLI library — corpus's **first documented rails/Thor library** + **second documented Ruby CLI library**.  gzr is Looker's (Google Looker Data Sciences team) Content Management CLI for the Looker BI platform — manages alerts, attributes, permissions, queries, roles, plans, groups, LookML models, connections, users, dashboards, looks, folders, sessions, and projects via the Looker API.  133 GH stars.  Sister tools: lookml (LookML compiler), bigquery CLI (in corpus), tableau-server-client.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented rails/Thor library** + **second documented Ruby CLI library**.  Ruby ecosystem now has 2 documented CLI libraries (R280 + R358 Thor) joining the multi-library leaders.  Adds Thor (rails/Thor — ~4400 GH stars) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy.  Thor is THE de-facto Ruby CLI framework — used by Bundler, Rails generators (bin/rails), Vagrant, Chef, Jekyll, Middleman, Foreman, Capistrano, CocoaPods, Padrino, Fastlane, fpm, and ALMOST EVERY major Ruby CLI tool + EVERY Rails generator (`rails generate scaffold` etc. use Thor under the hood).
  - `_gzr` (1-stem; looker-open-source/gzr lib/gzr/cli.rb; **rails/Thor library** — `require 'thor'` + `class CLI < Thor` brings Thor into scope; **15 global flags (`--debug`/`--host`/`--port`/`--client_id`/`--client_secret`/`--ssl`/`--verify_ssl`/`--timeout`/`--http_proxy`/`--force`/`--su`/`--width`/`--token`/`--token_file`/`-v/--version`) + 16 top-level subcommands (`version` + `alert` + `attribute` + `permission` + `query` + `role` + `plan` + `group` + `model` + `connection` + `user` + `dashboard` + `look` + `folder`/`space` + `session` + `project`) + `class CLI < Thor` CLASS INHERITANCE + `desc 'verb', 'description'` + `def verb; ...; end` RUBY METHOD-AS-COMMAND + `class_option :name, type: :T, default: V, desc: 'X'` GLOBAL OPTIONS + `option :name, type: :T, ...` PER-COMMAND OPTIONS + `register SubClass, 'name', 'usage', 'desc'` SUBCOMMAND-AS-SEPARATE-CLASS REGISTRATION + `map %w(--version -v) => :version` FLAG ALIAS HASH + `map space: :folder` SUBCOMMAND ALIAS + `self.exit_on_failure?` class method**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` line 24 (`require 'thor'` — Ruby REQUIRE of Thor; distributed via RubyGems as `thor` gem)
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` lines 30-32 (`class CLI < Thor; def self.exit_on_failure?; true; end` — CLASS INHERITANCE from Thor base class; `self.exit_on_failure?` controls whether Thor calls `exit 1` on command failure)
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` lines 34-47 (`class_option :debug, type: :boolean, default: false, desc: 'Run in debug mode'; class_option :host, type: :string, default: 'localhost', desc: 'Looker Host'; class_option :port, type: :string, default: '19999', desc: 'Looker API Port'; class_option :client_id, type: :string, desc: 'API Client Id'; class_option :ssl, type: :boolean, default: true, desc: 'Use ssl to communicate with host'; ...` — GLOBAL OPTIONS via `class_option :name, type: :T, default: V, desc: 'X'`; visible to all subcommands; `type:` values: `:string`/`:numeric`/`:boolean`/`:array`/`:hash`)
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` lines 53-57 (`desc 'version', 'gzr version'; def version; require_relative 'version'; puts "v#{Gzr::VERSION}"; end; map %w(--version -v) => :version` — RUBY METHOD `def version` BECOMES THE `version` SUBCOMMAND; `desc 'verb', 'description'` BEFORE the method describes it; `map %w(--version -v) => :version` REGISTERS `--version` and `-v` as ALIASES for the `version` subcommand)
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` line 58 (`map space: :folder` — SUBCOMMAND ALIAS: `gzr space ...` is dispatched as `gzr folder ...`)
    * `lib/gzr/cli.rb` lines 62-94 (`require_relative 'commands/alert'; register Gzr::Commands::Alert, 'alert', 'alert [SUBCOMMAND]', 'Command description...'; require_relative 'commands/attribute'; register Gzr::Commands::Attribute, 'attribute', 'attribute [SUBCOMMAND]', 'Command description...'; ...` — `register SubClass, 'verb', 'usage', 'description'` REGISTERS a SUBCOMMAND AS A SEPARATE THOR CLASS — `gzr alert init` dispatches to `Gzr::Commands::Alert#init`)
  - Critical extraction note: gzr uses **rails/Thor library** at lib/gzr/cli.rb:24 — corpus's **first documented rails/Thor library** + **second documented Ruby CLI library**.  Thor (rails/thor, originally by Yehuda Katz + Wycats — Rails core team; ~4400 GH stars on rails/thor) is THE de-facto Ruby CLI framework.  Used by virtually every major Ruby CLI tool: Bundler (`bundle install`/`bundle exec`/etc. all use Thor), Rails generators (`rails generate scaffold`/`rails new`/etc. use Thor under the hood — Thor is BUILT-IN to Rails), Vagrant (`vagrant up`/`vagrant ssh`), Chef (`knife`), Jekyll (`jekyll new`/`jekyll serve`), Middleman (`middleman build`/`middleman init`), Foreman (`foreman start`/`foreman export`), Capistrano (`cap deploy`), CocoaPods (`pod install`/`pod update`), Padrino (`padrino new`/`padrino g`), Fastlane (`fastlane run`/`fastlane lanes`), fpm (`fpm -s dir -t deb ...`), and HUNDREDS of others.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS:
    - **CLASS-INHERITANCE** — `class CLI < Thor` makes the class a Thor CLI runner
    - **RUBY METHOD-AS-COMMAND** — `def verb; ...; end` defines a CLI verb where the METHOD NAME becomes the SUBCOMMAND NAME (Thor uses Ruby's reflection to discover all public instance methods on the class)
    - **`desc 'verb', 'description'` BEFORE method** — describes the command for `--help` output
    - **`option :name, type: :T, default: V, desc: 'X'`** BEFORE method — per-command option
    - **`class_option :name, type: :T, default: V, desc: 'X'`** anywhere in class — GLOBAL option visible across all commands
    - **`type:` values: `:string`/`:numeric`/`:boolean`/`:array`/`:hash`** — type coercion at parse time
    - **`register SubClass, 'name', 'usage', 'description'`** — register a SUBCOMMAND-AS-SEPARATE-CLASS (allows nested subcommand groups: `gzr alert ...` dispatches to `Gzr::Commands::Alert#...`)
    - **`map %w(--version -v) => :version`** — flag-name-to-method-name mapping (aliases)
    - **`map space: :folder`** — subcommand alias
    - **`self.exit_on_failure?`** — class method controlling exit behavior on failure
  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (struct + macro), R322 Java JCommander (annotation on fields), R340 oclif (TS class-inheritance with static fields), R341 yargs (TS fluent builder), R343 commander.js (TS explicit-instance builder), R346 mainargs (Scala annotations on methods), R356 Symfony Console (PHP class-inheritance with attribute).  Thor's approach: CLASS-INHERITANCE + RUBY METHOD-AS-COMMAND via Ruby reflection + `register SubClass` for subcommand groups + `class_option`/`option` for global/per-command declarations — corpus's **first documented Ruby-method-name-as-command-name CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (9), Go (9), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 4, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), **Ruby (2 — R280 + Thor R358)**, Perl (R281), PHP 2, Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig 2, D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), Python 5 docopt (R352), Java 4 rvesse/airline (R353), Go 8 jessevdk/go-flags (R354), Zig 2 Hejsil/zig-clap (R355), PHP 2 Symfony Console (R356), Go 9 mitchellh/cli (R357), **Ruby 2 rails/Thor** (R358 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 97 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Ruby NOW HAS TWO documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: gzr uses **Thor Ruby-method-name-as-command-name CLI pattern** at lib/gzr/cli.rb:53-57 — `desc 'version', 'gzr version'; def version; require_relative 'version'; puts "v#{Gzr::VERSION}"; end`.  Thor uses Ruby's REFLECTION to discover all PUBLIC INSTANCE METHODS on the class and register them as CLI verbs — the METHOD NAME becomes the SUBCOMMAND NAME.  `desc 'verb', 'description'` IMMEDIATELY BEFORE the `def verb` BINDS the description to the next method definition.  Thor maintains internal state during class evaluation: each `desc` call STORES the description for the NEXT method to be defined; when the method gets defined, Thor binds the stored description to that method.  Distinct from R346 mainargs (Scala `@main` annotation on method — explicit annotation), R275 clap-derive (struct fields), R341 yargs (`.command('verb', desc, ...)` explicit registration), R343 commander.js (`program.command('verb').description('X')` explicit registration), R340 oclif (filesystem-hierarchy + class-with-static-fields), R322 JCommander (annotation on fields), R356 Symfony Console (PHP `#[AsCommand]` attribute on class).  Thor's approach: RUBY METHOD-AS-COMMAND via REFLECTION + `desc` BEFORE method for description — corpus's **first documented Ruby-method-via-reflection-as-command-name CLI pattern**.
