Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY-TWO library variants: Rust (9), Go (7), Python (5), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), **Java (4 — picocli R276 + JCommander R322 + args4j R323 + airline R353)**, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), Python 5 docopt (R352), **Java 4 rvesse/airline** (R353 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 92 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Java NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries (picocli + JCommander + args4j + airline).
  - Critical extraction note: ontop uses **airline OptionType-explicit-scope-declaration CLI pattern** at OntopEndpoint.java:18-23 — `@Option(type = OptionType.COMMAND, name = {"--portal"}, title = "endpoint portal file", description = "...")` declares this option is COMMAND-SCOPED (visible only when this command is invoked).  Airline's `OptionType` enum has THREE VALUES: `OptionType.COMMAND` for command-level flag (most common — flag only valid when its containing command is invoked), `OptionType.GLOBAL` for top-level global flag (visible at all subcommand levels), `OptionType.GROUP` for subcommand-group-level flag (visible only when its parent group is invoked).  Caller DECLARES SCOPE AT THE ANNOTATION rather than relying on lexical position in the class hierarchy.  Distinct from R276 picocli (scope is IMPLICIT from `@Option` placement in the class hierarchy — if `@Option` is on a field of a subcommand class, it's command-scoped; if on a base class, it's inherited), R322 JCommander (scope is IMPLICIT from object passed to `parser.parse(thing)`), R341 yargs `.option(name, {global: true})` (boolean flag for global vs default command-scoped), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(global = true)]` (boolean attribute).  airline's approach: EXPLICIT THREE-VALUE OptionType ENUM for scope declaration — corpus's **first documented OptionType-explicit-three-value-enum-scope-declaration CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ontop uses **airline @Env-separate-annotation-for-env-var-fallback CLI pattern** at OntopEndpoint.java:21 + 26 + 31 + 35 + 44 + 49 + 53 + 57 + 60 — `@Env("ONTOP_PORTAL_FILE")` + `@Env("ONTOP_CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS")` + `@Env("ONTOP_LAZY_INIT")` + many others.  Airline's `@Env("VAR_NAME")` is a SEPARATE ANNOTATION that adds an ENVIRONMENT VARIABLE FALLBACK to the field's CLI option — if the user doesn't provide the flag on the command line but the env var is set, airline uses the env var's value as if the user had typed `--flag=$ENV_VALUE`.  Distinct from R276 picocli `@Option(names = "--foo", paramLabel = "X", description = "Y", arity = "1", defaultValue = "...", showDefaultValue = ALWAYS, **envVars = {"FOO_VAR", "ALT_FOO_VAR"}**)` (env vars are an ARGUMENT KEY of `@Option` annotation), R342 alexflint/go-arg `arg:"--foo,env:FOO_VAR"` (combined into single tag string), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(env = "FOO_VAR")]` (attribute argument), R345 cyclopts `cyclopts.config.Env("PREFIX_")` (global prefix-based env fallback at app level not per-flag).  airline's approach: SEPARATE `@Env("VAR_NAME")` ANNOTATION for env var fallback — composable with other annotations like `@Option`, `@BashCompletion`, `@RequiredOnlyIf` — corpus's **first documented @Env-separate-annotation-stackable-with-other-annotations CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ontop uses **airline @RequiredOnlyIf-conditional-required CLI pattern** at OntopEndpoint.java:44 + 49 — `@RequiredOnlyIf(names = {"--predefined-queries"}) String predefinedConfig;` + `@RequiredOnlyIf(names = {"--predefined-config"}) String predefinedQueries;`.  Airline's `@RequiredOnlyIf(names = {"X"})` CONDITIONAL CONSTRAINT makes a flag REQUIRED ONLY IF one of the listed names was given.  Mutual: if user gives `--predefined-queries`, then `--predefined-config` becomes required, AND vice versa.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(requires = "X")]` (similar semantic but uses `requires` attribute), R322 JCommander `validateWith = X.class` (separate validator class), R276 picocli `@ArgGroup(exclusive = false, multiplicity = "1")` (group-based mutual-requirement), R278 decline `(opt1, opt2).mapN((a, b) => ...)` (Applicative combinator — both required), R341 yargs `.implies({"a": "b", "b": "a"})` (method-call mutual implication).  airline's approach: `@RequiredOnlyIf(names = {"X"})` ANNOTATION declares CONDITIONAL REQUIRED constraint inline — corpus's **first documented @RequiredOnlyIf-conditional-required-via-annotation CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ontop uses **airline @BashCompletion-built-in-completion-hint CLI pattern** at OntopEndpoint.java:22 + 47 + 52 + 56 — `@BashCompletion(behaviour = CompletionBehaviour.FILENAMES)`.  Airline's `@BashCompletion(behaviour = CompletionBehaviour.X)` annotation declares COMPLETION HINTS at the option level — caller can specify `FILENAMES` (complete filenames), `DIRECTORIES` (complete directories), `SYSTEM_COMMANDS` (complete from $PATH), or custom hints.  Airline can then GENERATE a bash completion script from these hints (`airline-bash-completion` tool).  Distinct from R276 picocli `picocli-shell-jline2` + separate AutoComplete tool (completion is a SEPARATE feature, requires generating + sourcing a shell script), R275 clap-derive `clap_complete` separate crate, R322 JCommander has no built-in completion support — typically handled separately.  airline's approach: COMPLETION HINTS as INLINE ANNOTATION on options (no separate generation needed for runtime hints) — corpus's **first documented @BashCompletion-inline-completion-hint-annotation CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_druid` (apache/druid — IN CORPUS, Apache Druid CLI uses Airline), `_ksql` (confluentinc/ksql 303 stars — check corpus; uses Airline), `_cassandra-nodetool` (apache/cassandra — IN CORPUS, nodetool uses Airline), `_neptune-export` (aws/neptune-export 15 stars — uses Airline, AWS Labs niche tool with narrow surface), `_graylog` (Graylog2/graylog2-server — IN CORPUS), `_mirror` (stephenh/mirror — IN CORPUS, blacklisted).  Probed alternative Java CLI libraries: `_apache-commons-cli` users (would be 5th Java CLI library; most users are old academic/research tools with narrow surfaces; HOLD), `_jopt-simple`-based tools (would be 5th or 6th; HOLD), `_picocli-spring-boot-starter`-based (Spring Boot picocli integration — would be picocli variant; HOLD).  Considered using ksql but checked it's a substantial multi-component project that would require deeper extraction.  Chose ontop: 852 GH stars (real production Ontology-Based Data Access platform from Free University of Bozen-Bolzano), 8 visible top-level subcommands + 1 hidden + 1 subcommand group with 4 sub-verbs + 9 endpoint flags + common mapping/ontology/properties flags + EVERY MAJOR airline PATTERN (OptionType three-value scope, @Env separate annotation, @BashCompletion inline hints, @RequiredOnlyIf conditional required, CliBuilder explicit command-class list, subcommand group via .withGroup().withCommand(), rich ParseException hierarchy).
  - Blacklist update: `ontop` added.  Corpus count: 28,775 → 28,776 (+1).

- **R352 — mqttwarn source-direct (highly configurable MQTT message router with notification plugins in Python; docopt library)** (1 file new) — pivots from sjl/adopt (R351 mahogany) to a FIFTH Python CLI library — corpus's **first documented docopt library** + **fifth documented Python CLI library**.  mqttwarn is mqtt-tools's MQTT message router — subscribes to MQTT topics, ROUTES MESSAGES to NOTIFICATION PLUGINS (HTTP webhooks, Slack/Discord/Telegram, email, syslog, file writes, custom user-defined functions, etc.).  Configured via INI file or JSON inline.  974 GH stars.  Sister tools: home-assistant (in corpus), node-red (in corpus), mosquitto (in corpus, MQTT broker).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented docopt library** + **fifth documented Python CLI library** — Python ecosystem now has 5 documented CLI libraries (argparse + click + fire + cyclopts + docopt).  Adds docopt (Vladimir Keleshev's docopt) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: docopt is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from every other CLI library documented — **THE CLI SPECIFICATION IS THE DOCSTRING ITSELF**.  docopt parses the function's `__doc__` STRING to derive the spec, then matches argv against the spec.  The docstring is FORMATTED in a specific grammar inspired by classical man-page Usage:/Options: conventions: `Usage:` header followed by usage lines, `Options:` header with option list and descriptions.  Conventions: `[]` = optional, `<>` = positional placeholder, `|` = alternatives, `...` = repeat, `--` = end-of-options marker.  After parsing, docopt returns a DICT keyed by literal flag/command/positional name from the docstring.  Inspires implementations in many other languages (docopt.go, docopt-rs, docopt-cs, etc.) — has become a CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI SPECIFICATION FORMAT.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 91.
  - `_mqttwarn` (1-stem; mqtt-tools/mqttwarn mqttwarn/commands.py; **docopt library** — `from docopt import docopt` brings the docopt function into scope; **2 commands (`make-config` + `make-udf`) + 5 flags (`--config`/`--config-file`/`--plugin`/`--options`/`--data`) + `--version` + `-h/--help` + DEFAULT ACTION (no args = run service) + DOCSTRING-AS-SPEC (`run.__doc__` contains the entire CLI specification in `Usage:`/`Options:` format) + `docopt(commandline_schema, version=...)` PARSE returning DICT keyed by literal flag/command/positional names from the docstring + `commandline_schema = run.__doc__.format(program=APP_NAME)` STRING-FORMAT TEMPLATING for dynamic program name in the docstring**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `mqttwarn/commands.py` line 12 (`from docopt import docopt` — Python IMPORT of docopt; distributed via PyPI as `docopt` package)
    * `mqttwarn/commands.py` lines 22-46 (`def run(): """\nUsage:\n  {program} [make-config]\n  {program} [make-udf]\n  {program} [--config=] [--config-file=] [--plugin=] [--options=] [--data=]\n  {program} --version\n  {program} (-h | --help)\n\nNo options:\n  mqttwarn will start as a service.\n\nInteractive options:\n  [--config=]               Use configuration settings from JSON string\n  [--config-file=]          Use configuration settings from JSON file\n  ...\n"""` — DOCSTRING contains the ENTIRE CLI SPECIFICATION using `Usage:`/`Options:` format; `{program}` is a STRING-FORMAT PLACEHOLDER substituted at runtime via `.format(program=APP_NAME)`)
    * `mqttwarn/commands.py` lines 51-55 (`commandline_schema = run.__doc__.format(program=APP_NAME)` + `options = docopt(commandline_schema, version=APP_NAME + " " + __version__)` — RUNTIME EXTRACTION of the docstring + STRING-FORMAT TEMPLATING substitution + DOCOPT PARSE returning DICT)
    * `mqttwarn/commands.py` lines 60-89 (`if options["make-config"]: ...; elif options["make-udf"]: ...; elif options["--plugin"] and options["--options"]: ...; else: run_mqttwarn(...)` — CALLER ACCESSES parsed values via DICT KEYS that EXACTLY MATCH the literal flag/command/positional names from the docstring; `options["make-config"]` is True if user typed `mqttwarn make-config`, `options["--plugin"]` is the value of `--plugin=` if given)
  - Critical extraction note: mqttwarn uses **docopt library** at commands.py:12 — corpus's **first documented docopt library** + **fifth documented Python CLI library**.  Vladimir Keleshev's docopt is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from every other CLI library documented so far in this taxonomy: **THE CLI SPECIFICATION IS THE DOCSTRING ITSELF**.  Every other library uses some FORM of programmatic spec — struct fields with attributes (clap-derive R275, kong R334, argh R344, go-arg R342, core:flags R338), class methods with annotations (oclif R340, mainargs R346), declarative struct fields (typer/click/argparse R-?), reducer-functions (adopt R351), method-chain builder (yargs R341, commander.js R343, argparse Lua R349), enum-with-variants (clap-derive Subcommand, argh Subcommand).  docopt INVERTS THIS — the user writes the DOCSTRING in a man-page-inspired grammar (`Usage:` header + usage lines + `Options:` header + option descriptions), and docopt PARSES THE DOCSTRING TO DERIVE THE PARSER SPEC.  Caller never explicitly declares flags, positionals, commands, types — docopt INFERS THEM FROM THE DOCSTRING TEXT.  Grammar conventions: `[]` = optional, `<>` = positional placeholder, `|` = alternatives, `...` = repeat, `--` = end-of-options marker, `Usage:` line literal flags use `--name=` to indicate value-taking.  After parsing, returns a DICT keyed by the LITERAL FLAG/COMMAND/POSITIONAL NAMES from the docstring — caller accesses via `options["--config"]`, `options["make-config"]`, `options["<file>"]`.  Vladimir Keleshev's original Python docopt INSPIRED IMPLEMENTATIONS IN MANY OTHER LANGUAGES (docopt.go, docopt-rs, docopt-cs, docopt-java, docopt-rb, etc.) — docopt has become a CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI SPECIFICATION FORMAT.  Distinct from R-? click (decorator-based, no docstring parsing), R-? argparse (procedural builder), R-? fire (auto-CLI from function/class signature), R345 cyclopts (PEP 593 Annotated[] for METADATA + docstring for HELP TEXT — but the SPEC comes from PARAMETER SIGNATURES not the docstring).  docopt's approach: DOCSTRING-AS-SPEC + DICT-KEYED-BY-LITERAL-NAMES — corpus's **first documented docstring-as-spec CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY-ONE library variants: Rust (9), Go (7), **Python (5 — argparse + click + fire + cyclopts + docopt)**, Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 3, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt (R351), **Python 5 docopt** (R352 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 91 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Python NOW HAS FIVE documented CLI libraries (argparse + click + fire + cyclopts + docopt).
  - Critical extraction note: mqttwarn uses **docopt docstring-as-spec parsing CLI pattern** at commands.py:22-46 — the function `run()` has a DOCSTRING that IS the CLI specification: `"""Usage:\n  {program} [make-config]\n  {program} [make-udf]\n  {program} [--config=] [--config-file=] [--plugin=] [--options=] [--data=]\n  {program} --version\n  {program} (-h | --help)\n\nNo options:\n  mqttwarn will start as a service.\n\nInteractive options:\n  [--config=]               Use configuration settings from JSON string\n  ...\n"""`.  docopt PARSES this docstring at runtime to derive the parser spec.  The docstring follows a specific grammar inspired by man-page conventions: `Usage:` HEADER + USAGE LINES (each line is one valid command-line form) + `Options:` HEADER + OPTION LIST with descriptions.  CONVENTIONS: `[arg]` = optional arg, `(a | b)` = alternatives, `[options]` = "all defined options accepted here", `--flag=ARG` = flag with required value, `<positional>` = positional placeholder, `...` = repeat.  docopt's APPROACH: caller writes WHAT THEY ALREADY WRITE for documentation (the help text), and docopt USES IT AS THE SPEC.  This is the OPPOSITE of every other library which separates SPEC (code) from HELP TEXT (descriptions/docstrings).  Distinct from R-? click (decorators on Python functions describe the CLI), R-? cyclopts (PEP 593 Annotated[] metadata + docstring for help-text-only), R-? argparse (procedural builder API), R275 clap-derive (struct + attributes), R322 JCommander (annotations).  docopt's approach: DOCSTRING-AS-SPEC — caller writes ONE TEXT BLOCK that serves BOTH AS HELP TEXT AND AS PARSER SPEC — corpus's **first documented docstring-as-both-help-text-and-parser-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mqttwarn uses **docopt dict-keyed-by-literal-name CLI pattern** at commands.py:60-89 — `if options["make-config"]: ...; elif options["make-udf"]: ...; elif options["--plugin"] and options["--options"]: ...; else: run_mqttwarn(configfile=options["--config-file"])`.  docopt's PARSED RESULT is a Python DICT keyed by the LITERAL NAMES FROM THE DOCSTRING — exactly as they appear (including `--`, `-`, brackets, etc.).  `options["make-config"]` is `True` if user typed `mqttwarn make-config` (positional command); `options["--config"]` is the value-string of `--config=X` if given (else None); `options["<file>"]` is the value of `<file>` positional.  The DICT KEYS PRESERVE THE EXACT TEXT from the docstring — no automatic name-mangling.  CALLER PATTERN-MATCHES on the dict via `if/elif` chain to dispatch to handlers.  Distinct from R-? click (parsed values bound to FUNCTION PARAMETER NAMES — `--config` becomes parameter `config`), R-? cyclopts (parsed values bound to FUNCTION PARAMETERS via PEP 593), R275 clap-derive (parsed values bound to STRUCT FIELDS), R322 JCommander (parsed values bound to ANNOTATED FIELDS).  docopt's approach: DICT KEYED BY EXACT DOCSTRING TEXT — no name-mangling, caller pattern-matches dictionary keys — corpus's **first documented dict-keyed-by-exact-docstring-text-no-mangling CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mqttwarn uses **docopt string-format-templating in docstring CLI pattern** at commands.py:51 — `commandline_schema = run.__doc__.format(program=APP_NAME)`.  The docstring contains `{program}` PLACEHOLDER tokens that get SUBSTITUTED at runtime via Python's `.format(program=APP_NAME)` string-format method.  This allows the SAME DOCSTRING to be used with DIFFERENT PROGRAM NAMES (useful for re-packaging mqttwarn under different binary names, or for testing).  docopt parses the SUBSTITUTED docstring, not the literal one.  Distinct from R-? argparse `parser.prog = "X"` (separate setter), R-? click `@click.command(name="X")` (decorator), R275 clap-derive `#[command(name = "X")]` (attribute), R341 yargs `.scriptName("X")` (method).  docopt's approach: STRING-FORMAT TEMPLATING in the docstring + RUNTIME `.format(...)` substitution before docopt parse — corpus's **first documented string-format-templating-in-docstring CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_jedi` (davidhalter/jedi — IN CORPUS; uses docopt internally in sith.py helper script not main CLI — jedi's user-facing CLI uses different library), other niche docopt consumers (mostly research scripts or one-off tools).  Probed alternative novel libraries: `_typer`-based Python tools (would be 6th Python CLI library — most well-known typer consumers like aider/marimo/memray/prefect/sgpt ALL IN CORPUS already, HOLD), `_plac`-based tools (would be 7th Python CLI library; obscure but unique; HOLD), `_PowerShell` `param()` block tools (PowerShell scripts as standalone Unix binaries are rare; HOLD), `_R + optparse` (R-as-standalone-Bioconda-binary tools use `.R` extension in binary name which is unusual for zsh completions; HOLD).  Considered alternative docopt users: `_xonsh` (probably IN CORPUS), `_buildlib` (narrow).  Chose mqttwarn: 974 GH stars (real production MQTT message router used in IoT/home-automation/monitoring deployments worldwide), 2 commands (`make-config` + `make-udf`) + 5 flags + `--version` + `-h/--help` + DEFAULT ACTION + DOCSTRING-AS-SPEC + `docopt(schema, version=...)` PARSE returning DICT + STRING-FORMAT TEMPLATING for dynamic program name, full mqttwarn/commands.py exercising EVERY MAJOR docopt PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `mqttwarn` added.  Corpus count: 28,774 → 28,775 (+1).

- **R351 — mahogany source-direct (StumpWM-like Wayland compositor in Common Lisp; sjl/adopt library)** (1 file new) — pivots from integrii/flaggy (R350 nodeadm) to a THIRD Common Lisp CLI library — corpus's **first documented sjl/adopt library** + **third documented Common Lisp CLI library**.  mahogany is a TILING window manager for Wayland modeled after StumpWM (the venerable Common Lisp tiling WM for X11).  Keyboard-driven, with a Common Lisp REPL embedded in the compositor for live customization.  Maintained by the StumpWM project.  364 GH stars.  Sister tools: stumpwm (X11 CL tiling WM, in corpus), sway (Wayland C tiling WM, in corpus), river (Wayland Zig tiling WM, in corpus), hyprland (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented sjl/adopt library** + **third documented Common Lisp CLI library** — Common Lisp ecosystem now has 3 documented CLI libraries (R291 unix-opts + R295 clingon + R351 adopt) joining the multi-library leaders.  Also a TAXONOMY MILESTONE — this is the **90th cross-language CLI library variant documented**.  Adds sjl/adopt to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: adopt is Steve Losh's "A Damn OPTion parsing library" (also author of bobbin/cl-losh/cacl/temperance/cl-chip8/etc.).  Design philosophy: SMALL + COMPOSABLE + PARTIAL-APPLICATION-FRIENDLY.  34 GH stars on sjl/adopt; used by mahogany, clpm (Common Lisp Package Manager), and many Steve Losh tools.  Adopt's DISTINGUISHING FEATURE: REDUCER FUNCTION per option — caller specifies how to COMBINE multiple occurrences of a flag.  `(constantly t)` reducer = "any occurrence sets true" (boolean); `(lambda (prev cur) cur)` = "last-occurrence wins" (override semantics); `(lambda (prev cur) (cons cur prev))` = "accumulate into list" (repeatable).  This makes adopt VERY COMPOSABLE — caller CONTROLS THE MERGE SEMANTICS for each flag explicitly.  Distinct from R291 unix-opts (DECLARATIVE list of plists with no reducer concept), R295 clingon (CLASS-INSTANCE-based with `clingon:make-command`).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 90 — MAJOR TAXONOMY MILESTONE.
  - `_mahogany` (1-stem; stumpwm/mahogany lisp/main.lisp; **sjl/adopt library** — `(adopt:make-interface ... :contents (list ...))` brings adopt's interface constructor into scope; **4 flags (`-h/--help` + `-q/--no-init-file` + `-d/--enable-debugger` + `-l/--loglevel <LEVEL>`) + LOGLEVEL CHOICES (TRACE/DEBUG/INFO/WARN/ERROR/FATAL/IGNORE) + `(adopt:make-interface :name :summary :usage :help :contents)` INTERFACE CONSTRUCTOR + `(adopt:make-option SYMBOL-NAME :long :short :help :parameter :initial-value :reduce)` OPTION CONSTRUCTOR + REDUCER FUNCTION per option for OCCURRENCE-COMBINATION SEMANTICS + `(adopt:parse-options interface argv)` PARSE returning HASH-TABLE + free-args**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `lisp/main.lisp` `%build-cmd-line-parser` function — defines all 4 options + builds the interface
    * `lisp/main.lisp` lines 85-90 (`(let ((help-option (adopt:make-option 'help :long "help" :short #\\h :help "Display help and exit." :reduce (constantly t)))` — BOOLEAN FLAG: `:reduce (constantly t)` makes any occurrence set the value to T regardless of input)
    * `lisp/main.lisp` lines 91-96 (`(no-init-option (adopt:make-option 'no-init-file :long "no-init-file" :short #\\q :help "..." :reduce (constantly t)))` — second boolean flag with same reducer)
    * `lisp/main.lisp` lines 97-102 (`(enable-debugger (adopt:make-option 'enable-debugger :long "enable-debugger" :short #\\d :help "..." :reduce (constantly t)))` — third boolean flag)
    * `lisp/main.lisp` lines 103-110 (`(loglevel (adopt:make-option 'loglevel :long "loglevel" :short #\\l :parameter "[loglevel]" :initial-value (first +LOGLEVEL-SELECTIONS+) :help (format nil "Specify loglevel ~{~A~^|~}" +LOGLEVEL-SELECTIONS+) :reduce (lambda (prev level) (declare (ignore prev)) (string-upcase level))))` — VALUE-TAKING OPTION with `:parameter "[loglevel]"` placeholder + `:initial-value` default + `:reduce` LAMBDA that IGNORES previous and UPPERCASES the new value (LAST-OCCURRENCE-WINS-WITH-UPPERCASE-TRANSFORM semantics))
    * `lisp/main.lisp` lines 112-121 (`(adopt:make-interface :name "mahogany" :summary "Keyboard driven titling window manager for Wayland" :usage "[OPTIONS]" :help "Mahogany is a tiling window manager for Wayland modeled after StumpWM." :contents (list help-option no-init-option loglevel enable-debugger))` — INTERFACE CONSTRUCTOR with metadata + `:contents` LIST OF OPTIONS)
  - Critical extraction note: mahogany uses **sjl/adopt library** at lisp/main.lisp `%build-cmd-line-parser` — corpus's **first documented sjl/adopt library** + **third documented Common Lisp CLI library** + **NINETIETH cross-language CLI library variant**.  Steve Losh's adopt is "A Damn OPTion parsing library" — design philosophy: SMALL + COMPOSABLE + PARTIAL-APPLICATION-FRIENDLY.  Key concept: each option has a REDUCER FUNCTION `:reduce FN` that specifies HOW MULTIPLE OCCURRENCES OF THE SAME FLAG COMBINE.  This is fundamentally different from other CLI libraries which usually have FIXED semantics per flag type.  Common reducers:
    - `(constantly t)` — boolean: any occurrence = T
    - `(lambda (prev cur) cur)` — last-occurrence wins
    - `(lambda (prev cur) (cons cur prev))` — accumulate into list
    - Custom lambdas for arbitrary combination semantics
  Distinct from R291 Common Lisp (unix-opts or similar — DECLARATIVE plist-of-options, no reducer), R295 Common Lisp clingon (CLASS-INSTANCE-based with `clingon:make-command` + handler closures, no reducer concept), R275 Rust clap-derive (struct + macro, FIXED semantics from type), R322 Java JCommander (annotations + FIXED semantics from type), R341 yargs (per-flag CONFIG OBJECT with `coerce` option but no general reducer concept), R346 mainargs (parameter type inference, no reducer).  adopt's approach: REDUCER FUNCTION PER OPTION + COMPOSABLE OPTION CONSTRUCTION via `make-option` + COMPOSABLE INTERFACE CONSTRUCTION via `make-interface :contents (list ...)` — caller controls MERGE SEMANTICS explicitly.  34 GH stars on sjl/adopt; used by mahogany (364 stars), clpm (CL Package Manager), and many Steve Losh tools.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to NINETY library variants: Rust (9), Go (7), Python (4), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), **Common Lisp (3 — unix-opts R291 + clingon R295 + adopt R351)**, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), Go 7 integrii/flaggy (R350), **Common Lisp 3 sjl/adopt** (R351 — NEW).  **NINETY-VARIANT MILESTONE**: 90 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Common Lisp NOW HAS THREE documented CLI libraries (unix-opts + clingon + adopt).
  - Critical extraction note: mahogany uses **adopt reducer-function-per-option CLI pattern** at lisp/main.lisp lines 85-110 — `(adopt:make-option 'help ... :reduce (constantly t))` for BOOLEAN flags + `(adopt:make-option 'loglevel ... :reduce (lambda (prev level) (declare (ignore prev)) (string-upcase level)))` for LAST-WINS-WITH-UPPERCASE-TRANSFORM.  adopt's `:reduce FN` is a FUNCTION OF TWO ARGUMENTS `(PREV CUR)` that returns the new accumulated value.  PREV is the previous accumulated value (or `:initial-value` if first); CUR is the newly-parsed value (typically a string for value-taking options, or `t` for boolean flags).  adopt CALLS THE REDUCER ONCE PER OCCURRENCE of the flag, accumulating the result.  Common patterns: `(constantly t)` ignores both args returns T (boolean), `(lambda (p c) c)` returns last value (last-wins), `(lambda (p c) (cons c p))` accumulates into list (repeatable), `(lambda (p c) (parse-integer c))` parse-and-replace.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (action attribute: `ArgAction::SetTrue`, `ArgAction::Append` — closed set of named actions), R341 yargs (per-flag `coerce` function for parsing but no general reducer), R344 argh (Vec<T> implies append, bool implies set-true — no caller-customization), R342 alexflint/go-arg (`separate` directive for repeatable, type-driven for others).  adopt's approach: CALLER PROVIDES ARBITRARY REDUCER LAMBDA per option for FULL CONTROL over occurrence-combination semantics — corpus's **first documented reducer-function-per-option-as-arbitrary-lambda CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mahogany uses **adopt make-option-with-symbol-name CLI pattern** at lisp/main.lisp lines 85-110 — `(adopt:make-option 'help :long "help" :short #\\h :help "..." :reduce (constantly t))` + `(adopt:make-option 'no-init-file :long "no-init-file" :short #\\q :help "..." :reduce (constantly t))` + `(adopt:make-option 'enable-debugger :long "enable-debugger" :short #\\d :help "..." :reduce (constantly t))` + `(adopt:make-option 'loglevel :long "loglevel" :short #\\l :parameter "[loglevel]" :initial-value (first +LOGLEVEL-SELECTIONS+) :help "..." :reduce (lambda (prev level) ...))`.  First positional argument to `adopt:make-option` is the OPTION'S NAME as a SYMBOL (e.g. `'help`, `'no-init-file`, `'enable-debugger`, `'loglevel`).  Parsed values are STORED IN A HASH TABLE keyed by this SYMBOL — caller looks up via `(gethash 'flagname args)`.  This DECOUPLES the SYMBOL-IDENTITY of the option from its `--long-name` and `-short-char` — caller can rename the long/short flag without affecting lookup, and vice versa.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (FIELD NAME on annotated struct is the lookup key), R275 clap-derive (FIELD NAME on struct), R341 yargs (LONG FLAG NAME string is the lookup key), R346 mainargs (PARAMETER NAME in method signature).  adopt's approach: SYMBOL as the LOOKUP IDENTITY (decoupled from --long/-short) — corpus's **first documented symbol-as-decoupled-lookup-identity CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mahogany uses **adopt make-interface-with-contents-list CLI pattern** at lisp/main.lisp lines 112-121 — `(adopt:make-interface :name "mahogany" :summary "Keyboard driven titling window manager for Wayland" :usage "[OPTIONS]" :help "Mahogany is a tiling window manager for Wayland modeled after StumpWM." :contents (list help-option no-init-option loglevel enable-debugger))`.  adopt's `make-interface` takes METADATA via keyword args (`:name`/`:summary`/`:usage`/`:help`) + `:contents` LIST OF OPTIONS pre-built via `make-option`.  This SEPARATES OPTION CONSTRUCTION (via `make-option`) from INTERFACE CONSTRUCTION (via `make-interface`) — caller can BUILD OPTIONS INDEPENDENTLY, COMBINE THEM into different interfaces, partial-apply, conditionally include, etc.  Distinct from R341 yargs (chained `.option().option().option()` calls on shared instance — no separation), R343 commander.js (chained `.option().command().option()` on Command instance — no separation), R275 clap-derive (struct fields ARE the options — coupled), R338 Odin core:flags (struct fields ARE the options — coupled).  adopt's approach: SEPARATE OPTION CONSTRUCTORS + SEPARATE INTERFACE CONSTRUCTOR with `:contents` LIST — corpus's **first documented separate-option-vs-interface-construction-with-contents-list CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_clpm` (lisp-mirror/clpm 11 stars CL Package Manager — uses adopt, would be sister demo; HOLD for future round), `_ssb-consum-up` (Orange-OpenSource 2 stars — too narrow), `_dancamper-cli` (template repo — narrow), `_seurat-read.R` (ebi-gene-expression-group/r-seurat-scripts 18 stars — R+optparse Bioconda script, but `.R` extension in binary name is unusual for zsh completions; HOLD for future round when an R CLI with cleaner naming convention is found), Steve Losh's tools that use adopt (bobbin/cl-losh/cacl) — most are libraries not standalone CLIs.  Probed `_litllr` (Dirk Eddelbuettel's R `r` binary — written in C, doesn't use R optparse), `_radian` (Python-based REPL, uses Python argparse — Python ecosystem already documented).  Considered using clpm as sister adopt demo but its CLI surface (sync/install/etc.) requires more documentation than mahogany's 4-flag clean surface.  Chose mahogany: 364 GH stars (real production Wayland compositor in Common Lisp from the StumpWM project), 4 flags (`-h/--help` + `-q/--no-init-file` + `-d/--enable-debugger` + `-l/--loglevel <LEVEL>` with LOGLEVEL CHOICES) + `adopt:make-interface` INTERFACE CONSTRUCTOR + `adopt:make-option` OPTION CONSTRUCTOR + REDUCER FUNCTION per option for OCCURRENCE-COMBINATION SEMANTICS + SYMBOL as LOOKUP IDENTITY + `:contents` LIST OF OPTIONS for separation-of-concerns, full lisp/main.lisp `%build-cmd-line-parser` exercising EVERY MAJOR adopt PATTERN, real production binary distributed via Quicklisp + ASDF + various CL distribution mechanisms.
  - Blacklist update: `mahogany` added.  Corpus count: 28,773 → 28,774 (+1).

- **R350 — nodeadm source-direct (AWS EKS Node Admin bootstrap CLI shipped in AWS-managed EKS AMIs; integrii/flaggy library)** (1 file new) — pivots from mpeterv/argparse (R349 ypp) to a SEVENTH Go CLI library — corpus's **first documented integrii/flaggy library** + **seventh documented Go CLI library**.  nodeadm is the AWS Labs Node Admin — runs on EC2 instances as they boot into an EKS cluster and bootstraps the containerd + kubelet daemons + retrieves config from IMDS user-data or file URIs.  Lives inside the amazon-eks-ami repo (2650 GH stars).  Sister tools: kubeadm (Kubernetes upstream bootstrapper, in corpus), k3s (in corpus), eksctl (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented integrii/flaggy library** + **seventh documented Go CLI library** — Go ecosystem now has SEVEN documented CLI libraries (cobra/urfave-cli/kingpin/spf13-pflag/alecthomas-kong/alexflint-go-arg/integrii-flaggy) — extends Go's documented-CLI-library count to second place behind Rust 9.  Adds integrii/flaggy to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: flaggy is Eric Greer's (integrii) Go CLI parser — designed as a POINTER-BASED IMPERATIVE BUILDER alternative to cobra/urfave-cli that AVOIDS reflection complexity.  Flags BIND TO USER-PROVIDED POINTERS: `flaggy.Bool(&dest, "d", "debug", "help")` binds a global flag to `&dest`; `subcmd.String(&dest, "c", "config", "help")` binds a subcommand-scoped flag.  Subcommands via `flaggy.NewSubcommand("verb")` constructor + `parent.AttachSubcommand(sub, position)` registration (position is the positional ARG INDEX at which this subcommand can appear — 1 means "first positional", 2 means "second positional", etc.).  Used by lazydocker (51K stars), lazygit (78K stars), kuberhealthy (K8s synthetic checks), nodeadm (AWS EKS bootstrap), sleep-on-lan, and many others.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 89.
  - `_nodeadm` (1-stem; awslabs/amazon-eks-ami nodeadm/cmd/nodeadm/*.go + nodeadm/internal/cli/*.go + integrii/flaggy library; **integrii/flaggy library** — `import "github.com/integrii/flaggy"` brings flaggy into scope; **1 global flag (`-d/--development`) + 2 top-level subcommands (`config` + `init`) + 2 nested config sub-subcommands (`check` + `dump`) + 4 init flags + 2-3 flags per config sub-subcommand + POINTER-BASED FLAG BINDING + `flaggy.Bool(&dest, "d", "long", "help")` GLOBAL FLAG + `flaggy.NewSubcommand(name)` SUBCOMMAND CONSTRUCTOR + `sub.String(&dest, "s", "long", "help")` PER-SUBCOMMAND FLAG + `sub.StringSlice(&dest, "s", "long", "help")` REPEATABLE STRING FLAG + `parent.AttachSubcommand(sub, position)` SUBCOMMAND ATTACHMENT WITH POSITION + `sub.Used` POST-PARSE PRESENCE-CHECK + `flaggy.Parse()` TERMINAL PARSE + `flaggy.ShowHelpAndExit(msg)` HELP-AND-EXIT helper**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `nodeadm/cmd/nodeadm/main.go` (`m := cli.Main{ Name: "nodeadm", Description: "From zero to Node faster than you can say Elastic Kubernetes Service", AdditionalHelp: "http://...", Commands: []cli.Command{ config.NewConfigCommand(), initcmd.NewInitCommand() } }; m.Run()` — TOP-LEVEL ENTRY POINT instantiates a cli.Main struct with NAME/DESCRIPTION/ADDITIONALHELP/COMMANDS — internal wrapper around flaggy that abstracts the subcommand registration boilerplate)
    * `nodeadm/internal/cli/container.go` (`func NewCommandContainer(name, description string) CommandContainer { cmd := flaggy.NewSubcommand(name); cmd.Description = description; ... }` + `func (c *cmdContainer) AddCommand(cmd Command) { c.cmds = append(c.cmds, cmd); c.cmd.AttachSubcommand(cmd.Flaggy(), 1) }` — INTERNAL COMMAND-CONTAINER abstraction that wraps `flaggy.NewSubcommand` + `parent.AttachSubcommand(child, 1)` where the `1` is the POSITIONAL ARG INDEX at which the child subcommand can appear)
    * `nodeadm/internal/cli/options.go` (`flaggy.Bool(&opts.DevelopmentMode, "d", "development", "Enable development mode for logging.")` — GLOBAL FLAG REGISTERED via top-level `flaggy.Bool()` — flaggy's package-level functions register flags at the TOP-LEVEL parser scope; per-subcommand flags use METHODS on the subcommand object instead)
    * `nodeadm/internal/cli/options.go` (`func RegisterFlagConfigSources(c *flaggy.Subcommand, configSources *[]string) { c.StringSlice(configSources, "c", "config-source", "...") }` — HELPER FUNCTION that REGISTERS a `-c/--config-source` REPEATABLE STRING FLAG on the given subcommand — used to share flag definitions across multiple subcommands that all accept the same config-source flag)
    * `nodeadm/cmd/nodeadm/init/init.go` (`c.cmd.StringSlice(&c.daemons, "d", "daemon", "...")` + `c.cmd.StringSlice(&c.skipPhases, "s", "skip", "...")` + `cli.RegisterFlagConfigCache(c.cmd, &c.configCache)` + `cli.RegisterFlagConfigSources(c.cmd, &c.configSources)` — `init` subcommand-scoped flag bindings + delegate to helpers for shared flags)
    * `nodeadm/cmd/nodeadm/config/root.go` (`func NewConfigCommand() cli.Command { container := cli.NewCommandContainer("config", "Manage configuration"); container.AddCommand(NewCheckCommand()); container.AddCommand(NewDumpCommand()); return container.AsCommand() }` — NESTED SUBCOMMAND GROUPING: `config` is a container that ATTACHES `check` and `dump` as nested children)
  - Critical extraction note: nodeadm uses **integrii/flaggy library** at internal/cli/container.go:4 — corpus's **first documented integrii/flaggy library** + **seventh documented Go CLI library**.  integrii/flaggy is Eric Greer's Go CLI parser (1500+ GH stars on integrii/flaggy) — POINTER-BASED IMPERATIVE BUILDER alternative to cobra/urfave-cli that AVOIDS reflection complexity.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS: (1) FLAGS BIND TO USER-PROVIDED POINTERS via `flaggy.Bool(&dest, "d", "debug", "help")` — caller pre-declares the destination variable, flaggy populates it at parse time; (2) NO REFLECTION — unlike R334 alecthomas/kong (struct tags + reflection) and R342 alexflint/go-arg (struct tags + reflection); (3) NESTED SUBCOMMANDS via `flaggy.NewSubcommand("verb")` + `parent.AttachSubcommand(child, position)` where POSITION is the POSITIONAL ARG INDEX (1, 2, 3...) at which the child subcommand can appear; (4) `sub.Used` POST-PARSE PRESENCE-CHECK lets caller dispatch by checking which subcommand was selected; (5) `flaggy.Parse()` TERMINAL PARSE plus `flaggy.ShowHelpAndExit(msg)` helper for early-exit help.  Used by lazydocker (jesseduffield/lazydocker 51225 stars — IN CORPUS), lazygit (jesseduffield/lazygit 78697 stars — IN CORPUS), kuberhealthy (K8s synthetic checks 2238 stars), nodeadm (AWS EKS bootstrap 2650 stars on amazon-eks-ami), sleep-on-lan (458 stars), and many others.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (proc-macro on struct, COMPILE-TIME CODEGEN), R322 Java JCommander (annotations + RUNTIME REFLECTION), R334 Go alecthomas/kong (struct tags + RUNTIME REFLECTION), R342 alexflint/go-arg (struct tags + RUNTIME REFLECTION), R338 Odin core:flags (struct tags + RTTI), R340 oclif (class + static fields), R341 yargs (fluent builder + `.argv`), R343 commander.js (explicit instance + `.parse()`).  flaggy's approach: POINTER-BASED IMPERATIVE BUILDER + NO REFLECTION + EXPLICIT FLAG BINDING.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-NINE library variants: Rust (9), **Go (7 — cobra/urfave-cli/kingpin/spf13-pflag/alecthomas-kong/alexflint-go-arg/integrii-flaggy)**, Python (4), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua 2, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse (R349), **Go 7 integrii/flaggy** (R350 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 89 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Go NOW HAS SEVEN documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: nodeadm uses **flaggy pointer-based-flag-binding CLI pattern** at internal/cli/options.go:27 + cmd/nodeadm/init/init.go:30-31 — `flaggy.Bool(&opts.DevelopmentMode, "d", "development", "Enable development mode for logging.")` + `c.cmd.StringSlice(&c.daemons, "d", "daemon", "specify one or more of containerd and kubelet. This is intended for testing and should not be used in a production environment.")` + `c.cmd.StringSlice(&c.skipPhases, "s", "skip", "phases of the bootstrap you want to skip")`.  flaggy's POINTER-BASED FLAG BINDING means the caller PRE-DECLARES the destination variable (`opts.DevelopmentMode` of type `bool`, `c.daemons` of type `[]string`, etc.) and passes `&dest` to the appropriate registration function (`flaggy.Bool` for global bool, `c.cmd.StringSlice` for subcommand-scoped repeatable string).  flaggy POPULATES the destination at parse time via Go pointer assignment — NO REFLECTION, NO struct-tag DSL, NO codegen.  Each TYPE has its own registration function: `flaggy.Bool`/`flaggy.Int`/`flaggy.String`/`flaggy.IntSlice`/`flaggy.StringSlice`/`flaggy.Duration` (global) + same methods on `*flaggy.Subcommand` (per-subcommand).  Distinct from R334 kong (`Foo string \`kong:"name='X'"\`` struct tag), R342 alexflint/go-arg (`Foo string \`arg:"--foo"\`` struct tag), R275 clap-derive (`foo: String` struct field + `#[arg(...)]` attribute), R338 Odin core:flags (`foo: string \`args:"name=X"\`` struct tag).  flaggy's approach: POINTER-BASED FLAG BINDING via `&dest` PRE-DECLARED VARIABLE + TYPE-SPECIFIC REGISTRATION FUNCTION per supported type — corpus's **first documented pointer-based-flag-binding-with-type-specific-registration CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: nodeadm uses **flaggy AttachSubcommand-with-positional-index CLI pattern** at internal/cli/container.go:31 — `c.cmd.AttachSubcommand(cmd.Flaggy(), 1)`.  flaggy's `parent.AttachSubcommand(child, position)` registers `child` as a subcommand of `parent` AT THE GIVEN POSITIONAL ARG INDEX.  `position == 1` means "child can appear as the first positional arg after parent"; `position == 2` means "as the second positional", etc.  This is a POSITION-AWARE subcommand registration distinct from other libraries which assume position 1.  Allows mid-arg subcommand chains like `tool flag1 flag2 verb arg1 arg2` where `verb` is at position 1 RELATIVE TO ITS PARENT (after flags).  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum` (positional-implicit, always after parent flags), R334 alecthomas/kong `kong:"cmd"` tag (positional-implicit), R342 alexflint/go-arg `arg:"subcommand:X"` (positional-implicit), R340 oclif filesystem-hierarchy-walking (subcommand position implicit from filesystem structure).  flaggy's approach: EXPLICIT POSITIONAL INDEX in `AttachSubcommand(child, N)` — corpus's **first documented AttachSubcommand-with-explicit-positional-index CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: nodeadm uses **flaggy sub.Used post-parse presence-check CLI pattern** at internal/cli/container.go:36-43 — `for _, cmd := range c.cmds { if cmd.Flaggy().Used { return cmd.Run(log, opts) } } flaggy.ShowHelpAndExit("No command specified")`.  After `flaggy.Parse()` returns, each registered subcommand has a `.Used` BOOL field that flaggy sets to `true` IF that subcommand was selected on the command line.  Caller iterates over subcommands and dispatches to the one with `.Used == true`.  No `.Used` set ⇒ no subcommand was given ⇒ caller dispatches to default action or shows help via `flaggy.ShowHelpAndExit(msg)`.  Distinct from R342 alexflint/go-arg POINTER-TO-STRUCT NIL-CHECK (subcommand pointer is nil if not used), R275 clap-derive ENUM-VARIANT MATCH (caller pattern-matches on enum), R334 kong CONTEXT.RUN reflection-based dispatch.  flaggy's approach: `sub.Used` BOOL FIELD set by flaggy at parse time + CALLER ITERATES and dispatches on first matching `.Used` — corpus's **first documented sub.Used-bool-field-post-parse-presence-check CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: nodeadm uses **flaggy package-level-function-for-global-vs-method-for-subcommand CLI pattern** at options.go:27 (global) vs init.go:30 (subcommand) — `flaggy.Bool(&opts.DevelopmentMode, "d", "development", "...")` (PACKAGE-LEVEL function for GLOBAL flag) vs `c.cmd.StringSlice(&c.daemons, "d", "daemon", "...")` (METHOD on `*flaggy.Subcommand` for PER-SUBCOMMAND flag).  flaggy distinguishes scope via Go's PACKAGE-LEVEL FUNCTIONS (globals) vs INSTANCE METHODS (subcommand-scoped): `flaggy.Bool(...)` registers a flag at the ROOT scope (visible to all subcommands); `subcmd.Bool(...)` registers a flag VISIBLE ONLY when that subcommand is invoked.  Distinct from R341 yargs `.option("name", { global: true })` (per-flag global directive), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(global = true)]` (attribute), R340 oclif inheritance-from-base-class (`extends BaseCommand`).  flaggy's approach: PACKAGE-LEVEL FUNCTION for GLOBAL + INSTANCE METHOD for SCOPED — corpus's **first documented package-level-function-vs-instance-method-for-scope-distinction CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_lazydocker` (jesseduffield/lazydocker 51225 stars — IN CORPUS; uses flaggy but already documented under different attribution), `_lazygit` (jesseduffield/lazygit 78697 stars — IN CORPUS; uses flaggy but already documented), `_kuberhealthy` (2238 stars — only 3 flags `-c/-d/--forceMaster`, thin surface vs nodeadm's nested-subcommand structure), `_sleep-on-lan` (SR-G/sleep-on-lan 458 stars — narrow Wake-on-LAN sleeper), `_hkmgr` (bensallen/hkmgr — narrow xhyve helper).  Probed alternative Go CLI libraries: `_mitchellh/cli`-based tools (mitchellh/cli would be 8th Go CLI library — `cli.NewCLI` + `cli.Commands` map; HOLD), `_jessevdk/go-flags`-based tools (would be 8th or 9th; struct-tag-driven similar to kong but different DSL; HOLD), `_pborman/getopt`-based tools (GNU-getopt-port; HOLD).  Considered `_lazycomposer` (alternative jesseduffield/lazycomposer — niche).  Probed `_kuberhealthy` and `_sleep-on-lan` but their surfaces are thin compared to nodeadm.  Chose nodeadm: 2650 GH stars on amazon-eks-ami (AWS-official EKS Node Admin bootstrap CLI), 1 global flag + 2 top-level subcommands (`config` + `init`) + 2 nested config sub-subcommands (`check` + `dump`) + 4 init flags + 2-3 flags per config sub-subcommand + POINTER-BASED FLAG BINDING + global vs subcommand scope distinction + AttachSubcommand-with-positional-index + sub.Used post-parse dispatch + flaggy.ShowHelpAndExit helper, full nodeadm/* exercising EVERY MAJOR flaggy PATTERN, real production binary shipped in every AWS-managed EKS AMI used by thousands of EKS deployments globally.
  - Blacklist update: `nodeadm` added.  Corpus count: 28,772 → 28,773 (+1).

- **R349 — ypp source-direct (Yet a PreProcessor — Lua-based text/markdown preprocessor in luax distribution; mpeterv/argparse library)** (1 file new) — pivots from at-grandpa/clim (R348 savi) to a SECOND Lua CLI library — corpus's **first documented mpeterv/argparse library** + **second documented Lua CLI library**.  ypp is Christophe Delord's (CDSoft) text preprocessor — part of the `luax` Lua-5.4-based interpreter+REPL+standalone-executable-compiler project.  Processes `@`-prefixed Lua expressions in text/markdown, supports image generation (SVG/PDF/PNG), Lua script include semantics, and Make-style dependency tracking (`--MD`/`--MF`/`--MT`).  Part of luax (206 GH stars on the GitHub mirror; current home at codeberg.org/cdsoft/luax).  Sister tools: m4 (in corpus), gpp (in corpus), pandoc (in corpus, similar @-syntax preprocessor extensions), erb (Ruby ERB, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented mpeterv/argparse library** + **second documented Lua CLI library**.  mpeterv/argparse is THE de-facto Lua CLI library (289 GH stars) used by luacheck (IN CORPUS), busted (IN CORPUS), ldoc (IN CORPUS), and many other Lua tools.  Lua ecosystem now has 2 documented CLI libraries.  Adds mpeterv/argparse to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: argparse is METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER using Lua's COLON SYNTAX (`:`) which invokes a method on the receiver and RETURNS THE RECEIVER for chaining.  `require "argparse"()` MODULE-AS-CALLABLE creates a parser; `parser : name "X"` sets program name; `parser : flag "-X" : description "Y"` registers a boolean flag with chained per-flag config; `parser : option "-X" : argname "PLACEHOLDER" : count "*" : choices {"a", "b"}` registers a value-taking flag with placeholder text, repeatability marker, and restricted choices; `parser : argument "name" : args "*"` registers a positional argument with variadic count; `parser:parse()` returns a TABLE of parsed values keyed by flag name (or `:target "field-name"` override).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 88.
  - `_ypp` (1-stem; CDSoft/luax ypp/ypp.lua + mpeterv/argparse library; **mpeterv/argparse library** — `require "argparse"()` brings the parser-factory into scope; **14 flags (`-v`/`-a`/`-l`/`-e`/`-D`/`-p`/`-o`/`-t`/`-s`/`--MT`/`--MF`/`--MD`/`--img`/`--meta`) + 1 positional (variadic input files) + `parser:name(X)` SET PROGRAM NAME + `parser:description(X)` SET DESCRIPTION + `parser:epilog(X)` SET HELP FOOTER + `parser:flag(X)` BOOLEAN FLAG registration + `parser:option(X)` VALUE-TAKING FLAG registration + `parser:argument(X)` POSITIONAL ARG registration + `:description(X)` chained per-flag description + `:target(field)` chained destination field rename + `:argname(PLACEHOLDER)` chained value placeholder + `:count "*"` chained repeatability marker (Lua-string-arg syntax `:count "*"` equivalent to `:count("*")` due to Lua's no-paren-for-single-string-arg syntax) + `:args "*"` chained variadic-positional marker + `:choices {"svg", "pdf", "png"}` chained restricted-choices LIST + `:action(fn)` chained callback closure + `parser:parse()` TERMINAL PARSE returning TABLE**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section line 1 (`local parser = require "argparse"()` — Lua REQUIRE statement returns the argparse module; the module is itself CALLABLE (a function-table) — `()` invokes it to construct a new parser instance)
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section lines 2-4 (`: name "ypp" : description(("ypp %s\\nYet a PreProcessor"):format(_YPP_VERSION)) : epilog "For more information, see https://codeberg.org/cdsoft/luax"` — CHAINED METHOD CALLS using Lua's COLON SYNTAX; each `:method(arg)` invokes the method on the previous return value and RETURNS the receiver for further chaining; `:method "string"` is shorthand for `:method("string")` (Lua omits parens for single-string args))
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section lines 6-8 (`parser : flag "-v" : description "Show ypp version" : action(function(_, _, _, _) print(_YPP_VERSION); os.exit() end)` — `:flag(X)` registers boolean flag; `:action(fn)` registers a callback closure invoked when flag matches; closure receives 4 args (parser, params, value, options))
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section lines 15-19 (`parser : option "-l" : description "Execute a Lua script" : argname "script" : count "*" : action(function(_, _, script, _) load_script(script) end)` — `:option(X)` registers VALUE-TAKING flag; `:argname(PLACEHOLDER)` declares the value placeholder in help text; `:count "*"` marks the flag REPEATABLE (0 or more occurrences) — distinct from `:count "1"` (exactly one), `:count "?"` (0 or 1), `:count "+"` (1 or more))
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section lines 67-72 (`parser : option "-t" : description "Set the default format of generated images" : target "image_format" : choices { "svg", "pdf", "png" }` — `:choices LIST` declares RESTRICTED choice set; `:target "field-name"` RENAMES the destination field in the parsed table (parsed flag stored as `args.image_format` rather than `args.t`))
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` argparse section lines 100-107 (`parser : argument "input" : description "Input file" : args "*" : action(function(_, _, names, _) if #names == 0 then names = {"-"} end ... end)` — `:argument(NAME)` registers POSITIONAL ARGUMENT (not a flag); `:args "*"` marks the positional VARIADIC (0+ values consumed))
    * `ypp/ypp.lua` line 109 (`return F.patch(parser:parse(), {output=output})` — `parser:parse()` TRIGGERS PARSING and returns a Lua TABLE keyed by flag/target/argument names with parsed values; F.patch is luax's table-merge helper to overlay closure-captured state)
  - Critical extraction note: ypp uses **mpeterv/argparse library** at ypp.lua argparse section line 1 — corpus's **first documented mpeterv/argparse library** + **second documented Lua CLI library**.  mpeterv/argparse is Peter Melnichenko's argparse — THE DE-FACTO Lua CLI library (289 GH stars).  Used by luacheck (Lua linter — IN CORPUS), busted (Lua test framework — IN CORPUS), ldoc (Lua documentation generator — IN CORPUS), and many other Lua tools including ypp.  argparse uses METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER with Lua's COLON SYNTAX — `parser : flag "-v" : description "..." : action(fn)` chains via colon-method-call on the receiver returning the receiver.  argparse also leverages Lua's NO-PAREN-FOR-SINGLE-STRING-ARG syntax: `:name "ypp"` is equivalent to `:name("ypp")` since Lua allows omitting parens when the only arg is a string literal.  Distinct from R341 yargs (TypeScript fluent-builder with `.argv` terminal accessor), R343 commander.js (TypeScript explicit-instance with `program.parse()` final), R346 mainargs (Scala annotation-based on method), R275 clap-derive (Rust proc-macro on struct), R322 JCommander (Java annotations on fields).  argparse's approach: METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER + LUA COLON SYNTAX + REQUIRE-MODULE-AS-CALLABLE for parser construction.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (9), Go (6), Python (4), Crystal 4, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), **Lua (2 — Lua R285 + mpeterv/argparse R349)**, C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim (R348), **Lua 2 mpeterv/argparse** (R349 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 88 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Lua NOW HAS TWO documented CLI libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: ypp uses **mpeterv/argparse method-chain-with-colon-syntax CLI pattern** at ypp.lua argparse section lines 1-7 — `local parser = require "argparse"() : name "ypp" : description "..." : epilog "..."` + `parser : flag "-v" : description "Show ypp version" : action(function() ... end)`.  Lua's COLON SYNTAX `obj:method(args)` is equivalent to `obj.method(obj, args)` — implicitly passes the receiver as the FIRST ARGUMENT, allowing METHOD-CHAINING when each method returns `self`.  argparse leverages this for a FLUENT BUILDER API where each method call MUTATES the parser/flag spec AND returns the receiver.  Combined with Lua's NO-PAREN-FOR-SINGLE-STRING-ARG shorthand (`:name "X"` ≡ `:name("X")`), the API looks remarkably terse for what it does.  Distinct from R341 yargs (JavaScript `.method()` chain — yargs uses dot-syntax not colon, no implicit-receiver), R343 commander.js (similar JS dot-syntax chain), R346 mainargs (Scala annotation-based, no method-chain), R275 clap-derive (Rust proc-macro, no method-chain).  argparse's approach: COLON-SYNTAX METHOD CHAIN + NO-PAREN-STRING-LITERAL shorthand — corpus's **first documented colon-syntax-method-chain-with-no-paren-string CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ypp uses **mpeterv/argparse `:count` quantifier-string CLI pattern** at ypp.lua argparse section lines 18 + 25 + 31 + 37 — `: count "*"` on `-l`/`-e`/`-D`/`-p` flags marks them REPEATABLE.  argparse's `:count` accepts a QUANTIFIER STRING (similar to regex quantifiers): `"*"` = 0 or more, `"+"` = 1 or more, `"?"` = 0 or 1, `"N"` = exactly N, `"M-N"` = M to N occurrences.  Same syntax used for `:args` on positional arguments to declare VARIADIC consumption.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `Vec<T>` field type (no separate quantifier, Vec implies *), R344 argh `Vec<T>` field type (same), R342 alexflint/go-arg `arg:"separate"` directive (binary distinction — single vs repeatable), R346 mainargs `Seq[T]` parameter type (implies *).  argparse's approach: `:count "QUANTIFIER"` and `:args "QUANTIFIER"` STRING DSL for repetition specifications — corpus's **first documented quantifier-string-for-count-and-args CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ypp uses **mpeterv/argparse module-as-callable factory CLI pattern** at ypp.lua argparse section line 1 — `local parser = require "argparse"()`.  argparse's MODULE table is ITSELF CALLABLE — Lua's `__call` metamethod is set on the module table so that INVOKING the module (`module()`) constructs a NEW PARSER INSTANCE.  This is a Lua-idiom-specific MODULE-AS-FACTORY-FUNCTION pattern that lets users write `require "X"()` to both LOAD the module AND CONSTRUCT an instance in one expression.  Distinct from R346 Scala mainargs `ParserForMethods(this)` (explicit factory function call), R275 Rust clap-derive `Cli::parse()` (struct's static method), R341 yargs `yargs.command()` (module-level singleton + method calls).  argparse's approach: MODULE TABLE IS CALLABLE — `require "argparse"()` LOADS + CONSTRUCTS in one expression — corpus's **first documented module-as-callable-factory CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ypp uses **mpeterv/argparse `:target` field-rename CLI pattern** at ypp.lua argparse section line 68 — `: target "image_format"` on the `-t` flag.  argparse's `:target("field-name")` RENAMES the destination field in the parsed table — instead of `args.t` the parsed value lands at `args.image_format`.  Useful when the user-facing flag NAME differs from the desired API/code field NAME (e.g. short cryptic flag `-t` but readable field `image_format`).  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(rename_all = "snake_case")]` (struct-level rename), R322 JCommander `@Parameter(names = "-t", defaultValueExpression = "...")` (no rename, field name is the destination), R346 mainargs (parameter name IS the destination), R341 yargs `{alias: "X"}` (alias adds another flag name, doesn't rename target).  argparse's approach: `:target("X")` chained method RENAMES the destination field — corpus's **first documented chained-target-rename-for-destination-field CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_luacheck` (mpeterv/luacheck — IN CORPUS already; canonical argparse consumer), `_busted` (Olivine-Labs/busted — IN CORPUS already; uses argparse), `_ldoc` (lunarmodules/ldoc — IN CORPUS already; uses argparse), `_luax` (CDSoft/luax 206 stars top-level binary — build.lua argparse usage is minimal 1-flag surface, much smaller than ypp's 14-flag surface; HOLD for future round when richer top-level luax usage discovered), `_lua` (DUP — `lua` is the language interpreter, has its own completion), `_libmoon` (tumi8/libmoon 0 stars — too narrow).  Probed `_moonscript` (likely IN CORPUS or DUP).  Considered using ypp's sister tool `_lsvg` (also part of luax — SVG generator) — could be future round.  Chose ypp: real production text preprocessor part of luax distribution (206 stars), 14 flags + 1 variadic positional, full ypp.lua exercising EVERY MAJOR mpeterv/argparse PATTERN (`:flag`/`:option`/`:argument` + `:count "*"` + `:args "*"` + `:choices LIST` + `:target` rename + `:argname PLACEHOLDER` + `:action(fn)` callback + `:description`/`:name`/`:epilog` metadata + module-as-callable factory + colon-syntax method chain + no-paren-string-literal shorthand).
  - Blacklist update: `ypp` added.  Corpus count: 28,771 → 28,772 (+1).

- **R348 — savi source-direct (Savi compiler — a fast Pony-influenced object-capability language; Crystal at-grandpa/clim library)** (1 file new) — pivots from RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347 uw8) to a FOURTH Crystal CLI library — corpus's **first documented at-grandpa/clim library** + **fourth documented Crystal CLI library**.  savi is Joe Eli McIlvain's Savi language compiler — built on the Pony actor-model + object-capability foundation but with a more modern ergonomic syntax.  Compiles via LLVM to native code.  The savi compiler itself is written in Crystal.  157 GH stars.  Sister tools: pony (R298 Pony stdlib cli, in corpus), rustc (Rust, in corpus), zig (Zig, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented at-grandpa/clim library** + **fourth documented Crystal CLI library** — Crystal ecosystem now has 4 documented CLI libraries (R269 stdlib OptionParser + R299 Admiral + R332 commander.cr + R348 clim) joining the multi-library leaders.  Adds clim (at-grandpa/clim) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: clim is CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-DSL via `class < Clim` + `main do ... end` BLOCK + TYPED OPTIONS via `type: T` parameter leveraging Crystal's strong static type system (UInt32/String/Bool/UInt16 etc.) + NESTED SUBCOMMANDS via `sub "name" do ... end` blocks INSIDE main + SUBCOMMAND ALIASES via `alias_name "shortname"` + POSITIONAL ARGUMENTS via `argument "name", type: T, required: ...`.  Distinct from R269 Crystal stdlib OptionParser (`parser.on(...)` method-chain, no subcommands), R299 Admiral (CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-MACROS via `class < Admiral::Command` + `define_flag :name, type: T`), R332 commander.cr (BLOCK-BUILDER via `Commander::Command.new do |cmd| ... end`).  clim's distinguishing TYPED-OPTIONS feature: `option "-p NUMBER", "--port=NUMBER", type: UInt32, desc: "..."` — when parsed, OPTION VALUE IS COERCED to the declared type AT PARSE TIME (string "3001" becomes UInt32 3001).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 87.
  - `_savi` (1-stem; savi-lang/savi main.cr; **at-grandpa/clim library** — `require "clim"` brings clim into scope; **14 top-level options (`-h/--help` + `-v/--version` + `-b/--backtrace` + `-r/--release` + `--fix` + `--no-debug` + `--with-runtime-asserts` + `--llvm-ir` + `--llvm-keep-fns` + `--llvm-optimize-nothing` + `--print-perf` + `-X/--cross-compile=TRIPLE` + `-C/--cd=DIR` + `-p/--pass=NAME`) + 5+ nested subcommands (`info` + `server` + `eval` + `run` + `deps` + `add` + `fmt`) + subcommand aliases (`s`/`e`/`r`) + positional arguments + `class Cli < Clim` CLASS INHERITANCE + `main do ... end` BLOCK declaration + TYPED OPTIONS via `type: Bool/String/UInt32/UInt16` + `option "-X NAME", "--long=NAME", desc: "Y", type: T, default: V` syntax + `sub "name" do ... end` NESTED SUBCOMMAND BLOCKS + `alias_name "n"` per-subcommand alias + `argument "name", type: T, required: true/false, desc: "..."` POSITIONAL ARGS + `run do |opts, args| ... end` HANDLER BLOCK**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `main.cr` line 3 (`require "clim"` — Crystal REQUIRE statement bringing clim into scope; clim is distributed via Crystal's `shards` package manager as `at-grandpa/clim`)
    * `main.cr` lines 8-22 (`module Savi\n  class Cli < Clim\n    main do\n      desc "Savi compiler."\n      usage "savi [sub_command]. Default sub_command in build"\n      version [...].join("\\n"), short: "-v"\n      help short: "-h"\n      option "-b", "--backtrace", desc: "Show backtrace on error", type: Bool, default: false\n      option "-r", "--release", desc: "Compile in release mode (i.e. with optimizations)", type: Bool, default: false\n      ...\n      option "-X", "--cross-compile=TRIPLE", desc: "Cross compile to the given target triple"\n      option "-C", "--cd=DIR", desc: "Change the working directory"\n      option "-p NAME", "--pass=NAME", desc: "Name of the compiler pass to target"\n      run do |opts, args|\n        ...\n      end` — CLASS INHERITANCE `class Cli < Clim` + `main do ... end` BLOCK with DSL-style spec declaration; `option "..."` declares each flag with `type: T` for COMPILE-TIME-TYPED parse + `default:` for default value)
    * `main.cr` lines 45-65 (`sub "info" do\n  desc "get information"\n  usage "savi info [version|llvm-version|bin-path]"\n  help short: "-h"\n  argument "kind", type: String, desc: "kind of information to get"\n  run do |opts, args|\n    case args.kind\n    when "version" -> puts Savi::VERSION\n    ...\n    end\n  end\nend` — `sub "name" do ... end` NESTED SUBCOMMAND BLOCK inside `main do ... end`; `argument "name", type: T, desc: "..."` declares POSITIONAL ARG accessed via `args.kind`)
    * `main.cr` lines 66-76 (`sub "server" do\n  alias_name "s"\n  desc "run lsp server"\n  ...\n  run do |opts, args| Savi::Server.new.run end\nend` — `alias_name "shortname"` inside sub block registers an ALIAS for the subcommand)
    * `main.cr` lines 77-95 (`sub "eval" do\n  alias_name "e"\n  desc "evaluate code"\n  usage "savi eval [code] [options]"\n  help short: "-h"\n  argument "code", type: String, required: true, desc: "code to evaluate"\n  option ... \n  run do |opts, args|\n    options = ...\n    Cli.eval args.code, options, opts.backtrace\n  end\nend` — `argument "code", type: String, required: true` declares REQUIRED POSITIONAL; opts and args are accessed via `opts.backtrace` and `args.code`)
  - Critical extraction note: savi uses **at-grandpa/clim library** at main.cr:3 — corpus's **first documented at-grandpa/clim library** + **fourth documented Crystal CLI library** (joining R269 stdlib OptionParser, R299 Admiral, R332 commander.cr).  at-grandpa/clim is the Crystal CLI framework — distinct from Admiral's CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-MACROS (`class < Admiral::Command` + `define_flag :name, type: T`), distinct from commander.cr's BLOCK-BUILDER (`Commander::Command.new do |cmd| ... end`), distinct from stdlib OptionParser's `parser.on(...)` method-chain.  clim combines: (1) CLASS INHERITANCE via `class Cli < Clim`; (2) BLOCK DSL via `main do ... end` containing SPEC DECLARATIONS; (3) TYPED OPTIONS via `type: T` parameter leveraging Crystal's strong static type system — value PARSED FROM STRING and COERCED TO T at parse time; (4) NESTED SUBCOMMANDS via `sub "verb" do ... end` blocks INSIDE main (clim supports arbitrary nesting depth); (5) SUBCOMMAND ALIASES via `alias_name "shortname"`; (6) POSITIONAL ARGUMENTS via `argument "name", type: T, required: ...` — accessed via `args.name` in the run block.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (proc-macro on struct), R322 Java JCommander (annotations + reflection), R340 oclif (class + static fields), R341 yargs (imperative builder + `.argv` accessor), R343 commander.js (imperative builder + `program.parse()`).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (9), Go (6), Python (4), **Crystal (4 — stdlib OptionParser R269 + Admiral R299 + commander.cr R332 + clim R348)**, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args (R347), **Crystal 4 at-grandpa/clim** (R348 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 87 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Crystal NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries (stdlib OptionParser + Admiral + commander.cr + clim).
  - Critical extraction note: savi uses **clim class-inheritance-with-main-do-block + DSL CLI pattern** at main.cr:8-22 — `class Cli < Clim\n  main do\n    desc "..."\n    usage "..."\n    version [...].join("\\n"), short: "-v"\n    help short: "-h"\n    option "-b", "--backtrace", desc: "...", type: Bool, default: false\n    ...\n    run do |opts, args| ... end\n  end\nend`.  clim's pattern: (1) CLASS INHERITS from `Clim`; (2) CLASS BODY contains a `main do ... end` block that's a DSL for declaring the top-level CLI; (3) BLOCK contains spec statements (`desc`, `usage`, `version`, `help`, `option`, `argument`, `run`) + NESTED `sub do ... end` BLOCKS for subcommands.  Distinct from R299 Crystal Admiral `class < Admiral::Command` + `define_flag :name, type: T, default: V` (Admiral uses MACRO calls for flags, clim uses BLOCK DSL), R332 Crystal commander.cr `Commander::Command.new do |cmd| ... end` (commander.cr uses BLOCK BUILDER on an INSTANCE, clim uses BLOCK BUILDER inside a CLASS DECLARATION), R269 Crystal stdlib OptionParser `parser.on(...)` (method-chain on parser instance).  clim's approach: CLASS-INHERITANCE + `main do ... end` BLOCK DSL + nested `sub do ... end` SUBCOMMAND BLOCKS — corpus's **first documented class-inheritance-with-main-do-block-DSL CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: savi uses **clim typed-options-via-type-parameter CLI pattern** at main.cr:17-21 + 46 (cocol.cr:55 similarly) — `option "-b", "--backtrace", desc: "Show backtrace on error", type: Bool, default: false` + `option "-p NUMBER", "--port=NUMBER", type: UInt32, desc: "Change port (default 3001)"` (in cocol).  clim's `option` declaration ACCEPTS a `type: T` parameter where T is a CRYSTAL TYPE: `Bool`, `String`, `Int32`, `UInt32`, `UInt16`, `Float64`, etc.  Value PARSED from CLI string and COERCED to T at parse time — caller accesses `opts.port` as an actual `UInt32` value, not as a string.  Type T's CRYSTAL `from_string`/`new` method determines coercion behavior — caller gets COMPILE-TIME TYPE SAFETY in the run block.  Distinct from R299 Admiral `define_flag :name, type: T` (similar but uses MACRO syntax with symbol-name-and-type), R275 clap-derive (Rust type-driven inference from struct field types), R346 mainargs (Scala type-driven inference from method param types), R322 JCommander (uses `type = X.class` annotation key — Java requires explicit conversion class).  clim's approach: `type: T` PARAMETER on `option` declaration + Crystal's TYPE SYSTEM coerces at parse time — corpus's **first documented type-parameter-with-Crystal-type-coercion CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: savi uses **clim nested-sub-do-block CLI pattern** at main.cr:45-95+ — `sub "info" do\n  desc "..."\n  usage "..."\n  argument "kind", type: String, desc: "..."\n  run do |opts, args|\n    case args.kind\n    when "version" -> puts ...\n    ...\n    end\n  end\nend` + `sub "server" do\n  alias_name "s"\n  ...\nend` + `sub "eval" do\n  alias_name "e"\n  argument "code", type: String, required: true, desc: "..."\n  ...\nend`.  clim's `sub "name" do ... end` NESTED INSIDE `main do ... end` declares SUBCOMMANDS — each sub block has its own DSL declarations (`desc`, `usage`, `argument`, `option`, `run`).  `alias_name "n"` inside a sub block REGISTERS A SUBCOMMAND ALIAS (so `savi server` and `savi s` both invoke the LSP server).  ARBITRARY NESTING DEPTH supported.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { ... }` (enum-of-variants), R342 alexflint/go-arg POINTER-TO-STRUCT field with `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag, R340 oclif FILESYSTEM-HIERARCHY-WALKING (commands are files), R346 mainargs MULTIPLE `@main` METHODS (each becomes subcommand at object level).  clim's approach: NESTED `sub "name" do ... end` BLOCKS inside `main do ... end` + `alias_name "n"` for subcommand aliases — corpus's **first documented nested-sub-do-block-with-alias_name CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: savi uses **clim argument-positional-via-argument-keyword CLI pattern** at main.cr:48 + 80 + 102 — `argument "kind", type: String, desc: "kind of information to get"` + `argument "code", type: String, required: true, desc: "code to evaluate"` + `argument "name", type: String, required: false, desc: "Name of the manifest to compile"`.  clim's `argument "name", type: T, required: true/false, desc: "..."` declares POSITIONAL ARGUMENTS — accessed via `args.name` in the `run do |opts, args| ... end` block (where `args` is a Crystal struct-like accessor object generated by clim's macros).  `required: true` makes the positional MANDATORY, `required: false` makes it OPTIONAL.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(index = N)]` (index attribute), R332 commander.cr `arg.name = "X"` (block builder on Argument), R341 yargs `'verb [arg]'` (positional embedded in name string), R343 commander.js `<arg>`/`[arg]` (angle/square brackets in option string).  clim's approach: SEPARATE `argument "name", type: T, required: ...` declaration KEYWORD (parallel to `option`) — corpus's **first documented argument-keyword-parallel-to-option-keyword CLI pattern**.
