  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_cocol` (cocol-project/cocol 21 stars, blockchain prototyping — uses clim but with simpler 7-flag surface, would be a SISTER demo; HOLD for future round), `_ncal` (pes18fan/ncal 1 star — DUP with stock _ncal nepali calendar in corpus), `_lead` (spinscale 1 star — narrow elasticsearch alerting), `_crystaltwin` (incubaid 0 stars — too narrow), `_keycloak_csv_user_import` (kill9zombie — narrow keycloak utility), `_torrent_client` (lbarasti — narrow torrent client).  Probed alternative Crystal CLI libraries: `_cling`-based tools (devnote-dev/cling — would be 5th Crystal CLI library; HOLD for future round), `_climate.cr`-based tools (Sija/climate.cr — would be 6th Crystal CLI library; HOLD for future round).  Considered using cocol instead of savi but savi has richer surface (14 top-level options + 5+ subcommands + aliases + positional args + run handlers vs cocol's 7 flags + single main).  Probed R (statistical) with `optparse` package — found multiple Bioconductor/CRAN R-script-based tools using it (alfred reppattern.R 155 stars, nf-core/atacseq deseq2_qc.r) but most are INTERNAL HELPER SCRIPTS invoked by parent C++/Python pipelines as `Rscript SCRIPT.R --flags` not standalone binaries on PATH; HOLD for future round when an R-as-standalone-binary tool is found.  Chose savi: 157 GH stars (real Crystal-implemented compiler for the Savi language), 14 top-level options + 5+ nested subcommands (`info` + `server`/`s` + `eval`/`e` + `run`/`r` + `deps` + `add` + `fmt`) + subcommand aliases + positional arguments via `argument` keyword + TYPED OPTIONS via `type: T` parameter + class inheritance `class Cli < Clim` + `main do ... end` BLOCK DSL + nested `sub do ... end` SUBCOMMAND BLOCKS + `alias_name "shortname"` per-subcommand alias + `run do |opts, args| ... end` HANDLER, full main.cr exercising EVERY MAJOR clim PATTERN.
  - Blacklist update: `savi` added.  Corpus count: 28,770 → 28,771 (+1).

- **R347 — uw8 source-direct (MicroW8 WebAssembly-based size-coding fantasy console in Rust; RazrFalcon/pico-args library)** (1 file new) — pivots from com-lihaoyi/mainargs (R346 scala-monitor) to a NINTH Rust CLI library — corpus's **first documented RazrFalcon/pico-args library** + **ninth documented Rust CLI library**.  uw8 is exoticorn (Dennis Ranke)'s MicroW8 — a size-coding fantasy console targeting WebAssembly carts (≤256 KB cart, ≤16 KB compressed) with a 320x240 framebuffer + PSG-style audio.  Inspired by PICO-8 and TIC-80 but uses WASM as the cart format.  245 GH stars.  Sister tools: WASM-4 (similar concept, in corpus), pico-8 (Lua-based proprietary), TIC-80 (multi-language cart-based fantasy console, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented RazrFalcon/pico-args library** + **ninth documented Rust CLI library** — Rust ecosystem now has NINE documented CLI libraries (clap-derive R275, bpaf R330, google/argh R344, pico-args R347, plus historical entries) — EXTENDING Rust's lead as MOST-DOCUMENTED CLI-language with 9 distinct libraries.  Adds RazrFalcon/pico-args (Yevhenii Reizner's pico-args) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: pico-args is the SMALLEST possible Rust CLI parser — NO DERIVE, NO PROC-MACRO, ZERO DEPENDENCIES (no clap, no syn, no quote, no proc-macro2 chain).  Compiled size is ~3 KB added to binary vs clap's ~300 KB.  Caller IMPERATIVELY consumes args via methods on `pico_args::Arguments`: `Arguments::from_env()` → entry point; `args.subcommand()` → consume next positional as subcommand string; `args.contains(["-x", "--long"])` → boolean flag presence check (consumes); `args.opt_value_from_str(["-x", "--long"])?` → optional value lookup (consumes); `args.value_from_str("--required")?` → required value lookup; `args.free_from_str()?` → next free positional argument with default `FromStr` parser; `args.free_from_os_str(parser_fn)?` → next positional with custom parser closure.  CALLER WRITES help text manually (no auto-generation).  Used when binary size is critical — size-coding tools, tiny embedded binaries, demoscene productions.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 86.
  - `_uw8` (1-stem; exoticorn/microw8 src/main.rs + uw8-tool/src/main.rs; **RazrFalcon/pico-args library** — `use pico_args::Arguments` brings pico-args into scope; **6 subcommands (`version` + `run` + `pack` + `unpack` + `compile` + `filter-exports` + `help`) + 8 flags for `run` (`-t/--timeout` + `-b/--browser` + `-w/--watch` + `-p/--pack` + `-u/--uncompressed` + `-l/--level` + `-o/--output` + `-m/--no-audio`) + 2 flags for `pack` (`-u/--uncompressed` + `-l/--level`) + 1 flag for `compile` (`-d/--debug`) + 2-3 positional args per subcommand + Arguments::from_env() ENTRY + args.subcommand() SUBCOMMAND-AS-STRING DISPATCH + args.contains([-x, --long]) BOOLEAN-FLAG + args.opt_value_from_str([-x, --long]) OPTIONAL-VALUE + args.free_from_str() NEXT-POSITIONAL + args.free_from_os_str(parser_fn) NEXT-POSITIONAL-WITH-CUSTOM-PARSER + MANUAL HELP TEXT (caller writes it)**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main.rs` line 7 (`use pico_args::Arguments` — RUST USE statement bringing pico-args into scope; pico-args is distributed via crates.io with NO peer dependencies — just `pico-args = "0.5"` in Cargo.toml)
    * `src/main.rs` line 16 (`let mut args = Arguments::from_env()` — pico-args ENTRY POINT: parses `std::env::args_os()` into an Arguments struct that the caller IMPERATIVELY consumes via methods)
    * `src/main.rs` lines 22-43 (`match args.subcommand()?.as_deref() { Some("version") => ..., Some("run") => run(args), Some("pack") => pack(args), Some("unpack") => unpack(args), Some("compile") => compile(args), Some("filter-exports") => filter_exports(args), Some("help") | None => { ... print help ... } Some(other) => { ... unknown command ... } }` — `args.subcommand()?` returns `Result<Option<String>>` — the next positional arg as a string, consumed from the args structure; CALLER PATTERN-MATCHES on the Option<String> to dispatch)
    * `src/main.rs` lines 56-79 (`let watch_mode = args.contains(["-w", "--watch"]); let timeout: Option<u32> = args.opt_value_from_str(["-t", "--timeout"])?; if args.contains(["-p", "--pack"]) { ... if args.contains(["-u", "--uncompressed"]) { pack = pack.uncompressed(); } if let Some(level) = args.opt_value_from_str(["-l", "--level"])? { ... } } if let Some(path) = args.opt_value_from_os_str::<_, _, bool>(["-o", "--output"], |s| Ok(s.into()))? { config.output_path = Some(path); }` — `args.contains([SHORT, LONG])` returns `bool` (consumed); `args.opt_value_from_str([SHORT, LONG])?` returns `Result<Option<T>>` where T is inferred from the bind site type annotation `Option<u32>`; `args.opt_value_from_os_str([SHORT, LONG], CUSTOM_PARSER_CLOSURE)?` returns `Result<Option<T>>` using a CUSTOM CLOSURE for parsing OsStr → T (used for paths))
    * `src/main.rs` line 95 (`let filename = args.free_from_os_str::<PathBuf, bool>(|s| Ok(s.into()))?` — `args.free_from_os_str(CUSTOM_PARSER_CLOSURE)?` consumes the next FREE (positional) argument; turbofish syntax annotates the type and the bool is a dummy error type)
    * `src/main.rs` lines 32-39 (the help text is HAND-WRITTEN in print statements — pico-args does NOT auto-generate help text; caller is responsible for writing usage strings themselves)
  - Critical extraction note: uw8 uses **RazrFalcon/pico-args library** at main.rs:7 — corpus's **first documented RazrFalcon/pico-args library** + **ninth documented Rust CLI library**.  RazrFalcon/pico-args is Yevhenii Reizner's pico-args — the SMALLEST POSSIBLE Rust CLI parser.  Designed for ABSOLUTE MINIMUM compiled binary size: NO derive macros (no proc-macro2/syn/quote dependency chain), NO reflection, NO help-text auto-generation, NO subcommand machinery (caller dispatches manually).  Compiled size: ~3 KB added to binary vs clap-derive's ~300 KB.  Used by tuc, microw8, harfbuzz/rustybuzz, gfx-rs/wgpu (xtask), rerun-io/rerun (run-wasm), oxc-project/oxc.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (proc-macro on struct, ~300 KB), R330 Rust bpaf (functional combinator, ~80 KB), R344 Rust google/argh (proc-macro, code-size-optimized but still ~100 KB), gumdrop (predates argh, similar derive-based approach), lexopt (token-iterator-based, similar minimal-size goal as pico-args but different API style: lexopt is `while let Some(arg) = parser.next()? { match arg { ... } }` token-by-token).  pico-args's approach: COMPLETELY MANUAL CALLER-CONSUMES API + NO MACROS + NO DEPS — caller imperatively calls `args.contains(["-x", "--long"])`, `args.opt_value_from_str(...)?`, `args.subcommand()?`, `args.free_from_str()?` etc. + CALLER WRITES HELP TEXT MANUALLY.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-SIX library variants: **Rust (9 — clap-derive R275 + bpaf R330 + argh R344 + pico-args R347 + plus historical entries)**, Go (6), Python (4), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala 2, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs (R346), **Rust 9 RazrFalcon/pico-args** (R347 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 86 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Rust NOW HAS NINE documented CLI libraries — EXTENDING Rust's lead.
  - Critical extraction note: uw8 uses **pico-args imperative-caller-consumes API CLI pattern** at main.rs:16 + 22 + 56-79 — `let mut args = Arguments::from_env()` + `args.subcommand()?.as_deref()` + `args.contains(["-w", "--watch"])` + `args.opt_value_from_str(["-t", "--timeout"])?` + `args.opt_value_from_os_str(["-o", "--output"], |s| Ok(s.into()))?` + `args.free_from_os_str::<PathBuf, bool>(|s| Ok(s.into()))?`.  pico-args has NO DECLARATIVE SPEC — caller IMPERATIVELY consumes args via methods on `Arguments`.  Each `contains`/`opt_value_from_str`/`free_from_str` call CONSUMES matching args from the internal state and returns the result.  CALLER controls the ORDER of consumption.  After all expected args are consumed, caller can call `args.finish()` to check for unrecognized leftover args.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (declarative struct + auto-parse), R330 bpaf (functional combinators), R344 argh (declarative struct + derive macro), R345 cyclopts (declarative function signature + Annotated), R346 mainargs (declarative method signature + annotations).  pico-args's approach: IMPERATIVE CALLER-CONSUMES API — no spec, no derive, no reflection, no help-text generation — caller writes all the matching logic — corpus's **first documented imperative-caller-consumes-no-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: uw8 uses **pico-args contains-vs-opt_value-vs-free distinction CLI pattern** at main.rs:56-95 — `args.contains(["-w", "--watch"])` for BOOLEAN FLAGS (returns bool; consumes the flag if present), `args.opt_value_from_str(["-t", "--timeout"])?` for OPTIONAL VALUES (returns `Result<Option<T>>`; consumes the flag AND its value if present), `args.value_from_str(["--required", "-r"])?` for REQUIRED VALUES (returns `Result<T>`; errors if not present), `args.free_from_str()?` for NEXT POSITIONAL (returns `Result<T>` for the next non-flag arg using default `FromStr`), `args.free_from_os_str(closure)?` for NEXT POSITIONAL WITH CUSTOM PARSER (caller-supplied closure for OsStr → T conversion).  These methods FOR CALLER DECIDE the semantics of each arg.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive's TYPE-DRIVEN inference (`bool` → flag, `Option<T>` → optional, `Vec<T>` → repeatable), R346 mainargs's TYPE-DRIVEN inference (`Boolean`/`Flag`/`Option[T]`/`Seq[T]`), R344 argh's TYPE-DRIVEN inference (similar).  pico-args's approach: SEPARATE METHOD per ARG SEMANTIC — corpus's **first documented separate-method-per-arg-semantic CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: uw8 uses **pico-args subcommand-as-string-via-args.subcommand() CLI pattern** at main.rs:22 — `match args.subcommand()?.as_deref() { Some("run") => run(args), Some("pack") => pack(args), ... Some("help") | None => { ... print help ... } }`.  pico-args's `args.subcommand()` returns `Result<Option<String>>` — the next positional arg as a string, CONSUMED from the args structure.  CALLER PATTERN-MATCHES on the Option<String> to DISPATCH to handler functions, passing the remaining `args` (now without the subcommand) to the handler.  NO SUBCOMMAND REGISTRY — caller writes the match arm.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { ... }` (enum-of-variants), R344 argh `#[argh(subcommand)] enum { Foo(Foo), Bar(Bar) }` (enum-of-variants), R346 mainargs MULTIPLE `@main` methods (each becomes a subcommand), R342 alexflint/go-arg POINTER-TO-STRUCT field with `arg:"subcommand:X"` (Go reflection).  pico-args's approach: `args.subcommand()` returns STRING + CALLER MATCH-ARM DISPATCH — corpus's **first documented args.subcommand()-string-returning-with-caller-match-arm-dispatch CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: uw8 uses **pico-args manual-help-text CLI pattern** at main.rs:32-39 — `println!("uw8 {}", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")); println!(); println!("Usage:"); println!("  uw8 run [-t/--timeout <frames>] [--b/--browser] [-w/--watch] [-p/--pack] [-u/--uncompressed] [-l/--level] [-o/--output <out-file>] <file>"); ...`.  pico-args does NOT auto-generate help text — caller is FULLY RESPONSIBLE for writing the usage string in `println!` statements.  This is a deliberate design choice for minimum binary size — auto-generated help text requires storing flag specs as data structures that bloat the compiled binary.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (auto-generates rich help from doc comments + attributes), R344 argh (auto-generates help from doc comments), R346 mainargs (auto-generates help from `@arg(doc = "...")` + method docstring), R345 cyclopts (auto-generates help from docstring).  pico-args's approach: MANUAL HELP TEXT — corpus's **first documented manual-help-text CLI pattern** (caller writes help; library provides no help-generation).
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_tuc` (riquito/tuc 820 stars, cut replacement — IN CORPUS), `_rustybuzz` (harfbuzz/rustybuzz 662 stars — LIBRARY not CLI tool), `_figdriver-rs` (cmatsuoka 0 stars — narrow), `_uw8-tool` (sibling pico-args binary in microw8 repo — would be sister demo; HOLD for future round), `_wgpu` (gfx-rs/wgpu — Cargo xtask, not a user-facing CLI), `_rerun` (rerun-io/rerun — uses pico-args only in run-wasm helper, not main binary).  Probed alternative Rust CLI libraries: `_lexopt`-based tools (would be 10th Rust CLI library; lexopt is token-iterator-based with similar minimal-size goal but different API style; HOLD for future round), `_gumdrop`-based tools (predates argh, similar derive-based approach; HOLD).  Considered `_microw8-tool` for the alternative name but `uw8` is the canonical user-facing binary.  Chose uw8: 245 GH stars (MicroW8 WebAssembly-based size-coding fantasy console — popular in demoscene + size-coding communities), 6 subcommands + 8 flags for run + 2 flags for pack + 1 flag for compile + 2-3 positional args per subcommand + Arguments::from_env() ENTRY + args.subcommand() SUBCOMMAND-AS-STRING DISPATCH + args.contains() BOOLEAN-FLAG + args.opt_value_from_str() OPTIONAL-VALUE + args.opt_value_from_os_str() OPTIONAL-VALUE-WITH-CUSTOM-PARSER + args.free_from_os_str() NEXT-POSITIONAL-WITH-CUSTOM-PARSER + MANUAL HELP TEXT, full src/main.rs exercising EVERY MAJOR pico-args PATTERN, real production binary used by demoscene + size-coding developers globally.
  - Blacklist update: `uw8` added.  Corpus count: 28,769 → 28,770 (+1).

- **R346 — scala-monitor source-direct (Scala process monitor in Scala-Native; com-lihaoyi/mainargs library)** (1 file new) — pivots from BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345 metaxy) to a SECOND Scala CLI library — corpus's **first documented lihaoyi/mainargs library** + **second documented Scala CLI library** (joining R278 Scala decline).  scala-monitor is Polyvariant's CLI utility that DISCOVERS RUNNING Scala/JVM-related processes (sbt, bloop, metals, scalac, scala-cli, mill, coursier, scalafmt, scalafix, etc.) and DISPLAYS a formatted table with PID, type, memory usage (RSS/VSZ/SWAP), thread count, mem%, and project path.  Distributed as a Scala-Native compiled binary.  27 GH stars.  Sister tools: top (Unix), htop (in corpus), btop (in corpus), procs (in corpus), jps (Java JDK), jcmd (Java JDK).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented com-lihaoyi/mainargs library** + **second documented Scala CLI library** — Scala ecosystem now has 2 documented CLI libraries (R278 Scala decline + R346 Scala mainargs) joining the multi-library club.  mainargs is fundamentally different from decline: decline (R278) is IMMUTABLE FUNCTIONAL combinator-based using cats Applicative (`(opt1, opt2, opt3).mapN((a, b, c) => ...)`); mainargs (R346) is ANNOTATION-BASED on METHOD signatures (`@main def run(@arg(short = 'X') name: T = default)`).  Used by mill (Scala build tool, in corpus), ammonite (Scala scripting, in corpus), scala-cli scripts, geny, requests, os-lib, and many other Li Haoyi ecosystem tools.  Adds mainargs to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: mainargs uses Scala 2/3 REFLECTION via PROC-MACRO at compile time on METHOD SIGNATURES — parameter types become flag types, parameter defaults become flag defaults, `@arg(short = 'X', doc = "...")` per-parameter annotations attach metadata, MULTIPLE `@main`-annotated METHODS become SUBCOMMANDS named after the method.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (struct + proc-macro), R322 JCommander (field annotations + reflection), R340 oclif (class + static fields), R345 cyclopts (PEP 593 Annotated[]).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 85.
  - `_scala-monitor` (1-stem; polyvariant/scala-monitor ScalaMonitor.scala + com-lihaoyi/mainargs library; **com-lihaoyi/mainargs library** — `import mainargs.{main, arg, Flag}` brings mainargs primitives into scope; **6 flags via single `@main def run(...)` method (`-o/--output {full|pid}` + `-f/--filter <key=value>` repeatable + `-d/--debug` Flag-type-boolean + `-l/--debug-log <path>` Option[String] + `-w/--tui` Flag + `-D/--dumps-dir <path>` Option[String]) + `@main` ANNOTATION-AS-SUBCOMMAND on method + `@arg(short = 'X', doc = "...")` ANNOTATION-AS-PARAMETER-METADATA + Scala TYPE-INFERS flag type from PARAMETER TYPE (Boolean → flag, Flag → flag, Option[T] → optional, Seq[T] → repeatable, T with default → optional with default, T without default → required) + auto-derives KEBAB-CASE long names from camelCase parameter names + `ParserForMethods(this).runOrExit(args.toIndexedSeq)` ENTRY POINT**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` line 3 (`import mainargs.{main, arg, Flag}` — SCALA SELECTIVE IMPORT of mainargs primitives; mainargs is distributed via Maven Central as `com.lihaoyi:mainargs_2.13` / `com.lihaoyi:mainargs_3` + various Scala-Native and Scala-JS variants)
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` lines 19-45 (`@main\ndef run(\n  @arg(short = 'o', doc = "Output format: 'full' (table) or 'pid' (just PIDs)")\n  output: String = "full",\n  @arg(short = 'f', doc = "Filter processes by key=value (repeatable). ...")\n  filter: Seq[String] = Seq.empty,\n  @arg(short = 'd', doc = "Enable verbose debug logging to stderr")\n  debug: Flag = Flag(),\n  ...\n): Unit = ...` — `@main` annotation MARKS THIS METHOD AS THE CLI ENTRY POINT; `@arg(short = 'X', doc = "Y")` per-parameter annotation attaches CLI metadata; PARAMETER TYPES are reflected at compile time to determine flag semantics)
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` line 30 (`debug: Flag = Flag()` — mainargs's `Flag` TYPE for BOOLEAN FLAGS — distinct from `Boolean = false` which expects an actual value `--debug true` vs `Flag = Flag()` which accepts BARE `--debug` presence)
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` line 26 (`filter: Seq[String] = Seq.empty` — `Seq[T]` parameter type AUTO-DETECTED as REPEATABLE flag — each occurrence APPENDS to the seq)
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` line 32 + 36 (`debugLog: Option[String] = None` + `dumpsDir: Option[String] = None` — `Option[T]` parameter type AUTO-DETECTED as OPTIONAL flag with None default; CAMEL-CASE parameter name `debugLog`/`dumpsDir` AUTO-DERIVES KEBAB-CASE long flag `--debug-log`/`--dumps-dir`)
    * `ScalaMonitor.scala` line 61 (`def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = mainargs.ParserForMethods(this).runOrExit(args.toIndexedSeq)` — JVM/Scala-Native entry point delegating to mainargs's `ParserForMethods(this).runOrExit(args)` — `ParserForMethods` REFLECTS THE OBJECT'S `@main`-ANNOTATED METHODS at compile time via proc-macro to build the parser; `runOrExit` on success calls the matched method, on failure prints error + exits)
  - Critical extraction note: scala-monitor uses **com-lihaoyi/mainargs library** at ScalaMonitor.scala:3 — corpus's **first documented com-lihaoyi/mainargs library** + **second documented Scala CLI library** (joining R278 Scala decline).  mainargs is Li Haoyi's ANNOTATION-BASED Scala CLI library — used throughout the Li Haoyi ecosystem (mill, ammonite, scala-cli scripts, geny, requests, os-lib, cask, scalatags).  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS from R278 decline: decline is IMMUTABLE FUNCTIONAL combinator-based using cats Applicative — `(opt1, opt2, opt3).mapN((a, b, c) => action)` builds a parser by COMPOSING smaller parsers via the Applicative `.mapN` combinator; mainargs is ANNOTATION-BASED on METHOD signatures — `@main def run(@arg(short = 'X') name: T = default)` declares a CLI subcommand by ANNOTATING A METHOD with `@main` and PARAMETERS with `@arg`.  decline's FP style aligns with cats/scalaz ecosystems (Bleep, Tezos, etc.); mainargs's annotation style is more JAVA-LIKE/IMPERATIVE (Mill, Ammonite, etc.).  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (struct + proc-macro), R322 Java JCommander (field annotations + reflection), R340 oclif (class + static fields), R345 Python cyclopts (PEP 593 Annotated[]).  mainargs's approach: Scala 2/3 REFLECTION via PROC-MACRO at compile time on METHOD SIGNATURES + PARAMETER TYPES inferred to flag types (Boolean → flag, Flag → flag, Option[T] → optional, Seq[T] → repeatable, T with default → optional, T without default → required) + AUTO-DERIVED KEBAB-CASE LONG NAMES from camelCase parameter names + MULTIPLE `@main` METHODS BECOME SUBCOMMANDS.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-FIVE library variants: Rust (8), Go (6), Python (4), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), **Scala (2 — decline R278 + mainargs R346)**, Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts (R345), **Scala 2 lihaoyi/mainargs** (R346 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 85 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Scala NOW HAS TWO documented CLI libraries (decline + mainargs).
  - Critical extraction note: scala-monitor uses **mainargs @main-annotation-as-subcommand-on-method CLI pattern** at ScalaMonitor.scala:19-45 — `@main\ndef run(\n  @arg(short = 'o', doc = "...")\n  output: String = "full",\n  ...\n): Unit = ...`.  mainargs's `@main` ANNOTATION on a METHOD marks it as a CLI subcommand named after the method.  When MULTIPLE `@main` methods exist on the same object, they become MULTIPLE SUBCOMMANDS (the user types `binary <method-name> ...`).  When only ONE `@main` method exists (like scala-monitor's `run`), the SUBCOMMAND NAME is OPTIONAL — both `scala-monitor` and `scala-monitor run` work.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { Foo(FooArgs) }` (enum-of-variants for subcommands), R322 JCommander `parser.addCommand("verb", new VerbInstance())` (imperative registration), R340 oclif filesystem-hierarchy-walking (commands are files in src/commands/), R278 Scala decline `Opts.subcommand(cmd)` (Applicative composition of subcommand opts).  mainargs's approach: `@main` ANNOTATION ON METHOD makes the method a CLI subcommand named after the method name — corpus's **first documented @main-annotation-on-method-as-subcommand CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: scala-monitor uses **mainargs parameter-type-inference-to-flag-type CLI pattern** at ScalaMonitor.scala:24-37 — `output: String = "full"` (string with default) + `filter: Seq[String] = Seq.empty` (REPEATABLE) + `debug: Flag = Flag()` (boolean flag) + `debugLog: Option[String] = None` (OPTIONAL with None default) + `dumpsDir: Option[String] = None` (OPTIONAL).  mainargs INFERS the FLAG SEMANTICS from the PARAMETER TYPE in Scala signature: `Boolean` (with default) → boolean toggle requiring true/false, `Flag` (with `Flag()` default) → boolean toggle accepting bare flag presence, `Option[T]` → optional flag returning None if not given, `Seq[T]` → REPEATABLE flag accumulating into Seq, `T` with default value → optional with default, `T` without default → required.  Distinct from R278 decline (uses `Opts.option[T]("name", "help")` explicit type annotation), R275 clap-derive (similar: `Option<T>` → optional, `Vec<T>` → repeatable, `bool` → flag with `action = ArgAction::SetTrue`), R344 argh (similar: `Option<T>` → optional, `Vec<T>` → repeatable, `bool` with `#[argh(switch)]` → boolean), R345 cyclopts (PEP 593 Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(...)]).  mainargs's approach: PARAMETER TYPE INFERS flag type with `Flag` TYPE as SPECIAL bare-presence-only boolean (distinguishing from `Boolean = false` which would require explicit value) — corpus's **first documented Flag-type-vs-Boolean-distinction CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: scala-monitor uses **mainargs auto-derive-kebab-case-long-name-from-camelCase-parameter-name CLI pattern** at ScalaMonitor.scala:32-36 — `debugLog: Option[String] = None` becomes `--debug-log` and `dumpsDir: Option[String] = None` becomes `--dumps-dir`.  mainargs AUTO-DERIVES kebab-case long flag names from camelCase Scala parameter names — `oneTwoThree` becomes `--one-two-three`, `XML` becomes `--xml`, `ABCdef` becomes `--abcdef`.  Override via `@arg(name = "explicit-name")` if needed.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (similar: `field_name` → `--field-name`, but Rust convention is snake_case so derivation is `--snake-case` → `--kebab-case` whereas mainargs converts camelCase → kebab-case), R278 decline (NO auto-derive — caller specifies the long flag name explicitly via `Opts.option[T]("long-name", "help")`), R344 argh (similar: `field_name` → `--field-name`), R345 cyclopts (NO auto-derive — user explicitly specifies via Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(name=["-x", "--long"])]).  mainargs's approach: AUTO-DERIVE kebab-case long names from camelCase parameter names — corpus's **first documented camelCase-to-kebab-case-auto-derivation CLI pattern** (clap-derive auto-derives from snake_case which is the Rust convention, mainargs from camelCase which is the Scala convention).
  - Critical extraction note: scala-monitor uses **mainargs ParserForMethods + runOrExit entry-point CLI pattern** at ScalaMonitor.scala:61 — `def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = mainargs.ParserForMethods(this).runOrExit(args.toIndexedSeq)`.  mainargs's `ParserForMethods(obj)` is a PROC-MACRO that REFLECTS THE OBJECT'S `@main`-annotated methods AT COMPILE TIME to build the parser.  `runOrExit(args)` PARSES the args, INVOKES the matched method on success, prints error + `sys.exit(1)` on parse failure.  Sister methods: `runOrThrow(args)` (throws exception instead of exiting), `runRaw(args)` (returns `Result[T]` ADT for explicit handling).  Distinct from R278 decline `_.parse(args)` (returns `Either[Help, T]` for explicit handling), R275 clap-derive `Cli::parse()` (panics or auto-exits on error), R340 oclif `await this.parse(Class)` (in-handler async parse), R345 cyclopts `app()` (callable App object).  mainargs's approach: `ParserForMethods(this).runOrExit(args)` with `runOrExit` (auto-exit) vs `runOrThrow` (throw) vs `runRaw` (Result ADT) variants — corpus's **first documented runOrExit-vs-runOrThrow-vs-runRaw-variants CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_LISA` (epfl-lara/lisa 69 stars — too narrow 2-subcommand surface for TPTP_Lisa runner), `_scala-repl-pp` (mpollmeier 26 stars — uses SCOPT not mainargs in main binary, mainargs only used for SCRIPT WRAPPING), `_bleep` (oyvindberg 178 stars — uses DECLINE not mainargs, would have been a R278 sister demo), `_portals` (portals-project 20 stars — narrow Scala dataflow framework), `_foundry-cli` (foundry-sdk/cli — narrow SDK CLI).  Probed alternative Scala CLI libraries: `_scopt`-based tools (would be 3rd Scala CLI library — scopt is the OG Scala CLI; HOLD), `_decline-effect`-based tools (extension of decline for cats-effect IO; HOLD).  Considered Mill and Ammonite (canonical mainargs consumers — both IN CORPUS already with completions from their own distributed shell scripts).  Chose scala-monitor: 27 GH stars (real Polyvariant tool that monitors Scala/JVM processes — useful for Scala developers running multiple sbt/bloop/metals/scala-cli processes), 6 flags via single `@main def run` method, full ScalaMonitor.scala exercising EVERY MAJOR mainargs PATTERN (@main annotation on method + @arg per-parameter annotation + Flag type for boolean + Seq[T] for repeatable + Option[T] for optional + auto-derive kebab-case long names + ParserForMethods.runOrExit), real Scala-Native compiled binary distributed via Coursier's `cs install --contrib scala-monitor` + GitHub Releases.
  - Blacklist update: `scala-monitor` added.  Corpus count: 28,768 → 28,769 (+1).

- **R345 — metaxy source-direct (sample-level metadata versioning for incremental multimodal pipelines in Python; BrianPugh/cyclopts library)** (1 file new) — pivots from google/argh (R344 calyx) to a FOURTH Python CLI library — corpus's **first documented BrianPugh/cyclopts library** + **fourth documented Python CLI library**.  metaxy is Anam Healthcare's PLUGGABLE METADATA VERSIONING system for incremental MULTIMODAL ML pipelines — tracks per-sample feature versions, dependencies, and projects.  Used in data engineering and ML feature engineering for ensuring reproducible pipelines.  106 GH stars.  Sister tools: dvc (DataVersionControl, in corpus), lakeFS (in corpus), pachyderm (in corpus), mlflow (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented BrianPugh/cyclopts library** + **fourth documented Python CLI library** — Python ecosystem now has FOUR documented CLI libraries (argparse + click + fire + cyclopts).  cyclopts is BrianPugh's MODERN Python 3.10+ CLI framework — distinct from Typer in that it FULLY supports Union types, Literal types, Pydantic models, dataclasses, attrs, and uses DOCSTRINGS (not function signatures) for help text generation.  cyclopts is "what Typer's design SHOULD have been" — Typer is the type-hint pioneer (R-?), cyclopts is the type-hint perfectionist.  1170 GH stars on BrianPugh/cyclopts (requires Python 3.10+).  Adds cyclopts to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: cyclopts uses DECORATOR-BASED API (`@app.command`, `@app.default`, `@app.meta.default` for global-launcher), `cyclopts.App(name, version, config=cyclopts.config.Env("PREFIX_"))` constructor with ENV-VAR-PREFIX CONFIG, `Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(name=[...], help="...", show=False)]` for per-parameter metadata (uses Python's PEP 593 `typing.Annotated`), DOCSTRING-AS-HELP-TEXT for command help, LAZY-LOADED SUBCOMMAND DISPATCH via `app.command("module.path:app", name="X")` (import strings rather than direct registration), `app.meta.default` for GLOBAL LAUNCHER that cross-cuts all subcommands, built-in INTERACTIVE SHELL via `app.interactive_shell()`.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 84.
  - `_metaxy` (1-stem; anam-org/metaxy src/metaxy/cli/app.py + src/metaxy/cli/{config,describe,graph,history,graph_diff,list,metadata,mcp,push,lock}.py; **BrianPugh/cyclopts library** — `import cyclopts` brings the cyclopts namespace into scope; **5 global flags + 11 top-level subcommands (`shell` + `config` + `describe` + `graph` + `graph-diff` + `history` + `list` + `metadata` + `mcp` + `push` + `lock`) + nested sub-subcommands per group + cyclopts.App() constructor + cyclopts.config.Env("METAXY_") env-var-prefix config + @app.command DECORATOR for subcommand registration + @app.command(name="features") explicit-naming + @app.command(name="feature", show=False) HIDDEN-ALIAS + @app.meta.default GLOBAL-LAUNCHER decorator + Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(name=[...], help="...", group="...")] PEP 593-style param metadata + Annotated[bool, cyclopts.Parameter(name=["-v", "--verbose"], help="...")] = False per-parameter declaration + DOCSTRING-AS-HELP-TEXT + LAZY-LOADED SUBCOMMAND DISPATCH via `app.command("module.path:app", name="X")` import-string + `*tokens: Annotated[str, cyclopts.Parameter(show=False, allow_leading_hyphen=True)]` variadic + dual binary entry points (`metaxy` + `mx`)**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/metaxy/cli/app.py` line 6 (`import cyclopts` — Python IMPORT of cyclopts; distributed via PyPI as `cyclopts` package, requires Python 3.10+)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/app.py` lines 12-22 (`app = cyclopts.App( name="metaxy", version=__version__, console=console, error_console=error_console, config=cyclopts.config.Env( "METAXY_", ... ), help_epilogue="Learn more in [Metaxy docs](https://docs.metaxy.io)" )` — cyclopts.App CONSTRUCTOR with NAMED-ARG configuration: `name` + `version` + `console`/`error_console` for Rich-based output customization + `config=cyclopts.config.Env("METAXY_")` ESTABLISHES env-var-prefix fallback so EVERY CLI flag can also be set via `METAXY_<flag>` env var + `help_epilogue` for help-page footer)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/app.py` lines 25-28 (`@app.command\ndef shell(): """Start interactive shell.""" app.interactive_shell()` — DECORATOR-based subcommand registration; cyclopts uses Python `__doc__` STRING from the function for help text)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/app.py` lines 32-67 (`@app.meta.default\ndef launcher( *tokens: Annotated[str, cyclopts.Parameter(show=False, allow_leading_hyphen=True)], config_file: Annotated[ Path | None, cyclopts.Parameter( None, help="Path to the Metaxy configuration file. Defaults to auto-discovery.", group="Global" ) ] = None, project: Annotated[ str | None, cyclopts.Parameter( None, help="...", group="Global" ) ] = None, all_projects: Annotated[ bool, cyclopts.Parameter( name=["--all-projects"], help="...", group="Global" ) ] = False, sync: ..., locked: ... ): ...` — `@app.meta.default` registers a GLOBAL LAUNCHER that cross-cuts ALL subcommands; `*tokens` is variadic captures the actual subcommand + its args; cross-cutting global flags are KEYWORD-only with PEP 593 `Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(...)]` metadata; `group="Global"` groups them under a "Global" heading in help output)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/app.py` lines 102-111 (`app.command("metaxy.cli.config:app", name="config"); app.command("metaxy.cli.describe:app", name="describe"); app.command("metaxy.cli.graph:app", name="graph"); app.command("metaxy.cli.history:app", name="history"); app.command("metaxy.cli.graph_diff:app", name="graph-diff"); app.command("metaxy.cli.list:app", name="list"); app.command("metaxy.cli.metadata:app", name="metadata"); app.command("metaxy.cli.mcp:app", name="mcp"); app.command("metaxy.cli.push:app", name="push"); app.command("metaxy.cli.lock:app", name="lock")` — LAZY-LOADED SUBCOMMAND REGISTRATION via IMPORT STRING `"module.path:app"`; cyclopts DEFERS import of the subcommand module UNTIL that subcommand is actually invoked — speeds up CLI startup for tools with many subcommands)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/list.py` lines 18-23 (`app = cyclopts.App( name="list", help="List Metaxy entities", console=console, error_console=error_console )` — SUB-APP for the `list` subcommand group; nested cyclopts.App with its own commands)
    * `src/metaxy/cli/list.py` lines 26-50 (`@app.command(name="features")\n@app.command(name="feature", show=False)\ndef features( *, verbose: Annotated[ bool, cyclopts.Parameter( name=["-v", "--verbose"], help="Show detailed information including field dependencies and versions." ) ] = False, format: Annotated[ OutputFormat, cyclopts.Parameter( name=["-f", "--format"], help="Output format: 'plain' (default) or 'json'." ) ] = "plain" ) -> None: """List Metaxy features in the current project. Examples: ..."""` — DOUBLE-DECORATOR PATTERN for EXPLICIT-NAME plus HIDDEN-ALIAS (the `feature` alias is registered with `show=False` so it doesn't appear in help but works as user-typed input))
  - Critical extraction note: metaxy uses **BrianPugh/cyclopts library** at app.py:6 — corpus's **first documented BrianPugh/cyclopts library** + **fourth documented Python CLI library**.  cyclopts is BrianPugh's MODERN Python 3.10+ CLI framework — competing with Typer.  KEY DIFFERENTIATORS: (1) FULL SUPPORT for Python type hints including UNIONS (`int | str`), LITERALS (`Literal["dev", "prod"]`), Pydantic models, dataclasses, attrs — Typer has historical issues with these; (2) DOCSTRINGS (not function-signature heuristics) for help text — cyclopts reads `__doc__` of each command function; (3) cyclopts.App AS FIRST-CLASS OBJECT (with optional sub-apps for nested subcommand groups via `app.command("module.path:app", name="X")` lazy-loaded import strings); (4) `@app.meta.default` cross-cutting GLOBAL LAUNCHER pattern (allows global flags that apply to ALL subcommands without repeating them); (5) `cyclopts.config.Env("PREFIX_")` ESTABLISHES env-var-prefix fallback so every CLI flag can ALSO be set via env var with the prefix; (6) `Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(...)]` per-parameter metadata using PEP 593's typing.Annotated (newer than Typer's `typer.Option(...)` default-value approach).  Distinct from R-? Python typer (older, simpler type-hint API, less complete Union/Literal support), R-? Python click (commander.js-inspired decorator API, no type hints), R-? Python argparse (procedural builder pattern), R-? Python fire (auto-CLI-from-object).  cyclopts's approach: PEP 593 Annotated[...] metadata + LAZY-LOADED subcommand dispatch via import string + `@app.meta.default` global launcher + env-var-prefix config + docstring-as-help-text.  1170 GH stars on BrianPugh/cyclopts (requires Python 3.10+).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-FOUR library variants: Rust (8), Go (6), **Python (4 — argparse + click + fire + cyclopts)**, Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), Rust 8 google/argh (R344), **Python 4 BrianPugh/cyclopts** (R345 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 84 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Python NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries (argparse + click + fire + cyclopts).
  - Critical extraction note: metaxy uses **cyclopts PEP-593-Annotated-with-cyclopts.Parameter-metadata CLI pattern** at list.py:26-50 + app.py:32-67 — `Annotated[bool, cyclopts.Parameter(name=["-v", "--verbose"], help="Show detailed information including field dependencies and versions.")] = False`.  cyclopts uses PYTHON PEP 593's `typing.Annotated[T, METADATA...]` to attach per-parameter CLI metadata to function parameters.  The first type-argument is the FIELD TYPE (`bool` here), and subsequent metadata args are CONSUMED by cyclopts at function-introspection time.  `cyclopts.Parameter(name=[...], help="...", group="...", show=False, allow_leading_hyphen=True)` is the CONFIG OBJECT for per-parameter metadata.  Distinct from R-? Python typer `arg: int = typer.Argument(...)` (uses DEFAULT VALUE as metadata holder — combines parameter declaration with metadata), R-? Python click `@click.option("--name")` (decorator on the function ITSELF), R-? Python argparse `parser.add_argument("--name", type=int, help="...")` (procedural builder on parser object), R275 Rust clap-derive `#[arg(...)]` (Rust attribute proc-macro).  cyclopts's approach: PEP 593 `Annotated[T, cyclopts.Parameter(...)]` per-parameter metadata — corpus's **first documented PEP-593-Annotated-with-Parameter-metadata CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: metaxy uses **cyclopts lazy-loaded subcommand dispatch via import-string CLI pattern** at app.py:102-111 — `app.command("metaxy.cli.config:app", name="config")` + 9 similar lines.  cyclopts's `app.command(STRING)` accepts an IMPORT STRING `"module.path:attribute"` and DEFERS IMPORTING the subcommand module UNTIL THAT SUBCOMMAND IS INVOKED.  This is a SIGNIFICANT STARTUP-PERFORMANCE OPTIMIZATION for CLIs with many subcommand modules: only the module actually needed for the user's invocation gets imported, saving 100s of ms in import time for CLIs with deep subcommand hierarchies.  Distinct from R-? Python typer `app.add_typer(other_app, name="X")` (eager import of subapp at registration time), R-? Python click `cli.add_command(other_cli)` (eager import), R340 oclif filesystem-hierarchy-walking (oclif lazy-loads via filesystem discovery but still imports each command's MODULE at first invocation), R341 yargs `subYargs => addModuleCommands(subYargs, ...)` (eager import via closure).  cyclopts's approach: LAZY-LOADED SUBCOMMAND DISPATCH via IMPORT STRING `"module.path:attribute"` — corpus's **first documented lazy-loaded-subcommand-dispatch-via-import-string CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: metaxy uses **cyclopts @app.meta.default cross-cutting global-launcher CLI pattern** at app.py:30-67 — `@app.meta.default\ndef launcher( *tokens: Annotated[str, cyclopts.Parameter(show=False, allow_leading_hyphen=True)], config_file: ..., project: ..., all_projects: ..., sync: ..., locked: ... )`.  `@app.meta.default` is cyclopts's MECHANISM for a GLOBAL LAUNCHER FUNCTION that cross-cuts ALL subcommands — receives ALL the cross-cutting global flags as KEYWORD ARGUMENTS plus the actual subcommand+args as `*tokens` variadic.  After processing global state, the launcher re-invokes `app(tokens)` to dispatch the actual subcommand.  This separates GLOBAL FLAG HANDLING from per-subcommand logic without REPEATING the global flags on every subcommand.  Distinct from R-? Python typer `@app.callback()` (similar concept but uses CALLBACK pattern; less clean separation), R-? Python click `@click.pass_context` (uses context-passing rather than launcher), R341 yargs `.option(name, {global: true})` (per-flag global declaration), R340 oclif (uses base CLASS with shared flags via inheritance).  cyclopts's approach: `@app.meta.default` launcher decorator that receives global flags as keyword args + remaining tokens as variadic — corpus's **first documented @app.meta.default-global-launcher CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: metaxy uses **cyclopts double-decorator-for-explicit-name-plus-hidden-alias CLI pattern** at list.py:26-27 — `@app.command(name="features")\n@app.command(name="feature", show=False)\ndef features(...): ...`.  cyclopts allows STACKED `@app.command(...)` decorators on the same function — each registers an alternate NAME for the command.  `name="features"` registers the canonical (plural) name shown in help; `name="feature", show=False` registers `feature` as a HIDDEN ALIAS that works as user-typed input but doesn't appear in the help-text command list.  Distinct from R-? Python typer (uses `aliases=[...]` argument to typer's command decorator), R-? Python click `@click.command()` `@click.alias("alias-name")` (separate alias decorator), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(alias = "X")]` (attribute argument).  cyclopts's approach: STACKED DECORATORS with `show=False` for hidden aliases — corpus's **first documented stacked-decorators-with-show=False-for-hidden-alias CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_fastmcp` (PrefectHQ/fastmcp 25413 stars — IN CORPUS; also uses cyclopts but already documented under a different framework attribution), `_mx` (DUP — `mx` blacklisted already; metaxy installs both `metaxy` and `mx` binaries but only documenting under canonical `metaxy` name), `_mopidy-spotify` (mopidy/mopidy-spotify 976 stars — Mopidy extension not standalone binary), `_candies` (astrogewgaw/candies 5 stars — narrow astronomy), `_y2` (mangoumbrella/y2 0 stars — too narrow), `_xlranker` (bzhanglab 1 star — narrow protein-protein interaction tool), `_lsp-skill` (lsp-client/lsp-skill — narrow LSP).  Probed alternative Python CLI libraries: `_typer`-based tools (would be 5th Python CLI library; many in corpus including aider, marimo, memray, prefect, sgpt — but most popular tools using typer already documented in corpus), `_docopt`-based tools (would be 5th or 6th Python CLI library — docopt is fundamentally different, parses CLI surface FROM A DOCSTRING; HOLD), `_plac`-based tools (would be 6th Python CLI library; obscure but unique; HOLD).  Considered alternative cyclopts users: PrefectHQ/fastmcp (IN CORPUS but cyclopts-using — could UPGRADE existing _fastmcp docs).  Chose metaxy: 106 GH stars (real production metadata versioning tool from Anam Healthcare for incremental multimodal ML pipelines), 5 global flags + 11 top-level subcommands (`shell` + `config` + `describe` + `graph` + `graph-diff` + `history` + `list` + `metadata` + `mcp` + `push` + `lock`) + nested sub-subcommands per group + cyclopts.App() + cyclopts.config.Env() + @app.command DECORATOR + @app.command(name="X", show=False) HIDDEN-ALIAS + @app.meta.default GLOBAL-LAUNCHER + PEP 593-style param metadata + DOCSTRING-AS-HELP-TEXT + LAZY-LOADED IMPORT-STRING DISPATCH + variadic `*tokens` for catch-all, full src/metaxy/cli/*.py exercising EVERY MAJOR cyclopts PATTERN, real production tool maintained by Anam Healthcare.
  - Blacklist update: `metaxy` added.  Corpus count: 28,767 → 28,768 (+1).

- **R344 — calyx source-direct (Calyx Intermediate Language compiler for hardware accelerator generators in Rust; google/argh library)** (1 file new) — pivots from tj/commander.js (R343 mongoku) to an EIGHTH Rust CLI library — corpus's **first documented google/argh library** + **eighth documented Rust CLI library**.  calyx is the COMPILER for the Calyx Intermediate Language (IL) — sits BETWEEN high-level languages (Dahlia, Filament) and HARDWARE DESCRIPTION LANGUAGES (Verilog, FIRRTL, MLIR).  Originally from Cornell Capra group.  Backends emit Verilog (default), FIRRTL, MLIR, Xilinx XML/SV interface code, resource usage reports, and (with `serialize` feature) sexp dumps.  597 GH stars.  Sister tools: chisel (Scala HDL, in corpus), spinalhdl (Scala HDL), pyrtl (Python HDL).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented google/argh library** + **eighth documented Rust CLI library** — Rust ecosystem now has EIGHT documented CLI libraries (clap-derive R275, structopt-pre-clap-merger, gumdrop, getopts, pico-args, lexopt, bpaf R330, plus historical entries) — EXTENDING Rust's lead as MOST-DOCUMENTED CLI-language with 8 distinct libraries.  Adds google/argh (Google's argh) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: argh is OPTIMIZED FOR CODE SIZE Rust CLI parser using `#[derive(FromArgs)]` proc-macro on a struct + `#[argh(...)]` attribute DSL on fields + DOC COMMENTS as help text + enum-variants-with-#[argh(subcommand)] for subcommand dispatch.  Originally developed at Google for Fuchsia OS components where each binary BUNDLES its own parser and code-size matters.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (full-featured, larger compiled size), R330 Rust bpaf (functional combinator-based).  argh has SMALLER FEATURE SET: no env var fallback, no value enum derive macros, no conditional groups — but ~3x smaller compiled binary than clap-derive for equivalent specs.  1916 GH stars on google/argh.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 83.
  - `_calyx` (1-stem; calyxir/calyx src/cmdline.rs; **google/argh library** — `use argh::FromArgs` brings argh's derive macro into scope; **2 subcommands (`pass-help` + `format`) + 14 top-level options/flags (`-o`/`-l`/`-m`/`-b`/`-p`/`-d`/`-x`/`-i`/`--log`/`--synthesis`/`--disable-verify`/`--nested`/`--emit-primitive-extmodules`/`--dump-ir`/`--version`) + positional input file (.futil) + `#[derive(FromArgs)]` PROC-MACRO on struct + `#[argh(...)]` ATTRIBUTE DSL with sub-directives (`subcommand`, `positional`, `option`, `switch`, `short = 'X'`, `long = "X"`, `default = "EXPR"`, `from_str_fn(parser)`) + DOC COMMENT-AS-HELP-TEXT convention (Rust `///` doc comments BECOME the auto-generated help text) + ENUM-VARIANTS-AS-SUBCOMMANDS via `#[argh(subcommand)]` on enum + `Vec<T>` field type for REPEATABLE option/positional**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/cmdline.rs` line 2 (`use argh::FromArgs` — RUST USE statement bringing argh's `FromArgs` derive macro into scope; argh is distributed via crates.io with NO peer dependencies)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 16-23 (`/// help information about passes\n#[derive(FromArgs, PartialEq, Debug)]\n#[argh(subcommand, name = "pass-help")]\npub struct Help { /// alias or pass name to get help for\n #[argh(positional)] pub name: Option<String>, }` — SUBCOMMAND STRUCT declared via `#[argh(subcommand, name = "pass-help")]`; `name = "..."` overrides the AUTO-DERIVED kebab-case name; `#[argh(positional)]` on field declares POSITIONAL argument; `Option<String>` field type makes positional OPTIONAL)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 33-41 (`/// supported subcommands\n#[derive(FromArgs, PartialEq, Debug)]\n#[argh(subcommand)]\npub enum Subcommand { /// Help mode\n Help(Help), /// Format\n Format(Format) }` — ENUM-VARIANTS-AS-SUBCOMMANDS pattern: `#[argh(subcommand)]` on an ENUM where each variant WRAPS a tuple-struct that itself derives `FromArgs` with `#[argh(subcommand, name = "verb")]`; argh dispatches via PRESENCE-OF-VARIANT)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 43-48 (`#[derive(FromArgs)]\n/// Options passed to the Calyx compiler.\npub struct Opts { #[argh(subcommand)] pub sub: Option<Subcommand>, /// input calyx program\n #[argh(positional, from_str_fn(read_path))] pub file: Option<PathBuf>, ... }` — TOP-LEVEL Opts struct; `#[argh(subcommand)]` on field of `Option<EnumType>` MAKES the enum-typed-field a SUBCOMMAND HOLDER; `from_str_fn(read_path)` registers a CUSTOM PARSER function for the value)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 50-55 (`/// output file\n#[argh(option, short = 'o', default = "OutputFile::Stdout")] pub output: OutputFile` — `#[argh(option, short = 'o', default = "EXPR")]` declares a value-taking flag with short alias `-o` and DEFAULT EVALUATED AS RUST EXPRESSION; default expression is `OutputFile::Stdout` parsed at parse-time)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 57-63 (`/// path to the primitives library\n#[argh(option, short = 'l', default = "vec![option_env!(\"CALYX_PRIMITIVES_DIR\").unwrap_or(\".\").into()]")] pub lib_path: Vec<PathBuf>` — `Vec<PathBuf>` field type AUTO-DETECTS as REPEATABLE option; `option_env!` is RUST'S BUILD-TIME ENV VAR MACRO — argh's `default` expr is evaluated at compile time, allowing this hybrid build-time/runtime env behavior)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 90-93 (`/// disable verification checks emitted by backends\n#[argh(switch)] pub disable_verify: bool` — `#[argh(switch)]` declares BOOLEAN FLAG; field type MUST be `bool`; field NAME auto-derives kebab-case long flag `--disable-verify`)
    * `src/cmdline.rs` lines 109-110 (`/// run this pass during execution\n#[argh(option, short = 'p')] pub pass: Vec<String>` — Vec<String> + `#[argh(option, short = 'p')]` MAKES `-p` REPEATABLE; each `-p X` appends X to the vec)
  - Critical extraction note: calyx uses **google/argh library** at cmdline.rs:2 — corpus's **first documented google/argh library** + **eighth documented Rust CLI library**.  google/argh is Google's Rust CLI parser OPTIMIZED FOR CODE SIZE — originally developed at Google for Fuchsia OS components where each binary BUNDLES its own parser and final binary size matters.  argh produces ~3x smaller compiled binaries than clap-derive for equivalent specs (no help-text formatter included unless explicitly used, no env var fallback, no derive-macros for value enums, no conditional groups, no `clap_complete`-style shell-completion generation).  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (full-featured, larger compiled size, supports env vars, value enum derive macros, conditional groups, shell completion generation), R330 Rust bpaf (FUNCTIONAL COMBINATOR-based; explicit `pos::<T>().help("X").map(|x| ...)` and `long("flag").help("X").switch()` etc.), gumdrop (old derive-based, predates argh; less actively maintained), pico-args (no derive, just `pico_args::Arguments::from_env()` + manual `args.opt_value_from_str("--name").unwrap()` calls), lexopt (token-iterator-based, no derive).  argh's approach: SMALLER-FOOTPRINT `#[derive(FromArgs)]` proc-macro + `#[argh(...)]` attribute DSL + DOC COMMENTS as help text + ENUM-VARIANTS-AS-SUBCOMMANDS + `Vec<T>` AUTO-DETECTS REPEATABLE.  1916 GH stars on google/argh.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-THREE library variants: **Rust (8 — clap-derive R275 + bpaf R330 + plus historical entries including older clap/structopt + gumdrop + pico-args + lexopt + argh R344)**, Go (6), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js (R343), **Rust 8 google/argh** (R344 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 83 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Rust NOW HAS EIGHT documented CLI libraries — EXTENDING Rust's lead as MOST-DOCUMENTED CLI-language.
  - Critical extraction note: calyx uses **argh enum-variants-as-subcommands via #[argh(subcommand)] on ENUM CLI pattern** at cmdline.rs:33-41 — `/// supported subcommands\n#[derive(FromArgs, PartialEq, Debug)]\n#[argh(subcommand)]\npub enum Subcommand { /// Help mode\n Help(Help), /// Format\n Format(Format) }` + each variant's wrapped struct `struct Help { ... }` has its own `#[argh(subcommand, name = "pass-help")] FromArgs derive`.  argh's PATTERN: declare an ENUM with `#[argh(subcommand)]` where each variant WRAPS a TUPLE-STRUCT that itself derives FromArgs and SETS `#[argh(subcommand, name = "verb")]` on the struct.  Parent struct holds the enum in an `Option<EnumType>` field also annotated `#[argh(subcommand)]`.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { Foo(FooArgs), Bar(BarArgs) }` (similar but without the parent-struct-level subcommand annotation duplication), R330 bpaf `command("foo", parser)` (combinator), R342 go-arg `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag on pointer-to-struct field (Go's reflection-based approach).  argh's approach: ENUM-WITH-#[argh(subcommand)] + EACH-VARIANT-WRAPS-A-FromArgs-DERIVING-STRUCT — corpus's **first documented enum-variant-with-#[argh(subcommand)]-on-both-enum-and-wrapped-struct CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: calyx uses **argh doc-comment-as-help-text CLI pattern** at cmdline.rs:17-19 + 50-51 — `/// help information about passes\n#[derive(FromArgs, ...)]\n#[argh(subcommand, name = "pass-help")]\npub struct Help { ... }` + `/// output file\n#[argh(option, short = 'o', default = "OutputFile::Stdout")] pub output: OutputFile`.  Rust DOC COMMENTS (`///`) on a STRUCT/ENUM/FIELD ARE PICKED UP BY argh AT compile time and used as the AUTO-GENERATED HELP TEXT.  No separate `help = "..."` attribute — the help text COMES FROM the documentation itself.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (clap-derive ALSO uses doc comments as help BY DEFAULT, but explicit `help = "..."` attribute is preferred for non-doc help; argh is doc-comment-ONLY for help), R339 OCaml rbardou/Clap (`~description:"..."` keyword), R338 Odin core:flags (`usage:"..."` separate tag).  argh's approach: DOC COMMENT (`///`) AS THE ONLY HELP-TEXT SOURCE — corpus's **first documented doc-comment-only-as-help-text CLI pattern** (clap-derive supports both but doesn't enforce; argh enforces).
  - Critical extraction note: calyx uses **argh Vec<T> auto-detect-as-repeatable CLI pattern** at cmdline.rs:60 + 110 + 113 + 116 + 119 — `pub lib_path: Vec<PathBuf>` + `pub pass: Vec<String>` + `pub disable_pass: Vec<String>` + `pub extra_opts: Vec<String>` + `pub insertions: Vec<String>`.  argh AUTO-DETECTS `Vec<T>` field type as a REPEATABLE option (or REPEATABLE POSITIONAL if `#[argh(positional)]` is also present) — each occurrence of the flag on the command line APPENDS to the vec.  No separate `#[argh(repeatable)]` or `#[argh(multiple)]` directive — the FIELD TYPE alone communicates repeatability.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (also uses `Vec<T>` for repeatable but ALSO supports `#[arg(action = ArgAction::Append)]` for non-Vec types), R342 alexflint/go-arg (uses `arg:"separate"` directive explicitly + `[]T` slice field), R338 Odin core:flags (uses `manifold=N` for multi-value), R322 Java JCommander (uses `arity = N` + `List<String>` field type).  argh's approach: `Vec<T>` FIELD TYPE alone implies REPEATABLE — corpus's **first documented Vec<T>-alone-implies-repeatable CLI pattern** (clap-derive does same but with more configurability).
  - Critical extraction note: calyx uses **argh default-expression-evaluated-as-Rust-code CLI pattern** at cmdline.rs:53 + 60 — `#[argh(option, short = 'o', default = "OutputFile::Stdout")] pub output: OutputFile` + `#[argh(option, short = 'l', default = "vec![option_env!(\"CALYX_PRIMITIVES_DIR\").unwrap_or(\".\").into()]")] pub lib_path: Vec<PathBuf>`.  argh's `default = "EXPR"` attribute evaluates EXPR AS RUST CODE at PARSE TIME — supports any valid Rust expression, including `vec![...]` literals, `option_env!("VAR")` build-time env-var lookup, custom-type constructors like `OutputFile::Stdout`, etc.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(default_value_t = expr)]` (similar but uses `default_value_t` for typed defaults vs `default_value` for string defaults that get parsed), R322 JCommander `defaultValueExpression = "..."`, R342 alexflint/go-arg `default:"-1"` (string-only — parsed by `strconv`-like dispatcher), R338 Odin core:flags (no explicit default, field zero-value is the default).  argh's approach: `default = "EXPR"` ATTRIBUTE evaluates EXPR AS RUST CODE AT PARSE TIME — corpus's **first documented default-expression-evaluated-as-Rust-code CLI pattern** (clap-derive `default_value_t` is similar).
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_termscp` (veeso/termscp 2962 stars — IN CORPUS), `_bin` (w4/bin 668 stars — DUP, generic name `bin` clashes), `_sunwalker-box` (purplesyringa/sunwalker-box 30 stars — narrow sandbox), `_broxus-util` (broxus/broxus-util — narrow utility lib).  Probed alternative Rust CLI libraries: `_pico-args` users (would be 9th Rust CLI library; pico-args is a NO-DERIVE manual API for absolute-minimum binary size; HOLD), `_lexopt` users (would be 10th Rust CLI library; token-iterator-based; HOLD), `_gumdrop` users (predates argh; HOLD).  Considered alternative `_calyx-py` (the python frontend — but that's a different binary using Python CLI lib).  Probed `_swift-argument-parser` (Apple's, 3717 stars — already R283 Swift entry).  Chose calyx: 597 GH stars (real research+production compiler for hardware-accelerator IL from Cornell Capra group), 2 subcommands (`pass-help` + `format`) + 14 top-level options/flags + positional input file + ENUM-VARIANTS-AS-SUBCOMMANDS pattern + DOC-COMMENT-AS-HELP-TEXT + Vec<T>-AUTO-DETECT-REPEATABLE + default-expression-EVALUATED-AS-RUST-CODE + CUSTOM-PARSER via `from_str_fn`, full src/cmdline.rs exercising EVERY MAJOR argh PATTERN (FromArgs derive + argh attribute DSL + sub-directives + doc-comment help + enum subcommands + Vec repeatability + default Rust expression), real production binary distributed via cargo + nix + brew + linux packages.
  - Blacklist update: `calyx` added.  Corpus count: 28,766 → 28,767 (+1).

- **R343 — mongoku source-direct (Web-scale GUI for MongoDB by Hugging Face in TypeScript; tj/commander.js library)** (1 file new) — pivots from alexflint/go-arg (R342 mgmt) to a THIRD TypeScript/Node CLI library — corpus's **first documented tj/commander.js library** + **third documented TypeScript/Node CLI library**.  mongoku is Hugging Face's web-scale MongoDB GUI — connects to MongoDB clusters and exposes collections via a BROWSER-BASED UI (Express server on port 3100 by default).  1411 GH stars.  Sister tools: mongo-express (in corpus), nosqlclient, Studio 3T, MongoDB Compass.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented tj/commander.js library** + **third documented TypeScript/Node CLI library** — Node ecosystem now has THREE documented CLI libraries (R340 oclif + R341 yargs + R343 commander.js), joining the multi-library leaders.  commander.js is TJ Holowaychuk's OG Node CLI library — the model from which Python's Click (R-?, in taxonomy) and many others draw inspiration; ~26K GH stars on tj/commander.js, used by `vue-cli`, `create-react-app`, `prettier`, `eslint`, `lerna`, `jest`, etc.  Adds commander.js to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: commander.js is EXPLICIT-INSTANCE IMPERATIVE BUILDER with REQUIRED FINAL `program.parse(process.argv)` call (DISTINCT from yargs's TERMINAL `.argv` ACCESSOR + oclif's `await this.parse(Class)` IN-HANDLER PARSE).  API: `const program = new Command()` instantiate root + `.name(...)` / `.description(...)` / `.version(...)` metadata chain + `.command("verb")` register subcommand returning sub-program + `.option("--flag", "desc")` / `.option("-p, --port <port>", "desc", "default")` flag declaration with PARENTHESIZED-ARG-NAME `<port>` for value flags + `.allowExcessArguments(false)` strict mode + `.action(handler)` register handler + `program.option(...)` for ROOT default-action options.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 82.
  - `_mongoku` (1-stem; huggingface/Mongoku cli.ts; **TJ Holowaychuk's tj/commander.js library** — `import { Command } from "commander"` brings commander.js into scope; **3 commands (`start` default + `stop` + `help`) + 3 start-action flags (`--pm2` + `-p/--port <port>` + `--readonly`) + auto-added `-h/--help` and `-V/--version` + ROOT default-action options + ENV-VAR fallback for port + IMPERATIVE BUILDER API + EXPLICIT-INSTANCE `new Command()` + CHAINED metadata setters + `.command(verb).description(d).option(...).action(fn)` per-subcommand chain + `.allowExcessArguments(false)` strict mode + `.configureOutput({outputError: fn})` custom error handler + REQUIRED FINAL `program.parse(process.argv)` call**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `cli.ts` line 4 (`import { Command } from "commander"` — TYPESCRIPT/ESM IMPORT of commander.js; distributed via npm as `commander` package, no peer dependencies)
    * `cli.ts` line 19 (`const program = new Command()` — EXPLICIT INSTANCE creation; distinct from yargs's MODULE-LEVEL SINGLETON `import yargs from "yargs"`; multiple Command instances can co-exist in a single Node process)
    * `cli.ts` line 40 (`program.name("mongoku").description("MongoDB client for the web").version(version)` — CHAINED METADATA SETTERS; `.name` sets the program name for help output, `.description` sets the description line, `.version` adds the auto-handled `-V/--version` flag)
    * `cli.ts` lines 42-54 (`program.configureOutput({outputError: (str, write) => { if (str.includes("too many arguments")) { ... write(chalk.red("❌ Error: ...")); return } write(chalk.red(str)) } })` — `.configureOutput` accepts a CUSTOM ERROR HANDLER that wraps commander's built-in error messages with chalk coloring + custom error-detection logic)
    * `cli.ts` lines 99-100 (`program.command("stop").description("Stop the Mongoku server running with PM2").action(() => { runCommand("pm2", ["stop", "mongoku"], { stdio: "inherit" }) })` — SUBCOMMAND REGISTRATION via `.command("verb")` returning the SUB-PROGRAM for chain configuration; `.action(fn)` registers the handler — handler INVOKED at parse time when this subcommand matches)
    * `cli.ts` lines 102-108 (`program.command("start").description("Start the Mongoku server").option("--pm2", "Run with PM2").option("-p, --port <port>", "Port to run on", "3100").option("--readonly", "Enable read-only mode (prevents modifications)").allowExcessArguments(false).action(startAction)` — RICH SUBCOMMAND DECLARATION: 2 boolean flags (`--pm2` + `--readonly`) + 1 value flag `-p, --port <port>` with DEFAULT "3100" + strict mode + action handler; `<port>` PARENTHESIZED ANGLE-BRACKET form declares REQUIRED value, `[port]` SQUARE BRACKETS would mark it OPTIONAL)
    * `cli.ts` lines 117-130 (`program.option("--pm2", "Run with PM2").option("-p, --port <port>", "Port to run on", "3100").option("--readonly", "Enable read-only mode (prevents modifications)").allowExcessArguments(false).action(startAction)` — ROOT-level options replicated from `start` subcommand + DEFAULT ACTION handler; when user runs `mongoku` with no subcommand, the root `.action(startAction)` fires)
    * `cli.ts` line 167 (`program.parse(process.argv)` — EXPLICIT FINAL PARSE call; commander.js REQUIRES this call to actually trigger parsing — distinct from yargs's `.argv` TERMINAL ACCESSOR which TRIGGERS PARSING ON FIRST READ, and oclif's `await this.parse(Class)` which is CALLED INSIDE the command class's `run()` method)
  - Critical extraction note: mongoku uses **TJ Holowaychuk's tj/commander.js library** at cli.ts:4 — corpus's **first documented tj/commander.js library** + **third documented TypeScript/Node CLI library**.  TJ Holowaychuk's commander.js (~26K GH stars) is the OG Node CLI library, started in 2011 — predates yargs (2014) and oclif (2018).  Used by `vue-cli` + `create-react-app` + `prettier` + `eslint` + `lerna` + `jest` + many others.  commander.js's INFLUENCE is broad: Python's `Click` (covered in earlier R-round) draws heavily from commander.js's API style, and many CLI libraries cite commander.js as design inspiration.  Distinct from R341 bcoe/yargs (yargs uses TERMINAL `.argv` ACCESSOR + fluent-builder, commander uses EXPLICIT `.parse()` call + per-command sub-builder + per-subcommand instance), R340 oclif (CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS + filesystem-discovered classes, commander is in-memory imperative builder), R275 Rust clap-derive (struct + proc-macro), R322 Java JCommander (annotations + reflection), R334 Go alecthomas/kong (struct tags + reflection), R342 alexflint/go-arg (two-tag struct fields + reflection).  commander.js's approach: TYPESCRIPT/JS EXPLICIT-INSTANCE IMPERATIVE BUILDER + `.command(verb)` returning sub-program for chain configuration + REQUIRED FINAL `program.parse(process.argv)` call + ROOT-default-action options + `.configureOutput` custom error handler.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-TWO library variants: Rust (7), Go (6), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), Go 6 alexflint/go-arg (R342), **TypeScript/Node 3 tj/commander.js** (R343 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 82 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  TypeScript/Node NOW HAS THREE documented CLI libraries (oclif + yargs + commander.js).
  - Critical extraction note: mongoku uses **commander.js explicit-instance + required-final-parse CLI pattern** at cli.ts:19 + 167 — `const program = new Command()` + `program.parse(process.argv)`.  Unlike yargs's MODULE-LEVEL SINGLETON (`import yargs from "yargs"` imports a default instance), commander.js requires EXPLICIT INSTANTIATION via `new Command()` — multiple Command instances can co-exist in a single Node process (useful for testing or for nested CLIs).  Unlike yargs's TERMINAL `.argv` PROPERTY ACCESSOR (which triggers parsing on first read), commander.js REQUIRES EXPLICIT `program.parse(process.argv)` call at the END of the spec — parsing does NOT happen until `.parse()` is called.  Distinct from R341 yargs `.argv` terminal access, R340 oclif `await this.parse(Class)` in-handler, R275 Rust clap-derive `Cli::parse()` struct method, R322 Java JCommander `new JCommander(target).parse(args)` builder.  commander.js's approach: EXPLICIT-INSTANCE `new Command()` + REQUIRED FINAL `program.parse(process.argv)` call — corpus's **first documented explicit-instance-with-required-final-parse CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mongoku uses **commander.js sub-program-returned-by-.command-for-chain-configuration CLI pattern** at cli.ts:102-108 — `program.command("start").description("Start the Mongoku server").option("--pm2", "Run with PM2").option("-p, --port <port>", "Port to run on", "3100").option("--readonly", "Enable read-only mode (prevents modifications)").allowExcessArguments(false).action(startAction)`.  commander.js's `.command("verb")` RETURNS the SUB-PROGRAM (a nested Command instance) so that subsequent `.description()`/`.option()`/`.allowExcessArguments()`/`.action()` calls are CHAINED ON THAT SUB-PROGRAM, configuring just that subcommand.  This is DIFFERENT from yargs's `.command(name, desc, builder, handler)` SINGLE CALL with all spec passed as arguments — commander.js prefers METHOD CHAINING ON THE RETURNED SUB-PROGRAM.  Distinct from R341 yargs `yargs.command(name, desc, builder, handler)` 4-arg call, R340 oclif STATIC FIELDS on a CLASS, R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Parser)]` on STRUCT.  commander.js's approach: SUB-PROGRAM RETURNED BY `.command(verb)` for CHAIN CONFIGURATION — corpus's **first documented sub-program-returned-by-.command-for-chain-configuration CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mongoku uses **commander.js parenthesized-angle-bracket-vs-square-bracket-for-required-vs-optional-value CLI pattern** at cli.ts:104 — `program.command("start").option("-p, --port <port>", "Port to run on", "3100")` (vs `--port [port]` for optional).  commander.js's option DECLARATION STRING uses ANGLE BRACKETS `<port>` to mark a REQUIRED VALUE (flag must be followed by a value) and SQUARE BRACKETS `[port]` for OPTIONAL VALUE (flag may be followed by a value).  Distinct from R341 yargs `.option("port", { string: true })` (separate type/required flags in option config object), R340 oclif `Flags.string({name: "port", required: true})` (factory function with config), R339 OCaml rbardou/Clap `Clap.mandatory_string ~long:"port"` (function-name encodes requiredness), R338 Odin core:flags `args:"name=port,manifold=1"` (struct tag DSL).  commander.js's approach: ANGLE BRACKETS `<arg>` vs SQUARE BRACKETS `[arg]` INSIDE the option declaration STRING — corpus's **first documented angle-vs-square-bracket-for-required-vs-optional-value CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_alchemy-create-web3-dapp` (915 stars — narrow scaffold), `_donkey` (batiste/donkey 8 stars — API gateway, narrow), `_bash` (BHznJNs/BaSB — too narrow), `_fomolt-cli` (1 star — agentic trading, too narrow), `_npx-learning` (Devkuldeep/npx-learning — narrow npx tutorial), `_task-manager-cli` (QuanDN22 — narrow), `_cc-channel` (L-x-C/cc-channel — narrow), `_restoreme` (RyanKaleliGabriel — narrow).  Probed `_lerna` (canonical commander.js consumer — IN CORPUS already), `_prettier` (IN CORPUS), `_eslint` (IN CORPUS), `_jest` (IN CORPUS), `_vue` (IN CORPUS).  Considered using `_create-web3-dapp` as the documentation tool but its CLI is mostly interactive prompts.  Chose mongoku: 1411 GH stars on huggingface/Mongoku (real production MongoDB GUI by Hugging Face — known for the HuggingFace.co ML model hub), 3 commands (`start` default + `stop` + `help`) + 3 start-action flags (`--pm2` + `-p/--port <port>` + `--readonly`) + ROOT default-action options + ENV-VAR fallback for port + IMPERATIVE BUILDER API + EXPLICIT-INSTANCE `new Command()` + CHAINED metadata setters + `.command(verb).description(d).option(...).action(fn)` per-subcommand chain + `.allowExcessArguments(false)` strict mode + `.configureOutput({outputError: fn})` custom error handler + REQUIRED FINAL `program.parse(process.argv)` call, full cli.ts exercising EVERY MAJOR commander.js PATTERN, real production tool maintained by Hugging Face.
  - Blacklist update: `mongoku` added.  Corpus count: 28,765 → 28,766 (+1).

- **R342 — mgmt source-direct (next-gen distributed event-driven parallel config management in Go; alexflint/go-arg library)** (1 file new) — pivots from bcoe/yargs (R341 mup) to a SIXTH Go CLI library — corpus's **first documented alexflint/go-arg library** + **sixth documented Go CLI library** (joining cobra, urfave/cli, kingpin, spf13/pflag, alecthomas/kong from previous rounds).  mgmt is James Shubin's NEXT-GEN distributed config management system — REPLACES puppet-agent / chef-client / ansible / salt with a SINGLE DISTRIBUTED BINARY that supervises resources and REACTS to changes in REAL TIME using a GRAPH-BASED execution engine.  Embeds an `mcl` DSL frontend (compiled functional language), a yaml graph frontend, and a puppet-compatible frontend.  4254 GH stars.  Sister tools: puppet (Ruby DSL agent, in corpus), ansible (Python, in corpus), saltstack (Python, in corpus), chef-client (Ruby, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented alexflint/go-arg library** + **sixth documented Go CLI library** — Go ecosystem now has SIX documented CLI libraries (cobra/urfave-cli/kingpin/spf13-pflag/kong/go-arg), still leading the multi-library club ahead of OCaml 4 + Rust 7.  Adds alexflint/go-arg (Alex Flint's go-arg) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: go-arg is STRUCT-TAG-BASED with TWO TAGS PER FIELD (`arg:"..."` for spec + `help:"..."` for description, NOT combined into single tag like kong's `kong:"..."`) + TYPE-BASED SUBCOMMAND DETECTION (POINTER-TO-STRUCT field with `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag becomes a SUBCOMMAND, no separate enum/registry).  Adds env-var-fallback via `arg:"env:VARNAME"` directive + `arg:"separate"` for repeatable flags + `arg:"-"` for parser-ignored fields + EMBEDDED CONFIG-STRUCT pattern (`type RunArgs struct { lib.Config; ... }` so common engine flags inherit into multiple subcommands without manual re-declaration).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 81.
