  - `_mgmt` (1-stem; purpleidea/mgmt cli/cli.go + cli/{run,deploy,check,setup,firstboot,docs,tools}.go + lib/main.go Config; **alexflint/go-arg library** — `import "github.com/alexflint/go-arg"` brings go-arg into scope; **1 top-level flag (`--license`) + 9 top-level subcommands (`check`/`run`/`deploy`/`setup`/`firstboot`/`docs`/`tools`/`etcd`/`provisioner`) + 5 frontend sub-verbs (`empty`/`lang`/`yaml`/`puppet`/`langpuppet`) used by run/deploy/check + 3 setup sub-verbs (`pkg`/`svc`/`firstboot`) + 11 embedded lib.Config engine flags (`--hostname`/`--prefix`/`--tmp-prefix`/`--allow-tmp-prefix`/`--no-watch`/`--no-stream-watch`/`--no-deploy-watch`/`--no-autoedges`/`--no-autogroup`/`--noop`/`--sema`) + 5 deploy-specific flags (`--ssh-url`/`--ssh-hostkey`/`--seeds`/`--no-git`/`--force`) + STRUCT-TAG-BASED declarative API + TWO TAGS PER FIELD (`arg:"..."` + `help:"..."`) + POINTER-TO-STRUCT subcommand detection via `arg:"subcommand:X"` + EMBEDDED CONFIG-STRUCT for flag inheritance + ENV-VAR FALLBACK via `arg:"env:VARNAME"` + REPEATABLE FLAGS via `arg:"separate"` + PARSER-IGNORED FIELDS via `arg:"-"`**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `cli/cli.go` lines 45-46 (`import ( "github.com/alexflint/go-arg" )` — Go IMPORT of go-arg library; distributed via Go modules at `github.com/alexflint/go-arg`)
    * `cli/cli.go` lines 56-58 (`func init() { if _, err := arg.NewParser(arg.Config{}, &Args{}); err != nil { panic(errwrap.Wrapf(err, "invalid args cli struct")) } }` — `init()` PRE-VALIDATES the struct at startup via `arg.NewParser(arg.Config{}, &Args{})` — go-arg DETECTS spec errors BEFORE any user input)
    * `cli/cli.go` lines 60-90 (`type Args struct { License bool \`arg:"--license" help:"display the license and exit"\`; CheckCmd *CheckArgs \`arg:"subcommand:check" help:"check code on this machine"\`; RunCmd *RunArgs \`arg:"subcommand:run" help:"run code on this machine"\`; DeployCmd *DeployArgs \`arg:"subcommand:deploy" help:"deploy code into a cluster"\`; SetupCmd *SetupArgs \`arg:"subcommand:setup" help:"setup some bootstrapping tasks"\`; ... ; version string \`arg:"-"\`; description string \`arg:"-"\` }` — TOP-LEVEL `Args` struct with TWO TAGS PER FIELD (`arg:"..."` for the SPEC + `help:"..."` for description); POINTER-TO-STRUCT fields with `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag declare SUBCOMMANDS; `arg:"-"` excludes the field from parsing)
    * `cli/deploy.go` lines 19-39 (`type DeployArgs struct { SSHURL string \`arg:"--ssh-url" help:"transport the etcd client connection over SSH to this server"\`; SSHHostKey string \`arg:"--ssh-hostkey" help:"use this ssh known hosts key when connecting over SSH"\`; Seeds []string \`arg:"--seeds,separate,env:MGMT_SEEDS" help:"default etcd client endpoints"\`; Noop bool \`arg:"--noop" help:"globally force all resources into no-op mode"\`; Sema int \`arg:"--sema" default:"-1" help:"globally add a semaphore to all resources with this lock count"\`; NoGit bool \`arg:"--no-git" help:"don't look at git commit id for safe deploys"\`; Force bool \`arg:"--force" help:"force a new deploy, even if the safety chain would break"\`; NoAutoEdges bool \`arg:"--no-autoedges" help:"skip the autoedges stage"\`; NoAutoGroup bool \`arg:"--no-autogroup" help:"skip the autogroup stage"\`; DeployEmpty *cliUtil.EmptyArgs \`arg:"subcommand:empty" help:"deploy empty payload"\`; ... }` — sub-COMMAND-args struct showing MULTIPLE TAG DIRECTIVES per `arg` tag: `arg:"--seeds,separate,env:MGMT_SEEDS"` declares LONG FLAG + REPEATABLE + ENV FALLBACK in one comma-separated directive list; `default:"-1"` is a SEPARATE TAG for default value)
    * `cli/run.go` lines 19-27 (`type RunArgs struct { lib.Config; RunEmpty *cliUtil.EmptyArgs \`arg:"subcommand:empty" help:"..."\`; RunLang *cliUtil.LangArgs \`arg:"subcommand:lang" help:"..."\`; RunYaml *cliUtil.YamlArgs \`arg:"subcommand:yaml" help:"..."\`; RunPuppet *cliUtil.PuppetArgs \`arg:"subcommand:puppet" help:"..."\`; RunLangPuppet *cliUtil.LangPuppetArgs \`arg:"subcommand:langpuppet" help:"..."\` }` — `lib.Config` EMBEDDED STRUCT (no field name, just type) — go-arg WALKS THROUGH the embedded struct's fields as if they were declared inline; this is how mgmt SHARES the same 11+ engine flags across `run`/`check`/`deploy`/etc. WITHOUT REPEATING THEM)
    * `lib/main.go` lines 30-90 (`type Config struct { Program string \`arg:"-"\`; Version string \`arg:"-"\`; Debug bool \`arg:"-"\`; Logf func(format string, v ...interface{}) \`arg:"-"\`; Hostname *string \`arg:"--hostname" help:"hostname to use"\`; Prefix *string \`arg:"--prefix,env:MGMT_PREFIX" help:"..."\`; TmpPrefix bool \`arg:"--tmp-prefix" help:"..."\`; AllowTmpPrefix bool \`arg:"--allow-tmp-prefix" help:"..."\`; NoWatch bool \`arg:"--no-watch" help:"..."\`; ...; Noop bool \`arg:"--noop" help:"..."\` }` — the `lib.Config` struct embedded into many subcommand-args structs; `*string` POINTER-TO-STRING fields encode OPTIONAL strings (nil = not provided))
  - Critical extraction note: mgmt uses **alexflint/go-arg library** at cli.go:45 — corpus's **first documented alexflint/go-arg library** + **sixth documented Go CLI library** (joining cobra, urfave/cli, kingpin, spf13/pflag, alecthomas/kong).  alexflint/go-arg is Alex Flint's go-arg — STRUCT-TAG-BASED CLI parser using a TWO-TAG CONVENTION: `arg:"..."` tag for the SPEC + `help:"..."` tag for the description (NOT combined into a single tag).  This contrasts with R334 alecthomas/kong's SINGLE COMBINED `kong:"..."` TAG with `key='value'` DSL.  go-arg's SPEC tag uses COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES inside a SINGLE tag string: `arg:"--seeds,separate,env:MGMT_SEEDS"` declares LONG FLAG + REPEATABLE + ENV FALLBACK.  TYPE-BASED SUBCOMMAND DETECTION: POINTER-TO-STRUCT field with `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag IS the subcommand; presence of non-nil pointer at parse-time AFTER parsing INDICATES which subcommand was used.  EMBEDDED CONFIG-STRUCTS for flag inheritance: `type RunArgs struct { lib.Config; ... }` — go-arg walks the embedded struct's fields as if inlined.  Distinct from R334 alecthomas/kong (single `kong:"..."` tag with key='value' DSL + `cmd` tag for subcommands), R275 Rust clap-derive (proc-macro compile-time codegen), R338 Odin core:flags (backtick struct tag + RTTI), R322 Java JCommander (`@Parameter` annotation), R340 oclif (class-inheritance-with-static-fields).  go-arg's approach: TWO-TAG STRUCT FIELD declarative API + TYPE-BASED SUBCOMMAND DETECTION + COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES IN `arg:"..."` STRING + EMBEDDED CONFIG-STRUCTS for flag inheritance.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY-ONE library variants: Rust (7), **Go (6 — cobra/urfave-cli/kingpin/spf13-pflag/kong/go-arg)**, Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif (R340), TypeScript/Node yargs (R341), **Go 6 alexflint/go-arg** (R342 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 81 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Go NOW HAS SIX documented CLI libraries — extends Go's lead as MOST-DOCUMENTED CLI-language with 6 distinct libraries.
  - Critical extraction note: mgmt uses **alexflint/go-arg two-tag-per-field declarative CLI pattern** at cli.go:62-89 + deploy.go:19-39 — `License bool \`arg:"--license" help:"display the license and exit"\`` and `Seeds []string \`arg:"--seeds,separate,env:MGMT_SEEDS" help:"default etcd client endpoints"\``.  go-arg uses TWO TAGS PER STRUCT FIELD: `arg:"..."` for the SPEC (flag names, positional/subcommand directives, env-var fallback, separate-repeatable flag, default tag offset, etc.) + `help:"..."` for the DESCRIPTION used in auto-generated help output.  Distinct from R334 kong (SINGLE COMBINED `kong:"..."` tag with `key='value'` DSL including `help='...'`), R275 clap-derive (single `#[arg(short = 'x', long = "alpha", help = "...")]` attribute), R338 Odin core:flags (single `args:"name=X,pos=N" usage:"..."` two-tag like go-arg but COMMA-SEPARATED inside `args:`).  go-arg's approach: TWO TAGS PER FIELD with the `arg:"..."` tag using COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES — corpus's **first documented two-tag-per-field-with-comma-directives CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mgmt uses **alexflint/go-arg pointer-to-struct-as-subcommand-via-presence CLI pattern** at cli.go:62-87 + cli.go:117-150 (Run method) — `CheckCmd *CheckArgs \`arg:"subcommand:check" help:"check code on this machine"\`; RunCmd *RunArgs \`arg:"subcommand:run" help:"run code on this machine"\`; DeployCmd *DeployArgs \`arg:"subcommand:deploy" help:"deploy code into a cluster"\`; ...` and `if cmd := obj.CheckCmd; cmd != nil { return cmd.Run(ctx, data) }; if cmd := obj.RunCmd; cmd != nil { return cmd.Run(ctx, data) }; ...`.  go-arg DETECTS SUBCOMMAND USAGE by checking whether the POINTER-TO-STRUCT field IS NON-NIL after parse — go-arg's parser SETS the pointer to the parsed struct ONLY if that subcommand was on the CLI; otherwise the pointer stays nil.  The CALLER PATTERN-MATCHES on which pointer is non-nil to dispatch.  Distinct from R334 alecthomas/kong (uses `kong.Vars` + `ctx.Run` REFLECTIVE DISPATCH on subcommand names), R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { Foo, Bar }` (enum-with-variants, exhaustively pattern-matched), R270 Haskell `subparser (command "verb" parser)` (Applicative composition).  go-arg's approach: POINTER-TO-STRUCT FIELD with `arg:"subcommand:X"` tag + PRESENCE-CHECK via `obj.XCmd != nil` to dispatch — corpus's **first documented pointer-to-struct-as-subcommand-via-presence CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mgmt uses **alexflint/go-arg embedded-config-struct for flag inheritance CLI pattern** at run.go:19 + check.go:18 + deploy.go:18 — `type RunArgs struct { lib.Config; RunEmpty *cliUtil.EmptyArgs \`arg:"subcommand:empty"\`; ... }` + `type CheckArgs struct { lib.Config; CheckLang *cliUtil.LangArgs \`arg:"subcommand:lang"\` }` + many others.  go-arg WALKS THROUGH EMBEDDED STRUCT FIELDS as if INLINED into the parent struct.  When `type X struct { lib.Config }` has the `lib.Config` struct embedded (no field name, just type), go-arg WALKS THROUGH the embedded struct's fields and treats their `arg:"..."` tags as if declared on the parent struct.  This is mgmt's MECHANISM for SHARING 11+ engine flags (`--hostname`/`--prefix`/`--tmp-prefix`/...) across `run`/`check`/`deploy`/many other subcommands WITHOUT REPEATING the same flag declarations 5+ times.  Distinct from R334 kong (no embedded-struct-walking; kong looks at struct tags only on direct fields), R275 clap-derive (uses `#[command(flatten)] base: BaseArgs` ATTRIBUTE for similar effect), R322 JCommander (no direct equivalent), R340 oclif (uses `extends BaseCommand` class inheritance).  go-arg's approach: EMBEDDED STRUCT FIELD (no name, just type) implicitly INLINES the embedded struct's fields — corpus's **first documented embedded-struct-implicit-inlining CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mgmt uses **alexflint/go-arg multi-directive-comma-separated-arg-tag CLI pattern** at deploy.go:23 — `Seeds []string \`arg:"--seeds,separate,env:MGMT_SEEDS" help:"default etcd client endpoints"\``.  go-arg's `arg:"..."` STRING contains COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES that EACH affect the spec: `--seeds` declares the LONG FLAG name; `separate` marks the flag as REPEATABLE (each occurrence pushes onto the slice); `env:MGMT_SEEDS` ESTABLISHES AN ENVIRONMENT-VARIABLE FALLBACK (value is read from `$MGMT_SEEDS` if flag not given on CLI).  Other go-arg directives: `positional` (POSITIONAL ARG instead of flag), `positional,required` (REQUIRED POSITIONAL), `required` (REQUIRED FLAG), `subcommand:X` (SUBCOMMAND), `-` (PARSER-IGNORED), `placeholder:Y` (HELP VALUE PLACEHOLDER), default:Z (separate `default:"Z"` tag).  Distinct from R334 kong `kong:"name='X',cmd,required,short='x',default='V',env='E'"` (SINGLE tag with KEY='VALUE' DSL), R275 clap-derive `#[arg(short = 'a', long = "alpha", env = "ALPHA", required = true)]` (Rust attribute argument), R338 Odin core:flags `args:"name=X,pos=N"` (struct tag with `=` syntax).  go-arg's approach: COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES inside a SINGLE `arg:"..."` STRING — corpus's **first documented multi-directive-comma-separated-arg-tag CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_meshbird` (3525 stars, distributed private networking — IN CORPUS not strictly verified but mesh bird's CLI is internal-config-driven, narrower than mgmt's command tree), `_libaws` (nathants/libaws 449 stars, AWS shipping CLI — narrower surface), `_grh` (bin3xish477/grh 2 stars — too narrow), `_yabtago` (igxactly/yabtago — too narrow), `_redis-tool` (bcap/redis-tool — narrow).  Considered `_botkube` (kubeshop/botkube — uses go-arg internally for plugin command flags, but main CLI uses different framework), `_kiteco-public` (kiteco/kiteco-public — uses go-arg for an internal kite-go tool only).  Probed Go alternatives: `_flaggy` users (would be 7th Go CLI library; HOLD), `_mitchellh/cli` users (HOLD), `_pebbe/zmq4` (not a CLI lib).  Chose mgmt: 4254 GH stars (real production config management replacing puppet/chef/ansible/saltstack), 9 top-level subcommands + 5 frontend sub-verbs (`empty`/`lang`/`yaml`/`puppet`/`langpuppet`) used by run/deploy/check + 3 setup sub-verbs (`pkg`/`svc`/`firstboot`) + 11 embedded lib.Config engine flags + 5 deploy-specific flags + EMBEDDED-CONFIG-STRUCT inheritance + POINTER-TO-STRUCT subcommand detection + TWO-TAG-PER-FIELD declarative API + COMMA-SEPARATED DIRECTIVES + ENV-VAR fallback + REPEATABLE FLAGS, full cli/*.go exercising EVERY MAJOR go-arg PATTERN, real production binary distributed via `go install github.com/purpleidea/mgmt@latest`.
  - Blacklist update: `mgmt` added.  Corpus count: 28,764 → 28,765 (+1).

- **R341 — mup source-direct (Meteor-up production-quality Meteor.js deployment tool in JavaScript; bcoe/yargs library)** (1 file new) — pivots from oclif framework (R340 hackmd-cli) to a SECOND TypeScript/Node CLI library — corpus's **first documented bcoe/yargs library** + **second documented TypeScript/Node CLI library**.  mup (binary name) is Meteor-up — Zachary Anker's production-quality Meteor.js deployment tool that installs Docker + MongoDB + reverse-proxy with Let's Encrypt on remote servers via SSH, then ships locally-built Meteor bundles to them.  1269 GH stars.  Sister tools: capistrano (Ruby deploy DSL, in corpus), ansible (in corpus), pm2 (in corpus), forever (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented bcoe/yargs library** + **second documented TypeScript/Node CLI library** — Node ecosystem now has TWO documented CLI libraries (R340 oclif + R341 yargs), joining the multi-library club alongside Rust 7, OCaml 4, Go 5, Crystal 3, Java 3, Mojo 2, Common Lisp 2, Clojure 2, Erlang 2, C++ 2, Nim 2, Free Pascal 2, Scheme 4-dialect club.  Adds bcoe/yargs (BENJAMIN COE's yargs, formerly nodejs/yargs, now under the yargs/ org) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: yargs is IMPERATIVE FLUENT-BUILDER API where each `.command(name, desc, builder, handler)` call REGISTERS a subcommand into a shared `yargs` instance + chained `.option(name, {description, alias, string/boolean/number/array, requiresArg, demandOption, default, choices})` calls add per-command flags + terminal `.argv` accessor TRIGGERS PARSING.  Distinct from R340 oclif CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS (oclif is filesystem-discovered classes; yargs is in-memory builder).  yargs has 11K+ GH stars, dependency of webpack-cli + mocha + many others.  Plugin systems supported via `loadPlugins()` patterns (mup's approach: walks `src/plugins/<plugin>/commands.js`, calls `yargs.command(plugin, desc, subYargs => addModuleCommands(subYargs, ...))` to register each plugin's commands as a nested subcommand group).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 80.
  - `_mup` (1-stem; zodern/meteor-up src/index.js + src/plugins/{default,docker,meteor,mongo,proxy}/commands.js; **bcoe/yargs library** — `import yargs from 'yargs'` brings the fluent-builder yargs instance into scope; **6 global options (`--settings`/`--config`/`--servers`/`--verbose`/`--show-hook-names` + `-v/--version` + `-h/--help`) + 9 top-level default commands (`init`/`setup`/`deploy`/`logs`/`reconfig`/`restart`/`start`/`stop`/`ssh`) + 4 PLUGIN COMMAND GROUPS each with sub-verbs (`meteor` 9 verbs + `docker` 6 verbs + `mongo` 6 verbs + `proxy` 9 verbs) + IMPERATIVE FLUENT-BUILDER API + `.command(name, desc, builder, handler)` SUBCOMMAND REGISTRATION + `.option(name, {description, alias, string, boolean, number, requiresArg})` PER-OPTION DECLARATION + `.usage`/`.version`/`.scriptName`/`.strict`/`.help`/`.epilogue`/`.alias`/`.global` METHOD-CHAIN BUILDER + `.argv` TERMINAL ACCESSOR + DYNAMIC PLUGIN-BASED COMMAND REGISTRATION via `loadPlugins()` walking `src/plugins/<plugin>/commands.js` + nested subYargs builder for plugin subcommand groups**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/index.js` line 10 (`import yargs from 'yargs'` — TYPESCRIPT/ESM IMPORT of the yargs library; bcoe/yargs is distributed via npm + has no peer dependencies beyond Node's stdlib)
    * `src/index.js` lines 70-100 (`let program = yargs.usage('\nUsage: mup <command> [args]').version(pkg.version).alias('v', 'version').global('version', false).option('settings', {description: 'Path to Meteor settings file', requiresArg: true, string: true}).option('config', {...}).option('servers', {...}).option('verbose', {...}).option('show-hook-names', {...}).strict(true).scriptName('mup').alias('h', 'help').epilogue('...').help('help')` — fluent-builder method-chain: `.usage` for usage line + `.version` for version string + `.alias` for short-flag alias + `.global` to mark non-global + `.option(name, {description, requiresArg, string/boolean})` for each option + `.strict(true)` to reject unknown flags + `.scriptName` for `$0` substitution + `.epilogue` for help footer + `.help` to enable `--help`)
    * `src/index.js` lines 16-19 (`yargs.help(false); const preAPI = new MupAPI(process.cwd(), process.argv, yargs.argv); const config = preAPI.getConfig(false); let pluginList = []` — INITIAL `yargs.argv` ACCESS triggers PARSING; result accessed for config loading BEFORE plugins are loaded so plugins can register their own commands AFTER initial parse)
    * `src/index.js` lines 102-118 (`Object.keys(modules).forEach(moduleName => { if (moduleName !== 'default' && modules[moduleName].commands) { yargs.command(moduleName, modules[moduleName].description, subYargs => { addModuleCommands(subYargs, modules[moduleName], moduleName); }, () => { yargs.showHelp('log'); }) } else if (moduleName === 'default') { addModuleCommands(yargs, modules[moduleName], moduleName) } })` — PLUGIN-BASED COMMAND REGISTRATION: each plugin's `commands.js` exports are registered into yargs as either top-level commands (`default` plugin) or nested subcommand groups via `yargs.command(group, desc, subYargs => addModuleCommands(subYargs, module, group))`)
    * `src/plugins/meteor/commands.js` lines 5-50 (`export const setup = { description: 'Prepare server to deploy meteor apps', handler: commandHandlers.setup }; export const deploy = { description: 'Deploy meteor apps', builder(subYargs) { return subYargs.option('cached-build', { description: 'Use build from previous deploy', boolean: true }); }, handler: commandHandlers.deploy }; export const destroy = { ..., builder(subYargs) { return subYargs.option('force', {...}); }, handler: ... }` — PER-COMMAND BUILDER FUNCTION accepts `subYargs` and returns it after chained `.option()` calls; this is yargs's MECHANISM for per-command flags)
    * `src/plugins/meteor/commands.js` lines 39-63 (`export const logs = { description: 'View meteor app\\'s logs', builder(yargs) { return yargs.strict(false).option('tail', { description: '...', alias: 't', number: true }).option('follow', { description: 'Follow log output', alias: 'f', boolean: true }); }, handler: commandHandlers.logs }` — `.option(name, {description, alias, number/boolean})` builder with TYPE COERCION (`number: true` parses to JavaScript number; `boolean: true` flags it as no-arg boolean); `.strict(false)` ALLOWS additional unknown flags to pass through to docker logs)
    * `src/plugins/meteor/commands.js` lines 96-103 (`export const debug = { name: 'debug [server]', description: 'Debug the meteor app', builder(yargs) { yargs.strict(false); }, handler: commandHandlers.debugApp }` — `name: 'debug [server]'` SQUARE-BRACKETED POSITIONAL declaration; yargs INTERPRETS the `name` string as a yargs-syntax COMMAND DECLARATION with positional args)
  - Critical extraction note: mup uses **bcoe/yargs library** at index.js:10 — corpus's **first documented bcoe/yargs library** + **second documented TypeScript/Node CLI library**.  bcoe/yargs is Benjamin Coe's yargs — the OLDEST and MOST-WIDELY-USED Node CLI library (11K+ GH stars).  yargs IMPLEMENTS the IMPERATIVE FLUENT-BUILDER API where each `.command(name, desc, builder, handler)` call REGISTERS a subcommand into the shared yargs instance, and TERMINAL `.argv` PROPERTY ACCESS triggers parsing.  Distinct from R340 oclif CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS (oclif is filesystem-discovered command classes; yargs is in-memory imperative builder), R275 Rust clap-derive (compile-time codegen from struct attributes), R322 Java JCommander (annotations + runtime reflection), R334 Go alecthomas/kong (struct tags + runtime reflection), R339 OCaml rbardou/Clap (global mutable parser state + imperative consumer calls), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (string-of-defaults).  yargs's approach: TYPESCRIPT/JS IMPERATIVE FLUENT-BUILDER + `.command()` subcommand registration + `.option()` flag declaration + `.argv` terminal-access-triggers-parsing + nested `subYargs` builder for per-command flags + plugin-friendly via shared instance.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to EIGHTY library variants: Rust (7), Go (5), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), TypeScript/Node oclif `@oclif/core` (R340), **TypeScript/Node 2 bcoe/yargs** (R341 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 80 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  TypeScript/Node NOW HAS TWO documented CLI libraries (oclif + yargs).
  - Critical extraction note: mup uses **bcoe/yargs imperative-fluent-builder + .argv-terminal-access-triggers-parsing CLI pattern** at index.js:70-100 — `let program = yargs.usage('...').version(pkg.version).alias('v', 'version').global('version', false).option('settings', {description: 'Path to Meteor settings file', requiresArg: true, string: true}).option('config', {...}).option('servers', {...}).option('verbose', {boolean: true}).option('show-hook-names', {boolean: true}).strict(true).scriptName('mup').alias('h', 'help').epilogue('...').help('help'); ... ; program = program.argv;`.  yargs's `.argv` is a TERMINAL ACCESSOR — accessing it TRIGGERS PARSING of the previously-built spec against `process.argv`.  This is FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT from oclif's class-inheritance-with-static-fields and clap-derive's struct-attributes approach.  Each `.command()`/`.option()`/`.usage()` returns the SAME shared yargs instance for METHOD-CHAINING.  Distinct from R340 oclif `await this.parse(Class)` (explicit parse call inside command class), R275 clap-derive `Cli::parse()` (struct's static method), R322 JCommander `parser.parse(*argv)` (builder + separate parse call), R339 OCaml rbardou/Clap (consumer calls auto-trigger parse at first call).  yargs's approach: IMPERATIVE FLUENT-BUILDER + `.argv` TERMINAL-ACCESSOR-TRIGGERS-PARSING — corpus's **first documented .argv-terminal-accessor-triggers-parsing CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mup uses **bcoe/yargs nested-subYargs-builder for per-command flags CLI pattern** at meteor/commands.js:9-18 + 21-29 — `export const deploy = { description: 'Deploy meteor apps', builder(subYargs) { return subYargs.option('cached-build', { description: 'Use build from previous deploy', boolean: true }); }, handler: commandHandlers.deploy }`.  Each command's `builder(subYargs)` function takes a NESTED yargs INSTANCE scoped to that subcommand and returns it after chained `.option()` calls.  This nests command-specific flags UNDER the subcommand without polluting the global flag namespace.  Distinct from R340 oclif `static flags = {help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), ...}` (per-class flags record), R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Args)] struct CommandArgs { ... }` (per-variant struct fields), R322 JCommander `parser.addCommand("verb", new VerbObj()) where VerbObj has @Parameter fields` (per-command instance with field annotations).  yargs's approach: NESTED `subYargs` BUILDER FUNCTION per command — corpus's **first documented nested-subYargs-builder-for-per-command-flags CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mup uses **bcoe/yargs square-bracketed-positional-in-name-string CLI pattern** at meteor/commands.js:96-103 — `export const debug = { name: 'debug [server]', description: 'Debug the meteor app', builder(yargs) { yargs.strict(false); }, handler: commandHandlers.debugApp }`.  The `name` STRING `'debug [server]'` INCLUDES a SQUARE-BRACKETED POSITIONAL ARG declaration; yargs PARSES the name string as YARGS-SYNTAX command declaration with positional args.  Bracket conventions: `[arg]` = OPTIONAL positional, `<arg>` = REQUIRED positional, `<arg...>` = VARIADIC positional, `[arg...]` = OPTIONAL VARIADIC.  Distinct from R340 oclif `static args = {...}` (separate field for args), R275 clap-derive `arg = clap::Arg::new("name").index(1)` (programmatic positional declaration), R322 JCommander `@Parameter` annotation without name = positional, R270 Haskell `strArgument` (separate function for positional).  yargs's approach: SQUARE-BRACKETED POSITIONAL ARG declaration INSIDE the name STRING — corpus's **first documented square-bracketed-positional-in-name-string CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mup uses **bcoe/yargs plugin-based-command-registration via shared-instance CLI pattern** at index.js:102-118 — `Object.keys(modules).forEach(moduleName => { if (moduleName !== 'default' && modules[moduleName].commands) { yargs.command(moduleName, modules[moduleName].description, subYargs => { addModuleCommands(subYargs, modules[moduleName], moduleName); }, () => { yargs.showHelp('log'); }) } })`.  PLUGINS LOAD DYNAMICALLY at startup; each plugin's `commands.js` EXPORTS a record of command definitions; the loader CALLS `yargs.command(plugin, desc, subYargs => addModuleCommands(subYargs, module, plugin))` on the SHARED yargs INSTANCE to register the plugin's commands as a NESTED SUBCOMMAND GROUP.  Plugin's `addModuleCommands(builder, module, moduleName)` then ITERATES over the module's `commands` export and calls `builder.command(name, command.description, command.builder, commandWrapper(moduleName, commandName))` for each.  Distinct from R340 oclif filesystem-hierarchy-walking (plugins are filesystem-rooted classes that oclif discovers at startup), R322 JCommander `parser.addCommand("verb", new VerbInstance())` (imperative but per-command not per-plugin-group).  yargs's approach: SHARED yargs INSTANCE + plugin's loader CALLS `.command()` to REGISTER subcommand groups — corpus's **first documented plugin-based-command-registration-via-shared-yargs-instance CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_terminalizer` (faressoft/terminalizer 16129 stars — IN CORPUS already), `_devtunnel` (microsoft/dev-tunnels — IN CORPUS already), `_onerepo` (paularmstrong/onerepo 131 stars — narrow monorepo tool, would have been a sister demo for yargs).  Probed `_commander.js`-based CLIs (would be 3rd Node CLI library but search returned no clean hits within token budget; HOLD), `_meow`-based CLIs (would be 4th Node CLI library; HOLD), `_typer` (Python, would be 4th Python CLI library; HOLD), `_docopt` (multi-language, would be a HYBRID entry; HOLD), `_serverless` (probably IN CORPUS), `_webpack-cli` (IN CORPUS), `_eslint` (IN CORPUS), `_prettier` (IN CORPUS), `_aws-cdk` (cdk command, IN CORPUS).  Considered `_optparse` for R-language entry (CRAN optparse package — HOLD, would benefit a future round), `_powershell` for PowerShell `param()` blocks (HOLD).  Chose mup: 1269 GH stars (real production Meteor deployment), 6 global flags + 9 default-plugin commands + 4 PLUGIN COMMAND GROUPS each with 6-9 sub-verbs (`meteor` 9 / `docker` 6 / `mongo` 6 / `proxy` 9) + DYNAMIC PLUGIN-BASED COMMAND REGISTRATION via shared yargs instance, full src/index.js + src/plugins/*/commands.js exercising EVERY MAJOR yargs PATTERN (imperative fluent-builder + .argv terminal access + nested subYargs builder + square-bracketed positionals in name string + plugin-based command registration), real production tool used by thousands of Meteor app deployments.
  - Blacklist update: `mup` added.  Corpus count: 28,763 → 28,764 (+1).

- **R340 — hackmd-cli source-direct (HackMD collaborative markdown editor command-line client in TypeScript; Salesforce `@oclif/core` framework)** (1 file new) — pivots from OCaml rbardou/Clap (R339 mazeppa) into the Node.js/TypeScript ecosystem to document the FIRST TypeScript/Node CLI library — corpus's **first documented TypeScript/Node entry** + **first documented oclif framework**.  hackmd-cli is the official HackMD.io command-line client; manages collaborative-markdown notes + folders + teams via the HackMD REST API.  143 GH stars.  Sister tools: notion-cli (in corpus), bear-cli (Bear notes), obsidian-cli (Obsidian).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented TypeScript/Node CLI library entry** — a stunning gap that this round closes; JavaScript/TypeScript is arguably THE most-deployed CLI tooling ecosystem (Node's npm registry hosts ~3M packages, many CLIs), and despite countless JS-based CLIs in the corpus, no R-round had explicitly documented a Node CLI library in the taxonomy before now.  Adds Salesforce-maintained `@oclif/core` (Heroku's CLI framework, now under Salesforce after Salesforce's Heroku acquisition) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: oclif is CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS declarative API where each command is a TYPESCRIPT CLASS extending a base `Command` class with STATIC FIELDS (`static description = '...'`, `static flags = {...}`, `static args = {...}`, `static examples = [...]`, `static aliases = [...]`) + async `run()` method dispatched via REFLECTION at parse time.  Commands live in `src/commands/<verb>.ts` with NESTED subcommand groups via `src/commands/<group>/<verb>.ts` — oclif WALKS THE FILESYSTEM HIERARCHY at startup to discover commands (no central command registry).  9535 GH stars on oclif/oclif (the framework generator); used by Salesforce CLI, Heroku CLI, Shopify CLI, Twilio CLI, Stripe CLI, AWS Amplify CLI.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 79.
  - `_hackmd-cli` (1-stem; hackmdio/hackmd-cli src/commands/*.ts + src/commands/{notes,folders,team-notes,team-folders}/*.ts + oclif/core API; **Salesforce `@oclif/core` framework** — `import {Flags, ux} from '@oclif/core'` brings the decorator-style command framework into scope; **6 top-level commands (`export`/`history`/`login`/`logout`/`whoami`/`teams`) + 4 subcommand groups (`notes`/`folders`/`team-notes`/`team-folders`) each with sub-verbs (`create`/`delete`/`index`/`update` + `order` for folders) + universal `-h/--help` + 7 `ux.table.flags()` table formatting flags (`--columns`/`--sort`/`--filter`/`--csv`/`--extended`/`--no-truncate`/`--no-header`/`--output`) + per-command flags via `static flags = {...}` declared by each command CLASS + ENUM-VALIDATED choices for `--readPermission`/`--writePermission` (owner/signed_in/guest) + `--commentPermission` (disabled/forbidden/owners/signed_in_users/everyone) + FILESYSTEM-HIERARCHY-WALKING command discovery + `async run()` method dispatch via REFLECTION + `await this.parse(Command)` in-handler argv re-parsing**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/commands/whoami.ts` lines 1-3 (`import {Flags, ux} from '@oclif/core'; import HackMDCommand from '../command'; export default class Whoami extends HackMDCommand` — TYPESCRIPT IMPORT of `@oclif/core` primitives + CLASS-INHERITANCE from base `HackMDCommand` which extends oclif's `Command`)
    * `src/commands/whoami.ts` lines 5-15 (`static description = 'Show current user information'; static examples = [\`$ hackmd-cli whoami\n...\`]; static flags = {help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), ...ux.table.flags()}` — STATIC FIELDS on the CLASS encode metadata: `description` for short help text, `examples` for help-page examples, `flags` for accepted flags; `...ux.table.flags()` spreads oclif's BUILT-IN table-formatting flags into the per-command flags map)
    * `src/commands/whoami.ts` lines 16-19 (`async run() { const {flags} = await this.parse(Whoami); ...` — ASYNC `run()` METHOD on the class; `await this.parse(Whoami)` RE-PARSES argv against THIS command's flags + args at runtime — oclif provides `this.parse(commandClass)` as the bridge between class-static spec and runtime flag extraction)
    * `src/commands/export.ts` lines 5-16 (`import {noteId} from '../flags'; export default class Export extends HackMDCommand { static description = 'Export note content'; static flags = {help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), noteId}` — FLAGS DEFINED IN SHARED MODULE `../flags.ts` then SPREAD into per-command flag maps; reusable composition pattern enabled by oclif's flat record-of-flags representation)
    * `src/commands/notes/create.ts` lines 1-20 (`import {Flags, ux} from '@oclif/core'; import HackMDCommand from '../../command'; import {commentPermission, editor, noteContent, notePermission, noteTags, noteTitle, parentFolderId} from '../../flags'; export default class CreateCommand extends HackMDCommand` — NESTED SUBCOMMAND under `notes/create.ts` extends the same base command; oclif discovers it via FILESYSTEM HIERARCHY at startup → `hackmd-cli notes create ...`)
    * `src/commands/notes/create.ts` lines 38-50 (`static flags = {commentPermission, content: noteContent, editor, help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), parentFolderId, readPermission: notePermission, writePermission: notePermission, tags: noteTags, title: noteTitle}` — DENSE FLAG SPECIFICATION using FLAG ALIASING (`content: noteContent` means "field named content backed by exported notepad flag definition"); typescript object shorthand `commentPermission` (auto-aliased) and explicit aliasing `content: noteContent` coexist)
    * `src/commands/` filesystem listing (TOP-LEVEL files: `export.ts`, `history.ts`, `login.ts`, `logout.ts`, `whoami.ts`, `teams.ts`; GROUPS as DIRECTORIES: `folders/`, `notes/`, `team-folders/`, `team-notes/` each containing `create.ts`/`delete.ts`/`index.ts`/`update.ts`/`order.ts` — oclif's filesystem-hierarchy DISPATCH MEANS `hackmd-cli notes create` maps to `src/commands/notes/create.ts`'s CreateCommand class)
  - Critical extraction note: hackmd-cli uses **Salesforce `@oclif/core` framework** at whoami.ts:1 — corpus's **first documented TypeScript/Node CLI library** + **first documented oclif framework**.  oclif is Heroku's (now Salesforce's) Open CLI Framework — written in TypeScript, designed for building MULTI-COMMAND CLI APPLICATIONS with rich plugin ecosystems.  Used by: Salesforce CLI, Heroku CLI, Shopify CLI, Twilio CLI, Stripe CLI, AWS Amplify CLI, HackMD CLI, Cypress, oclif itself (9535 stars).  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive (Rust struct + `#[derive(Parser)]` proc-macro), R322 Java JCommander (Java class + `@Parameter` annotation + reflection), R334 Go alecthomas/kong (Go struct + `kong:"..."` struct tag + reflection), R338 Odin core:flags (Odin struct + backtick `args:"..."` tag + RTTI), R339 OCaml rbardou/Clap (OCaml imperative consumer mechanism + global state).  oclif's approach: TypeScript CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS declarative API + ASYNC `run()` method + FILESYSTEM-HIERARCHY-WALKING command discovery (no central registry, `src/commands/<verb>.ts` becomes `cli verb`; `src/commands/<group>/<verb>.ts` becomes `cli group verb`) + PLUGIN SYSTEM via `oclif-plugin-*` packages (autocomplete, help, version are all separable plugins) + RICH BUILT-INS via `ux` module (table formatter, action spinners, prompts).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SEVENTY-NINE library variants: Rust (7), Go (5), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 4, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap (R339), **TypeScript/Node Salesforce `@oclif/core`** (R340 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 79 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  TypeScript/Node added to the documented-CLI-language set.
  - Critical extraction note: hackmd-cli uses **oclif class-inheritance-with-static-fields declarative CLI pattern** at whoami.ts:5-15 — `export default class Whoami extends HackMDCommand { static description = 'Show current user information'; static examples = [...]; static flags = {help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), ...ux.table.flags()}; async run() { const {flags} = await this.parse(Whoami); ... } }`.  oclif's framework reads STATIC FIELDS off the command CLASS via REFLECTION: `description` (short help), `examples` (help page examples), `flags` (record of flag-name -> Flag-definition), `args` (record of arg-name -> Arg-definition), `aliases` (command aliases).  Async `run()` is the dispatched handler; `this.parse(commandClass)` re-parses argv WITHIN the handler against the class's flags to return `{flags, args, argv}`.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive `#[derive(Parser)] struct Cli` (struct fields + macro-generated parsing), R322 Java JCommander `@Parameter` annotations on FIELDS of an instance (not a class), R338 Odin core:flags STRUCT (not a class, no inheritance), R270 Haskell `<*>` applicative combinator (no class).  oclif's approach: TYPESCRIPT CLASS-INHERITANCE-WITH-STATIC-FIELDS + async `run()` + `this.parse(Class)` BRIDGE — corpus's **first documented class-inheritance-with-static-fields CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hackmd-cli uses **oclif filesystem-hierarchy-walking command discovery CLI pattern** (src/commands/ filesystem listing) — TOP-LEVEL `src/commands/<verb>.ts` files become top-level `<verb>` commands; DIRECTORIES `src/commands/<group>/<verb>.ts` become `<group> <verb>` subcommand pairs.  oclif's `Config` walks the `commands` directory at startup, IMPORTS each `.ts`/`.js` file as a module, looks for the `default` export (the command class), and registers it.  NO CENTRAL COMMAND REGISTRY — adding a new subcommand means just adding a new `.ts` file in the right directory.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum { Foo, Bar }` (enum exhaustively lists subcommands), R322 JCommander `parser.addCommand("verb", new VerbInstance())` (imperative registration), R270 Haskell `subparser (command "verb" parser <> command "verb2" parser2)` (Applicative composition).  oclif's approach: FILESYSTEM-HIERARCHY-WALKING command discovery — corpus's **first documented filesystem-hierarchy-walking-command-discovery CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hackmd-cli uses **oclif shared-flag-definitions-and-spread-into-static-flags CLI pattern** at notes/create.ts:8-15 + 38-50 — `import {commentPermission, editor, noteContent, notePermission, noteTags, noteTitle, parentFolderId} from '../../flags'; ...; static flags = {commentPermission, content: noteContent, editor, help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), parentFolderId, readPermission: notePermission, writePermission: notePermission, tags: noteTags, title: noteTitle}`.  Each FLAG DEFINITION (a `Flags.string({...})`/`Flags.boolean({...})`/etc. call) is EXPORTED FROM A SHARED MODULE `../flags.ts`; per-command `static flags = {...}` SPREADS them into the per-command flags record via TYPESCRIPT OBJECT SHORTHAND (`commentPermission` auto-aliases to `commentPermission: commentPermission`) or EXPLICIT FIELD-NAME ALIASING (`content: noteContent` means "field `content` backed by `noteContent` flag definition" — useful when the API field name differs from the shared definition's symbol).  REUSABLE COMPOSITION ENABLED by oclif's flat-record-of-flags representation.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (no shared flag definitions, each `#[arg(...)]` attribute is per-field), R322 JCommander `@DynamicParameter` (key-value parameters, not shared definitions), R338 Odin core:flags (struct fields embed all metadata, no sharing).  oclif's approach: SHARED FLAG DEFINITIONS exported from a SHARED MODULE + SPREAD into per-command `static flags` — corpus's **first documented shared-flag-definitions-and-spread-into-static-flags CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hackmd-cli uses **oclif ux.table.flags() builtin-table-formatting-flags-spread CLI pattern** at whoami.ts:11 — `static flags = {help: Flags.help({char: 'h'}), ...ux.table.flags()}`.  oclif's `ux` module provides BUILT-IN TABLE FORMATTING for command output: `ux.table([rows], columns)` renders a table to stdout.  Its companion `ux.table.flags()` is a HELPER that RETURNS a RECORD OF FLAG DEFINITIONS: `--columns=CSV`, `--sort=COL`, `--filter=PROPERTY=VALUE`, `--csv`, `--extended`, `--no-truncate`, `--no-header`, `--output=FORMAT` (csv/json/yaml).  CALLER SPREADS these flags into their own command's `static flags` via `...ux.table.flags()` so EVERY TABLE-OUTPUTTING command supports the same set of table-formatting toggles.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (no built-in shared formatters), R322 JCommander (no shared formatter system), R338 Odin core:flags (no formatter helpers), R333 Mojo argmojo (no formatter helpers).  oclif's approach: `ux.table.flags()` returns a RECORD that SPREADS into the command's flags record — corpus's **first documented ux-table-flags-builtin-spread CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_oclif` (oclif/oclif framework generator at 9535 stars — would be useful but the BARE `oclif` binary's CLI is mostly project scaffolding which is in-corpus already; would be a META-DEMO of "oclif using oclif"; HOLD), `_shopify` (shopify/cli at 702 stars — too large and tenant-multitenant to fully extract), `_snipli` (0xMukesh/snipli 155 stars — narrow code-snippet sharer), `_gh-search-cli` (feinoujc/gh-search-cli 120 stars — niche GitHub search wrapper), `_wowcup` (codeaholicguy/wowcup 179 stars — World Cup 2018 demo, stale), `_autodot` (ajmalsiddiqui/autodot 76 stars — narrow dotfile manager), `_pike` (DUP existing), `_carp` (DUP existing), `_fennel` (DUP existing).  Probed alternative TypeScript/Node libraries: `_yargs`-based CLIs (yargs is the closest competitor to oclif but its CLI clients use IMPERATIVE BUILDER PATTERN `yargs.command('verb', 'desc', builder, handler).demandCommand().argv` rather than class-inheritance — HOLD for future round), `_commander.js`-based CLIs (also IMPERATIVE BUILDER `program.command('verb').option('--flag <X>').action(handler)` — HOLD), `_meow`-based CLIs (`meow` is much smaller, mostly help-text formatting — HOLD).  Considered `_typer` (Python, would be 4th Python CLI library — HOLD), `_docopt` (multi-language, would be a hybrid entry — HOLD).  Chose hackmd-cli: 143 GH stars (real production HackMD client used by HackMD users to script note management), 6 top-level commands + 4 subcommand groups + 18 total sub-verbs + ux.table.flags spread + class-inheritance-with-static-fields + filesystem-hierarchy-walking discovery + shared-flag-definitions + async `run()` method dispatch, full src/commands/ filesystem exercising EVERY MAJOR oclif PATTERN (class inheritance + static field metadata + flag spreads + nested subcommand directories + ux.table.flags), real production tool from HackMD.io.
  - Blacklist update: `hackmd-cli` added.  Corpus count: 28,762 → 28,763 (+1).

- **R339 — mazeppa source-direct (modern positive supercompiler for call-by-value functional languages in OCaml; rbardou/Clap library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338 odinfmt) into the modern functional-language ecosystem to document the FOURTH OCaml CLI library — corpus's **first documented rbardou/Clap library** + **fourth documented OCaml CLI library** (joining R271 OCaml Cmdliner, R325 OCaml stdlib Arg, R331 OCaml Jane Street Core.Command).  mazeppa is a positive supercompiler that performs program specialisation on a small functional language (terms over symbols + integer/string/tuple literals) and emits residual programs.  Authored by Hirrolot (known for small focused tools like metalang99, datatype99, mlatu).  478 GH stars.  Sister tools: PE (partial evaluators) like Sloth, mwhile.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented rbardou/Clap library** + **fourth documented OCaml CLI library** — OCaml NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries (Cmdliner + stdlib Arg + Jane Street Core.Command + rbardou/Clap), TIED with Rust 7's PER-LIBRARY COUNT as a percentage of total taxonomy.  Wait, Rust has 7, OCaml has 4 — Rust still leads.  Still, four OCaml libraries documented is significant.  Adds rbardou/Clap (NOT to be confused with Rust's `clap` — distinct library, completely different API) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: `Clap` is an IMPERATIVE CONSUMPTION-MECHANISM CLI parser for OCaml.  Calls to `Clap.flag`, `Clap.mandatory_string`, `Clap.optional_string`, `Clap.default_string` IMPLICITLY CONSUME from a global parser state initialised by `Clap.parse`.  `Clap.case "verb" ~description @@ fun () -> handler` builds a subcommand case; `Clap.subcommand [cases...]` assembles them into a dispatch table.  23 GH stars on rbardou/clap library itself but used by Tezos blockchain tools + mazeppa + several other OCaml CLIs.  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 78.
  - `_mazeppa` (1-stem; hirrolot/mazeppa bin/main.ml + rbardou/clap library; **OCaml `rbardou/Clap` library** — `open Clap` brings the Clap namespace into scope; **4 subcommands (`run` + `translate` + `check` + `eval`) + 5 documented flags across subcommands + `Clap.default_string ~long ~description default` STRING-WITH-DEFAULT + `Clap.flag ~set_long ~description false` BOOLEAN FLAG + `Clap.mandatory_string ~long ~description ()` REQUIRED STRING + `Clap.optional_string ~long ~description ()` OPTIONAL STRING + `Clap.case "verb" ~description @@ fun () -> handler` SUBCOMMAND CASE BUILDER + `Clap.subcommand [cases...]` SUBCOMMAND ASSEMBLER + IMPERATIVE CONSUMPTION via GLOBAL PARSER STATE**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 195-200 (`let get_target_dir () = Clap.default_string ~long:"target-dir" ~description:"The target directory to use." "target"` — `Clap.default_string` accepts NAMED LABEL ARGUMENTS `~long:"X"` + `~description:"..."` followed by POSITIONAL DEFAULT VALUE; returns STRING after CONSUMING from PARSER STATE)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 202-209 (`let get_inspect () = Clap.flag ~set_long:"inspect" ~description:"..." false` — `Clap.flag` accepts `~set_long:"X"` + `~description` followed by POSITIONAL DEFAULT BOOLEAN; returns BOOL after CONSUMING)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 211-219 (`let get_language () = Clap.mandatory_string ~long:"language" ~description:"..." ()` — `Clap.mandatory_string` accepts `~long` + `~description` + `()` UNIT TERMINATOR; ERRORS if argument NOT PROVIDED; returns STRING after CONSUMING)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 230-237 (`let get_dump_header_to () = Clap.optional_string ~long:"dump-header-to" ~description:"..." ()` — `Clap.optional_string` returns `STRING OPTION` (Some/None) after CONSUMING; differs from `mandatory_string` in that NO ARG = `None` rather than ERROR)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 240-269 (`let get_command () = let open Options in [ (Clap.case "run" ~description:"Run the supercompiler." @@ fun () -> let target_dir = get_target_dir () in let inspect = get_inspect () in fun () -> supercompile { target_dir; inspect }); (Clap.case "translate" ~description:"Translate a program to some target language." @@ fun () -> let language = get_language () in ...); ... ] |> Clap.subcommand` — `Clap.case` builds a SUBCOMMAND CASE by COMPOSING NAME + DESCRIPTION + CONTINUATION-PASSING HANDLER; case CONSUMES flag-getters INSIDE its handler body; `Clap.subcommand [...]` ASSEMBLES cases into DISPATCH TABLE)
    * `rbardou/clap` README (`Clap.parse` is called IMPLICITLY by the FIRST `Clap.*` consumer call; PARSER STATE is GLOBAL across all `Clap.*` calls in the program; each `Clap.flag`/`Clap.mandatory_string`/etc. CONSUMES from the global state in DECLARATION ORDER; UNCONSUMED ARGS at end of execution TRIGGER ERROR)
  - Critical extraction note: mazeppa uses **OCaml `rbardou/Clap` library** at main.ml:196 — corpus's **first documented rbardou/Clap library** + **fourth documented OCaml CLI library**.  rbardou/Clap is by Romain Bardou (Tezos engineer, OCaml hacker) — `Clap` is an IMPERATIVE CONSUMPTION-MECHANISM CLI parser distinct from R271 OCaml Cmdliner (`Term.(const fn $ arg $ arg)` APPLICATIVE-COMBINATOR, builds an immutable spec via `($)` operator), R325 OCaml stdlib Arg (`Arg.parse spec_list anonymous_fn usage_msg`, HANDLER-CALLBACK per flag with mutable side-effects in handlers), R331 OCaml Jane Street Core.Command (`Command.basic ~summary @@ let%map_open.Command x = required ... and y = required ... in fn x y`, PPX-extended Let-binding accumulator).  Clap's approach: GLOBAL MUTABLE PARSER STATE + IMPERATIVE CONSUMER CALLS (`Clap.flag`, `Clap.mandatory_string`, `Clap.optional_string`, `Clap.default_string`) that CONSUME from the global state in DECLARATION ORDER + `Clap.case + Clap.subcommand` for subcommand dispatch.  23 GH stars on rbardou/clap library; used by Tezos blockchain tools + mazeppa + several other OCaml CLIs.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SEVENTY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (7), Go (5), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), **OCaml 4 (Cmdliner R271 + stdlib Arg R325 + Jane Street Core.Command R331 + rbardou/Clap R339)**, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), Odin stdlib `core:flags` (R338), **OCaml 4 rbardou/Clap** (R339 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 78 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  OCaml NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries (Cmdliner + stdlib Arg + Jane Street Core.Command + rbardou/Clap).
  - Critical extraction note: mazeppa uses **OCaml `rbardou/Clap` imperative-consumption-mechanism CLI pattern** at main.ml:195-237 — `Clap.default_string ~long:"target-dir" ~description:"..." "target"` (returns STRING) / `Clap.flag ~set_long:"inspect" ~description:"..." false` (returns BOOL) / `Clap.mandatory_string ~long:"language" ~description:"..." ()` (returns STRING; ERRORS if missing) / `Clap.optional_string ~long:"dump-header-to" ~description:"..." ()` (returns STRING OPTION).  Clap maintains a GLOBAL MUTABLE PARSER STATE; each `Clap.flag`/`Clap.mandatory_string`/`Clap.optional_string`/`Clap.default_string` CONSUMES a flag from the state in DECLARATION ORDER.  After all consumers have run, `Clap.close` (or implicit close at program end) ERRORS if UNCONSUMED ARGS remain.  Distinct from R271 OCaml Cmdliner `Term.(const fn $ arg $ arg)` APPLICATIVE-COMBINATOR (no global state, builds immutable spec via `$` operator), R325 OCaml stdlib Arg `Arg.parse spec_list anon_fn usage` HANDLER-CALLBACK (per-flag handlers with mutable side-effects), R331 OCaml Jane Street `Command.basic ~summary @@ let%map_open.Command x = required ... in fn x` PPX-extended Let-binding accumulator (mutates a Type_constructor accumulator).  Clap's approach: GLOBAL MUTABLE PARSER STATE + IMPERATIVE CONSUMER CALLS in DECLARATION ORDER — corpus's **first documented imperative-consumption-mechanism-with-global-parser-state CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mazeppa uses **OCaml `rbardou/Clap` Clap.case + Clap.subcommand list-of-cases dispatch CLI pattern** at main.ml:240-269 — `[ (Clap.case "run" ~description:"Run the supercompiler." @@ fun () -> let target_dir = get_target_dir () in let inspect = get_inspect () in fun () -> supercompile { target_dir; inspect }); (Clap.case "translate" ~description:"..." @@ fun () -> let language = get_language () in ...); ... ] |> Clap.subcommand`.  `Clap.case "verb" ~description @@ fun () -> handler` is a CONTINUATION-PASSING HANDLER BUILDER: outer `fun () -> ...` is the SPEC-DECLARATION continuation (calls flag-getters that consume from the global parser state when the subcommand is selected); inner `fun () -> ...` returned by the spec is the RUN HANDLER (called by `Clap.subcommand` after all flag-getters succeed).  TWO-LEVEL CONTINUATION-PASSING: spec-declaration time + run time.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[derive(Subcommand)] enum` (enum-of-variants for subcommands), R322 JCommander `@Parameters(commandNames = ...)` (separate annotation), R270 Haskell `subparser (... command "verb" ...)` (Applicative subparser), R334 Go alecthomas/kong `Run Run kong:"cmd"` (Run METHOD on struct).  Clap's approach: TWO-LEVEL CONTINUATION-PASSING HANDLER BUILDER + LIST-OF-CASES dispatch via `Clap.subcommand` — corpus's **first documented two-level-continuation-passing-handler-builder CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mazeppa uses **OCaml `rbardou/Clap` mandatory-vs-optional-vs-default-vs-flag arity-by-type CLI pattern** at main.ml:195-237 — `Clap.default_string ~long ~description default_value` returns STRING (always succeeds, default applies if missing) / `Clap.flag ~set_long ~description default_bool` returns BOOL (always succeeds) / `Clap.mandatory_string ~long ~description ()` returns STRING (ERRORS if missing — `()` unit terminator marks "no default") / `Clap.optional_string ~long ~description ()` returns `STRING OPTION` (Some on present, None on missing).  Clap encodes the FLAG'S ARITY/REQUIREDNESS in the FUNCTION NAME (mandatory/optional/default/flag) rather than via a separate `~required:true` keyword argument or `~default:None/Some _` keyword.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive `#[arg(required = true)]` / `#[arg(default_value_t = ...)]` (attribute key-value), R322 JCommander `@Parameter(required = true)`, R270 Haskell `strArgument` vs `strOption` (positional vs flag, no required/optional/default distinction in name), R311 Guile `getopt-long` (single API, requirements expressed via assoc-list pairs).  Clap's approach: ARITY/REQUIREDNESS encoded in CONSUMER FUNCTION NAME — corpus's **first documented arity-by-function-name CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_pike` (DUP with existing _pike in corpus — JamesWoolfenden/pike IAM policy generator already uses the `pike` name slot; would also conflict with Pike language interpreter), `_carp` (DUP with existing _carp in corpus), `_fennel` (DUP with existing _fennel in corpus).  Investigated Pike language interpreter (pikelang/Pike using `Getopt.find_all_options` in `lib/master.pike.in` — full Pike `pike` binary CLI: `-V/--compat`, `-v/--version`, `--dumpversion`, `-h/--help`, `--features`, `--info`, `-e/--execute`, `--debug-without`, `-E/--preprocess`, `-M/--module-path`, `-I/--include-path`, `-P/--program-path`, `--show-paths`, `-w/--warnings`, `-W/--woff/--no-warnings`, `--autoreload`, `-m`, `--compiler-trace`, `--assembler-debug`, `--optimizer-debug`, `--debug`, `--trace`, `-x` — Pike's `Getopt` library uses LIST-OF-`({name, kind, flag_string_or_list})` SPECS where `kind` is `Getopt.NO_ARG`/`Getopt.HAS_ARG`/`Getopt.MAY_HAVE_ARG`; full Pike Getopt library would be 79th taxonomy variant — HOLD FOR FUTURE ROUND when `_pike` slot is freed).  Probed PicoLisp (small ecosystem, no production user-facing CLI tools beyond pil itself), MoonBit (too new, no real CLI tools yet), Lua mpeterv/argparse (no clean R285-vs-new-Lua delta available).  Considered hirrolot/mazeppa: 478 GH stars (production supercompiler), 4 subcommands (`run` + `translate` + `check` + `eval`) + 5 flags across subcommands + EVERY MAJOR rbardou/Clap PATTERN (Clap.default_string / Clap.flag / Clap.mandatory_string / Clap.optional_string + Clap.case continuation-passing + Clap.subcommand list-dispatch), full bin/main.ml exercising every major rbardou/Clap primitive (IMPERATIVE CONSUMPTION via GLOBAL PARSER STATE + arity-by-function-name + two-level continuation-passing subcommand handlers + list-of-cases dispatch).  Chose mazeppa: real production OCaml binary using OCaml's `rbardou/Clap` library — 4th OCaml CLI library after Cmdliner + stdlib Arg + Jane Street Core.Command; OCaml NOW HAS FOUR documented CLI libraries.
  - Blacklist update: `mazeppa` added.  Corpus count: 28,761 → 28,762 (+1).

- **R338 — odinfmt source-direct (Odin code formatter shipped with ols Odin Language Server; Odin stdlib `core:flags` package)** (1 file new) — pivots from Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337 clogs) into the modern systems-language ecosystem to document the FIRST Odin CLI library — corpus's **first documented Odin entry**.  odinfmt is the canonical Odin code formatter, shipped as part of DanielGavin/ols (the Odin Language Server) at 996 GH stars.  Every Odin developer installs odinfmt alongside the LSP for editor-integration formatting.  Reads `odinfmt.json` config + formats Odin source files via ols's internal `src:odin/format` + `src:odin/printer` modules.  Sister tools: gofmt (Go formatter, stock), rustfmt (Rust formatter, stock), clang-format (C/C++ formatter), zigfmt (Zig formatter built into `zig fmt`).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Odin entry**.  Odin is Ginger Bill's (Bill Hall's) systems language for game development and high-performance code — STATIC TYPING + RTTI (runtime type information) + NO GC + EXPLICIT CONTEXT + DISTINCT-INTEGER-TYPES (no implicit conversions) + STRUCT-PACKING + SIMD-FIRST-CLASS.  Compiler at github.com/odin-lang/Odin (~7300 stars, written in C++).  Adds Odin to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: Odin's stdlib `core:flags` package is RTTI-DRIVEN — an anonymous-or-named STRUCT with FIELDS ANNOTATED VIA BACKTICK-DELIMITED STRUCT TAGS (`args:"name=X,pos=N,manifold=K"` + `usage:"..."`) becomes the spec, and `flags.parse(&args, os.args[1:], .Unix)` POPULATES THE STRUCT IN-PLACE via Odin's RUNTIME TYPE INFORMATION (RTTI walks struct fields via `reflect` package primitives).  Two parsing styles supported: `.Odin` (`-flag`, `-flag:value`, `-map:key=value`) + `.Unix` (`-flag` or `--flag`, `--flag=arg`, `--flag arg`).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 77.
  - `_odinfmt` (1-stem; DanielGavin/ols tools/odinfmt/main.odin + odin-lang/Odin core/flags/{parsing,doc}.odin; **Odin stdlib `core:flags` package** — `import "core:flags"` brings the flags namespace into scope; **3 documented flags (2 boolean + 1 single-value) + 1 positional pos=0 + RTTI-DRIVEN ANONYMOUS-STRUCT-AS-SPEC + BACKTICK-DELIMITED STRUCT TAGS DSL (`args:"name=X,pos=N,manifold=K"` + `usage:"..."`) + DUAL `flags.parse(&args, slice, .Style)` + `flags.parse_or_exit(&args, slice)` ENTRY POINTS + Parsing_Style ENUM (`.Odin` vs `.Unix`) + DISCRIMINATED-UNION error return (`Parse_Error | Open_File_Error | Help_Request | Validation_Error`) + `flags.write_usage(stream, type, prog_name, style)` AUTO-USAGE-PRINT + IN-PLACE STRUCT MUTATION via RTTI**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `tools/odinfmt/main.odin` line 3 (`import "core:flags"` — Odin IMPORT bringing core:flags namespace into scope; `core:` is Odin's first-party stdlib path prefix)
    * `tools/odinfmt/main.odin` lines 14-19 (`Args :: struct { write: bool `args:"name=w" usage:"write the new format to file"`, stdin: bool `usage:"formats code from standard input"`, path: string `args:"pos=0" usage:"set the file or directory to format"`, config: string `usage:"path to a config file"` }` — NAMED-STRUCT-TYPE-AS-SPEC with BACKTICK-DELIMITED STRUCT TAGS; each field declares a flag via `args:"..."` and `usage:"..."` tags)
    * `tools/odinfmt/main.odin` line 42 (`flags.parse_or_exit(&args, os.args)` — CONVENIENCE PARSE ENTRY POINT that on error PRINTS USAGE + EXITS, vs the explicit `flags.parse(&args, slice, .Style) -> Error` that returns an error union for caller-handled dispatch)
    * `core/flags/parsing.odin` lines 5-11 (`Parsing_Style :: enum { Odin, Unix }` — Odin's stdlib offers TWO parsing styles; `.Odin` accepts `-flag`, `-flag:value`, `-map:key=value`; `.Unix` accepts `-flag`/`--flag`, `--flag=value`, `--flag value (manifold value...)`)
    * `core/flags/parsing.odin` lines 36-46 (`parse :: proc(model: ^$T, args: []string, style: Parsing_Style = .Odin, validate_args: bool = true, strict: bool = true, allocator := context.allocator, loc := #caller_location) -> (error: Error)` — generic-over-`$T` parse procedure; takes POINTER TO MODEL STRUCT + SLICE OF ARGS + STYLE; returns Error UNION discriminated by parse phase)
    * `core/flags/parsing.odin` lines 50-95 (`switch style { case .Odin: for arg in args { error = parse_one_odin_arg(model, &parser, arg); ... } case .Unix: future_args: int; current_flag: string; for i := 0; i < len(args); i += 1 { future_args, current_flag, error = parse_one_unix_arg(model, &parser, arg); for ; future_args > 0; future_args -= 1 { i += 1; ... } } }` — dispatches on STYLE enum to two different ARG-WALKING LOOPS; Unix style supports MULTI-VALUE FLAGS via `manifold=N` future-args counter)
    * `Samudevv/runic` runic.odin lines 49-79 (REFERENCE example of FULL ERROR-UNION SWITCH dispatch: `if flags_err := flags.parse(&args, os.args[1:], .Unix); flags_err != nil { switch e in flags_err { case flags.Parse_Error: fmt.eprintfln("{}: {}", e.reason, e.message); os.exit(1); case flags.Open_File_Error: ...; case flags.Help_Request: flags.write_usage(...); os.exit(0); case flags.Validation_Error: ... } panic("unreachable") }` — shows the 4-variant error union with PER-VARIANT HANDLING; odinfmt itself uses `parse_or_exit` convenience that does this internally)
  - Critical extraction note: odinfmt uses **Odin stdlib `core:flags` package** at main.odin:3 — corpus's **first documented Odin entry**.  Odin is Ginger Bill's (Bill Hall's) systems language designed for game development and high-performance code — STATIC TYPING + RTTI + NO GC + EXPLICIT CONTEXT + DISTINCT-INTEGER-TYPES + STRUCT-PACKING + SIMD-FIRST-CLASS.  Compiler at github.com/odin-lang/Odin (~7300 stars).  core:flags is Odin's FIRST-PARTY CLI parser — distinct from go-style `flag.String("name", "default", "usage")` BUILDER (R332 commander.cr also has builder), distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive PROC-MACRO (compile-time codegen), distinct from R322 Java JCommander ANNOTATION + RUNTIME REFLECTION (Java's runtime reflection model is fundamentally different — JVM ClassReflection vs Odin RTTI), distinct from R334 Go alecthomas/kong STRUCT-TAG-BASED (closest analog — but kong uses Go's `reflect.StructTag` text DSL whereas Odin's core:flags uses Odin's NATIVE-RTTI on struct tag strings).  Odin's approach: RTTI-DRIVEN STRUCT-TAG declarative API with TWO PARSING STYLES (Odin-native `-flag:value` + UNIX `-flag value`) + DISCRIMINATED-UNION ERROR RETURN (`Parse_Error | Open_File_Error | Help_Request | Validation_Error`) + IN-PLACE STRUCT MUTATION.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SEVENTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (7), Go (5), Python (3), Crystal 3, Haskell (R270), OCaml 3, C (R272), Java 3, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 2, Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 2, Erlang 2, Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Clojure 2, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 2, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal 2, Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo 2, Chicken Scheme `args` egg (R324), Lean 4 Cli (R326), Bigloo Scheme `args-parse` macro (R327), Rust 7 bpaf (R330), Crystal 3 commander.cr (R332), Go 5 alecthomas/kong (R334), Modula-2 stdlib `Arguments` (R335), Janet bakpakin/spork `argparse` (R336), Inko stdlib `std.optparse` (R337), **Odin stdlib `core:flags`** (R338 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 77 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Odin added to the documented-CLI-language set.
  - Critical extraction note: odinfmt uses **Odin `core:flags` RTTI-driven struct-tag declarative CLI pattern** at main.odin:14-19 — `Args :: struct { write: bool `args:"name=w" usage:"write the new format to file"`, stdin: bool `usage:"formats code from standard input"`, path: string `args:"pos=0" usage:"set the file or directory to format"`, config: string `usage:"path to a config file"` }`.  Odin's BACKTICK-DELIMITED STRUCT TAGS encode flag metadata as TEXT DSL inside backticks; `core:flags` USES `reflect.struct_field_by_name` + `reflect.struct_tag_lookup` (Odin RTTI primitives) to walk fields at PARSE TIME.  TAG KEYS: `name=X` (long flag name), `pos=N` (positional argument at index N), `manifold=K` (flag takes K values), `required` (must be supplied), `indistinct` (allows abbreviations).  TAG `usage="..."` provides help text.  WHEN `args:"..."` IS OMITTED, flag name DEFAULTS TO FIELD NAME.  Distinct from R275 Rust clap-derive `#[arg(...)]` (proc-macro compile-time codegen — codegen runs during `cargo build`), R322 Java JCommander `@Parameter(names = "...")` (annotation, RUNTIME Java reflection — JVM reflection model), R334 Go alecthomas/kong `kong:"name='X',cmd"` (Go struct tag, RUNTIME Go reflection — `reflect.StructTag.Get`).  Odin's approach: BACKTICK STRUCT TAGS + RTTI INTROSPECTION at PARSE TIME — corpus's **first documented backtick-struct-tag-with-RTTI-introspection CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: odinfmt uses **Odin `core:flags` dual-parse-entry-point CLI pattern** at main.odin:42 — `flags.parse_or_exit(&args, os.args)` vs the explicit `flags.parse(&args, os.args[1:], .Unix) -> Error`.  core:flags provides TWO PARSE ENTRY POINTS: (1) `parse_or_exit` is the CONVENIENCE wrapper that on error PRINTS USAGE to stderr + CALLS `os.exit(1)` (no caller code required, useful for simple CLIs); (2) `parse(&args, slice, .Style)` returns an `Error` UNION for CALLER-HANDLED DISPATCH (4-variant union: `Parse_Error | Open_File_Error | Help_Request | Validation_Error`).  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (only `Cli::parse()` panics on error), R322 JCommander (throws `ParameterException`), R333 Mojo argmojo `DecimoArgs.parse_from_command(cmd^)` (single return type).  Odin's approach: DUAL ENTRY POINTS for CONVENIENCE vs EXPLICIT-ERROR-HANDLING — corpus's **first documented dual-parse-entry-point CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: odinfmt uses **Odin `core:flags` dual-parsing-style CLI pattern** at parsing.odin:5-11 — `Parsing_Style :: enum { Odin, Unix }`.  core:flags supports TWO PARSING STYLES: `.Odin` style (`-flag`, `-flag:value`, `-map:key=value`) is Odin's NATIVE FLAG SYNTAX matching the Odin compiler's own CLI; `.Unix` style (`-flag` or `--flag`, `--flag=value`, `--flag value`, `--flag v1 v2 ...` for manifold) matches getopt-long conventions.  Programs CHOOSE STYLE at parse-call time.  odinfmt itself uses `parse_or_exit` which defaults to `.Odin` style.  Distinct from R272 C getopt (one style only — GNU getopt-long), R275 clap-derive (one style only — GNU long+short), R290 Racket `command-line` (Racket-specific style, no toggle), R322 JCommander (Java style, no toggle), R311 Guile `getopt-long` (single style).  Odin's approach: RUNTIME-CHOSEN PARSING STYLE via enum argument — corpus's **first documented runtime-chosen-parsing-style CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: odinfmt uses **Odin `core:flags` discriminated-union-error-return CLI pattern** (from runic.odin reference at parsing.odin:46) — `flags.parse` returns `Error` which is a UNION of 4 PHASE-SPECIFIC error types: `Parse_Error` (with `.reason` + `.message`), `Open_File_Error` (with `.filename` + `.errno`), `Help_Request` (signaled when user passes `-help`), `Validation_Error` (with `.message`).  Caller dispatches via Odin's `switch e in flags_err { case flags.Parse_Error: ...; case flags.Open_File_Error: ...; case flags.Help_Request: ...; case flags.Validation_Error: ... }` PATTERN-MATCH-ON-UNION-VARIANT syntax.  `Help_Request` IS-AN-ERROR-VARIANT (not a special separate flow) — this lets callers detect `-help` invocation as the same control flow path as parse errors.  Distinct from R275 clap-derive (`clap::Error` single type with kind enum), R322 JCommander throws `ParameterException` (exception, not return value), R311 Guile (raises exception via `error`), R334 Go alecthomas/kong (calls `ctx.FatalIfErrorf(err)` rather than returning typed errors).  Odin's approach: DISCRIMINATED-UNION ERROR RETURN with HELP-AS-ERROR-VARIANT — corpus's **first documented discriminated-union-error-return-with-help-as-error-variant CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_runic` (Samudevv/runic 88 stars, C-ABI bindings generator — would be SISTER demo exercising `flags.parse` + `.Unix` style + full 4-variant error-union switch dispatch; HOLD FOR FUTURE ROUND), `_hephaistos` (Znarf64/hephaistos 27 stars — Hephaistos Shading Lang driver, narrow), `_draupnir` (odin-arsenal/draupnir 3 stars — project scaffold, narrow), `_crux` (FourteenBrush/crux 3 stars — WIP Minecraft server, narrow), `_callisto` (Bazzagibbs/callisto — editor with narrow surface), `_maker` (FuriouZz/maker — build tool with narrow surface).  Considered `_ols` (the Odin Language Server binary itself — uses internal arg handling for `--stdio` protocol, NOT a CLI tool per se, used by editors).  Probed `_carp` (Carp Lisp compiler — Haskell source NOT Odin), `_inko` (Inko language driver — Rust source, COVERED via R337 std.optparse).  Chose odinfmt: 996 GH stars on ols repo (canonical Odin formatter installed by EVERY Odin developer alongside the LSP), 4 documented flags (1 short-name `-w` + 2 long-only `-stdin`/`-config` + 1 positional `pos=0`) using Odin's `core:flags` RTTI-DRIVEN STRUCT-TAG declarative API + `flags.parse_or_exit(&args, os.args)` CONVENIENCE PARSE ENTRY POINT, full `Args` struct exercising EVERY MAJOR core:flags PATTERN (RTTI-driven struct-tag spec, dual parse entry points `parse` vs `parse_or_exit`, dual parsing styles `.Odin` vs `.Unix`, discriminated-union error return with help-as-error-variant), real production formatter that ships with ols and is editor-integrated by every Odin developer.
  - Blacklist update: `odinfmt` added.  Corpus count: 28,760 → 28,761 (+1).

- **R337 — clogs source-direct (Git-trailer changelog generator in Inko; Inko stdlib `std.optparse` module)** (1 file new) — pivots from Janet bakpakin/spork (R336 jwno) into the modern-concurrent-language ecosystem to document the FIRST Inko CLI library — corpus's **first documented Inko entry**.  clogs is a changelog generator authored by Yorick Peterse, the creator of the Inko language himself.  Reads conventional-commit-style trailers from `git log` output, groups them under markdown changelog sections, and emits a release entry to stdout.  25 GH stars.  Sister tools: git-cliff (Rust changelog tool, in corpus), conventional-changelog (Node, in corpus), towncrier (Python, in corpus), git-chglog (Go, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Inko entry**.  Inko is Yorick Peterse's language for building CONCURRENT software with confidence — uses LIGHTWEIGHT PROCESSES + DETERMINISTIC MEMORY MANAGEMENT (no GC) + STATIC TYPING + ALGEBRAIC EFFECTS-ish error handling via `Result[T, E]`.  1277 GH stars on the inko-lang/inko compiler repo.  Adds Inko to the cross-language CLI taxonomy: Inko's stdlib `std.optparse` module is an OPINIONATED, LIGHTWEIGHT getopts-inspired parser with FULL UNICODE OPTION SUPPORT (`--é` is a valid long flag, `-å` is a valid short flag via extended grapheme clusters).  API: `Options.new(name)` constructor + `opts.flag(short, long, desc)` for boolean flags + `opts.single(short, long, value_name, desc)` for single-value options + `opts.multiple(short, long, value_name, desc)` for repeatable options + `opts.parse(args) -> Result[Matches, Error]` + `matches.contains?(name)` (predicate-with-question-mark naming convention) + `matches.value(name) -> Option[String]` + `matches.values(name) -> Array[String]` + `matches.remaining -> Array[String]` (positional args left over).  Extends cross-language CLI library taxonomy variant count to 76.
  - `_clogs` (1-stem; yorickpeterse/clogs src/clogs.inko + inko-lang/inko std/src/std/optparse.inko; **Inko stdlib `std.optparse` module** — `import std.optparse (Options)` selective-import the `Options` type; **4 documented flags (2 boolean + 2 single-value) + `-h/--help` short+long + `-v/--version` short+long + `-c/--config CONFIG` single-value with VALUE-PLACEHOLDER METADATA + `-e/--end REF` single-value with VALUE-PLACEHOLDER METADATA + 2 positional dispatch verbs (`init` + `X.Y.Z` semver) + `Options.new('clogs')` PROGRAM-NAME-IN-CONSTRUCTOR + `opts.usage =` / `opts.description =` FIELD ASSIGNMENT for help-text metadata + `opts.parse(arguments) -> Result[Matches, Error]` RESULT-RETURNING PARSE + `matches.contains?(name)` PREDICATE-WITH-QUESTION-MARK + `matches.value(name) -> Option[String]` OPTION-RETURNING ACCESSOR + `matches.remaining.get(0)` POSITIONAL-ARGS via separate Matches accessor**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/clogs.inko` line 7 (`import std.optparse (Options)` — Inko SELECTIVE IMPORT bringing `Options` type into scope from stdlib `std.optparse` module)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 21-22 (`let opts = Options.new('clogs'); opts.usage = '[OPTIONS] [VERSION | COMMAND]'` — `Options.new` constructor with PROGRAM NAME; `opts.usage` FIELD ASSIGNMENT for usage line)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 23-27 (`opts.description = 'Examples\n  clogs 1.2.3   # ...'` — `opts.description` FIELD for examples block; multi-line via Inko's regular string literal)
    * `src/clogs.inko` line 28 (`opts.flag('h', 'help', 'Show this help message')` — `opts.flag(short, long, desc)` registers BOOLEAN FLAG with short and long names + description string)
    * `src/clogs.inko` line 29 (`opts.flag('v', 'version', 'Show the version')` — second boolean flag)
    * `src/clogs.inko` line 30 (`opts.single('c', 'config', 'CONFIG', 'Use the specified configuration file')` — `opts.single(short, long, value_name, desc)` registers SINGLE-VALUE OPTION; VALUE_NAME shows as placeholder in auto-generated help — e.g. `-c, --config CONFIG`)
    * `src/clogs.inko` line 31 (`opts.single('e', 'end', 'REF', 'The end of the commit range (default: HEAD)')` — fourth flag, single-value)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 33-36 (`let matches = match opts.parse(arguments) { case Ok(v) -> v; case Error(e) -> throw e.to_string }` — `opts.parse(args)` returns `Result[Matches, Error]`; CALLER PATTERN-MATCHES on Result with `match ... case Ok(v) ... case Error(e) ...`)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 38-42 (`if matches.contains?('help') { let _ = output.write(opts.to_string); return Result.Ok(nil) }` — `matches.contains?` PREDICATE WITH QUESTION-MARK NAMING CONVENTION; `opts.to_string` returns AUTO-GENERATED HELP STRING)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 49-51 (`let config_path = matches.value('config').map(fn (v) { v.to_path }).or_else(fn { working_directory.join(CONFIG_FILE) })` — `matches.value(name)` returns `Option[String]`; CALLER CHAINS `.map(fn (v) { v.to_path })` to convert to Path + `.or_else(fn { ... })` for default)
    * `src/clogs.inko` lines 53-67 (`match matches.remaining.get(0) { case Ok('init') -> ... case Ok(val) -> match Version.parse(val) { ... } case _ -> ... }` — `matches.remaining` is `Array[String]` of POSITIONAL ARGS LEFT OVER; `.get(0)` returns `Result[String, OutOfBounds]`)
