  - Critical extraction note: gdfuse uses **OCaml `Arg.Set`/`Arg.Set_string`/`Arg.Set_int`/`Arg.Unit`/`Arg.String` type-specific spec variants** at gdfuseCli.ml:80-145 — OCaml's `Arg.spec` is a SUM TYPE with one constructor per type: `Arg.Set ref_bool` (set bool to true), `Arg.Clear ref_bool` (set bool to false), `Arg.Set_string ref_str` (bind next argv to ref), `Arg.Set_int ref_int` (bind next argv to int ref), `Arg.Set_float ref_float`, `Arg.Unit (fun () -> ...)` (call thunk on match), `Arg.String (fun s -> ...)` (call fn with string arg), `Arg.Int (fun i -> ...)`, `Arg.Float (fun f -> ...)`, `Arg.Bool (fun b -> ...)`, `Arg.Rest (fun s -> ...)` (call fn with all remaining args), `Arg.Symbol (sym_list, fn)` (constrained enum), `Arg.Tuple (spec_list)` (consume multiple args).  Distinct from R322 JCommander (reflection-based type detection — single annotation), R270 Haskell `ReqArg ctor` (constructor variant with arg-string).  OCaml's approach: SEPARATE Arg.SPEC_VARIANT per primitive type — corpus's **first documented type-specific-Set-variant CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gdfuse uses **OCaml `ref` mutable cells as parser binding targets** at gdfuseCli.ml:23-45 — `let show_version = ref false in` creates a MUTABLE REFERENCE CELL.  OCaml is immutable-by-default; `ref` is the explicit opt-in for mutability.  Each parsed value lives in its own ref cell, accessed via `!show_version` and `show_version := true`.  Distinct from R304 Ada `aliased` keyword (explicit pointer opt-in), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (no opt-in — `config const` naturally mutable at parse time), R322 JCommander (annotated field).  OCaml's approach: REF CELLS as the binding target for parser output — corpus's **first documented ref-cell-as-parser-target CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gdfuse uses **OCaml `Arg.parse_argv ~current ARGV SPEC ANON_FUN USAGE` with anon-fun callback for positionals** at gdfuseCli.ml:148-151 — `Arg.parse_argv ~current:(ref 0) argv arg_specs (fun s -> mountpoint := s) usage`.  The 4th argument is the ANON-FUN CALLBACK called for EACH POSITIONAL ARG.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (bare `@Parameter`-as-positional with no `names`), R311 Guile `(option-ref options '() #f)` (empty-list-key lookup), R313 Idris 2 `ReturnInOrder ctor` argOrder policy, R270 Haskell returns `[String]` of non-options.  OCaml's approach: ANON-FUN CALLBACK invoked once per positional — corpus's **first documented anon-fun-callback-for-positionals CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gdfuse uses **OCaml `Arg.align` spec-list-help-alignment CLI pattern** at gdfuseCli.ml:79 — `Arg.align [...]` is a HELPER that takes a list of `(NAME, SPEC, HELP)` tuples and ALIGNS the help text columns for nicely-formatted output.  Distinct from R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (help_text passed as full pre-formatted string), R311 Guile `usageInfo` (auto-formats from spec), R317 Forth handwritten `." ... " cr`.  OCaml's `Arg.align` is a PRE-PROCESSING pass over the spec list that pads each help entry — corpus's **first documented spec-list-help-alignment CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gdfuse uses **OCaml `Arg.Help` and `Arg.Bad` exception-based control flow** at gdfuseCli.ml:184-186 — `try ... with | Arg.Help message -> Help message | Arg.Bad message -> Error message`.  `Arg.Help` is raised when the user passes `-help` or `--help`; `Arg.Bad` is raised on parse errors or via user code (e.g. `raise (Arg.Bad "Invalid mount option")`).  Distinct from R311 Guile (no exceptions — alist return), R313 Idris 2 (Result discriminated union), R322 JCommander `CmdLineException` (custom exception).  OCaml's approach: STANDARD EXCEPTIONS as control-flow signals — corpus's **first documented Arg.Help-and-Arg.Bad-exception CLI pattern**.

Probed `ocaml-community/omd` (162 stars, OCaml Markdown library — but omd has been refactored and now uses Cmdliner not Arg; STRONG alternative if Arg switched), `vult-dsp/vult` (538 stars, DSP transcompiler — uses Arg but spec spread across multiple files), `maranget/hevea` (118 stars, LaTeX-to-HTML — also uses Arg but smaller surface), `pirapira/bamboo` (324 stars, Ethereum language — narrow CLI), `astrada/google-drive-ocamlfuse` (CHOSEN — 5932 stars, one of the most popular OCaml end-user tools, has 23-option clean Arg spec in single gdfuseCli.ml file).  Chose gdfuse: 5932 GH stars (one of the most popular OCaml tools), 23 options in clean single 188-line gdfuseCli.ml exercising EVERY MAJOR OCaml Arg PATTERN (ref-cell binding, Arg.align help-alignment, Arg.Set/Arg.Set_string/Arg.Set_int type-specific variants, Arg.Unit/Arg.String callback variants for custom parsing, Arg.parse_argv with anon-fun callback for positionals, exception-based Arg.Help/Arg.Bad control flow), real binary distributed via OPAM + Homebrew + Linux distros, used by thousands of users to mount Google Drive on Linux/macOS.

- **R324 — massmine source-direct (social-media data collector in Chicken Scheme; `args` egg)** (1 file new) — pivots from Java args4j (R323 amidst) into the Chicken Scheme ecosystem — corpus's **first documented Chicken Scheme CLI library** + **third documented Scheme dialect CLI library** (joining R290 Racket `racket/cmdline` and R311 Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)`).  massmine is "Your Access To Data" — a CLI tool for collecting data from social media APIs (Twitter, Reddit, Wikipedia, Tumblr, Web, YouTube).  Authored by Nicholas M. Van Horn & Aaron Beveridge (academic project for digital-humanities researchers).  73 GH stars.  Sister tools: snscrape (Python social-network scraper, in corpus), twint (Twitter scraper, in corpus), vader-sentiment-analyzer (Python NLP tools).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Chicken Scheme CLI library** — adds the `args` egg (Chicken's most popular CLI parser; UNQUOTED-SYMBOL flag-name pairs that auto-expand to FOUR variants per flag (`-h`/`-help`/`--h`/`--help`); `#:none`/`#:required`/`#:optional` Scheme-keyword-as-arg-shape; embedded BODY THUNK per option that runs at match time; `(make-parameter VALUE)` parameter objects as CLI state holders; `(import egg-name)` flat-egg-namespace import) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (63 library variants now).  Scheme dialects now THREE: Racket + Guile + Chicken.
  - `_massmine` (1-stem; n3mo/massmine massmine.scm; **Chicken Scheme `args` egg** — Chicken's most popular CLI library, installable via `chicken-install args`; **17 named options + `(SHORT_SYM LONG_SYM)` UNQUOTED-SYMBOL pair flag spec auto-expanding to FOUR variants per flag + `#:none`/`#:required`/`#:optional` keyword-as-arg-shape + EMBEDDED BODY THUNK per option (executed when option matches) + `(make-parameter VALUE)` parameter objects as CLI state holders + R7RS-style `(import (chicken file) ...)` sublibrary imports + flat-egg-namespace `(import args ...)` import + `args:usage opts` auto-help generator**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `massmine.scm` line 1 (`#! /usr/bin/env csi -s` — Chicken Scheme SHEBANG via `csi` (Chicken Scheme Interpreter), `-s` invokes script mode)
    * `massmine.scm` lines 27-31 (`(import (chicken file) (chicken process-context) ...) ... (import args clucker oauth-client srfi-13 srfi-19 pathname-expand utf8)` — Chicken Scheme R7RS-STYLE IMPORTS with `(chicken SUBLIB)` for stdlib SUBMODULES and plain `egg-name` for installed eggs)
    * `massmine.scm` lines 37-53 (`(define do-splash? (make-parameter #t))` + `(define output-to-file? (make-parameter #f))` etc. — Chicken Scheme PARAMETER PATTERN; `(make-parameter INITIAL_VALUE)` creates a PARAMETER OBJECT — a function that returns the current value when called without args, and sets it when called with one arg)
    * `massmine.scm` lines 119-160 (`(define opts (list (args:make-option (h help) #:none "Help information" (massmine-help options)) (args:make-option (v version) #:none "Version information" (print-version)) (args:make-option (p project) #:required "Create project" (create-project-directory)) (args:make-option (a auth) #:required "Credentials file" (custom-cred-path #t)) ...))` — `args:make-option` is the Chicken `args` egg's option constructor; each spec is `(args:make-option (SHORT_SYM LONG_SYM) ARG_KEYWORD HELP_TEXT BODY_THUNK)`)
    * `massmine.scm` line 122 (`(args:make-option (h help) #:none "Help information" (massmine-help options))` — `#:none` is the KEYWORD ARGUMENT for "no value" (boolean flag); other keywords: `#:required` (mandatory arg), `#:optional` (optional arg))
    * `massmine.scm` line 156 (`(args:make-option (no-splash) #:none "Inhibit splash screen" (do-splash? #f))` — SINGLE-NAME variant; when ONLY ONE symbol is in the list, the egg generates ONLY the long form `--no-splash`)
    * `massmine.scm` line 167 (`(print (args:usage opts))` — auto-generated USAGE text from the opts list; `args:usage` walks the option specs and emits a formatted help table)
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken Scheme `args` egg** at massmine.scm:31 + 119 — corpus's **first documented Chicken Scheme CLI library** + **third documented Scheme dialect CLI library** (joining R290 Racket `racket/cmdline` and R311 Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)`).  The `args` egg is Chicken Scheme's most popular CLI argument parser, installable via `chicken-install args`.  Distinct from R290 Racket `racket/cmdline` (hygienic macro DSL), R311 Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)` (quoted-assoc-list spec), R313 Idris 2 `System.GetOpts` (Haskell-port `MkOpt` constructor list).  `args` egg uses `(args:make-option (SHORT LONG) #:keyword HELP_TEXT BODY)` — UNQUOTED SYMBOLS for short/long names + KEYWORD ARGUMENT for arg-shape + BODY THUNK that runs when the option matches.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SIXTY-THREE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java 1 picocli (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), **Racket racket/cmdline (R290)**, Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), **Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311)**, Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo `alias`-as-spec (R321), Java 2 JCommander (R322), Java 3 args4j (R323), **Chicken Scheme `args` egg** (R324 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 63 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Scheme dialects now THREE: Racket + Guile + Chicken.
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken `args:make-option (SHORT_SYM LONG_SYM) #:KEYWORD HELP_TEXT BODY_THUNK` CLI pattern** at massmine.scm:119-160 — `(args:make-option (h help) #:none "Help" (massmine-help options))`.  The `(h help)` is a list of UNQUOTED SYMBOLS giving short and long names; `#:none` is the KEYWORD ARGUMENT for arg-shape; the trailing form is the BODY THUNK executed when the option matches.  Distinct from R322 JCommander `@Parameter(names = arrayOf("-X", "--long"))` (annotation), R311 Guile `((NAME (single-char #\X) (value #t/#f)))` (quoted alist), R270 Haskell `Option [Char] [String]` (typed constructor list).  `args` egg's CALL-SITE EXPRESSION FORM with EMBEDDED BODY THUNK — corpus's **first documented embedded-body-thunk-per-option CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken `args` egg four-variants-per-flag-from-two-symbols** at massmine.scm:122-160 — `(h help)` automatically generates FOUR command-line variants: `-h`, `-help`, `--h`, `--help`.  The egg synthesizes all four from the two-symbol list.  Distinct from R322 JCommander `arrayOf("-X", "--long")` (explicit listing), R309 SML `short = "vV"` (multi-char string per short only), R311 Guile `((NAME (single-char #\X)))` (one short + one long).  `args` egg's approach: AUTO-EXPANSION from two-symbol list to all four dash-prefix variants — corpus's **first documented four-variant-per-flag-from-two-symbols CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken `args` egg `#:none`/`#:required`/`#:optional` keyword-as-arg-shape** at massmine.scm:122-160 — `#:none` (boolean flag), `#:required` (mandatory arg), `#:optional` (optional arg).  Distinct from R311 Guile `(value #t/#f/optional)` (also keyword-style but uses BOOLEANS for the arg-required state), R313 Idris 2 `NoArg/ReqArg/OptArg` constructors (typed variants), R270 Haskell `NoArg/ReqArg/OptArg` (same as Idris 2), R304 Ada postfix-marker `-X:`/`-X?`.  Chicken `args` egg's approach: SCHEME KEYWORD ARGUMENTS (`#:KEYWORD`) for arg-shape — corpus's **first documented keyword-as-arg-shape CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken Scheme `(make-parameter VALUE)` parameter objects for CLI state** at massmine.scm:37-53 — `(define do-splash? (make-parameter #t))` creates a PARAMETER OBJECT.  `(do-splash?)` reads the value; `(do-splash? #f)` sets it.  Used as the CLI state holders that option bodies mutate.  Distinct from R290 Racket `(parameterize)` blocks (dynamic extent), R311 Guile (uses plain variables), R313 Idris 2 (uses records).  Chicken's approach: PARAMETER OBJECTS as STATE HOLDERS that combine reading and writing through the same function — corpus's **first documented parameter-objects-as-CLI-state pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: massmine uses **Chicken Scheme `(import egg-name)` R7RS-style egg-as-library import** at massmine.scm:27-31 — `(import args clucker oauth-client srfi-13 srfi-19 pathname-expand utf8)` imports CHICKEN EGGS (Chicken's package format) by plain name.  No version pinning at import site — eggs are installed system-wide via `chicken-install egg-name`.  Distinct from R270 Haskell `import Data.Foo` (module paths), R311 Guile `(use-modules (ice-9 getopt-long))` (R7RS sublibrary notation for stdlib), R272 C `#include <header.h>`.  Chicken's approach: FLAT EGG NAMESPACE accessible via `(import EGG_NAME)` — corpus's **first documented flat-egg-namespace CLI import pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_csi` (Chicken Scheme interpreter — in corpus + blacklisted), `_chicken-install` (Chicken egg installer, in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `bintracker/bintracker` (441 stars, chiptune music workstation — but bintracker is GUI-focused, the args egg usage in schemta-exe.scm is a build-tool helper; smaller CLI), `register-dynamics/orc` (7 stars, Open Registers Compendium — narrow CLI), `1dotd4/git-overview` (3 stars, git overview tool), `josebaezmedina/sls` (0 stars).  Chose massmine: 73 GH stars (academic data-collection tool), 17 named options + Chicken-args-egg `(h help)` 4-variant auto-expansion, single 200-line massmine.scm exercising EVERY MAJOR Chicken `args` egg PATTERN (`csi -s` script shebang, R7RS-style `(import (chicken file) ...)` sublibrary imports + flat egg imports `(import args ...)`, `(make-parameter VALUE)` parameter objects for CLI state holders, `args:make-option (SHORT LONG) #:KEYWORD HELP BODY_THUNK` with embedded body thunks, `#:none`/`#:required`/`#:optional` keyword-as-arg-shape, `(no-splash)` single-name variant for long-only flags, `args:usage opts` auto-help generation), real binary distributed as compiled Chicken script + AUR package.
  - Blacklist update: `massmine` added.  Corpus count: 28,746 → 28,747 (+1).

- **R323 — amidst source-direct (Advanced Minecraft Interface and Data/Structure Tracker; `args4j` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Java JCommander (R322 kobalt) deeper into the Java ecosystem to document the THIRD Java CLI library — corpus's **first documented args4j library** + **third documented Java CLI library** (joining R276 picocli + R322 JCommander).  amidst is the Advanced Minecraft Interface and Data/Structure Tracker — visualizes Minecraft world generation (biomes, structures, slime chunks, stronghold positions) for any seed and world type without actually generating chunks.  Authored by toolbox4minecraft (founder: Skidam).  2264 GH stars.  Sister tools: chunkbase tools (web-based seed mapping), MCASelector (chunk selector), seedfinder/mcseedfinder/cubitec tools.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented args4j library** — adds args4j (org.kohsuke.args4j-args4j, Kohsuke Kawaguchi 2005 — Jenkins CI author; SINGLE-DASH FLAG default unlike JCommander/picocli, declarative `depends`/`forbids` mutex arrays, `OptionHandler<T>` SPI for multi-token-consuming custom converters, `ParserProperties.defaults().with...()` builder-as-config) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (62 library variants now).  Java now has **THREE documented CLI library variants** (picocli + JCommander + args4j) — joining the multi-library tier with Rust 6 and Go 4.
  - `_amidst` (1-stem; toolbox4minecraft/amidst src/main/java/amidst/Amidst.java + src/main/java/amidst/CommandLineParameters.java; **Java `args4j` library** — org.kohsuke.args4j-args4j on Maven Central; **11 @Option fields + SINGLE-DASH FLAG default (e.g. `-mcpath` not `--mcpath`) + `class CommandLineParameters { @Option ... }` annotated-class spec + `depends = { "-otherFlag" }` declarative required-with-other-flag + `forbids = { "-otherFlag" }` declarative mutex + `handler = X.class` custom OptionHandler<T> SPI with multi-token-consuming `parseArguments(Parameters params)` + `ParserProperties.defaults().withShowDefaults(false).withUsageWidth(120).withOptionSorter(null)` builder-as-config + `new CmdLineParser(beanInstance, ParserProperties)` constructor + `parser.parseArgument(args)` + `parser.printUsage(System.out)` auto-help**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main/java/amidst/Amidst.java` lines 11-13 (`import org.kohsuke.args4j.CmdLineException;` + `CmdLineParser` + `ParserProperties` — standard Java IMPORT for args4j)
    * `src/main/java/amidst/CommandLineParameters.java` lines 7-11 (`import org.kohsuke.args4j.Option;` + `OptionDef` + `OptionHandler` + `Parameters` + `Setter` — args4j SPI imports for CUSTOM TYPE HANDLERS)
    * `CommandLineParameters.java` lines 22-30 (`public class CommandLineParameters { @Option(name = "-mcpath", usage = "...", metaVar = "<directory>") public volatile Path dotMinecraftDirectory; ... }` — args4j ANNOTATED CLASS with SINGLE-DASH FLAGS BY DEFAULT)
    * `CommandLineParameters.java` lines 35-42 (`@Option(name = "-mcjar", ..., depends = { "-mcjson" }, forbids = { "-profile" })` — `depends` declares REQUIRED-WITH-OTHER-FLAG, `forbids` declares MUTEX)
    * `CommandLineParameters.java` lines 82-90 (`@Option(name = "-seed", handler = SeedHandler.class, usage = "...", metaVar = "<string>") public volatile WorldSeed initialSeed;` — `handler = X.class` for CUSTOM TYPE CONVERTER)
    * `CommandLineParameters.java` lines 124-138 (`public static class SeedHandler extends OptionHandler<WorldSeed> { ... public int parseArguments(Parameters params) throws CmdLineException { WorldSeed seed = WorldSeed.fromUserInput(params.getParameter(0)); setter.addValue(seed); return 1; } public String getDefaultMetaVariable() { return "STRING"; } }` — custom OptionHandler implementation; `parseArguments` returns count of tokens consumed (multi-token-consuming))
    * `Amidst.java` lines 53-62 (`CmdLineParser parser = new CmdLineParser(parameters, ParserProperties.defaults().withShowDefaults(false).withUsageWidth(120).withOptionSorter(null)); try { parser.parseArgument(args); } catch (CmdLineException e) { ... parser.printUsage(System.out); System.exit(2); }` — PARSE INVOCATION with `ParserProperties` builder)
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **Java `args4j` library** at CommandLineParameters.java:7-11 + Amidst.java:11-13 — corpus's **first documented args4j library** + **third documented Java CLI library** (joining R276 picocli + R322 JCommander).  args4j (org.kohsuke.args4j-args4j on Maven Central) was created by Kohsuke Kawaguchi (Jenkins CI author) in 2005 — PREDATES JCommander (2010) and picocli (2017).  Used internally by Jenkins CLI, SonarQube CLI, and many older Java tools.  Now maintained by the community (Kawaguchi handed it off in ~2015).  Distinguishing features: SINGLE-DASH FLAG default (vs JCommander/picocli's double-dash), `depends`/`forbids` declarative arrays, `OptionHandler<T>` SPI for multi-token-consuming custom converters.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SIXTY-TWO library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), **Java 1 picocli (R276)**, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo `alias`-as-spec (R321), **Java 2 JCommander (R322)**, **Java 3 args4j** (R323 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 62 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Java now has THREE documented CLI library variants (picocli + JCommander + args4j) — joining the multi-library tier with Rust 6 and Go 4.
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **args4j single-dash-default-flag CLI pattern** at CommandLineParameters.java throughout — all flag names use SINGLE-DASH: `-mcpath`, `-mcjar`, `-biome-profiles`, `-world-type`, `-help`, `-version`.  args4j defaults to single-dash names — a UNIX traditional convention.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (`--long` double-dash default, `-X` short-dash), R276 picocli (`--long` default).  args4j's single-dash default is a HISTORICAL UNIX style — corpus's **first documented single-dash-default-flag CLI pattern**.  args4j CAN use `--long` if explicitly specified in `name = "--long"`, but the convention is single-dash even for multi-character flag names.
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **args4j `depends` and `forbids` declarative arrays** at CommandLineParameters.java:35-42, 44-51, 54-60, 87-93 — `@Option(name = "-mcjar", ..., depends = { "-mcjson" }, forbids = { "-profile" })` declares that `-mcjar` REQUIRES `-mcjson` also be given, and FORBIDS `-profile` from being given simultaneously.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (no built-in depends/forbids — requires custom IParameterValidator), R276 picocli (`scope = ` and `defaultValue =` but no native depends/forbids), R315 Eiffel Gobo `AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST` (mutex via first-class object structure).  args4j's approach: DECLARATIVE `depends`/`forbids` STRING ARRAYS in the annotation — corpus's **first documented declarative-depends-and-forbids CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **args4j `OptionHandler<T>` SPI for custom type converters** at CommandLineParameters.java:82-90 + 124-160 — `@Option(handler = SeedHandler.class)` references a static nested class `SeedHandler extends OptionHandler<WorldSeed>` that converts the argv string to a `WorldSeed` domain object.  Distinct from R322 JCommander `converter = X.class` (uses `IStringConverter<T>` interface — SINGLE-arg conversion only), R276 picocli `@Option converter = X.class` (uses `ITypeConverter<T>`), R314 Mercury `option_data` typed-union default.  args4j's `OptionHandler<T>` is MORE POWERFUL because `parseArguments(Parameters params)` can CONSUME MULTIPLE TOKENS (returning the count) — useful for options like `-foo a b c d` that take a sub-list.  Corpus's **first documented multi-token-consuming-handler CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **args4j `ParserProperties.defaults().with...()` builder for parser config** at Amidst.java:56-60 — `new CmdLineParser(parameters, ParserProperties.defaults().withShowDefaults(false).withUsageWidth(120).withOptionSorter(null))`.  `ParserProperties` is args4j's config-builder pattern: METHOD-CHAINED setters `withShowDefaults`, `withUsageWidth`, `withOptionSorter`, `withUsageHeader`, etc.  Distinct from R322 JCommander (no equivalent config builder — properties set via the `JCommander` instance), R276 picocli (`CommandLine(cmd).setUsageHelpAutoWidth(true)` etc.).  args4j's approach: SEPARATE `ParserProperties` CONFIG BUILDER object — corpus's **first documented ParserProperties-builder-as-config CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: amidst uses **args4j multi-token-consuming custom OptionHandler subclass** at CommandLineParameters.java:124-138 — `parseArguments(Parameters params)` consumes N argv tokens and returns the count consumed (here `return 1` = consume one).  `getDefaultMetaVariable()` provides the default help-text placeholder.  Distinct from R322 JCommander `IStringConverter<T>.convert(String)` (single-arg conversion only), R276 picocli `ITypeConverter<T>.convert(String)` (same).  args4j's approach: MULTI-TOKEN-CONSUMING handlers via the `int parseArguments(Parameters)` SPI — corpus's **first documented multi-token-consuming-handler CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_picocli` (would refer to the library not a CLI), `_jenkins` (Jenkins CI controller, already in corpus — but Jenkins uses args4j internally for its CLI; would be a SISTER demo but with much larger command surface), `_kobalt` / `_gradle` / `_maven` / `_sbt` (JVM build systems already in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `gitblit-org/gitblit` (2352 stars, pure Java Git server — but gitblit's args4j usage is spread across many subcommand servlets; harder to extract source-direct), `tedskovsky/audiveris` (0 stars on this fork, real audiveris is at audiveris/audiveris and has different CLI now), `jenkins-infra/crawler` (28 stars, Jenkins internal tool — narrow use case), `MadsNielsen/jtemp` (Jenkins fork example — too internal), `myleott/JGibbLabeledLDA` (academic ML tool, narrow), `tedskovsky/audiveris` (fork of older version).  Chose amidst: 2264 stars (popular Minecraft visualization tool), 11 @Option annotations in single 160-line CommandLineParameters.java exercising EVERY MAJOR args4j PATTERN (single-dash flag defaults, `depends`/`forbids` declarative arrays, custom OptionHandler<T> subclasses with multi-token-consuming parseArguments, ParserProperties.defaults().with...() builder for parser config, metaVar placeholders), real Minecraft tool distributed via GitHub releases as runnable JAR + AUR package (`amidst-bin`).
  - Blacklist update: `amidst` added.  Corpus count: 28,745 → 28,746 (+1).

- **R322 — kobalt source-direct (Kotlin-based JVM build system by Cédric Beust; `JCommander` library — by Beust himself)** (1 file new) — pivots from Mojo `alias`-as-spec (R321 moty) into the canonical Java/Kotlin JVM ecosystem — corpus's **first documented JCommander library** + **second documented Java CLI library** (joining R276 picocli).  kobalt is a Kotlin-based build system for the JVM authored by **Cédric Beust** (TestNG author + JCommander author — so kobalt's CLI is JCommander's CANONICAL real-world use case by the library's own author).  437 GH stars.  Sister tools: gradle (Groovy-based JVM build system, in corpus), maven (XML-based JVM build, in corpus), sbt (Scala build, in corpus), ant (in corpus).  Kobalt aimed to be more Kotlin-idiomatic than Gradle.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented JCommander library** — adds JCommander (com.beust:jcommander, Maven Central, since 2010; field-annotation-as-spec, bare-`@Parameter`-as-positional, array-of-strings-for-all-names with no short/long type distinction, multi-long-name array aliases, reflection-based type conversion) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (61 library variants now).  Java joins the multi-library club (after Rust 6, Go 4, Python 3, Crystal 2, Common Lisp 2, Nim 2, Clojure 2, C++ 2): TWO Java CLI library variants now documented (picocli + JCommander).
  - `_kobalt` (1-stem; cbeust/kobalt modules/kobalt-plugin-api/src/main/kotlin/com/beust/kobalt/Args.kt + src/main/kotlin/com/beust/kobalt/Main.kt; **Java/Kotlin `JCommander` library** — com.beust:jcommander on Maven Central; **28+ flags + positional targets + field-annotation-as-spec via `class Args { @Parameter ... }` + bare `@Parameter` for positionals + `@Parameter(names = arrayOf("-X", "--long"), description = "...")` for flags + multi-name array aliases (short + long packed together) + multi-long-name aliases like `--help, --usage` + reflection-based type conversion (`Int`, `Boolean`, `String?`, `List<String>`) + Kotlin companion-object constants inside `@Parameter(names = arrayOf(Args.SEQUENTIAL))` + `JCommander(args)` constructor + `result.parse(*argv)` Kotlin-spread parse + `jc.usage()` auto-help**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main/kotlin/com/beust/kobalt/Main.kt` line 3 (`import com.beust.jcommander.JCommander` — standard Java/Kotlin IMPORT for the JCommander library)
    * `modules/kobalt-plugin-api/src/main/kotlin/com/beust/kobalt/Args.kt` line 5 (`class Args { @Parameter ... }` — JCOMMANDER ANNOTATED CLASS; the CLASS itself becomes the SPEC; field-level `@Parameter` annotations define each flag)
    * `Args.kt` line 8 (`@Parameter var targets: List<String> = arrayListOf()` — `@Parameter` WITHOUT `names` declares the POSITIONAL ARGS field)
    * `Args.kt` line 11 (`@Parameter(names = arrayOf("-bf", "--buildFile"), description = "The build file") var buildFile: String? = "kobalt/src/Build.kt"` — Kotlin `arrayOf("-bf", "--buildFile")` packs SHORT AND LONG NAMES into ONE array; JCommander distinguishes short from long by number of leading dashes)
    * `Args.kt` line 45 (`@Parameter(names = arrayOf("--help", "--usage"), ...) var usage: Boolean = false` — MULTI-LONG-NAME ALIASES; both `--help` and `--usage` map to same field)
    * `Args.kt` line 52 (`@Parameter(names = arrayOf("--log"), description = "...") var log: Int = Constants.LOG_DEFAULT_LEVEL` — TYPED FIELD with reflection-based auto-conversion of argv to `Int`)
    * `Args.kt` lines 92, 95 (`companion object { const val SEQUENTIAL = "--sequential" } ... @Parameter(names = arrayOf(Args.SEQUENTIAL), ...)` — Kotlin COMPANION OBJECT CONSTANT used INSIDE the `@Parameter` annotation)
    * `Main.kt` lines 50-56 (`private fun parseArgs(argv: Array<String>): Main.RunInfo { val args = Args(); val result = JCommander(args); result.parse(*argv); ... return Main.RunInfo(result, args) }` — PARSE INVOCATION: `JCommander(args)` constructor with the annotated instance, then `result.parse(*argv)` (Kotlin spread operator `*argv` to unpack the array))
    * `Main.kt` line 42 (`if (args.usage) { jc.usage(); return 0 }` — JCommander auto-generates USAGE OUTPUT via `jc.usage()`)
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **Java/Kotlin `JCommander` library** at Main.kt:3 + Args.kt — corpus's **first documented JCommander library** + **second documented Java CLI library** (joining R276 picocli).  JCommander (com.beust:jcommander, Maven Central) was created by Cédric Beust in 2010 as a JSR-310-influenced replacement for Apache Commons CLI.  Distinct from R276 picocli (auto-generated usage tables, more annotations, ANSI color support) by Remko Popma — picocli has overtaken JCommander in popularity since ~2018 but JCommander remains widely used in legacy Java projects.  Distinct from Apache Commons CLI (older, more verbose), args4j (Kohsuke Kawaguchi — Jenkins author).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SIXTY-ONE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), **Java 1 picocli (R276)**, Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), Mojo `alias`-as-spec (R321), **Java 2 JCommander** (R322 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 61 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Java joins the multi-library club: TWO Java CLI library variants now documented.
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **JCommander field-annotation-as-spec pattern** at Args.kt (whole file) — `class Args { @Parameter ... }` annotates fields with `@Parameter` directly.  The CLASS itself becomes the SPEC; the parser uses reflection to discover all annotated fields.  Distinct from R276 picocli `@Command class` + `@Option` + `@Parameters` (split annotations), R305 C++ CLI11 (struct + add_option method-chain), R270 Haskell `Option` constructor list, R311 Guile quoted-assoc-list.  JCommander's approach: SINGLE `@Parameter` annotation for both options AND positionals — corpus's **first documented unified-annotation-for-options-and-positionals CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **JCommander bare-`@Parameter`-as-positional CLI pattern** at Args.kt:8 — `@Parameter var targets: List<String> = arrayListOf()` declares the POSITIONAL ARGS field by OMITTING the `names` parameter.  All non-flag argv tokens accumulate into this field.  Distinct from R276 picocli `@Parameters` (different annotation), R305 C++ CLI11 positional-name-without-leading-dash, R307 Vala GLib empty-string-name OptionEntry sentinel, R316 C++ boost positional_options_description with `.add("name", -1)` mapping.  JCommander's approach: BARE `@Parameter` WITH NO `names` = POSITIONAL — corpus's **first documented bare-annotation-as-positional CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **JCommander array-of-strings-for-all-names CLI pattern** at Args.kt:11+ — `@Parameter(names = arrayOf("-bf", "--buildFile"), ...)` packs SHORT AND LONG NAMES into the same `String[]` array, with no short-vs-long type distinction.  Distinct from R316 C++ boost `"help,h"` comma-string (also packs but separated by comma), R270 Haskell `Option [Char] [String]` two-list (separate short-char-list and long-string-list), R313 Idris 2 `['?', 'h']` Char-list + `["help"]` String-list (same separation).  JCommander's approach: SINGLE ARRAY of STRINGS for ALL names — the parser distinguishes short from long by the number of leading dashes in each entry.  Corpus's **first documented array-of-strings-for-all-names CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **JCommander multi-long-name array aliases** at Args.kt:45 — `@Parameter(names = arrayOf("--help", "--usage"), ...)` declares MULTIPLE LONG-NAME aliases for the same field.  Distinct from R309 SML `short = "vV"` (string-of-chars for multi-short — but SHORTS only), R313 Idris 2 `["help", "halp"]` long-string-list-aliases (multi-longs in a separate list).  JCommander supports MULTIPLE LONG-NAME aliases by listing them in the SAME `names = arrayOf(...)` array — corpus's **first documented multi-long-name-array CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **JCommander reflection-based-type-conversion CLI pattern** at Args.kt:52+ — `var log: Int = Constants.LOG_DEFAULT_LEVEL` — JCommander auto-detects the field's TYPE via reflection (`Int`, `Boolean`, `String?`, `List<String>`) and converts the argv value accordingly.  No explicit `:parse-fn` or converter needed for primitives.  Distinct from R311 Guile `(value #t)` (boolean-or-symbol arg-shape), R313 Idris 2 `ReqArg Input "<file>"` (constructor variant), R318 Clojure tools.cli `:parse-fn` (explicit transformer).  JCommander's approach: TYPE-DRIVEN AUTO-CONVERSION via reflection — corpus's **first documented reflection-based-type-conversion CLI pattern**.  For complex types, JCommander supports `converter = XYZ.class` (used by sister tool moditect for `Path` and `Instant` types).
  - Critical extraction note: kobalt uses **Kotlin companion-object-constant-in-annotation CLI pattern** at Args.kt:92-95 — `companion object { const val SEQUENTIAL = "--sequential" }` then `@Parameter(names = arrayOf(Args.SEQUENTIAL), ...)`.  Kotlin's `companion object` constants are COMPILE-TIME-VISIBLE INSIDE annotations (Kotlin compiler resolves the reference at annotation-processing time).  Distinct from R276 picocli (no companion-object pattern in Java), R305 C++ CLI11 (#define or static const).  Corpus's **first documented companion-object-constant-in-annotation CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_picocli` (would refer to the LIBRARY itself, not a CLI), `_gradle` / `_maven` / `_sbt` / `_ant` (JVM build systems all in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `Sayi/poi-tl` (5102 stars but poi-tl CLI is a small subcommand wrapping the Apache POI template engine — focused CLI), `moditect/moditect` (578 stars, Java module system tool — simpler 5-flag CLI than kobalt; STRONG sister demo: also uses JCommander with @Parameter + IStringConverter for Path and Instant types; DEFERRED for separate round if needed), `99soft/t2t` (0 stars, RDF migration tool — too small), `Accla/graphulo` (82 stars MIT Lincoln Lab — but graphulo is a library not standalone CLI), `BoD/android-contentprovider-generator` (615 stars but android-specific narrow use case).  Chose kobalt: 437 GH stars, AUTHORED BY THE JCOMMANDER AUTHOR (Cédric Beust — canonical real-world use case), 28+ @Parameter annotations in single 99-line Args.kt exercising EVERY MAJOR JCommander PATTERN (field-annotation-as-spec, bare-@Parameter-as-positional, multi-name arrays for short+long aliases, multi-long-name aliases like --help/--usage, reflection-based type conversion for Int/Boolean/String?/List<String>, Kotlin companion-object-constant-in-annotation, JCommander(args) constructor + result.parse(*argv) two-call parse, jc.usage() auto-help), real Kotlin build system binary distributed via Maven/Brew.
  - Blacklist update: `kobalt` added.  Corpus count: 28,744 → 28,745 (+1).

- **R321 — moty source-direct (Python static type checker in Mojo; handwritten `collect_args` with COMPILE-TIME `alias`-as-spec)** (1 file new) — pivots from Chapel `config` keyword (R320 arkouda_server) into the **Mojo modern-ML-systems-language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Mojo CLI pattern**.  moty is a Python static type checker written in Mojo — checks Python source files for type errors using Mojo's compile-time type system.  Authored by Samuel Ibarra (IbarraSamuel).  0 GH stars (recent project) but selected for cleanest Mojo-idiomatic CLI demo.  Sister tools: mypy (mature Python type checker, in corpus), pyright (Microsoft Python type checker, in corpus), ruff (Python linter, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: 60 CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI LIBRARY VARIANTS documented — corpus has now reached SIXTY distinct CLI libraries.  Mojo joins as Chris Lattner's 2023-era Python-superset ML systems language by Modular Inc, with NO formal CLI library in stdlib yet (just `sys.argv()` primitive); the documented pattern is HANDWRITTEN parametric `collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS]()` with COMPILE-TIME `alias`-as-spec lists.
  - `_moty` (1-stem; IbarraSamuel/moty src/cli.mojo + src/config.mojo; **Mojo stdlib `sys.argv()` primitive + handwritten parametric `collect_args` function with compile-time `alias` spec lists** — no formal Mojo CLI library exists yet; **1 positional + 1 option + 1 flag + `alias NAME = [StaticString("...")]` compile-time list aliases + `@fieldwise_init` decorator-driven auto-constructor + parametric `collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS]()` compile-time-parameter function + `out self` constructor convention + `^` move-ownership operator + struct-with-traits-and-aliases for enum-like types + Mojo Python-style `import sys` + `sys.argv()` function-call argv accessor**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/cli.mojo` line 1 (`import sys` — Mojo Python-style IMPORT directive; Mojo is a Python SUPERSET — most Python syntax works, plus Mojo-only extensions for compile-time metaprogramming)
    * `src/config.mojo` lines 4-6 (`alias POSITIONAL = [StaticString("path")]` + `alias ARGUMENTS = [StaticString("log-type")]` + `alias FLAGS = [StaticString("strict")]` — Mojo `alias` COMPILE-TIME LIST DECLARATIONS; `StaticString` is Mojo's compile-time-known string type)
    * `src/cli.mojo` lines 4-12 (`@fieldwise_init struct ArgValues: var arguments: List[StaticString]; var options: Dict[StaticString, StaticString]; var flags: List[StaticString]; fn __init__(out self): self.arguments = {}; ...` — Mojo STRUCT with `@fieldwise_init` decorator auto-generating constructor; `out self` is the OUT-PARAM convention)
    * `src/cli.mojo` lines 15-19 (`fn collect_args[positional: List[StaticString], arguments: List[StaticString], flags: List[StaticString]](out arg_values: ArgValues):` — Mojo PARAMETRIC FUNCTION with COMPILE-TIME PARAMETER LIST `[...]`; caller invokes as `collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS]()`)
    * `src/cli.mojo` line 20 (`var argvs = sys.argv()` — Mojo's `sys.argv()` FUNCTION-CALL form returns `List[StaticString]`; differs from Python's `sys.argv` ATTRIBUTE access)
    * `src/cli.mojo` lines 30-45 (handwritten argv loop with Python-style for-range, `arg.startswith("--")` Mojo string methods (Python-identical API), `arg.lstrip("--")` for flag-name extraction)
    * `src/config.mojo` lines 30-50 (`struct Config(Writable): var path: Path; var log_type: LogType; var strict: Bool; fn __init__(out self): var arg_values = collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS](); self = Self(arg_values^)` — `self = Self(arg_values^)` uses Mojo's `^` MOVE OPERATOR to transfer ownership)
    * `src/config.mojo` lines 7-27 (`struct LogType(Copyable, EqualityComparable, Writable): alias silent = LogType("verbose"); alias verbose = LogType("silent")` — Mojo STRUCT with TRAITS conformance `(Copyable, EqualityComparable, Writable)` + ALIAS-VARIANTS for enum-like behavior)
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo stdlib `sys.argv()` primitive + handwritten parametric `collect_args` function with compile-time `alias` spec lists** at cli.mojo:1+config.mojo:4-6 — corpus's **first documented Mojo CLI pattern**.  Mojo has NO FORMAL CLI library in its stdlib (Mojo is too new — v0.26 stdlib has just `sys.argv()` primitive returning `List[StaticString]`).  moty's approach: define COMPILE-TIME `alias` lists for POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, and FLAGS spec, then pass them as COMPILE-TIME TYPE PARAMETERS to a generic `collect_args[positional, arguments, flags]()` function.  The parser walks the argv at runtime but the SPEC is COMPILED IN.  Distinct from R311 Guile `(getopt-long args SPEC)` (runtime spec data), R317 Forth/Gforth `next-arg` + handwritten matching (no spec data), R313 Idris 2 `MkOpt SHORT_CHARS LONG_STRINGS ArgDescr HELP` (runtime spec data), R272 C POSIX getopt (runtime option-string).  Mojo's approach: COMPILE-TIME `alias` LISTS + PARAMETRIC PARSER FUNCTION — corpus's **first documented compile-time-list-as-parameter CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to SIXTY library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), Roc weaver (R319), Chapel `config` keyword (R320), **Mojo `alias`-as-spec + parametric `collect_args`** (R321 — NEW).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: 60 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo `alias NAME = VALUE` compile-time alias-as-spec pattern** at config.mojo:4-6 — `alias POSITIONAL = [StaticString("path")]` declares a COMPILE-TIME ALIAS bound to a list literal.  Mojo's `alias` is a TYPE-LEVEL CONSTANT — the VALUE is fixed at compile time and embedded directly into instantiation sites.  Distinct from R272 C `#define` (textual macro), R290 Racket `(define-syntax)` (macro), R302 Clojure babashka.cli `(def ...)` (runtime binding).  Mojo's approach: `alias` AS COMPILE-TIME TYPED CONSTANT — corpus's **first documented compile-time-alias-as-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo parametric `collect_args[positional, arguments, flags]()` compile-time-parameter function** at cli.mojo:15-19 — `fn collect_args[positional: List[StaticString], arguments: List[StaticString], flags: List[StaticString]](out arg_values: ArgValues):`.  The `[...]` bracket-enclosed parameter list is Mojo's COMPILE-TIME PARAMETER LIST.  Parameters can be TYPES OR COMPILE-TIME VALUES known at function-instantiation time.  Caller invokes as `collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS]()` — the alias lists are RESOLVED at the call site.  Distinct from R270 Haskell type parameters (only types, not values), R272 C no compile-time parameters, R313 Idris 2 dependent types (any Type can be passed but Idris uses curly braces).  Mojo's approach: COMPILE-TIME-VALUE-PARAMETERS allow passing LISTS of STRINGS as compile-time parameters — corpus's **first documented compile-time-list-as-parameter CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo `^` move-ownership operator into config struct** at config.mojo:35 — `self = Self(arg_values^)`.  Mojo's `^` is the MOVE OPERATOR — transfers ownership of the value rather than copying.  Sister to Rust's `std::mem::take` or move semantics, Swift's `consume`, C++ `std::move`.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 (struct with reference binding), R312 Pascal Create-Run-Free.  Mojo's approach: MOVE-OWNERSHIP parsed values into the config struct — corpus's **first documented move-ownership-into-config CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo `@fieldwise_init` decorator for auto-constructor** at cli.mojo:4 — `@fieldwise_init struct ArgValues:` auto-generates a constructor accepting all fields.  Distinct from R270 Haskell record types (auto-accessors but no field-init decorator), R313 Idris 2 `record Options where constructor MkOptions ...` (explicit constructor named).  Mojo's approach: DECORATOR-DRIVEN AUTO-CONSTRUCTOR via `@fieldwise_init` — corpus's **first documented decorator-driven-constructor CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: moty uses **Mojo `struct LogType(Copyable, EqualityComparable, Writable):` traits-with-alias-variants enum pattern** at config.mojo:7-27 — `struct LogType(Copyable, EqualityComparable, Writable):` declares a struct CONFORMING TO multiple traits (analog to Rust trait bounds, Swift protocols).  `alias silent = LogType("verbose")` and `alias verbose = LogType("silent")` declare ENUM-LIKE VARIANTS as COMPILE-TIME aliases inside the struct.  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 ADT `data Option = Help | Input String | ...` (true sum type), R305 C++ CLI11 enum.  Mojo's approach: STRUCT-WITH-ALIAS-VARIANTS for enum-like types — corpus's **first documented struct-with-alias-as-enum CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_mojo` (Mojo interpreter/compiler, would shadow `mojo file ARGS` invocation — Mojo has no "compiled binary" pathway documented yet for most tools), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `tairov/llama2.mojo` (2124 stars, famous LLaMA 2 inference in Mojo — but typically invoked as `mojo llama2.mojo ARGS` not as compiled binary; binary name `llama2` would conflict with karpathy/llama2.c), `forfudan/argmojo` (14 stars — THE LIBRARY ITSELF for Mojo argparse; not a CLI tool), `tobanteAudio/pffdtd` (2 stars — uses Python.import_module("argparse") via Mojo Python interop, interesting but minimal CLI), `IbarraSamuel/moty` (CHOSEN — 0 stars, but CLEANEST native-Mojo CLI demo using the `alias` compile-time spec pattern), `mgrotkowski/mojolox` (0 stars, Lox interpreter — too minimal: only one positional).  Chose moty: real Python static type checker tool with clean 100-line cli.mojo + config.mojo exercising EVERY MAJOR native-Mojo CLI PATTERN (Python-style `import sys`, `alias NAME = [StaticString("...")]` compile-time list aliases, `@fieldwise_init` decorator-driven auto-constructor, parametric `collect_args[POSITIONAL, ARGUMENTS, FLAGS]()` compile-time-parameter function, `out self` constructor convention, `^` move-ownership operator, struct-with-traits-and-aliases for enum-like types), real Mojo binary distributed via pixi (Mojo's package manager).
  - Blacklist update: `moty` added.  Corpus count: 28,743 → 28,744 (+1).

- **R320 — arkouda_server source-direct (HPC data-analytics backend in Chapel; `config const`/`config var`/`config param` language-level CLI mechanism)** (1 file new) — pivots from Roc weaver (R319 roc-start) into the **Chapel HPC parallel-computing ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Chapel CLI mechanism**.  arkouda_server is Arkouda's BACKEND — a Chapel parallel program that mimics NumPy's ndarray semantics at HPC scale.  Driven from a Python client (the `arkouda` Python package) that sends commands over ZeroMQ to this server.  Arkouda's design: Python REPL for ergonomics + Chapel server for distributed parallel performance.  Authored by Bears-R-Us (US DoD-affiliated research lab).  280 GH stars.  Sister tools: arkouda Python client (in PyPI), chpl (Chapel compiler — in corpus as `_chpl`), zmq tools (ZeroMQ underlies the client/server RPC).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Chapel CLI mechanism** — adds Chapel's `config` KEYWORD (LANGUAGE-LEVEL feature where the COMPILER auto-generates a CLI parser by walking the AST for `config const`/`config var`/`config param` declarations; no library, no spec table, no parser construction; ZERO USER CODE required) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (59 library variants now).  Chapel joins as one of only TWO documented LANGUAGE-LEVEL CLI mechanisms (alongside R317 Forth's "language-as-library" — but Forth has no auto-parsing; Chapel does).
  - `_arkouda_server` (1-stem; Bears-R-Us/arkouda src/ServerConfig.chpl + src/arkouda_server.chpl; **Chapel `config` keyword (language-level CLI mechanism)** — compiler auto-generates parser from module-scope `config` declarations; **14 user-facing config flags + ~12 Chapel runtime built-in flags + `config const`/`config var`/`config param` three-variant declaration-as-flag + type inference from initializer + explicit `: TYPE` annotation + enum-variant-auto-match for `LogLevel.INFO`-style flags + `private config` module-scoping with `--ModuleName.NAME` disambiguation + `try! getEnv(NAME, DEFAULT)` env-var fallback in config initializer + compiler-walks-AST CLI parser generation**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/arkouda_server.chpl` line 5 (`use ServerConfig;` — Chapel `use` directive imports module's public symbols)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 55 (`config const deployment = Deployment.STANDARD;` — Chapel CONFIG CONST DECLARATION; `config` keyword on a module-scope declaration AUTO-EXPOSES the binding as a CLI flag)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 60 (`config const trace = true;` — TYPE-INFERRED BOOLEAN config; parser accepts `--trace=true|false|0|1`)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 65 (`config var logLevel = LogLevel.INFO;` — `config var` MUTABLE variant + ENUM-typed; accepts `--logLevel=LogLevel.DEBUG` or short form `--logLevel=DEBUG`)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 80 (`config const ServerPort = 5555;` — INT-typed config inferred from literal)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 100 (`config const pythonVersion:string = "Please set during compilation";` — EXPLICITLY TYPED via `: TYPE` annotation)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 110 (`config const serverConnectionInfo: string = try! getEnv("ARKOUDA_SERVER_CONNECTION_INFO", "");` — config with FALLBACK FROM ENV VAR via `try! getEnv` in initial expression)
    * `src/ServerConfig.chpl` line 80, 100 (multiple `config param` decls — COMPILE-TIME parameter variant; settable only via `-sNAME=VAL` to the chpl compiler at build time)
    * `src/arkouda_server.chpl` line 12 (`private config const logLevel = ServerConfig.logLevel;` — Chapel CONFIG with CROSS-MODULE REFERENCE; `private` qualifier scopes to current module; when same NAME exists in multiple modules, must use `--ModuleName.NAME=VALUE` qualified syntax)
    * `src/arkouda_server.chpl` lines 21-25 (`proc main() { coforall daemon in getServerDaemons() { daemon.run(); } }` — Chapel `coforall` parallel for-loop; CLI parsing happens IMPLICITLY by the Chapel compiler before `main()` is called)
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel `config const`/`config var`/`config param` language-level CLI mechanism** at ServerConfig.chpl:55+ et seq — corpus's **first documented Chapel CLI mechanism**.  Chapel's `config` KEYWORD is a LANGUAGE-LEVEL FEATURE, not a library.  Any module-scope declaration qualified with `config` becomes a CLI FLAG auto-generated by the Chapel compiler.  The compiler walks the AST for `config` declarations and emits a parser at compile time.  Distinct from EVERY OTHER CLI LIBRARY in the corpus — no library, no spec table, no parser construction.  Closest analog: R287 Nim parseopt (token iterator at language level) but Nim parseopt is still a stdlib module that the caller invokes.  Chapel is the ONLY language in the corpus where the CLI mechanism is COMPILER-PROVIDED with ZERO USER CODE required.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-NINE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), Roc weaver (R319), **Chapel `config` keyword** (R320 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 59 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Chapel joins the taxonomy as one of only TWO documented LANGUAGE-LEVEL CLI mechanisms (alongside R317 Forth's "language-as-library" — but Forth has no auto-parsing; Chapel does).
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel `config const`/`config var`/`config param` declaration-as-flag pattern** at ServerConfig.chpl:55-110 et seq — `config const NAME = VALUE` auto-exposes the binding as a CLI FLAG.  Three variants: `config const NAME = VALUE` (immutable flag, runtime-set, unchangeable after init), `config var NAME = VALUE` (mutable flag, runtime-set, reassignable), `config param NAME = VALUE` (compile-time parameter, set via `-sNAME=VAL` to chpl compiler).  Type INFERRED from initializer or DECLARED via `: TYPE` annotation.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (caller writes option string), R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec).  Chapel's approach: DECLARATION-AS-FLAG — corpus's **first documented declaration-as-flag CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel enum-variant-auto-match CLI flag values** at ServerConfig.chpl:65-66 — `config var logLevel = LogLevel.INFO;` accepts both `--logLevel=LogLevel.DEBUG` (FULL qualified name) and `--logLevel=DEBUG` (Chapel resolves the SHORT form).  Sister to R295 CL clingon `:option-type :choice :choices`, R304 Ada `Define_Alias` for enum aliases.  Chapel's approach: ENUM-VARIANT-AUTO-MATCH — corpus's **first documented enum-variant-auto-match CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel `private config` module-scoped disambiguation** at arkouda_server.chpl:12 — `private config const logLevel = ServerConfig.logLevel;` scopes the config to the current module.  When the same config NAME exists in multiple modules, the user must DISAMBIGUATE with `--ModuleName.NAME=VALUE` qualified syntax.  E.g. `--ServerConfig.logLevel=DEBUG` vs `--logLevel=DEBUG` in some other module.  Distinct from EVERY other CLI library — corpus's **first documented qualified-module-prefix-for-disambiguation CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel `try! getEnv(NAME, DEFAULT)` env-var fallback in config initializer** at ServerConfig.chpl:110 — `config const serverConnectionInfo: string = try! getEnv("ARKOUDA_SERVER_CONNECTION_INFO", "");`.  Chapel's `try!` is non-recoverable error propagation (panics on failure); `getEnv` reads OS env vars.  The pattern: CONFIG DEFAULT is RESOLVED AT RUNTIME via an arbitrary Chapel expression that can include env-var reads.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (no env-var integration), R313 Idris 2 (no built-in env support).  Chapel's approach: USER-CODE ENV-VAR FALLBACK as a `getEnv` call in the initial expression — corpus's **first documented env-var-as-config-default CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: arkouda_server uses **Chapel compiler AUTO-GENERATES CLI parser from AST walk** at compile time — the Chapel compiler walks every module's AST looking for `config` declarations, then EMITS a parser that reads argv and INITIALIZES each config variable from its corresponding CLI flag.  No user code participates in CLI parsing.  No `parse()` call, no `main()` argument unpacking, no spec table.  Distinct from EVERY OTHER CLI MECHANISM in the corpus — Chapel's approach is COMPILER-PROVIDED ZERO-USER-CODE CLI PARSING.  Closest analog: R287 Nim parseopt (still requires user `for kind, key, val in getopt():` loop), R317 Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (still requires user matching).  Chapel is truly UNIQUE: ZERO USER CODE for CLI handling.  Corpus's **first documented compiler-walks-AST-for-config CLI mechanism**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_chpl` (Chapel compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `pnnl/chgl` (33 stars, Chapel HyperGraph Library — library not standalone tool), `ParRes/Kernels` (474 stars, PRK benchmark suite — many small example programs but no canonical user-facing binary), `femto-dev/femto` (111 stars, bioinformatics sequence indexing — has CLI but config decls are spread across many files), `Bears-R-Us/arkouda` (CHOSEN — 280 stars, real production HPC data analytics server with focused ServerConfig.chpl).  Chose arkouda_server: real binary distributed via pip-installable Python client + Chapel compilation, ~15 user-facing config flags in clean ServerConfig.chpl exercise every major Chapel `config` PATTERN (config const for immutable, config var for mutable, config param for compile-time, type inference from initializer, explicit type annotation `: string`, enum-typed config, env-var fallback via `try! getEnv(NAME, DEFAULT)`, `private config` for module-scoping), real production server used at DoD labs and HPC sites for terabyte-scale array analytics.
  - Blacklist update: `arkouda_server` added.  Corpus count: 28,742 → 28,743 (+1).

- **R319 — roc-start source-direct (Roc project scaffolder; smores56/`weaver` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Clojure tools.cli (R318 kaocha) into the **Roc pure-functional-language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Roc CLI library**.  roc-start is a CLI tool for initializing new Roc projects and managing dependencies for existing projects.  Authored by Ian McLerran (imclerran).  20 GH stars.  Sister tools: roc (Roc compiler — in corpus), roc-lang/basic-cli (Roc CLI platform — library, not standalone tool), weaver (the CLI library itself; namespace collision with Google ServiceWeaver `_weaver` in corpus — DIFFERENT TOOL).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Roc CLI library** — adds smores56/weaver (Cli.weave record-builder with `<-` arrow, Opt.flag / Opt.maybe_str / Param.str_list typed constructors, |> Cli.map pipe-mapped post-parse transformers, SubCmd.optional + SubCmd.finish for subcommand dispatch with constructor-as-mapper) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (58 library variants now).  Roc joins the taxonomy as a 2022-era pure-functional-with-uniqueness-types language by Richard Feldman.
  - `_roc-start` (1-stem; imclerran/roc-start src/ArgParser.roc; **Roc `weaver` library** — smores56/weaver, the de-facto Roc CLI argument parser; **5 subcommands (app, package, upgrade, update, config) + global verbosity option + `{ Cli.weave <- field: value, ... }` record-arrow-weave spec pattern + `Opt.flag`/`Opt.maybe_str`/`Param.str_list` typed option constructors + `|> Cli.map(FN)` pipe-mapped post-parse transformers + `SubCmd.optional([sub1, ...]) + SubCmd.finish({mapper: Ctor})` constructor-as-mapper subcommand dispatch + `|> Cli.finish({name, version, authors, description, text_style}) |> Cli.assert_valid` pipeline finalization-and-validation + URL-tarball-dependency Roc app manifest**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main.roc` lines 1-13 (`app [main!] { cli: platform "URL", weaver: "URL", ... }` — Roc APP MANIFEST with NAMED ENTRY POINT `main!` (`!` suffix is Roc's EFFECT MARKER for I/O-performing functions) and URL-tarball DEPENDENCY MAP)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` line 3 (`import weaver.Cli` — Roc module IMPORT directive with DOT-NOTATION module path AFTER package alias)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` lines 33-43 (`cli_parser = { Cli.weave <- verbosity: Opt.maybe_str({short:"v", long:"verbosity", help:"..."}) |> Cli.map(verbosity_to_log_level), subcommand: SubCmd.optional([...]), } |> Cli.finish({name:"roc-start", version:"v0.7.3", authors:[...], description:"...", text_style:Color}) |> Cli.assert_valid` — `Cli.weave` is the WEAVER LIBRARY'S TOP-LEVEL COMBINATOR; `{ COMBINATOR <- field: value, ... }` is Roc's RECORD-BUILDER SYNTAX with LEADING COMBINATOR)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` lines 34-35 (`Opt.maybe_str(...) |> Cli.map(verbosity_to_log_level)` — `Opt.maybe_str` OPTIONAL STRING OPTION constructor + `|> Cli.map(FN)` POST-PARSE TRANSFORMER via Roc's `|>` PIPE OPERATOR)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` line 36 (`subcommand: SubCmd.optional([update_subcommand, app_subcommand, package_subcommand, upgrade_subcommand, config_subcommand])` — SubCmd.optional takes LIST of SUBCOMMAND BUILDERS)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` lines 60-95 (app_subcommand) (`{ Cli.weave <- force: Opt.flag({...}), no_plugin: Opt.flag({...}), filename: Opt.maybe_str({...}) |> Cli.map(default_filename) |> Cli.map(with_extension), platform: Opt.maybe_str({...}) |> Cli.map(platform_name_and_version_with_default), packages: Param.str_list({name:"packages", help:"..."}) |> Cli.map(package_names_and_versions), } |> SubCmd.finish({name:"app", description:"...", mapper:App})` — subcommand spec with `Param.str_list` for REPEATABLE POSITIONALS and `mapper:App` ADT constructor binding)
    * `src/ArgParser.roc` line 92 (`mapper: App` — SubCmd.finish's `mapper` binds the parsed subcommand to an ADT CONSTRUCTOR)
    * `src/main.roc` line 76 (`when ArgParser.parse_or_display_message(args, to_os_raw) is Ok({ verbosity, subcommand }) -> ... Err(message) -> ...` — Roc's `when` PATTERN-MATCH expression on the parser result)
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `weaver` library** at ArgParser.roc:3 — corpus's **first documented Roc CLI library**.  `weaver` is smores56/weaver, the de-facto Roc CLI argument parser.  Distributed as a tarball URL in the Roc package format (Roc has no central package registry — packages are referenced by GitHub release URLs with content-addressable hashes).  Distinct from raw `cli.Arg` (Roc basic-cli platform's argv accessor with `to_os_raw`), Roc's `Stdin`/`Stdout` modules.  weaver adds: typed option constructors (`Opt.flag`, `Opt.maybe_str`, `Opt.maybe_u64`), typed positional constructors (`Param.str`, `Param.str_list`), subcommand combinators (`SubCmd.optional`, `SubCmd.required`, `SubCmd.finish`), `Cli.weave` record-builder, `Cli.map` pipe-mapped post-parse transformers, `Cli.finish` finalization with name/version/authors/description metadata, `Cli.assert_valid` compile-time validation.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli (R318), **Roc weaver** (R319 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 58 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Roc joins the taxonomy as a 2022-era pure-functional-with-uniqueness-types language by Richard Feldman.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `{ Cli.weave <- field: value, ... }` record-arrow-weave CLI spec pattern** at ArgParser.roc:33-43, 73-94, 105-122 — Roc's `{ COMBINATOR <- field: value, ... }` is the RECORD-BUILDER SYNTAX with a LEADING COMBINATOR.  Each field's value is "WOVEN" into the parser result via the combinator.  Sister to R270 Haskell `<*>` applicative-style (each field assembles via `(<*>)`), R303 Gleam Glint's `glint.add(at:, do:)` pipe-builder, R313 Idris 2 `MkOpt` constructor.  Roc's `<-` ARROW INSIDE RECORD-LITERAL is UNIQUE — the combinator (here `Cli.weave`) determines how the fields combine.  Corpus's **first documented record-arrow-weave CLI spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `|> Cli.map(FN)` pipe-mapped post-parse transformers** at ArgParser.roc:34-35, 76-78, 79-81, 92, 132, 137 — `Opt.maybe_str(...) |> Cli.map(verbosity_to_log_level)` applies a POST-PARSE TRANSFORMER via Roc's `|>` PIPE OPERATOR.  Each `Cli.map` transforms the parsed value through a pure function.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli `:assoc-fn` (mutation hook), R318 Clojure tools.cli `:parse-fn` (replace-the-string transformer), R311 Guile `(option-ref options 'NAME #f)` (caller-side transformation post-parse).  Roc's approach: PURE-FUNCTIONAL POST-PARSE MAPPING via `|>` pipe — composable, lazy, type-safe.  Corpus's **first documented pipe-mapped-post-parse CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `SubCmd.optional([sub1, sub2, ...]) + SubCmd.finish({mapper: Ctor})` constructor-as-mapper subcommand dispatch** at ArgParser.roc:36, 90-94, 99-104, 119-122, 129-132, 138-141 — `SubCmd.optional(LIST)` declares an OPTIONAL subcommand chosen from a list.  Each subcommand is built via `{ Cli.weave <- ... } |> SubCmd.finish({ name, description, mapper })` where `mapper` BINDS the parsed subcommand to an ADT CONSTRUCTOR.  When the user runs `roc-start app pkg1 pkg2`, weaver produces `App { packages: [...], force: false, ... }` as the parsed value.  Distinct from R302 babashka.cli `:fn HANDLER` (handler called directly with parsed opts map), R313 Idris 2 ADT-as-parsed-value (similar but no `mapper` field — uses constructor arity directly).  Roc's approach: CONSTRUCTOR-AS-MAPPER — parsed subcommand becomes a TAGGED ADT variant for caller to pattern-match.  Corpus's **first documented constructor-as-mapper CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `Param.str_list({ name: "packages", help: "..."})` for REPEATABLE POSITIONALS** at ArgParser.roc:82-83, 108-110 — `Param.str_list` accepts ZERO or MORE string positional args.  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 `glint.named_arg` (typed single positional), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (comma-separated array via single flag), R272 C POSIX getopt (positionals via argv after parsing).  Roc's approach: TYPED `Param.str_list` constructor for repeatable positionals — sister to R303 Gleam Glint `strings_flag` plural-suffix but Roc's is for POSITIONALS not FLAGS.  Corpus's **first documented `Param.str_list` CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `app [main!] { dependency: "https://github.com/.../tarball-hash.tar.br" }` URL-tarball-dependency app manifest** at main.roc:1-13 — Roc's APP MANIFEST declares dependencies via URL.  `cli: platform "URL"` declares the PLATFORM (Roc's effect-providing runtime); `weaver: "URL"` declares a library dependency.  Each URL points to a CONTENT-ADDRESSABLE TARBALL on GitHub releases (hash in the path).  No central package registry.  Distinct from R270 Haskell Hackage, R311 Guile no-package-manager, R272 C `#include <header.h>` headers.  Corpus's **first documented URL-tarball-dependency CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: roc-start uses **Roc `|> Cli.finish({...}) |> Cli.assert_valid` pipeline finalization-and-validation chain** at ArgParser.roc:43-52 — the parser is FINALIZED via `|> Cli.finish({ name, version, authors, description, text_style })` and VALIDATED via `|> Cli.assert_valid` (compile-time check that the spec is consistent).  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 (no separate finalization — `getOpt` is called directly), R305 C++ CLI11 (no separate validation step — relies on runtime parse errors), R302 babashka.cli (no validation step).  Roc's approach: PIPELINE-FINALIZATION-AND-VALIDATION — corpus's **first documented pipeline-finalization-and-validation CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_roc` (Roc compiler in corpus + blacklisted), `_weaver` (in corpus but for Google ServiceWeaver — DIFFERENT TOOL, namespace collision with Roc's weaver library).  Skipped `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `smores56/roc-query` (1 star, GraphQL client example — small CLI), `isaacvando/gob` (19 stars, stack-based language in Roc but `gob.roc` uses manual arg parsing without weaver).  Chose roc-start: 20 GH stars (Ian McLerran), 5 subcommands (app, package, upgrade, update, config) + global verbosity, extensive use of weaver patterns (Cli.weave record-builder with `<-` arrow, Opt.flag / Opt.maybe_str / Param.str_list typed constructors, |> Cli.map post-parse transformers, SubCmd.optional + SubCmd.finish for subcommand dispatch with mapper constructors, |> Cli.finish / |> Cli.assert_valid pipeline finalization), real binary distributed via Roc package manager (URL tarballs).
  - Blacklist update: `roc-start` added.  Corpus count: 28,741 → 28,742 (+1).

- **R318 — kaocha source-direct (Clojure test runner; org.clojure/`tools.cli` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317 sprite-tool) back into the Clojure ecosystem to document the SECOND Clojure CLI library — corpus's **first documented `clojure.tools.cli` library** + **second documented Clojure CLI library** (joining R302 babashka.cli).  kaocha is a Clojure test runner — modern, full-featured next-gen test runner with plugins, parallel execution, watch mode, multiple reporters, and EDN-based configuration.  Authored by Arne Brasseur (lambdaisland).  860 GH stars.  Sister tools: clojure (Clojure CLI itself, in corpus), lein (Leiningen, in corpus), midje (older test framework, in corpus), eftest (test runner, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented org.clojure/tools.cli library** — adds `tools.cli` (the OFFICIAL Clojure CLI library maintained as part of org.clojure library set; vector-of-vectors-as-spec, `--[no-]NAME` negatable booleans, `:parse-fn`/`:assoc-fn` separate hooks, single-map-with-keyword-keys return) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (57 library variants now).  Clojure expands to 2 documented libraries joining the multi-library club with Rust 6, Go 4, Python 3, Crystal 2, Common Lisp 2, Nim 2, C++ 2.
  - `_kaocha` (1-stem; lambdaisland/kaocha src/kaocha/runner.clj; **Clojure `org.clojure/tools.cli` library** — the OFFICIAL Clojure CLI library, maintained by Sean Corfield and the Clojure team; **13 flags + positional test suites + vector-of-vectors spec + `--[no-]NAME` negatable-boolean syntax + `:default`/`:parse-fn`/`:assoc-fn` keyword-value spec args + custom parse functions for keywords/symbols + `accumulate` :assoc-fn for REPEATABLE options + `cli/parse-opts` returning single map with `:options`/`:arguments`/`:errors`/`:summary` keys + partial-auto-help via `:summary` key wrapped with custom usage banner**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` line 10 (`[clojure.tools.cli :as cli]` — Clojure `:as` namespace alias; org.clojure/tools.cli is the OFFICIAL Clojure CLI library, maintained as part of the org.clojure library set)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` lines 25-58 (`(def cli-options [["-c" "--config-file FILE" "Config file to read." :default "tests.edn"] [nil "--print-config" "..."] [nil "--[no-]fail-fast" "..."] [nil "--reporter SYMBOL" "..." :parse-fn (fn [s] ...) :assoc-fn accumulate] ...])` — VECTOR OF VECTOR-SPECS; each option spec is a Clojure vector with POSITIONAL elements `[SHORT_FLAG LONG_DESCR HELP_TEXT :key value ...]`)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` line 30 (`[nil "--[no-]fail-fast" "Stop testing after the first failure."]` — `--[no-]NAME` is tools.cli's NEGATABLE-BOOLEAN syntax; expands at parse time into both `--NAME` and `--no-NAME`)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` lines 35-39 (`[nil "--reporter SYMBOL" "..." :parse-fn (fn [s] ...) :assoc-fn accumulate]` — `:parse-fn` is TYPE-COERCION FUNCTION; `:assoc-fn accumulate` makes the option REPEATABLE via `(fn [m k v] (update m k (fnil conj []) v))`)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` lines 23-24 (`(defn parse-kw [value] (keyword (if (= \: (first value)) (subs value 1) value)))` — Clojure helper converts string to keyword, stripping leading `:` if present)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` line 21 (`(defn- accumulate [m k v] (update m k (fnil conj []) v))` — the `:assoc-fn accumulate` helper; `fnil` wraps `conj` to use `[]` as the default when the key is missing)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` lines 149, 158 (`(cli/parse-opts args cli-options)` — the parser invocation; returns a SINGLE MAP with `:options` parsed opts, `:arguments` remaining positionals, `:errors` parse errors as strings, `:summary` formatted help text generated FROM the spec)
    * `src/kaocha/runner.clj` lines 167-172 (`(run {:config config :options options :errors errors :suites suites :summary (str "USAGE:\n\nbin/kaocha [OPTIONS]... [TEST-SUITE]...\n\n" summary "\n\nOptions may be repeated multiple times...")})` — caller WRAPS the tools.cli summary in a custom usage banner; tools.cli's `:summary` is JUST THE OPTIONS TABLE)
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **Clojure `org.clojure/tools.cli` library** at runner.clj:10+149 — corpus's **first documented `clojure.tools.cli` library** + **second documented Clojure CLI library** (joining R302 babashka.cli).  org.clojure/tools.cli is the OFFICIAL Clojure CLI library — maintained as part of the org.clojure library set.  Distinct from R302 babashka.cli (third-party, bundled with Babashka runtime; MAP-PER-COMMAND data spec), R311 Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)` (Scheme stdlib; quoted-assoc-list spec).  tools.cli's API style is VECTOR-OF-VECTORS spec (one inner vector per option) vs babashka.cli's MAP-PER-COMMAND.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), **Clojure 1 babashka.cli (R302)**, Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), Forth/Gforth `next-arg` (R317), **Clojure 2 org.clojure/tools.cli** (R318 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 57 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Clojure expands to 2 documented libraries (babashka.cli + tools.cli) joining the multi-library club with Rust 6, Go 4, Python 3, Crystal 2, Common Lisp 2, Nim 2, C++ 2.
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **tools.cli vector-of-vectors-as-spec CLI pattern** at runner.clj:25-58 — `(def cli-options [[SHORT LONG_DESCR HELP_TEXT :key value ...] ...])`.  Each option spec is a Clojure vector with POSITIONAL elements followed by KEYWORD-VALUE pairs.  Sister to R302 babashka.cli (map per command) but tools.cli is FLATTER — all options in one global vector, no per-subcommand nesting.
