Distinct from R311 Guile (`((NAME (single-char #\X) (value #t/#f)) ...)` nested-list spec).  Corpus's **first documented vector-of-vectors-as-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **tools.cli `--[no-]NAME` negatable-boolean syntax** at runner.clj:30-32 — `[nil "--[no-]fail-fast" ...]` means passing `--fail-fast` sets the option true, passing `--no-fail-fast` sets it false.  tools.cli expands `--[no-]NAME` at parse time into both variants.  Distinct from R290 Racket `command-line` (separate flags for on/off), R311 Guile `getopt-long` (no equivalent — caller defines two flags), R272 C POSIX getopt (no negatable flags), R313 Idris 2 (no native negation).  tools.cli's approach: PARENTHESIZED-`no`-INFIX-TOGGLE — corpus's **first documented negatable-boolean-via-bracket-no-infix CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **tools.cli `:parse-fn` + `:assoc-fn` separate hooks** at runner.clj:35-39, 41-46, 48-49 — `:parse-fn` transforms the string value to its typed form; `:assoc-fn` controls HOW the value is stored (default: REPLACES, custom: ACCUMULATES).  `:assoc-fn accumulate` makes the option REPEATABLE — each occurrence appends to a vector via `(fn [m k v] (update m k (fnil conj []) v))`.  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 (Maybe vs List in record), R311 Guile `(value #t)` (no per-option transformation), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec with comma-separated arrays).  tools.cli's approach: `:parse-fn` + `:assoc-fn` AS INDEPENDENT HOOKS — caller controls type coercion and accumulation separately.  Corpus's **first documented separate-parse-and-assoc-functions CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **tools.cli single-map-with-keyword-keys parse-result** at runner.clj:149, 158 — `(cli/parse-opts args cli-options)` returns a single Clojure map with `:options` (parsed opts), `:arguments` (remaining positionals), `:errors` (any parse errors as strings), `:summary` (formatted help text generated from the spec).  Distinct from R302 babashka.cli `cli/dispatch` (subcommand-aware, returns parsed-opts map directly), R311 Guile `getopt-long` (alist return), R313 Idris 2 `getOpt` (MkResult 4-tuple).  tools.cli returns a SINGLE MAP with NAMED KEYS — Clojure-idiomatic destructuring.  Corpus's **first documented single-map-with-keyword-keys CLI return pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kaocha uses **tools.cli partial-auto-help via `:summary` key** at runner.clj:167-172 — `(cli/parse-opts ...)` returns `:summary` containing just the OPTIONS TABLE.  Caller wraps it with custom USAGE: header and trailing notes.  Distinct from R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (help_text passed to set_args — full help text), R311 Guile `usageInfo` helper (generates whole help), R313 Idris 2 `usageInfo` helper (same as Guile).  tools.cli's approach: PARTIAL-AUTO-HELP — library generates the option table, caller wraps with usage line.  Corpus's **first documented partial-auto-help-options-table CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_clojure` (Clojure CLI in corpus), `_lein` (Leiningen in corpus + blacklisted), `_naga` (Naga rules engine already in corpus), `_bb` / `_neil` / `_bbin` (babashka tools, in corpus), `_clj-kondo` / `_cljfmt` / `_shadow-cljs` (Clojure tools in corpus).  Skipped `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `walmartlabs/lacinia` (1856 stars but it's a library, perf.clj is just an example), `noprompt/frak` (1132 stars but transforms regex collections — narrow CLI), `ftomassetti/effectivejava` (506 stars but query tool with simple CLI), `constacts/ragtacts` (92 stars, RAG framework — main.clj is small), `benzap/eden` (142 stars, embedded scripting language — REPL-focused), `lambdaisland/kaocha` (CHOSEN — 860 stars, popular Clojure test runner).  Chose kaocha: real production test framework used by thousands of Clojure projects, 13 options in a single 200-line runner.clj, exercises EVERY MAJOR tools.cli PATTERN (`:require [clojure.tools.cli :as cli]` import, vector-of-vectors spec, `--[no-]NAME` negatable boolean, `:default` / `:parse-fn` / `:assoc-fn` keyword-value spec args, custom parse functions for keywords/symbols, `accumulate` :assoc-fn for repeatable options, `cli/parse-opts` returns single map with `:options`/`:arguments`/`:errors`/`:summary` keys, partial-auto-help via `:summary` key wrapped with custom usage banner), real binary at `bin/kaocha` distributed via Clojars and Homebrew (`brew install kaocha` from `lambdaisland/brew` tap).
  - Blacklist update: `kaocha` added.  Corpus count: 28,740 → 28,741 (+1).

- **R317 — sprite-tool source-direct (sprites.dev AI-agent CLI in Forth (Gforth); stdlib `next-arg` + handwritten matching)** (1 file new) — pivots from C++ boost::program_options (R316 leela) into the **Forth concatenative-language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Forth CLI pattern**.  sprite-tool is a CLI tool for managing AI coding agents on sprites.dev — push plans, auto-checkpoint, monitor progress.  Authored by phiat (3 GH stars; described in README as "mad opus 4.6 porting speedrunning").  The Forth port at `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` is one of several implementations in the sprite-scripts repo.  Sister tools: sprite (the underlying sprites.dev CLI client), bd (Beads dependency tracker used internally).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Forth CLI pattern** — Forth has NO FORMAL CLI PARSER LIBRARY in the stdlib (the language's philosophy is "language-as-library"); `next-arg` is Gforth's argv-accessor primitive, and the caller writes parsing logic by hand using `str=` and stack operations.  Adds Forth/Gforth to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (56 library variants now).  Forth joins as one of the OLDEST languages still in use (Chuck Moore, 1968) with the MOST MINIMAL CLI handling — language-as-library where the parser IS the application code.
  - `_sprite-tool` (1-stem; phiat/sprite-scripts ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs; **Gforth stdlib `next-arg` primitive + handwritten matching** — no formal CLI library; **4 subcommands (launch/push/pull/watch) + handwritten flag matching via `2dup s"..."  str= if 2drop ... then` + nested `then`-chains for flag dispatch + `next-arg` Gforth primitive returning `(addr len)` on stack + `s"..."  str=` literal-string-on-stack matching + `{ var }` Gforth locals syntax + `." ... " cr` handwritten help text + `main bye` fall-through-top-level invocation + Gforth `--` separator handling + `create NAME N allot` static buffer allocation**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` line 1 (`#! /usr/bin/env gforth` — Forth SHEBANG via Gforth, the GNU Forth implementation)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` line 4 (`\ Subcommands: launch, push, pull, watch` — Forth `\` BACKSLASH-COMMENT-TO-EOL line comment; sister `( ... )` INLINE COMMENT also standard)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 11-14 (`4096 constant cmd-max` + `create cmdbuf cmd-max allot` — Forth STATIC MEMORY ALLOCATION; no heap, uses static memory)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 462-471 (`: collect-args ( -- ) 0 arg-count ! begin next-arg dup 0<> while ... repeat then 2drop ;` — `next-arg` is GFORTH'S PRIMITIVE returning `(addr len)` or `(0 0)` when exhausted; stack-based loop accumulates argv)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 477-481 (`: arg-is-help? ( i -- flag ) fetch-arg 2dup s" --help" str= if 2drop true exit then s" -h" str= ;` — handwritten flag matching via `s"..."  str=`)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 488-516 (`: do-launch ( -- ) ... begin idx arg-count @ < parsing and while ... 2dup s" --dry-run" str= if 2drop 1 flag-dry-run ! ... else 2dup s" --no-checkpoint" str= if ... else 2dup s" --upload" str= if ... else ... then then then ...` — DEEPLY NESTED `else 2dup s"..."  str= if ... then` chain; each `then` closes one `if`-`else`)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 490, 493 (`0 { idx }` and `true { parsing }` — Gforth LOCAL VARIABLES; `{ name }` declares a local bound to top-of-stack value; `to idx` STORES a new value)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 391-446 (`: usage-main ( -- ) ." Usage: sprite-tool <command> [args...]" cr cr ... ;` — HELP TEXT via handwritten `." ... " cr` calls; no auto-generation)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` lines 945-957 (`: main ( -- ) next-arg dup 0= if 2drop usage-main then 2dup s" --" str= if 2drop next-arg then ... 2dup s" launch" str= if 2drop collect-args do-launch exit then ... ; main bye` — TOP-LEVEL DISPATCH; `main bye` at file bottom invokes main then exits)
    * `ports/forth/sprite-tool.fs` line 952 (`2dup s" --" str= if 2drop next-arg then` — handles GFORTH'S `--` SEPARATOR convention)
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Gforth stdlib `next-arg` primitive + handwritten matching** at sprite-tool.fs:468 et seq — corpus's **first documented Forth CLI pattern**.  Forth has NO FORMAL CLI PARSER LIBRARY in the stdlib — the language's philosophy is that every program builds the abstractions it needs from PRIMITIVES.  `next-arg` is Gforth's argv-accessor primitive that returns `(addr len)` pairs on the stack (or `(0 0)` when exhausted); the caller writes the parsing logic by hand using `str=` for string comparison and stack operations for control flow.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (provides at least an option-string abstraction), R287 Nim parseopt (token iterator at language level), R311 Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)` (full spec language).  Forth's approach: LANGUAGE-AS-LIBRARY — the CLI parser IS the application code.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-SIX library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ 1 CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), C++ 2 boost::program_options (R316), **Forth/Gforth `next-arg`** (R317 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 56 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Forth joins the taxonomy as one of the OLDEST languages still in use (Chuck Moore, 1968) with the MOST MINIMAL CLI handling.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Forth handwritten-stack-string-equality matching** at sprite-tool.fs:477-481, 500-516, 949-957 — `2dup s" --help" str= if 2drop ... then` is the canonical pattern.  `s" ..."` creates an `(addr len)` pair on the stack; `str=` compares two `(addr len)` strings on the stack and pushes a Forth flag (true/false).  `2dup` duplicates the top 2 cells (preserving the arg-string for next match), `2drop` cleans them when matched.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (option-string parsed internally), R311 Guile `getopt-long` spec, R307 Vala GLib OptionEntry table.  Forth's approach: HANDWRITTEN STRING-EQUALITY MATCHING — caller writes the matching code explicitly using stack operations.  Corpus's **first documented handwritten-stack-string-equality CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Forth nested `then`-chains for flag dispatch** at sprite-tool.fs:500-516 — DEEPLY NESTED `else 2dup s"..."  str= if ... else 2dup ...` chain.  Forth has no `match`/`case` for strings, so each flag becomes ANOTHER nested `if ... else ... then` arm.  Each `then` CLOSES one `if`-`else`.  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 ADT pattern-match, R290 Racket `match`, R311 Guile `case`/`cond`, R314 Mercury `;`-separated Prolog clauses.  Forth's approach: NESTED-`then` CHAINS — corpus's **first documented nested-if-chain CLI dispatch pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Gforth `{ var }` local-variable syntax** at sprite-tool.fs:490, 493 — `0 { idx }` and `true { parsing }` declare locals bound to the top-of-stack value.  `to idx` STORES a new value to the local.  Distinct from `variable VAR` / `VAR !` (Forth global variable + `!` store) — locals are scoped to the word, globals are module-level.  Sister to R311 Guile `(let ((var value)) ...)` (lexical bindings) but Gforth locals have RPN syntax.  Corpus's **first documented Gforth-locals-`{ }`-syntax CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Forth handwritten `." ... "` usage text** at sprite-tool.fs:391-446 — HELP TEXT generation via inline `." text" cr` STRING-OUTPUT-LITERAL.  Forth's `." text"` is a COMPILE-TIME-STRING + PRINT-WORD combined token; `cr` prints carriage return.  No `--help` auto-generation because there's no formal spec to generate from — each usage block is hand-written.  Distinct from R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (help_text passed as array to set_args), R311 Guile `usageInfo` helper, R313 Idris 2 `usageInfo` helper.  Forth's approach: HANDWRITTEN HELP via successive `." ... " cr` calls.  Corpus's **first documented handwritten-`."`-help CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Forth fall-through-top-level invocation** at sprite-tool.fs:966 — `main bye` at the FILE BOTTOM invokes the `main` word then calls `bye` to exit Gforth.  Forth files are INTERPRETED LINEARLY — when Gforth reaches `main bye`, it RUNS those words immediately.  Distinct from R311 Guile (must `(main args)` inside `(define (main ...))`), R313 Idris 2 `main : IO ()` (IO monad), R310 Fortran `program iapwscli` (named program block), R305 C++ `int main(int argc, char** argv)` (formal main function).  Forth's approach: NO MAIN FUNCTION — the entire script is just words; the LAST WORDS in the file ARE the entry-point invocation.  Corpus's **first documented fall-through-top-level CLI invocation pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sprite-tool uses **Forth `s"..."  str=` literal-string-on-stack pattern** at sprite-tool.fs:477-481, 500-516 — `s" --help"` is a COMPILE-TIME STRING LITERAL that pushes `(addr len)` onto the stack at runtime.  Distinct from R272 C `"..."` (C string literal — null-terminated), R270 Haskell `"..."` (immutable String type), R313 Idris 2 `"..."` (typed String literal).  Forth's approach: STRING-AS-STACK-PAIR — each string is TWO stack cells `(address, length)` rather than a pointer or boxed value.  This is the QUINTESSENTIAL Forth string convention, affecting how every CLI matching operation is expressed.  Corpus's **first documented `(addr, len)`-stack-pair string CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_beadm` (STOCK DUP — illumos boot-environment admin tool has `_beadm` in `/opt/homebrew/share/zsh/site-functions` and stock zsh; ironic since `illumos/illumos-gate beadm.4th` is itself Forth code!), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh), `_gforth` (Gforth itself in corpus).  Skipped `_melter` (declined per previous round — Hacking Team Win32 PE backdoor injector; security-policy concern).  Probed `kanaka/mal impls/forth/core.fs` (Mal Forth implementation — REPL, not CLI tool), `forthy42/gforth heartbleed.fs` (specific exploit demo), various `manwar/perlweeklychallenge-club` Forth challenge solutions (one-shot scripts not real CLIs), `harrypower/Rpi_Gforth_GPIO` (Pi GPIO project — not CLI surface), `ronaaron/reva argv.f` (Reva Forth, different Forth dialect — would document a different "library"; deferred), `openbios/openfirmware cpu/mips/argv.fth` (OpenFirmware, embedded — too specialized), `CoSyBob/CoSy examples/zipdb.f` (CoSy Forth — different dialect; deferred), `illumos/illumos-gate beadm.4th` (illumos uses Forth for boot environment management — STOCK DUP with `_beadm`).  Chose sprite-tool: 3 GH stars (small but real user-facing tool), 4 subcommands (launch/push/pull/watch) in single 966-line sprite-tool.fs, exercises EVERY MAJOR Gforth CLI PATTERN (shebang, `\` line comments and `( ... )` inline, `create NAME N allot` static buffer allocation, `variable` globals, `{ var }` Gforth locals, `next-arg` stdlib primitive argv accessor, `s"..."  str=` literal-string-on-stack matching, nested `then`-chains for flag dispatch, handwritten `." ... " cr` help text, `main bye` fall-through-top-level invocation, Gforth `--` separator handling), real binary distributed as a shebang script.
  - Blacklist update: `sprite-tool` added.  Corpus count: 28,739 → 28,740 (+1).

- **R316 — leela source-direct (Leela Go board-game AI by Gian-Carlo Pascutto in C++; `boost::program_options`)** (1 file new) — pivots from Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315 gecop) into the **C++ `boost::program_options` ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented `boost::program_options` CLI library** + **second documented C++ CLI library** (joining R305 CLI11).  leela is Leela Go — Gian-Carlo Pascutto's computer Go program combining Monte Carlo simulations and Neural Networks.  Pascutto is a major figure in computer game AI (originally created Sjeng chess engine, later Stockfish-inspired Leela Chess Zero / lc0).  This `Leela` binary is the GO (board game) flavor — predecessor to leela-zero (LZ; AlphaGo-style training).  Speaks GTP for integration with Go GUIs (Sabaki, GoGui).  75 GH stars.  Sister tools: leela-zero (cleaner AlphaGo-Zero-style engine), katago (modern Go AI), lc0 (Leela Chess Zero — chess variant of the same concept).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented `boost::program_options` library** — adds `boost::program_options` (the CLASSIC widely-used C++ CLI library from Boost since 2004, requires linking against `libboost_program_options`) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (55 library variants now).  C++ joins the multi-library club (after Rust 6, Go 4, Python 3, Crystal 2, Common Lisp 2, Nim 2): TWO C++ CLI library variants now documented.
  - `_leela` (1-stem; gcp/Leela Leela.cpp; **C++ `boost::program_options` library** — Boost since 1.32 (2004), Vladimir Prus; **13 visible options + tuner options behind USE_TUNER + `po::options_description` spec object + `add_options()` returning an `options_description_easy_init` with overloaded `operator()` for chained spec calls + `"long,short"` comma-separated-names-in-one-string naming + `po::value<T>()->default_value(N)` typed-value with method-chained default + separate `h_desc` "Hidden options" composition via `all.add(v_desc).add(h_desc)` + `po::positional_options_description` with `.add("name", N)` positional-to-option-name mapping (with `-1` for unlimited) + `po::command_line_parser(argc, argv).options(all).positional(p_desc).run()` method-chain pipeline parser + `po::store(RESULT, vm)` + `po::notify(vm)` + `vm.count("name")` flag-presence check + `vm["name"].as<T>()` typed-cast retrieval**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `Leela.cpp` line 9 (`#ifdef USE_OPTIONS #include <boost/program_options.hpp> #endif` — header include behind compile-time guard; `boost/program_options.hpp` is the UMBRELLA HEADER pulling in 6+ component headers; requires LINKING against `libboost_program_options`)
    * `Leela.cpp` line 30 (`namespace po = boost::program_options;` — idiomatic NAMESPACE ALIAS at function/translation-unit scope)
    * `Leela.cpp` line 32 (`po::options_description v_desc("Allowed options");` — declares SPEC OBJECT; string arg is GROUP HEADING in --help)
    * `Leela.cpp` lines 33-46 (`v_desc.add_options() ("help,h", ...)("gtp,g", ...)("threads,t", po::value<int>()->default_value(cfg_num_threads), ...) ;` — overloaded-paren-operator chain; each `("name,short", TYPE, "help")` adds one option)
    * `Leela.cpp` line 36 (`po::value<int>()->default_value(cfg_num_threads)` — TYPED VALUE DESCRIPTOR with METHOD-CHAINED default)
    * `Leela.cpp` lines 81-86 (`po::options_description h_desc("Hidden options"); h_desc.add_options() ("arguments", po::value<std::vector<std::string>>()); ... po::options_description all("All options"); all.add(v_desc).add(h_desc);` — separate hidden options group composed with visible)
    * `Leela.cpp` lines 88-89 (`po::positional_options_description p_desc; p_desc.add("arguments", -1);` — positional args map to OPTION NAME "arguments"; `-1` means UNLIMITED COUNT)
    * `Leela.cpp` lines 91-99 (`po::variables_map vm; try { po::store(po::command_line_parser(argc, argv).options(all).positional(p_desc).run(), vm); po::notify(vm); } catch(...)` — METHOD-CHAIN PIPELINE PARSER pattern)
    * `Leela.cpp` line 101 (`if (vm.count("help") || vm.count("arguments")) { ... }` — `count(NAME)` returns NUMBER of times option was seen)
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **C++ `boost::program_options` library** at Leela.cpp:9+30 — corpus's **first documented `boost::program_options` library** + **second documented C++ CLI library** (joining R305 CLI11).  `boost::program_options` is the CLASSIC widely-used C++ CLI library from Boost (introduced in Boost 1.32, 2004; Vladimir Prus).  Boost-heavy: requires linking against `libboost_program_options` (NOT header-only unlike R305 CLI11).  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 (header-only, single `CLI/CLI.hpp` include), `cxxopts` (simpler header-only), R272 C POSIX getopt (lower-level, no help/error generation).  Boost is the OLDEST modern C++ CLI library — pre-dates CLI11 by ~12 years — and still widely used in projects with existing Boost dependencies.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-FIVE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), **C++ 1 CLI11 (R305)**, Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER (R315), **C++ 2 boost::program_options** (R316 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 55 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  C++ joins the multi-library club: TWO C++ CLI library variants now documented (CLI11 + Boost).
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost overloaded-paren-operator CLI spec pattern** at Leela.cpp:33-46 — `v_desc.add_options() ("help,h", ...)("gtp,g", ...)("threads,t", ...) ;`.  `add_options()` returns an `options_description_easy_init` object whose `operator()` is overloaded to accept option specs.  CHAINING is via REPEATED `operator()` calls — each `("name,short", TYPE_VALUE, "help")` adds one option.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 `add_option(name, ref, help)` (method-chain via `->`), R272 C POSIX getopt (option-string), R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec).  Boost's approach: OVERLOADED-OPERATOR-PAREN CHAIN — C++-unique idiom exploiting operator overloading.  Corpus's **first documented overloaded-paren-operator CLI spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost comma-separated-names-string CLI naming** at Leela.cpp:34-46 — `"help,h"` packs LONG + SHORT names into ONE STRING with a COMMA SEPARATOR.  Distinct from R270 Haskell `Option [Char] [String]` (separate lists), R309 SML `{short = "vV", long = ["verbose"]}` (separate fields), R313 Idris 2 `MkOpt ['h'] ["help"] ...` (positional args).  Boost's approach: COMMA-SEPARATED-NAMES-IN-ONE-STRING — corpus's **first documented comma-separated-names-string CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost separate-description-for-hidden-options pattern** at Leela.cpp:81-86 — TWO separate `options_description` objects: `v_desc` ("visible") for --help display, `h_desc` ("hidden") to catch unrecognized positionals.  Then COMPOSED via `all.add(v_desc).add(h_desc)`.  The parser sees BOTH but only `v_desc` is shown in --help.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (no hidden options concept), R301 Nim cligen (no hidden flags), R305 C++ CLI11 (`->group("")` to hide from help — single description).  Boost's approach: SEPARATE-DESCRIPTION-FOR-HIDDEN — corpus's **first documented separate-description-for-hidden-options CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost positional-to-option-name-mapping** at Leela.cpp:88-89 — `p_desc.add("arguments", -1);`.  `positional_options_description::add(NAME, MAX_COUNT)` maps argv positions to OPTION NAMES.  `-1` means UNLIMITED COUNT (all remaining positionals go to "arguments").  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 `ReturnInOrder Input` (wraps each positional in a constructor), R305 C++ CLI11 (positional detected by no-leading-dash name), R307 Vala GLib (empty-string-name OptionEntry sentinel).  Boost's approach: POSITIONAL-INDEX-TO-OPTION-NAME MAP — corpus's **first documented positional-to-option-name-mapping CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost method-chain-pipeline parser** at Leela.cpp:91-99 — `po::store(po::command_line_parser(argc, argv).options(all).positional(p_desc).run(), vm); po::notify(vm);` — multi-stage pipeline: (1) `command_line_parser(argc, argv)` builder, (2) `.options(all)` attach spec, (3) `.positional(p_desc)` attach positionals, (4) `.run()` execute, returns intermediate, (5) `po::store(RESULT, vm)` write to vm, (6) `po::notify(vm)` run notifiers/validators.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 `CLI11_PARSE(app, argc, argv)` macro (single call), R311 Guile `(getopt-long args SPEC)` (function call), R313 Idris 2 `getOpt POLICY DESCS ARGS` (3-arg call).  Boost's approach: METHOD-CHAIN PIPELINE PARSER — corpus's **first documented method-chain-pipeline CLI parser pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: leela uses **boost `vm.count("name")` flag-presence check** at Leela.cpp:101,116-118 — `vm.count("help")` returns the NUMBER of times the option was seen (0 for missing, 1+ for present).  Then `vm["name"].as<T>()` retrieves the typed value.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 (`app.count(name)` also works but values are usually bound by reference at spec time), R312 Pascal `HasOption('h', 'help')` (paired-short-and-long), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 `lget('flag')` (typed accessor function), R311 Guile `(option-ref options 'help #f)` (alist lookup).  Boost's approach: VARIABLES_MAP-COUNT-AND-CAST — separate operations for "was it present?" and "what value?".  Corpus's **first documented count-and-cast CLI value-retrieval pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_pcb2gcode` (491 stars, already in corpus), `_delly` / `_alfred` (bioinformatics, already in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh), `_melter` (SKIPPED — Hacking Team malware injector for Win32 PE backdoors; security-policy concern: leaked-surveillance-vendor code for weaponizing executables), `_avatar` (108 stars, generic name).  Probed `tobiasrausch/alfred` (in corpus), `gear-genomics/tracy` (122 stars, Sanger sequencing trace tool — strong candidate but specialized bioinformatics CLI), `ncbi/SKESA` (126 stars, bacterial assembler from NCBI — strong but spread spec across multiple files), `tudelft3d/prepair` (114 stars, geometry repair tool — smaller CLI surface), `gcp/Leela` (CHOSEN — 75 stars, Gian-Carlo Pascutto's Go-playing AI program), `fastio/1store` (1331 stars but server with many subsystems; smaller CLI for top-level), `aaalgo/kgraph` (387 stars, k-NN library but spec inside complex template code), `royshil/SfM-Toy-Library` (539 stars but binary name `SfMToyUI` is CamelCase awkward for zsh), `Taywee/args` (THE LIBRARY ITSELF, not a CLI).  Chose leela: real tournament-level Go AI program by major computer-game-AI researcher (creator of Leela Chess Zero), 13 visible options + many tuner options behind USE_TUNER, exercises EVERY MAJOR boost::program_options PATTERN (options_description, overloaded-paren-operator chain, comma-separated names, typed-value with default_value, hidden-description-composition, positional_options_description with name-mapping, command_line_parser pipeline, variables_map count+cast), clean single-file spec, real binary distributed via Makefile and AUR (`leela-go`).
  - Blacklist update: `leela` added.  Corpus count: 28,738 → 28,739 (+1).

- **R315 — gecop source-direct (Gobo Eiffel Cop conformance validation suite; Gobo `AP_PARSER` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314 mmc-doc) into the **Eiffel Design-by-Contract language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Eiffel CLI library**.  gecop ("Gobo Eiffel Cop") is an Eiffel language conformance validation suite — runs a test suite against various Eiffel compilers (gec, gelint, ISE EiffelStudio, etc.) and reports compatibility.  Authored by Eric Bezault as part of the Gobo Eiffel Project (62 GH stars).  Sister tools: gec (Gobo Eiffel Compiler, in corpus as `_gec`), gecc (Gobo Eiffel C Compilation, DEFERRED for separate round), gedoc (Gobo Eiffel Documentation generator, DEFERRED), gelint (Gobo Eiffel Lint, DEFERRED), gepp (Gobo Eiffel Preprocessor, DEFERRED).  Multi-binary Gobo toolchain offers 3+ future R-rounds.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Eiffel CLI library** — adds Gobo `AP_PARSER` library (multiple-inheritance-with-export-controls, Design-by-Contract preconditions, typed option classes `AP_FLAG`/`AP_STRING_OPTION`/`AP_INTEGER_OPTION`, `.extend(VALUE)` enum restriction, `AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST` for mutex options, instantiate-configure-append spec pattern) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (54 library variants now).
  - `_gecop` (1-stem; gobo-eiffel/gobo tool/gecop/src/gecop.e; **Eiffel Gobo `AP_PARSER` library** — library/argument package from the Gobo Eiffel Project, the most popular Eiffel CLI library; **10 options + version flag + multiple inheritance via `UT_GOBO_CLI`/`KL_SHARED_EXCEPTIONS`/etc. + multiple creation procedures (`execute`/`execute_with_arguments`/`execute_with_arguments_and_error_handler`) + Design-by-Contract preconditions `require a_args_not_void: a_args /= Void` + typed option classes `AP_FLAG`/`AP_STRING_OPTION`/`AP_INTEGER_OPTION` + `make_with_long_form("name")` constructor + `.extend(VALUE)` for enum allowed values + Eiffel `'V'` single-quote char-literal short flag + `AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST` first-class mutex structure + `l_parser.options.force_last(OPT)` registration + `parse_array(args)` parse invocation + `was_found` boolean accessor**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 9 (`class GECOP` — Eiffel CLASS DECLARATION; ALL-UPPERCASE CLASS NAMES are Eiffel convention)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` lines 11-25 (`inherit UT_GOBO_CLI, KL_SHARED_EXCEPTIONS, KL_SHARED_ARGUMENTS, UT_SHARED_ISE_VARIABLES, UT_SHARED_GOBO_VARIABLES` — Eiffel MULTIPLE INHERITANCE; `UT_GOBO_CLI` provides shared CLI infrastructure; `export {NONE} all end` clauses hide inherited features)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` lines 28-32 (`create execute, execute_with_arguments, execute_with_arguments_and_error_handler` — Eiffel CREATE CLAUSE lists MULTIPLE CREATION PROCEDURES; any of these three procedures can serve as the constructor)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` lines 528-535 (`require a_args_not_void: a_args /= Void no_void_arg: across a_args as i_arg all i_arg /= Void end` — Eiffel DESIGN-BY-CONTRACT PRECONDITIONS; named typed assertions checked at compile and runtime)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 535 (`create l_parser.make` — Eiffel INSTANTIATION; `create OBJ.PROC` calls the `PROC` creation procedure)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` lines 538-548 (`create tool_option.make_with_long_form ("tool")` + `tool_option.set_description("...")` + `tool_option.extend("ge")` for each allowed value — AP_STRING_OPTION with ENUMERATED ALLOWED VALUES via `.extend(VALUE)`; parser rejects values not in the enum)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 589 (`create version_flag.make ('V', "version")` — AP_FLAG with EIFFEL CHARACTER LITERAL `'V'`; Eiffel uses SINGLE QUOTES for char literals like C/Pony/Hare)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 535-589 (`l_parser.options.force_last (tool_option)` per option — REGISTRATION via Gobo DS_LIST method; INSTANTIATE-CONFIGURE-APPEND pattern)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` lines 591-592 (`create l_list.make (version_flag) l_parser.alternative_options_lists.force_last (l_list)` — AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST is a Gobo-specific FIRST-CLASS mutex structure)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 595 (`l_parser.parse_array (a_args)` — PARSE INVOCATION; parser MUTATES the previously-created option instances)
    * `tool/gecop/src/gecop.e` line 597 (`if version_flag.was_found then ...` — `AP_FLAG.was_found` boolean accessor)
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Gobo Eiffel `AP_PARSER` library** at gecop.e:528 — corpus's **first documented Eiffel CLI library**.  Gobo Argument Parser library (library/argument/) is part of the Gobo Eiffel Project (62 GH stars, Eric Bezault et al, 2006-2018), the most-used Eiffel third-party libraries collection.  Distinct from raw `ARGUMENTS_32` Eiffel stdlib class (just provides `argument_count` + `argument(I)` accessors; no parser — caller writes manual matching), and from EiffelBase's `KL_ARGUMENT_LIST` (also low-level access).  Gobo AP_PARSER adds: typed option classes (`AP_FLAG`/`AP_STRING_OPTION`/`AP_INTEGER_OPTION`/`AP_BOOLEAN_OPTION`), enum-restriction via `.extend(VALUE)`, alternative-options-lists for mutex, automatic help generation.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-FOUR library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313), Mercury stdlib getopt_io (R314), **Eiffel Gobo AP_PARSER** (R315 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 54 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Eiffel multiple-inheritance-with-feature-export-controls CLI pattern** at gecop.e:11-25 — `inherit UT_GOBO_CLI, KL_SHARED_EXCEPTIONS, ...`.  Eiffel supports MULTIPLE INHERITANCE with explicit feature-level EXPORT STATUS control (`export {NONE} all end` hides all inherited features from clients).  Distinct from R312 Pascal `class(TCustomApplication)` (single inheritance), R305 C++ CLI11 (no inheritance — composition), R313 Idris 2 (no inheritance).  Eiffel's approach: MULTIPLE INHERITANCE WITH EXPORT CONTROL — corpus's **first documented multiple-inheritance-with-export-controls CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Eiffel Design-by-Contract preconditions in CLI parse routine** at gecop.e:528-535 — `require a_args_not_void: a_args /= Void no_void_arg: across a_args as i_arg all i_arg /= Void end` uses Eiffel's UNIQUE DESIGN-BY-CONTRACT mechanism.  `require` block introduces named preconditions; each is a typed assertion that the COMPILER checks (compile-time) AND the RUNTIME verifies (when assertions are enabled).  Distinct from EVERY other CLI library in the corpus — Eiffel is the ONLY language with built-in Design-by-Contract (Bertrand Meyer, 1985).  Sister features: `ensure` postconditions, `invariant` class invariants, `check` inline assertions.  Corpus's **first documented Design-by-Contract CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Gobo `AP_STRING_OPTION.extend(VALUE)` for ENUM allowed values** at gecop.e:541-548 — `tool_option.extend("ge")` / `.extend("ise")` / `.extend("gec")` etc. add ALLOWED VALUES to the option.  The parser will REJECT values not in the enum.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `:option-type :choice :choices '("a" "b")` (constructor kwarg), R305 C++ CLI11 `->check(CLI::IsMember(set))` (validator method), R301 Nim cligen (no enum restriction at spec time).  Gobo's approach: METHOD-CHAIN EXTEND for ENUM — corpus's **first documented extend-for-enum CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Eiffel `'V'` single-quote char-literal short flag** at gecop.e:589 — `create version_flag.make ('V', "version")`.  AP_FLAG's creation procedure takes `(SHORT_CHAR, LONG_STR)`.  Eiffel uses SINGLE QUOTES for CHARACTER LITERALS (like C, Pony, Hare).  Sister to R291 CL `#\h`, R292 Erlang `$h`, R298 Pony `'p'`, R300 V `` `n` ``, R308 Hare `'1'`, R311 Guile `#\X`.  **EIGHTH character-literal CLI cluster language**: CL `#\X` (R291, R295), Erlang `$X` (R292), Pony `'X'` (R298), V `` `X` `` (R300), Hare `'X'` (R308), Guile `#\X` (R311), **Eiffel `'X'`** (R315 — NEW).  Corpus's **eighth character-literal CLI cluster language**.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Gobo `AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST` for mutex options** at gecop.e:591-592 — `create l_list.make (version_flag) l_parser.alternative_options_lists.force_last (l_list)`.  AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST is a Gobo-specific SET OF OPTIONS where ONLY ONE may be specified at a time (mutual exclusion).  When `-V` is specified, OTHER options in the same list become INVALID.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (no built-in mutex), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (no mutex), R303 Gleam Glint (no mutex), R305 C++ CLI11 `->excludes(...)` method, R306 Elixir Optimus (no mutex).  Gobo's approach: ALTERNATIVE-OPTIONS-LIST as a FIRST-CLASS structure — corpus's **first documented alternative-options-list-as-first-class CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gecop uses **Eiffel instantiate-configure-append option-spec pattern** at gecop.e:538 et seq — each option is INSTANTIATED with `create OPT.make_with_long_form (NAME)`, CONFIGURED via setter calls (`set_description`, `set_parameter_description`, `extend`), then APPENDED to the parser's option list with `l_parser.options.force_last (OPT)`.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature spec — no append), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (vector of maps constructed at once), R307 Vala GLib (struct-literal table built statically), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec).  Gobo's approach: INSTANTIATE-CONFIGURE-APPEND — each option is BUILT UP STEP-BY-STEP before registration.  Corpus's **first documented instantiate-configure-append CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_gec` (Gobo Eiffel Compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted — uses AP_PARSER but ~50+ option surface), `_alr` (Ada package manager, not Eiffel), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `simple-eiffel/simple_graphviz` (0 stars — uses MANUAL argument-parsing via ARGUMENTS_32, NOT a CLI library; deferred for handwritten-parser-pattern round), `oligot/epm` (Eiffel Package Manager — uses AP_PARSER but only 1 subcommand 'sync' — smaller surface than gecop), `EiffelWebFramework/EWF gewf` (46 stars but framework-internal, less standalone), `gobo-eiffel/gobo gecc` (Gobo Eiffel C Compilation — 2-option surface, sister to gecop; DEFERRED for separate round), `gobo-eiffel/gobo gedoc` (Gobo Eiffel Doc generator — sister tool; DEFERRED), `gobo-eiffel/gobo gelint` (Eiffel linter; sister tool, DEFERRED), `imustafin/brie_doom` (11 stars — DOOM source port; specialized).  Chose gecop: 10 options + version flag in a single 600-line gecop.e (real conformance-test driver distributed with Gobo Eiffel), exercises EVERY MAJOR Gobo AP_PARSER PATTERN (multiple inheritance with UT_GOBO_CLI, multiple creation procedures, DBC preconditions, AP_STRING_OPTION/AP_INTEGER_OPTION/AP_FLAG typed option classes, `make_with_long_form` constructor, `.extend()` for enum values, `'V'` Eiffel char-literal short flag, AP_ALTERNATIVE_OPTIONS_LIST for mutex, `l_parser.options.force_last` registration, `parse_array` parse invocation, `was_found` boolean accessor), real binary distributed via Gobo's installer + brew/AUR packages.  Sister Gobo tools provide 3+ future R-rounds.
  - Blacklist update: `gecop` added.  Corpus count: 28,737 → 28,738 (+1).

- **R314 — mmc-doc source-direct (Mercury documentation lookup tool; stdlib `getopt_io` module)** (1 file new) — pivots from Idris 2 System.GetOpts (R313 ttc-converter) into the **Mercury logic-functional language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Mercury CLI library**.  mmc-doc is a Mercury documentation lookup tool — pulls up Mercury stdlib documentation by module name, searches stdlib modules for predicate/function declarations.  Authored by jrfondren (6 GH stars).  Sister tools: mmc (Mercury compiler; in corpus as `_mmc`), the Mercury compiler itself which uses an internal getopt_io-based CLI for its hundreds of compiler flags.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Mercury CLI library** — adds Mercury stdlib `getopt_io` (Prolog-fact-as-option-spec pattern with strong static types and determinism annotations, typed-union `option_data` defaults, predicates-as-first-class-values config, `!IO` destructive-update state-threading) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (53 library variants now).  Mercury joins the taxonomy as the SECOND logic-language CLI library after R297 SWI-Prolog optparse — corpus now documents CLI libraries in BOTH MAJOR LOGIC-PROGRAMMING DIALECTS.
  - `_mmc-doc` (1-stem; jrfondren/mmc-doc mmcdoc.m → binary `mmc-doc` per Makefile `BIN=mmc-doc`; **Mercury stdlib `getopt_io` module** — Mercury's standard-library CLI parser, IO-aware variant; **11 flags + 2 positionals + Prolog-fact-style `short_option('h', help).` predicates + `long_option("help", help).` parallel long-name facts + `option_defaults(NAME, bool(no))` typed-union default-value predicate + `:- type option ---> help ; version ; ...` discriminated-union ADT + `:- pred ... is semidet/is multi` determinism annotations + `option_ops_multi(short_option, long_option, option_defaults)` predicates-as-first-class-values config + `process_options_io(Config, RawArgs, Args, ResOpts, !IO)` parse with `!IO` state-threading + `lookup_bool_option`/`lookup_maybe_string_option` type-specific accessors**) decoded source-direct from:

    * `mmcdoc.m` lines 14-23 (`:- type option ---> help ; version ; local_docs ; no_cache ; list_modules ; refresh_cache ; index ; backup ; grep ; comment ; restrict.` — Mercury DISCRIMINATED UNION TYPE; `--->` introduces the constructors; each constructor separated by `;`)
    * `mmcdoc.m` lines 25-33 (`:- pred short_option(char::in, option::out) is semidet.` + `short_option('h', help).` ... — Mercury PREDICATE with MODE ANNOTATIONS; `char::in`/`option::out` are mode bindings; `is semidet` is determinism category; the CLAUSES are Prolog-style FACTS pattern-matching the input char and binding the option ADT constructor)
    * `mmcdoc.m` lines 35-43 (`:- pred long_option(string::in, option::out) is semidet.` + parallel long-name facts — separate predicate from short_option; Mercury's getopt_io takes SHORT and LONG as SEPARATE predicate arguments)

    * `mmcdoc.m` line 92 (`Config = option_ops_multi(short_option, long_option, option_defaults),` — Mercury constructs a CONFIG STRUCT carrying the three predicates as FIRST-CLASS VALUES; `option_ops_multi/3` is the getopt_io constructor for MULTI-CLAUSE option spec)
    * `mmcdoc.m` line 93 (`getopt_io.process_options_io(Config, RawArgs, Args, ResOpts, !IO),` — PARSE INVOCATION; returns `Args` (positionals) and `ResOpts` (parsed options as `ok(...)` or `error(...)`); `!IO` is Mercury's I/O STATE THREADING SYNTACTIC SUGAR)
    * `mmcdoc.m` lines 95-96 (`( ResOpts = ok(Options), getopt_io.lookup_bool_option(Options, backup, Backup),` — type-specific accessor; sister functions: `lookup_int_option`, `lookup_string_option`, `lookup_maybe_string_option`, `lookup_accumulating_option`)
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury stdlib `getopt_io` module** at mmcdoc.m:7 — corpus's **first documented Mercury CLI library**.  Mercury's stdlib `getopt_io` is the IO-AWARE variant of `getopt` — handles option parsing with explicit I/O state threading.  Implementation at `library/getopt_io.m` in the Mercury source tree.  Distinct from R297 SWI-Prolog `optparse` (term-based specs, no IO threading), R270 Haskell `optparse-applicative` (applicative-functor), R313 Idris 2 `System.GetOpts` (Haskell-port, list-of-MkOpt).  Mercury's approach uses Prolog-style FACT-PATTERNS for the option spec but adds STRONG STATIC TYPES and DETERMINISM annotations (`is semidet`/`is multi`). Mercury joins the taxonomy as the SECOND logic-language CLI library after R297 SWI-Prolog optparse — corpus now documents CLI libraries in BOTH MAJOR LOGIC-PROGRAMMING DIALECTS.
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury Prolog-fact-as-option-spec CLI pattern** at mmcdoc.m:25-43 — `short_option('h', help).` and `long_option("help", help).` are Prolog-style FACTS pattern-matching the input char/string and binding the corresponding option ADT constructor.  Mercury's strong typing (`:- pred short_option(char::in, option::out) is semidet`) makes these facts TYPED — the compiler enforces char→option mapping.  Distinct from R297 SWI-Prolog `optparse` (term-based OptSpec list), R313 Idris 2 `MkOpt ['h'] ["help"] ...` (positional constructor args), R270 Haskell `Option ['h'] ["help"] ...` (ADT constructor).  Mercury's approach is UNIQUE: the option spec IS the PROLOG-FACT-DATABASE, queried by the parser.  Corpus's **first documented Prolog-fact-as-option-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury `option_data` typed-union default-value spec** at mmcdoc.m:45-56 — `option_defaults(help, bool(no)).` and `option_defaults(restrict, maybe_string(no)).` use the `option_data` TAGGED-UNION type with variants `bool(yes|no)` boolean default, `int(N)` integer default, `string("...")` string default, `maybe_string(yes("...") | no)` optional string, `accumulating([...])` list-append default.  Distinct from R313 Idris 2 `NoArg ctor / ReqArg ctor "<plc>"` (constructor variant), R311 Guile `(value #t/#f/optional)` (boolean-or-symbol), R309 SML `NoArg/ReqArg/OptArg` constructor variants (with thunks/constructors).  Mercury's approach: TYPED-DEFAULT-AS-DISCRIMINATED-UNION — corpus's **first documented typed-union-default-value CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury `option_ops_multi(short_option, long_option, option_defaults)` predicates-as-first-class values** at mmcdoc.m:92 — Mercury constructs a CONFIG STRUCT carrying THREE PREDICATES as values.  `option_ops_multi/3` is the getopt_io constructor.  The predicates are passed BY NAME, not by inlining their data.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (option-string), R305 C++ CLI11 (struct + reference binding), R313 Idris 2 `descs : List $ OptDescr Option` (data list with all info inline).  Mercury's approach: PREDICATES-AS-FIRST-CLASS-VALUES — the option spec is DEFERRED to runtime predicate queries.  Corpus's **first documented predicates-as-data CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury `!IO` destructive-update state-threading** at mmcdoc.m:93+ — `getopt_io.process_options_io(Config, RawArgs, Args, ResOpts, !IO)` uses Mercury's `!IO` SYNTACTIC SUGAR for I/O state threading.  `!IO` desugars to TWO arguments `IO0, IO` (input and output state) — Mercury enforces SINGLE-THREADED I/O at the type level via the `io::di` (destructive input) and `io::uo` (unique output) modes.  Distinct from R270 Haskell `IO` monad (also linear I/O), R311 Guile (no I/O typing — side effects implicit), R272 C POSIX getopt (no I/O abstraction), R313 Idris 2 `HasIO io => io ...` (effect-aware but similar to Haskell).  Mercury's approach: `!Var` STATE-PASS-THROUGH SUGAR — corpus's **first documented destructive-state-threading CLI parse pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: mmc-doc uses **Mercury `:- mode option_defaults(out, out) is multi` multi-mode determinism declaration** at mmcdoc.m:46 — the `option_defaults` predicate is declared MULTI-MODE: `(out, out) is multi` says when both args are output, the predicate has MULTIPLE SOLUTIONS (Mercury's term for "many answers via backtracking").  The getopt_io library calls this in `(out, out)` mode to enumerate ALL option-default pairs.  Distinct from `is det` (deterministic — exactly one answer), `is semidet` (zero or one answer), `is nondet` (any number of answers).  Sister to R297 SWI-Prolog (no mode declarations — Prolog is implicitly multi-mode).  Mercury's mode system is what distinguishes it from Prolog: STATIC TYPING + MODE ANNOTATIONS + DETERMINISM CATEGORIES.  Corpus's **first documented multi-mode-determinism-annotation CLI pattern**.

Probed `testock/mercury-template` (0 stars, niche scaffolding tool with very small CLI), `Mercury-Language/mercury hlds_out_module.m` (compiler-internal, no standalone binary).  Chose mmc-doc: 6 GH stars (small but real user-facing utility), 12 FLAGS in a single 250-line mmcdoc.m, exercises EVERY MAJOR Mercury getopt_io PATTERN (PROLOG-fact-style short_option/long_option/option_defaults predicates, ADT for parsed options, `option_data` typed-union default values, `option_ops_multi` config with predicates as first-class values, `process_options_io` parse with `!IO` state threading, `lookup_bool_option`/`lookup_maybe_string_option` type-specific accessors), real Mercury binary distributed via the project's Makefile (`BIN=mmc-doc`).

- **R313 — ttc-converter source-direct (Idris 2 TTC-to-JSON converter; `System.GetOpts` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312 pascalcoin_miner) into the **Idris 2 dependently-typed language ecosystem** — corpus's **first documented Idris 2 CLI library**.  ttc-converter is an Idris 2 utility for converting TTC files (Idris 2's serialized typechecker output format) to JSON for inspection.  Authored by spcfox (1 GH star).  Sister tools: idris2 (Idris 2 compiler, in corpus as `_idris2`), idris2-pack (Idris 2 package manager, binary `pack` in corpus).  Idris 2 TTC files are binary intermediate representations stored alongside .idr source files; this tool exposes their internals for debugging compiler issues.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Idris 2 CLI library** — adds `System.GetOpts` (idris-community/idris2-getopts, a port of Haskell's `System.Console.GetOpt` to Idris 2 — same library lineage as R270 Haskell and R309 SML) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (52 library variants now).  Idris 2 joins the taxonomy as the LATEST-GENERATION dependently-typed language with a real-world CLI library port.
  - `_ttc-converter` (1-stem; spcfox/ttc-converter src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr; **Idris 2 `System.GetOpts` library** — idris-community/idris2-getopts package, maintained by stefan-hoeck; **9 flags + 1 positional + record-with-named-constructor `Options` + ADT `Option` with typed-payload constructors + record-update `:=`/`$=`-section syntax + `MkOpt` 4-arity positional-constructor spec + multi-char-list short flags `['?', 'h']` + multi-string-list long flags `["help"]` + `NoArg`/`ReqArg`/`OptArg` argument-shape constructors + `getOpt POLICY DESCS ARGS` returning `MkResult` 4-tuple + `ReturnInOrder Input` argOrder policy + pattern-match-destructure-with-fallback `| _ => ...`**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 7 (`import System.GetOpts` — Idris 2 IMPORT directive; imports the entire System.GetOpts module)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` lines 14-21 (`record Options where constructor MkOptions help : Bool; input : Maybe String; ...` — Idris 2 RECORD with NAMED CONSTRUCTOR; `Maybe String` for OPTIONAL flags, `List EraseTag` for REPEATABLE flags)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` lines 30-36 (`data Option = Help | Input String | Output String | Format TTCFormat | Erase EraseTag | JQ` — Idris 2 ALGEBRAIC DATA TYPE; sum type with constructors carrying typed payloads)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` lines 38-43 (`applyOption Help = { help := True }`; `applyOption (Erase tag) = { erase $= (tag ::) }` — Idris 2 RECORD-UPDATE syntax: `{field := value}` SET, `{field $= func}` APPLY-FUNCTION; `(tag ::)` is a SECTION partially applying cons to a list)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 45 (`applyOptions = foldl (flip applyOption) initOptions` — FOLD OVER PARSED ADT LIST)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` lines 79-89 (`descs : List $ OptDescr Option = [MkOpt ['?', 'h'] ["help"] (NoArg Help) "Show help", MkOpt ['i'] ["input"] (ReqArg Input "<file>") "Input file name", ...]` — option specification list; each `MkOpt` is `MkOpt SHORT_CHARS LONG_STRINGS ArgDescr HELP`)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 81 (`MkOpt ['?', 'h'] ["help"] (NoArg Help) "Show help"` — MULTI-CHAR-LIST short flags: BOTH `-?` and `-h` map to the Help constructor; `List Char` rather than R309 SML's string-of-chars)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 90 (`usage = usageInfo "Usage: ttc-converter [options] <file>\n\nOptions:" descs` — `usageInfo` auto-generates `--help` text from the same descs list)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 100 (`let (MkResult opts [] [] []) = getOpt (ReturnInOrder Input) descs args | _ => pure $ Left InvalidOptions` — PARSE INVOCATION; `MkResult` 4-tuple-record `(opts, positionals, unrecognized, errors)`; `| _ => ...` is Idris 2's IF-PATTERN-MATCH-FAILS-CONTINUE syntax)
    * `src/TTCConverter/CommandLine.idr` line 100 (`(ReturnInOrder Input)` — argOrder POLICY wraps EACH POSITIONAL with the `Input` constructor; tri-state policy `Permute | RequireOrder | ReturnInOrder` — SAME as R309 SML and R270 Haskell, confirming Haskell→SML→Idris 2 port lineage)
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 `System.GetOpts` library** at CommandLine.idr:7 — corpus's **first documented Idris 2 CLI library**.  `System.GetOpts` is from the idris-community/idris2-getopts package (3 GH stars; maintained by stefan-hoeck) — a port of Haskell's `System.Console.GetOpt` to Idris 2.  Distinct from R270 Haskell `optparse-applicative` (newer applicative-style library), R270 Haskell `System.Console.GetOpt` (the ORIGINAL — Idris 2 port preserves the API), R309 SML stdlib GetOpt (different language port of the same library).  Sister-libraries: HuwCampbell/optparse-idris (port of optparse-applicative to Idris 1).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-TWO library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), Free Pascal TCustomApplication (R312), **Idris 2 System.GetOpts** (R313 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 52 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 record-update-with-`:=` and `$=`-section CLI pattern** at CommandLine.idr:39-43 — `applyOption Help = { help := True }` uses Idris 2's RECORD-UPDATE syntax: `{field := value}` SET field, `{field $= func}` APPLY function to field.  E.g. `{ erase $= (tag ::) }` cons-prepends `tag` onto the existing `erase` list via SECTION `(tag ::)` (partial application of cons).  Distinct from R270 Haskell (record-update via `r { field = val }` no `$=`), R309 SML (record-update `{record where field = val}` differs), R290 Racket (`struct-copy` macro), R305 C++ CLI11 (direct struct-field mutation).  Idris 2's approach: BANG-EQUAL `:=` for SET + DOLLAR-EQUAL `$=` for FUNCTION-APPLY-TO-FIELD — corpus's **first documented `$=`-section record-update CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 `MkOpt SHORT_CHARS LONG_STRINGS (NoArg|ReqArg|OptArg) HELP` 4-arity constructor spec** at CommandLine.idr:80-89 — each `MkOpt` entry has POSITIONAL ARGS: (1) `List Char` short flags, (2) `List String` long flags, (3) `ArgDescr a` argument shape (`NoArg ctor` / `ReqArg ctor "<placeholder>"` / `OptArg ctor "<placeholder>"`), (4) `String` help text.  Distinct from R270 Haskell `Option [Char] [String] ArgDescr String` (SAME shape — Idris 2 preserves the Haskell type, just renames Option to MkOpt), R309 SML `{short, long, desc, help}` (labelled record — same fields, different syntax), R311 Guile quoted-assoc-list (different structure entirely).  Idris 2's approach: POSITIONAL CONSTRUCTOR ARGS — corpus's **first documented Idris-2-constructor-as-option-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 multi-char-list short flags** at CommandLine.idr:81 — `MkOpt ['?', 'h'] ["help"] (NoArg Help) "Show help"` — the FIRST positional arg is a LIST OF CHARS.  Both `-?` and `-h` map to the `Help` constructor.  Sister to R309 SML `short = "vV"` (string-of-chars) but Idris 2 uses EXPLICIT LIST SYNTAX `[...]` rather than a string.  Distinct from R304 Ada (one short per option), R307 Vala GLib (single char in OptionEntry), R308 Hare (single char in tuple), R311 Guile (`(single-char #\X)` — ONE short).  Idris 2's approach: TYPED LIST OF CHARS — type system enforces it's a list, not a string.  Corpus's **second documented multi-character-short-flag pattern (Idris 2 list — joins R309 SML string)**.
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 `getOpt POLICY DESCS ARGS` returning `MkResult` 4-record pattern** at CommandLine.idr:100-103 — `let (MkResult opts [] [] []) = getOpt (ReturnInOrder Input) descs args | _ => pure $ Left InvalidOptions` destructures the parse result into `(opts, positionals, unrecognized, errors)`.  The `| _ => ...` is Idris 2's IF-PATTERN-MATCH-FAILS-CONTINUE syntax — if the destructure doesn't match exactly, execute the fallback.  Distinct from R270 Haskell (similar but uses CASE-OF), R309 SML (tuple pattern match), R311 Guile (alist accessor `option-ref`).  Idris 2's approach: PATTERN-MATCH-DESTRUCTURE-WITH-FALLBACK — corpus's **first documented pattern-match-destructure-with-fallback CLI parse-result handling pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: ttc-converter uses **Idris 2 `ReturnInOrder Input` argOrder policy** at CommandLine.idr:100 — `ReturnInOrder ctor` wraps EACH POSITIONAL with the `Input` constructor and adds it to the parsed-options list (so positionals are INTERLEAVED with flags in the returned ADT list).  Tri-state policy `Permute | RequireOrder | ReturnInOrder` — SAME tri-state as R309 SML GetOpt and R270 Haskell `System.Console.GetOpt` — confirming the Haskell→SML→Idris 2 port lineage.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (only Permute via getopt_long default), R304 Ada (Permute only), R307 Vala GLib (Permute only — no opt-out).  Corpus's **third documented tri-state-argOrder-policy CLI library (joining R309 SML and R270 Haskell)**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_idris2` (Idris 2 compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_pack` (stefan-hoeck/idris2-pack — its binary is `pack`, already in corpus and blacklisted — uses System.GetOpts but separate harvest round would dup the binary name), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `typedefs/typedefs` (371 stars — uses HuwCampbell/optparse-idris (Idris 1 port of optparse-applicative, NOT Idris 2's System.GetOpts; deferred for separate round when an Idris 1 / Idris 2 distinction matters more), `idris-community/idris2-getopts` (3 stars — THE LIBRARY ITSELF; no real binary), `HuwCampbell/optparse-idris` (6 stars — Idris 1 library port), `statebox/idris-stbx-core` (small, specialized).  Chose ttc-converter: 1 GH star but the CLEANEST canonical Idris 2 System.GetOpts example in a single 100-line CommandLine.idr, exercises EVERY MAJOR System.GetOpts PATTERN (record + ADT + applyOption fold + descs list + MkOpt 4-arity spec + MULTI-CHAR-LIST short flags + ReturnInOrder argOrder + usageInfo helper + MkResult 4-tuple destructure with fallback), real binary distributed via Idris 2's package system.

- **R312 — pascalcoin_miner source-direct (PascalCoin cryptocurrency miner in Free Pascal; FCL stdlib `TCustomApplication`)** (1 file new) — pivots from Guile `(ice-9 getopt-long)` (R311 whiffle) into the **Free Pascal language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Free Pascal CLI library**.  pascalcoin_miner is Albert Molina's reference miner for the PascalCoin cryptocurrency — supports GPU mining via OpenCL (DelphiCL bindings) and CPU mining; connects to a PascalCoin server (solo mining) or pool over JSON-RPC.  332 GH stars (PascalCoinDev/PascalCoin).  Sister tools: full PascalCoin daemon (pascalcoin_daemon, DEFERRED), wallet applications, pool-manager scripts.  PascalCoin is notable for being a P2P cryptocurrency that does NOT require historical operations (accounts are first-class).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Free Pascal CLI library** — adds FCL `TCustomApplication` (OOP-INHERITANCE-BASED CLI design — subclass the framework, override methods, instantiate in main) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (51 library variants now).
  - `_pascalcoin_miner` (1-stem; PascalCoinDev/PascalCoin src/pascalcoin_miner.pp; **Free Pascal FCL `TCustomApplication`** — ships with the Free Pascal Compiler in the `custapp` unit of FCL (Free Component Library); **11 flags + class-inheritance-based CLI spec via `TPascalMinerApp = class(TCustomApplication)` + `CheckOptions(SHORT_SPEC, LONG_SPEC)` twin-string-spec validator + POSIX-getopt-extended short-spec with `::` for OPTIONAL args (FCL extension) + space-separated long-opt spec with `:` for arg-takers + `HasOption('short', 'long')` short-and-long-pair-accessor + `GetOptionValue('short', 'long')` string-return-caller-coercion + `procedure WriteHelp; virtual;` virtual-method help-text override + standard `Create(nil) / Run / Free` explicit-create-run-free-triad main block**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` line 30 (`uses Classes, SysUtils, CustApp, crt, ...` — Pascal `uses` clause imports units; `CustApp` is the unit name; `TCustomApplication` is the class inside; part of FCL Free Component Library — Free Pascal's stdlib)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` lines 35-58 (`TPascalMinerApp = class(TCustomApplication) ... procedure DoRun; override; ... procedure WriteHelp; virtual; ... end;` — Pascal CLASS DECLARATION inheriting from `TCustomApplication`; subclass overrides `DoRun` and `WriteHelp`)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` line 433 (`ErrorMsg:=CheckOptions('hp:d:s::c:n::t:u::x::l::', 'help platform: device: server: cpu: minername: testmode: user: pwd: logfile: logall: outputlogs:');` — TCustomApplication.CheckOptions takes TWO STRINGS: SHORT-OPT spec + LONG-OPT spec; short-opt syntax extends POSIX getopt with `::` for OPTIONAL ARGS (FCL extension); returns empty string on success, error message on failure)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` lines 441, 446, 456, 462, 466, 510 (`HasOption('p','platform')` + `HasOption('h', 'help')` + `HasOption('outputlogs')` — `HasOption` accepts EITHER 1 ARG (long-only) OR 2 ARGS (short + long pair); returns Pascal boolean)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` lines 221, 235, 256, 366, 463, 510 (`GetOptionValue('p','platform')` + sister for each typed option; returns the option's STRING VALUE; caller does manual type coercion via `StrToIntDef`, `StrToFloat`, etc.)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` line 56 (`procedure WriteHelp; virtual;` — virtual method declaration in class; subclass overrides at line 540 to emit custom help text)
    * `src/pascalcoin_miner.pp` lines 566-572 (`var Application: TPascalMinerApp; begin Application:=TPascalMinerApp.Create(nil); Application.Title:='Pascal Miner'; Application.Run; Application.Free; end.` — STANDARD TCustomApplication MAIN BLOCK; `Create(nil)`/`Run`/`Free` is the CANONICAL idiom)
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Free Pascal `TCustomApplication`** from FCL at pascalcoin_miner.pp:30+35 — corpus's **first documented Free Pascal CLI library**.  TCustomApplication is the abstract BASE CLASS in FCL's `custapp` unit that provides the CLI infrastructure: `DoRun` (override-to-run-main), `CheckOptions` (validate argv against spec), `HasOption` (check flag presence), `GetOptionValue` (retrieve flag value as string), `WriteHelp` (virtual method for help text), `ExeName` (program executable path).  Ships with Free Pascal Compiler — no external dep.  Distinct from Delphi's `System.SysUtils` lower-level `ParamStr(N)` argv access (no parsing), and from the `getopts` library on Object Pascal / Lazarus.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY-ONE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), Guile (ice-9 getopt-long) (R311), **Free Pascal TCustomApplication** (R312 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 51 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Pascal class-inheritance-as-CLI-spec pattern** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:35 — `TPascalMinerApp = class(TCustomApplication)` declares a SUBCLASS of `TCustomApplication`.  The subclass OVERRIDES `DoRun` (the entry point), provides `WriteHelp` (virtual method), and stores per-instance state in fields.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 (struct + ->method-chain — no inheritance), R306 Elixir Optimus (keyword list — no class), R307 Vala GLib (struct-literal table — no class), R309 SML opaque structure (no inheritance — modules instead), R311 Guile (quoted-assoc-list — no class).  Pascal's approach: OOP INHERITANCE — subclass the framework, override methods, instantiate in main.  Sister to Java's similar pattern but Pascal's is more focused.  Corpus's **first documented class-inheritance-as-CLI-spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **TCustomApplication twin-string-spec CheckOptions** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:433 — TWO SEPARATE STRINGS: short-opt spec + long-opt spec.  Short-opt syntax extends POSIX getopt with `::` for OPTIONAL ARGS (a FCL extension — not in C POSIX getopt).  Long-opt syntax is a SPACE-SEPARATED list of names with `:` suffix for arg-taking opts.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (option-string only, no long opts), R304 Ada postfix-marker in switch-literal string, R307 Vala GLib struct-literal table, R310 Fortran M_CLI2 single-string with `:` as long-short-alias.  TCustomApplication's approach: TWIN STRING SPECS — corpus's **first documented twin-string-spec (short + long separate) CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Pascal `HasOption('shortname', 'longname')` short-and-long-pair-accessor** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:441 et seq — `HasOption` accepts EITHER 1 ARG (long-only) OR 2 ARGS (short + long pair).  Returns Pascal boolean.  E.g. `HasOption('p','platform')` checks for `-p` OR `--platform`.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (caller switch-cases on return value), R305 C++ CLI11 (struct fields set by reference binding), R306 Elixir Optimus (struct field access), R307 Vala GLib (`opt_NAME` bound globals).  Pascal's approach: PER-OPTION TYPE-BOOLEAN ACCESSOR CALL — caller writes one `HasOption` call per option to check.  Corpus's **first documented short-and-long-pair-accessor CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Pascal `GetOptionValue('s','long')` string-return-caller-coercion** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:221, 235, 256, 366, 463, 510 — `GetOptionValue` returns the option's STRING VALUE; caller does manual type coercion via `StrToIntDef` (string-to-int with default), `StrToFloat`, etc.  No automatic typing.  Distinct from R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (typed-accessor functions `iget`/`dgets`/`lget`), R301 Nim cligen (proc-param type inference), R305 C++ CLI11 (compile-time-typed reference binding).  Pascal's approach: STRING-RETURN + CALLER-DRIVEN COERCION via `StrTo*Def` family — gives caller full control over default-on-parse-error.  Corpus's **first documented string-return-caller-coercion CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Pascal virtual-method WriteHelp override** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:56 (declaration) + line 540 (implementation) — `procedure WriteHelp; virtual;` declared in TCustomApplication; the subclass OVERRIDES it to emit custom help text.  Distinct from R304 Ada `Set_Usage` method (sets usage string at config time), R307 Vala GLib `oc.set_summary(...)` (fluent setter), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (help_text PARAMETER to set_args call).  Pascal's approach: VIRTUAL-METHOD-OVERRIDE — the help generation logic lives in Pascal CODE that the subclass overrides.  Corpus's **first documented virtual-method help-text CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pascalcoin_miner uses **Pascal Create-Run-Free triad in main block** at pascalcoin_miner.pp:566-572 — STANDARD TCustomApplication MAIN BLOCK: `Application:=TPascalMinerApp.Create(nil); Application.Title:='Pascal Miner'; Application.Run; Application.Free;`.  `Create(nil)` instantiates with no owner, `Run` (which invokes `DoRun` internally), `Free` explicitly deallocates.  Distinct from R311 Guile (pure functional, no object), R307 Vala GLib (GLib reference counting auto-cleanup), R304 Ada (no destructor in this style), R305 C++ CLI11 (RAII via destructors).  Pascal's approach: EXPLICIT CREATE-RUN-FREE TRIAD — Pascal has no automatic destructors, so the caller must call `Free`.  Corpus's **first documented explicit-create-run-free-triad CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_fpc` / `_lazbuild` / `_pas2ut` / `_releaser` (FPC compiler & tooling, all in corpus — they use Free Pascal but most use lower-level ParamStr argv access rather than TCustomApplication).  Skipped `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Skipped `0xsp-SRD/mortar` (1504 GH stars — but it's an AV/EDR/XDR EVASION TOOL targeted at security product circumvention; security-policy concern, deferred).  Probed `synopse/mORMot JSONconsole.lpr` (821 stars — sample code in a huge ORM/SOA framework; harder to extract a focused CLI), `bgrabitmap dev/releaser` (232 stars — but is sister releaser tool, deferred until separate round), `Bread80/Quiche quichecc.lpr` (8 stars — Pascal Z80 cross-compiler, but Work-in-Progress), `varianus/ovoplayerctrl` (65 stars — but is a control tool for ovoplayer GUI, smaller surface), `fpc/FPCSource pas2ut` (already in corpus as `_pas2ut`).  Chose pascalcoin_miner: 332 GH stars (PascalCoinDev), 11 FLAGS in a single 575-line pascalcoin_miner.pp, exercises EVERY MAJOR TCustomApplication PATTERN (class inheritance with `class(TCustomApplication)`, CheckOptions twin-string-spec, HasOption short-+-long pair accessor, GetOptionValue string-return-coercion, virtual WriteHelp override, Create-Run-Free main-block triad), real binary in an actively-developed cryptocurrency project.
  - Blacklist update: `pascalcoin_miner` added.  Corpus count: 28,734 → 28,735 (+1).

- **R311 — whiffle source-direct (Scheme-to-C compiler in Guile Scheme; stdlib `(ice-9 getopt-long)` module)** (1 file new) — pivots from Fortran M_CLI2 (R310 iapws) into the **Guile Scheme language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Guile Scheme CLI library** + corpus's **first Scheme-dialect CLI library other than Racket** (R290 racket/cmdline).  whiffle is Andy Wingo's lightweight Scheme compiler that emits C — used as a research vehicle for garbage-collector design (fixed-size, growable, adaptive; mark-and-sweep, semi-space copying, parallel marker) and JIT/warmup strategies.  Authored by Andy Wingo (Igalia; core Guile developer & GCC GIMPLE backend designer).  75 GH stars.  Sister tools: guile (Guile Scheme interpreter, in corpus as `_guile`), gcc (GNU C compiler, in corpus), Andy's Whippet GC research code.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: 50 CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI LIBRARY VARIANTS — the corpus has now documented FIFTY DISTINCT CLI LIBRARIES across 50 different language ecosystems.  Guile joins the taxonomy as the SECOND Scheme dialect (Racket was the first at R290).
  - `_whiffle` (1-stem; wingo/whiffle bin/whiffle; **Guile Scheme stdlib `(ice-9 getopt-long)` module** — ships with the Guile interpreter at `module/ice-9/getopt-long.scm`; **14 flags + INPUT-FILE positional + extra ARGs + quoted-assoc-list spec + `(single-char #\X)` Scheme char-literal short flags (SEVENTH character-literal CLI cluster language — `#\X` form joins Common Lisp via the Lisp family lineage) + `(value #t/#f/optional)` arg-shape spec + `(getopt-long args SPEC)` pure-functional parser + `(option-ref options 'NAME DEFAULT)` accessor + `(option-ref options '() #f)` positionals-under-empty-list-key**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `bin/whiffle` line 23 (`(use-modules (ice-9 format) (ice-9 getopt-long) (ice-9 match) ...)` — Guile USE-MODULES form imports modules at top level; `(ice-9 getopt-long)` is the stdlib module path)
    * `bin/whiffle` lines 56-69 (the SPEC is a QUOTED ASSOCIATION LIST `'((NAME (single-char #\X) (value #t/#f)) ...)` — each entry has NAME (long flag symbol) and property-pair sub-lists)
    * `bin/whiffle` line 57 entry 1 (`(expr (single-char #\e) (value #t))` — `--expr/-e` option with required arg; `(single-char #\e)` uses Scheme's `#\X` char-literal syntax)
    * `bin/whiffle` line 58 entry 2 (`(help (single-char #\h) (value #f))` — `--help/-h` BOOLEAN flag; `(value #f)` = no arg)
    * `bin/whiffle` line 60 entry 4 (`(preserve-builddir (value #f))` — long-only `--preserve-builddir` BOOLEAN flag; no short-char clause)
    * `bin/whiffle` line 56 (`(getopt-long args SPEC)` — PARSER ENTRY POINT; returns an ASSOCIATION LIST `((NAME . VALUE) ...)` of parsed options)
    * `bin/whiffle` lines 71, 74-78, 169 (`(option-ref options 'NAME DEFAULT)` — ACCESSOR; looks up symbol-keyed entry, returns DEFAULT if missing)
    * `bin/whiffle` line 75 (`(option-ref options '() #f)` — POSITIONALS retrieved under the EMPTY-LIST KEY `'()` — Guile getopt-long's UNIQUE convention)
  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Guile Scheme stdlib `(ice-9 getopt-long)` module** at bin/whiffle:23 — corpus's **first documented Guile Scheme CLI library** + corpus's **first Scheme-dialect CLI library other than Racket** (R290 racket/cmdline).  Guile's `(ice-9 getopt-long)` ships with the Guile interpreter (no external dep); implementation at `module/ice-9/getopt-long.scm` in the Guile source tree.  Originally based on Bash's getopt_long conventions but adapted to Scheme idiom (alist spec, alist return).  Distinct from R290 Racket `racket/cmdline` (hygienic macro generating parser at expansion time), R272 C POSIX getopt (option string), R287 Nim parseopt (token iterator).  Guile's `getopt-long` is FUNCTIONAL-DESIGN: takes data, returns data.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FIFTY library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), Fortran M_CLI2 (R310), **Guile (ice-9 getopt-long)** (R311 — NEW).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: 50 CROSS-LANGUAGE CLI LIBRARY VARIANTS — the corpus has now documented FIFTY DISTINCT CLI LIBRARIES across 50 different language ecosystems.
  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Guile quoted-assoc-list as CLI spec** at whiffle:57-69 — `'((NAME (single-char #\X) (value #t/#f)) ...)` is the CANONICAL getopt-long spec form.  Each entry is a NESTED LIST with NAME-and-property-pairs.  Pure Scheme data — manipulated like any other list.  Sister to R302 Clojure babashka.cli (vector-of-maps) but Guile uses LISTS rather than maps.  Distinct from R290 Racket `command-line` (hygienic macro DSL), R287 Nim parseopt (token iterator with caller-written loop), R307 Vala GLib (C struct-literal table), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec).  Corpus's **first documented quoted-assoc-list-as-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Scheme `#\X` char-literal for short-flag binding** at whiffle:57 et seq — `(single-char #\e)` uses Scheme's `#\X` CHARACTER-LITERAL syntax for the short-flag name.  Sister to R291 CL `#\h`, R292 Erlang `$h`, R295 CL clingon `#\A`, R298 Pony `'p'`, R300 V `` `n` ``, R308 Hare `'1'`.  **SEVENTH character-literal CLI cluster language** now: CL `#\X` (R291, R295), Erlang `$X` (R292), Pony `'X'` (R298), V `` `X` `` (R300), Hare `'X'` (R308), **Guile `#\X`** (R311 — Scheme variant of the Lisp `#\` convention).  Guile shares the `#\X` form with Common Lisp via the Lisp family lineage — Scheme R5RS/R6RS/R7RS all spec the `#\X` char-literal.  Corpus's **second documented `#\X`-pattern language (Scheme dialect — joins CL)**.
  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Guile `(value #t/#f/optional)` arg-shape spec** at whiffle:57-69 — within each option entry, the `(value ...)` clause specifies whether the option takes an arg.  Three valid forms: `(value #t)` REQUIRED arg, `(value #f)` BOOLEAN flag (no arg), `(value optional)` OPTIONAL arg.  Sister to R270 Haskell System.Console.GetOpt (`ReqArg`/`NoArg`/`OptArg` constructors — discriminated union), R309 SML stdlib GetOpt (`NoArg`/`ReqArg`/`OptArg` variants ported from Haskell), R304 Ada postfix-markers (`-X:` / `-X?`), R307 Vala GLib (OptionArg enum).  Guile's approach: SYMBOL-IN-INNER-CLAUSE — `(value SYM)` where SYM is one of `#t`/`#f`/`optional`.  Distinct because the discriminator is at the SCHEME-DATUM level (boolean or symbol) rather than at the type-system level.  Corpus's **first documented value-clause-with-boolean-or-symbol arg-shape CLI pattern**.
