  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Guile `(option-ref options '() #f)` for positionals** at whiffle:75 — the EMPTY-LIST KEY `'()` is Guile getopt-long's CONVENTION for retrieving the POSITIONAL ARGS list.  The parsed alist has entries `((NAME . VALUE) ...)` for flags AND a single `('() . positional-list)` entry for leftover args.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli `:args->opts [:name]` (positional bound to named option), R303 Gleam Glint `named_arg` (typed-name positional), R304 Ada `Get_Argument` (stateful iterator), R307 Vala GLib empty-string-name OptionEntry sentinel, R308 Hare `cmd.args` (field access on parsed struct), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 `args=>unnamed` (rename-on-import).  Guile's approach: POSITIONALS-UNDER-EMPTY-LIST-KEY — the alist association is `('() . positionals-list)`.  Corpus's **first documented empty-list-key-as-positional CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: whiffle uses **Guile `(getopt-long args SPEC)` pure-functional parser** at whiffle:56 — `getopt-long` is a PURE FUNCTION that takes `args` (full argv as a Scheme list) plus the spec, and RETURNS the parsed alist.  No mutation, no side effect, no global state.  Distinct from R304 Ada Getopt (mutates aliased variables via Access pointers), R307 Vala GLib `oc.parse(ref args)` (mutates argv), R310 Fortran M_CLI2 `set_args` (global-module-state side effect).  Sister to R302 Clojure babashka.cli `cli/parse-opts` (also pure functional), R306 Elixir Optimus `Optimus.parse` (functional version of the bang variant), R303 Gleam Glint `glint.execute` (returns Result discriminated union).  Guile's approach: DATA-IN-DATA-OUT — corpus's **second documented pure-functional-parsed-alist CLI pattern** (joining the Lisp family with R290 Racket and R302 Clojure babashka.cli).
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_guile` (Guile interpreter, already in corpus), `_mu` (1699 stars, in corpus already), `_cat` / `_hello` (in corpus / OS dups), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `lepton-eda/lepton-netlist` (260 stars — uses Guile getopt-long but spec lives in shared lepton/ scheme module, not in a single self-contained file), `artyom-poptsov/guile-ssh sssh` (74 stars — Guile SSH bindings example, small surface), `Pingus/pingus-level` (210 stars — but uses ad-hoc argument loop, not full getopt-long), `isamert/jaro` (89 stars — bash wrapper around a small Guile core, harder to extract), `yawaramin/ggspec` (27 stars — testing framework, not user CLI), `git-for-windows/git-sdk-64 frisk.scm` (0 stars, niche), `Distrotech/mailutils guimb` (mailutils internals), `spk121/guile100 cat.scm` (CAT EXAMPLE, would dup with OS cat).  Chose whiffle: 75 stars (Andy Wingo, core Guile dev), 14 FLAGS + 1 positional in a single 201-line bin/whiffle (Guile-shebang Scheme script), exercises EVERY MAJOR `(ice-9 getopt-long)` PATTERN (quoted-assoc-list spec, (single-char #\X) char-literal short flags, (value #t/#f) arg-shape, option-ref for retrieval, option-ref with '() for positionals), real binary in Andy Wingo's research-grade Whiffle Scheme-to-C compiler project.
  - Blacklist update: `whiffle` added.  Corpus count: 28,733 → 28,734 (+1).

- **R310 — iapws source-direct (light/heavy water thermodynamics CLI in Fortran; urbanjost/`M_CLI2` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309 elton) into the **Fortran language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Fortran CLI library**.  iapws is a Fortran CLI for computing properties of light and heavy water according to the IAPWS (International Association for the Properties of Water and Steam) formulations — fundamental thermodynamic data used in steam-turbine engineering and atmospheric science.  Authored by Milan Skocic (7 GH stars).  Sister tools: coolprop (broader fluid-property C++ library, not in corpus), refprop (NIST REFPROP, proprietary).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Fortran CLI library** — adds urbanjost/M_CLI2 (single-string-packed spec, response-file expansion, named-import-with-rename via `=>`, type-named-accessor-function retrievers, comma-separated value parsing into arrays) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (49 library variants now).  Fortran joins the taxonomy as one of the oldest languages with active CLI tooling (Fortran 77 / 90 / 95 / 2003 / 2008 / 2018 / 2023 evolution).
  - `_iapws` (1-stem; MilanSkocic/iapws app/main.f90; **Fortran `M_CLI2` library** — urbanjost/M_CLI2, distributed via fpm Fortran Package Manager; **5 subcommands (kh, kd, psat, Tsat, wp) + single-string-packed spec via `set_args('--temperature:T 25.0 --gas:g O2 --D2O', help, version)` + `:` long-short-alias separator + inline defaults + `set_mode('strict')` / `set_mode('response_file')` configuration + `get_subcommand()` first-positional dispatch + per-arm-respec subcommand pattern + `get_args`/`iget`/`dgets`/`lget` type-named-accessor retrievers + comma-separated values parsed into Fortran arrays + `args=>unnamed` named-import-with-rename**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `app/main.f90` line 3 (`use M_CLI2, only: set_args, set_mode, iget, lget, get_args, dgets, args=>unnamed, get_subcommand, set_mode` — Fortran `use, only:` IMPORT directive with `=>` RENAME-ON-IMPORT syntax; `args=>unnamed` renames `unnamed` to `args` in importing scope)
    * `app/main.f90` line 165-166 (`call set_mode('strict')` + `call set_mode('response_file')` — `'strict'` rejects unknown flags; `'response_file'` enables `@file.rsp` shell-response-file expansion)
    * `app/main.f90` line 167 (`cmd = get_subcommand()` — M_CLI2's SUBCOMMAND RETRIEVAL returns the FIRST POSITIONAL argv element)
    * `app/main.f90` lines 169-228 (`select case (cmd) ... case ("kh") ... case ("kd") ... case ("psat") ... case ("Tsat") ... case ("wp") ... case default` — Fortran SELECT-CASE on subcommand string; each arm CALLS `set_args` with a FRESH SPEC describing THAT SUBCOMMAND'S flags)
    * `app/main.f90` line 171 (`call set_args('--temperature:T 25.0 --fugacity:f 1 --gas:g O2 --D2O --listgases', help_text, version_text)` — M_CLI2's PRIMARY ENTRY POINT; SINGLE-STRING SPEC contains ALL flags with DEFAULT VALUES INLINE; syntax `--long:short DEFAULT` with `:` as LONG-SHORT-ALIAS SEPARATOR; boolean flags toggled by presence)
    * `app/main.f90` lines 173-176 (`call get_args('g', gas)`, `call get_args('f', f)`, `call get_args('T', T)` — typed retrieval; type inferred from target array; `get_args` handles COMMA-SEPARATED VALUES `-T 25,100` parses into a 2-element array)
    * `app/main.f90` line 184 (`if (lget('D2O')) then heavywater = 1` — `lget` is the BOOLEAN GETTER returning Fortran `logical`)
    * `app/main.f90` line 188 (`if (lget('listgases')) then write(...) gases2(heavywater); stop` — checks boolean flag presence then exits)
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **Fortran `M_CLI2` library** at main.f90:3 — corpus's **first documented Fortran CLI library**.  M_CLI2 (urbanjost/M_CLI2) is the de-facto modern Fortran CLI library, distributed via fpm (Fortran Package Manager — itself in corpus as `_fpm`).  Distinct from `iso_fortran_env` command_argument_count + get_command_argument (Fortran 2003 stdlib for raw argv access; no parsing — caller writes manual loops).  M_CLI2 provides: single-string-packed spec, named-import-with-rename via `=>`, response-file expansion (`@file.rsp`), automatic help/version generation, type-coercion via `get_args` / `iget` / `dgets` / `lget`, comma-separated value parsing into arrays.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-NINE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), Standard ML stdlib GetOpt (R309), **Fortran M_CLI2** (R310 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 49 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Fortran joins the taxonomy as one of the oldest languages with active CLI tooling.
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **M_CLI2 single-string-packed spec via `set_args(SPEC_STRING, HELP, VERSION)`** at main.f90:171 et seq — `set_args('--temperature:T 25.0 --fugacity:f 1 --gas:g O2 --D2O --listgases', help_text, version_text)`.  The SPEC STRING contains ALL flags with their defaults INLINE.  Syntax: `--long:short DEFAULT` (option with default), `--long:short` (option no default), `--long` (boolean flag), `--long DEFAULT` (option with default, no short).  The `:` is the LONG-SHORT-ALIAS SEPARATOR.  Boolean flags are toggled by presence.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (option-string `"a:b:cd"` — short flags only), R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature), R304 Ada postfix-marker (`-X:`), R307 Vala GLib (OptionEntry struct table), R308 Hare (tagged-union array).  M_CLI2's SINGLE-STRING SPEC is UNIQUE: ALL the flag metadata lives in ONE compact string.  Corpus's **first documented single-string-packed-spec CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **Fortran `args=>unnamed` rename-on-import** at main.f90:3 — `use M_CLI2, only: ..., args=>unnamed, ...`.  Fortran's `use, only:` IMPORT directive accepts `LOCAL_NAME=>ORIGINAL_NAME` rename mappings.  Here `unnamed` (M_CLI2's internal positional-args array) is renamed to `args` in the importing scope.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli (`:as cli` namespace alias), R303 Gleam Glint (no rename), R307 Vala GLib (no rename — `using` brings the entire namespace), R308 Hare (no rename — `use getopt;` brings entire module).  Fortran's approach: PER-NAME RENAME at IMPORT TIME — finer-grained than whole-namespace aliasing.  Corpus's **first documented named-import-with-rename CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **M_CLI2 `set_mode('response_file')` for `@file.rsp` expansion** at main.f90:166 — `'response_file'` mode enables argv tokens like `@options.rsp` to be REPLACED with the contents of that file, line-by-line, before option parsing proceeds.  This is the UNIX ARGUMENT-FILE convention from `gcc @file` and `javac @file`.  Distinct from R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (no response-file support), R307 Vala GLib (`oc.parse(ref args)` no mode), R308 Hare (single mode), R309 SML GetOpt (tri-state argOrder only).  Corpus's **first documented response-file-expansion CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **M_CLI2 typed-retrieval functions `get_args` / `iget` / `dgets` / `lget`** at main.f90:173-188 — different functions for different return types: `get_args(NAME, target_array)` (typed by target), `iget(NAME)` (integer scalar), `lget(NAME)` (logical/bool), `dgets(NAME)` (allocatable double array).  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (parsed values returned directly via dispatch), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (single parsed-opts map), R303 Gleam Glint (typed accessor functions via use-callback closure), R305 C++ CLI11 (reference-bound at spec time), R306 Elixir Optimus (ParseResult struct with options/args fields), R308 Hare (anonymous tuple `opt.0`/`opt.1`).  M_CLI2's approach: TYPE-NAMED ACCESSOR FUNCTIONS — Fortran's accessors are GLOBAL-MODULE functions.  Corpus's **first documented type-named-accessor-function CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: iapws uses **Fortran `select case (cmd) case ("kh") case ("kd") ... case default` per-arm-respec subcommand dispatch** at main.f90:169-228 — each subcommand arm RE-CALLS `set_args` with a FRESH SPEC describing THAT SUBCOMMAND'S flags.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli (data-vector dispatch via `cli/dispatch` walks the command list once), R303 Gleam Glint (each `glint.add(at:, do:)` builds the tree at spec time, then `glint.execute` walks it), R306 Elixir Optimus (single `Optimus.parse!` returns atom-path tuple), R307 Vala GLib (no subcommand support natively).  M_CLI2's approach: CALLER-DRIVEN PER-SUBCOMMAND RE-SPEC — each subcommand has its OWN set_args call inside its case arm.  Corpus's **first documented per-arm-respec CLI dispatch pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_fpm` (Fortran Package Manager, already in corpus + blacklisted — uses M_CLI2 too but more complex 442-line CLI surface).  Skipped `_crest` (317 stars — uses HANDWRITTEN argv loops with `==` comparisons, not M_CLI2; deferred until handwritten-parser-pattern round).  Skipped `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `victorsndvg/FEconv` (107 stars, FE format converter — uses M_CLI2 but spec is split across module_args.f90 and module_feconv.f90; harder to extract source-direct), `MilanSkocic/iapws` (CHOSEN — 7 stars, 5 subcommands in single 442-line main.f90, exercises EVERY MAJOR M_CLI2 PATTERN), `urbanjost/M_CLI2/example/demo3.f90` (THE LIBRARY ITSELF example; not a real binary), `djean032/zfit_fort` (0 stars, too small), `urbanjost/general-purpose-fortran` (UTILITY DEMOS, not a single binary).  Lean detour from R309 not resumed: continues to await standalone-binary Cli-library example.  Chose iapws: SINGLE-FILE 442-line CLI with 5 subcommands (kh, kd, psat, Tsat, wp), exercises EVERY MAJOR M_CLI2 PATTERN (set_args single-string-packed spec, set_mode response-file expansion, get_subcommand for dispatch, get_args / iget / lget / dgets typed retrievers, args=>unnamed import rename, comma-separated value parsing), real numerical-computing binary distributed via fpm registry.
  - Blacklist update: `iapws` added.  Corpus count: 28,732 → 28,733 (+1).

- **R309 — elton source-direct (L10 Logic Programming Language frontend in Standard ML; stdlib `GetOpt` structure)** (1 file new) — pivots from Hare stdlib getopt (R308 hiq) into the **Standard ML language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Standard ML CLI library**.  elton is the FRONTEND BINARY of L10 (the L10 Logic Programming Language) — generates SML source-code databases from `.l10` files for integration with SML projects.  Authored by Rob Simmons (formerly CMU; HOL prover & l10 author).  10 GH stars.  L10 itself compiles to SML code, so elton is the SML-CODE-GENERATING side of the language.  Sister tools: mlton (SML compiler, already in corpus; targets `elton-mlton.sml` as the binary entry point), smlnj (alternative SML compiler, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Standard ML CLI library** — adds SML stdlib `GetOpt` structure (ADT-as-parsed-value, parametric-record-list spec, multi-character-short-flag-string, NoArg/ReqArg constructor variants, tri-state argOrder policy ported from Haskell's `System.Console.GetOpt`) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (48 library variants now).
  - `_elton` (1-stem; robsimmons/l10 sml/frontend/elton.sml; **Standard ML stdlib `GetOpt` structure** — part of SML Basis Library, available in MLton, SML/NJ, MLKit, Poly/ML, MoSML; **4 flags + 1 positional + ADT-as-parsed-value via user-defined `datatype options` + parametric `options GetOpt.opt_descr list` spec + multi-character-short-flag-string `"vV"` for `-v`/`-V` aliasing + `GetOpt.NoArg`/`GetOpt.ReqArg` constructor variants encoding arg-shape + `GetOpt.usageInfo` auto-help generation + `GetOpt.getOpt {argOrder, options, errFn}` parser with tuple `(opts, files)` return + `GetOpt.Permute`/`RequireOrder`/`ReturnInOrder` tri-state argOrder policy + `foldr processOpt INITIAL opts` functional state-threading through parsed ADT list**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 1 (`structure Elton:> sig ... end = struct ... end` — SML OPAQUE STRUCTURE with SIGNATURE; `:>` is the OPAQUE SEAL hiding internal types)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` lines 16-21 (`datatype options = Verbose | Help | Prefix of string | Sources of string | Directory of string` — SML ALGEBRAIC DATA TYPE; constructors `Verbose`/`Help` are NULLARY, `Prefix`/`Sources`/`Directory` each take one string)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 23 (`val options: options GetOpt.opt_descr list = [...]` — SML POLYMORPHIC LIST of `opt_descr` records; `options GetOpt.opt_descr` is POSTFIX TYPE APPLICATION — `GetOpt`'s `opt_descr` is parameterized over the user's option ADT)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 24 (`{short = "vV", long = ["verbose"], desc = GetOpt.NoArg (fn () => Verbose), help = "Print out signature and compiler messages."}` — VERBOSE flag spec; `short = "vV"` MULTI-LETTER SHORT FORM: both `-v` AND `-V` accepted as verbose flag (the `short` field is a STRING — each character is a separate short-flag alias))
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 26 (`desc = GetOpt.NoArg (fn () => Verbose)` — `NoArg` constructor + THUNK that returns the ADT tag when matched)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` lines 27-32 (REQUIRED-ARG flags: `GetOpt.ReqArg (Prefix, "l10")` — when matched, take next argv element, apply constructor `Prefix` to it, producing `Prefix "value"` tagged value; `"l10"` is the placeholder shown in help text)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 53 (`val usageinfo = GetOpt.usageInfo {header = "Usage: " ^ name ^ " [options] file.l10 ...\nOptions:", options = options} ^ "\n"` — `GetOpt.usageInfo` generates `--help` text from the same options-list)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` lines 62-66 (`val (opts, files) = GetOpt.getOpt {argOrder = GetOpt.Permute, options = options, errFn = errFn} args` — GetOpt.getOpt invocation returns TUPLE: parsed `opts: options list` + leftover POSITIONALS `files: string list`)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` line 63 (`argOrder = GetOpt.Permute` — tri-state argOrder policy: `Permute` (default, GNU-getopt-style reorder), `RequireOrder` (stop at first positional), `ReturnInOrder` (treat positionals as `--` args); SAME tri-state policy as Haskell's `System.Console.GetOpt` because SML's GetOpt is a PORT OF that Haskell library)
    * `sml/frontend/elton.sml` lines 68-69 (`val (prefix, sources, dir) = foldr processOpt ("l10", "l10.sources", OS.FileSys.getDir ()) opts` — RIGHT-FOLD over parsed options to thread state through; `processOpt` pattern-matches on each ADT constructor and updates the relevant tuple slot)
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **Standard ML stdlib `GetOpt` structure** at elton.sml:23, 62 — corpus's **first documented Standard ML CLI library**.  SML's stdlib `GetOpt` is part of the SML Basis Library (available in MLton, SML/NJ, MLKit, Poly/ML, MoSML).  Implementation at `sml/basis-library/system/getopt.sml` in the MLton tree.  Originally PORTED FROM Haskell's `System.Console.GetOpt` (which itself is a port of GNU getopt_long).  Distinct from R270 Haskell `optparse-applicative` (newer applicative-style library), R287 Nim stdlib `parseopt` (token iterator, lower-level), R272 C POSIX getopt (single-byte short flags only).  SML's GetOpt is FUNCTIONAL-DESIGN: opaque parser, type-parameterized opt_descr, tuple-return.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), Hare stdlib getopt (R308), **Standard ML stdlib GetOpt** (R309 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 48 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML ADT-as-parsed-value CLI pattern** at elton.sml:16-21 — `datatype options = Verbose | Help | Prefix of string | Sources of string | Directory of string`.  The parsed option becomes a TAGGED VALUE in this ADT.  Each flag/option PRODUCES a CONSTRUCTED ADT VALUE; the caller then pattern-matches the parsed `options list` to drive behaviour.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 (struct fields bound by reference), R306 Elixir Optimus (atom-keyed map in the ParseResult struct), R307 Vala GLib (typed-flag enum + bound-variable references), R308 Hare getopt (anonymous tuple of char + string).  SML's approach: PARSED OPTION IS A TAGGED VALUE IN A USER-DEFINED ADT — corpus's **first documented ADT-as-parsed-value CLI pattern**.  Sister to R270 Haskell optparse-applicative (which can also build into any value, but uses applicative composition rather than user-defined ADT).
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML parametric-record-list option specification** at elton.sml:23 — `val options: options GetOpt.opt_descr list = [...]`.  SML's `opt_descr` is a RECORD TYPE PARAMETERIZED OVER the user's option ADT.  The `options GetOpt.opt_descr` notation is SML's TYPE APPLICATION (postfix: type-arg comes BEFORE the type-constructor).  Each record has four labelled fields: `short` (string), `long` (list of strings), `desc` (variant), and `help` (string).  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature), R306 Elixir Optimus (keyword list), R307 Vala GLib (struct-literal table), R308 Hare getopt (tagged-union array).  SML's approach: PARAMETRIC-RECORD-LIST — `opt_descr` is GENERIC over the user's ADT.  Corpus's **first documented postfix-type-application CLI spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML multi-character-short-flag-string** at elton.sml:24 — `short = "vV"`.  The `short` field is a STRING — EACH CHARACTER IS A SEPARATE SHORT-FLAG ALIAS.  Thus `-v` AND `-V` both match the same verbose flag.  Distinct from R304 Ada `Define_Switch` (only one short character per option), R307 Vala GLib (single char in OptionEntry), R308 Hare getopt (single char tuple field), R306 Elixir Optimus (single `short:` string).  SML's STRING-OF-CHARS-IS-MULTIPLE-SHORTS is UNIQUE in the corpus.  Corpus's **first documented multi-character-short-flag-string CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML GetOpt.NoArg/ReqArg desc-constructor variants** at elton.sml:24-37 — the `desc` field is a tagged-variant: `GetOpt.NoArg (fn () => Verbose)` for no-arg flag (thunk produces the parsed value), `GetOpt.ReqArg (Prefix, "l10")` for required-arg option (CONSTRUCTOR + placeholder name), `GetOpt.OptArg (fn s => ..., "l10")` for optional-arg (sml-getopt variant).  Each variant encodes BOTH the arg-shape AND how to construct the parsed value.  Distinct from R304 Ada postfix-marker (`-X:` / `-X?`), R307 Vala GLib OptionArg enum, R308 Hare tuple-arity-discriminator.  Corpus's **first documented constructor-variant-encodes-arg-shape CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML GetOpt.Permute argOrder tri-state policy** at elton.sml:63 — `argOrder = GetOpt.Permute` is the GNU-getopt-style default: REORDER argv so options precede positionals.  Three argOrder policies: `Permute` (default), `RequireOrder` (stop at first positional), `ReturnInOrder` (treat positionals as `--` args).  This SAME tri-state policy exists in R270 Haskell `System.Console.GetOpt` because SML's GetOpt is a PORT OF that Haskell library.  Distinct from R307 Vala GLib (no equivalent — always permutes), R308 Hare getopt (no equivalent — single mode).  Corpus's **first documented tri-state-argOrder-policy CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: elton uses **SML foldr through parsed-ADT-list state-threading** at elton.sml:68-69 — `val (prefix, sources, dir) = foldr processOpt ("l10", "l10.sources", OS.FileSys.getDir ()) opts`.  SML's `foldr` applies `processOpt` right-to-left across the parsed `opts` list, threading a tuple-of-state through.  `processOpt` pattern-matches on each ADT constructor and updates the relevant tuple slot.  Distinct from R287 Nim parseopt (caller-driven for-loop), R307 Vala GLib (bindings mutated in-place by parser), R308 Hare (for-..-iteration with switch-case).  SML's approach: FOLD-RIGHT THROUGH PARSED ADT LIST — corpus's **first documented foldr-through-parsed-ADT CLI handling pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_mlton` / `_smlnj` / `_mosml` / `_polyml` / `_mlkit` (SML compilers all in corpus; they have their own complex CLIs but most use ad-hoc CommandLine.arguments rather than stdlib GetOpt).  Skipped `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Lean detour: probed `leanprover/verso` (339 stars but uses HANDWRITTEN indexed-inductive-type parsing — not Lean's `Cli` library; deferred), `argumentcomputer/yatima` (146 stars — `Cli` usage commented out in current revision), `argumentcomputer/ix` (73 stars — `Cli` usage commented out), `hhu-adam/lean-i18n` (12 stars — uses Lean Cli but only as `lake exe i18n` target, not a standalone binary; deferred until a standalone-binary Cli-library example surfaces).  Standard ML probed: `diku-dk/sml-getopt` (6 stars, THE LIBRARY ITSELF — examples directory has demos but no real binary), `Sciumo/TL_System` (0 stars, niche), `bocchino/AppleCore` (6 stars, AppleCore assembler — uses GetOpt but smaller surface), `EESI/nbc` (1 star, Naive Bayes Classifier, narrow CLI).  Chose `robsimmons/l10/elton`: 10 stars (L10 Logic Programming Language by Rob Simmons), 4 flags including a MULTI-CHARACTER-SHORT-FLAG-STRING (`"vV"` for `-v`/`-V`), full GetOpt pattern exercise (datatype ADT + opt_descr record list + NoArg/ReqArg constructors + usageInfo + getOpt returns tuple + foldr through parsed list), real binary distributed as part of the L10 toolchain.  Single source file makes extraction clean.
  - Blacklist update: `elton` added.  Corpus count: 28,731 → 28,732 (+1).

- **R308 — hiq source-direct (himitsu query client in Hare; stdlib `getopt` module)** (1 file new) — pivots from Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307 umockdev-run) into the **Hare language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Hare CLI library**.  hiq is the QUERY CLIENT for himitsu — Drew DeVault's secure key store; connects to the `himitsud` daemon over a Unix domain socket, sends commands (add / del / edit / lock / unlock / query), receives matched entries.  Part of a 4-binary cluster from sourcehut-mirrors/himitsu: himitsud (daemon; DEFERRED for separate round), himitsu-store (low-level secret storage; DEFERRED), hiprompt-tty (TTY-based prompt helper; DEFERRED), **hiq** (CHOSEN).  Sister tools: pinentry-curses / pinentry-gtk (analogous keyring prompts in the GnuPG ecosystem), keyctl (kernel keyring).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Hare CLI library** — adds Hare stdlib `getopt` (tagged-union help array with tuple-arity-as-option-shape discriminator, char-literal short flags, variadic spread parse, defer cleanup, `..`-iteration, tuple-index access) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (47 library variants now).
  - `_hiq` (1-stem; sourcehut-mirrors/himitsu cmd/hiq/main.ha; **Hare stdlib `getopt` module** — Hare's standard-library CLI parser, ships with the Hare compiler at `hare/getopt/+linux.ha`; **11 flags (10 boolean + 2 options-with-args) + 1 positional + tagged-union `[_]getopt::help` array with 3-arm discriminator (bare str / 2-tuple / 3-tuple) + Hare `'X'` single-quoted char-literal short flags + `getopt::parse(os::args, usage...)` variadic-spread parse + `defer getopt::finish(&cmd)` cleanup + `for (let opt .. cmd.opts)` `..`-iteration + tuple-index `opt.0`/`opt.1` access + bare `case =>` catch-all default**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 6 (`use getopt;` — Hare USE directive; imports the stdlib `getopt` module; ships with the Hare compiler — no external dep)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` lines 26-39 (`let usage: [_]getopt::help = [ ... ];` — Hare ARRAY-LITERAL spec; `[_]TYPE` is Hare's INFERRED-LENGTH ARRAY syntax; `getopt::help` is a TAGGED-UNION type with THREE arms: bare `str` = command-description / positional-placeholder, `(char, str)` 2-tuple = BOOLEAN FLAG, `(char, str, str)` 3-tuple = OPTION WITH ARG; the FIRST bare str is the COMMAND DESCRIPTION; subsequent bare strings are POSITIONAL placeholders)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` lines 26-28 (DESCRIPTION + BOOLEAN FLAGS — `('1', "only accept one match")`, `('a', "add a key")`, `('d', "decrypt private keys")`, `('e', "edit entries by stdin")`, `('l', "soft lock the keyring")`, `('L', "hard lock the keyring")`, `('Q', "terminate the daemon")`, `('R', "remove matching keys")`, `('s', "strict query")` — Hare's `'X'` single-quoted char literal is the SHORT FLAG NAME)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` lines 29, 32 (OPTIONS WITH ARGS — `('c', "key/value pair", "change the value of a pair. Can be used multiple times.")` and `('F', "field", "select a field for output")` — 3-arg tuple `(short_char, arg_name, help_text)` = OPTION WITH ARG; TUPLE ARITY ENCODES the option's SHAPE)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 39 (`"<query>",` — bare string literal as POSITIONAL placeholder; the angle-brackets are CONVENTION, not Hare syntax)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 41 (`const cmd = getopt::parse(os::args, usage...);` — VARIADIC SPREAD via `usage...` suffix; Hare's `...` is the ARRAY-TO-VARIADIC SPREAD OPERATOR; `getopt::parse` returns a `command` struct containing parsed `opts: [](char, str)` tuples + leftover `args: []str`)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 42 (`defer getopt::finish(&cmd);` — Hare DEFER statement; `getopt::finish` frees the parsed command's allocations; sister to Zig's `defer` (R288), Go's `defer`)
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 51 (`for (let opt .. cmd.opts) {` — Hare `..` ITERATION OPERATOR positioned BETWEEN binding and iterable; reads as "for opt over cmd.opts")
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` line 52 (`switch (opt.0) {` — TUPLE FIELD ACCESS via `.0` / `.1` numeric indexing; `opt.0` is short-flag char; `opt.1` is arg-value (empty for boolean flags))
    * `cmd/hiq/main.ha` lines 53-74 (CASE ARMS — `case 'X' =>` followed by statements; arms are SEMICOLON-SEPARATED inside a `switch` block; bare `case =>` is the CATCH-ALL DEFAULT)
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare stdlib `getopt` module** at hiq/main.ha:6 — corpus's **first documented Hare CLI library**.  Hare's stdlib `getopt` ships with the Hare compiler (no external dep); located at `hare/getopt/+linux.ha` in the stdlib source tree.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (lower-level, no help/error generation), R287 Nim stdlib parseopt (token iterator), R300 V stdlib flag (single-shot factory + cascade-style config).  Hare's `getopt` is SLIGHTLY HIGHER-LEVEL than C's getopt: takes a `[]getopt::help` ARRAY of typed tuples, returns a `command` struct with parsed opts + remaining args.  Generates `--help` output AUTOMATICALLY from the help array.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), Vala GLib.OptionContext (R307), **Hare stdlib getopt** (R308 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 47 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare tagged-union `getopt::help` array with tuple-arity-as-option-shape discriminator** at hiq/main.ha:26-39 — `let usage: [_]getopt::help = [...]` is an array of TAGGED UNION values with THREE arms: (a) bare `str` = command-description / positional-placeholder, (b) `(char, str)` 2-tuple = boolean flag, (c) `(char, str, str)` 3-tuple = option with arg.  Hare's tagged-union system DISPATCHES based on TUPLE ARITY — distinct from R304 Ada postfix-markers in the switch-literal string (`-X:` / `-X?`), R307 Vala GLib `OptionArg` enum value, R301 Nim cligen (proc-param type inference), R287 Nim parseopt (`kind: cmdShort | cmdLong | cmdArgument`).  Hare's approach: TUPLE-ARITY-AS-TYPE-DISCRIMINATOR — corpus's **first documented tuple-arity-as-spec-shape CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare `'X'` char-literal short-flag name** at hiq/main.ha:26-39 — `('1', "only accept one match")` uses Hare's SINGLE-QUOTE CHAR LITERAL for the short-flag name.  Sister to R291 CL `#\h`, R292 Erlang `$h`, R298 Pony `'p'`, R295 CL clingon `#\A`, R300 V `\`n\``.  **SIXTH character-literal CLI cluster language now**: CL `#\X`, Erlang `$X`, Pony `'X'`, clingon (CL) `#\X`, V `\`X\``, **Hare `'X'`**.  Hare uses SINGLE QUOTES (like Pony and C) — distinct from V's backticks and the Lisp `#\` convention.  Corpus's **sixth character-literal CLI cluster language**.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare `...` variadic-spread for spec passing** at hiq/main.ha:41 — `getopt::parse(os::args, usage...)` spreads the `usage` array into variadic args.  Hare's `...` is the ARRAY-TO-VARIADIC SPREAD OPERATOR (post-fix on the array).  Sister to JS spread, Python `*usage`, Rust `usage[..]`.  Distinct from R304 Ada (positional Define_Switch calls — no variadic spread), R307 Vala GLib (registration via `add_main_entries(table)` — array passed by reference), R306 Elixir Optimus (keyword-list passed as single arg).  Hare's approach: VARIADIC SPREAD AT CALL SITE — the spec is an ordinary array until parse-time when it's flattened into variadic.  Corpus's **first documented array-spread-variadic CLI spec passing pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare `defer getopt::finish(&cmd)` for cleanup** at hiq/main.ha:42 — Hare's `defer` statement schedules a cleanup expression to run when the enclosing scope exits.  `getopt::finish(&cmd)` frees the parsed command's allocations.  Sister to Zig's `defer` (R288), Go's `defer`.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (GC-managed), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (JVM GC), R305 C++ CLI11 (RAII destructors), R306 Elixir Optimus (BEAM GC), R307 Vala GLib (GLib reference counting).  Hare's approach: MANUAL DEFER for resource cleanup — corpus's **first documented defer-managed-CLI-cleanup pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare `for (let opt .. cmd.opts)` for-..-iteration** at hiq/main.ha:51 — Hare's `..` is the RANGE-FOR ITERATION OPERATOR.  Positioned BETWEEN the binding and the iterable.  Reads as "for opt over cmd.opts".  Distinct from C's tripartite `for (init; cond; incr)`, Rust's `for x in iter`, Zig's `for (slice) |item|`, R287 Nim parseopt's `for kind, key, val in getopt():`.  Hare's `..`-operator-form echoes Rust's range syntax (`0..10`) but in a different position.  Corpus's **first documented `..` ITERATION OPERATOR over parsed opts CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hiq uses **Hare tuple-index `opt.0` / `opt.1` access** at hiq/main.ha:52 + multiple case arms — `switch (opt.0)` dispatches on the SHORT-FLAG CHAR; `opt.1` provides the OPTION ARG VALUE (empty for boolean flags).  Hare tuples are anonymous structs accessed by NUMERIC INDEX rather than field name.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (named proc params), R305 C++ CLI11 (struct fields), R306 Elixir Optimus (`%Optimus.ParseResult{options: %{site: site}}` pattern match), R287 Nim parseopt (`kind` / `key` / `val` triple-binding in the for loop).  Hare's approach: ANONYMOUS-TUPLE-INDEXED-BY-NUMBER — sister to Rust's `tuple.0` / `tuple.1` and Zig's `tuple[0]` / `tuple[1]`.  Corpus's **first documented tuple-index-field-access CLI parser pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_hare` (Hare compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `sourcehut-mirrors/himitsu` family — chose `hiq` over sister `himitsud` (DAEMON, more complex surface; DEFERRED for separate future round), `himitsu-store` (low-level CLI; DEFERRED), `hiprompt-tty` (interactive prompt; DEFERRED).  Other probed Hare tools: `xdavidwu/motion-control` (0 stars, niche), `krebsnebel4441/hearelf` (0 stars, learning project), `dspx-plcr/mart` (0 stars, narrow runner tool), `eblade/paz` (2 stars, password tool but small CLI), `gyreas/hare-lsp` (0 stars, LSP server is non-CLI), `Hojun-Cho/powerctl-clone` (0 stars, niche).  Chose hiq: 11 flags + 1 positional in one CLEAN 130-line main.ha, exercises EVERY MAJOR Hare getopt PATTERN (tagged-union help array with all 3 arms, char-literal short flags, variadic-spread parse, defer cleanup, for-..-iteration, tuple-index access, switch-case dispatch), real binary from Drew DeVault's sourcehut ecosystem (sister to mrsh / aerc / wlroots / sway / scdoc), published in Drew's KISS-package-style git repo.  Sister himitsud / himitsu-store / hiprompt-tty deferred for future R-rounds.
  - Blacklist update: `hiq` added.  Corpus count: 28,730 → 28,731 (+1).

- **R307 — umockdev-run source-direct (kernel-mock testbed runner in Vala; `GLib.OptionContext` stdlib library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Elixir Optimus (R306 unicli) into the **Vala / GNOME tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Vala CLI library** + **first documented GLib OptionContext binding**.  umockdev-run is the runner half of umockdev — a kernel-mock framework that LOADS device descriptions / ioctl dumps / pcap USB captures / evemu event files into a TESTBED, then runs a program under that testbed via LD_PRELOAD.  Used by the libinput, NetworkManager, udisks2, upower, and udev test suites.  Authored by Martin Pitt (Canonical; later Red Hat).  355 GH stars.  Sister tools in same project: umockdev-record (DEFERRED to separate round — same GLib.OptionContext usage, different binary), umockdev-wrapper (bash helper, not Vala).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Vala CLI library** — adds `GLib.OptionContext` (the C-ABI struct-literal table pervasively used across the GNOME ecosystem; bindable from C, Vala, Genie, Python via PyGObject, JavaScript via GJS, Lua via lgi) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (46 library variants now).
  - `_umockdev-run` (1-stem; martinpitt/umockdev src/umockdev-run.vala; **Vala `GLib.OptionContext` library** — GLib stdlib parser at `glib/goption.h` + `glib/goption.c`, pervasively used across GNOME; **6 OptionArg.FILENAME_ARRAY repeatable flags + 1 OptionArg.NONE boolean + 1 OptionArg.STRING_ARRAY positional via empty-name sentinel + `const GLib.OptionEntry[] options = {...}` null-terminated static struct-literal table + Vala `[CCode (array_length=false, array_null_terminated=true)]` C-ABI attribute on bindings + `new OptionContext("usage string")` constructor + `oc.set_summary(...)` method-call config + `oc.add_main_entries(options, null)` table registration + `oc.parse(ref args)` mutating-argv parse + exception-based error handling via try/catch on `Error`**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` lines 23-39 (Vala `[CCode (...)]` ATTRIBUTE block — ten parsed-value bindings declared with `[CCode (array_length=false, array_null_terminated=true)] static string[] opt_NAME;`; instructs the valac compiler how to marshal the field to/from C calling-convention)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` lines 41-60 (`const GLib.OptionEntry[] options = { ... };` — static array of OptionEntry struct literals; each entry is a 7-field tuple `{long-name, short-name-char, flags-bitmask, OptionArg-enum, ref var, help-string, arg-placeholder-string}`; `{null}` sentinel terminates the table)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 41 entry 1 (`{"device", 'd', 0, OptionArg.FILENAME_ARRAY, ref opt_device, "Load an umockdev-record device description ... Can be specified multiple times.", "filename"}` — REPEATABLE FILENAME-ARRAY option; each `-d FILE` invocation appends to `opt_device`)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 57 (`{"", 0, 0, OptionArg.STRING_ARRAY, ref opt_program, "", ""}` — EMPTY-LONG-NAME ENTRY for POSITIONAL ARGS; GLib's convention is that an entry with `""` for long-name and `0` for short-name catches all remaining argv as a STRING_ARRAY)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 58 (`{"version", 0, 0, OptionArg.NONE, ref opt_version, "Output version information and exit"}` — `OptionArg.NONE` is the BOOLEAN FLAG marker; bound variable is `bool`)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 78 (`var oc = new OptionContext ("-- program [args..]");` — OptionContext constructor takes the USAGE STRING displayed after the program name in `--help` output)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 79 (`oc.set_summary ("Run a program under an umockdev testbed.");` — fluent method-call for help summary)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` line 80 (`oc.add_main_entries (options, null);` — register the static option table; `null` is the GETTEXT TRANSLATION DOMAIN — when non-null, GLib runs help strings through gettext for i18n)
    * `src/umockdev-run.vala` lines 81-84 (`try { oc.parse (ref args); } catch (Error e) { stderr.printf("Error: %s\n", e.message); return 1; }` — Vala's `ref` keyword passes the argv array BY REFERENCE; GLib MUTATES the array, removing parsed flags and leaving only positionals)
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **Vala `GLib.OptionContext`** at umockdev-run.vala:78 — corpus's **first documented Vala CLI library** + **first documented GLib OptionContext binding**.  GLib.OptionContext is the GLib C library's CLI parser (header `<glib/goption.h>`, implementation in `glib/goption.c`).  Used by nearly every GNOME C/Vala/Genie program — gtk-launch, gnome-settings-daemon, gnome-control-center, evolution, totem, rhythmbox, banshee, pitivi, brasero, etc.  Bound from C, Vala, Genie, Python (via GObject-Introspection PyGObject), JavaScript (GJS), Lua (lgi).  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (lower-level, no help/error generation), R305 C++ CLI11 (third-party single-header), R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (different postfix-marker syntax).  GLib.OptionContext is C-CALLER-FACING but FULLY GOBJECT-INTROSPECTED — every binding sees the same API.  Corpus's **first documented C-ABI-table-with-GObject-introspection CLI library**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-SIX library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), Elixir Optimus (R306), **Vala GLib.OptionContext** (R307 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 46 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **Vala `OptionEntry[]` static struct-literal table** at umockdev-run.vala:41-60 — `const GLib.OptionEntry[] options = { {entry-1}, {entry-2}, ..., {null} };` is GLib's CANONICAL spec form.  Each entry is a 7-field struct literal: `{long-name, short-name-char, flags, OptionArg-enum, ref var, help-string, arg-placeholder-string}`.  The `{null}` sentinel terminates the table (C-style null termination — Vala's `OptionEntry[]` is bound to GLib's `GOptionEntry*` C array).  Distinct from R304 Ada Define_Switch (positional method args), R305 C++ CLI11 (chained method calls), R306 Elixir Optimus (keyword list).  GLib's approach: PURE DATA TABLE — the spec is a STATIC ARRAY of POD STRUCTS, parseable without any helper functions.  Corpus's **first documented null-terminated-static-struct-table CLI pattern** — a C-ism preserved through to Vala via direct ABI binding.
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **GLib `OptionArg.FILENAME_ARRAY` for repeatable typed flags** at umockdev-run.vala:41 et seq — GLib's OptionArg enum has 6 main variants: `.NONE` (boolean), `.STRING` (single string), `.INT` (single integer), `.FILENAME` (single filename — UTF-8 vs locale handling distinct), `.STRING_ARRAY` (repeatable string), `.FILENAME_ARRAY` (repeatable filename), plus `.CALLBACK` (custom function).  Each `-d FILE` invocation APPENDS to the bound `string[]`.  Distinct from R303 Gleam Glint `strings_flag` (plural-suffix names the repeatable variant), R301 Nim cligen (auto-detected via `seq[T]`), R306 Elixir Optimus (`multiple: true` in option spec).  GLib's approach: ENUM-VALUE-DETERMINES-MULTIPLICITY — the SAME variable type (`string[]`) is bound regardless, but the OptionArg enum value tells the parser whether to store-or-append.  Corpus's **first documented OptionArg-enum-typed CLI parser pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **GLib empty-string-name positional sentinel** at umockdev-run.vala:57 — `{"", 0, 0, OptionArg.STRING_ARRAY, ref opt_program, "", ""}` is the GLib CANONICAL way to capture remaining argv as positionals.  An OptionEntry with `""` long-name AND `0` short-name catches all NON-FLAG argv tokens into the bound `string[]`.  Distinct from R303 Gleam Glint `named_arg` (named-position), R304 Ada `Get_Argument` (stateful iterator), R306 Elixir Optimus `args: [name: [...]]` keyword list, R305 C++ CLI11 (positional detected by no-leading-dash name).  GLib's approach: an EMPTY-NAME OptionEntry is the POSITIONAL CAPTURE SENTINEL.  Corpus's **first documented empty-string-as-positional-sentinel CLI pattern** — a convention rooted in the C-table's avoidance of special positional syntax.
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **Vala `[CCode (array_length=false, array_null_terminated=true)] static string[] opt_NAME;` C-ABI attribute on parser-bound variables** at umockdev-run.vala:23-35 — Vala's `[CCode (...)]` attribute controls how the Vala field is marshalled to/from C calling convention.  GLib's `string[]` (which is C's `char **`) is by default expected to carry an explicit length parameter, but GLib actually uses NULL TERMINATION (sentinel `NULL` at end).  The attribute tells valac: "this string[] is null-terminated, no separate length field".  Distinct from R304 Ada `aliased` (storage qualifier for pointer-of-variable), R305 C++ CLI11 (template-deduced from reference type), R306 Elixir Optimus (no type annotation needed — dynamic).  Vala's approach: C-ABI ATTRIBUTES on the binding side, to match the C ABI of the underlying parser.  Corpus's **first documented C-ABI-attribute-on-binding CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: umockdev-run uses **GLib `oc.parse (ref args)` mutating-argv parse-pattern** at umockdev-run.vala:82 — Vala's `ref` keyword passes the argv `string[]` BY REFERENCE; GLib MUTATES the array, removing parsed flags and leaving only positionals (CALLER MUST RE-READ args.length after parse).  Distinct from R304 Ada Getopt(Config) (single-shot, no argv mutation — bindings get values), R305 C++ CLI11_PARSE macro (immutable argc/argv, parsed-values bound to struct fields), R306 Elixir Optimus (immutable input, returns parsed tuple), R303 Gleam Glint (Result discriminated union return).  GLib's approach: ARGV-AS-MUTABLE-STATE — the same array is both input and output of parsing.  Common in C-style APIs.  Corpus's **first documented mutating-argv CLI parse pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_pdfpc` (1828-star Vala PDF presenter — ALREADY IN CORPUS), `_tiv` (terminal image viewer, ALREADY IN CORPUS), `_avizo` (notification daemon, ALREADY IN CORPUS), `_valadoc` / `_vapigen` (Vala toolchain — in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh).  Probed `BuddiesOfBudgie/budgie-desktop` (1315 stars but the main panel daemon's --help surface is minimal; many subordinate binaries are GUI-focused), `ueno/libkkc` (Japanese input library, 112 stars — bound to GLib but `kkc.vala` is more interactive than CLI), `RidgeRun/gstd` (GStreamer Daemon, 29 stars), `nick92/coffee` (news feed, 42 stars — minimal CLI surface), `tiliado/nuvolaplayer` (Web media player, 331 stars — GUI app), `martinpitt/umockdev` (CHOSEN — 355 stars, real testing framework used by libinput / NetworkManager / udisks2 / upower / udev test suites, umockdev-run.vala exercises EVERY MAJOR GLib.OptionContext PATTERN in a clean 213-line file).  Chose umockdev-run: 7 FILENAME_ARRAY repeatable flags + 1 NONE boolean + 1 STRING_ARRAY positional via empty-name sentinel, full OptionEntry[] static-table demonstration, Vala `[CCode (...)]` C-ABI attributes on bindings, `ref args` argv-mutation parse pattern, exception-based error handling.  Sister `umockdev-record` deferred for separate future round (different binary, similar GLib.OptionContext usage).
  - Blacklist update: `umockdev-run` added.  Corpus count: 28,729 → 28,730 (+1).

- **R306 — unicli source-direct (UniFi controller CLI in Elixir; funbox/`Optimus` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from C++ CLI11 (R305 hibp_search) into the **Elixir language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Elixir CLI library**.  unicli queries and controls Ubiquiti UniFi devices/networks/clients/vouchers/alerts from the command line, talking to the UniFi controller's HTTP API.  Authored by apognu (15 GH stars).  Sister tools: ubnt-discover (UniFi device discovery, not in corpus), unifi-protect-viewer (UniFi camera viewer).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Elixir CLI library** — adds funbox/Optimus (atom-list subcommand dispatch + compile-time module-attribute spec-reuse + pattern-match-as-validator) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (45 library variants now).  Elixir joins Erlang (R292) on the BEAM VM — corpus now documents CLI libraries on BOTH BEAM languages.
  - `_unicli` (1-stem; apognu/unicli lib/unicli.ex + lib/cli.ex; **Elixir `Optimus` library** — funbox/optimus, the de-facto modern Elixir CLI library; **3-LEVEL NESTED SUBCOMMAND TREE (35 commands total) + Elixir keyword-list spec + atom-keyed nested `subcommands:` + `@site` compile-time module-attribute spec-reuse at 24 sites + pattern-match anonymous-function parsers (`fn "on" -> {:ok, true}; "off" -> {:ok, false} end`) + `parser: :string`/`:integer`/`:float` typed-parser atoms + `Optimus.new!`/`Optimus.parse!` raising-pair convention + `[:devices | subcommands]` atom-list-cons subcommand-path destructuring + `allow_double_dash: true` POSIX `--` sentinel opt-in + `allow_unknown_flags: false` strict-mode declaration**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `lib/unicli.ex` line ~90 (`parser = Optimus.new!(CLI.options()) |> Optimus.parse!(args)` — Optimus's PRIMARY ENTRY POINT pair; Elixir pipe `|>` chain; both `!` variants raise on error per Elixir convention)
    * `lib/unicli.ex` lines ~100-120 (subcommand DISPATCH via Elixir pattern match: `case parser do {[:devices | subcommands], %Optimus.ParseResult{} = options} -> UniCLI.Devices.run(...)`; CONS-CELL DESTRUCTURING `[:devices | subcommands]` splits head from tail; head is the first subcommand atom, tail is the rest)
    * `lib/cli.ex` lines 2-9 (`@site` MODULE ATTRIBUTE — Elixir SHARED-OPTION REUSE pattern; `@site [site: [short: "-s", long: "--site", help: "...", default: "default", parser: :string]]`; referenced as `options: @site` at 24 subcommand spec sites; Elixir expands the attribute LEXICALLY at compile time, so this is true SPEC-LEVEL DRY with ZERO RUNTIME COST)
    * `lib/cli.ex` line 14 (`allow_double_dash: true` — Optimus opt-in for POSIX `--` sentinel convention; argv tokens after `--` are treated as POSITIONALS regardless of leading dashes)
    * `lib/cli.ex` line 13 (`allow_unknown_flags: false` — explicit strict-mode declaration; Optimus rejects unknown flags by default)
    * `lib/cli.ex` line 18 (`subcommands: [sites: [...], devices: [...], ...]` — Elixir keyword list; `[atom_key: value, ...]` desugars to `[{:atom_key, value}, ...]`; arbitrary nesting supported)
    * `lib/cli.ex` lines 88-92 (locate's state arg: `parser: fn "on" -> {:ok, true}; "off" -> {:ok, false}; _ -> {:error, "only 'on' and 'off' are accepted"} end` — Elixir multi-clause anonymous function pattern-matches on input and returns `{:ok, parsed_value}` or `{:error, message}`; PATTERN-MATCH-AS-VALIDATOR with TRANSFORM (converts "on"/"off" to true/false))
    * `lib/cli.ex` multiple sites (`parser: :string` / `parser: :integer` / `parser: :float` — Optimus TYPE-PARSER ATOMS; built-in coercion functions)
    * `lib/cli.ex` line 4 (`short: "-s"` + `long: "--site"` — Optimus explicit short/long flag keys in the spec keyword list)
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Elixir `Optimus` library** at unicli.ex:~90 — corpus's **first documented Elixir CLI library**.  funbox/optimus is the de-facto modern Elixir CLI library — supports nested subcommand trees, typed args, custom parser closures, and global + per-command options.  Distinct from Elixir's stdlib `OptionParser` (low-level token loop, similar to R287 Nim parseopt — used by `mix` tasks but no subcommand support), `cli` (less popular), and Erlang's `getopt:parse` (R292 emqtt — same BEAM VM but Erlang flavor).  Optimus adds the SUBCOMMAND TREE that stdlib OptionParser lacks.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-FIVE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), C++ CLI11 (R305), **Elixir Optimus** (R306 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 45 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Elixir joins Erlang (R292) on the BEAM VM — corpus now documents CLI libraries on BOTH BEAM languages.
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Optimus `[atom_path]` subcommand dispatch** at unicli.ex:~100-120 — Optimus returns a tuple `{[atom, atom, ...], %Optimus.ParseResult{}}` where the atom-list is the SUBCOMMAND PATH the user took (e.g. `unicli devices ports list` → `[:devices, :ports, :list]`).  Elixir CASE-MATCHES on the atom list with the CONS-CELL DESTRUCTURING `[:devices | subcommands]` — head is the first atom, tail is the rest.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli (path as VECTOR of STRINGS, prefix-matched against command list), R303 Gleam Glint (path as LIST of STRINGS, longest-prefix-matched).  Optimus uses ELIXIR ATOMS — interned-symbol identifiers compiled into the BEAM VM atom table; cheaper than strings for case-match dispatch (atoms are integer-compared, strings are byte-compared).  Corpus's **first documented atom-list-subcommand-path CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Elixir module-attribute spec-reuse** at cli.ex:2-9 — `@site [site: [short: "-s", long: "--site", help: "which site to talk to", default: "default", parser: :string]]` defines a SHARED OPTION SPECIFICATION at COMPILE TIME via the `@` attribute syntax.  Referenced as `options: @site` at 24 subcommand spec sites in cli.ex.  Elixir expands the attribute LEXICALLY at compile time, so `@site` is INLINED at each reference site — this is a true SPEC-LEVEL DRY mechanism with ZERO RUNTIME COST.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli's `(add-global-aliases COMMANDS)` (RUNTIME map over the data spec), R303 Gleam Glint's `glint.add(at:, do:)` (no spec-level reuse — each subcommand defines its own flags), R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (no equivalent).  Corpus's **first documented compile-time-attribute-spec-reuse CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Optimus pattern-match function as inline validator** at cli.ex:88-92 (locate's state arg) — `parser: fn "on" -> {:ok, true}; "off" -> {:ok, false}; _ -> {:error, "only 'on' and 'off' are accepted"} end`.  Elixir's MULTI-CLAUSE ANONYMOUS FUNCTIONS pattern-match on the input value and return either `{:ok, parsed_value}` or `{:error, message}`.  Distinct from R305 C++ CLI11 `->check(CLI::IsMember({"on","off"}))` (validator object), R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (caller validates post-Getopt), R303 Gleam Glint (typed `int_flag` / `string_flag` only).  Optimus's approach: PATTERN-MATCH-AS-VALIDATOR — sister to R295 CL clingon's `:option-type :choice :choices '("on" "off")` but arbitrarily more powerful (the parser can also TRANSFORM the value, here converting "on"/"off" to `true`/`false`).  Corpus's **first documented pattern-match-as-validator CLI parser pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Optimus `parser: :atom` typed-parser specification** at cli.ex multiple sites — `parser: :string`, `parser: :integer`, `parser: :float`.  Each parser atom triggers the matching built-in coercion function.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (auto-derives from proc param TYPE — `int`/`bool`/`seq[T]`), R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (no parser config — the variable's TYPE determines parsing via aliased's static type), R305 C++ CLI11 (template-deduces from reference target type).  Optimus uses EXPLICIT atom specs because Elixir is DYNAMICALLY TYPED — there's no compile-time target-type to deduce from.  Corpus's **first documented atom-key-typed-parser CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: unicli uses **Optimus `Optimus.new!` / `Optimus.parse!` raising-pair convention** at unicli.ex:~90 — Elixir's CONVENTION is to provide PAIRS of functions: `name!` raises an exception on error, `name` returns `{:ok, _}` | `{:error, _}` tagged tuple.  `Optimus.new!(spec) |> Optimus.parse!(args)` is the IMPERATIVE error-via-exception variant.  Distinct from R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line (Getopt raises Exit_From_Command_Line on help), R305 C++ CLI11_PARSE macro (try/catch wrapper around parse), R303 Gleam Glint (glint.execute returns Result discriminated union).  Optimus integrates with Elixir's BANG-CONVENTION — if you want error tuples back, you'd write `with {:ok, parser} <- Optimus.new(spec), do: Optimus.parse(parser, args)` instead.  Corpus's **first documented raising-pair-convention CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_iex` / `_livebook` / `_elixir` / `_mix` / `_hex` (Elixir stdlib tools all in corpus — and they use stdlib OptionParser not Optimus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh), `_lake` / `_elan` / `_lean4` (Lean ecosystem in corpus), `_alr` (Alire Ada in corpus).  Probed `sysread/fnord` (AI code archaeologist, 41 stars — but uses ESCRIPT + Optimus with auto-generated spec from Burrito; harder to extract source-direct), `proxyconf/proxyconf` (Envoy proxy control plane, 15 stars — uses Optimus but spec is spread across many files), `pcharbon70/aiex` (Elixir AI agent, 5 stars — newer/less stable), `croesnick/taskbeam` (0 stars — too new), `matthewsinclair/arca_cli` (0 stars — CLI framework, not a CLI tool), `savonarola/optimus` (THE LIBRARY ITSELF, 21 stars — not a CLI), `QuinnWilton/beam_spy` (1 star — too small).  Chose unicli: 15 stars, FULL THREE-LEVEL NESTED subcommand tree (35 commands total — devices → ports → enable, networks → wlan → list, clients → guests → authorize, radius → users → create), `@site` compile-time module-attribute spec-reuse DEMONSTRATED at 24 sites in cli.ex, custom pattern-match parser closure for `state` arg (`"on"`→true/`"off"`→false), 7-flag `vouchers create` subcommand exercising integer parser + custom Timex.Duration parser closure.  Single-file cli.ex (442 lines) makes source-direct extraction clean.  Stable v0.0.1 release.
  - Blacklist update: `unicli` added.  Corpus count: 28,728 → 28,729 (+1).

- **R305 — hibp_search source-direct (Have-I-Been-Pwned offline password search in C++; CLIUtils/CLI11 library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304 vaniton) into the **C++ language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented C++ CLI library**.  hibp_search performs high-performance offline queries against a downloaded Have I Been Pwned password database — compares a plaintext password (or hash) against billions of breached credentials.  Authored by oschonrock (64 GH stars).  Part of a 10-binary cluster (`hibp_search`, `hibp_topn`, `hibp_sort`, `hibp_dupes`, `hibp_diff`, `hibp_download`, `hibp_convert`, `hibp_server`, `hibp_build_filter`, `hibp_query_filter`) — the other 9 sister binaries are DEFERRED to future rounds; they all use CLI11 with the same factored-function `define_options(CLI::App&, cli_config_t&)` pattern.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented C++ CLI library** — adds CLIUtils/CLI11 (header-only single-include, compile-time-typed reference binding, fluent `->check(...)` method-chain validators) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (44 library variants now).  Corpus now documents CLI libraries in BOTH C (R272 POSIX getopt) and C++ — the most pervasive systems-language family.
  - `_hibp_search` (1-stem; oschonrock/hibp app/hibp_search.cpp; **C++ `CLI11` library** — CLIUtils/CLI11, ~5000-line single-header `CLI/CLI.hpp`, C++11 minimum / C++17 recommended; **2 required positionals + 4 bool flags + 1 ranged unsigned (CLI::Range 15-25) + factored `define_options(CLI::App&, cli_config_t&)` function + struct-field reference-binding via add_option/add_flag + fluent `->required()` / `->check(CLI::Range(...))` method-chain validators + `CLI11_PARSE` macro for parse-driver-and-error-flow boilerplate**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` line 5 (`#include <CLI/CLI.hpp>` — CLI11 single-header include; ~30 sub-headers under `CLI/` are transitively included; no `.cpp` link step needed)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` lines 22-30 (`struct cli_config_t { std::string db_filename; std::string plain_text_password; bool toc = false; bool hash = false; bool ntlm = false; bool sha1t64 = false; unsigned toc_bits = 20; };` — C++ PLAIN AGGREGATE struct holding parsed values; bound by reference to CLI11 add_option/add_flag)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` line 34 (`void define_options(CLI::App& app, cli_config_t& cli)` — FACTORED FUNCTION pattern: separates spec from main() for reusability; same pattern used by all 10 sister hibp_* binaries)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` lines 36-39 (`app.add_option("db_filename", cli.db_filename, "...")->required();` — POSITIONAL detected by name having NO leading dash; `->required()` is the fluent method-chain validator)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` lines 45-52 (FOUR `app.add_flag(NAME, REF, HELP)` calls — bool reference binding, PRESENCE-ONLY toggle)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` lines 54-58 (`app.add_option("--toc-bits", cli.toc_bits, fmt::format("..."))->check(CLI::Range(15, 25));` — VALIDATOR CHAINED via ->check(...); CLI::Range is CLI11's built-in inclusive-range validator)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` line 113 (`CLI::App app;` — bare-default-constructed App; description not provided here)
    * `app/hibp_search.cpp` line 115 (`CLI11_PARSE(app, argc, argv);` — CPP MACRO expands to `try { app.parse(argc, argv); } catch (const CLI::ParseError &e) { return app.exit(e); }`; packages standard parser-driver-and-error-flow boilerplate)
  - Critical extraction note: hibp_search uses **C++ `CLI11` library** at hibp_search.cpp:5 — corpus's **first documented C++ CLI library**.  CLI11 is CLIUtils/CLI11, the de-facto modern C++ CLI library — header-only, single `CLI/CLI.hpp` include (~5000 lines), no external deps, C++11 minimum / C++17 recommended.  Distinct from R272 C POSIX getopt (which DOES work in C++ but is stylistically C — getopt_long returns a single int, manual switch-case dispatch).  CLI11 is fully C++ template-based: type-safe binding to struct fields via reference, fluent method-chain API, expression-template error reporting.  Distinct from BOOST::PROGRAM_OPTIONS (Boost-heavy, link-step required), cxxopts (simpler header-only but less feature-rich), gflags (Google, global-state).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-FOUR library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), Ada GNAT.Command_Line (R304), **C++ CLI11** (R305 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 44 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  ALSO: corpus now documents CLI libraries in BOTH C and C++ — the most pervasive systems-language family.
  - Critical extraction note: hibp_search uses **C++ `CLI11` reference-binding pattern** at hibp_search.cpp:36-58 — each `app.add_option(NAME, REF, HELP)` call takes a REFERENCE to the struct field where the parsed value will be written.  CLI11's template machinery deduces the target type from the reference type — `std::string&` binds string parsing, `bool&` binds bool, `unsigned&` binds unsigned int.  Type errors are caught at COMPILE TIME via SFINAE.  Distinct from R304 Ada `'Access` (explicit pointer-of-aliased-variable; opt-in via `aliased` qualifier), R272 C POSIX getopt (no binding — caller switch-cases on getopt's return value), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (parsed map), R303 Gleam Glint (closure-bound).  CLI11's approach: COMPILE-TIME-TYPED REFERENCE BINDING — closest analog: Boost.Program_Options `po::value<T>(&target)` but CLI11 deduces type whereas Boost requires explicit `<T>` template.  Corpus's **first documented compile-time-typed-reference-binding CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hibp_search uses **CLI11 `->check(CLI::Range(...))` chained validator pattern** at hibp_search.cpp:58 — `app.add_option("--toc-bits", cli.toc_bits, ...)->check(CLI::Range(15, 25))`.  CLI11's add_option returns an `Option*` pointer; method-chain `->check(...)` attaches a VALIDATOR OBJECT to the option.  CLI11 ships built-in validators: `CLI::Range(MIN, MAX)` (inclusive integer range), `CLI::ExistingFile`, `CLI::ExistingDirectory`, `CLI::NonexistentPath`, `CLI::PositiveNumber`, `CLI::IsMember(set)`, plus any callable `[](auto& v){...}` lambda.  Validators run BEFORE the value reaches the bound reference — rejection is caught by CLI11_PARSE's macro-injected try/catch.  Distinct from R304 Ada (no equivalent; caller validates post-Getopt), R301 Nim cligen (validates via Nim type coercion at parseInt time), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (no built-in validators — caller checks the map).  Closest analog: argparse `type=int, choices=range(15, 26)`.  Corpus's **first documented chainable-method-validator CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hibp_search uses **CLI11 `->required()` chained-validator-via-no-arg method** at hibp_search.cpp:39 — `app.add_option("db_filename", cli.db_filename, "...")->required();`.  CLI11 distinguishes REQUIRED from OPTIONAL via the FLUENT METHOD `->required()` rather than via a constructor parameter.  Sister to but distinct from R270 Haskell optparse-applicative's `Mandatory` parser type (type-level distinction), R304 Ada GNAT.Command_Line's Initial-value-IS-default convention (no explicit required marker; missing values use Initial), R301 Nim cligen's no-default-equals-required inference (proc params without `=DEFAULT` are required).  CLI11's method-call approach makes required-ness ORTHOGONAL to type and binding — easy to make a field optional without changing its type.  Corpus's **first documented method-call-marks-required CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hibp_search uses **CLI11 `CLI11_PARSE` macro** at hibp_search.cpp:115 — `CLI11_PARSE(app, argc, argv);` is a CPP MACRO that expands to `try { app.parse(argc, argv); } catch (const CLI::ParseError &e) { return app.exit(e); }`.  The macro packages the standard parser-driver-and-error-flow boilerplate so user code stays clean.  Distinct from R304 Ada Getopt single-shot (raises Exit_From_Command_Line exception), R301 Nim cligen dispatch (compile-time macro emits the parser), R302 Clojure babashka.cli cli/dispatch (returns the parsed-opts map), R303 Gleam Glint glint.execute (returns Result discriminated union).  CLI11's macro is the ONLY way to get correct CLI11 error handling — manual parse-and-catch is possible but the macro is preferred.  Corpus's **first documented parser-driver-as-cpp-macro CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped sister `_hibp_topn` / `_hibp_sort` / `_hibp_dupes` / `_hibp_diff` / `_hibp_download` / `_hibp_convert` / `_hibp_server` / `_hibp_build_filter` / `_hibp_query_filter` (DEFERRED for separate future rounds — each has its own distinct CLI surface and they all share the CLI11 `define_options(CLI::App&, cli_config_t&)` factored-function pattern).  Probed `pisa-engine/pisa` (1048 GH stars, academic search engine — dozens of binaries (queries, compress_inverted_index, parse_collection, etc.), too large for a single round; deferred for cluster-style harvest), `xifan2333/fcitx5-vinput` (245 stars, voice input for Fcitx5 — subcommand registration split across multiple files via RegisterConfigCli/RegisterControlCli factored modules; harder to extract source-direct), `NitroCao/mydocker` (1 star, too small), `arnemertz/fix` (21 stars, but the project is being restarted), `codehz/cobblectl` (1 star, uses non-standard user-defined-literal `_str`/`_flag`/`_vstr` CLI11 wrappers — non-idiomatic), `willkill07/lox-cpp` (0 stars, only 1 positional — too minimal), `xmc-aalto/dismecpp` (7 stars, large-scale ML classifier — narrow surface).  Chose hibp_search: 64 stars in a real serious project, clean CLI11 surface in ONE FILE (2 required positionals + 4 bool flags + 1 ranged unsigned), exercises EVERY MAJOR CLI11 PATTERN (App constructor, add_option vs add_flag, ->required(), ->check(CLI::Range), CLI11_PARSE macro), real binary distributed via the project's CMake build, and the 9 sister hibp_* binaries provide future R-round material.
  - Blacklist update: `hibp_search` added.  Corpus count: 28,727 → 28,728 (+1).

- **R304 — vaniton source-direct (TON-blockchain vanity-address generator in Ada; `GNAT.Command_Line` stdlib library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Gleam Glint (R303 gleepack) into the **Ada language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Ada CLI library**.  vaniton is a vanity-address generator for The Open Network (TON) blockchain wallets — generates 24-word secret phrases, computes wallet addresses, filters by user-supplied pattern, multi-threaded.  Pure Ada 2012, supports Linux and FreeBSD.  Authored by AntonMeep (69 GH stars).  Sister tools: alr (Alire Ada package manager, already in corpus as `_alr`), gnatmake (Ada compiler driver, already in corpus as `_gnatmake`), gprbuild (GNAT Project build, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Ada CLI library** — adds AdaCore stdlib `GNAT.Command_Line` (imperative `aliased`/`'Access` pointer-registration + `Define_Switch`/`Define_Alias`/`Getopt` triple) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (43 library variants now).
  - `_vaniton` (1-stem; AntonMeep/vaniton source/vaniton.adb; **Ada `GNAT.Command_Line` library** — AdaCore stdlib that ships with every GNAT compiler at `gcc/ada/g-comlin.ads`/`g-comlin.adb`; **6 switches with `:`/`=` postfix-marker syntax + 13 `Define_Alias` value-embedded shortcuts + `aliased` storage qualifier + `'Access` pointer-registration + `not DEFAULT` inverted-default boolean pattern + `Exit_From_Command_Line` exception-based help-flag handling + `Get_Argument` stateful-iterator positional retrieval + `Set_Usage` Ada named-argument call**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `source/vaniton.adb` line 5 (`with GNAT.Command_Line; use GNAT.Command_Line;` — Ada `with` import + `use` namespace-opener; GNAT.Command_Line is AdaCore's stdlib parser at `gcc/ada/g-comlin.{ads,adb}`)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` line 13 (`Config : Command_Line_Configuration;` — Ada PRIVATE-TYPE declaration; opaque handle that accumulates switch definitions via mutating Define_Switch calls)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` lines 14-19 (six `aliased` parser-bound variables: `Number_Of_Workers : aliased Integer;`, `Wallet_Kind_String : aliased String_Access := new String'("V3_R2");`, `Case_Sensitive : aliased Boolean := False;`, etc.  `aliased` is Ada's marker meaning "this variable can have a pointer taken to it"; without it, Ada's strong aliasing discipline would reject the later `'Access` references at compile time)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` lines 28-43 (`Set_Usage (Config, Usage => "[switches] [pattern]", Help => "...")` — Ada NAMED-ARGUMENT calls with `=>` arrow; distinct from R303 Gleam Glint's `at:`/`do:` colon-style named-arg labels)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` lines 45-65 (six `Define_Switch` calls with postfix-marker switch literals: `"-X"` no-arg, `"-X:"` short with required arg, `"-X?"` short with optional arg, `"--long"` no-arg, `"--long="` long with required arg, `"--long?"` long with optional arg.  For BOOLEAN switches the 6-arg form `Define_Switch (Config, ACCESS, "-c", "--case-sensitive", HELP, "", not DEFAULT)` is used — the 6th argument is the value to set on match, with `not DEFAULT` reading as "flip the default")
    * `source/vaniton.adb` lines 66-78 (THIRTEEN `Define_Alias` calls — each maps an ABBREVIATED token like `-wv4` to an expanded form like `--wallet=V4_R2`; GNAT.Command_Line REWRITES the alias BEFORE option parsing proceeds; value is BAKED INTO the flag itself)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` line 80 (`Getopt (Config);` — single statement triggers the parse; mutates `aliased` bindings through the stored `'Access` pointers; no return value)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` line 83 (`Pattern : constant String := Get_Argument;` — stateful-iterator positional retrieval; successive Get_Argument calls yield the next positional)
    * `source/vaniton.adb` lines 105-107 (`when Exit_From_Command_Line => GNAT.OS_Lib.OS_Exit (0);` — exception-based help-flag handling; GNAT.Command_Line RAISES this standard exception when the user passes `-h`/`--help` and the main procedure catches it for clean exit)
    * `source/wallets.ads` (`type Wallet_Kind is (Simple_R1, Simple_R2, Simple_R3, V2_R1, V2_R2, V3_R1, V3_R2, V4_R1, V4_R2);` — Ada enum values used as completion candidates for `--wallet=` argument)
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada `GNAT.Command_Line` stdlib library** at vaniton.adb:5 — corpus's **first documented Ada CLI library**.  `GNAT.Command_Line` is AdaCore's stdlib CLI parser (Ada source at `gcc/ada/g-comlin.ads`/`g-comlin.adb` in the GCC tree).  Ships with EVERY GNAT compiler — no external dependency.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (third-party library), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (bundled with Babashka runtime), R303 Gleam Glint (third-party hex.pm package).  Ada's approach is STDLIB-FIRST like R287 Nim parseopt (stdlib), R297 SWI-Prolog optparse (stdlib), R298 Pony stdlib cli, R300 V stdlib flag.  Corpus's **first documented GNAT-stdlib-bundled CLI library**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-THREE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), Gleam Glint (R303), **Ada GNAT.Command_Line** (R304 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 43 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada `aliased` storage qualifier + `'Access` attribute for parser-bound variables** at vaniton.adb:14-19 — `Number_Of_Workers : aliased Integer;` declares the variable as ALIASED (Ada's marker meaning "this variable can have a pointer taken to it").  Then `Define_Switch (Config, Number_Of_Workers'Access, "-j:", "--threads=", ...)` passes the variable's POINTER (via the `'Access` ATTRIBUTE, Ada's address-of operator).  Without `aliased`, Ada's STRONG ALIASING DISCIPLINE would reject the `'Access` reference at compile time.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (compile-time macro generates parser binding direct to proc params — no pointer needed), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (parsed values returned in a map — no mutation), R303 Gleam Glint (parsed values returned via `use`-callback closures — immutable).  Ada's approach: EXPLICIT POINTER REGISTRATION at spec time — the parser writes through the pointer into the caller's variable.  Corpus's **first documented explicit-pointer-registration CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada `Define_Switch` postfix-marker switch literal** at vaniton.adb:45-65 — GNAT.Command_Line's API is IMPERATIVE: `Define_Switch` calls accumulate switch specs into the Config record, then a single `Getopt (Config)` triggers the parse.  The switch strings use POSTFIX MARKERS: `"-X"` (short flag, no arg / boolean), `"-X:"` (short flag with REQUIRED arg — the `:` SUFFIX is the argument marker), `"-X?"` (short flag with OPTIONAL arg), `"--long"` (long flag, no arg), `"--long="` (long flag with REQUIRED arg — the `=` SUFFIX is the argument marker), `"--long?"` (long flag with OPTIONAL arg).  Distinct from POSIX getopt strings (`"a:b"` style at R272 C getopt) and from R281 Perl Getopt::Long `"name=s"` suffix syntax — Ada uses POSTFIX SYMBOL CHARACTERS within the switch literal.  Corpus's **first documented postfix-symbol-in-switch-literal CLI pattern** — combines C-getopt brevity with Perl-style required-vs-optional distinction.
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada `Define_Alias` for VALUE-EMBEDDED short flags** at vaniton.adb:66-78 — 13 `Define_Alias` calls map ABBREVIATED tokens like `-wv4` to expanded long forms like `--wallet=V4_R2`.  Distinct from conventional short flags (`-w V4_R2` is also valid because the underlying switch is `-w:`) — aliases provide ABBREVIATED-VALUE-COMBOS where the value is BAKED INTO the flag itself.  When the user types `-wv4`, GNAT.Command_Line REWRITES it to `--wallet=V4_R2` BEFORE option parsing proceeds.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli `:aliases {:T :tool}` (short-name → long-name map; same-value alias), R301 Nim cligen `short={"output":'o'}` (one-char short for a long), R285 Lua argparse (no value-embedded alias).  Corpus's **first documented value-embedded-alias CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada inverted-default boolean pattern** at vaniton.adb:50-65 — `Define_Switch (Config, Case_Sensitive'Access, "-c", "--case-sensitive", "...", "", not Case_Sensitive)`.  The 6th argument is the VALUE TO SET when the flag is matched — `not Case_Sensitive` reads as "set to the OPPOSITE of the current default".  Since `Case_Sensitive` defaults to False, passing `-c` sets it to True.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (default in proc param), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (default via `cli/parse-opts :spec {:default ...}`), R303 Gleam Glint (default via builder method).  Ada's approach: EXPLICIT VALUE COMPUTATION at spec time via `not DEFAULT`.  Reads as the flag FLIPS the default — and works correctly even when defaults are dynamically computed.
