Corpus's **first documented inverted-default-via-not-expression CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: vaniton uses **Ada exception-based help-flag handling** at vaniton.adb:105-107 — `when Exit_From_Command_Line => GNAT.OS_Lib.OS_Exit (0);`.  When the user passes `-h` or `--help`, GNAT.Command_Line RAISES the standard exception `Exit_From_Command_Line` (declared in `GNAT.Command_Line` itself).  The main procedure's `exception` block catches it and exits with status 0.  Sister to R295 CL clingon's condition system but Ada-flavored: CHECKED EXCEPTION control flow for help-text emission.  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon's `--help` flag (auto-handled by macro), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (returns `:help true` in parsed map for caller to check), R303 Gleam Glint (returns `glint.Help(help)` constructor in Result).  Ada's approach is EXCEPTION-AS-EARLY-EXIT — semantically equivalent to a `goto end` from the parser into the caller's exception block.  Corpus's **first documented exception-based-help-flag CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_alr` (Alire Ada package manager, already in corpus + blacklisted — uses GNAT.Command_Line + Cobra-style subcommand framework; complex surface), `_gnatmake` / `_gprbuild` / `_gprinstall` / `_gprclean` (GNAT project toolchain, all in corpus), `_iex` / `_livebook` / `_elixir` (Elixir tools in corpus), `_unison` (DUP with stock zsh — would violate the no-stock-zsh-dups feedback rule).  Probed `dmbaturin/hvinfo` (Ada hypervisor detector, 18 stars — uses GNAT.Command_Line but smaller surface), `OpenAADL/ocarina` (AADL model processor, 76 stars — complex multi-binary toolchain), `stcarrez/ada-awa` (Ada Web Application framework, 109 stars — but the framework itself isn't a CLI; only `awa-commands-info` is a library helper), `AdaCore/gnatstudio` (IDE, enormous codebase), `AntonMeep/vaniton` (CHOSEN — 69 stars, 6 switches with full `:`/`=` postfix-marker surface, 13 `Define_Alias` calls, real binary published in `alire.toml`, exercises the Ada-specific `aliased`/`'Access` pointer-registration pattern).  Chose vaniton: focused single-binary tool with EVERY MAJOR GNAT.Command_Line PATTERN represented (Set_Usage, Define_Switch with all 4 postfix markers, Define_Alias for value-embedded shortcuts, Getopt single-shot parse, Exit_From_Command_Line exception handler, Get_Argument positional retrieval), and stable v0.10.0 release.
  - Blacklist update: `vaniton` added.  Corpus count: 28,726 → 28,727 (+1).

- **R303 — gleepack source-direct (Gleam standalone-executable packager; TanklesXL/`glint` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Clojure babashka.cli (R302 bbin) into the **Gleam language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Gleam CLI library**.  gleepack builds Gleam apps into standalone executables, optimised for CLIs, TUIs, and dev tools — performs dead-code elimination and target-specific release compilation.  Authored by yoshi-monster (10 GH stars).  Sister tools: gleam (Gleam compiler, already in corpus as `_gleam`), gleescript (Gleam-to-Erlang escript builder, NOT in corpus — different library), birdie (Gleam snapshot testing, NOT in corpus — uses HANDWRITTEN parser, not Glint).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Gleam CLI library** — adds Glint (chained pipe-builder + `use`-callback CPS) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (42 library variants now).
  - `_gleepack` (1-stem; yoshi-monster/gleepack src/gleepack.gleam; **Gleam `glint` library**; **9 top-level subcommands + 5 nested `targets {available,installed,add,remove,clean}` subcommands + pipe-style `|>` builder + named-arg labels (`at:`/`do:`) + `use`-keyword CPS syntax for flag/command binding + `glint.string_flag` vs `glint.strings_flag` plural-naming distinction + `glint.named_arg` typed positional + triadic `(named, args, flags)` callback signature + `glint.path_help(at:, put:)` intermediate-node help + `glint.as_module` escript-vs-`gleam run` entry-point mode flag + Glint subcommand path as Gleam list of strings**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` line 17 (`import glint` — Gleam package import; non-namespaced unless qualified)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` line 1 (`import argv.{Argv}` — separate Gleam `argv` package; `{Argv}` is constructor-list unqualified-import syntax, sister to Haskell's `import Data.Maybe (Maybe(..))`)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` lines 22-43 (the CLI is CONSTRUCTED with glint's PIPE-STYLE BUILDER: `glint.new() |> glint.as_module |> glint.with_name(...) |> glint.pretty_help(...) |> glint.add(at: [...], do: ...)` — each `|>` step returns a NEW Glint value rather than mutating; immutability-by-default forces functional composition)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` lines 26-39 (`glint.add(at: ["targets", "available"], do: targets.available())` — subcommand path is a GLEAM LIST OF STRINGS; arbitrary-depth nesting supported; `at:` and `do:` are Gleam NAMED-ARGUMENT LABELS)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` line 33 (`glint.path_help(at: ["targets"], put: targets.group_help)` — attach help string to an INTERMEDIATE NODE that has no command body; the `targets` group is purely a namespace)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` line 24 (`glint.as_module` — marks this as an ESCRIPT entry point (`./gleepack args`) vs the implicit `gleam run -m gleepack -- args` mode; strips the redundant leading argv element that `gleam run -m` would prepend)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` line 45 (`let Argv(arguments:, ..) = argv.load()` — Gleam RECORD-PATTERN DESTRUCTURING with `..` for unmatched fields; `arguments:` is the PUNNED bind)
    * `src/gleepack.gleam` lines 47-52 (`case glint.execute(cli, arguments)` — returns `Result(glint.Help | glint.Out(...))` DISCRIMINATED UNION which is `case`-matched on the four constructors `Help`, `Out(Ok(Nil))`, `Out(Error(reason))`, and the toplevel `Error(message)`)
    * `src/gleepack/command/build.gleam` lines 19-65 (build subcommand spec — uses Gleam's `use` CALLBACK SYNTAX: `use <- glint.command_help("...")` injects help text via CPS; `use output <- glint.flag(glint.string_flag("output") |> glint.flag_help("..."))` binds a typed accessor — the result `output` is a function-of-flags that retrieves the flag's value when called as `output(flags)`; `use _, _entries, flags <- glint.command` introduces the COMMAND BODY with three args: NAMED, POSITIONAL, FLAGS)
    * `src/gleepack/command/build.gleam` line 50 (`glint.strings_flag("target")` — REPEATABLE multi-value flag; parses `--target X --target Y --target Z` as a Gleam `List(String)`; distinct from `glint.string_flag` (single) and `glint.int_flag` (int))
    * `src/gleepack/command/targets.gleam` (add/remove subcommands — `use slug <- glint.named_arg("slug")` declares a NAMED POSITIONAL ARGUMENT; the `named` arg in the `glint.command` body's arity-3 callback exposes a helper to retrieve named-positional values by name)
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **Glint pipe-style builder with named-argument labels** at gleepack.gleam:22-43 — Gleam's `|>` pipe operator chains `glint.new()` through a series of `glint.add(at: [...], do: ...)` calls.  Each `|>` step takes the previous Glint value and returns a NEW Glint (Gleam's IMMUTABILITY-BY-DEFAULT forces each step to build a new value rather than mutate).  Sister to but distinct from R270 Haskell optparse-applicative `<**>` chain (which uses applicative `<*>` rather than `|>`) and R285 Lua argparse `parser:option(...)` chain (mutates the parser via method-chaining).  Glint uses GLEAM NAMED-ARGUMENT LABELS `at:` and `do:` — a Gleam-specific syntax that names parameters at the call site.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature-as-spec compile-time macro), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (vector-of-maps data spec), R296 Julia Comonicon (function-signature-as-spec compile-time macro), R295 CL clingon (CLOS subclassing), R290 Racket `command-line` (s-expression DSL macro), and R287 Nim parseopt (manual `for kind, key, val` loop).  Documents the **chained-pipe-builder-with-named-args CLI pattern** — Gleam-specific.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-TWO library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), Clojure babashka.cli (R302), **Gleam Glint** (R303 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 42 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **Gleam `use` callback-syntax for flag/command binding** at command/build.gleam:19-65 — `use FLAG <- glint.flag(SPEC)` is Gleam's CONTINUATION-PASSING-STYLE syntax sugar: it desugars to `glint.flag(SPEC, fn(FLAG) { REST_OF_BLOCK })`.  The Glint API uses this so flag SPECS and the COMMAND BODY live in the same `pub fn command()` block without callback nesting.  Sister to (but distinct from) Haskell's do-notation, R295 CL clingon's `:options (list (clingon:make-option ...))` constructor list, R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature-as-spec), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (vector of `:cmds`/`:fn` maps), and R285 Lua argparse (parser:option chain).  Glint's CPS-syntax-sugar approach uses Gleam's general-purpose `use` keyword to flatten what would otherwise be a deeply nested callback chain.  Corpus's **first documented use-keyword-CPS CLI spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **Glint subcommand path as Gleam list-of-strings** at gleepack.gleam:26-39 — `glint.add(at: ["targets", "available"], do: targets.available())` — the SUBCOMMAND PATH is a Gleam list of strings.  Two-element list `["targets", "available"]` means the command is invoked as `gleepack targets available`.  Glint supports ARBITRARY-DEPTH NESTING by extending the list.  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon (`@cast`-generated subcommand tree from nested namespaces), R301 Nim cligen (no built-in subcommand support — needs `dispatchMulti`).  Sister to R302 Clojure babashka.cli (flat vector with longest-prefix `:cmds` matching) — Glint and babashka.cli BOTH use FLAT LISTS with PATH-VECTORS rather than command trees.  Closest analog: cobra's `Command.AddCommand(...)` tree — but Glint uses a FLAT LIST rather than a tree.  Glint also supports `glint.path_help(at:, put:)` to attach a help string to an INTERMEDIATE NODE (like `targets`) that has no command body — see gleepack.gleam:33.  Documents the **flat-list-path-with-intermediate-help-strings CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **Glint `strings_flag` plural-naming for repeatable multi-value flags** at command/build.gleam:50 — `glint.strings_flag("target")` parses `--target X --target Y --target Z` as a Gleam `List(String)`.  Distinct from `glint.string_flag` (single-string) and `glint.int_flag` (single-int).  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon's `Vector{String}` arg-type (positional-only), R285 Lua argparse `:args("*")` (positional-only), and R301 Nim cligen `seq[T]` (positional-only via type inference).  Glint's `strings_flag` is REPEATABLE FLAG (each `--target X` appends; sister to argparse's `action='append'` and clap's `Vec<String>` with `#[arg(num_args = 1.., action = ArgAction::Append)]`).  Corpus's **first documented Glint plural-flag-suffix-naming pattern** — Glint distinguishes `string_flag` vs `strings_flag` by NOUN PLURALITY in the function name (a Gleam-specific API-design pattern).
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **Glint `named_arg` for typed positional arguments** at command/targets.gleam (add/remove subcommands) — `use slug <- glint.named_arg("slug")` declares a NAMED POSITIONAL ARGUMENT.  The `named` arg in the `glint.command` body's arity-3 callback `use named, _, _ <-` exposes a helper to retrieve named-positional values by name.  Distinct from R302 Clojure babashka.cli's `:args->opts [:script/lib]` (positional bound to named OPTION in the unified map), R301 Nim cligen (positional via `seq[int]` type at end of proc-param list), and R298 Pony's `positional` block (separate from `options` block).  Glint splits the command callback into THREE parameters: `(named, args, flags)` — `named` for named positionals declared via `named_arg`, `args` for overflow free-form positionals, `flags` for `--flag` values.  Corpus's **first documented triadic-callback CLI pattern (named/args/flags separation)**.
  - Critical extraction note: gleepack uses **`glint.as_module` ESCRIPT-vs-`gleam-run` mode flag** at gleepack.gleam:24 — Glint supports TWO entry-point styles: `as_module` (Erlang ESCRIPT — `./gleepack args`) vs the implicit default (`gleam run -m gleepack -- args`).  `as_module` strips the redundant leading argv element that `gleam run -m` would prepend.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (no entry-point mode — compile-time `dispatch` macro is always-on), R302 Clojure babashka.cli (Babashka-specific `*file*` ↔ `babashka.file` main-guard at runtime).  Gleam's `as_module` explicitly signals which runtime convention applies.  Corpus's **first documented Gleam-specific entry-point-mode-flag CLI pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_gleam` (Gleam compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_iex` / `_livebook` / `_elixir` / `_mix` / `_hex` (all Elixir tools already in corpus), `_clojure-lsp` / `_cljfmt` / `_nbb` / `_shadow-cljs` / `_fennel` / `_janet` / `_hy` (Clojure family already in corpus), `_unison` (ships in stock zsh at `/opt/homebrew/share/zsh/functions/_unison` + `/usr/share/zsh/5.9/functions/_unison` — DUP, would violate the no-stock-zsh-dups feedback rule), `_roc` / `_koka` / `_grain` / `_idris` / `_idris2` / `_mercury` / `_mlton` / `_polyml` / `_smlnj` / `_gnatmake` / `_valadoc` / `_vapigen` (exotic langs already in corpus).  Probed `birdie` (Gleam snapshot testing, 199 GH stars — uses HANDWRITTEN parser at birdie/internal/cli, NOT Glint; deferred for separate handwritten-parser-pattern round), `gleescript` (escript builder — minimal CLI surface), `pulsar` (Tkdefender88 static site gen, 1 star — too small), `bygg` (atomfinger Gleam initializr, 7 stars — smaller surface than gleepack), `releam` (pedraal release tool, 0 stars — too new), `wisp/wrserver` (server-bootstrap, not a real CLI), `monitor_bear` (nerdyworm, narrow surface), `glinter` (pairshaped, linter — uses Glint but smaller subcommand tree), `nqcc_gleam` (hiphoox compiler-tutorial, 2 stars).  Chose gleepack: 10 GH stars, 9 SUBCOMMAND FILES with clean Glint surface (pipe-builder + use-syntax + strings_flag plural-naming + path_help group + named_arg positional + as_module escript mode), real packaged tool with hex.pm release, NESTED SUBCOMMAND TREE `targets {available, installed, add, remove, clean}` (exercises Glint's arbitrary-depth subcommand-path-as-list pattern), and EVERY MAJOR GLINT PATTERN represented in one repo.
  - Blacklist update: `gleepack` added.  Corpus count: 28,725 → 28,726 (+1).

- **R302 — bbin source-direct (Babashka script installer in Clojure; `babashka.cli` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Nim cligen (R301 adorn) into the **Clojure language tooling cluster** — corpus's **first documented Clojure CLI library**.  bbin is **the** standard Babashka script installer — installs any Babashka script or project with one command, from a Maven coord, Git URL, raw URL, or local path.  Authored by rads (Rad Cooper) under the babashka GitHub org (182 GH stars; `babashka/brew/bbin` Homebrew tap).  Sister tools: bb (Babashka itself, already in corpus as `_bb`), neil (Clojure project tool, already in corpus as `_neil`).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Clojure CLI library** — adds `babashka.cli` (data-vector dispatch) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (41 library variants now).
  - `_bbin` (1-stem; babashka/bbin src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj; **Clojure `babashka.cli` library**; **9 subcommands + `cli/dispatch` data-vector dispatcher + `:cmds` vector subcommand path + nested `["migrate" "auto"]` subcommand + `:fn` handler-of-one-arg + `:args->opts [:script/lib]` positional-to-named-option binding + `:aliases {:T :tool}` short-flag map + post-spec-decoration `add-global-aliases` (functional composition) + `remove nil?` for conditional `upgrade` command + Clojure qualified-keyword flags `--git/sha` / `--mvn/version` / `--local/root` (slash-in-flag-name) + `*file*` ↔ `(System/getProperty "babashka.file")` main-guard for dual-library-and-binary**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` line 6 (`[babashka.cli :as cli]` — Clojure `:require` alias form; aliases namespace to local symbol `cli`)
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` line 80 (`(cli/dispatch (full-commands run-opts') main-args {})` — babashka.cli PRIMARY ENTRY POINT; takes a SEQ of command maps + the argv + an opts map; walks the command list, prefix-matches argv against `:cmds` vectors, routes to `:fn` for matching command)
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` lines 24-58 (the COMMAND VECTOR — each map: `{:cmds VEC :fn FN :args->opts VEC :aliases MAP}`; `:cmds` is the SUBCOMMAND PATH as a VECTOR of strings; empty vector `[]` is DEFAULT command; multi-element vectors like `["migrate" "auto"]` are NESTED SUBCOMMANDS matched left-to-right)
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` lines 18-22 (`add-global-aliases` — injects `{:h :help}` into every command's `:aliases` map via `(map #(assoc-in % [:aliases :h] :help) commands)`; functional composition over spec mutation)
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` line 60 (`(remove nil?)` — drops the conditional `upgrade` command at runtime when `util/upgrade-enabled?` returns false; corpus's first documented runtime command-list filtering)
    * `src/babashka/bbin/cli.clj` lines 88-90 (`(when (= *file* (System/getProperty "babashka.file")) (util/check-min-bb-version) (apply -main *command-line-args*))` — Babashka MAIN-FILE GUARD pattern; Clojure's `*file*` dynamic var vs Babashka's `babashka.file` system property; runtime equality check because bb interprets scripts at load time)
    * `README.md` "Supported Options" + "CLI" sections (10 install options: `--as`, `--git/sha`, `--git/tag`, `--git/url`, `--latest-sha`, `--local/root`, `--main-opts`, `--mvn/version`, `--ns-default`, `--tool/-T`; plus subcommands: install, uninstall, ls, bin, version, help, migrate, migrate auto, upgrade, commands)
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **`babashka.cli` declarative dispatch** at cli.clj:80 — `dispatch` is a DECLARATIVE PREFIX-DISPATCHER: takes a VECTOR of command maps, prefix-matches the argv against `:cmds` vectors, then routes to the `:fn` for the matching command.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (proc-signature-as-spec compile-time macro), R296 Julia Comonicon (function-signature-as-spec compile-time macro), R295 CL clingon (CLOS subclassing for subcommands), R290 Racket `command-line` (s-expression DSL macro), and R287 Nim parseopt (manual `for kind, key, val in getopt():` loop).  babashka.cli's approach: a PLAIN CLOJURE DATA STRUCTURE (vector of maps) IS THE COMMAND SPEC.  No macros, no records, no DSL.  Pure data.  Documents the **plain-data-as-CLI-spec pattern** — sister to (but distinct from) R295 CL clingon's `make-command` constructor (which returns a CLOS instance, not raw plist).  Closest analog: Go cobra's `&cobra.Command{...}` struct literal — but Clojure goes further because the vector of command maps can be COMPUTED at runtime by ordinary `(map ...)` / `(filter ...)` / `(remove nil?)` calls (see line 60 `remove nil?` for the conditional `upgrade` command).  Corpus's **first documented compute-the-spec-with-data-functions CLI pattern**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY-ONE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), Nim 2 cligen (R301), **Clojure babashka.cli** (R302 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 41 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **qualified-keyword flag names** like `--git/sha`, `--mvn/version`, `--local/root`, `--http/url` at README.md "Supported Options" — Clojure's QUALIFIED KEYWORDS (`:git/sha` is a keyword with namespace `git` and name `sha`) round-trip DIRECTLY into the CLI: `--git/sha VALUE` parses to `{:git/sha "VALUE"}` in the opts map.  The SLASH in the flag is the Clojure-keyword namespace separator.  Distinct from EVERY OTHER CLI LIBRARY in the corpus — no other language's CLI library accepts SLASHES in long-flag names.  Documents the **qualified-keyword-as-flag pattern** — Clojure-unique.  Trade-off: SLASHES in flag names confuse shells that interpret them as path separators in path-expansion contexts, but babashka.cli's parser reads `--git/sha` as a single token before the shell would care.  Corpus's **first documented slash-in-long-flag-name CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **nested subcommand vectors** at cli.clj:35 — `:cmds ["migrate" "auto"]` is a TWO-ELEMENT subcommand path: `bbin migrate auto` matches this command, while `bbin migrate` (without `auto`) matches the SEPARATE entry at cli.clj:40 with `:cmds ["migrate"]`.  babashka.cli's `dispatch` performs LONGEST-PREFIX matching: when the argv starts with `migrate auto`, the two-element entry wins over the one-element entry.  Distinct from R269 Crystal OptionParser (no subcommands), R298 Pony (single-level subcommand only), R296 Julia Comonicon (`@cast` macro generates `subcmd subsubcmd` from nested namespaces).  Closest analog: cobra's `Command.AddCommand(...)` tree.  babashka.cli's data-vector approach is FLATTER: nested subcommands live as longer `:cmds` vectors in the SAME flat list, rather than as a tree of Command objects.  Corpus's **first documented flat-list-with-longest-prefix-match nested subcommand pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **`:args->opts [:script/lib]` positional-to-option BINDING** at cli.clj:31 — `:args->opts [:script/lib]` declares that the FIRST POSITIONAL argv (after the subcommand) is bound to the option keyword `:script/lib` in the parsed opts map.  So `bbin install io.github.babashka/neil` parses to `{:script/lib "io.github.babashka/neil"}`.  Distinct from R287 Nim parseopt (positional accumulates in a separate seq), R298 Pony (positional declared in `positional` block of command_spec), R296 Julia Comonicon (`function fn(positional, ...)` arg-list), and R301 Nim cligen (`colNums: seq[int]` at end of proc-param list).  babashka.cli is UNIQUE: positionals are explicitly NAMED via `:args->opts` so they live in the SAME opts map as the `--flag` options.  No `args` field separate from `opts`.  Documents the **positional-as-named-option pattern** — corpus's **first documented unified-positionals-and-flags map**.
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **post-spec-decoration via functional composition** at cli.clj:18-22 — `add-global-aliases` is a function that MAPS `(assoc-in % [:aliases :h] :help)` over the command vector, INJECTING the global `-h` → `--help` alias into every command's `:aliases` map.  This is FUNCTIONAL COMPOSITION over a DATA SPEC — distinct from declarative-only specs (R296 Julia Comonicon's `@main` macro defines globals at the macro-expansion site; R298 Pony's `command_spec` has a fixed `options` list).  babashka.cli's spec is JUST DATA, so it can be TRANSFORMED by any Clojure data function before being passed to `dispatch`.  Corpus's **first documented post-spec-decoration-via-map pattern** — illustrates that PURE-DATA SPECS unlock COMPOSITION techniques that CLOSED-OVER spec macros cannot offer.
  - Critical extraction note: bbin uses **`*file*` ↔ `babashka.file` main-guard for dual-library-and-binary** at cli.clj:88-90 — `(when (= *file* (System/getProperty "babashka.file")) (util/check-min-bb-version) (apply -main *command-line-args*))`.  Clojure's `*file*` is the CURRENTLY-LOADED FILE path (dynamic var); Babashka's `babashka.file` system-property is the FILE PASSED ON THE BB COMMAND LINE.  When they're equal, the namespace is being run AS THE MAIN ENTRY POINT (not just loaded as a library).  Babashka equivalent of Python's `if __name__ == "__main__":` and R301 Nim's `when isMainModule:`.  Distinct from R301 Nim cligen (`when isMainModule:` is COMPILE-TIME), R296 Julia Comonicon (`@main` macro at file bottom), and R295 CL clingon (`main()` defun called from REPL or `sbcl --script`).  Babashka Clojure uses a RUNTIME EQUALITY CHECK because `bb` interprets scripts at script-load time, not at compile time.  Corpus's **first documented Babashka `*file*` guard pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_bb` (Babashka itself, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_neil` (Babashka tool, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_clojure-lsp` (already in corpus), `_cljfmt` (already in corpus), `_clj-kondo` (already in corpus + blacklisted), `_joker` / `_planck` / `_lumo` / `_nbb` / `_shadow-cljs` / `_fennel` / `_janet` / `_hy` (all already in corpus).  Probed `polylith` (Clojure modular workspace, 601 stars, uses `clojure.tools.cli` — different library; deferred for separate round), `kaocha` (test runner, uses `clojure.tools.cli`), `eastwood` (linter, complex CLI), `clj-new` (deps-style tool, no standalone CLI), `deps-new` (same), `cli-matic` (the LIBRARY itself, not a CLI tool).  Chose bbin: AUTHORED BY BABASHKA ORG (rads), 9 subcommands with rich `:cmds`/`:fn`/`:args->opts`/`:aliases` surface, 10 install options including Clojure-unique slash-keyword flags `--git/sha` / `--mvn/version` / `--local/root`, real Homebrew package `babashka/brew/bbin`, stable 0.2.x version, used by the broader Babashka script ecosystem.
  - Blacklist update: `bbin` added.  Corpus count: 28,724 → 28,725 (+1).

- **R301 — adorn source-direct (c-blake/bu column-aware text filter in Nim; Nim `cligen` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from V stdlib `flag` (R300 hntop) into the **Nim `cligen` library cluster** — corpus's first documented Nim cligen CLI, second Nim CLI library after R287's stdlib `parseopt`.  adorn is a column-aware text filter that adds user-supplied prefixes and suffixes to selected columns of a delimited input stream, preserving ambient text — part of c-blake/bu, a 77-star collection of "Basic But-For Utility" Nim programs (catz, cfold, cstats, dups, fage, etc.) by Charles Blake (also the author of `cligen` itself).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: expands the Nim slot in the cross-language CLI taxonomy from 1 to 2 variants — corpus now documents BOTH stdlib `parseopt` (R287 nimpretty) and the de-facto standard third-party `cligen`.  Total library taxonomy: 40 variants.
  - `_adorn` (1-stem; c-blake/bu adorn.nim; **Nim cligen library**; **9 params + cligen `dispatch` compile-time macro + proc-signature-as-CLI-spec + `seq[int]` positional varargs + Nim char literal `'\n'` for default row delimiter + explicit `short={"output":'o'}` override table + `help={...}` per-param help table + `when isMainModule:` dual-library-and-binary guard + case-preserved CLI flag naming (`--O0`/`--rowDlm` verbatim from proc params)**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `adorn.nim` line 2 (`import cligen, cligen/[sysUt, mfile, mslice, osUt], std/parseutils` — Nim third-party `cligen` import + bracket-list submodule import + stdlib `parseutils`)
    * `adorn.nim` lines 10-11 (proc `adorn(colNums: seq[int], origin=1, O0=false, prefix=ess, suffix=ess, input="", rowDlm='\n', delim="w", output="")` — proc SIGNATURE defines the CLI surface: `seq[int]` = positional varargs, scalar defaults map to `--long` flags, char default `'\n'` is Nim char literal)
    * `adorn.nim` lines 12-15 (`## ... \`input\`-\`output\` filter to adorn fields ...` — Nim `##` DOC-COMMENT block lifted by cligen into the `--help` banner)
    * `adorn.nim` line 45 (`when isMainModule: dispatch adorn, help={...}, short={"output":'o'}` — cligen `dispatch` MACRO walks the proc signature at COMPILE TIME and generates the argv parser)
    * `adorn.nim` lines 46-53 (`help={"colNums": "...", "origin": "...", "O0": "shorthand for --origin=0", "prefix": "...", "suffix": "...", "input": "...", "rowDlm": "...", "delim": "...", "output": "..."}` — nine help-text entries, one per proc param)
    * `adorn.nim` line 54 (`short={"output":'o'}` — explicit short-flag override table: `--output` gets `-o`; all other shorts AUTO-DERIVED by cligen from first letter, with collisions suppressed)
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **Nim `cligen` third-party library** at adorn.nim:2 — c-blake/cligen is the **most popular Nim CLI library** by Charles Blake (also author of `bu`).  Distinct from Nim stdlib `parseopt` (R287 nimpretty) which is a TOKEN ITERATOR: caller drives a `getopt` loop and manually dispatches.  cligen is DECLARATIVE-VIA-MACRO: caller writes a NORMAL NIM PROC, then `dispatch procName` at compile time generates the argv parser + calls the proc.  Documents the **proc-signature-as-CLI-spec via compile-time macro pattern** — sister to R296 Julia Comonicon's function-signature-as-spec and R266 Python Click's decorator-on-function, but DIFFERENT from R287 Nim parseopt's iterator-driven loop.  cligen is to Nim what Click is to Python, Comonicon is to Julia, and Typer is to Python: SIGNATURE-AS-SPEC frameworks that eliminate spec/code duplication.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to FORTY library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 OptionParser (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim 1 parseopt (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2 Admiral (R299), V stdlib `flag` (R300), **Nim 2 cligen** (R301 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 40 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **cligen `dispatch` compile-time macro** at adorn.nim:45 — `dispatch adorn, help={...}, short={...}` is a Nim MACRO INVOCATION that walks the `adorn` proc signature AT COMPILE TIME, generates an argv parser, and emits a call to `adorn(...)` with parsed values.  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon's `@cast`/`@main` macros (also compile-time, but Julia-flavored macros expand to typed function bodies) and R290 Racket's `command-line` macro (s-expression spec → parser at expansion).  Nim macros run during compilation, have full AST access to the proc, and emit Nim code that's then type-checked.  Documents the **compile-time-macro-walks-proc-signature CLI pattern** — the cligen approach treats the proc itself as the spec; no DSL, no decorators (Nim has no decorators), no separate config blob.  Corpus's **first documented compile-time-macro-walks-proc-signature CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **explicit short-flag override table** `short={"output":'o'}` at adorn.nim:54 — cligen AUTO-DERIVES short flags from the first letter of each long name (so `--origin` would get `-o`, `--output` would also try `-o`, etc.) and resolves collisions by SUPPRESSING the conflicting short.  The `short` table EXPLICITLY assigns or overrides — here `--output` is given `-o`, displacing any auto-derived `-o` from `--origin`.  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon (docstring-driven short discovery via `-X, --long <arg>` lines), R298 Pony `OptionSpec.string("name", "...", 'p', "default")` (short is a positional arg of the option-spec call), and R295 CL clingon `:short-name #\A` (kwarg).  cligen's approach: shorts default to auto-derivation, but a `short` table lets you ENFORCE specific shorts.  Value is a Nim char literal `'o'` — single-quoted as a single-byte character.  Corpus's **first documented short-override-table CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **CAPITAL-CASE-PRESERVED Nim param `O0`** at adorn.nim:10 — `O0=false` is a Nim proc parameter that becomes the CLI flag `--O0` LITERALLY (cligen does NOT lowercase or kebab-case it).  Per the inline help at line 48: `"O0": "shorthand for --origin=0"`.  Documents the **case-preserved CLI flag naming pattern** — distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon's snake_case→kebab-case auto-conversion (`output_dir` → `--output-dir`).  Nim's CamelCase and capitalized identifiers are PRESERVED VERBATIM by cligen — `O0` stays `O0`, `rowDlm` stays `rowDlm`.  Trades typing convenience (kebab-case is faster to type) for ROUND-TRIP IDENTITY (the flag name matches the proc-param name exactly).  Corpus's **first documented identifier-preserved CLI naming**.
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **`when isMainModule:` guard** at adorn.nim:45 — Nim's `when isMainModule:` is a COMPILE-TIME CONDITIONAL that includes the block only when the module is the main entry point (analog to Python's `if __name__ == "__main__":`).  This means `adorn.nim` can be IMPORTED as a library (the `proc adorn` is exported) without triggering the CLI dispatch — only when COMPILED as a binary does the `dispatch adorn` macro generate the entry point.  Documents the **dual-library-and-binary pattern** — distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon (separate `install()` function), R298 Pony `primitive CLI` (no dual usage), and R297 Prolog `main() :- ...` (separate predicate).  Nim's `when isMainModule:` is COMPILE-TIME — the library-vs-binary decision is made before the binary is built.  Corpus's **first documented dual-library-and-binary CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: adorn uses **`seq[int]` positional varargs** at adorn.nim:10 — `colNums: seq[int]` is a Nim sequence-of-int proc parameter that cligen interprets as the POSITIONAL VARARG slot: every non-flag argv token after option parsing accumulates into this seq.  Distinct from R287 Nim parseopt (manual loop, caller appends to a seq), R298 Pony (`positional` arg-spec list inside `command_spec`), and R296 Julia Comonicon (`function fn(args::Vector{String})` arg list).  cligen recognizes `seq[T]` at the END of the proc-param list as varargs by convention.  Corpus's **first documented seq-as-positional-varargs CLI pattern in Nim**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_nimpretty` (R287 nimpretty stdlib parseopt is the FIRST Nim CLI in corpus; this round is the SECOND Nim slot).  Probed `nimterop/nimterop loaf.nim` (cligen `dispatchMulti` with single `find` subcommand — would be `loaf find ...` but smaller surface than adorn; deferred), `c-blake/cligen util/complgen.nim` (cligen's own completion generator — meta-tool; complex), `godot-nim/gdext-nim coronation/src/coronation.nim` (Godot C++/Nim binding generator), `genotrance/feud feud.nim` (game/scripting tool), `pysan3/minorg minorg.nim` (small adoption), `jhbadger/nimbioseq examples/checkFastq.nim` (bioinformatics example), `jakobfriedl/conquest src/client/main.nim` (CTF tool), various nibSV/scinim/yourutils probes.  Chose adorn: AUTHORED BY CLIGEN AUTHOR (Charles Blake), 9-param proc with canonical cligen pattern surface (positional varargs via seq[int], multiple typed defaults, explicit `short={}` override, `help={}` table), real binary published in c-blake/bu nimble package (bu.nimble bin=@[..., "adorn", ...]), stable 0.18.13 version.
  - Blacklist update: `adorn` added.  Corpus count: 28,723 → 28,724 (+1).

- **R300 — hntop source-direct (V Hacker News top-stories reader; V stdlib `flag` module)** (1 file new) — **MILESTONE R300, THE THREE-HUNDREDTH HARVEST ROUND**.  Pivots from Crystal Admiral (R299 hetzner-k3s) into the **V (vlang) language tooling cluster** — corpus's first documented V CLI.  hntop is a terminal Hacker News top-stories reader in V — fetches top stories from the HN Firebase API, prints with title/score/comments/user/optional source URL.  Sister tools: vlang/v compiler (already in corpus as `_v`), vlang/coreutils (V port of GNU coreutils — many would dup with stock OS coreutils).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented V CLI** — adds V stdlib `flag` module to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (39 library variants now).
  - `_hntop` (multi-stem: `hntop` + `hn_top`; BafS/hn-top hn.v; **V stdlib `flag` module**; **2 flags + V factory + cascade-style config via successive statements on mut binding + skip_executable + typed `.int`/`.bool`/`.string` methods + V backtick `'X'` char literal + `:=` direct-return-from-spec-call binding + finalize() with Result-or-block error handling**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `hn.v` line 4 (`import flag` — V stdlib package import)
    * `hn.v` line 40 (`mut fp := flag.new_flag_parser(os.args)` — V FACTORY + mut binding)
    * `hn.v` lines 41-44 (`fp.application('hn_top')` / `fp.version('v0.1.0')` / `fp.description('Show top HN news')` / `fp.skip_executable()` — V cascade-via-successive-statements config)
    * `hn.v` line 45 (`top_num := fp.int('num', \`n\`, 5, 'number of top news to show')` — V `fp.int(LONG, SHORT, DEFAULT, HELP)` method with backtick char literal `\`n\``)
    * `hn.v` line 46 (`source_urls := fp.bool('source_urls', \`u\`, false, 'show source urls')` — `fp.bool` for boolean flag)
    * `hn.v` lines 48-52 (`fp.finalize() or { eprintln(err); println(fp.usage()); return }` — V `finalize()` returns `![]string` Result; `or { ... }` is V's UNIQUE Result-fallback block)
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V stdlib `flag` module** at hn.v:4 — V's stdlib has a CANONICAL flag-parsing module (`vlib/flag/flag.v`).  Distinct from V's SEPARATE `cli` module (`vlib/cli/cli.v`) which provides a clap-style command tree.  `flag` is the LIGHTWEIGHT, IMPERATIVE option (favored for small scripts); `cli` is the DECLARATIVE, subcommand-friendly option (favored for larger apps with subcommands).  Documents the **stdlib-flag-vs-stdlib-cli distinction** — V is one of the few languages with TWO stdlib CLI modules at different abstraction levels.  Sister to Pony's single stdlib `cli` (R298 lutra).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-NINE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 (R269 OptionParser), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), Crystal 2: Admiral (R299), **V stdlib `flag`** (R300 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 39 cross-language CLI library variants documented at the R300 round mark.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V backtick character literal `\`n\`` for short flags** at hn.v:45-46 — V uses BACKTICKS for character literals (e.g. `\`n\`` is the byte value 110 = ASCII 'n').  Single quotes are V's STRING literals; double quotes are also strings.  Sister to R291 CL `#\h`, R292 Erlang `$h`, R295 CL clingon `#\A`, R298 Pony `'p'`.  **FIVE character-literal CLI cluster languages now**: CL `#\X`, Erlang `$X`, Pony `'X'`, clingon (CL) `#\X`, V `\`X\``.  V is UNIQUE in using BACKTICKS — every other char-literal language uses some form of `#\` (Lisp family) or `$` (Erlang) or single quote (Pony).  Corpus's **fifth character-literal CLI flag pattern (V's backtick variant)**.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V `mut` binding for mutable parser handle** at hn.v:40 — `mut fp := flag.new_flag_parser(os.args)` — `mut` declares the binding as MUTABLE; without `mut`, V's IMMUTABILITY-BY-DEFAULT would reject the subsequent `fp.application(...)` calls because those methods take `mut` receivers.  Distinct from R290 Racket (immutable command spec built via macro), R298 Pony (handled by `?` partial annotation), and R296 Julia (function signature is the spec — no binding needed).  Similar to Rust's `let mut` but uses keyword `mut` BEFORE the binding instead of `let mut`.  Corpus's **first documented mutability-keyword-required CLI parser pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V Result-type `or { ... }` fallback for parse errors** at hn.v:48-52 — `fp.finalize() or { eprintln(err); println(fp.usage()); return }`.  V's `finalize()` returns `![]string` (Result-typed string array): either the leftover positional args OR an error.  `or { ... }` is V's UNIQUE Result-fallback block — runs ONLY when the Result is an error; inside the block, `err` is implicitly bound to the IError value.  Distinct from R298 Pony `try ... end` (exception-style), R293 Tcl `try { ... } trap` (error-code list), R296 Julia exception-based, and R295 CL clingon Common Lisp condition system.  V's `or { }` is a HYBRID: type-checked Result + EARLY-RETURN block.  No throw-catch unwinding required.  Corpus's **first documented V Result-or-block CLI error handling**.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V `:=` declare-and-bind for parsed values** at hn.v:45/46 — `top_num := fp.int(...)` and `source_urls := fp.bool(...)`.  V's `:=` is the SHORT VARIABLE DECLARATION operator (sister to Go's `:=`).  The parsed value is RETURNED DIRECTLY from the `int`/`bool`/`string` method calls — NOT stored on the parser as a field to retrieve later.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `(cli:getopt opts :key)` (parser holds, lookup by key), R294 Pharo `flags.<name>` accessor (parser holds), and R297 Prolog `parse_opt(OptName(Value))` (term arrives in handler).  V's approach is the MOST DIRECT: declare the variable AT the option-spec call site.  V's static typing means each call's return type matches the method (`fp.int` returns int, `fp.bool` returns bool, `fp.string` returns string).  Corpus's **first documented direct-return-from-spec-call CLI parser**.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **V cascade-style parser config WITHOUT separator** at hn.v:41-44 — four consecutive method calls on `fp`.  Each is a separate V statement (no separator; V uses newline-as-statement-terminator like Python and Go).  Distinct from R293 Tcl semicolon-separated, R294 Pharo cascade `;` (semicolon-separated messages to SAME receiver), and R298 Pony `.>` chained-mutation.  V's "cascade" is really just SUCCESSIVE STATEMENTS on a mut binding — no special syntax.  Documents the **statement-sequence parser configuration pattern** — implicit cascading via IMMUTABILITY-BY-DEFAULT + mut bindings.
  - Critical extraction note: hntop uses **separate-setter-for-help-metadata** at hn.v:41-43 — `fp.application` + `fp.version` + `fp.description` are SEPARATE setter calls (not constructor args).  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `make-command :name "..."` (constructor kwarg), R294 Pharo `(ClapCommandSpec id: #pillar) description: '...'` (cascade), and R296 Julia `@main` (docstring-driven).  V's approach is the MOST FRAGMENTED: each metadata field is its own method call.  Trade-off: more verbose at the call site, but lower-arity methods.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_v` (V compiler, already in corpus + blacklisted), vlang/coreutils ports (would dup with stock OS coreutils — mktemp, cat, ls, etc. already have system completions).  Probed `cjyeats73/cos350_projects vtest-parser.v` (project-internal tool), `phoenixr-codes/im2ansi` (smaller adoption), `greenian/ian-vfetch` (author-namespaced), `qtc-de/rpv` (Windows-focused), `medvednikov/v2_old` (abandoned), `fdigeron/xertools sqlrunner.v` (no obvious binary).  Chose hntop: clean focused V `flag` spec in one self-contained file with EVERY major V flag-module pattern represented, real GitHub project (24 stars, niche but stand-alone), README explicitly documents the binary name as `hntop`.  Multi-stem completion (`hntop` + `hn_top`) covers both the compiled-binary name AND the self-declared application name from `fp.application('hn_top')`.
  - Blacklist update: `hntop` + `hn_top` added.  Corpus count: 28,722 → 28,723 (+1).

- **R299 — hetzner-k3s source-direct (Hetzner Cloud k3s cluster manager in Crystal; Admiral library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Pony stdlib `cli` (R298 lutra) into the **Crystal Admiral library cluster** — corpus's first documented Crystal Admiral CLI, second Crystal CLI library after R269's stdlib `OptionParser`.  hetzner-k3s is the easiest way to create production-ready Kubernetes clusters on Hetzner Cloud (3,574 GH stars).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: expands the Crystal slot in the cross-language CLI taxonomy from 1 to 2 variants — corpus now documents BOTH stdlib `OptionParser` (R269) and popular third-party `Admiral` Crystal CLI libraries.  Total library taxonomy: 38 variants.
  - `_hetzner-k3s` (1-stem; vitobotta/hetzner-k3s src/hetzner-k3s.cr; **Crystal Admiral library**; **5 subcommands (create/delete/upgrade/releases/run) + inheritance-plus-macro CLI + nested-inner-class subcommand classes + `define_flag` typed-declaration macro + `register_sub_command` explicit-class-reference routing + runtime mutex on --command/--script in RunCmd + leading-dashes-in-long-name upstream quirk preserved verbatim + bottom-of-file `CLI.run` static dispatch**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` line 1 (`require "admiral"` — Crystal Admiral third-party shard import)

    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 36-69 (Create class: --config -c required, --quiet -q, --skip-current-ip-validation)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 71-99 (Delete class: --config -c required, --force, --quiet -q)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 101-130 (Upgrade class: --config -c required, --new-k3s-version required, --force, --quiet -q)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` line 108 (`long: "--new-k3s-version"` — UPSTREAM QUIRK preserved verbatim: leading double-dashes inconsistent with `long: "config"` at line 42)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 132-144 (Releases class: no flags)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 146-207 (RunCmd class: --config -c required, --command, --script, --instance + RUNTIME XOR validation at lines 180-188)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` lines 209-217 (define_version VERSION; define_help description; FIVE register_sub_command calls)
    * `src/hetzner-k3s.cr` line 232 (`Hetzner::K3s::CLI.run` bottom-of-file dispatch)
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **Crystal `Admiral` library** (jwaldrip/admiral.cr) — the **most popular third-party Crystal CLI library**.  Sister tools also using Admiral: mint-lang/mint compiler, psykube, snipline/snipcli, jbox-web/stacker, djmaze/swm, refi64/rcmake, marghidanu/decompose.  Distinct from Crystal stdlib `OptionParser` (R269) which uses block-builder DSL.  Admiral uses CRYSTAL INHERITANCE + MACROS: `class MyCLI < Admiral::Command` with `define_flag` and `register_sub_command` macros.  Documents the **inheritance-plus-macro CLI pattern** — distinct from R269 OptionParser block-builder, R285 Lua argparse builder, R295 CL clingon `make-option`, R296 Julia `@main`-on-function (no class).  Similar to Java picocli's `@Command(subcommands = {...})` (R276) but substitutes ANNOTATION-CLASS DISCOVERY with CLASS-INHERITANCE + MACRO EXPANSION.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-EIGHT library variants — Crystal slot now has TWO entries: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal 1 (R269 OptionParser), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), Pony stdlib cli (R298), **Crystal 2: Admiral** (R299 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 38 cross-language CLI library variants documented; Crystal slot has 2 distinct library implementations.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **NESTED INNER CLASSES for subcommand classes** at hetzner-k3s.cr:36/71/101/132/146 — each subcommand (Create, Delete, Upgrade, Releases, RunCmd) is defined as a NESTED INNER CLASS inside `Hetzner::K3s::CLI`.  Subcommand classes inherit from `Admiral::Command` (NOT from the outer CLI class).  Distinct from R294 Pharo pillar (separate top-level classes per subcommand) and R290 Racket `#:subcommands` (data list).  Crystal's nested classes share namespace with the outer class but inherit independently.  Corpus's **first documented nested-inner-class subcommand CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **`define_flag NAME : TYPE, description:, long:, short:, required:, default:` macro** — `define_flag` is Admiral's CRYSTAL MACRO that EXPANDS at compile time to generate a typed flag-accessor method.  Crystal macro syntax `NAME : TYPE` IS THE FLAG DECLARATION; the macro generates `flags.<name>` getter at compile time.  Distinct from R294 Pharo cascade `addFlag:` (runtime), R295 CL clingon `:long-name "..."` (keyword arg), R296 Julia `tune::Bool=false` (function signature).  Crystal Admiral's macro parses the TYPED DECLARATION and generates appropriate parsing code for the type at compile time.  `required: true` keyword arg emits a runtime check that fails-fast if missing.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **`register_sub_command NAME : CLASS, description: "..."` macro for subcommand routing** at hetzner-k3s.cr:213-217 — five calls wire each subcommand class to a subcommand NAME.  Distinct from R294 Pharo `pillarCommandsList do: [ ... addSubcommand: each ]` (auto-discovery via `allSubclasses`), R292 Erlang pattern-match dispatch `main(["sub" | Argv])`, and R296 Julia `@cast` (Comonicon cast macro).  Crystal Admiral's approach is EXPLICIT REGISTRATION — the developer lists each subcommand by class reference.  Corpus's **first documented explicit-class-reference subcommand registration**.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **leading-double-dashes-in-long-name upstream quirk** at hetzner-k3s.cr:108 — `long: "--new-k3s-version"` has a LEADING `--` while OTHER `long:` specs in the same file use bare names (e.g. `long: "config"` at line 42, `long: "quiet"` at line 49).  Admiral STRIPS leading dashes internally so both forms work, but the inconsistency is preserved verbatim in upstream source — likely a developer-side copy-paste mistake.  Documents the **source-direct preservation of upstream inconsistency** — important reminder that source-direct extraction MUST faithfully reflect the spec as written, INCLUDING quirks.  Corpus's **first documented Admiral leading-dashes quirk**.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **runtime mutually-exclusive flag validation** in RunCmd at hetzner-k3s.cr:180-188 — `if command_provided && script_provided` / `unless command_provided || script_provided` enforces XOR semantics on `--command` and `--script` (exactly ONE must be provided).  Joins the runtime cross-flag validation cluster: R252 saya (clap builder manual mutex), R291 Lem CL `when (and tcp stdio)`, R292 emqtt Erlang `{undefined, undefined}` case (mutually-required), R298 lutra Pony (no validation, all optional).  FIVE rounds now showing this convergent pattern across SIX languages (Rust, CL, Erlang, Crystal, Lua) where the upstream lib lacks declarative mutex support.
  - Critical extraction note: hetzner-k3s uses **bottom-of-file `CLI.run` static dispatch** at hetzner-k3s.cr:232 — `Hetzner::K3s::CLI.run` kicks off the whole CLI dispatch.  Admiral's `Command.run` is a CLASS METHOD that: (1) parses ARGV against the registered subcommand tree, (2) selects the matching subcommand class, (3) instantiates that class, (4) calls the instance's `run` method.  Distinct from R297 Prolog `main() :- ...` predicate and R296 Julia `@main` decorator.  Crystal Admiral's `.run` is a STATIC ENTRY POINT that handles dispatch — no separate `main`.  Corpus's **first documented static-entry-point CLI dispatcher**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_mint` (already in corpus + blacklisted; refers to UNRELATED Swift Mint package manager at yonaskolb/Mint — name collision with mint-lang/mint Crystal compiler), `_decompose` (already in corpus + blacklisted; marghidanu/decompose docker-compose helper).  Probed `psykube/psykube`, `caravel-tool/caravel`, `snipline/snipcli`, `jbox-web/stacker`, `djmaze/swm`, `creadone/dl`, `refi64/rcmake`.  Chose hetzner-k3s: 3,574-star real production tool, complete Admiral spec in one self-contained file with FIVE subcommands representing distinct Admiral patterns (define_flag/define_help/define_version/register_sub_command), runtime mutex validation in RunCmd, AND an upstream inconsistency at line 108 worth documenting source-direct.
  - Blacklist update: `hetzner-k3s` added.  Corpus count: 28,721 → 28,722 (+1).

- **R298 — lutra source-direct (minimal SSH manager in Pony; Pony stdlib `cli` package)** (1 file new) — pivots from SWI-Prolog `library(optparse)` (R297 plwm) into the **Pony language tooling cluster** — corpus's first documented Pony CLI.  lutra is a minimal Secure-Shell manager in Pony — manages SSH hosts (nodes) with optional username, port, and key per host.  Sister tools: corral (Pony package manager, ALREADY in corpus from unrelated Microsoft Boogie lineage), ponyc (Pony compiler, ALREADY in corpus as `_pony`).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Pony CLI** — adds Pony stdlib `cli` package to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (37 library variants now).
  - `_lutra` (1-stem; code-monad/lutra lutra/cli.pony; **Pony stdlib `cli` package**; **9 options + 2 positional args + `add_help` virtual subcommand + Pony `?` partial annotation + `.>` chained-mutation operator + union-type parse return + match on Command|CommandHelp|SyntaxError + type-as-factory-method `OptionSpec.string`/`.bool` + Pony `'X'` character literals + `primitive CLI` as namespace**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `lutra/cli.pony` line 1 (`use "cli"` — Pony stdlib package import)
    * `lutra/cli.pony` lines 3-19 (`primitive CLI fun parse(args, envs): (Command | (U8, String)) => try match CommandParser(_spec()?).parse(args, envs) | let c: Command => c | let h: CommandHelp => (0, h.help_string()) | let e: SyntaxError => (1, e.string()) end ...` — typed-union return + three-arm match)
    * `lutra/cli.pony` lines 23-52 (`CommandSpec.leaf("lutra", "A minimal Secure-Shell manager", [ OptionSpec.string("port", "...", 'p', "22") ... ], [ ArgSpec.string("node", "...", "") ... ])?.>add_help("help", "Get this page.")?` — spec builder with chained mutation)
    * Options: -p/--port (string default "22"), -i/--identify, -k/--key, -l/--list, -a/--add, -d/--delete, -u/--update, -f/--apply, -s/--default
    * Positional args: node, dest
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **Pony stdlib `cli` package** (no third-party dependency) — Pony, unlike Common Lisp (R291/R295) and Erlang (R292), has a CANONICAL STDLIB CLI package shipped with every ponyc installation (lives at `packages/cli/` in the ponyc source tree).  Pony's `cli` package is a FIRST-CLASS LANGUAGE FEATURE: `CommandSpec`, `OptionSpec`, `ArgSpec`, `CommandParser`, `Command`, `CommandHelp`, `SyntaxError` are all exposed types.  Distinct from R296 Julia Comonicon (third-party), R297 Prolog `library(optparse)` (stdlib), R294 Pharo Clap (third-party port of Rust clap).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), SWI-Prolog optparse (R297), **Pony stdlib cli** (R298 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 37 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **Pony PARTIAL (`?`) annotation for error propagation + `.>` chained-mutation operator** at lutra/cli.pony:23/52 — `fun _spec(): CommandSpec ? => ...` declares the function as PARTIAL (can raise an error), and the spec-build expression ends with `)?` to propagate any error from `CommandSpec.leaf(...)`.  At line 52, `?.>add_help("help", "...")?` uses BOTH the partial-call annotation `?` AND the CHAINED-MUTATION operator `.>` (calls `add_help` on the result, returns the receiver).  UNIQUE to Pony.  Pony's `?` is CHECKED at compile time; the caller MUST handle the partial via `try ... end` or propagate further.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `make-option` (no error annotation), R296 Julia `@main` (runtime exceptions), and R294 Pharo cascade (no partial annotation).  Corpus's **first documented Pony-partial-with-chained-mutation CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **Pony UNION TYPE return + three-arm match** at lutra/cli.pony:5/12-15 — `parse(...) : (Command | (U8, String))` returns EITHER a parsed Command OR a tuple `(U8, String)` (exit code + error message).  Inside, the match expression has THREE arms: `Command` (success), `CommandHelp` (user requested help), `SyntaxError` (parse failure).  Distinct from R292 Erlang `getopt:parse` `{ok, {Opts, _Args}}` / `{error, {Reason, Data}}` (tagged-tuple result), R293 Tcl `cmdline::getoptions` `array set params` (mutating + try/trap), and R296 Julia (return is the function's return; errors are exceptions).  Pony's approach uses STATIC UNION TYPES + COMPILE-TIME EXHAUSTIVENESS CHECKING — the match must cover all union members or the compiler rejects.  Corpus's **first documented union-type CLI parse result with compile-checked exhaustive match**.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **Pony CHARACTER LITERAL `'p'` for short flags** at lutra/cli.pony:29/31/33/35/37/39/41/43/45 — `OptionSpec.string("port", "...", 'p', "22")` passes `'p'` as the SHORT flag.  In Pony, `'p'` is a CHARACTER LITERAL of type `U8` (8-bit unsigned integer = ASCII code).  Single quotes for character literals, double quotes for strings — clear lexical distinction.  Sister to R291 CL `#\p`, R292 Erlang `$p`, R295 CL clingon `#\A`.  FOUR character-literal CLI cluster languages now: CL `#\X`, Erlang `$X`, Pony `'X'`, clingon (CL) `#\X`.  Corpus's **fourth character-literal CLI flag pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **type-method-named factory: `OptionSpec.string`, `OptionSpec.bool`, `ArgSpec.string`** at lutra/cli.pony:28-49 — the TYPE NAME (`string`, `bool`) is the METHOD NAME on `OptionSpec` and `ArgSpec`.  Each method takes `(name, description, short, default)` for OptionSpec or `(name, description, default)` for ArgSpec.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `:string`/`:integer`/`:choice` keyword args, R292 Erlang `string`/`integer`/`boolean` atom tag in 5-tuple, and R294 Pharo `(ClapFlag id: #name)` class-name discriminator.  Pony's approach: the TYPE IS THE METHOD — `OptionSpec.string(...)` is a CONSTRUCTOR for string-typed option specs.  Compile-time type-safe.  Corpus's **first documented type-as-factory-method CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **PRIMITIVE CLI as namespace** at lutra/cli.pony:3 — `primitive CLI fun parse(...) ...` declares `CLI` as a PONY PRIMITIVE (a stateless, singleton type used as a namespace).  Plus the entry-point function lives ON the primitive (`CLI.parse(args, envs)` is the call shape).  Pony PRIMITIVES are similar to Scala objects or Java static classes but more constrained: they have NO FIELDS, NO CONSTRUCTOR, and are GLOBALLY UNIQUE.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `(def command ...)` (variable binding), R294 Pharo class-side method (class as namespace), and R296 Julia `module BenchPkg`.  Corpus's **first documented primitive-as-CLI-namespace pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lutra uses **`add_help` auto-virtual-subcommand** at lutra/cli.pony:52 — `)?.>add_help("help", "Get this page.")?` AUTOMATICALLY adds a virtual `help` subcommand to the spec.  When user types `lutra help`, the parser returns a `CommandHelp` (case at line 13), which the entry-point converts to exit 0 + help text output.  Convergent with R294 Pharo `addHelp` cascade message (also library-auto), R295 CL clingon (auto-generates --help) and R290 Racket `#:once-each [("-h" "--help") ...]` (manual).  Pony's `add_help` adds it as a SUBCOMMAND (`lutra help`), not as `--help` flag — distinct UX choice.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_corral` (already in corpus + blacklisted; refers to UNRELATED Microsoft boogie-org/corral — name collision with ponylang/corral), `_pony` (already in corpus + blacklisted; covers ponyc compiler).  Probed `ponylang/postgres examples/temporal-example.pony` (example, not real CLI), `ponylang/ponyc examples/commandline/main.pony` (example), `sblessing/pony-savina savina.pony` (benchmark suite, not single CLI), `mfelsche/pony-ast examples/compile/compile.pony` (example), `eohippus eohippus-lsp/_options.pony` (LSP server, no obvious binary), `credo` (seantallen-org/credo — but corpus already has `_credo` from unrelated Elixir credo).  Chose lutra: clean focused CLI spec in one self-contained file with EVERY major Pony cli-package pattern represented (CommandSpec.leaf, OptionSpec.string/bool, ArgSpec.string, '?' partial annotation, '.>' chained mutation, union return type, match on Command|CommandHelp|SyntaxError, character literals for short flags), real GitHub project.
  - Blacklist update: `lutra` added.  Corpus count: 28,720 → 28,721 (+1).

- **R297 — plwm source-direct (X11 window manager in Prolog; SWI-Prolog library(optparse))** (1 file new) — pivots from Julia Comonicon.jl (R296 benchpkg) into the **Prolog language tooling cluster** — corpus's first documented Prolog CLI.  plwm is an X11 window manager written entirely in Prolog (297 GH stars; AUR-packaged) using XLib via Prolog's foreign-function interface (`use_foreign_library(plx)`).  Sister tools: swipl (SWI-Prolog interpreter, already in corpus), gprolog, eclipse-clp, scryer-prolog.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Prolog CLI** — adds SWI-Prolog `library(optparse)` to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (36 library variants now).
  - `_plwm` (1-stem; Seeker04/plwm src/plwm.pl; **SWI-Prolog library(optparse)**; **5 flags + list-of-lists Prolog spec with compound-term inner elements + quoted-atom-for-uppercase short flag + multi-clause predicate dispatch + forall-member iteration + atom-typed CLI parsing + fact-as-spec-binding**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/plwm.pl` line 22 (`:- use_module(library(optparse)).` — SWI-Prolog stdlib import)
    * `src/plwm.pl` lines 2247-2259 (`opts_spec([ [opt(help), type(boolean), default(false), shortflags([h]), longflags([help]), help('...')], [opt(version), ...], [opt(log), type(atom), default(stdout), shortflags([l]), longflags([log]), ...], [opt(check), type(boolean), default(false), shortflags(['C']), longflags([check]), ...], [opt(config), type(atom), default(unset), shortflags([c]), longflags([config]), ...] ]).` — Prolog FACT defining opts_spec/1; spec is LIST OF LISTS of compound terms)
    * `src/plwm.pl` lines 2261-2278 (`parse_opt/1` — five Prolog clauses dispatching on `OptName(Value)` compound-term shape: `parse_opt(help(true)) :- ...`, `parse_opt(version(true)) :- ...`, etc.)
    * `src/plwm.pl` lines 2283-2313 (`main() :- ..., opts_spec(OptsSpec), opt_arguments(OptsSpec, Opts, _), forall(member(Opt, Opts), parse_opt(Opt)), ...` — entry-point call + forall+member iteration)
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **SWI-Prolog `library(optparse)`** — the **SWI-Prolog STANDARD LIBRARY** CLI module shipped with every SWI-Prolog installation (lives at `library/optparse.pl` in swipl-devel).  Different from `library(main)` which provides the NEWER `opt_parse/4` API.  `library(optparse)` is the legacy but well-supported API: `opt_arguments(+OptsSpec, -Opts, -PositionalArgs)`.  Documents the **Prolog predicate-based CLI parser pattern** — distinct from R296 Julia function-signature-as-spec, R295 CL clingon `make-option` calls, and R294 Pharo cascade messages.  In Prolog, the SPEC IS A FACT (`opts_spec([...]).`) and the PARSE is a PREDICATE CALL (`opt_arguments(OptsSpec, Opts, _)`).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-SIX library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2 clingon (R295), Julia (R296), **SWI-Prolog library(optparse)** (R297 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 36 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **list-of-lists Prolog SPEC with compound-term inner elements** at plwm.pl:2253-2259 — the spec is a Prolog LIST `[ [...], [...], ... ]` where each inner list contains COMPOUND TERMS like `opt(help)`, `type(boolean)`, `default(false)`, `shortflags([h])`, `longflags([help])`, `help('...')`.  Each element is a PROLOG COMPOUND-TERM PREDICATE — `opt(X)` is a one-arg functor application, NOT a key-value pair.  `library(optparse)` extracts spec data by pattern-matching on these specific functor names.  Distinct from R292 Erlang's 5-tuple `{Name, Short, Long, ArgSpec, Help}` (POSITIONAL tuple) and R295 CL clingon `:long-name "..."` (KEYWORD ARG).  Prolog's approach is HOMOICONIC: the spec IS Prolog data that can be inspected, manipulated, and rebuilt at runtime via standard Prolog term operations.  Corpus's **first documented compound-term-spec-element CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **atom-vs-quoted-atom for short-flag character literal** at plwm.pl:2254/2255/2256/2257/2258 — short flags are `shortflags([h])` (lowercase atom, unquoted) but `shortflags(['C'])` at line 2257 (uppercase capital letter atom REQUIRES quotes because Prolog treats unquoted capital-leading tokens as VARIABLES, not atoms).  Documents the **Prolog quoted-atom-required-for-uppercase pattern** — UNIQUE to Prolog (no other language has this specific syntactic constraint).  In Prolog, `Variable` (capital first letter) is parsed as a logic variable; `'C'` (single-quoted) forces atom interpretation.  The `-C` short flag would FAIL to parse without quotes around the atom.  Corpus's **first documented Prolog quoting-rule CLI quirk**.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **predicate-multi-clause dispatch on parsed-option compound terms** at plwm.pl:2266-2278 — `parse_opt(help(true)) :- ...` is FIVE separate Prolog clauses (one per option) that match on the COMPOUND TERM SHAPE arriving from `opt_arguments/3`.  Each parsed option becomes `OptName(Value)` (e.g. `help(true)`, `log('/var/log/plwm.log')`, `check(false)`).  Distinct from R285 Lua argparse table-field access, R290 Racket `match-let-values`, R295 CL clingon `getopt opts :key`, and R296 Julia direct-kwarg access.  Prolog's pattern-matching is FIRST-CLASS — each clause is independently selected by the runtime's unification algorithm.  Corpus's **first documented multi-clause predicate dispatch CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **`forall/2 + member/2` for option iteration** at plwm.pl:2291 — `forall(member(Opt, Opts), parse_opt(Opt))` iterates over the parsed options list, calling `parse_opt/1` for each.  `forall/2` is Prolog's "for all elements, succeed if predicate succeeds" meta-predicate.  Distinct from R285 Lua's `for k, v in pairs(args) do` loop and from R296 Julia's direct kwarg access.  Prolog has no explicit iteration; iteration is achieved via meta-predicates that recursively backtrack.  Corpus's **first documented Prolog meta-predicate CLI iteration**.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **Prolog `type/1` discriminator on `boolean` and `atom`** at plwm.pl:2254-2258 — `type(boolean)` makes `--check` and `--help` parse as `true`/`false` Prolog atoms; `type(atom)` makes `--config` and `--log` parse as arbitrary atoms.  Other supported types include `integer`, `float`, `term`, and `compound` (per SWI-Prolog optparse docs).  Prolog's `boolean` parses to a Prolog atom `true` or `false` (NOT a Prolog logical truth value or a 0/1 integer — they're atoms used by Prolog convention).  Corpus's **first documented atom-typed CLI parser**.
  - Critical extraction note: plwm uses **Prolog FACT (`opts_spec(...)` with no body)** as the spec binding at plwm.pl:2253-2259 — `opts_spec([...]).` is a Prolog FACT (predicate with no body, just a `.` terminator).  Facts are STATIC ASSERTIONS in the Prolog database, queryable anytime via `opts_spec(X)` to retrieve the list.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `(def command ...)` (serapeum def), R294 Pharo class-side method, R291 CL `defparameter *command-line-spec*` (special var).  In Prolog, EVERYTHING IS A PREDICATE — facts are predicates with single unification clauses.  The fact `opts_spec([...])` is INDISTINGUISHABLE from a query result.  Corpus's **first documented Prolog-fact-as-CLI-spec**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_swipl` (SWI-Prolog interpreter, already in corpus + blacklisted), `_gprolog`/`_pforth`/`_forth` (already in corpus), `_iforth`/`_sforth`/`_atlast`/`_vfx` (Forth implementations probed but don't use any recognizable typed-CLI lib).  Probed `terminusdb` (graph DB CLI, 30+ subcommands + subsubcommands in 2898-line file — too big for one round; deferred to a future focused round), `xsd2json` (XSD-to-JSON converter, smaller but niche), pharos `ooprolog` (CMU SEI binary analysis, proprietary-adjacent), LogicalContracts `le_command` (Logical English controlled-natural-language compiler, very niche).  Chose plwm: focused 5-option spec in one self-contained file, uses the standard `library(optparse)` API, all major Prolog CLI patterns represented, and is a REAL X11 window manager binary (297 GH stars, in AUR).
  - Blacklist update: `plwm` added.  Corpus count: 28,719 → 28,720 (+1).

- **R296 — benchpkg source-direct (AirspeedVelocity.jl Julia package benchmarker; Comonicon.jl)** (1 file new) — pivots from Common Lisp clingon (R295 kiln-sprof) into the **Julia language tooling cluster** — corpus's first documented Julia CLI.  benchpkg is AirspeedVelocity.jl's primary benchmarking CLI — runs benchmarks of a Julia package across multiple git revisions and reports JSON results.  Sister tools: `benchpkgtable`, `benchpkgplot` — all three installed by `julia -e 'using AirspeedVelocity; AirspeedVelocity.install()'` to `~/.julia/bin`.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Julia CLI** — adds Julia Comonicon.jl (function-signature-as-CLI-spec model) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (35 library variants now).
  - `_benchpkg` (1-stem; MilesCranmer/AirspeedVelocity.jl src/BenchPkg.jl; **Julia Comonicon.jl library**; **1 positional + 12 kwargs + function-signature-as-spec + docstring-driven short flags + semicolon-delimited positional/kwarg split + Bool-as-flag-not-value + snake_case→kebab-case auto-conversion + type-annotation-drives-parse + `@main` compile-time macro**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/BenchPkg.jl` lines 1-5 (`module BenchPkg ... using Comonicon` — Comonicon import)
    * `src/BenchPkg.jl` lines 7-51 (Julia DOCSTRING above `@main`: `# Arguments / # Options / # Flags` sections drive short-flag discovery via patterns like `-r, --rev <arg>`)
    * `src/BenchPkg.jl` line 52 (`Comonicon.@main function benchpkg(...)` — `@main` macro decorator)
    * `src/BenchPkg.jl` lines 53-65 (function signature: `package_name::String=""; rev::String="{DEFAULT},dirty", output_dir::String=".", script::String="", exeflags::String="", add::String="", tune::Bool=false, url::String="", path::String="", bench_on::String="", filter::String="", nsamples_load_time::Int=5, dont_print::Bool=false` — 1 positional + 12 kwargs)
    * `src/BenchPkg.jl` lines 67-115 (body uses each kwarg directly without unpacking)
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **Julia `Comonicon.jl` library** (comonicon/Comonicon.jl) — the **modern Julia CLI framework** by Roger Luo.  Alternatives in the Julia ecosystem: ArgParse.jl (older, Python ArgParse port; data-as-spec), DocOpt.jl (docopt port), ArgMacros.jl.  Distinct from ArgParse.jl's `@add_arg_table! s begin ... end` (data-as-spec) — Comonicon uses the **JULIA FUNCTION SIGNATURE ITSELF as the CLI spec**: positional arguments are function positionals, options are keyword arguments, defaults are `=` syntax, types are Julia type annotations.  Documents the **function-signature-as-CLI-spec pattern** — distinct from R291 CL `command-line-arguments` plist spec, R292 Erlang 5-tuple spec, R294 Pharo Smalltalk cascade spec, and R295 CL clingon `make-option` calls.  Comonicon's signature-as-spec is similar to Python fire/typer but EXPLICIT about types via Julia's typed annotations.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-FIVE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), Common Lisp 2: clingon (R295), **Julia Comonicon.jl** (R296 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 35 cross-language CLI library variants documented; first function-signature-as-spec library.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **docstring-as-help-source** at BenchPkg.jl:7-51 — the Julia docstring ABOVE the `@main` function provides ALL CLI help text, AND its `# Arguments / # Options / # Flags` sections drive SHORT-NAME DISCOVERY (e.g. `-r, --rev <arg>` in the `# Options` section tells Comonicon that `rev` has short alias `-r`).  No separate annotation, no `@option(short = 'r')` attribute — the DOCSTRING IS THE SPEC.  Distinct from R291 CL `#\h` character literal annotation, R292 Erlang `$h` character literal, R294 Pharo `(ClapFlag id: #version)` symbol identifier, and R295 clingon `:short-name #\A` keyword arg.  Comonicon's approach makes the CLI surface AUTO-DOCUMENT itself.  Corpus's **first documented docstring-driven CLI spec**.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **Julia semicolon-separated positional/kwarg split** at BenchPkg.jl:52-66 — Julia syntax `function foo(a, b; c=1, d=2)` PUTS `a, b` as positionals and `c, d` as keyword arguments, separated by `;`.  Comonicon maps positionals to CLI positionals and kwargs to CLI options.  Distinct from Python `def foo(a, b=None, *, c=None)` (where `*` separates positional-or-kwarg from kwarg-only) and from R295 CL `(&key tcp stdio ...)` (where `&key` separates required from keyword).  Julia's semicolon syntax is structurally simpler.  Corpus's **first documented semicolon-delimited CLI spec**.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **Julia Bool-typed kwarg → CLI flag** at BenchPkg.jl:59/65 — `tune::Bool=false` becomes `--tune` (CLI flag, no value), and `dont_print::Bool=false` becomes `--dont-print`.  Comonicon SPECIAL-CASES `Bool`-typed kwargs as boolean flags rather than `--tune=true`/`--tune=false` value pairs.  Distinct from R291 CL `(:type Boolean :optional t)` (typed but value-required) and R292 Erlang `boolean` ArgSpec (also value-required: `--enable-ssl true`).  Corpus's **first documented Bool-as-flag-not-value CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **Julia snake_case → CLI kebab-case auto-conversion** at BenchPkg.jl:55/62/64/65 — `output_dir::String="."` becomes `--output-dir`, `bench_on::String=""` becomes `--bench-on`, `nsamples_load_time::Int=5` becomes `--nsamples-load-time`, `dont_print::Bool=false` becomes `--dont-print`.  Underscores in Julia kwarg names AUTO-TRANSLATE to dashes in CLI flag names — a Comonicon convention.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `:long-name "all-threads"` (explicit kebab-case string) and R291 CL `("help" #\h)` (explicit string).  Corpus's **first documented underscore→dash auto-conversion CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **type-annotation-drives-parse** at BenchPkg.jl:64 — `nsamples_load_time::Int=5` annotates the kwarg with Julia's `Int` type.  Comonicon uses this annotation to PARSE the CLI value: a string arriving from argv gets `parse(Int, ...)` applied before binding.  `String` kwargs bypass parsing.  Distinct from R295 CL clingon `:integer` kind keyword (CLOS class), R292 Erlang `{integer, 300}` ArgSpec tuple, and R294 Pharo `(self positional: #port) asInteger` (explicit at-read coerce).  Comonicon's approach is the MOST IMPLICIT: the type annotation IS the parse instruction.  Corpus's **first documented type-annotation-drives-parse CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: benchpkg uses **`@main` compile-time macro** at BenchPkg.jl:52 — at COMPILE TIME the macro parses the docstring for help text + short flags, walks the function signature for positionals/kwargs/types/defaults, generates a wrapper that parses argv against the spec, and adds an `install` entry point so `julia -e 'using BenchPkg; BenchPkg.install()'` writes a `~/.julia/bin/benchpkg` launcher script.  Distinct from R294 Pharo `<commandline>` pragma (runtime registration), R291 CL `defparameter *command-line-spec*` (runtime data), and R266 Python Click `@click.command()` decorator (runtime).  Corpus's **first documented compile-time CLI macro**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_weave` (already in corpus + blacklisted; conflicts with Weave.jl's `weave` binary).  Probed `Mads.jl src/madsjl.jl` (uses `julia -q --color=yes` shebang but is the Madsjl wrapper; ships as a script, not a typical CLI binary), `WebIO.jl publish.jl` (internal publish script not exposed as binary), TAPER's `correction_multi.jl` (ArgParse.jl-based but invoked as `julia correction_multi.jl ...` — not a standalone binary).  Chose benchpkg: complete Comonicon spec in one self-contained file with ALL major Comonicon patterns represented (positional + 12 kwargs + Bool flags + typed Int + String defaults + docstring-driven short flags + snake_case→kebab-case + `@main` macro), installed as a real binary at `~/.julia/bin/benchpkg`, and part of a 3-binary suite so the sister tools are also future harvest targets.
  - Blacklist update: `benchpkg` added.  Corpus count: 28,718 → 28,719 (+1).

- **R295 — kiln-sprof source-direct (Kiln Common Lisp profiling wrapper; dnaeon/clingon library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Pharo Smalltalk Clap (R294 pillar) to **clingon — second Common Lisp CLI library variant** (corpus already had R291 `command-line-arguments`).  kiln-sprof is Kiln's profiling wrapper — runs any other kiln command under SBCL's statistical profiler (sb-sprof), then dumps a flat or call-graph report.  Kiln is "Practical Scripting in Common Lisp" — a multicall-binary infrastructure (BusyBox-style: `kiln xyz` or `kiln-xyz` via symlink).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: expands the Common Lisp slot in the cross-language CLI taxonomy from 1 to 2 variants — corpus now documents BOTH major CL CLI libraries (`command-line-arguments` and `clingon`).  Total library taxonomy: 34 variants.
  - `_kiln-sprof` (1-stem; ruricolist/kiln scripts/sprof.lisp; **Common Lisp clingon library**; **9 options + 4 distinct clingon kinds (:flag/:choice/:integer/:string) + opaque-parsed-object pattern + `:local-nicknames` package alias + kind-keyword-as-first-positional + CLOS-class-backed :choice with item enumeration + host-variable-as-default + serapeum `def` command binding + upstream-limitation-noted-in-comment**) decoded source-direct from:
