    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 1-12 (`(defpackage :kiln/scripts/sprof ... (:local-nicknames (:cli :clingon)))` — Common Lisp `:local-nicknames` package-level alias binding `cli` → `clingon`)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 14-75 (`(def options (list (cli:make-option ...) ...))` — 9 options as Lisp list of `make-option` calls; first arg is KIND keyword)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 16-22 (`:flag :long-name "all-threads" :short-name #\A :initial-value :true` — short character-literal #\A; corpus's 3rd character-literal CLI flag after R291 CL #\h and R292 Erlang $h)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 29-35/36-42/62-68/69-75 (four `:choice` options with `:items '(...)` enumeration)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 43-48 (`--max-samples :integer :initial-value sb-sprof:*max-samples*` — default from SBCL special variable at load time)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 55-61 (TODO comment "Float class." documents upstream `:float` type missing; worked around with `:string` + parse-float)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 77-80 (`(def command (cli:make-command :name "kiln-sprof" :options options))` — serapeum `def` command-object binding)
    * `scripts/sprof.lisp` lines 82-94 (`(let* ((opts (cli:parse-command-line command args)) ...) (cli:getopt opts :key))` — opaque opts object + per-key access)
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **Common Lisp `clingon` library** (dnaeon/clingon) — the **modern, popular CL CLI library**, distinct from R291's `command-line-arguments` (older, list-data-spec style).  Clingon's design is heavily inspired by Go's cobra and Rust's clap — COMMAND OBJECTS hold OPTION OBJECTS, parsed via `cli:parse-command-line CMD ARGS` returning an OPAQUE PARSED-OPTIONS object.  Per-option access via `cli:getopt opts :key-symbol`.  Documents the **opaque-parsed-object-with-keyword-access pattern** — distinct from R291 CL `command-line-arguments` `handle-command-line *spec* 'handler :command-line args` (DISPATCH to handler) and R292 Erlang `getopt:parse` `{ok, {Opts, _Args}}` (tuple destructure).  In clingon, the parser does NOT dispatch — the caller `getopt`s each key by hand.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-FOUR library variants — Common Lisp slot now has TWO entries: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp 1 (R291 command-line-arguments), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), Pharo Smalltalk (R294), **Common Lisp 2: clingon** (R295 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 34 cross-language CLI library variants documented; Common Lisp slot has 2 distinct library implementations.
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **`:local-nicknames` package alias** at sprof.lisp:3-5 — `(defpackage :kiln/scripts/sprof ... (:local-nicknames (:cli :clingon)))` — the SBCL/CCL/ECL extension `:local-nicknames` binds the symbol `:cli` as a LOCAL ALIAS for `:clingon` within this package only.  Documents the **package-local-alias-for-CLI-namespace pattern** — distinct from R291 CL `(:command-line-arguments)` direct full-name reference and from Erlang `-import(proplists, [get_value/2])` (Erlang import).  Common Lisp's `:local-nicknames` is an SBCL-pioneered de-facto standard that allows SHORT ALIASES without polluting the global namespace.  Corpus's **first documented local-nicknames CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **kind-keyword as `make-option` first argument** at sprof.lisp:16/23/29/36/43/49/56/62/69 — every `cli:make-option` call's FIRST positional argument is a TYPE KEYWORD: `:flag` (boolean), `:choice` (enumerated), `:integer`, `:string`.  Followed by `:long-name`, `:short-name`, `:initial-value`, `:key`, `:description` keyword arguments.  Distinct from R291 CL `command-line-arguments` plist-style `(("name" #\h) :type STRING :optional t)` (type is a NAMED kwarg) and R292 Erlang 5-tuple `{Name, Short, Long, ArgSpec, Help}` (type is 4th positional with optional `{Type, Default}` tuple).  Clingon's kind keyword resolves to a CLOS CLASS (`clingon.options:option-flag`, `clingon.options:option-choice`, etc.) at `make-option` time via `find-class` dispatch.  Corpus's **first documented kind-keyword-dispatched CLI option**.
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **CLOS-class-backed `:choice` option type with item enumeration** at sprof.lisp:29-35/36-42/62-68/69-75 — four `:choice` options enumerate their legal values via `:items '("flat" "graph")`.  Clingon validates against the items list at parse time and emits a clear error message listing legal values.  TWO Common Lisp CLI variants now demonstrate choice handling differently (R291 CL had `:type X` typed but no choice enumeration; R295 clingon adds explicit `:choice :items '(...)`).
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **lazy default referencing host special variable** at sprof.lisp:46/59 — `:initial-value sb-sprof:*max-samples*` and `:initial-value (princ-to-string sb-sprof:*sample-interval*)`.  The DEFAULT for `--max-samples` is the CURRENT VALUE of SBCL's `sb-sprof:*max-samples*` special variable AT LOAD TIME — NOT a literal.  Documents the **host-variable-as-default pattern** — distinct from R294 Pharo lazy block default (re-evaluated at parse time), R285 Lua argparse `:default(8080)` (literal), and R287 Nim parseopt manual default assignment.  Clingon defaults are EVALUATED ONCE at `make-option` time and captured.  Plus the TODO comment at line 55 ("TODO Float class.") documents that clingon LACKS a `:float` option type — the developer worked around by using `:string` and parsing manually via `parse-float` at line 88.  Documents both **upstream limitation-noted-in-comment** and **string-as-float-stand-in** patterns.
  - Critical extraction note: kiln-sprof uses **`(def command ...)` global binding for command spec** at sprof.lisp:77-80 — `def` here is the `serapeum:def` macro (NOT standard `defparameter` or `defvar`).  Distinct from R291 CL `defparameter *command-line-spec*` (special variable convention with earmuffs) and from R294 Pharo class-side `commandSpecification` method.  Clingon convention is `def command` (no earmuffs) because the command is NOT meant to be dynamically rebound at runtime.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_kiln` itself (would cover the broader multicall-binary entry point; but each kiln subscript has its OWN clingon spec living in scripts/<name>.lisp — deferred until a follow-up round).  Probed `lisp-chat-admin` (ryukinix/lisp-chat — IRC-style chat tool, unmaintained), `ocicl` (templates/cli/src/main.lisp is a TEMPLATE not real CLI; src/ocicl.lisp does NOT use clingon).  `ciel` (CIEL Common Lisp REPL) already in corpus from unrelated AOSC-Dev/ciel-rs Rust lineage — no conflict but distinct tool.  Chose kiln-sprof: complete clingon spec in one self-contained file with all major clingon kinds (`:flag`, `:choice`, `:integer`, `:string`) represented, host-variable defaults, AND a TODO comment marking an upstream limitation — rare documentation gold.
  - Blacklist update: `kiln-sprof` added.  Corpus count: 28,717 → 28,718 (+1).

- **R294 — pillar source-direct (Pharo Smalltalk markup toolchain; Pharo `Clap` library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Tcl `cmdline::getoptions` (R293 tawk) into the **Pharo Smalltalk language tooling cluster**.  pillar is a **markup syntax and toolchain** for writing books, websites, and slides — Pharo's answer to Pandoc.  Sister tools: Pharo (Smalltalk VM), Iceberg (Git client), Metacello (package manager).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Pharo Smalltalk CLI** — adds Pharo `Clap` (port of Rust clap) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (33 library variants now).
  - `_pillar` (1-stem; pillar-markup/pillar src/Pillar-Cli/Clap*.class.st; **Pharo Smalltalk Clap library**; **5 subcommands (archetype/build/serve/updateBuild/updateTemplate) + Smalltalk cascade-chain builder + auto-subcommand discovery via allSubclasses + pragma-annotated class-method spec + multi-positional with lazy-block default + explicit-coerce-at-read + hasFlag: Boolean-message dispatch**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarCommand.class.st` lines 18-44 (`(ClapCommandSpec id: #pillar) description: '...'; commandClass: self; addHelp; add: (ClapFlag id: #version); yourself` + `pillarCommandsList do: [ :each | command addSubcommand: each ]` — cascade spec, --version flag, auto-discover subcommands)
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarArchetypeCommand.class.st` lines 16-29 (`commandName ^'archetype'`; `addPositional: #requestedArchetype`)
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarBuildCommand.class.st` lines 33-64 (`commandName ^'build'`; `addFlag: #templatable`, `addFlag: #all`, `addFlag: #mainRoot`; `addPositional: #format`; `addPositional: #requestedFiles spec: [ ... defaultValue: [ #('index.md') ]; multiple: true ]`)
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarServeCommand.class.st` lines 19-40 (`commandName ^'serve'`; `addPositional: #baseurl defaultValue: ''`; `addPositional: #port defaultValue: [ 8080 ]`; `addFlag: #watch`)
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarUpdateBuildCommand.class.st` lines 17-28 (`commandName ^ 'updateBuild'`; spec has only addHelp)
    * `src/Pillar-Cli/ClapPillarUpdateTemplateCommand.class.st` lines 17-29 (`commandName ^ 'updateTemplate'`; `addPositional: #requestedTemplate`)
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **Pharo Smalltalk `Clap` library** — a Smalltalk port of Rust's clap CLI library.  Spec is built via Smalltalk's **CASCADE MESSAGE chain** (semicolon `;`) — `(ClapCommandSpec id: #pillar) description: '...'; commandClass: self; addHelp; add: (ClapFlag id: #version); yourself` returns the receiver (`yourself`) at the END after configuring it via successive messages.  Documents the **cascade-chain builder pattern** — distinct from R277 Kotlin clikt (DSL receiver lambda), R278 Scala decline (combinator), and R276 Java picocli (annotations).  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-THREE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp (R291), Erlang (R292), Tcl (R293), **Pharo Smalltalk Clap** (R294 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 33 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **auto-subcommand discovery via `allSubclasses`** at ClapPillarCommand.class.st:46-50 — `self pillarCommandsList do: [ :each | command addSubcommand: each ]` where `pillarCommandsList` returns `self allSubclasses select: [ :each | each isAbstract not ] thenCollect: [ :each | each commandSpecification ]`.  All concrete subclasses of `ClapPillarCommand` become subcommands AUTOMATICALLY — no manual registration.  Documents the **runtime-class-introspection subcommand discovery pattern** — distinct from R290 Racket explicit `#:subcommands` list, R276 picocli `@Command(subcommands = {...})` annotation array, R278 Scala decline `Opts.subcommand`, and R292 Erlang `main(["sub" | Argv])` pattern-match.  Smalltalk's `allSubclasses` walks the RUNTIME class hierarchy.  Corpus's **first documented runtime-introspection subcommand discovery**.
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **pragma-annotated class-method spec** — `<commandline>` pragma on `commandSpecification` class-side method is the Smalltalk REGISTRATION HOOK that Clap scans for to build the command tree.  Distinct from Java `@Command` annotations on classes (R276 picocli) and Python `@click.command` decorators on functions (R266 uvicorn).  Pragma is on the METHOD, the class is the unit of registration.  Documents the **pragma-on-method/class-as-unit pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **multi-value positional with lazy-block default** at ClapPillarBuildCommand.class.st:58-62 — `addPositional: #requestedFiles spec: [ :positional | positional description: '...'; defaultValue: [ #('index.md') ]; multiple: true ]`.  The `multiple: true` modifier makes this a VARIADIC positional (consumes ALL remaining argv).  The `defaultValue:` block returns `#('index.md')` (Smalltalk LITERAL ARRAY syntax) when no positional values are given.  Documents the **block-as-default + multiple-positional pattern** — distinct from R285 Lua argparse `:args("*")` string-count modifier and R276 picocli `@Parameters(arity = "0..*")`.  Smalltalk's default-as-BLOCK is LAZY — only evaluated when the positional is missing.  Corpus's **first documented lazy-block-default CLI positional**.
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **explicit-coerce-at-read pattern** at ClapPillarServeCommand.class.st:34-37/71-73 — `addPositional: #port spec: [ :positional | positional defaultValue: [ 8080 ] ]` and at runtime `port := (self positional: #port) asInteger`.  The default value is a Smalltalk `SmallInteger` (8080) but the parsed CLI value arrives as a `String` — explicit `asInteger` conversion required.  Distinct from R292 Erlang `getopt:parse` typed atoms (parser converts), R287 Nim `parseopt` `parseInt(p.val)` (caller converts but parser is iterator), and R276 picocli `@Parameters(type = Integer.class)` (annotation-driven conversion).  Smalltalk's late binding + dynamic typing means Clap does NOT pre-convert; the application calls `asInteger` / `asNumber` / `asFileReference` at the read site.  Corpus's **first documented explicit-coerce-at-read CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: pillar uses **`hasFlag:` Boolean-message dispatch** at ClapPillarBuildCommand.class.st:73-75 — `(self hasFlag: #templatable) ifTrue: [ project beTemplatable ]`.  `hasFlag:` returns a Boolean and is checked via `ifTrue:` / `ifFalse:` blocks (Smalltalk's pattern-matched control flow on Boolean).  In Smalltalk, `ifTrue:` is itself a MESSAGE TO BOOLEAN — `true ifTrue: [ ... ]` evaluates the block, `false ifTrue: [ ... ]` does nothing.  Distinct from R285 Lua argparse table-field access (`args.verbose`), R290 Racket flag closures (`(*debug*)`), and R292 Erlang `proplists:get_bool(enable_ssl, NOpts)`.  Corpus's **first documented Boolean-message dispatch CLI**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped older `PR*CommandLineHandler` subclasses (legacy Pharo `CommandLineHandler`-based API; superseded by the `Clap*` classes in the same package).  Probed jvdsandt/pharo-aws-toolbox (AWS Lambda runtime, Pharo-internal), feenkcom/lepiter `LeHtmlBookExportCommandLineHandler` (lepiter is feenkcom's Pharo notebook tool — not a public CLI binary), GunterMueller/ST_STX_Fork (Smalltalk/X jupyter integration, niche), pharo-project/pharo-core various BasicCommandLineHandler subclasses (would conflict with `pharo` itself which is too generic).  Chose pillar: complete Clap-based Pharo CLI, five subcommands with distinct surface (positional, flags, multi-positional, optional positional), and spec is built per-subcommand-class with `<commandline>` pragma — clean source-direct extraction surface.
  - Blacklist update: `pillar` added.  Corpus count: 28,716 → 28,717 (+1).

- **R293 — tawk source-direct (awk-in-Tcl with CSV support; Tcl tcllib cmdline::getoptions)** (1 file new) — pivots from Erlang `getopt:parse` (R292 emqtt) into the **Tcl language tooling cluster**.  tawk is an **awk implementation in Tcl** with CSV support and safe-mode script execution.  Reads patterns/actions written in Tcl rather than awk syntax, runs them against each line of input.  Sister tools: tclsh (Tcl shell), wish (Tk shell), tcllib (Tcl stdlib), Tcl::Tk (CPAN wrapper).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Tcl `cmdline::getoptions`-based CLI** — adds Tcl `cmdline` (tcllib's standard CLI library) to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (32 library variants now).
  - `_tawk` (1-stem; shawnw/tawk tawk; **Tcl tcllib cmdline::getoptions library**; **8 flags + list-of-lists option spec + .arg-suffix-as-takes-value + mutating-argv-by-name + try/trap error-code-list + safe-interp sandbox + interp time limit + CSV mode-plus-policy flags**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `tawk` lines 25-29 (`package require cmdline` dependency declaration)
    * `tawk` lines 480-489 (`set options { {f.arg ""} {F.arg whitespace} {csv} {quoteall} {quotechar.arg \"} {safe} {timeout.arg 0} {version} }` — Tcl list-of-lists spec, 8 entries)
    * `tawk` line 490 (`set usage {[OPTIONS] ['script'] [VAR=VALUE | FILENAME] ...}`)
    * `tawk` line 493 (`array set params [::cmdline::getoptions argv $options $usage]` — getoptions MUTATES argv in-place, returns dict)
    * `tawk` lines 494-548 (param dispatch via `$params(name)`)
    * `tawk` lines 552-554 (`try { ... } trap {CMDLINE USAGE} {msg _} { puts stderr $msg; exit 0 }` — error-code-list trap)
    * `tawk` lines 498-501 (`if {$params(safe)} { set i [interp create -safe] }` — safe interp sandbox via CLI flag)
    * `tawk` lines 503-506 (`interp limit $i time -seconds DEADLINE` — wall-clock time limit on interp)
    * `tawk` lines 513-525 (csv mode with -quotechar and -quoteall policy flags)
  - Critical extraction note: tawk uses **Tcl `cmdline::getoptions`** — corpus's **first documented Tcl `cmdline` CLI**.  `cmdline` is tcllib's canonical CLI library (`package require cmdline`).  `cmdline::getoptions argv $options $usage` MUTATES `argv` in-place (Tcl pass-by-name via variable name), removing the parsed flags and leaving the remaining positionals.  Returns a Tcl dict (assoc list) of parsed params, here unpacked via `array set params [...]`.  Documents the **MUTATING-argv parser pattern** — distinct from R287 Nim `parseopt` (iterator), R288 Zig `argsAlloc` (returns slice), R289 D `std.getopt` (mutates args via `ref`), R291 CL `command-line-arguments` (dispatch via funcall), and R292 Erlang `getopt:parse` (returns `{ok, {Opts, _Args}}` tuple).  Tcl mutates the variable named by the CALLER's string — uplevel semantics.  Corpus's **first documented mutating-argv-by-name CLI**.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-TWO library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp (R291), Erlang (R292), **Tcl cmdline** (R293 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 32 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: tawk uses **`.arg`-suffix-as-takes-value pattern** at tawk:480-489 — each option's name is either `NAME` (boolean flag) or `NAME.arg` (takes a value).  The `.arg` suffix is a STRING CONVENTION embedded in the option name itself — parsed by `cmdline::getoptions` as a marker meaning "this option takes a value".  Distinct from R292 Erlang's 5-tuple with separate ArgSpec field, R291 CL plist `:type X :optional t`, R290 Racket's `#:args (fname problem)`.  Corpus's **first string-suffix-as-type-marker CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: tawk uses **Tcl `try`/`trap` for parser error recovery** at tawk:552-554 — `try { ... } trap {CMDLINE USAGE} {msg _} { puts stderr $msg; exit 0 } on error {msg} { puts stderr "Error: $msg"; exit 1 }`.  `cmdline::getoptions` raises a Tcl error with error-code `{CMDLINE USAGE}` when -? / --help is passed OR when parsing fails.  Tcl's `try`/`trap` matches errors by ERROR CODE (a Tcl list pattern), not by exception class.  Documents the **error-code-list trap pattern** — distinct from Python's `argparse.ArgumentError`, Ruby's `OptionParser::ParseError`, and Java picocli's `ParameterException`.  Tcl error codes are STRUCTURED LISTS (e.g., `{CMDLINE USAGE}`, `{POSIX ENOENT}`) used for pattern matching.  Corpus's **first documented error-code-list trap CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: tawk uses **safe-interp script execution** at tawk:498-501 — `if {$params(safe)} { set i [interp create -safe] }` — the `safe` flag creates a SANDBOXED Tcl interpreter (`interp create -safe`) for executing the user's script.  Tcl's safe interp removes dangerous commands (`file`, `exec`, `socket`, etc.) and restricts I/O.  Documents the **CLI-flag-controlled sandbox pattern** — convergent with `nodejs --experimental-permission` and Lua `5.1` sandbox-via-setfenv, but UNIQUE in being a FIRST-CLASS BUILT-IN of the host language.  No other CLI in the corpus exposes safe-interp sandbox-creation as a top-level CLI flag.  Corpus's **first sandbox-via-CLI-flag**.
  - Critical extraction note: tawk uses **interp time limit** at tawk:503-506 — `interp limit $i time -seconds [expr {[clock seconds] + $params(timeout)}]` — the `-timeout N` flag sets a WALL-CLOCK TIME LIMIT on the sandboxed interp.  When exceeded, the interp is torn down with a `LIMIT TIME` error.  Documents the **CLI-driven wall-clock interp limit pattern** — distinct from `timeout(1)` external-process alarm and from RLIMIT-based shell `ulimit`.  Tcl's `interp limit` is INTERP-INTERNAL — the runtime tracks deadlines per-interp, not per-process.  Corpus's **first documented interp-internal time-limit CLI**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_pebble` (already in corpus + blacklisted), `_piaware` (FlightAware ADS-B receiver, blacklisted), `_pirehose` (FlightAware data hose tool, niche).  Probed tcllib's `modules/yaml/yaml.tcl` (library, not a CLI), Hog-CERN (CERN FPGA tooling, too niche), various Altera/Quartus FPGA tcl scripts (vendor build scripts, not real CLIs), ActiveState/tdk `tclapp.tcl` (proprietary Tcl Dev Kit).  Chose tawk: standalone Tcl awk-in-Tcl tool, 8 distinct options with EVERY major cmdline pattern variant (.arg-with-default, .arg-no-default, boolean, default-with-backslash-escape), well-documented usage line, and `cmdline::getoptions` invocation is unambiguous.
  - Blacklist update: `tawk` added.  Corpus count: 28,715 → 28,716 (+1).

- **R292 — emqtt source-direct (EMQX MQTT 5.0 client; Erlang jcomellas/getopt:parse)** (1 file new) — pivots from Common Lisp `command-line-arguments` (R291 lem-language-server) into the **Erlang language tooling cluster**.  emqtt is the **MQTT 5.0 client (CLI + Erlang lib)** from EMQX — connects to MQTT brokers as publisher, subscriber, or raw connection.  Sister tools: rebar3 (Erlang build), kerl (Erlang version manager), relx (Erlang release), elvis (Erlang linter).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Erlang `getopt:parse`-based CLI** — adds Erlang `jcomellas/getopt` to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (31 library variants now).
  - `_emqtt` (1-stem; emqx/emqtt src/emqtt_cli.erl; **Erlang jcomellas/getopt library**; **3 subcommands (connect/pub/sub) + 23 shared connection opts + 7 pub-only opts + 5 sub-only opts + Erlang macro-composed CLI spec + Erlang `$X` character literals + cons-cell subcommand dispatch + typed ArgSpec atoms + runtime mutually-required validation**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 26-29 (`-define(HELP_OPT, [{help, undefined, "help", boolean, "Help information"}])` — module attribute macro)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 31-49 (`-define(CONN_SHORT_OPTS, [...])` — 8 short-switch opts: host/port/iface/protocol_version/username/password/clientid/keepalive)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 52-93 (`-define(CONN_LONG_OPTS, [...])` — 15 long-only opts: max_inflight/will-*/enable-*/tls-version/CAfile/cert/key/sni/verify/log-level)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` line 95 (`-define(CONNECT_OPTS, ?CONN_SHORT_OPTS ++ ?HELP_OPT ++ ?CONN_LONG_OPTS)` — macro concat)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 97-108 (`-define(PUB_OPTS, ?CONN_SHORT_OPTS ++ [topic/qos/retain] ++ ?HELP_OPT ++ ?CONN_LONG_OPTS ++ [payload/file/repeat/repeat_delay])` — pub spec)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 110-120 (`-define(SUB_OPTS, ?CONN_SHORT_OPTS ++ [topic/qos] ++ ?HELP_OPT ++ ?CONN_LONG_OPTS ++ [retain_as_publish/retain_handling/print])` — sub spec)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 122-143 (`main(["connect" | Argv]) -> {ok, {Opts, _Args}} = getopt:parse(?CONNECT_OPTS, Argv)` — cons-cell subcommand dispatch + getopt:parse call per branch)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` line 146 (`io:format("Usage: ~s pub | sub | connect [--help]~n", ...)` — usage line lists three subcommands)
    * `src/emqtt_cli.erl` lines 137-142 (pub `{Payload, File}` case: `{undefined, undefined} -> log_halt("Error: missing --payload or --file~n", [])` — runtime mutually-required check)
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **Erlang `getopt:parse` library** (jcomellas/getopt) — corpus's **first documented Erlang getopt:parse CLI**.  `jcomellas/getopt` is the de-facto standard Erlang getopt library used by rebar/rebar3/elvis/sup/aefate/weatherreport.  Different from BEAM stdlib `init:get_argument` (low-level) and from Elixir's `OptionParser`.  Each option spec is a 5-tuple: `{Name :: atom(), Short :: char() | undefined, Long :: string() | undefined, ArgSpec :: undefined | atom() | {atom(), Default}, HelpString :: string()}`.  ArgSpec atom controls typed parsing (integer | string | boolean | atom).  Documents the **5-tuple option-spec pattern** — distinct from R290 Racket macro-DSL and R291 Common Lisp plist `(:type X :optional t)`.  Extends cross-language CLI taxonomy to THIRTY-ONE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal (R269), Haskell (R270), OCaml (R271), C (R272), Java (R276), Kotlin (R277), Scala (R278), Dart (R279), Ruby (R280), Perl (R281), PHP (R282), Swift (R283), F# (R284), Lua (R285), C# (R286), Nim (R287), Zig (R288), D (R289), Racket (R290), Common Lisp (R291), **Erlang jcomellas/getopt** (R292 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 31 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **Erlang module-attribute MACROS for spec composition** at emqtt_cli.erl:26/31/52/95/97/110 — `-define(NAME, Expr).` creates compile-time MACROS, then `?NAME` expands them.  CONNECT_OPTS/PUB_OPTS/SUB_OPTS are built via `?CONN_SHORT_OPTS ++ ?HELP_OPT ++ ?CONN_LONG_OPTS ++ [pub-only-extra-opts]` — COMPILE-TIME LIST CONCATENATION via `++`.  Documents the **compile-time macro-composed CLI spec pattern** — distinct from R290 Racket macro DSL (`multi-command-line`), R278 Scala decline's `Command(...)` runtime composition, and R276 picocli inheritance via `@Command(subcommands = ...)`.  Plus the pub-only extras are SPLICED IN at the end (after long opts) — `++ [{payload, ...}, ...]`.  Corpus's **first documented compile-time macro-concat CLI spec**.
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **typed ArgSpec atoms** at emqtt_cli.erl:32-93 — each option's 4th tuple element is the type discriminator: `{string, "localhost"}` → string with default; `integer` → required int; `{integer, 300}` → int with default; `{atom, 'v5'}` → Erlang symbol with default; `{boolean, false}` → bool with default; `boolean` → required bool; `string` → required string.  Documents the **typed-with-default tuple pattern** — distinct from R289 D `std.getopt`'s lambda-callback, R288 Zig std.process.argsAlloc's manual parsing, and R287 Nim parseopt's untyped string returns.  In Erlang's `getopt:parse`, the type discriminator is DATA (tuple), not a generic type parameter.  Corpus's **first documented typed-atom CLI discriminator**.
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **Erlang character literals `$h` `$p` `$I` `$V` etc.** at emqtt_cli.erl:32-93 — `$X` syntax is the Erlang character literal (integer codepoint).  `$h` is 104, not a string.  Sister to R291 Common Lisp `#\h` (CL character literal) and distinct from R281 Perl `'h|help'` pipe syntax.  Two character-literal CLI cluster languages now: Erlang `$X`, Common Lisp `#\X`.  Corpus's **second character-literal CLI flag pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **subcommand dispatch via pattern-matching on Erlang list head** at emqtt_cli.erl:122/126/131 — `main(["connect" | Argv]) -> ...` uses Erlang's cons-cell pattern `[H | T]` to destructure the argv list.  The string "connect" matches the first element; remaining args go to Argv, then passed to `getopt:parse(?CONNECT_OPTS, Argv)`.  Three subcommand clauses + fallback `main(_Argv) -> io:format("Usage: ...")`.  Documents the **cons-cell subcommand dispatch pattern** — distinct from R290 Racket's `multi-command-line #:subcommands` macro (declarative), R278 Scala decline `Opts.subcommand` (combinator), and R276 picocli `@Command(subcommands = {...})` (annotation array).  Erlang's approach uses RUNTIME PATTERN MATCHING.  Corpus's **first documented cons-cell subcommand dispatch**.
  - Critical extraction note: emqtt uses **runtime mutually-required validation** at emqtt_cli.erl:137-142 for `pub` subcommand — `{Payload, File}` case expression: `{undefined, undefined} -> log_halt("Error: missing --payload or --file~n", [])` — the pub subcommand REQUIRES one of --payload or --file.  Documents the **runtime mutually-required pattern** — convergent with R291 Lem's runtime tcp+stdio mutex (mutually-exclusive variant) and R252 saya (mutex-via-clap-builder).  Three rounds now showing **runtime cross-flag validation when library lacks declarative support**: R252 saya (clap manual), R291 Lem (CL `when (and tcp stdio)`), R292 emqtt (Erlang case `{undefined, undefined}`).  Corpus's **third runtime cross-flag validation pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: skipped `_elvis` (already in corpus at more_src/_elvis), `_rebar3` (corpus + brew stock), `_rebar` (brew stock), `_sup` (blacklisted), `_relx` (blacklisted), `_kerl` (corpus + blacklisted).  Probed jcomellas/getopt examples (too generic, not a real CLI), basho/rebar (deprecated Erlang build), 2600hz/kazoo `core/sup/src/sup.erl` (Kazoo internal tool, blacklisted as sup), aeternity/aefate (FATE VM inspector, niche), apache/couchdb weatherreport (CouchDB diagnostic, namespace conflict with weatherreport gem).  Chose emqtt: well-known MQTT tool, three clear subcommands, rich getopt:parse spec with all type variants represented (string/integer/boolean/atom with-and-without defaults).
  - Blacklist update: `emqtt` added.  Corpus count: 28,714 → 28,715 (+1).

- **R291 — lem-language-server source-direct (Lem editor LSP server; Common Lisp command-line-arguments)** (1 file new) — pivots from Racket racket/cmdline (R290 cassius) into the **Common Lisp tooling cluster**.  lem-language-server is the **Lem editor's Language Server Protocol implementation** — provides LSP support for the Lem Emacs-like editor written in Common Lisp.  Sister to SBCL, Quicklisp, clingon (alternative CL CLI library).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Common Lisp CLI** — adds Common Lisp to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (30 library variants now).
  - `_lem-language-server` (1-stem; lem-project/lem extensions/language-server/cli.lisp; **Common Lisp command-line-arguments library**; **5 flags + LIST-BASED CLI SPEC + Lisp character-literal short names + data-driven dispatch + Common Lisp keyword args + runtime mutex validation**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` lines 6-26 (`*command-line-spec*` defparameter as list-based CLI spec)
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` lines 7-11 (option spec format: `(("name" #\short-char) :type X :optional t :documentation "...")`)
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` line 25 (`("help" #\h)` Lisp character literal short name)
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` lines 28-32 (data-driven dispatch via `handle-command-line *spec* 'run-server :command-line args`)
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` lines 34-37 (manual error/help via library helper)
    * `extensions/language-server/cli.lisp` lines 39-52 (Common Lisp keyword arg handler with defaults + runtime mutex check)
  - Critical extraction note: lem-language-server uses **`command-line-arguments`** library — corpus's **first documented Common Lisp CLI**.  `command-line-arguments` is **one of** the standard CL CLI libraries (others include `clingon`, `unix-opts`, `defcommand`).  Used by Lem, screenshotbot, and many Common Lisp tooling projects.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to THIRTY library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), C# System.CommandLine (R286), Nim parseopt (R287), Zig std.process.argsAlloc (R288), D std.getopt (R289), Racket racket/cmdline (R290), **Common Lisp command-line-arguments** (R291 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 30 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: lem-language-server uses **`defparameter *command-line-spec*` LIST-BASED CLI SPEC stored as Lisp data** at cli.lisp:6-26 — the CLI specification is a regular Lisp data structure (a list of lists), NOT a macro or compiled struct.  Each option is a list `(("name" #\short-char) :type X :optional t :documentation "...")`.  Documents the **data-as-CLI-spec pattern** — distinct from Racket's macro-based DSL (R290 cassius), Java picocli annotations (R276), and Swift property wrappers (R283).  In Common Lisp, the CLI spec is FIRST-CLASS DATA that can be inspected, modified, or generated at runtime.  Plus the spec is bound to a global parameter, allowing multiple functions (`main`, `command-line-error`) to reference the same spec.  Corpus's **first documented data-as-CLI-spec pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lem-language-server uses **Lisp CHARACTER LITERAL `#\h`** at cli.lisp:25 for the SHORT NAME — `("help" #\h)` is the option name list: long name string + short character.  Common Lisp's `#\X` syntax is the character literal (e.g., `#\h` is the character 'h', not the string "h").  Documents the **Lisp character-literal for short flag** — distinct from Rust's `'h'` char literal (R260 dua uses `short = 'A'`), Ruby's string `"-h"` (R280 reek), and Perl's pipe-syntax `'h|help'` (R281 cpanm).  Lisp's character literal is type-distinct from strings.  Corpus's **first documented Lisp character-literal CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: lem-language-server uses **DATA-DRIVEN DISPATCH** at cli.lisp:28-32 — `command-line-arguments:handle-command-line *spec* 'run-server :command-line args` passes the SPEC DATA + HANDLER SYMBOL + ARGS as a single function call.  The library handles parsing AND invokes the handler with the parsed values.  Documents the **data + handler-symbol dispatch pattern** — distinct from clap derive's struct-population (R247+) and from Click's decorator stack (R266 uvicorn).  In Common Lisp, `'run-server` is a QUOTED SYMBOL that names the handler function; the library resolves it at runtime via `funcall`.  Plus the handler signature `(&key tcp stdio (port 10007) log-file help)` uses Common Lisp **keyword arguments with defaults** — the parsed CLI values are passed as `:tcp t :port 10007 ...` keyword args.  Corpus's **first documented data-driven handler-symbol dispatch**.
  - Critical extraction note: lem-language-server uses **runtime MUTEX validation** at cli.lisp:44-46 — `(when (and tcp stdio) (command-line-error "These arguments cannot be used together: (tcp, stdio)") (return-from run-server))` — the handler manually checks for incompatible flag combinations.  Documents the **runtime cross-flag mutex pattern** — distinct from R250 sozo's clap `#[group(multiple = false)]` (declarative), R249 cairo-profiler's `conflicts_with` attribute (declarative), and Symfony Console's `@ArgGroup(exclusive = true)` (R276 checkstyle).  Common Lisp's approach uses IMPERATIVE if-checks in the handler — typical of data-driven CLI libraries that don't have declarative mutex support.  Plus the library (`command-line-arguments`) has no built-in mutex attribute; the developer encodes it manually.  Pattern documented in 2 languages now: Common Lisp (R291 lem-language-server) + Saya (R252 — saya does runtime cross-flag validation in Rust).
  - Hunt-skip notes: clingon (alternative Common Lisp CLI library used by intro example, roswell2, screenshotbot) for future round.  Other Common Lisp libraries: `unix-opts`, `defcommand`.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks remaining unfilled.  Tcl/Tk command parsing, Smalltalk CLIs remaining.
  - Blacklist: added `lem-language-server`.
  - Corpus 28,713 → 28,714.

- **R290 — cassius source-direct (Racket-based CSS layout solver; Racket racket/cmdline)** (1 file new) — pivots from D std.getopt (R289 sdfmt) into the **Racket / Scheme language tooling cluster**.  Cassius is the **Racket-based CSS layout engine solver** by uwplse — uses Z3 SMT solver to verify CSS layout properties.  Academic research project.  Sister to Racket itself, raco (Racket's CLI tool for packages).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Racket CLI** — adds Racket to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (29 library variants now).
  - `_cassius` (1-stem; uwplse/Cassius src/run.rkt; **Racket stdlib racket/cmdline**; **6 subcommands + 3 global multi-options + macro-based CLI DSL + S-expression option specs + #:multi / #:once-each / #:once-any sections + #:subcommands with per-subcommand options + #:args positional with defaults**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/run.rkt` line 1 (`(require racket/cmdline ...)`)
    * `src/run.rkt` lines 174-259 (`(module+ main` block with `multi-command-line` macro)
    * `src/run.rkt` lines 178-180 (`#:program "cassius"` keyword-arg macro invocation)
    * `src/run.rkt` lines 181-202 (`#:multi` section with `#:multi` option specs)
    * `src/run.rkt` line 182 (S-expression option spec: aliases + help + body)
    * `src/run.rkt` line 183 (option with value parameter `file-name`)
    * `src/run.rkt` lines 204-259 (`#:subcommands` with 6 subcommands)
    * `src/run.rkt` lines 228-233 (`dump` subcommand with its own `#:once-each` section)
  - Critical extraction note: Cassius uses **Racket's stdlib `racket/cmdline`** library at `(require racket/cmdline)` — corpus's **first documented Racket CLI** AND first use of the **Racket macro-based CLI DSL**.  `racket/cmdline` is **the** standard Racket CLI library — built into Racket (no package install needed).  Provides `command-line` (single command) and `multi-command-line` (with subcommands) MACROS that expand at compile time into argument parsing code.  Used by raco (Racket's package manager), drracket (Racket IDE), and most Racket CLI tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-NINE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), C# System.CommandLine (R286), Nim parseopt (R287), Zig std.process.argsAlloc (R288), D std.getopt (R289), **Racket racket/cmdline** (R290 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 29 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: Cassius uses **Racket MACRO-BASED CLI DSL** at run.rkt:178-259 — `multi-command-line` is a MACRO (not a function) that expands at compile time into argument parsing code.  The macro accepts a SEXPRESSION DSL with keyword arguments (`#:program`, `#:multi`, `#:subcommands`) and option specifications.  Documents the **Lisp macro-based CLI DSL** — distinct from Java picocli's annotations (R276), Kotlin clikt's property delegation (R277), and Swift property wrappers (R283).  Lisp macros enable the DSL to look like a built-in language construct.  Plus the action body for each option is PLAIN RACKET CODE that runs when the option is seen.  Corpus's **first documented Lisp macro-based CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: Cassius uses **S-EXPRESSION OPTION SPEC** at run.rkt:182-202 — each option is a list of the form `[(aliases) help-string body ...]` or with value parameter `[(aliases) param help-string body ...]`.  Documents the **S-expression-based option declaration** — distinct from clap derive's struct attributes (R247+), Click's decorators (R266 uvicorn), and Symfony's constructor calls (R282 peridot).  Racket's approach uses LIST STRUCTURE to encode option metadata.  Plus the use of QUOTED STRINGS (`"Turn on debug mode"`) for help text inside the list is idiomatic Racket.  Corpus's **first documented S-expression option spec**.
  - Critical extraction note: Cassius uses **`#:multi` vs `#:once-each` SECTION KEYWORDS** at run.rkt:181 + 229 — Racket cmdline distinguishes options that can appear MULTIPLE TIMES (`#:multi`) from those that should appear AT MOST ONCE (`#:once-each`).  `#:once-any` (not used here) restricts options to AT MOST ONE from a mutex set.  Documents the **declarative occurrence-count sections** — distinct from Lua argparse's `:count "N-M"` range (R285 luacheck) and clap derive's `Vec<T>` for repeatable (R260 dua).  Racket's approach groups options BY occurrence-count rather than per-option annotation.  Corpus's **first documented section-keyword occurrence grouping**.
  - Critical extraction note: Cassius uses **`#:subcommands` with `#:args` positional list** at run.rkt:204-259 — each subcommand is a list of the form `[name #:args (a b [c "default"]) body ...]`.  The `#:args` clause lists POSITIONAL PARAMETERS to bind from CLI args; `[c "default"]` syntax provides DEFAULT VALUES for optional positionals.  Documents the **declarative positional-with-defaults pattern** — distinct from clap derive's `Option<T>` field type (R260 dua), Click's `default=...` kwarg (R266 uvicorn), and Python argparse's `nargs='?'` (R267 alembic).  Racket's approach uses LIST STRUCTURE: bare name = required, `[name default]` = optional with default.  Plus subcommands can have their OWN `#:once-each` sub-option sections (see `dump` subcommand at run.rkt:228-233).  Corpus's **first documented per-subcommand option sections**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: raco (Racket's package manager) for future round — would document a richer Racket CLI.  Common Lisp CLIs, Scheme variants, Erlang/OTP applications remaining.  Tcl/Tk command parsing, Smalltalk CLIs remaining.
  - Blacklist: added `cassius`.
  - Corpus 28,712 → 28,713.

- **R289 — sdfmt source-direct (Snazzy D Compiler code formatter; D stdlib std.getopt)** (1 file new) — pivots from Zig std.process.argsAlloc (R288 cyber) into the **D language tooling cluster**.  sdfmt is the **D code formatter** from snazzy-d/sdc (Snazzy D Compiler) — formats D source code per the D style guide.  Sister to dmd / dub / dscanner (other D tools).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented D std.getopt CLI** — adds D to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (28 library variants now).
  - `_sdfmt` (1-stem; snazzy-d/sdc src/driver/sdfmt.d; **D stdlib std.getopt library**; **2 flags + variadic triplet pattern + inline-config-flag + auto-helpWanted + help_info.options reflection + GetOptException error handling**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` line 7 (`import std.getopt`)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` lines 9-15 (variadic `getopt(args, config, NAME, HELP, &VAR, ...)` call)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` line 11 (`std.getopt.config.caseSensitive` inline config flag)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` line 17 (`help_info.helpWanted` auto-detection)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` lines 23-34 (custom help formatting via `help_info.options` reflection)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` line 38 (`catch (GetOptException ex)`)
    * `src/driver/sdfmt.d` lines 10 + 14 (sdfmt's own self-referential `// sdfmt off` / `// sdfmt on` directives)
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **D's stdlib `std.getopt`** library at `import std.getopt` — corpus's **first documented D CLI**.  `std.getopt` is **the** standard D CLI parsing library — built into the D stdlib (Phobos).  Used by dmd (D compiler), dub (D package manager), dscanner, and most D CLI tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-EIGHT library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), C# System.CommandLine (R286), Nim parseopt (R287), Zig std.process.argsAlloc (R288), **D std.getopt** (R289 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 28 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **VARIADIC getopt call with NAME / HELP / ADDRESS triplets** at sdfmt.d:9-15 — each option is specified as 3 consecutive args: (1) NAME with optional `|short` suffix, (2) HELP text, (3) ADDRESS-OF target variable.  Documents the **D variadic triplet argument pattern** — distinct from Perl's `'name|short=type' => \var` pairs (R281 cpanm), Ruby's `parser.on(name, help) { |v| ... }` block (R280 reek), and Lua's `parser:option(name, help)` chained calls (R285 luacheck).  D's variadic approach is the MOST COMPACT for many flags — everything in a single function call.  Corpus's **first documented variadic triplet CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **`std.getopt.config.caseSensitive`** at sdfmt.d:11 — passes a CONFIG FLAG IN THE ARGS LIST.  D's std.getopt accepts config flags interleaved with option specifications.  Each `std.getopt.config.X` value applies to ALL options declared AFTER it (until another config flag overrides it).  Documents the **inline-config-flag pattern** — distinct from Perl Getopt::Long's `Getopt::Long::Configure("bundling")` called as a separate statement (R281 cpanm).  D's approach EMBEDS the config in the same call.  Other D config flags include `passThrough`, `keepEndOfOptions`, `noBundling`, `noBundlingValue`.  Corpus's **first documented inline-config CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **`help_info.helpWanted` auto-detection** at sdfmt.d:17 — D's std.getopt automatically detects when `--help` or `-h` was passed by the user.  The returned struct has a `.helpWanted` bool that's true if either was given.  Documents the **auto-help-detection** pattern — distinct from R287 nimpretty's manual `of "help", "h":` case dispatch and from R273 irust's hand-rolled `show_help` field.  D's approach is built into the library.  Corpus's **first documented auto-helpWanted detection**.
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **`help_info.options`** for METADATA REFLECTION at sdfmt.d:23-34 — `help_info.options` is an array of option metadata structs, each with `.optShort`, `.optLong`, `.help` fields.  Used for CUSTOM HELP FORMATTING: the developer iterates the array and writes customized help output.  Documents the **option-metadata reflection pattern** — D's std.getopt EXPOSES the parsed option definitions for runtime introspection.  Distinct from clap derive's no-direct-reflection (R247+) and from Symfony's inheritance-based help generation (R282 peridot).  Useful for tools that need NON-STANDARD help formatting (sdfmt uses a custom format with `%-16s` for option alignment).  Corpus's **first documented runtime option-metadata reflection**.
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **`catch (GetOptException ex)`** at sdfmt.d:38 — D's std.getopt throws an exception on parse errors (e.g., unknown flag).  The application catches it and handles gracefully.  Documents the **exception-based error handling** for CLI parsing in D — distinct from C's getopt return-code (R272 blink), Rust's Result types (R273+), and Go's manual checking (R263 lazygit).  D's approach is OO-style — errors are exceptions thrown by the library.  Pattern documented in 2 languages now: PHP Symfony Console (R282 peridot) + D std.getopt (R289 sdfmt).
  - Critical extraction note: sdfmt uses **`// sdfmt off` / `// sdfmt on`** SELF-REFERENTIAL DIRECTIVES at sdfmt.d:10 + 14 — sdfmt's own code-formatting directives are used INSIDE sdfmt's source to disable formatting around the getopt call (which uses unusual indentation for readability).  Documents the **self-referential meta-formatting pattern** — corpus's first documentation of a CLI tool USING ITS OWN DIRECTIVES in its source.  Distinct from `.editorconfig` (external config) and from clang-format pragmas (#define-style).  Plus the directives are line comments `//` which get preserved through formatting.
  - Hunt-skip notes: dub / dmd / dscanner (other D tools) for future rounds — could expand D coverage.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks as standalone binaries remaining unfilled.  Tcl/Tk command parsing, Racket CLI, Common Lisp CLIs remaining.
  - Blacklist: added `sdfmt`.
  - Corpus 28,711 → 28,712.

- **R288 — cyber source-direct (Cyber programming language; Zig stdlib std.process.argsAlloc)** (1 file new) — pivots from Nim parseopt (R287 nimpretty) into the **Zig language tooling cluster**.  cyber is the **Cyber programming language** by fubark — embedded dynamic scripting language (similar to Lua but with Zig influence).  Sister to zig (compiler), zigup (version manager), zls (language server).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Zig CLI** — adds Zig to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (27 library variants now).
  - `_cyber` (1-stem; fubark/cyber src/main.zig; **Zig stdlib std.process.argsAlloc**; **8 flags + 6 subcommands + allocator-based argv + defer cleanup + std.mem.eql slice equality + mixed flag/subcommand parsing with break-on-positional + silent unknown flag forwarding**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main.zig` lines 46-47 (`std.process.argsAlloc(alloc)` + `defer argsFree`)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 52-106 (manual while loop with index counter)
    * `src/main.zig` line 56 (`std.mem.eql(u8, arg, "-v")` for slice equality)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 139-146 (Command enum for subcommand dispatch)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 108-129 (Zig `switch (cmd) { .eval => ... }`)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 79-80 (silent acceptance of unknown flags for script forwarding)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 92-97 (break on first positional)
    * `src/main.zig` lines 321-355 (manual `help()` / `version()` procs with `\\` multi-line strings)
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **Zig's stdlib `std.process.argsAlloc`** at `const args = try std.process.argsAlloc(alloc)` — corpus's **first documented Zig CLI**.  Zig has NO dedicated CLI parsing library in the stdlib — only the low-level `argsAlloc` / `argsFree` functions that return the argv as `[][]const u8` (slice of string slices).  All higher-level parsing is HAND-ROLLED or via third-party libraries (zig-clap is popular).  cyber uses the LOWEST-LEVEL approach — manual while-loop parsing.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-SEVEN library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), C# System.CommandLine (R286), Nim parseopt (R287), **Zig std.process.argsAlloc** (R288 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 27 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **ALLOCATOR-BASED argv** at main.zig:46-47 — `std.process.argsAlloc(alloc)` takes an allocator parameter and the caller must `std.process.argsFree(alloc, args)` to release memory.  Documents the **explicit-allocator CLI pattern** — UNIQUE to Zig.  Every other language in the corpus's taxonomy has automatic memory management for argv (Rust's `std::env::args()` returns an iterator with lifetime tied to the program; Go's `os.Args` is a slice owned by the runtime; etc.).  Zig requires the developer to pass an allocator and free the result.  Documents Zig's **allocator-as-first-class-parameter** design philosophy — corpus's **first documented allocator-parameter CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **`defer` for cleanup** at main.zig:47 — `defer std.process.argsFree(alloc, args)` schedules the cleanup call to run at scope exit.  Documents Zig's **defer statement** — similar to Go's `defer` (R263 lazygit) but at the language level.  Combined with the allocator-based argv, this is Zig's RAII-like resource management.  Distinct from C's manual `free()` (R272 blink — easy to forget) and from Rust's `Drop` trait (automatic).  Pattern documented in 2 languages now: Go (R263 lazygit) + Zig (R288 cyber).
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **`std.mem.eql(u8, arg, "-v")`** for string comparison at main.zig:56-71 — Zig has NO operator overloading, so slice equality requires the stdlib `std.mem.eql` function call with the type parameter (`u8` for byte slices).  Documents the **explicit-type-parameter equality** pattern — distinct from clap derive's `match arg.as_str()` (R273 irust), Crystal's `==` (R269 shards), Lua's `==` (R285 luacheck).  Zig's verbosity is intentional — no hidden operations.  Corpus's **first documented `std.mem.eql` slice-equality CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **manual `while (i < args.len) : (i += 1)`** at main.zig:53 — Zig's `while` loop with **CONTINUATION EXPRESSION** (the `: (i += 1)` part).  Equivalent to a C-style `for (i = 1; i < args.len; i++)`.  Documents Zig's **while-with-continuation-expression syntax** — similar to C's for loop but using `while` keyword.  Plus the manual index counter allows `break;` to exit early (used at main.zig:96 to stop flag parsing after first positional).  Documents the **manual-iterator CLI parsing** — corpus's first explicit-index while-loop CLI parsing.
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **SILENT ACCEPTANCE of unknown flags** at main.zig:79-80 — the comment reads "Ignore unrecognized options so a script can use them."  Unknown flags are SILENTLY DROPPED (not added to a positional list, not errored).  Documents the **silent-unknown-flag pattern** for SCRIPT FORWARDING — distinct from R273 irust's `unknown_args` Vec collection (warns to stderr) and from clap derive's strict-unknown default (errors out).  Useful when a CLI wraps an underlying interpreter that needs to see its own flags.  Corpus's **first documented silent-unknown-flag forwarding** for embedded interpreter scenarios.
  - Critical extraction note: cyber uses **mixed flag/subcommand parsing with `break;`** at main.zig:81-105 — the same `while` loop handles BOTH flags (`-v`, `-r`, etc.) AND subcommands (`compile`, `fmt`, etc.).  When the first positional arg is encountered AND it's NOT a known subcommand, cyber sets `cmd = .eval` (default eval mode), saves the arg as `arg0`, and `break`s out of the parsing loop.  Documents the **mixed-flag/subcommand parsing with break-on-positional** pattern — distinct from clap derive's separate subcommand parsing (R247+) and from Click's `@click.group` (R266).  Zig's approach is the most MANUAL but also the most FLEXIBLE — the developer controls when to stop parsing.  Corpus's **first documented break-on-positional parsing**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: zig-clap (Zig third-party CLI library) for future round — would document a higher-level Zig CLI pattern.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks as standalone binaries remaining unfilled.  D language CLI, Tcl/Tk command parsing remaining.
  - Blacklist: added `cyber`.
  - Corpus 28,710 → 28,711.

- **R287 — nimpretty source-direct (Nim Pretty Printer; Nim stdlib parseopt)** (1 file new) — pivots from C# System.CommandLine (R286 fracjson) into the **Nim language tooling cluster**.  nimpretty is the **Nim Pretty Printer** (Nim formatter) by the nim-lang team — official Nim code formatter built into the Nim compiler.  Sister to nimble (Nim package manager, in corpus), Nim compiler itself.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Nim parseopt CLI** — adds Nim to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (26 library variants now).
  - `_nimpretty` (1-stem; nim-lang/Nim nimpretty/nimpretty.nim; **Nim stdlib parseopt library**; **8 flags + iterator-based parsing + CmdLineKind discriminator enum + multi-value case-branch dispatch + normalize() case-insensitive matching + Nim colon-separator format**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` line 17 (`import parseopt, strutils, os, sequtils`)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` lines 23-39 (USAGE string embedded)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` lines 41-49 (manual writeHelp / writeVersion procs)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` lines 134-156 (parseopt dispatch loop)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` line 144-155 (Nim case-of multi-value pattern for aliases)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` line 145 (`case normalize(key)` case-insensitive)
    * `nimpretty/nimpretty.nim` line 137-142 (cmdArgument with directory recursion)
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **Nim's stdlib `parseopt`** library at `import parseopt` — corpus's **first documented Nim parseopt CLI**.  `parseopt` is **the** standard Nim CLI parsing library — built into the Nim stdlib (no nimble install needed).  Used by Nim's compiler, nimsuggest, nimpretty, koch (Nim build tool).  Note: many Nim CLIs use the THIRD-PARTY `cligen` library instead, which provides higher-level auto-generation from proc signatures (similar to alembic's R267 introspection approach).  This round documents the LOW-LEVEL stdlib parseopt.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-SIX library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), C# System.CommandLine (R286), **Nim parseopt** (R287 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 26 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **ITERATOR-BASED parsing** at nimpretty.nim:134 — `for kind, key, val in getopt():` is a Nim iterator that yields a tuple `(kind: CmdLineKind, key: string, val: string)` for each parsed CLI token.  Documents the **Nim iterator-CLI pattern** — corpus's **first documented iterator-based CLI parsing**.  Distinct from clap derive's struct-population (R247+), Click's decorator stack (R266 uvicorn), and argparse's `parse_args()` returning a Namespace (R267 alembic).  Nim's iterator approach is closer to manual parsing (cf. R273 irust hand-rolled) but the LIBRARY provides the TOKENIZER.  The developer writes the DISPATCH logic via `case kind` + `case key`.  This is the **stream-processing CLI pattern**: parsing happens INCREMENTALLY as you iterate through tokens.  Corpus's first iterator-based CLI parsing.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **`cmd*` DISCRIMINATOR enum** at nimpretty.nim:136-156 — parseopt's `CmdLineKind` enum has 4 variants: `cmdArgument` (positional argument), `cmdLongOption` (`--long-name` flag), `cmdShortOption` (`-s` short flag), `cmdEnd` (end-of-input sentinel).  Used in `case kind` to dispatch handling per token type.  Documents the **enum-discriminator parsing pattern** — distinct from R275 jiff-cli's lexopt `Arg::Short(c) | Arg::Long(name) | Arg::Value(v)` enum (R275 used Rust pattern matching) and R272 blink's getopt() return value (C int with `case` dispatch).  Nim's enum is type-safe and exhaustive (compiler warns on missing case).  Corpus's **first documented CmdLineKind discriminator enum**.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **MULTIPLE VALUES per case branch** at nimpretty.nim:146, 147, 149, 150, 154 — Nim's `case` statement supports `of "output", "o", "out":` — a single branch matched by ANY of the listed values.  Documents the **multi-value case-branch pattern** in Nim — equivalent to Rust's match `"output" | "o" | "out"` (R273 irust) and Crystal's `when "a", "b":` (R269 shards).  Plus nimpretty uses `of "stdin", "":` to pair `--stdin` with the empty string (which corresponds to the bare `-` arg — typical Unix idiom).  Corpus's **first documented Nim multi-value case-branch CLI dispatch**.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **`normalize(key)`** at nimpretty.nim:145 — calls Nim stdlib's `normalize` function to lowercase the key (and remove underscores) for case-INSENSITIVE flag matching.  So `--output` / `--Output` / `--OUTPUT` / `--Out_put` ALL match `of "output":`.  Documents the **case-insensitive flag matching** via manual normalization — distinct from clap derive's case-sensitive default (R247+), Ruby OptionParser's case-sensitive (R280 reek), and R260 dua's `ignore_case = true` ValueEnum attribute (which is for VALUES, not FLAG NAMES).  Nim's approach is MANUAL — the developer calls `normalize()` explicitly.  Corpus's **first documented manual case-insensitive flag matching**.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **`parseopt` UNIVERSAL `key=val` format** at nimpretty.nim:148-152 — parseopt accepts BOTH `--indent:4` (Nim convention) AND `--indent=4` (POSIX convention) AND `--indent 4` (space-separated) for value-taking options.  Nim's COLON convention (`--indent:4`) is unique to Nim/Pascal-family languages.  Documents the **multi-format value-separator support** — parseopt is FLEXIBLE about how values follow flag names.  Distinct from getopt (C, R272 blink — requires `=` or space), clap derive (Rust, R247+ — requires `=` or space), and argparse (Python, R267 alembic — accepts both).  Nim/parseopt's `:` separator is a DIALECT-SPECIFIC convention but the library also accepts the universal `=` and space.  Corpus's **first documented colon-separator CLI value format**.
  - Critical extraction note: nimpretty uses **manual `cmdArgument` recursion for directory args** at nimpretty.nim:137-142 — when a CLI arg is a directory (checked via `dirExists(key)`), nimpretty uses `walkDirRec(key, skipSpecial = true)` to RECURSIVELY collect all `.nim` and `.nimble` files in the directory tree.  Documents the **arg-as-directory recursive pattern** — common in linters/formatters that accept either files or directories (cf. R285 luacheck which accepts both).  Plus the `addFileExt(".nim")` ensures bare names get the `.nim` extension auto-appended.
  - Hunt-skip notes: cligen (higher-level Nim CLI library) for future round — would document a different Nim CLI pattern.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks as standalone binaries remaining unfilled.  Zig std.process.argsAlloc, D language CLI, Tcl/Tk command parsing remaining.
  - Blacklist: added `nimpretty`.
  - Corpus 28,709 → 28,710.

- **R286 — fracjson source-direct (FracturedJson JSON formatter CLI; C# System.CommandLine)** (1 file new) — pivots from Lua argparse (R285 luacheck) into the **.NET / C# language tooling cluster**.  fracjson (FracturedJson CLI) is the **.NET JSON formatter** by j-brooke — reformats JSON documents to make them highly human-readable.  Sister to dotnet CLI, Microsoft.dotnet-* tools.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented C# System.CommandLine CLI** — adds C# to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (25 library variants now).
  - `_fracjson` (1-stem; j-brooke/FracturedJson Cli/CliParser.cs; **C# System.CommandLine library**; **30+ options + dual-naming-convention aliases + generic Option<T?> nullable tri-state + C# object initializer + DefaultValueFactory lambda + AcceptExistingOnly chained validator + RootCommand collection initializer**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 1-2 (`using System.CommandLine;` imports)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 182-192 (`Argument<FileInfo>` with C# object initializer)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 194-204 (`Option<FileInfo>` with chained `.AcceptExistingOnly()`)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 206-211 (`DefaultValueFactory = (_ => false)` lambda)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 213 + 232 (multiple aliases as constructor positional args)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 248-298 (Option<bool?> / Option<int?> nullable tri-state)
    * `Cli/CliParser.cs` lines 300-333 (RootCommand C# COLLECTION INITIALIZER syntax)
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **C#'s `System.CommandLine`** library — corpus's **first documented C# System.CommandLine CLI**.  `System.CommandLine` is **Microsoft's official .NET CLI library** — built into the .NET ecosystem.  Used by .NET's own CLI tools (dotnet, dotnet-* tools), PowerShell command parameters, and most modern C# CLI tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-FIVE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), Lua argparse (R285), **C# System.CommandLine** (R286 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 25 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Documents the **C# generic-type-parameter CLI declaration pattern**: `Option<int?>`, `Option<bool?>`, `Argument<FileInfo>` — strongly-typed options with C#'s generic type parameters.  Distinct from F# Argu's discriminated-union pattern (R284 fsix) — both .NET ecosystem but **C# uses generic types** while F# uses sum types.  Documents the **.NET CLI library divide**: System.CommandLine is for C# / generic-types; Argu is for F# / discriminated-unions.
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **C# OBJECT INITIALIZER syntax** at CliParser.cs:186-191, 198-203, 206-211 — C#'s `new T(args) { Prop1 = val1, Prop2 = val2 }` syntax sets properties after construction without calling individual setter methods.  Documents the **C# object-initializer CLI declaration pattern** — distinct from Cmdliner's pipeline syntax (R271 dune), Symfony's `addOption` imperative calls (R282 peridot), and Swift property wrappers (R283 yap).  C#'s syntax is the most CONCISE for setting multiple properties on a constructed object.  Corpus's **first documented C# object-initializer CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **multiple aliases as constructor positional args** at CliParser.cs:213, 229, 232, 235, etc. — `new Option<bool>("--minify")`, `new Option<int?>("--length", "--MaxTotalLineLength", "-l")` — the FIRST positional arg is the canonical name; ALL subsequent positional args are aliases.  Documents the **constructor-positional-aliases pattern** — distinct from Rust clap derive's `#[arg(short, long = "...", alias = "...")]` (R250 sozo), Click's `@click.option("--long", "-s")` decorator (R266 uvicorn), and Symfony's separate aliases array (R282 peridot).  C#'s approach is the MOST COMPACT for multiple aliases.  Plus fracjson uses **dual-naming-convention** for backward-compat: every option has BOTH a kebab-case short name (`--length`) AND a PascalCase library name (`--MaxTotalLineLength`).  Corpus's **first documented dual-naming-convention** for CLI backward-compat.
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **NULLABLE generic types** for tri-state options at CliParser.cs:248-298 — `Option<bool?>` / `Option<int?>` use C#'s `T?` nullable syntax to distinguish 3 states: NOT-SET (null) / FALSE / TRUE for booleans, or NOT-SET / int value for integers.  Documents the **nullable-tri-state pattern** in C# — distinct from clap derive's `Option<T>` field type (R260 dua — uses Rust's `Option`), Swift ArgumentParser's `T?` (R283 yap), and Click's `default=None` (R266 uvicorn).  All three approach the same problem (distinguishing default-not-set from explicit-default) but C#'s `T?` is LANGUAGE-LEVEL nullable types.  Used by fracjson to allow config file values to override defaults: CLI null → use config; CLI false → override config with false.  Corpus's **first documented C# nullable tri-state CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **`DefaultValueFactory = (_ => false)`** at CliParser.cs:210 — C# property initializer with a LAMBDA EXPRESSION for the default value.  `(_ => false)` is `argResult => false` (ignores arg result, returns false).  Documents the **closure-based default value factory** pattern in C# — distinct from R262 matugen's Rust `default_value = fn()` (free fn call), R266 uvicorn's Click `default=False` (literal value), and R267 alembic's argparse `default=False` (literal value).  C#'s lambda allows COMPUTED defaults that can reference the parse result.  Plus the `_` is C#'s discard pattern for unused parameters.  Corpus's **first documented lambda-default-factory pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **chained `.AcceptExistingOnly()`** at CliParser.cs:191 + 203 — System.CommandLine's chainable VALIDATOR method that requires the file argument to refer to an existing file.  Returns the option/argument for further chaining.  Documents the **chained-validator pattern** in System.CommandLine — similar to clap derive's `value_parser` (R248 cairo-coverage) and Click's `type=click.Path(exists=True)` (R266 uvicorn).  Plus System.CommandLine ships several stock validators: `AcceptExistingOnly` (file exists), `AcceptOnlyFromAmong(values)` (restricted values).
  - Critical extraction note: fracjson uses **C# COLLECTION INITIALIZER syntax** for RootCommand at CliParser.cs:300-333 — `new RootCommand(description) { opt1, opt2, opt3, ... }` adds all listed options/arguments to the root command via C#'s `IEnumerable.Add` pattern.  System.CommandLine's RootCommand implements `IEnumerable` so the initializer-list syntax calls `Add(opt)` for each element.  Documents the **collection-initializer RootCommand pattern** — distinct from Symfony Console's array-of-Definition (R282 peridot) and Swift ArgumentParser's `subcommands:` array (R283 yap).  C#'s approach is unique in REPURPOSING the collection-initializer syntax for CLI declaration.  Corpus's **first documented collection-initializer CLI declaration**.  **2 .NET CLI library variants documented now**: F# Argu (R284 fsix) + C# System.CommandLine (R286 fracjson) — .NET ecosystem CLI fully represented.
  - Hunt-skip notes: Many .NET tools available — Microsoft Build CLI, Azure CLI, PowerShell command parameters, Roslyn analyzers.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks as standalone binaries remaining unfilled.  More PHP tools / Dart Flutter tools / language CLIs for future rounds.
  - Blacklist: added `fracjson`.
  - Corpus 28,708 → 28,709.

- **R285 — luacheck source-direct rewrite (Lua static analyzer; Lua argparse library)** (1 file source-direct rewrite) — pivots from F# Argu (R284 fsix) into the **Lua language tooling cluster**.  luacheck is the **Lua static analyzer + linter** by lunarmodules — detects unused variables, undefined globals, redefined names.  Sister to selene (Rust Lua linter, in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Lua argparse CLI** — adds Lua to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (24 library variants now).  REWRITES the previously URL-cited `_luacheck` (was 52 lines incomplete) into source-direct 380+ line version with all 40+ flags + pattern documentation.
  - `_luacheck` (1-stem; lunarmodules/luacheck src/luacheck/main.lua; **Lua argparse library** by mpeterv; **40+ flags + 7 groups + 3 mutex pairs + 19 std values + 5 formatter values + method-chaining with `:` syntax + args/count 2-dimensional arity + `count "0-3"` range for `-q/-qq/-qqq` verbosity**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` line 1 (`local argparse = require "argparse"`)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` lines 24-35 (parser construction with help_max_width + positional args via `:args "+"`)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` lines 37-78 (filtering options group with method-chained option declarations)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` lines 81-101 (--std with 19 standard globals)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` line 147 (`:convert(tonumber)` value conversion)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` lines 187 + 196 + 228 (`parser:mutex(...)` declarative mutual exclusion)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` line 252 (`:count "0-3"` count range for -q verbosity)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` line 261 (`:hidden(true)` hidden flag pattern)
    * `src/luacheck/main.lua` line 264 (`:action(function() ... end)` inline closure action)
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **Lua's `argparse`** library by mpeterv at `local argparse = require "argparse"` — corpus's **first documented Lua argparse CLI**.  `argparse` is **the** most popular Lua CLI library — modeled after Python's argparse (R267 alembic) but adapted to Lua's syntax and idioms.  Used by luacheck, luarocks (Lua package manager), and most Lua tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-FOUR library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), F# Argu (R284), **Lua argparse** (R285 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 24 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Documents the **Lua method-chaining-with-colon-syntax CLI pattern**: `parser:flag("-q --quiet", "help"):count "0-3"`.  Distinct from Python argparse's positional-arg style (R267 alembic) — Lua's `:` syntax automatically passes the object as the first argument, enabling fluent method chaining without `obj.method(obj, ...)` boilerplate.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **Lua `:` method-call syntax** for CLI declaration — `obj:method(args)` is sugar for `obj.method(obj, args)` (passes obj as implicit first argument).  argparse returns `self` from setter methods enabling chained calls.  Documents the **Lua-specific colon-method CLI pattern** — distinct from other languages' fluent builder patterns (Rust clap builder R259, Dart args cascade R279, PHP Symfony Console imperative R282).  Each language has its own IDIOMATIC mechanism for builder-style chaining: Lua uses `:` syntax, Rust uses method-returning-self, Dart uses `..` cascade operator, Symfony uses `$this->` returning `$this`.  Corpus's **first documented Lua colon-method chaining CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`:count "0-3"`** at main.lua:252 for `-q/--quiet` — argparse's **count range** for repeated short-flag verbosity levels: `-q` (count=1) / `-qq` (count=2) / `-qqq` (count=3).  Each `q` in `-qqq` is counted separately by argparse's bundling logic.  Documents the **count-range repeated-short-flag verbosity pattern** — distinct from clap derive's `#[arg(action = ArgAction::Count)]` (R266 uvicorn uses this for `-v -vv -vvv`).  argparse's count-range is MORE EXPLICIT: declares the MIN-MAX repetition allowed.  Corpus's **first documented `count "N-M"` range syntax**.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`args` arity specification** at main.lua:34, 56, 62, 68, 74, etc. — `:args "+"` (one or more values), `:args "*"` (zero or more), `:args "?"` (optional), `:args "1"` (exactly one).  Combined with `:count "N"` for repeat occurrences: `:args "+":count "*"` means flag can appear any number of times, each time with one or more values.  Documents the **2-dimensional arity x count pattern** — distinct from argparse's `nargs` (R267 alembic — single dimension: values per occurrence) and from clap's `Vec<T>` field (R260 dua — auto-detected from type).  Lua argparse separates VALUES-PER-OCCURRENCE (`args`) from OCCURRENCE-COUNT (`count`).  Corpus's **first documented args + count 2-dimensional arity** pattern.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`parser:mutex(opt1, opt2)`** at main.lua:187 + 196 + 228 — argparse's declarative API for **mutual exclusion**.  `mutex(A, B)` means "A and B cannot both be set".  Used for `--config / --no-config` pairs and `--cache / --no-cache`.  Documents the **library-fn-based mutex** pattern — distinct from clap derive's `conflicts_with` attribute (R249 cairo-profiler), Ruby OptionParser's lack of built-in mutex (R280 reek manually handles), Symfony's `@ArgGroup(exclusive = true)` (R276 checkstyle).  Lua's approach uses a SEPARATE FN CALL passing the options to mutex together.  Corpus's **first documented function-call mutex pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`parser:group("name", opts...)`** at main.lua:37, 80, 144, 198, 204, 236, 243 — argparse's **declarative help-section grouping** via function call.  Each group bundles related flags under a named heading in the help output.  Documents the **function-call help-section grouping** — distinct from Cmdliner's `\`S` man-page sections (R271 dune), Ruby OptionParser's `parser.separator` (R280 reek), clap's `next_help_heading` (R251 torii), and Symfony's inheritance-based grouping (R282 peridot).  Lua's approach takes options AS FN ARGUMENTS, building groups programmatically.  7 groups in luacheck: filtering warnings, allowed globals, line length limits, config file options, file filtering, performance, output formatting.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`:action(function() ... end)`** at main.lua:264 — argparse's CUSTOM ACTION via inline Lua closure.  For `-v/--version`, the closure prints the version and calls `os.exit(exit_codes.ok)`.  Documents the **closure-based custom action pattern** in Lua — distinct from argparse's predefined action strings (`"store_false"`, `"concat"`, etc.).  Plus Lua's `function() ... end` is the inline function literal syntax.  Corpus's **first documented inline-closure action pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: luacheck uses **`:hidden(true)`** at main.lua:261 on `--profile` flag — argparse's method to HIDE a flag from `--help` output.  The flag still works at the CLI but isn't documented in help.  Documents the **hidden-flag pattern** in Lua argparse — convergent with R259 hyperfine's `.hide(true)` (Rust clap builder).  Pattern documented in 2 corpus rounds now (R259 hyperfine + R285 luacheck) across 2 languages.
  - Hunt-skip notes: luarocks (Lua package manager; also uses argparse) for future round.  C# System.CommandLine.NamingConventionBinder remaining unfilled.  Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks remaining.
  - Blacklist: `luacheck` already present.
  - Corpus 28,708 → 28,708 (REWRITE, no new file — R285 is a major source-direct rewrite of the previously URL-cited 52-line completion into a 380+ line source-direct version).

- **R284 — fsix source-direct (F# Interactive REPL extension; F# Argu library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Swift ArgumentParser (R283 yap) into the **F# language tooling cluster**.  FsiX is the **F# Interactive (fsi) REPL extension** by soweli-p — wraps `fsi.exe` with project/solution loading, daemon mode (JSON-RPC server), and library search path management.  Sister to other .NET CLIs (dotnet, fsi, paket).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented F# Argu CLI** — adds F# to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (23 library variants now).
  - `_fsix` (1-stem; soweli-p/FsiX FsiX/Args.fs; **F# Argu library**; **9 DU cases as flags + discriminated-union pattern + F# attributes on cases + tuple-typed multi-value flag + interface IArgParserTemplate help-text + [<Last>] trailing args**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` lines 1-4 (`module FsiX.Args` + `open Argu`)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` lines 5-6 (type aliases `Sep` and `Alt` for Argu attribute brevity)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` lines 13-22 (Arguments DU with 9 cases representing flags)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` line 16 (`[<Unique>] Daemon of host: string * port: int` — TUPLE-typed flag)
