    * `FsiX/Args.fs` line 17 (`[<Alt("-r"); Sep(":")>] Reference of assemblyFileName: string` — combined attributes)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` line 20 (`[<Alt("-l")>] Lib of folderList: string list` — LIST-typed flag)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` line 21 (`[<Last>] Other of args: string list` — trailing-args-after-`--`)
    * `FsiX/Args.fs` lines 23-32 (`interface IArgParserTemplate` with declarative `member s.Usage` pattern-match)
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **F#'s `Argu`** library at `open Argu` — corpus's **first documented F# Argu CLI**.  Argu is **the** standard F# CLI library — uses F#'s **discriminated unions** to model the CLI surface as a TYPE.  Used by FAKE (F# build tool), Paket (F# package manager), fsprojects/* (F# Foundation projects), and most F# tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-THREE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), Swift ArgumentParser (R283), **F# Argu** (R284 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 23 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Documents the **F# discriminated-union-based CLI pattern**: `type Arguments = | Sln of fileName: string | [<Unique>] Dir of workingDirectory: string | [<Alt("-r"); Sep(":")>] Reference of assembly: string | [<Last>] Other of args: string list` with `interface IArgParserTemplate` providing help text via pattern-match on cases.  Distinct from Rust clap derive's enum variants (R247+ sncast) — both use sum-types but F#'s discriminated unions allow ARBITRARY TUPLE TYPES in each case (e.g. `host: string * port: int` for a 2-tuple flag) while Rust enum variants need struct or tuple-struct forms.  F#'s approach is MORE FLEXIBLE for representing flag-value structures.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **F# attributes on DU cases via `[<Attribute>]` syntax** at Args.fs:15-21 — Argu's declarative attributes annotate each DU case to control parsing behavior: `[<Unique>]` (flag can only appear ONCE), `[<Alt("-r")>]` (alternative shorter CLI name), `[<Sep(":")>]` (value list separator), `[<Last>]` (must be the LAST positional; captures trailing args after `--`).  Plus attributes can be COMBINED with `;`: `[<Alt("-r"); Sep(":")>] Reference of ...`.  Documents the **F# attribute-based CLI metadata** pattern — distinct from Java picocli's annotations (R276) and Swift ArgumentParser's property wrappers (R283 yap) in surface syntax but conceptually similar.  F#'s attributes are CASE-LEVEL (annotate DU cases) while picocli's annotations are FIELD-LEVEL.  Corpus's **first documented F# attribute-on-DU-case CLI pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **`type Sep = CustomAssignmentOrSpacedAttribute`** and **`type Alt = AltCommandLineAttribute`** at Args.fs:5-6 — F#'s TYPE ALIAS feature to RENAME Argu attributes for brevity.  Without these aliases, the code would read `[<CustomAssignmentOrSpacedAttribute(":")>] Reference of ...` which is verbose.  Documents the **type alias for attribute renaming** F# idiom — unique to F# (and other ML-family languages).  Distinct from Rust's lack of attribute aliasing (you'd write `#[clap(...)]` always with the full name).  Corpus's first documented type-alias-for-attribute-shortening.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **TUPLE-TYPED flag values** at Args.fs:16 — `Daemon of host: string * port: int` declares a flag that takes **TWO VALUES**: a host string AND an integer port.  F#'s tuple types are first-class in DUs; Argu auto-parses 2 consecutive CLI tokens into a tuple.  User invocation: `fsix --daemon localhost 8080`.  Documents the **tuple-typed multi-value flag pattern** — distinct from clap derive's separate fields per value (R249 cairo-profiler uses two separate fields for host + port), Click's two separate `--host` and `--port` options (R266 uvicorn), and picocli's `arity = 2` attribute (R276 checkstyle).  F#'s approach is the MOST CONCISE for multi-value flags.  Corpus's **first documented tuple-typed flag values**.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **LIST-TYPED flag values** at Args.fs:20 — `Lib of folderList: string list` declares a flag taking a LIST of directories.  Argu collects multiple CLI tokens into the F# list.  Distinct from tuple-typed (fixed N values) — list-typed accepts ANY NUMBER of values.  Documents the **list-typed variadic flag** pattern — similar to clap derive's `Vec<String>` field type (R260 dua) but expressed via F#'s native list type.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **`interface IArgParserTemplate` with declarative `member s.Usage` pattern-match** at Args.fs:23-32 — implementing Argu's IArgParserTemplate interface on the Arguments DU type provides help text per case via F#'s native pattern-matching on the DU case to produce help text.  The `s` parameter is the DU value itself, with `_` discards for the case's payload (we only need the case discriminator for help text).  Distinct from clap derive's `#[doc = "..."]` doc-comment-as-help (R247+) and Click's `help="..."` keyword arg (R266 uvicorn).  F#'s approach uses LANGUAGE FEATURES (pattern matching, interfaces) rather than library-specific metadata.  Corpus's **first documented IArgParserTemplate interface pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: FsiX uses **`[<Last>]` attribute for trailing args after `--`** at Args.fs:21 — `[<Last>] Other of args: string list` captures all CLI tokens after the special `--` separator into a `string list`.  These are forwarded to fsi.exe.  Documents the **trailing-args-after-double-dash pattern** in Argu — convergent with R258 bevy's clap `last = true, name = "ARGS"` and Click's `nargs=-1` (R266).  F#'s `[<Last>]` attribute is the most TERSE form.  Used heavily in REPL wrappers (FsiX wraps fsi.exe) and shell-like tools.  Pattern documented in 3 corpus rounds across 3 languages: bevy (Rust R258), uvicorn (Python R266), fsix (F# R284) — strong evidence for cross-language trailing-args idiom.
  - Hunt-skip notes: FAKE / Paket (other F# Argu CLIs) for future rounds.  C# System.CommandLine, .NET System.CommandLine.NamingConventionBinder — different .NET CLI lib for future.  Lua argparse / Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks remaining unfilled.
  - Blacklist: added `fsix`.
  - Corpus 28,707 → 28,708.

- **R283 — yap source-direct (on-device speech transcription CLI; Swift ArgumentParser)** (1 file new) — pivots from PHP Symfony Console (R282 peridot) into the **Swift language tooling cluster**.  yap is the **on-device speech transcription CLI for macOS** by finnvoor — uses Apple's SpeechTranscriber framework for offline transcription.  Sister to other Swift CLIs (Amethyst window manager, R.swift).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Swift ArgumentParser CLI** — adds Swift to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (22 library variants now).
  - `_yap` (1-stem; finnvoor/yap Sources/yap/; **Swift ArgumentParser library**; **5 subcommands as types + @main entry point + property wrapper declarations + EnumerableFlag protocol + transform: closure + AsyncParsableCommand**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `Sources/yap/Yap.swift` lines 1-17 (top-level `@main struct Yap: AsyncParsableCommand` with `CommandConfiguration` declaring 5 subcommands)
    * `Sources/yap/Transcribe.swift` lines 7-46 (Transcribe subcommand with @Option/@Flag/@Argument property wrappers)
    * `Sources/yap/OutputFormat.swift` lines 5-9 (`enum OutputFormat: String, EnumerableFlag` — each case becomes a separate flag)
    * `Sources/yap/MCP.swift` line 9 (`commandName: "mcp"` override)
    * `Sources/yap/Listen.swift` line 14 (`AsyncParsableCommand` async run protocol)
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **Swift ArgumentParser** library at `import ArgumentParser` — corpus's **first documented Swift CLI** AND first use of the **ArgumentParser ecosystem**.  `ArgumentParser` is **the** standard Swift CLI library — official Apple-blessed (open source from the Swift project itself).  Used by Swift's own package manager (swift package), Apple's Vapor framework, server-side Swift tools, and macOS CLI tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-TWO library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), PHP Symfony Console (R282), **Swift ArgumentParser** (R283 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 22 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Documents the **Swift property-wrapper CLI declaration pattern**: `@main struct Yap: AsyncParsableCommand { static let configuration = CommandConfiguration(...); }` with field-level `@Option(name: .shortAndLong, help: "...") var locale: Locale = .current` declarations.  Distinct from Java picocli's `@Annotation` (R276) — both are struct/class-based with annotations on fields, but Swift property wrappers are LANGUAGE-LEVEL features (not runtime reflection like Java) AND ArgumentParser's wrappers generate code at compile-time via Swift macros.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **`@main` top-level entry point** at Yap.swift:5 — Swift's language-level annotation that marks a type as the program's entry point.  Combined with `AsyncParsableCommand` protocol, ArgumentParser auto-generates the main function that parses CLI args, dispatches to the appropriate subcommand, calls `run()`, and handles errors/exit codes.  Documents the **Swift @main + protocol-based entry point** — distinct from C's `int main()`, Rust's `fn main()`, Python's `if __name__ == "__main__"`, and Java's `public static void main`.  Swift's approach is **DECLARATIVE**: the protocol itself IS the entry point spec; the compiler/macro generates the actual main fn.  Corpus's **first documented `@main` annotation pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **5 SUBCOMMANDS declared as types** at Yap.swift:9-14 — `subcommands: [Transcribe.self, Listen.self, Dictate.self, ListenAndDictate.self, MCP_Command.self]` lists each subcommand's TYPE (`.self` returns the type metadata).  ArgumentParser inspects each type's `CommandConfiguration` at compile time to build the subcommand tree.  Documents the **subcommand-as-type pattern** — distinct from clap derive's enum variants (R247+ sncast), Click's nested decorators (R266 uvicorn), and decline's typed Command values (R278 fs2chat-client).  Swift's approach uses the TYPE SYSTEM itself for subcommand registration.  Plus `defaultSubcommand: Transcribe.self` declares the default behavior when no subcommand is given — running `yap input.mp3` is equivalent to `yap transcribe input.mp3`.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **`commandName: "mcp"`** at MCP.swift:9 — overrides the auto-derived CLI name (which would be `mcp_command` from the Swift type name `MCP_Command` via snake-case conversion) to be `mcp` instead.  Documents the **explicit CLI-name override** pattern in Swift ArgumentParser — useful when the Swift type name isn't the desired CLI name (e.g. due to Swift naming conventions like underscores or CamelCase-with-suffix).  Distinct from Rust clap derive's `#[command(name = "...")]` attribute and from R249 cairo-profiler's `#[clap(name = "cairo-profiler")]` (which served the same purpose).  Corpus's first documented Swift `commandName` override.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **`EnumerableFlag` protocol** at OutputFormat.swift:5 — Swift ArgumentParser's protocol that turns an enum into a **set of mutually-exclusive flag arguments**.  Each case becomes a SEPARATE FLAG: `--txt` / `--srt` / `--vtt` / `--json`.  The variable `outputFormat: OutputFormat = .txt` receives whichever flag was set.  Documents the **EnumerableFlag pattern** in Swift ArgumentParser — distinct from: (a) clap derive's `#[derive(ValueEnum)]` (R247+) which makes the enum a VALUE for a single option (e.g. `--format json`), (b) Click's `click.Choice([...])` (R266 uvicorn) — same pattern as clap's ValueEnum, (c) Symfony Console's `VALUE_REQUIRED` with suggestions array (R282 peridot).  Swift's `EnumerableFlag` is **UNIQUE**: it transforms an enum into MULTIPLE FLAGS rather than multiple VALUES of one flag.  Users write `yap transcribe --json input.mp3` instead of `yap transcribe --output-format json input.mp3`.  Documents the **2 enum-style CLI input patterns**: (a) enum-as-VALUE (one flag, multiple values), (b) enum-as-FLAGS (multiple flags, mutually exclusive).  Corpus's **first documented enum-as-multiple-flags pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **`transform: Locale.init(identifier:)`** at Transcribe.swift:14 — ArgumentParser's `transform:` parameter accepts a closure (or function reference) that converts the raw string CLI input to the target type.  `Locale.init(identifier:)` is a function reference that calls `Locale.init(identifier: rawString)` constructor.  Documents the **transform: closure pattern** for value conversion — distinct from clap derive's `value_parser` (R248 cairo-coverage), Click's `type=click.Path(exists=True)` (R266 uvicorn), and Cmdliner's `Arg.value & file` (R271 dune).  Swift's approach is the most TERSE — pass a function reference, ArgumentParser invokes it with the raw string.  Compile-time type-safe.  Corpus's **first documented Swift transform: pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: yap uses **`AsyncParsableCommand` protocol** instead of `ParsableCommand` at every subcommand — enables `async throws` in `run()` method.  ArgumentParser's main fn auto-awaits the async run method and propagates errors as exit codes.  Documents the **AsyncParsableCommand protocol** for async/await CLI execution — Swift's first-class concurrency support extended to CLI tools.  Distinct from Rust's `tokio::main` macro (which wraps an async fn) and from Python's manual `asyncio.run()` call.  Swift's approach is DECLARATIVE: change the conformance from `ParsableCommand` to `AsyncParsableCommand` and the library handles the async runtime.  Corpus's first documented Swift async CLI pattern.
  - Hunt-skip notes: Many Swift ArgumentParser CLIs available — Amethyst, R.swift, swift-lox, Fintoc, sergiocampama tools all good future candidates.  F# .NET CLI, Lua argparse, Erlang escript, Elixir mix tasks remaining unfilled.
  - Blacklist: added `yap`.
  - Corpus 28,706 → 28,707.

- **R282 — peridot source-direct (PHP BDD test framework; PHP Symfony Console library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Perl Getopt::Long (R281 cpanm) into the **PHP language tooling cluster**.  Peridot is the **event-driven BDD test framework for PHP** by peridot-php — modern BDD-style testing with describe/it syntax similar to Mocha (JavaScript) or RSpec (Ruby).  Sister to PHPUnit / Pest / Codeception.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented PHP Symfony Console CLI** — adds PHP to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (21 library variants now).
  - `_peridot` (1-stem; peridot-php/peridot src/Console/InputDefinition.php; **PHP Symfony Console library**; **11 options + 1 positional + class-based InputDefinition pattern + bitwise-OR enum combination + inline-style-markup help**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/Console/InputDefinition.php` lines 3-6 (Symfony Console imports)
    * `src/Console/InputDefinition.php` line 14 (extends Symfony's Definition base class)
    * `src/Console/InputDefinition.php` lines 19-37 (constructor-based setup with addArgument/addOption chained calls)
    * `src/Console/InputDefinition.php` line 28 (`VALUE_REQUIRED | VALUE_IS_ARRAY` bitwise OR for repeatable required-value option)
    * `src/Console/InputDefinition.php` line 23 (`<comment>(default: *.spec.php)</comment>` inline-style-markup)
  - Critical extraction note: Peridot uses **Symfony Console** library at `Symfony\Component\Console` — corpus's **first documented PHP Symfony Console CLI**.  `Symfony\Component\Console` is **the** de-facto standard PHP CLI library — used by Composer, PHPUnit, Laravel Artisan, Symfony Console applications, PHP-CS-Fixer, PHPStan, Pint, and most other PHP CLI tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY-ONE library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), Perl Getopt::Long (R281), **PHP Symfony Console** (R282 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 21 cross-language CLI library variants documented.  Documents the **class-based InputDefinition pattern**: `class InputDefinition extends Definition { public function __construct() { parent::__construct([]); $this->addArgument(new InputArgument('path', InputArgument::OPTIONAL, 'help')); $this->addOption(new InputOption('focus', 'f', InputOption::VALUE_REQUIRED, 'help')); } }`.  Distinct from Java picocli's `@Annotation` (R276) — both are class-based but Symfony's approach uses CONSTRUCTOR-METHOD invocation of `addArgument`/`addOption` while picocli uses ANNOTATIONS on fields.  Documents the **constructor-method-call CLI pattern** in PHP.
  - Critical extraction note: Peridot uses **`BITWISE OR` combining InputOption value-types** at InputDefinition.php:28 — `InputOption::VALUE_REQUIRED | InputOption::VALUE_IS_ARRAY` combines TWO flags: `VALUE_REQUIRED` (must take a value) AND `VALUE_IS_ARRAY` (repeatable, collects into array).  The default for an IS_ARRAY option is an array (`['spec']`).  Documents the **bitwise-OR enum combination pattern** in Symfony Console — distinct from clap derive's `Vec<T>` field type (R260 dua — type-system-driven) and from argparse's `action="append"` (R267 alembic — single keyword arg).  Symfony's approach is C-style: each value-type is a bit flag, OR them together to combine.  Documents the **InputOption value-type enum flags**: `VALUE_NONE` (boolean flag), `VALUE_REQUIRED` (must take a value), `VALUE_OPTIONAL` (value is optional), `VALUE_IS_ARRAY` (repeatable; combines via `|`), `VALUE_NEGATABLE` (auto-generates `--no-FLAG`; newer Symfony Console feature).  Corpus's **first documented bitwise-OR enum combination** in CLI declaration.
  - Critical extraction note: Peridot uses **Symfony Console XML-like style markup in help text** at InputDefinition.php:23 — `<comment>(default: *.spec.php)</comment>` embeds Symfony Console-specific style tags within help strings.  Symfony Console renders these as colored/styled output in the terminal.  Common tags: `<info>` (green), `<comment>` (yellow), `<question>` (cyan), `<error>` (red).  Custom tags can be defined via `OutputFormatter::setStyle()`.  Documents the **inline-style-markup pattern** in CLI help text — distinct from clap derive's plain strings (R247+) and from picocli's `${DEFAULT-VALUE}` / `${COMPLETION-CANDIDATES}` template interpolation (R276 checkstyle).  Symfony Console is one of the few CLI libraries with built-in TERMINAL-STYLED help output.  Corpus's **first documented inline-style-markup help pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: Peridot uses **Symfony Console's BUILT-IN globals** inherited from the Application base class — even though Peridot's InputDefinition only declares `--help` and `--version` explicitly, the parent Symfony Console Application provides several globals (`--help`, `--version`, `--quiet`, `--verbose`, `--ansi`, `--no-ansi`, `--no-interaction`).  Documents the **inherited-global-flags pattern** in class-based CLI libraries — distinct from clap derive's auto-`-h/--help` (R247+) and Click's `auto_envvar_prefix` (R266 uvicorn).  Symfony Console's globals are inherited via class hierarchy.  Plus Peridot REDECLARES `--help` and `--version` at InputDefinition.php:32-33 which OVERRIDES the inherited ones with its own short alias (`-h`, `-V`).
  - Hunt-skip notes: laravel/pint (PHP Symfony Console; SAME BINARY NAME as cloudflare/pint which is in corpus as Go-based Prometheus rule linter) — name collision deferred.  Composer / PHPUnit / Psalm / PHP-CS-Fixer / PHPStan all Symfony Console — could document a Symfony Console cluster in future rounds.  PHP picks remaining: pest / pestphp, hyperf, symfony itself, codeception.
  - Blacklist: added `peridot`.
  - Corpus 28,705 → 28,706.

- **R281 — cpanm source-direct (Perl module installer; Perl stdlib Getopt::Long)** (1 file new) — pivots from Ruby OptionParser (R280 reek) into the **Perl language tooling cluster**.  cpanm (cpanminus) is the **Perl module installer** by Tatsuhiko Miyagawa — the modern, fast, dependency-resolving alternative to CPAN.pm.  Used by virtually every Perl developer.  Sister to cpan / cpanfile / perlbrew / plenv.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Perl Getopt::Long CLI** — adds Perl to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (20 library variants now).
  - `_cpanm` (1-stem; miyagawa/cpanminus Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm; **Perl stdlib Getopt::Long**; **50+ options + bundling mode + suffix-syntax type spec + `!` auto-negatable + env-var-as-extra-argv + stdin pipe detection**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` line 15 (`use Getopt::Long ()`)
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` lines 148-237 (parse_options sub)
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` line 155 (`Getopt::Long::Configure("bundling")`)
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` lines 157-229 (50+ option declarations with suffix syntax)
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` line 152 (PERL_CPANM_OPT env var split)
    * `Menlo-Legacy/lib/Menlo/CLI/Compat.pm` line 231 (stdin pipe detection)
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **Perl's stdlib `Getopt::Long`** — corpus's **first documented Perl Getopt::Long CLI**.  `Getopt::Long` is **the** standard Perl CLI library — built into the Perl stdlib (no CPAN install needed).  Used by virtually every Perl CLI tool (cpanm, cpan, perlcritic, perltidy).  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWENTY library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), Ruby OptionParser (R280), **Perl Getopt::Long** (R281 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 20 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **Perl Getopt::Long's SUFFIX SYNTAX** at Compat.pm:157-229 — Getopt::Long encodes option metadata via SUFFIX CHARACTERS in the option name string: `'f|force'` = bare flag (`-f` or `--force`); `'n|notest!'` = `!` suffix marks AUTO-NEGATABLE (auto-generates `--notest` AND `--no-notest`); `'l|local-lib=s'` = `=s` suffix marks string value required; `'configure-timeout=i'` = `=i` integer value required; `'mirror=s@'` = `=s@` string array (repeatable; collects into arrayref).  Plus `|` separates short and long names.  Documents the **Perl Getopt::Long suffix-syntax-as-type-spec** pattern — distinct from clap derive's struct-field-type auto-detection (R260 dua), Click's `type=int` kwarg (R266 uvicorn), and Ruby OptionParser's inline-allowed-values-list (R280 reek).  Getopt::Long encodes ALL option metadata in the OPTION NAME STRING itself — most compressed CLI specification format in any language.  Corpus's **first documented suffix-syntax type spec**.
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **`Getopt::Long::Configure("bundling")`** at Compat.pm:155 — enables Getopt::Long's BUNDLING mode where short flags can be combined.  E.g. `-vqf` is equivalent to `-v -q -f`.  Without `bundling`, Getopt::Long would treat `-vqf` as a long option (which would fail).  Documents the **bundling-mode opt-in pattern** — corpus's first.  Distinct from POSIX getopt (R272 blink) which has bundling enabled by default and Go stdlib flag (R264 ctop) which doesn't support bundling at all.  Ruby OptionParser also doesn't have bundling.  Plus other Getopt::Long Configure options include `no_ignore_case`, `pass_through`, `gnu_compat`, `require_order` — each enables/disables specific behaviors.  Corpus's **first documented Configure opt-in mode**.
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **`!` auto-negatable on many flags** at Compat.pm:158, 160, 163, 176, 177, 179, 186, 187, 189, 190, 192, 198-204, 211-214, 225 — total of ~20 auto-negatable flags via the `!` suffix.  Each automatically gets a `--no-FLAG` form.  Documents the **5th auto-negatable pattern** now in the corpus: (a) Click slash-paired `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` (R266 uvicorn), (b) Rust clap explicit separate `--no-X` flag (typical), (c) Dart args auto-negatable OPT-OUT via `negatable: false` (R279 gpth), (d) Ruby OptionParser `'--[no-]flag'` explicit syntax (R280 reek), (e) **Perl Getopt::Long `!` suffix** (R281 cpanm).  5 enable/disable flag patterns NOW documented.  Corpus's **first documented `!` suffix for auto-negatable**.
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm reads **env var `PERL_CPANM_OPT` for extra ARGV entries** at Compat.pm:152 — `push @ARGV, grep length, split /\s+/, $self->env('OPT')` splits the env var by whitespace and appends to @ARGV.  Documents the **env-var-as-extra-argv pattern** in Perl — second documented occurrence (R269 shards had this for `SHARDS_OPTS` in Crystal).  CONVERGENT design pattern across Crystal + Perl — confirming this is a common cross-language Unix-tool idiom.  Pattern now in 2 languages (Crystal, Perl).
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **stdin pipe-detection** at Compat.pm:231 — `if (!@ARGV && $0 ne '-' && !-t STDIN) { push @ARGV, $self->load_argv_from_fh(\*STDIN); }` reads module names from STDIN if no args provided AND stdin is piped (not a TTY).  Documents the **stdin pipe-mode detection** pattern in Perl — 4TH language now (Rust irust R273, Rust koto R274, Dart gpth R279, Perl cpanm R281).  Pattern documented in **4 corpus rounds across 3 languages** — extremely strong evidence for the universal Unix-tool idiom.  Perl uses `-t STDIN` test (file-test operator that returns true for TTY).  Corpus's **first documented Perl `-t STDIN` pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: cpanm uses **sub { ... } callback handlers** at Compat.pm:157, 159, 164, 165, 166, 170, 171, 180, 181, 191, 193-197, 207, 219, 221-225 — ~25 callback handlers.  Used for: (a) updating multiple fields when a single flag is given, (b) computing default values via method calls (e.g. `$self->maybe_abs($_[1])`), (c) setting command dispatch (e.g. `'i|install' => sub { $self->{cmd} = 'install' }`).  Documents the **subroutine callback for complex flag handling** in Perl — distinct from simple scalar-reference binding (`\$self->{flag}`) which Getopt::Long auto-handles.  Plus cpanm uses callback-based subcommand dispatch — when `-i/--install` or `--info` or `--look` or `-U/--uninstall` is given, the callback sets `$self->{cmd}` to the appropriate subcommand name.  Documents the **callback-based subcommand dispatch via flags** pattern — unusual (most CLIs have explicit subcommand positionals; cpanm has subcommands AS FLAGS).
  - Hunt-skip notes: perlcritic / perltidy / cpan / podchecker future probes.  PHP Symfony Console (composer / phpunit) deferred — major remaining major CLI library.  Lua argparse / Erlang escript / Elixir mix tasks for completing language coverage.
  - Blacklist: added `cpanm`.
  - Corpus 28,704 → 28,705.

- **R280 — reek source-direct rewrite (Ruby code-smell detector; Ruby stdlib OptionParser)** (1 file source-direct rewrite) — pivots from Dart args (R279 gpth) into the **Ruby language tooling cluster**.  reek is the **Ruby code-smell detector** by troessner — static analysis for Ruby that detects code smells like Long Method, Long Parameter List, Duplicate Method Call.  Sister to rubocop (in corpus) / standardrb (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Ruby OptionParser CLI** — adds Ruby to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (19 library variants now).  REWRITES the previously URL-cited `_reek` (was 26 lines incomplete; had incorrect `--no-wiki-links` and spurious `code_climate` format) into source-direct 230+ line version with all 19 options verified.
  - `_reek` (1-stem; troessner/reek lib/reek/cli/options.rb; **Ruby stdlib OptionParser**; **19 options + `[no-]` explicit auto-negatable syntax + inline-allowed-values list + on_tail help-positioning + parser.separator section headers**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` line 40 (`@parser = OptionParser.new`)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 105-122 (configuration options: -c/--config / --smell / --stdin-filename)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 131-138 (formatter: -f/--format with inline allowed-values `[:html, :text, :yaml, :json, :xml, :github]`)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 152-192 (verbosity, location, progress: 6 `--[no-]flag` declarations)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 192-199 (sorting: --sort-by with `[:smelliness, :none]`)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 210-222 (exit codes: --success-exit-code / --failure-exit-code)
    * `lib/reek/cli/options.rb` lines 225-242 (utility: `on_tail` for -h/-l/-v at end of help)
  - Critical extraction note: reek uses **Ruby's stdlib `OptionParser`** at `OptionParser.new` — corpus's **first documented Ruby OptionParser CLI**.  `OptionParser` is **the** standard Ruby CLI library — built into the Ruby stdlib (no gem install needed).  Used by Bundler, rubocop, standardrb, and most Ruby tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to NINETEEN library variants: Rust (6), Go (4), Python (3), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), Dart args (R279), **Ruby OptionParser** (R280 — NEW).  Documents the **Ruby block-handler-per-flag pattern**: `parser.on('-c', '--config FILE', 'Help text') do |file| self.config_file = Pathname.new(file) end`.  Distinct from Crystal's OptionParser (R269 shards) despite the name similarity — Ruby's OptionParser is **the original**; Crystal's is inspired by it but uses different DSL.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 19 cross-language CLI library variants now documented.
  - Critical extraction note: reek uses **`'--[no-]flag'` EXPLICIT auto-negatable flag syntax** at options.rb:153, 160, 164, 168, 175, 186 — Ruby OptionParser's convention for declaring **both `--flag` AND `--no-flag`** in a single declaration.  The `[no-]` prefix in the long name tells OptionParser to generate both forms.  Documents the **Ruby `[no-]` explicit auto-negatable syntax** — distinct from: (a) Dart args (R279 gpth) auto-negatable OPT-OUT via `negatable: false` (default IS auto-negatable), (b) Click (R266 uvicorn) `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` PAIRED slash syntax, (c) Rust clap explicit separate `--no-X` flag.  Ruby's approach is OPT-IN syntactic sugar in the flag name itself.  **4 enable/disable flag patterns NOW documented**: (a) Click explicit slash-paired (R266), (b) Rust clap explicit separate (typical), (c) Dart args auto-negatable opt-out (R279), (d) Ruby OptionParser `[no-]` explicit syntax (R280).  Corpus's **first documented `[no-]` explicit auto-negatable syntax**.
  - Critical extraction note: reek uses **INLINE ALLOWED-VALUES LIST as 3rd positional** at options.rb:134 + 193 — Ruby OptionParser's signature is `parser.on(short, long, ALLOWED_LIST, help, help...)` where ALLOWED_LIST is a Ruby array of Symbols.  E.g. `parser.on('-f', '--format FORMAT', [:html, :text, :yaml, :json, :xml, :github], 'help') do |opt| ... end`.  When the user passes `--format yaml`, OptionParser validates that `"yaml"` matches one of the Symbols (`:yaml`) and converts it to the Symbol automatically.  Documents the **inline-allowed-values-list pattern** — distinct from clap's `value_parser = [...]` (R209), Click's `click.Choice([...])` (R266), Dart args's `allowed: [...]` (R279).  Ruby's approach is unique in passing the allowed list as a POSITIONAL ARG to the `.on` method rather than as a keyword arg.  Corpus's **first documented Ruby inline-allowed-values positional pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: reek uses **`parser.on_tail`** for utility options at options.rb:227, 231, 238 — OptionParser distinguishes between `.on(...)` (flag at CURRENT position in help) and `.on_tail(...)` (flag at the END of help).  Used for `-h/--help`, `-l/--list`, `-v/--version` — the "utility" flags that should appear at the bottom of `--help`.  Documents the **`on_tail` help-positioning pattern** — distinct from clap's auto-help-flag-position (always at top) and from Click's no-positioning-control.  Ruby OptionParser is one of the few CLI libraries that gives the developer EXPLICIT control over flag ORDERING in help text.  Corpus's **first documented help-flag-positioning control**.
  - Critical extraction note: reek uses **`parser.separator '...'`** at options.rb:106, 125, 132, 143, 211, 226 — OptionParser's API for adding visual section headers in the help output (e.g. "Configuration:", "Generate a todo list:", "Report format:", "Text format options:", "Exit codes:", "Utility options:").  Documents the **help-section-separator pattern** — distinct from Cmdliner's `\`S` man-page sections (R271 dune) and from clap's `next_help_heading` (R251 torii).  Ruby's `separator` is the most flexible: insert ANY string at the current position in help.  Plus the separator can include `\n` for blank lines.  Corpus's **first documented help-section-separator pattern in Ruby**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: rubocop (Ruby static analyzer; existing in corpus — could be source-direct rewritten in future round).  bundler / rake / many other Ruby tools.  Perl Getopt::Long (perl tools), Lua argparse, Erlang/escript args, PHP Symfony Console (composer/phpunit) — future probes.
  - Blacklist: `reek` already present.
  - Corpus 28,704 → 28,704 (REWRITE, no new file — R280 is a major source-direct rewrite of the previously URL-cited 26-line completion into a 230+ line source-direct version).

- **R279 — gpth source-direct (Google Photos Takeout Helper; Dart args package)** (1 file new) — pivots from Scala decline (R278 fs2chat-client) into the **Dart language tooling cluster**.  gpth (Google Photos Takeout Helper) is the **Dart-based Google Photos Takeout organizer** by TheLastGimbus — restructures the chaotic Google Photos Takeout zip into a clean year/month folder tree with album organization.  Sister to flutter-cli / dart-cli / pub.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Dart CLI** AND first use of the **Dart args package** — adds Dart to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (18 library variants now).
  - `_gpth` (1-stem; TheLastGimbus/GooglePhotosTakeoutHelper bin/gpth.dart; **Dart args package**; **10 flag/option declarations + cascade operator builder pattern + auto-negatable flags + allowed+allowedHelp restricted-values + stdin.hasTerminal pipe detection**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `bin/gpth.dart` line 29 (`final parser = ArgParser()`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` lines 30-64 (10 flag/option declarations using Dart cascade operator `..`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` line 30 (`addFlag('help', abbr: 'h', negatable: false)` — opt-out of auto-`--no-help`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` lines 45-51 (`addOption('albums', allowed: ..., allowedHelp: ..., defaultsTo: 'shortcut')`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` lines 56-58 (`addFlag('guess-from-name', defaultsTo: true)`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` line 67 (`parser.parse(arguments)` returns `ArgResults`)
    * `bin/gpth.dart` line 72 (`stdin.hasTerminal` pipe detection — convergent with R273 irust + R274 koto)
    * `lib/interactive.dart` lines 23-33 (albumOptions map with 4 values)
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **Dart's `package:args/args.dart`** library — corpus's **first documented Dart CLI** AND first use of the **Dart args package**.  `args` is **the** standard Dart CLI library — maintained by the Dart team itself.  Used by Dart's own `dart` CLI tool, pub, flutter, build_runner, and most Dart/Flutter tooling.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to EIGHTEEN library variants: Rust (6: clap derive/builder/v3/hand-rolled/pico_args/lexopt), Go (4: cobra/pflag/flaggy/stdlib flag), Python (3: Click/argparse/Typer), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), Kotlin clikt (R277), Scala decline (R278), **Dart args** (R279 — NEW).  Documents the **Dart args builder-pattern with cascade operator**: `final parser = ArgParser()..addFlag('help', abbr: 'h')..addOption('input', abbr: 'i', help: '...')`.  Distinct from picocli's annotations (R276 Java) and clikt's by-delegation (R277 Kotlin) and decline's applicative composition (R278 Scala).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 18 cross-language CLI library variants now documented.
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **Dart cascade operator `..`** at gpth.dart:30-64 — Dart's `expression..methodCall()` syntax invokes `methodCall()` on `expression` but returns `expression` itself (not the result of methodCall).  This makes chained method calls possible WITHOUT the method returning `this` (unlike Rust builder patterns or Kotlin .apply).  Documents the **Dart cascade operator** — a language-level feature distinct from builder-style fluent chaining in other languages.  Used pervasively for object initialization in Dart.  Equivalent in spirit to Kotlin's `apply { }` scope function and to method-returning-this builder patterns in Rust/Java, but operates AT THE LANGUAGE LEVEL.  Corpus's **first documented Dart cascade operator usage**.
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **auto-negatable flags with `negatable: false` opt-out** at gpth.dart:30 — Dart args **AUTO-GENERATES `--no-FLAG` form for every boolean flag UNLESS `negatable: false` is set**.  For example, `addFlag('cache-result', help: '...')` automatically creates BOTH `--cache-result` and `--no-cache-result` flags.  `addFlag('help', abbr: 'h', negatable: false)` disables the auto `--no-help` generation.  Documents the **auto-negatable flag pattern** in Dart args.  Distinct from Click's `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` PAIRED syntax (R266 uvicorn) which requires explicit slash-syntax declaration — Dart's approach is OPT-OUT (default is auto-generate the `--no-X` form for every flag).  Corpus's **first documented auto-negatable opt-out pattern** — a Dart-specific default-behavior choice.  Documents the **3 enable/disable flag patterns**: (a) explicit Click slash-paired `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` (R266), (b) Rust clap explicit separate `--no-X` flag (typical), (c) Dart args auto-negatable opt-out (R279).
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **`addOption('albums', allowed: [...], allowedHelp: {...}, defaultsTo: '...')`** at gpth.dart:45-51 — restricted-values option API in Dart args.  `allowed` accepts an `Iterable<String>` of valid values; `allowedHelp` accepts a `Map<String, String>` of per-value descriptions; `defaultsTo` sets the default.  When `--help` is shown, the per-value descriptions are rendered in a sub-section under the option's help.  Documents the **Dart args restricted-values API** — distinct from clap's `value_parser = [...]` (R209 slice), Click's `click.Choice([...])` (R266 uvicorn), and clikt's `choice("a", "b")` (R277).  Dart's approach uniquely supports **per-value help descriptions** alongside the allowed list — useful when each enum value has a distinct semantic.  Plus the `allowed` parameter can take a Map's `.keys` (Iterable) — convenient when the allowed values are derived from another data structure.  Corpus's **first documented allowed + allowedHelp paired API**.
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **`parser.parse(arguments)` returning `ArgResults`** at gpth.dart:67 — Dart args's parse method returns an `ArgResults` object that's queried via `res[key]` subscript syntax and iterated via `res.options` (Iterable of parsed flag names).  Plus `res.arguments` returns the leftover positional args (not consumed by flags).  Documents the **Dart ArgResults dynamic-map pattern** — similar to Python argparse's Namespace (R267 alembic) but returned as an iterable-map rather than an attribute object.  Distinct from clap derive's typed struct fields (R247+).  Useful for dynamic arg handling without typed structs.
  - Critical extraction note: gpth uses **`stdin.hasTerminal` Dart pipe detection** at gpth.dart:72 — checks if stdin is a TTY (interactive terminal).  Used to auto-enable interactive mode when no args are provided AND stdin is a terminal.  Documents the **`stdin.hasTerminal` Dart pattern** — same convergent design pattern as R273 irust + R274 koto (both Rust) which use `stdin.is_tty()`.  Pattern now documented in **3 corpus rounds across 2 languages**: irust (Rust), koto (Rust), gpth (Dart).  Strongly confirms this is the idiomatic Unix-tool pattern for "stdin pipe-mode detection".  Convergent design across languages — supports the hypothesis that "no args + pipe = read pipe; no args + tty = interactive" is the universal CLI idiom.
  - Hunt-skip notes: Dart args is also used by flutter / dart / pub / build_runner / many Dart tools — future rounds can extend Dart coverage.  Future probes: PHP Symfony Console (composer / phpunit), Ruby OptionParser (gems / bundler), Perl Getopt::Long (cpan / corelist), Lua argparse, Erlang/escript args, Elixir mix tasks (OptionParser).
  - Blacklist: added `gpth`.
  - Corpus 28,703 → 28,704.

- **R278 — fs2chat-client source-direct (Functional Streams for Scala chat client; Scala decline + cats-effect)** (1 file new) — pivots from Kotlin clikt (R277 svg2vd) into the **Scala language tooling cluster**.  fs2chat-client is the **FS2 (Functional Streams for Scala) Chat Client** — example chat application demonstrating fs2 + cats-effect streaming patterns, from the typelevel ecosystem.  Sister to fs2chat-server (same repo).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Scala CLI** AND first use of the **decline + cats-effect** ecosystem.  **JVM-language CLI trio NOW COMPLETE** via R276 picocli + R277 clikt + R278 decline cluster (Java / Kotlin / Scala).  17 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - `_fs2chat-client` (1-stem; typelevel/fs2-chat src/main/scala/fs2chat/client/ClientApp.scala; **Scala decline library + cats-effect**; **3 typed options + mapN applicative composition + mapValidated ValidatedNel error accumulation + IOApp entry-point**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `ClientApp.scala` line 4 (`import cats.effect.{ExitCode, IO, IOApp}`)
    * `ClientApp.scala` line 7 (`import com.monovore.decline._`)
    * `ClientApp.scala` line 9 (`object ClientApp extends IOApp:` — Scala 3 significant-indentation)
    * `ClientApp.scala` lines 11-28 (Command("name", "header") { Opts ... mapN { ... } } — applicative-functor composition)
    * `ClientApp.scala` lines 13-15 (`Opts.option[String](longName, help, shortName).map(Username.apply)`)
    * `ClientApp.scala` line 17 (`.withDefault("127.0.0.1")` default-value modifier)
    * `ClientApp.scala` line 18 (`.mapValidated(p => IpAddress.fromString(p).toValidNel("..."))`)
    * `ClientApp.scala` lines 30-39 (entry point: `argsParser.parse(args)` returns `Either[Help, T]`)
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **Scala's `decline`** library at `com.monovore.decline` — corpus's **first documented Scala CLI** AND first use of the **decline ecosystem**.  decline is a popular modern Scala CLI library built on **Applicative functors** (similar conceptually to Haskell's optparse-applicative R270 and OCaml's Cmdliner R271, but with Scala's cats typeclass library underneath).  Plus integrates with `cats-effect` for `IO`-based main fns.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to SEVENTEEN library variants (R276 added Java picocli; R277 added Kotlin clikt; R278 adds Scala decline).  **JVM-language CLI trio now COMPLETE**: Java picocli (annotations) + Kotlin clikt (by-delegation) + Scala decline (applicative-functor with cats).  Documents the **3 JVM-language CLI declaration styles**: (a) Java picocli `@Annotation` + runtime reflection (R276 checkstyle), (b) Kotlin clikt `by`-delegation + chained DSL (R277 svg2vd), (c) Scala decline `Command + Opts.mapN` typeclass-composition (R278 fs2chat-client).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 17 cross-language CLI library variants documented; JVM trio complete.
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **applicative-functor composition via `mapN`** at ClientApp.scala:11-28 — cats's `mapN` method on a tuple of Opts.  `(opt1, opt2, opt3).mapN { case (a, b, c) => ... }` is **applicative composition** — the tuple syntax uses cats's tuple-of-Opts → Opts of tuple lifting.  `mapN` then unwraps the parsed values and applies the combining function.  Documents the **Scala applicative-functor CLI composition** — distinct from Haskell's `<$> <*>` (R270 carp) and OCaml's `let+ ... and+` (R271 dune) in SURFACE syntax but IDENTICAL in semantics.  All three are applicative-functor patterns.  Documents the **applicative-functor pattern in 3 languages** now: Haskell (`<$> / <*>`), OCaml (`let+ / and+`), Scala (`(.., ..).mapN { .. }`).  Corpus's **first documented Scala mapN applicative pattern**.  Documents how the same fundamental abstraction (applicative functor) manifests differently across language syntaxes.
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **`Opts.option[T](longName, help, shortName)`** at ClientApp.scala:13-15 — decline's typed option API.  `Opts.option[String]("username", "Desired username", "u")` declares a `--username` option (and `-u` short) returning a `String`.  The TYPE PARAMETER `[String]` determines the parse type; decline auto-derives a `from-string` parser based on the type.  Documents the **type-parameter-driven option declaration** — distinct from clap derive's struct-field type auto-detection (R260 dua) and from Click's `type=int` kwarg (R266 uvicorn).  Scala's approach uses the type system explicitly: `Opts.option[Int]("port", ...)` for an Int, `Opts.option[String]` for a String, etc.  Compile-time type checking ensures the parsed value matches.
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **`.mapValidated(p => X.fromString(p).toValidNel("...error..."))`** at ClientApp.scala:18 + 22 — decline's validation API returning `ValidatedNel[String, T]`.  ValidatedNel (Validated with NonEmpty List of errors) accumulates errors across multiple options (unlike Either which short-circuits on first error).  Useful when you want to report ALL invalid options at once rather than one at a time.  Documents the **ValidatedNel error-accumulation pattern** — distinct from clap derive's Result-style first-error bail (R248 cairo-coverage `anyhow::ensure!`) and from R273 irust's manual unknown_args warnings.  Corpus's **first documented ValidatedNel error accumulation**.
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **`object ClientApp extends IOApp:`** at ClientApp.scala:9 + entry point `def run(args: List[String]): IO[ExitCode] = ...` at line 30 — Scala 3 significant-indentation syntax (`:` instead of `{`) AND `IOApp` from cats-effect (provides an IO-based main fn with automatic exit code mapping).  Documents the **Scala 3 indentation syntax** (vs legacy brace syntax) AND the **cats-effect IOApp entry-point pattern** (vs main fn returning Unit).  Distinct from typical Java `public static void main(String[] args)` and from typical Scala 2 `def main(args: Array[String]): Unit`.  cats-effect's IOApp lets the main fn return `IO[ExitCode]` which the runtime evaluates and exits with the proper code.  Corpus's **first documented IOApp pattern** (functional-effect-driven main fn).
  - Critical extraction note: fs2chat-client uses **`argsParser.parse(args)` returning `Either[Help, T]`** at ClientApp.scala:31 — decline's parse method returns Either where Left contains the help message (printed on parse failure / `--help` flag) and Right contains the parsed args tuple/struct.  Documents the **Either[Help, T] parse result pattern** — distinct from clap derive's panic-on-unknown (R247+) and from Click's exception-throwing (R266).  Scala's Either-based result is more functional — the caller pattern-matches on Left/Right and handles each case explicitly.  Cleaner separation between parsing and side-effecting execution.
  - Hunt-skip notes: fs2chat-server (sister tool) for future round.  tapir (typelevel HTTP API library; uses decline + CommandIOApp) deferred.  mantis (Ethereum client in Scala) uses decline — bigger CLI for future round.  scopt (older Scala CLI library) used by bfg-repo-cleaner / Chisel — alternative to decline for future round to document second Scala CLI library.
  - Blacklist: added `fs2chat-client`.
  - Corpus 28,702 → 28,703.

- **R277 — svg2vd source-direct (Shopify SVG-to-Android-VectorDrawable converter; Kotlin clikt library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Java picocli (R276 checkstyle) into the **Kotlin language tooling cluster**.  svg2vd is the **SVG-to-Android-VectorDrawable converter** from Shopify — converts SVG files into Android XML VectorDrawable resources for use in Android apps.  Sister to Android tooling (avocado / Android Studio's SVG-to-VD converter).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Kotlin CLI** AND first use of the **clikt** library — adds Kotlin to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (16 library variants now).
  - `_svg2vd` (1-stem; Shopify/svg2vd src/main/kotlin/Svg2Vd.kt; **Kotlin clikt library**; **4 flag options + 2 positional arguments + Kotlin `by` property delegation + chained DSL type conversion**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/main/kotlin/Svg2Vd.kt` lines 4-10 (imports from `com.github.ajalt.clikt.core.CliktCommand` and parameter packages)

    * `src/main/kotlin/Svg2Vd.kt` lines 18-19 (2 positional arguments via `argument(help).file(...).multiple()` chained DSL)
    * `src/main/kotlin/Svg2Vd.kt` lines 21-24 (4 flag options via `option(short, long, help).flag(default)` pattern)
    * `src/main/kotlin/Svg2Vd.kt` line 27 (`versionOption("0.1", help = "...")` in `init { }` block)
  - Critical extraction note: svg2vd uses **Kotlin's `clikt`** library at `com.github.ajalt.clikt` — corpus's **first documented Kotlin CLI** AND first use of the **clikt ecosystem**.  clikt is **the** standard Kotlin CLI library — authored by AJ Alt (also author of Mordant terminal library + ColorMath).  clikt is **Kotlin's spiritual equivalent of Python Click** (R266 uvicorn) — both are decorator/delegation-based DSLs for CLI declaration.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to SIXTEEN library variants (R276 added Java picocli; R277 adds Kotlin clikt): Rust (6: clap derive/builder/v3/hand-rolled/pico_args/lexopt), Go (4: cobra/pflag/flaggy/stdlib flag), Python (3: Click/argparse/Typer), Crystal OptionParser (R269), Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), OCaml Cmdliner (R271), C POSIX getopt (R272), Java picocli (R276), **Kotlin clikt** (R277 — NEW).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 16 cross-language CLI library variants documented.
  - Critical extraction note: svg2vd uses **Kotlin `by` property delegation** at Svg2Vd.kt:18-24 — the pattern `private val foo by option(...).flag(...)` uses Kotlin's **delegated properties** feature.  The `by` keyword tells Kotlin to use the right-hand-side (a `PropertyDelegateProvider`) as the property's getter implementation.  clikt's `option()` and `argument()` return delegate providers that wire up to clikt's parsing infrastructure.  Documents the **Kotlin `by` delegation CLI pattern** — corpus's first.  Distinct from picocli's annotations (R276) and from Python Click's decorators (R266).  clikt is the most idiomatic Kotlin CLI pattern because it uses Kotlin's first-class language features (delegation, type-safe builders, extension functions) rather than runtime reflection (like picocli) or decorator macros (like Click).  Documents the **3 JVM-language CLI declaration styles**: (a) Java picocli `@Annotation` + reflection (R276 checkstyle), (b) Kotlin clikt `by`-delegation + type-safe builders (R277 svg2vd), (c) Scala `case class` + cats-effect (deferred for future round).
  - Critical extraction note: svg2vd uses **chained DSL method-call type conversion** at Svg2Vd.kt:18-19 — `argument(help = "SVG files").file(exists = true, folderOkay = false).multiple()` chains 3 calls: (1) `argument(help = "...")` declares positional arg, (2) `.file(exists = true, folderOkay = false)` converts to `File` type with file-existence and type validation, (3) `.multiple()` collects into `List<File>`.  Documents the **clikt method-chaining DSL** for type conversion + arity + validation.  Distinct from Java picocli's enum-based type detection (R276) and from Click's `type=click.Path(exists=True)` parameter (R266 uvicorn).  clikt's chained-method approach is Kotlin-idiomatic and lets each method specialize the previous one's type.  Each `.method()` returns a new delegate provider that wraps the previous one's behavior.  Corpus's **first documented chained DSL method-call type conversion**.
  - Critical extraction note: svg2vd uses **`.flag(default = false)`** at Svg2Vd.kt:21-24 — clikt's `.flag()` modifier converts a regular option (which takes a value) into a boolean flag (no value; sets `true` when present).  The `default = false` is redundant (Boolean's default is false) but explicit for clarity.  Documents the **clikt option-to-flag conversion** — distinct from clap's `bool` field type (R260 dua auto-detects `bool` as flag) and from argparse's `action="store_true"` (R267 alembic).  clikt requires the explicit `.flag()` modifier to turn an option into a boolean flag (vs the default of taking a value).  Documents the **3 boolean-flag declaration patterns**: (a) auto-detect from type (clap), (b) explicit action keyword (argparse), (c) chained `.flag()` modifier (clikt).
  - Critical extraction note: svg2vd uses **`versionOption("0.1", help = "...")`** at Svg2Vd.kt:27 inside the `init { }` block — clikt's helper method that adds a `--version` option to the command.  The helper takes a version string AND an eager-callback (similar to Click's `is_eager=True`).  Documents the **clikt version-helper pattern** — distinct from picocli's `mixinStandardHelpOptions = true` (R276 checkstyle) and Click's `callback=print_version` (R266 uvicorn).  clikt's pattern: call `versionOption(...)` once in `init` block; clikt auto-adds `--version` and handles the early-exit.  Plus clikt auto-adds `--help` (no extra config needed).  Corpus's **first documented `versionOption` clikt helper**.  Documents the **4 standard-help-flag patterns now**: (a) clap derive auto-add, (b) Click manual decorator, (c) picocli mixin opt-in, (d) clikt versionOption helper.
  - Hunt-skip notes: leakcanary (square; Kotlin clikt with subcommands) for future round — would extend clikt subcommand documentation.  Many Android tooling using Kotlin clikt available.  metriql / PolymerLabs/arcs / other Kotlin tools deferred.
  - Blacklist: added `svg2vd`.
  - Corpus 28,701 → 28,702.

- **R276 — checkstyle source-direct rewrite (Java code-style verifier; Java picocli library)** (1 file source-direct rewrite) — pivots from Rust lexopt (R275 jiff-cli) into the **Java language tooling cluster**.  checkstyle is the **de-facto Java code-style verifier** — checks Java source against a configurable style guide (e.g. Google Java Style, Sun Code Conventions).  Used in CI pipelines for thousands of Java projects.  Sister to Java tooling (javac / javap / jshell / spotbugs / pmd).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Java picocli CLI** — adds Java to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (15 library variants now).  REWRITES the previously URL-cited `_checkstyle` (was 28 lines incomplete) into a source-direct 200+ line version.
  - `_checkstyle` (1-stem; checkstyle/checkstyle src/main/java/com/puppycrawl/tools/checkstyle/Main.java; **Java picocli library**; **17 @Option flags + 1 @Parameters positional + mixinStandardHelpOptions + template-string interpolation + enum-as-Option-type**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `Main.java` line 638 (top-level `@Command(name = "checkstyle", description = "...", mixinStandardHelpOptions = true)` on `CliOptions` nested class)

  - Critical extraction note: checkstyle uses **picocli** library — corpus's **first documented Java picocli CLI** AND first use of the **picocli ecosystem**.  picocli is **the** standard modern Java CLI library — used by Quarkus, GraalVM native-image, Helidon, Micronaut, Spring Boot's CLI, and most other enterprise Java tools.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to FIFTEEN library variants: Rust (clap derive/builder/v3/hand-rolled/pico_args/lexopt — 6); Go (cobra/pflag/flaggy/stdlib flag — 4); Python (Click/argparse/Typer — 3); Crystal OptionParser; Haskell optparse-applicative; OCaml Cmdliner; C POSIX getopt; **Java picocli** (R276 — NEW).  Documents the **picocli pattern**: annotation-driven CLI definition similar to Spring Boot's annotation-based configuration: `@Command(name = "...", description = "...", mixinStandardHelpOptions = true)` class annotation; `@Option(names = {"-x", "--long"}, description = "...")` field annotation marking a flag; `@Parameters(arity = "1..*", paramLabel = "...", description = "...")` field annotation marking positional args.  Distinct from Rust clap derive's struct-attribute approach (R247 sncast etc.) — picocli uses Java annotations which are RUNTIME-REFLECTED (slower than clap's compile-time macros but allows dynamic inspection).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 15 cross-language CLI library variants now documented.
  - Critical extraction note: checkstyle uses **`mixinStandardHelpOptions = true`** at Main.java:642 — picocli's **standard help-and-version mixin** that AUTO-ADDS `-h/--help` and `-V/--version` flags to the command.  Documents the **mixin pattern in picocli** — a reusable bundle of flags/options that can be added to any `@Command`.  Distinct from clap derive's manual `version = ...` + auto-`-h/--help` (R247+) and from Click's manual `--version` decorator (R266 uvicorn).  picocli ships several stock mixins (StandardHelpOptions, HelpCommand, etc.).  Corpus's **first documented picocli mixin** pattern.  Documents the **3 standard-help-flag patterns**: (a) clap derive auto-add (Rust), (b) Click manual `--version` decorator (Python), (c) picocli `mixinStandardHelpOptions` opt-in (Java).
  - Critical extraction note: checkstyle uses **picocli template interpolation in description strings** — `${DEFAULT-VALUE}` at Main.java:717 (rendered as the field's default value at help-text generation time) and `${COMPLETION-CANDIDATES}` at Main.java:745 (rendered as the enum's values at help-text generation time).  E.g. `description = "... Defaults to ${DEFAULT-VALUE}."` becomes `... Defaults to 1.` for `tabWidth = 8`.  And `description = "... Valid values: ${COMPLETION-CANDIDATES} ..."` becomes `... Valid values: XML, SARIF, PLAIN ...` for the OutputFormat enum.  Documents the **picocli template-string interpolation** pattern — distinct from clap derive's manual `default_value_t = ...` + auto-include in help (R260 dua) and from Click's `show_default=True` (R266 uvicorn).  picocli's approach lets the developer EXPLICITLY POSITION the default/enum values within the description text.  Corpus's **first documented picocli template interpolation**.
  - Critical extraction note: checkstyle uses **enum-as-Option-type** at Main.java:564-570 + 743-747 — the `OutputFormat` enum (XML / SARIF / PLAIN) is used as the field type of the `-f` option.  picocli auto-detects enum types and (a) restricts CLI values to the enum constants, (b) auto-renders the values via `${COMPLETION-CANDIDATES}`.  Documents the **enum-as-Option-type pattern** in picocli — equivalent to clap's `#[derive(ValueEnum)]` (R247+) and Click's `click.Choice([...])` (R266 uvicorn).  But picocli's approach is more minimal: just use a regular Java enum; picocli's reflection does the rest.
  - Critical extraction note: checkstyle uses **dual-purpose AST-printing flags** with mutual exclusion documented in help text — at Main.java:750-777 the `-t/--tree`, `-T/--treeWithComments`, `-j/--javadocTree`, `-J/--treeWithJavadoc` all share the constraint "can only be used on a single file and cannot be combined with other options" but the constraint is enforced AT RUNTIME (in validate()), not via picocli's declarative attributes.  Documents the **runtime mutex validation** pattern in picocli — distinct from R258 bevy's clap `conflicts_with_all = [...]` (declarative) and R250 sozo's `#[group(multiple = false)]` (declarative).  picocli has `@ArgGroup(exclusive = true)` for declarative mutex but checkstyle chose runtime enforcement for more flexible error messages.
  - Hunt-skip notes: many picocli users available for future rounds — Quarkus, GraalVM native-image, Spring Boot CLI, Micronaut.  Java tooling general: spotbugs, pmd, error-prone, palantir-java-format.
  - Blacklist: `checkstyle` already present.
  - Corpus 28,701 → 28,701 (REWRITE, no new file — R276 is a major source-direct rewrite of the previously URL-cited 28-line completion into a 200+ line source-direct version).

- **R275 — jiff-cli source-direct (BurntSushi jiff datetime library dev CLI; Rust lexopt token-level)** (1 file new) — pivots from pico_args (R274 koto) into the **lexopt token-level Rust CLI cluster**.  jiff-cli is the **development CLI for the jiff datetime library** from BurntSushi (the ripgrep / regex / aho-corasick / xsv author).  Sister to ripgrep / regex / xsv (all BurntSushi).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented lexopt Rust CLI** — adds a 6TH variant to the Rust CLI library taxonomy.
  - `_jiff-cli` (1-stem; BurntSushi/jiff crates/jiff-cli/; **Rust lexopt library** token-level Parser; **1 top-level subcommand + 6 generate sub-subs + Configurable trait pattern + manual Usage struct + per-subcommand USAGE constant**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `crates/jiff-cli/main.rs` lines 1-30 (main fn opens with env-based logger + dispatches to `cmd::run(&mut lexopt::Parser::from_env())`)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/cmd/mod.rs` lines 1-19 (top-level dispatch: USAGE string + match-on-cmd)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/cmd/generate/mod.rs` lines 1-40 (generate subcommand: 6 sub-subcommands)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/args/mod.rs` line 3 (`use lexopt::{Arg, Parser, ValueExt}`)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/args/mod.rs` lines 5-13 (Configurable trait)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/args/mod.rs` lines 15-72 (configure fn with iterate-through-targets-to-claim pattern)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/args/mod.rs` lines 79-91 (next_as_command helper)
    * `crates/jiff-cli/args/mod.rs` lines 121-193 (Usage struct + Usage::short/long formatters with textwrap word-wrapping)
  - Critical extraction note: jiff-cli uses **`lexopt`** library at `lexopt::Parser::from_env()` — corpus's **first documented `lexopt` Rust CLI**.  lexopt is a **token-level CLI parser** — even lower-level than pico_args (R274 koto).  lexopt gives you raw Arg tokens (`Arg::Short(c)`, `Arg::Long(name)`, `Arg::Value(v)`) and you build the entire parser yourself.  No flag declarations, no auto-help, no value parsing.  You write a `while let Some(arg) = p.next()? { match arg { ... } }` loop and dispatch manually.  Documents the **token-level CLI parser pattern** — between hand-rolled (R273 irust) and pico_args (R274 koto) in abstraction.  Extends the Rust CLI library sub-taxonomy to SIX variants (R273 added hand-rolled; R274 added pico_args; R275 adds lexopt): (a) clap derive, (b) clap builder, (c) clap v3, (d) hand-rolled (R273), (e) pico_args (R274), (f) **lexopt** (R275 — NEW).  lexopt is used by nushell, RustPython, regex, newsboat, ocrs, jiff — all serious Rust tools where the developer wants **fine-grained control** over arg parsing without clap's compile-time cost.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: Rust CLI library sub-taxonomy now 6 variants documented spanning the full spectrum.
  - Critical extraction note: jiff-cli uses **enum-based Arg representation** at args/mod.rs:21-58 — lexopt's `Arg` enum has variants `Short(char)`, `Long(&str)`, `Value(OsString)`.  The parsing loop matches on these via `match arg { Arg::Short('h') | Arg::Long("help") => ... }`.  Documents the **lexopt Arg-variant matching pattern** — distinct from pico_args's high-level `args.contains([short, long])` (R274 koto) and from clap's struct-attribute decorators.  lexopt's approach is closer to writing a parser by hand but with a tokenizer doing the dirty work.  Corpus's first documented enum-based Arg representation.
  - Critical extraction note: jiff-cli uses **`Configurable` trait pattern** at args/mod.rs:5-13 — a custom trait defining `configure(&mut self, p: &mut Parser, arg: &mut Arg) -> Result<bool>` + `usage() -> &[Usage]`.  Each subcommand defines its own struct that implements Configurable.  The central `configure` fn at args/mod.rs:15-72 takes `&mut [&mut dyn Configurable]` and iterates through ALL targets for each parsed arg, asking each "do you want this?".  First target to claim the arg wins.  Documents the **distributed-trait-dispatch arg-handler pattern** — corpus's first.  Useful when subcommand args are spread across modules and you want each module to own its arg handling.  Distinct from clap derive's centralized struct (which collects all args into one struct) and from pico_args's procedural style (which queries the parser linearly).
  - Critical extraction note: jiff-cli uses **manual Usage struct + Usage::short/long formatters** at args/mod.rs:121-193 — a custom struct `Usage { format: &'static str, short: &'static str, long: &'static str }` representing per-flag help text.  `Usage::short(&[Usage])` formats a two-column table (format | short description); `Usage::long(...)` formats with multi-paragraph long descriptions wrapped at 79 columns via `textwrap::wrap`.  The `-h` flag triggers short help; `--help` triggers long help.  Documents the **manual help-formatting infrastructure** pattern — lexopt has NO auto-help generation, so you build your own.  Distinct from R266 uvicorn's Click `show_default=True` (auto-included) and from R262 matugen's clap `help = "..."` per-arg (auto-rendered).  Plus jiff-cli's `-h` vs `--help` distinction — `-h` is short, `--help` is long.  Corpus's **first documented `-h` vs `--help` (length-based) distinction**.  Documents the **2 help-flag formats** (length-based vs same-format).  Reminiscent of `man -k` (apropos) vs `man -l` (long form).
  - Critical extraction note: jiff-cli uses **manual USAGE string at each level** at cmd/mod.rs:3-11 + cmd/generate/mod.rs:13-26 — each subcommand has its own USAGE constant printed via `anyhow::bail!(USAGE)`.  When `-h` or `--help` is given at any level, the subcommand's USAGE is printed and the program exits with an error.  Documents the **per-subcommand USAGE constant pattern** — distinct from clap derive's auto-generated help (which composes from doc comments) and from Cmdliner's man-page sections (R271 dune).  Corpus's first documented per-subcommand USAGE constant.  Demonstrates how lower-level libraries push more documentation burden onto the developer.
  - Hunt-skip notes: nushell (Rust shell using lexopt; massive) deferred.  RustPython (uses lexopt at settings.rs) deferred.  newsboat (RSS reader using lexopt) deferred.  ocrs (OCR tool using lexopt) deferred.  These are all major lexopt users — could expand the lexopt cluster documentation in future rounds.
  - Blacklist: added `jiff-cli`.
  - Corpus 28,700 → 28,701.

- **R274 — koto source-direct (Rust-based scripting language; pico_args minimalist Rust CLI)** (1 file new) — pivots from hand-rolled Rust (R273 irust) into the **pico_args minimalist Rust CLI cluster**.  koto is the **Rust-based scripting language** by irh — dynamically-typed; embeddable; designed for scripting and animations.  Sister to Lua / Janet (R270 carp Lisp) / Wren (small embeddable languages).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented pico_args Rust CLI** — adds a 5TH category to the Rust CLI library sub-taxonomy (now 5 sub-categories: clap derive / clap builder / clap v3 / hand-rolled / **pico_args**).  **Rust CLI sub-taxonomy NOW COMPLETE** via R273+R274 cluster (5 categories spanning heavyweight to lightweight to none).
  - `_koto` (1-stem; koto-lang/koto crates/cli/src/main.rs; **Rust pico_args library**; **10 flags + 1 script positional + script_args Vec<String> + stdin-as-input auto-detection (convergent with R273 irust)**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` line 77 (`pico_args::Arguments::from_env()`)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` lines 61-74 (KotoArgs struct with 12 fields)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` lines 76-116 (parse_arguments fn: procedural pico_args API)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` lines 79-88 (10 `args.contains([short, long])` flag checks)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` line 85 (`args.opt_value_from_str(["-c", "--config"])?`)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` line 90 (`args.subcommand()` for first positional)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` lines 92-100 (`args.finish()` for remaining)
    * `crates/cli/src/main.rs` lines 152-173 (stdin-as-input detection via `stdin.is_tty()`)
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **`pico_args`** library at `pico_args::Arguments::from_env()` (main.rs:77) — corpus's **first documented `pico_args` Rust CLI**.  pico_args is a **minimalist Rust CLI library** designed as an alternative to clap — much smaller (~1500 LOC vs clap's 100K+ LOC) and faster to compile (~50ms vs clap's ~2s).  Trade-offs: no auto-help generation, no validation, no subcommand routing, no ValueEnum.  Documents the **5-way Rust CLI library sub-taxonomy**: (a) clap derive (R247-R262, R268 — declarative, full-featured), (b) clap builder (R259 hyperfine — imperative, full-featured), (c) clap v3 (R261 replibyte — older clap), (d) hand-rolled (R273 irust — no library at all), (e) **pico_args** (R274 koto — minimalist library).  **Rust CLI sub-taxonomy NOW COMPLETE** via R273+R274 cluster: 5 categories covering the full spectrum from heavyweight (clap) to lightweight (pico_args) to none (hand-rolled).  Documents the **CLI library weight spectrum** — clap is the standard for full-featured CLIs; pico_args is the standard for "I want a library but minimal overhead"; hand-rolled is the minimum.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **`args.contains([short, long])`** at main.rs:79-88 — pico_args's flag-detection API takes a tuple of short and long names.  E.g. `args.contains(["-e", "--eval"])` returns `true` if either `-e` or `--eval` is in the args.  Documents the **pico_args tuple-argument flag-detection pattern** — distinct from clap derive's `#[arg(short = 'e', long = "eval")]` attribute declaration and from R273 irust's `match` `|` pattern.  Plus pico_args methods CONSUME the matching args from the internal arg list — so calling `contains` for the same flag twice returns `false` the second time.  Documents the **destructive-parse** pattern in pico_args.  Corpus's first documented destructive-parse CLI.
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **`args.opt_value_from_str(["-c", "--config"])?`** at main.rs:85 — pico_args's value-taking option API returning `Result<Option<T>>`.  The `from_str` suffix uses `T::from_str` to parse the value; alternatives include `from_fn` (custom parser fn) and `from_os_str` (raw OsString).  Documents the **pico_args value-parsing API trio** — corpus's first documented pico_args option API.  3 variants: `from_str` (FromStr trait), `from_fn` (custom closure), `from_os_str` (raw OS string).
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **`args.subcommand()`** at main.rs:90 — pico_args terminology where the FIRST POSITIONAL NON-FLAG ARG is called the "subcommand" (even though koto doesn't use it as a true subcommand — it's actually the script path / source).  Documents the **pico_args subcommand-as-first-positional** convention — distinct from clap derive's `#[arg]` positional (which is just a named arg) and from R273 irust's heuristic `.rs` extension check.  Plus pico_args has `free_from_str()` / `free_from_fn()` for typed positionals; koto uses `subcommand()` which returns `Option<String>`.  Corpus's **first documented `pico_args::subcommand()` API**.
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **`args.finish()`** at main.rs:93 — pico_args's "give me the rest" method.  Returns `Vec<OsString>` of all remaining args after the parsed flags/options/subcommand are consumed.  koto uses this for script_args (args forwarded to the script's `koto.args`).  Documents the **pico_args finish-and-collect-remaining pattern** — distinct from R263 lazygit's flaggy `flaggy.Args()` (Go) and from R266 uvicorn's Click variadic argument.  Plus koto's `finish().drain(..).map(|s| s.into_string()).collect::<Result<_, _>>()` converts OsString to String, bailing on non-UTF8 strings.  Documents the **OsString-to-String conversion pattern** for CLI args.
  - Critical extraction note: koto uses **stdin-as-input detection** at main.rs:167-172 — same as R273 irust!  When no script arg is provided AND stdin is NOT a TTY (i.e., it's piped), koto reads stdin to EOF and treats it as the script source.  Documents the **convergent design pattern**: both irust (R273) and koto (R274) — independently — implement the SAME stdin-as-input auto-detection.  This pattern is now documented in TWO corpus rounds, suggesting it's the **idiomatic Unix-tool pattern** for "no args + pipe = read from pipe".  3-way mode dispatch documented across both: (no args + pipe) → eval pipe, (no args + tty) → interactive REPL, (args) → load script.
  - Hunt-skip notes: numbat (existing in corpus) deferred.  janet, hare, gleam, odin-cli all existing.  Many fresh: lexopt-cli / structopt-bin / gumdrop-cli / argh-cli / tealdeer-bin / watchexec-cli / grex-cli for future rounds — could continue Rust CLI library taxonomy with each one (lexopt is another minimalist library).
  - Blacklist: added `koto`.
  - Corpus 28,699 → 28,700.

- **R273 — irust source-direct (cross-platform Rust REPL; HAND-ROLLED Rust CLI parsing)** (1 file new) — pivots from C POSIX getopt (R272 blink) into the **REPL tooling cluster**.  irust is the **cross-platform Rust REPL** by sigmaSd — interactive evaluator for Rust expressions and statements.  Sister to evcxr (Jupyter Rust kernel), rustyline (Rust REPL library), miri (Rust interpreter).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented library-less Rust CLI** — adds the "no library" category to the Rust CLI taxonomy (now 4 sub-categories: clap derive / clap builder / clap v3 / **hand-rolled**).
  - `_irust` (1-stem; sigmaSd/IRust crates/irust/src/args.rs + crates/irust/src/main.rs; **Rust hand-rolled CLI parsing**; **5 flags + 1 heuristic .rs positional + stdin-as-input auto-detection**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `crates/irust/src/args.rs` lines 5-14 (ParsedArgs struct with 7 fields)
    * `crates/irust/src/args.rs` lines 16-37 (parse_args fn: just a `match arg.as_str() { ... }` loop)
    * `crates/irust/src/args.rs` lines 27-32 (heuristic positional: `.rs` extension → script_path; else → unknown_args)
    * `crates/irust/src/main.rs` lines 13-16 (`std::env::args().skip(1).collect()` skips program name)
    * `crates/irust/src/main.rs` lines 19-46 (manual help text via println! with embedded multi-line string)
    * `crates/irust/src/main.rs` lines 47-50 (manual version handling)
    * `crates/irust/src/main.rs` lines 53-93 (stdin-as-input detection via `stdin.is_tty()` from crossterm)
  - Critical extraction note: irust uses **hand-rolled Rust CLI parsing** with NO library at all — corpus's **first documented library-less Rust CLI** at args.rs lines 16-37.  Documents the **hand-rolled CLI pattern in Rust** — distinct from R272 blink's C getopt (which uses the stdlib library) and distinct from every other Rust CLI in the corpus (which use clap derive / builder / v3).  Extends the Rust CLI sub-taxonomy to FOUR categories: (a) clap derive (R247-R262, R268), (b) clap builder (R259 hyperfine), (c) clap v3 (R261 replibyte), (d) **hand-rolled** (R273 irust).  Documents the **lib-vs-no-lib trade-off**: writing your own parser is more code but zero dependencies (great for small REPLs / simple tools); using clap brings auto-help, validation, ValueEnum, subcommand routing, but adds ~100ms to compile time and several transitive dependencies.  irust's parser is 38 lines total — feasible without a library because the CLI surface is tiny (5 flags + 1 positional).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: completes the Rust CLI library taxonomy.
  - Critical extraction note: irust uses **`|` pattern guards** in match arms at args.rs:21-25 — `"-h" | "--help" => parsed.show_help = true` is one arm with 2 patterns; Rust's `|` in a `match` pattern means "match if any of these patterns".  Documents the **Rust match-`|`-pattern for flag-alias handling** — the simplest way to declare short/long flag equivalence without a library.  Distinct from R263 lazygit's flaggy `String(&var, "h", "help", ...)` (which separates short and long params).  Corpus's first documented match-`|`-pattern flag aliasing.
  - Critical extraction note: irust uses **HEURISTIC POSITIONAL** at args.rs:27-32 — when an arg doesn't match any flag, irust checks if it ENDS WITH `.rs` (Rust source extension).  If yes, it's treated as a script_path; if no, it's added to `unknown_args` Vec for diagnostic reporting.  Documents the **extension-based positional disambiguation** pattern — useful when CLIs accept files of a specific type without requiring a flag prefix.  Distinct from clap derive's explicit `#[arg]` positional declarations.  Plus the code at main.rs:96-98 logs unknown_args to stderr WITHOUT exiting (warning, not error) — irust is tolerant of unknown args.  Corpus's **first documented extension-based positional disambiguation**.
  - Critical extraction note: irust uses **stdin-as-input detection** at main.rs:54-93 — `if args.is_empty()` AND `!stdin.is_tty()`, irust reads stdin to EOF and evaluates the content as a one-shot Rust expression.  This makes irust usable as a Unix filter: `echo "1 + 1" | irust` outputs `2`.  Documents the **pipe-mode auto-detection pattern** — distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's explicit file positional and from R265 moq's positional-args-required.  irust AUTO-DETECTS the mode based on whether stdin is a terminal.  Corpus's **first documented `stdin.is_tty()` mode-switching** pattern.  Used by many Unix tools (e.g. `cat -` reads stdin, `grep pattern` defaults to stdin when no file arg).  Pattern: `(no args + stdin is pipe)` → pipe mode; `(no args + stdin is tty)` → interactive REPL; `(args)` → load script.  3-way mode dispatch via state (args + stdin tty status).
  - Critical extraction note: irust uses **manual help-text string** at main.rs:19-46 — since there's no library to auto-generate `--help`, the developer writes the help text as a multi-line `println!` format string with embedded `{}` placeholders for VERSION and config_path.  Documents the **manual help-text pattern** — costs ~30 lines of code but offers full control over formatting.  Distinct from clap derive's `#[command(about = "...")]` auto-help.  Plus the embedded `{}` shows the dynamic config_path at runtime (where the user's config lives) — useful UX touch.  Documents the **3 help-generation patterns**: (a) library-auto (clap, Click, Cmdliner), (b) manual fn-builder (R262 matugen runtime version, R266 uvicorn print_version callback), (c) manual format-string (R273 irust).
  - Hunt-skip notes: evcxr (Rust Jupyter REPL — uses clap; would be a redundant pattern documentation) deferred.  janet (Janet language; existing in corpus — review for source-direct upgrade).  hare (Hare language; existing in corpus).  Many Java picocli / Kotlin CLIs for future round.
  - Blacklist: added `irust`.
  - Corpus 28,698 → 28,699.
