- **R272 — blink source-direct (tiniest x86-64-linux emulator; C POSIX getopt)** (1 file new) — pivots from OCaml (R271 dune) into the **C language tooling cluster**.  blink is the **tiniest x86-64-linux emulator** from jart (the cosmopolitan / ape / redbean author) — allows running x86-64 Linux binaries on any platform that supports POSIX (macOS / FreeBSD / Windows / WASM / etc.).  Sister to cosmocc / cosmopolitan / ape (all jart projects in spirit).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented C CLI using POSIX getopt(3)** — adds C to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (14 library variants now).
  - `_blink` (1-stem; jart/blink blink/blink.c; **C POSIX getopt(3)** via custom GetOpt wrapper; **10 short flags + preprocessor-gated help text + conditional-purpose flag + env-as-default-pre-getopt**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `blink/blink.c` line 93 (`#define OPTS "hvjemZs0L:C:"`)
    * `blink/blink.c` lines 95-131 (preprocessor-gated USAGE string)
    * `blink/blink.c` lines 266-323 (GetOpts fn with getopt-loop dispatch via switch)
    * `blink/blink.c` lines 268-278 (FLAG_* globals initialized from env vars BEFORE getopt loop)
    * `blink/blink.c` lines 313-318 (special cases: -v / -h / default)
  - Critical extraction note: blink uses **POSIX `getopt(3)`** via a custom `GetOpt` wrapper at blink.c:279.  Documents the **POSIX getopt(3) C CLI pattern** — corpus's first documented C CLI.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to FOURTEEN library variants (R269 added Crystal; R270 added Haskell; R271 added OCaml; R272 adds C): (a) Rust clap derive (R247-R262, R268), (b) Rust clap builder (R259), (c) Rust clap v3 (R261), (d) Go cobra (multiple), (e) Go pflag (multiple), (f) Go flaggy (R263), (g) Go stdlib flag (R264, R265), (h) Python Click (R266), (i) Python argparse (R267), (j) Python Typer (R268), (k) Crystal OptionParser (R269), (l) Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), (m) OCaml Cmdliner (R271), (n) **C POSIX getopt(3)** (R272 — NEW).  POSIX getopt is the **most primitive** CLI library: parses a single OPTS string (e.g. `"hvjemZs0L:C:"`) where each letter is a flag, and `:` suffix means "takes a value".  No long-option support in plain `getopt()` (GNU `getopt_long()` adds long options; blink uses the short-only form).  No subcommand support.  No help generation.  No validation.  Most C CLIs use getopt; some use the GNU extensions; some implement their own arg parser.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.
  - Critical extraction note: blink uses the **OPTS string `"hvjemZs0L:C:"`** at blink.c:93 — POSIX getopt option-string format: (a) Each letter is a flag (`h` / `v` / `j` / `e` / `m` / `Z` / `s` / `0`); (b) `:` suffix marks "takes a value" (`L:` for `-L PATH`, `C:` for `-C PATH`); (c) GNU extension: `::` for optional value (not used in blink); (d) GNU extension: leading `+` or `-` modifies parsing behavior (not used in blink).  Documents the **getopt OPTS string format** — corpus's **first documented POSIX getopt OPTS string**.  Distinct from clap's struct attributes, Click's decorator strings, argparse's add_argument calls.  Getopt's OPTS string is the most COMPRESSED CLI specification format — entire CLI surface in one string.
  - Critical extraction note: blink uses **preprocessor-gated help text** at blink.c:95-131 — the static C string USAGE has `#ifndef DISABLE_JIT`, `#ifndef DISABLE_STRACE`, `#ifndef NDEBUG`, `#ifndef DISABLE_OVERLAYS`, `#ifndef DISABLE_VFS`, `#ifndef TINY` directives interleaved with help lines.  When blink is compiled with `-DDISABLE_JIT`, the `-j` help line is REMOVED from the string and the `-j` flag itself isn't handled (the getopt switch case would still match but the action is conditionally compiled out via further `#ifdef`s).  Documents the **C preprocessor `#ifdef`-gated CLI surface** — corpus's first documented occurrence.  Distinct from Rust's `#[cfg(feature = "X")]` (R201/R250/R251), Go's `//go:build linux` build tags (R264 ctop), and Python's runtime `if HAS_FOO` checks.  C's #ifdef is the **earliest** (preprocessor stage) feature-gating pattern — happens before compilation.  Completes the **4-layer feature-gating granularity by language**: (a) C preprocessor `#ifdef` (R272 blink — earliest), (b) Rust `#[cfg]` (compile-time), (c) Go build tags (compile-time), (d) Python runtime checks (latest).
  - Critical extraction note: blink's `-C` flag is **DUAL-PURPOSE** at blink.c:302-311 — when both DISABLE_OVERLAYS and DISABLE_VFS are NOT defined, `-C PATH` sets `FLAG_overlays`.  When ONLY DISABLE_VFS is defined, `-C PATH` sets `FLAG_prefix`.  When BOTH are disabled, `-C PATH` writes an error message and does nothing.  Documents the **conditional-purpose flag pattern** — a single CLI flag's semantics depend on which features were compiled in.  Useful for compile-time-flexible CLIs (e.g. when shipping different variants of the same binary with different features enabled).  Corpus's **first documented conditional-purpose flag** (single flag with multiple distinct behaviors based on compile flags).
  - Critical extraction note: blink uses **env-vars as DEFAULTS for FLAG_ globals BEFORE getopt loop** at blink.c:268-278 — `FLAG_overlays = getenv("BLINK_OVERLAYS")`, `FLAG_prefix = getenv("BLINK_PREFIX")`, `FLAG_logpath = getenv("BLINK_LOG_FILENAME")`.  These are set BEFORE the getopt loop, so the corresponding CLI flags (`-C PATH`, `-L PATH`) OVERRIDE the env-var values when given.  Plus the env-var defaults are themselves wrapped in `#ifndef` directives so they only exist when the corresponding feature is enabled.  Documents the **env-as-default-pre-getopt pattern** — distinct from R263 lazygit's flaggy-default-as-env and R266 uvicorn's Click `auto_envvar_prefix`.  In C, this is implemented as imperative pre-getopt initialization.  Corpus's **first documented C env-as-default pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: blink uses **dual exit code from PrintUsage** at blink.c:315 (success: 0) and blink.c:317 (error: 48) — explicit `-h` returns 0; unknown options return 48.  The exit code 48 is unusual (not 1 or 2 like most CLIs).  Documents the **custom exit code pattern** — distinct from clap derive's auto exit codes (clap returns 0 for help, 2 for parse errors).  blink uses 48 perhaps as a convention for "CLI usage error" (similar to sysexits.h EX_USAGE = 64).  Corpus's first documented custom exit code on getopt unknown.
  - Hunt-skip notes: cgit / fastfetch-bin / neofetch-bin / cinit / mit-scheme (Lisp) / racket-bin (Lisp) FRESH candidates for future rounds.  cosmocc / cosmopolitan probe — sister tools to blink.
  - Blacklist: added `blink`.
  - Corpus 28,697 → 28,698.

- **R271 — dune source-direct (OCaml composable build system; OCaml Cmdliner)** (1 file new) — pivots from Haskell (R270 carp) into the **OCaml language tooling cluster**.  dune is the **OCaml composable build system** — the de-facto standard build tool for OCaml projects.  Sister to opam (OCaml package manager; uses Cmdliner too) and odoc (OCaml documentation; Cmdliner too).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented OCaml CLI** AND first use of the famous **Cmdliner** library — adds OCaml to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (13 library variants now).
  - `_dune` (1-stem; ocaml/dune bin/; **OCaml Cmdliner** library; **23 direct subcommands + 12 sub-command groups + Cmdliner pipeline syntax + OCaml `let+`/`and+` applicative-binding operators**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 3-46 (`let all : _ Cmdliner.Cmd.t list = ...` — list of subcommands split into `terms` (23 direct commands) and `groups` (12 command groups))
    * `bin/main.ml` line 19 (`Util.command_alias Printenv.command Printenv.term "printenv"` — explicit command alias via helper)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 60-96 (top-level `Cmd.info "dune" ~doc ~envs ~version ~man:[...]` with man-page-style documentation)
    * `bin/main.ml` line 98 (`Cmd.group info all` — composes terms + groups into the top-level group command)
    * `bin/main.ml` lines 105-118 (entrypoint with `Cmd.eval_value cmd ~catch:false` + custom exit code handling)
    * `bin/clean.ml` lines 13-46 (Clean command: `let+ builder = Common.Builder.term and+ paths = Arg.(...)`)
    * `bin/build.ml` lines 98-126 (Build command: 3 `Arg.(...)` with `opt_all` repeatable options)
  - Critical extraction note: dune uses **OCaml's `Cmdliner`** library — corpus's **first documented OCaml CLI** AND first use of the **Cmdliner ecosystem**.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to THIRTEEN library variants (R269 added Crystal; R270 added Haskell; R271 adds OCaml): (a) Rust clap derive (R247-R262, R268), (b) Rust clap builder (R259), (c) Rust clap v3 (R261), (d) Go cobra (multiple), (e) Go pflag (multiple), (f) Go flaggy (R263), (g) Go stdlib flag (R264, R265), (h) Python Click (R266), (i) Python argparse (R267), (j) Python Typer (R268), (k) Crystal OptionParser (R269), (l) Haskell optparse-applicative (R270), (m) **OCaml Cmdliner** (R271 — NEW).  Cmdliner is THE standard OCaml CLI library — used by dune, opam, odoc, merlin, utop, ocamlformat, and most of the OCaml ecosystem.  Cmdliner is INSPIRED BY Haskell optparse-applicative (R270) but uses OCaml's **applicative `let+` / `and+` syntax** introduced in OCaml 4.08.  Documents the **OCaml binding-operator applicative pattern**.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.
  - Critical extraction note: dune uses **`let+` / `and+` applicative binding operators** at clean.ml:14-15 + build.ml:100-126 — OCaml's syntactic sugar for applicative-functor binding (added in OCaml 4.08): `let+ builder = Common.Builder.term and+ paths = Arg.(value & pos_all string [] & info [...]) and+ aliases = Arg.(value & opt_all ... & info [...])`.  Each `and+` adds another sub-parser to compose into the full Term.  Documents the **OCaml `let+` / `and+` binding operator pattern** — distinct from Haskell's `<$>` / `<*>` (R270 carp) at the SURFACE syntax but IDENTICAL in semantics (both are applicative-functor composition).  OCaml's binding-operators are more readable for multi-variable patterns; Haskell's `<$> <*>` chain is more concise for single-record constructors.  Corpus's **first documented `let+` / `and+` binding operator** usage.  Documents the **applicative-functor pattern in two languages**: Haskell `<$>`/`<*>` (R270) and OCaml `let+`/`and+` (R271).
  - Critical extraction note: dune uses **`Arg.(value & opt_all ... & info [...])`** pipeline syntax at build.ml:103-114 — the `&` operator is OCaml's `|>` (pipe-forward) repurposed for Cmdliner argument construction: (a) `value` makes the arg's value optional (no error if missing); (b) `opt_all X default` accepts the option multiple times, accumulating values of type X with default; (c) `info ["name"] ~docv:"DOCV" ~doc:(Some "...")` metadata (long names, doc value placeholder, help text).  The chain `value & opt_all & info` builds up the argument spec by piping through each transformation.  Documents the **Cmdliner pipeline syntax** — distinct from clap derive's struct attributes (R247+) and from Haskell optparse-applicative's `<>` Semigroup append (R270 carp).  The Cmdliner pipeline is OCaml-specific but conceptually similar to Builder API patterns in other languages.
  - Critical extraction note: dune uses **`Cmd.group info all`** at main.ml:98 to compose 23 individual `Cmd.t` values + 12 sub-group `Cmd.t` values into a top-level group command.  Plus subcommands are organized into `terms` (flat list of commands) and `groups` (commands that themselves contain subcommands — e.g. `dune init project ...`, `dune rpc status`, `dune pkg lock`).  Documents the **Cmdliner `Cmd.group` composable subcommand pattern** — distinct from Click's nested `@click.group` decorator and from clap derive's `#[clap(subcommand)]` enum.  Each subcommand is a SEPARATE FILE in `bin/` (clean.ml, build.ml, install.ml, etc.) and its `command` value is collected in main.ml.  This is the **one-subcommand-per-file** convention common in OCaml CLIs.  Corpus's first documented one-subcommand-per-file structure.
  - Critical extraction note: dune uses **`man` field with ` \`S` / ` \`P` / ` \`Blocks` markers** at main.ml:70-95 + clean.ml:5-11 — Cmdliner's **man-page-style documentation** structure: ` \`S "DESCRIPTION"` starts a section; ` \`P {|paragraph text|}` is a paragraph (multi-line raw string); ` \`Blocks Common.help_secs` embeds a reusable block of common sections; `Common.examples [...]` renders an examples section from a list of (description, command) pairs.  Documents the **Cmdliner man-page documentation** pattern — generates a proper man-page formatted help output.  Distinct from clap's after_help/after_long_help (R247 sncast) which uses plain strings.  Cmdliner auto-formats sections, paragraphs, and examples into a structured help page that matches Unix `man` conventions.  Corpus's **first documented man-page-style structured help**.
  - Critical extraction note: dune defines **command aliases via a helper** at main.ml:18 — `Util.command_alias Printenv.command Printenv.term "printenv"` creates a `printenv` command that's an alias for the actual `Printenv.command`.  Documents the **helper-fn-based command alias** pattern — distinct from clap derive's `#[command(alias = "X")]` attribute (R250 sozo).  In Cmdliner, aliases require wrapping the term in a new Cmd.t with a different name.  More verbose but explicit.  Documents the **3 command-alias patterns**: (a) clap derive attribute (R250), (b) helper fn wrapping (R271 dune), (c) flaggy/Click attribute-based.
  - Hunt-skip notes: opam (uses same Cmdliner library) deferred for future round.  odoc / merlin / utop / ocamlformat all Cmdliner — consider for follow-on rounds.  Elixir mix already in corpus.  Erlang erl-bin / rebar3-bin deferred.
  - Blacklist: added `dune`.
  - Corpus 28,696 → 28,697.

- **R270 — carp source-direct (statically-typed Lisp compiler; Haskell optparse-applicative)** (1 file new) — pivots from Crystal (R269 shards) into the **Haskell language tooling cluster**.  carp is the **statically-typed Lisp** that compiles to C, designed for performance-critical applications.  Inspired by Clojure but with ownership tracking (like Rust).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Haskell CLI** AND first **applicative-functor-style** CLI library — adds Haskell to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (12 library variants now).
  - `_carp` (1-stem; carp-lang/Carp app/Main.hs; **Haskell `Options.Applicative`** library; **5 record fields composed via `<$>` / `<*>` applicative-style + 4 mutex modes via `<|>` Alternative + `<>` Semigroup for option attribute combination + `many`/`some`/`optional` modifiers**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `app/Main.hs` line 10 (`import Options.Applicative`)
    * `app/Main.hs` line 87 (`execParser $ Options.Applicative.info (parseFull <**> helper) fullDesc`)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 166-173 (FullOptions record with 5 fields)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 175-182 (parseFull Parser via applicative-style)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 184-204 (parseOther: 6 switch flags + 1 optional strOption)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 206-212 (parseExecMode: 4 mutex modes via `<|>` + default Repl via `pure`)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 214-215 (parseFiles via `many (argument str ...)`)
    * `app/Main.hs` lines 98-106 (runtime env-var check: CARP_DIR required unless --no-core)
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **Haskell's `Options.Applicative`** library — corpus's **first documented Haskell CLI** AND first **applicative-functor-style** CLI library.  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to TWELVE library variants (R269 added Crystal; R270 adds Haskell): (a) Rust clap derive (R247-R262, R268), (b) Rust clap builder (R259), (c) Rust clap v3 (R261), (d) Go cobra (multiple), (e) Go pflag (multiple), (f) Go flaggy (R263), (g) Go stdlib flag (R264, R265), (h) Python Click (R266), (i) Python argparse (R267), (j) Python Typer (R268), (k) Crystal OptionParser (R269), (l) **Haskell optparse-applicative** (R270 — NEW).  `optparse-applicative` is **the** standard Haskell CLI library — used by Cabal, Stack, GHC, Pandoc, etc.  Documents the **applicative-functor CLI pattern**: the CLI definition is composed via `<$>` (fmap) and `<*>` (apply) operators to combine independent flag parsers into a single Parser for the full options record.  Distinct from clap derive's struct-attribute (declarative), Click's decorator-stack (decorator-based), and argparse's imperative `add_argument` (procedural).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **applicative-functor composition** at parseFull (Main.hs:175-182): `FullOptions <$> parseExecMode <*> parseOther <*> many (strOption ...) <*> many (strOption ...) <*> parseFiles`.  Documents the **Haskell applicative-style CLI composition**: (a) `FullOptions <$> parser1` lifts the constructor into the Parser context (Functor's fmap); (b) `<*> parser2 <*> parser3 ...` applies each subsequent parser, accumulating arguments (Applicative's apply); (c) The order of `<*>` matches the order of fields in the record constructor.  Each sub-parser (parseExecMode, parseOther, etc.) is INDEPENDENT and composable.  Distinct from monadic composition (>>=) — applicative composition allows the CLI grammar to be statically analyzed (for help generation, parsing, etc.) because each parser doesn't depend on the result of previous parsers.  Documents the **Functor / Applicative typeclass pattern** — fundamental to Haskell idiomatic code.  Corpus's first documented Functor/Applicative-style CLI construction.
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **`<>` Semigroup operator for combining option attributes** at every option declaration (e.g. `long "no-core" <> help "Don't load Core.carp"`).  `<>` is Haskell's Semigroup/Monoid append; in optparse-applicative, option attributes (long / short / metavar / help / value / etc.) form a Monoid where they combine into a single Mod-context.  Documents the **`<>` attribute combinator** pattern — terse but powerful.  Distinct from clap derive's keyword-argument attributes (`long = "..."`, `short = 'X'`) and from Click's decorator kwargs (`"--name", help="..."`).  Haskell uses the Monoid abstraction to compose declarative metadata.  Corpus's first documented Semigroup/Monoid pattern for CLI attribute composition.
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **`<|>` Alternative typeclass for mutually-exclusive modes** at parseExecMode (Main.hs:207-212): `flag' Check ... <|> flag' Build ... <|> flag' BuildAndRun ... <|> Install <$> strOption ... <|> pure Repl`.  `<|>` chains options together; only ONE will match at parse time.  Plus `pure Repl` at the end provides the DEFAULT — if none of the explicit flags are given, ExecutionMode defaults to `Repl`.  Documents the **Alternative-functor mutex pattern** — distinct from R249 cairo-profiler's `conflicts_with = "list_samples"` (clap declarative mutex) and R250 sozo's `#[group(multiple = false)]` (clap group).  In Haskell, `<|>` is the natural way to express "one of these alternatives".  Plus `pure X` provides a default fallback — corpus's **first documented Alternative-with-default pattern**.  Cleaner than imperative defaults (e.g. Click's `default=None` + dispatch in fn body).
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **`flag'` (with apostrophe) vs `flag` distinction** at Main.hs:208-211 — `flag' Check ...` parses the flag and PRODUCES Check value if the flag is present; FAILS to parse if absent (unlike `flag` which provides a default).  Used in combination with `<|>` for mutex modes.  Documents the **`flag'` vs `flag` distinction** in optparse-applicative: (a) `flag default active mod` — provides both default-when-absent and active-when-present; never fails to parse; (b) `flag' active mod` — provides ONLY active-when-present; fails if absent (used in mutex with `<|>` so failure means "try the next alternative").  Corpus's first documented this subtle distinction.  Critical for understanding how Haskell composes alternative-style parsers.
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **`many (argument str ...)`** at Main.hs:215 — `many` from Alternative typeclass means "zero or more"; `argument str` is a positional argument with string parser.  Plus `many (strOption ...)` at Main.hs:180-181 for `--eval-preload` and `--eval-postload` (repeatable string options).  Documents the **`many` for repeatable args** pattern in optparse-applicative.  Distinct from clap derive's `Vec<T>` field (auto-detected) and from Click's `multiple=True` (explicit attribute).  Plus `optional (strOption ...)` at Main.hs:203 wraps in `Maybe`.  Documents **3 Haskell repeat/optional modifiers**: (a) `many` (zero or more), (b) `some` (one or more; not used in carp), (c) `optional` (zero or one, wrapped in Maybe).
  - Critical extraction note: carp uses **runtime env-var check** at Main.hs:98-106 — reads `CARP_DIR` from `SystemEnvironment.getEnvironment` AFTER CLI parsing.  If unset AND `--no-core` is not given, emits an error and exits.  Documents the **runtime env-var check pattern** in Haskell — distinct from R252 saya's Rust clap auto-bind via `#[clap(long, env)]` and from R266 uvicorn's Click `auto_envvar_prefix`.  Haskell's `optparse-applicative` does NOT have built-in env-var-fallback support; the application handles env var reading manually.  Documents the **trade-off**: cleaner CLI lib but more boilerplate for env-var integration.
  - Hunt-skip notes: cabal-cli / stack-cli (Haskell tools; same optparse-applicative library) deferred for future rounds.  mix-elixir / elixir-cli (Elixir tools) probe.  zig-cli (Zig language) probe.  Useful future probes for further language taxonomy expansion.
  - Blacklist: added `carp`.
  - Corpus 28,695 → 28,696.

- **R269 — shards source-direct (Crystal language package manager; Crystal OptionParser)** (1 file new) — pivots from Python CLI cluster (R266-R268) into the **Crystal language tooling cluster**.  shards is the **Crystal language package manager** — the equivalent of Ruby's bundler / Rust's cargo / JavaScript's npm for Crystal projects.  Sister to crystal (the Crystal compiler).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Crystal CLI** — adds a new language to the cross-language CLI taxonomy (Rust / Go / Python / Ruby / C / C++ / Shell / Crystal NEW).
  - `_shards` (1-stem; crystal-lang/shards src/cli.cr; **Crystal stdlib OptionParser** block-DSL; **11 BUILTIN_COMMANDS + 12 global flags + external subcommand discovery + SHARDS_OPTS env-var-as-extra-argv + Crystal compile-time macros**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/cli.cr` line 1 (`require "option_parser"`)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 5-17 (BUILTIN_COMMANDS array literal)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 19-40 (display_help_and_exit with heredoc help text)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 42-135 (Shards.run main fn with OptionParser block-DSL setup)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 48-72 (12 `opts.on(...)` flag declarations)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 74-133 (`opts.unknown_args` block for positionals + subcommand routing)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 124-129 (EXTERNAL SUBCOMMAND DISCOVERY via Process.find_executable)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 137-145 (cli_options merges ARGV with SHARDS_OPTS env; Crystal macro version branching)
    * `src/cli.cr` lines 154-167 (parse_args helper for build/run subcommand pass-through)
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **Crystal stdlib `OptionParser`** at `require "option_parser"` — corpus's **first documented Crystal CLI** (every prior corpus CLI was Rust/Go/Python/Ruby/C/C++/Shell).  Extends the **cross-language CLI taxonomy** to ELEVEN library variants: (a) Rust clap derive (R247-R262, R268), (b) Rust clap builder (R259), (c) Rust clap v3 (R261), (d) Go cobra (multiple), (e) Go pflag (multiple), (f) Go flaggy (R263), (g) Go stdlib flag (R264, R265), (h) Python Click (R266), (i) Python argparse (R267), (j) Python Typer (R268), (k) **Crystal OptionParser** (R269 — NEW).  Crystal's OptionParser is **block-DSL-based** — each flag is declared with a `opts.on(...) { ... }` call where the block is the handler.  Distinct from Rust clap's declarative struct/enum approach and from Python Click's decorator stack.  Crystal's idiom: imperative flag construction inside a `parse do |opts| ... end` block, with each flag's behavior expressed as a closure.
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **`opts.on("--jobs=N", "...") { |n| self.jobs = n.to_i }`** at cli.cr:67 — the value-taking flag pattern in Crystal OptionParser: `=N` in the flag spec means "this flag takes a value named N"; the block receives the value as a string; the block must do its own type conversion (`.to_i`).  Documents the **block-receives-string + manual-cast pattern** — Crystal's OptionParser does NOT auto-convert types (unlike argparse's `type=int`).  Plus the metavar `N` shown in `--help` output is just for documentation — no enforcement.  Distinct from R266 uvicorn's Click `type=int` (auto-convert) and R260 dua's `pub threads: usize` (Rust auto-convert via clap's ValueParser).
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **`opts.unknown_args do |args, options| ... end`** at cli.cr:74-133 — the block-DSL for handling **non-flag positional arguments**.  Crystal OptionParser separates "known" flags (declared via `opts.on(...)`) from "unknown_args" (the rest).  The block receives both `args` (positionals in order) and `options` (the original flag list).  shards uses this to ROUTE TO SUBCOMMANDS: `args[0]?` is the subcommand name; `args[1..-1]` are the subcommand's args.  Documents the **unknown_args-for-subcommand-routing pattern** — Crystal OptionParser has no built-in subcommand support, so subcommand dispatch is done via this block.  Distinct from clap derive's `#[clap(subcommand)]` enum (auto-routes) and from Click's `@click.group()` (auto-routes).  Corpus's first documented unknown_args block.
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **EXTERNAL SUBCOMMAND DISCOVERY** at cli.cr:124-129 — if the given subcommand isn't in BUILTIN_COMMANDS, shards tries to find `shards-<subcommand>` on `$PATH` via `Process.find_executable(program_name)`.  If found, it execs the external binary with the full ARGV.  Documents the **plugin-style subcommand pattern** — corpus's first documented occurrence (well-known in the wild: git extensions, cargo subcommands, gh extensions).  Distinct from clap derive's `#[command(external_subcommand)]` attribute (which auto-handles this) — shards implements the discovery manually.  Documents the **2 external-subcommand patterns**: (a) declarative via clap attribute, (b) manual via `find_executable` + `exec`.  Adding a new subcommand to shards is as simple as putting a `shards-foo` binary on PATH.
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **`SHARDS_OPTS` env var as additional CLI options source** at cli.cr:140 — `ENV.fetch("SHARDS_OPTS", "").split` (or `Process.parse_arguments(...)` in Crystal 1.0+) returns a list of extra flags that get appended to `ARGV.dup`.  So `SHARDS_OPTS="--no-color --jobs=4" shards install` is equivalent to `shards --no-color --jobs=4 install`.  Documents the **env-var-as-extra-argv pattern** — distinct from R252 saya's per-flag `env =` (Rust auto-bind one env var per flag) and R266 uvicorn's `auto_envvar_prefix` (Click auto-bind per option).  Shards' approach is a SINGLE env var that contributes MULTIPLE flags via splitting.  Useful when users want to set a constant set of preferences once.  Corpus's **first documented single-env-var-as-argv-additions pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: shards uses **Crystal MACROS at compile time** at cli.cr:139-143 — `{% if compare_versions(Crystal::VERSION, "1.0.0-0") > 0 %} ... {% else %} ... {% end %}` is a COMPILE-TIME version check that selects between two code paths depending on the Crystal compiler version.  Documents the **Crystal macro compile-time branching** pattern — distinct from Rust's `#[cfg(version("1.0"))]` (also compile-time) and from runtime version checks.  Crystal's `{% ... %}` is the macro syntax; the chosen branch is interpolated into the compiled code.  Plus the use of `Process.parse_arguments(...)` is itself a Crystal 1.0+ feature.  Corpus's **first documented Crystal macro pattern** in a CLI.
  - Hunt-skip notes: cabal-cli / stack-cli (Haskell tools) / mix-elixir / crystal-lang compiler / amber-lang / carp FRESH candidates for future rounds.
  - Blacklist: added `shards`.
  - Corpus 28,694 → 28,695.

- **R268 — typer source-direct (modern type-hint-driven Python CLI library; Typer)** (1 file new) — **COMPLETES** the 3-cluster Python CLI library documentation series (R266 Click uvicorn + R267 argparse alembic + R268 Typer typer).  typer is both the **modern type-hint-driven Python CLI library** from tiangolo (FastAPI author) AND the binary name for typer-cli (auto-generates CLIs from Python Typer apps in arbitrary files/modules).  Typer is built on Click but with cleaner ergonomics: function parameters with type hints become CLI args/options automatically, defaults become CLI defaults, no decorator stacking.  Sister to FastAPI / litestar / pydantic-cli.
  - `_typer` (1-stem; fastapi/typer typer/cli.py; **Python Typer library** (built on Click); **dynamically-injected `run` subcommand + nested Typer app via add_typer + factory-sentinel pattern + Path validation kwargs**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 7-8 (`import typer; import typer.core`)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 17-19 (Top-level `app = typer.Typer()` + nested `utils_app = typer.Typer()` + `app.add_typer(utils_app, name="utils")`)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 54-69 (TyperCLIGroup subclass overrides list_commands/get_command/invoke for dynamic subcommand injection)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 160-183 (main `@app.callback` with `cls=TyperCLIGroup, no_args_is_help=True`)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 278-313 (`@utils_app.command()` for `docs` with file_okay/dir_okay Path validators)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 33-51 (maybe_update_state side effect: parses ctx.params into module-level State)
    * `typer/cli.py` lines 67-69 (maybe_add_run + maybe_update_state called from TyperCLIGroup at EVERY parse phase)
  - Critical extraction note: typer uses the **Typer library** at `import typer` — corpus's **first documented Typer CLI**.  **COMPLETES the 3-way Python CLI library taxonomy** (NOW COMPLETE via R266+R267+R268 cluster): (a) argparse (R267 alembic — Python stdlib), (b) Click (R266 uvicorn — decorator-based), (c) **Typer** (R268 typer — type-hint-driven; built on Click; modern).  Documents the **Typer pattern**: define function params with type hints (`path_or_module: str`); use `typer.Argument(default)` for positionals; use `typer.Option(default, "--name", help="...")` for flags; default values become CLI defaults; type hints become CLI types (str/int/bool/Path/etc.).  Distinct from Click's `@click.option/@click.argument` decorator stack.  Typer wraps Click's mechanism but presents a cleaner API.  Distinct from argparse's imperative `parser.add_argument(...)` chains.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**: 3-way Python CLI taxonomy now COMPLETE across R266+R267+R268.
  - Critical extraction note: typer uses **`app = typer.Typer()` + `app.add_typer(utils_app, name="utils")`** at cli.py:17-19 — the **nested-Typer-app pattern** for subcommand groups.  `typer.Typer()` creates a CLI app instance; `add_typer(sub_app, name=...)` mounts the sub-app as a subcommand group.  Used for `typer utils docs ...` (where `utils` is the mounted group and `docs` is the `@utils_app.command()`).  Documents the **declarative-mount subcommand grouping** — distinct from Click's `@click.group()` decorator and from argparse's `add_subparsers()` + `add_parser()`.  Typer's approach: each `Typer()` instance is a fully-formed CLI; you compose them via `add_typer`.  Corpus's **first documented add_typer-mounted subcommand group**.
  - Critical extraction note: typer's CLI has a **DYNAMICALLY INJECTED `run` subcommand** via the custom `TyperCLIGroup` class at cli.py:54-69 — when `typer SOMEMODULE.py` is invoked, `TyperCLIGroup`'s overridden `list_commands` / `get_command` / `invoke` methods call `maybe_add_run_to_cli` which dynamically adds a `run` subcommand wrapping the user's discovered Typer app from the file/module.  So the CLI is: `typer myapp.py run --help` shows the user's Typer app's commands.  Documents the **runtime-injected subcommand pattern** — corpus's first.  The subcommand list is NOT FIXED at startup; depends on parsed positional `path_or_module`.  Powerful for "meta-CLI" tools that wrap arbitrary user CLIs.  Distinct from R267 alembic's introspection-driven registration (which happens ONCE at startup) — Typer-CLI's injection happens AT EVERY PARSE PHASE via the overridden Click Group methods.  Documents the **2 dynamic CLI patterns**: (a) startup-time introspection registration (R267 alembic), (b) every-parse-phase injection (R268 typer).
  - Critical extraction note: typer uses **`typer.Argument` / `typer.Option` factory functions** at cli.py:164-172 — `typer.Argument(None)` creates a positional with default `None`; `typer.Option(False, "--version", ... callback=print_version)` creates an option.  These are FACTORY FUNCTIONS that return special sentinel values; Typer's main fn introspects parameter defaults to discover CLI structure.  Documents the **factory-default sentinel pattern** in Typer — distinct from Click's `@click.option` decorator (which stacks on the function) and from argparse's `add_argument` calls.  Typer's sentinel-default approach lets the CLI definition be expressed IN THE FUNCTION SIGNATURE itself, not in separate decorators.  Corpus's **first documented Typer factory-sentinel pattern**.  Allows mixing optional and required args with type hints AND defaults that double as CLI metadata.
  - Critical extraction note: typer's `docs` subcommand uses **`typer.Option(None, ... file_okay=True, dir_okay=False)`** at cli.py:282-287 — the `--output` option accepts a Path with VALIDATION: file_okay=True (file is OK), dir_okay=False (dir is NOT OK).  Documents Typer's **Path-validation-by-keyword-args** pattern — when the parameter type is `Path | None`, Typer auto-detects it's a path and the additional `file_okay` / `dir_okay` / `exists` / `writable` / `readable` kwargs control validation behavior.  Distinct from R266 uvicorn's `type=click.Path(exists=True)` (Click explicit wrapper) — Typer's Path validation kwargs are passed DIRECTLY to typer.Option.  Corpus's **first documented Typer Path validation pattern**.  Reduces boilerplate vs Click.
  - Critical extraction note: typer's `--version` uses **the same eager callback pattern as Click** at cli.py:167-172 + 153-157 — `callback=print_version` with `print_version` raising `typer.Exit()` after echoing.  Documents how Typer's `--version` is implemented via Click's eager-callback under the hood (since Typer is built on Click).  Distinct from R266 uvicorn's explicit `is_eager=True, expose_value=False` — Typer wraps these as defaults of the Option factory.  Documents the **Typer-as-Click-wrapper** observation: every Click feature is accessible through Typer with different surface syntax.
  - Critical extraction note: typer uses **`no_args_is_help=True`** on the main callback at cli.py:160 — when the CLI is invoked with NO args (just `typer`), the help text is shown instead of running the callback fn.  Documents the **no_args_is_help shorthand** — Click has the same option as `@click.command(..., context_settings={...})`; Typer exposes it directly on the callback decorator.  Useful for top-level CLIs where running with no args is meaningless.  Corpus's first documented `no_args_is_help` attribute.
  - Hunt-skip notes: marimo / litestar already in corpus.  mkdocs-cli / sqlmodel-cli / pre-commit-hooks / litestar-cli / bento-cli / openinterpreter / inquirer FRESH for future rounds.
  - Blacklist: added `typer`.
  - Corpus 28,693 → 28,694.

- **R267 — alembic source-direct (SQLAlchemy database migrations; Python argparse + introspection)** (1 file new) — pivots from Python Click (R266 uvicorn) into the **argparse + introspection** subspace of the Python CLI taxonomy.  alembic is the **SQLAlchemy database migration tool** — the de-facto Python ORM migration system for SQLAlchemy-based projects.  Used by FastAPI / Flask / Django (in async mode) / Litestar projects.  Sister to Django's `manage.py migrate` / Rails' `db:migrate` / Knex.js migrations.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Python argparse CLI** — completes the **3-way Python CLI library taxonomy** (argparse / Click / Typer).
  - `_alembic` (1-stem; sqlalchemy/alembic alembic/config.py + alembic/command.py; **Python argparse + introspection-driven subcommand registration**; **15 subcommands + 6 global flags + 18 shared kwarg metadata + 3 positional metadata + per-command positional translation**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `alembic/config.py` line 3 (`from argparse import ArgumentParser`)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 690-952 (CommandLine class with introspection-driven registration)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 696-836 (_KWARGS_OPTS metadata dict for 18 kwargs)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 837-845 (_POSITIONAL_OPTS metadata dict for 3 positionals)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 846-848 (_POSITIONAL_TRANSLATIONS for per-command positional rename)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 851-907 (_generate_args with auto-iteration of `dir(command)`)
    * `alembic/config.py` lines 909-934 (register_command with `inspect.getfullargspec`)
    * `alembic/command.py` lines 25-832 (15 public function subcommands)
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **Python argparse** library at `from argparse import ArgumentParser` — corpus's **first documented Python argparse CLI**.  Completes the **3-way Python CLI library taxonomy** (NOW COMPLETE): (a) **argparse** (R267 alembic — Python stdlib), (b) **Click** (R266 uvicorn — decorator-based), (c) **Typer** (modern; type-hint-driven; deferred).  argparse is the stdlib choice; built-in to every Python install; declarative but verbose compared to Click; supports subcommands via `parser.add_subparsers()` + `subparser.add_parser(name, ...)`.  Distinct from Click's decorator stack in style — argparse builds the parser tree imperatively.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.  3-way Python CLI taxonomy NOW COMPLETE.
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **INTROSPECTION-DRIVEN SUBCOMMAND REGISTRATION** at config.py:893-905 — iterates `dir(command)` and registers every PUBLIC function in `alembic.command` as a subcommand automatically via `register_command(fn)`.  The subcommand NAME matches `fn.__name__`; the HELP text is read from `fn.__doc__` (first contiguous block); the ARGS are extracted from `inspect.getfullargspec(fn)`.  Positional args (no default) become argparse positionals; kwargs (with defaults) become argparse options.  Documents the **introspection-driven CLI pattern** — corpus's first.  Distinct from clap derive's struct-attribute approach and from Click's decorator stack.  Powerful because adding a new command to `alembic/command.py` AUTO-REGISTERS it in the CLI without modifying the CLI code.  But adds **runtime overhead** (inspection at startup) and reduces clarity (CLI surface implicit in function signatures).  Documents the **convention-over-configuration extreme** in Python CLI design.  Tradeoff: lower maintenance cost vs higher implicit-magic cost.
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **`_KWARGS_OPTS` + `_POSITIONAL_OPTS` PER-ARG METADATA DICTS** at config.py:696-848 — when registering a command, argparse needs to know each arg's type, action, help text, short/long flag names.  alembic stores these in 2 class-level dicts keyed by ARG NAME (not function name) — so the SAME arg `verbose` always gets `-v/--verbose action=store_true help="Use more verbose output"` regardless of which subcommand has it.  Documents the **shared-arg-metadata-by-name pattern** — DRY for CLIs where the same arg name appears in multiple subcommands.  18 kwargs documented in _KWARGS_OPTS (template / message / sql / tag / head / splice / depends_on / rev_id / version_path / branch_label / verbose / resolve_dependencies / autogenerate / rev_range / indicate_current / purge / package / check_heads).  3 positionals documented in _POSITIONAL_OPTS (directory / revision / revisions where `revisions` uses `nargs="+"` for "one or more").  Corpus's first documented **shared-arg-metadata-by-name** pattern in a CLI.
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **`_POSITIONAL_TRANSLATIONS = {command.stamp: {"revision": "revisions"}}`** at config.py:846-848 — a TRANSLATION DICT that renames the introspected positional arg from `revision` to `revisions` ONLY for the `stamp` subcommand (because `stamp` accepts multiple revisions but the function signature names it singular).  Documents the **per-command positional-name override pattern** — when the function signature name differs from the desired CLI positional name (e.g. for pluralization, semantic clarity).  Corpus's **first documented introspection-override mechanism** in a CLI.  Subtle but necessary when introspection meets semantic CLI design.  Documents the **escape hatch** for introspection-driven CLIs: when the convention doesn't fit the desired CLI surface, explicit override dicts override convention.
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **`action="version"`** at config.py:855 on `--version` — the argparse builtin `version` action automatically prints the version string (`"%%(prog)s %s" % __version__`) and exits 0.  No callback needed.  Documents the **argparse builtin `version` action** — Python stdlib argparse has a small set of builtin actions: store, store_const, store_true, store_false, append, append_const, count, help, version, extend (3.8+).  Distinct from R266 uvicorn's Click `--version` (which requires explicit `callback=print_version, expose_value=False, is_eager=True`).  argparse's `version` is one line; Click's needs 3 attributes.  Corpus's first documented argparse `action="version"`.  Documents the **3 version-flag patterns**: (a) argparse `action="version"` (1-line builtin), (b) Click eager callback (3-attr explicit), (c) clap derive `#[arg(action = ArgAction::Version)]` (1-attribute Rust).
  - Critical extraction note: alembic uses **`-c/--config action="append"`** at config.py:858-865 — the `--config` option can be specified MULTIPLE TIMES, with values accumulating into a list.  E.g. `alembic -c base.ini -c overlay.toml command` collects to `config = ['base.ini', 'overlay.toml']`.  Documents the **argparse `action="append"` pattern** — distinct from clap's `Vec<T>` (auto-multiple) and Click's `multiple=True`.  argparse's append is the explicit action.  Plus the help text describes the dual-config semantics: alembic.ini OR pyproject.toml OR BOTH.  Useful for CLIs that need to layer multiple configuration sources.  Documents the **3 repeatable-flag patterns**: (a) argparse `action="append"`, (b) Click `multiple=True`, (c) clap `Vec<T>` field type.
  - Hunt-skip notes: typer-cli (Typer modern Python CLI; needs source-direct work) — deferred to complete 3-pattern taxonomy.  fastapi-cli already in corpus.  Other Python tooling: poetry / pdm / black / mypy.
  - Blacklist: added `alembic`.
  - Corpus 28,692 → 28,693.

- **R266 — uvicorn source-direct (ASGI web server; Python Click library)** (1 file new) — pivots from Go stdlib flag (R264 ctop / R265 moq) into the **Python web serving cluster**.  uvicorn is the **ASGI web server** for Python — the de-facto standard ASGI server (alongside hypercorn / daphne).  Used to serve FastAPI / Starlette / Django (ASGI mode) / Litestar / etc.  Sister to gunicorn (not yet in corpus) and hypercorn.  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Python Click CLI** — every prior Python CLI in corpus used argparse or had its own grammar.
  - `_uvicorn` (1-stem; encode/uvicorn uvicorn/main.py + uvicorn/config.py; **Python Click library**; **~50 options + auto-envvar-prefix + Click-paired enable/disable flags + multiple=True + click.Path + click.Choice + eager callback + renamed-destination parameter**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `uvicorn/main.py` line 14 (`import click`)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` lines 34-36 (LEVEL_CHOICES / LIFESPAN_CHOICES / INTERFACE_CHOICES via `click.Choice(list(LOG_LEVELS.keys()))`)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` line 62 (`@click.command(context_settings={"auto_envvar_prefix": "UVICORN"})`)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` line 63 (`@click.argument("app", envvar="UVICORN_APP")`)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` lines 64-388 (~50 `@click.option` decorators)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` lines 81-95 (renamed-destination + multiple + click.Path)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` lines 213-242 (5 paired enable/disable flags via `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` slash syntax)
    * `uvicorn/main.py` lines 353-360 (`--version` with eager callback)
    * `uvicorn/config.py` lines 30-66 (Literal types + 6 protocol/level/lifespan/loop/interface dicts/lists)
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **Python Click** library at `import click` — corpus's **first documented Python Click CLI**.  Documents the **3-way Python CLI library taxonomy**: (a) **argparse** (Python stdlib; most primitive; required-arg-by-default semantics), (b) **Click** (most popular; decorator-based; auto-help; subcommand support; THIS CLI), (c) **Typer** (modern; type-hint-driven; built on Click but with cleaner ergonomics).  Plus minor libs: (d) docopt (parses help-text format), (e) fire (auto-CLI from any Python obj).  Documents the **Click decorator pattern**: `@click.command(...)` decorates the main fn; `@click.argument(name, ...)` for positionals; `@click.option(name, ...)` for flags; decorators STACK in REVERSE order (last decorator = first arg in fn signature).  Distinct from Rust clap derive's struct-based approach.  Click is the dominant Python CLI lib — used by Flask/Django-CLI/Black/many more.  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`@click.command(context_settings={"auto_envvar_prefix": "UVICORN"})`** at main.py:62 — the **AUTO-ENV-VAR-PREFIX feature** in Click.  Every option automatically gets a `UVICORN_<NAME>` env var fallback (with the option name uppercased + underscores instead of dashes).  E.g. `--host` reads from `UVICORN_HOST`, `--port` from `UVICORN_PORT`, `--workers` from `UVICORN_WORKERS`.  Documents the **auto-envvar-prefix pattern** — distinct from R252 saya's `#[clap(long, env)]` Rust shorthand (which auto-derives env names but requires the attribute on every flag).  Click's `auto_envvar_prefix` is GLOBAL — set once on the command, applies to every option.  Distinct from R260 dua's SYSTEMATIC `DUA_*` env names declared on each flag — uvicorn's auto-prefix is implicit.  Corpus's **first documented auto-envvar-prefix** Click feature.  Most efficient way to expose a full CLI configuration surface to env vars without per-flag annotation.
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`@click.option("--reload-dir", "reload_dirs", multiple=True, type=click.Path(exists=True))`** at main.py:82-87 — TWO key Click features: (1) **Renamed parameter** — the option is `--reload-dir` (singular, kebab-case) on the CLI, but the Python parameter that receives the value is `reload_dirs` (plural, snake_case).  The second positional string to `@click.option` is the DESTINATION variable name.  (2) **`multiple=True`** — the option can be specified multiple times; the destination becomes a `tuple` of values.  E.g. `--reload-dir /a --reload-dir /b` → `reload_dirs = ("/a", "/b")`.  Documents the **renamed-destination + multiple flag pattern** — Click's standard idiom for "repeatable option that collects into a Python sequence".  Distinct from clap derive's `Vec<T>` field (which auto-derives both the multiple semantics and the collection type).  Plus `type=click.Path(exists=True)` validates that the path exists at parse time.  Corpus's **first documented click.Path validator**.
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`--access-log/--no-access-log` PAIRED ENABLE/DISABLE FLAG** at main.py:213-218 — the option name uses the `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` slash-separated SYNTAX which Click recognizes specially.  This generates BOTH `--access-log` (sets to True) AND `--no-access-log` (sets to False).  Documents the **Click slash-paired enable/disable syntax** — distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's clap pattern (`#[arg(action = ArgAction::SetTrue)]` for True-only + separate explicit `--no-X` flag).  Click bundles both forms into one decorator.  uvicorn uses this for 5 flags: --access-log, --use-colors, --proxy-headers, --server-header, --date-header — all defaulting to True with the `--no-X` form for explicit disable.  Corpus's **first documented Click slash-paired enable/disable syntax**.  Documents the **3 enable/disable flag patterns**: (a) Rust clap explicit `--no-X` separate attribute, (b) clap derive `Option<bool>` (tri-state), (c) Click slash-paired `--FLAG/--no-FLAG` (single decorator).
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`--version` with `callback=print_version, expose_value=False, is_eager=True`** at main.py:353-360 — the **EAGER CALLBACK pattern** in Click: `callback=print_version` (fn to invoke when the flag is parsed), `expose_value=False` (DO NOT pass `version` as a parameter to the main fn — callback handles it), `is_eager=True` (process this option BEFORE other options, so `--version` works even if other required args missing).  Documents the **Click eager callback** pattern — distinct from clap derive's `#[arg(action = ArgAction::Version)]` (which clap auto-handles).  Useful for flags that short-circuit normal parsing (--help, --version, --completion, etc.).  Plus `callback` fn signature is `(ctx, param, value)` — 3-arg form.  Corpus's **first documented Click eager-callback pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`type=click.Choice(list(LOG_LEVELS.keys()))`** at main.py:34 + 208 — the **Click Choice ValueParser**.  Restricts the option to a fixed set of values.  Distinct from clap's `value_parser = [...]` slice or ValueEnum-derive.  Documents the **Click Choice pattern** — accepts a list of strings; validates input at parse time; emits "invalid choice: 'X'. (choose from 'a', 'b', 'c')" on mismatch.  uvicorn uses 3 Choice variants: LEVEL_CHOICES (6 levels: critical / error / warning / info / debug / trace), LIFESPAN_CHOICES (3 values: auto / on / off), INTERFACE_CHOICES (4 values: auto / asgi3 / asgi2 / wsgi).
  - Critical extraction note: uvicorn uses **`@click.argument("app", envvar="UVICORN_APP")`** at main.py:63 — the POSITIONAL `app` (the ASGI application import path like `mymodule:app`) has an explicit env var override.  Plus the positional is REQUIRED (Click's default for arguments).  Documents the **Click argument vs option distinction**: argument = positional + required by default; option = flag-prefixed + optional by default.  Plus the env var on argument is OVERRIDDEN if the positional is provided on CLI.  Distinct from R252 saya's `#[clap(long, env)]` (flag with env) — Click's `@click.argument(envvar=...)` is **positional with env-var fallback**.  Corpus's **first documented Click argument-envvar pattern**.
  - Hunt-skip notes: isort (Python argparse; 1225 lines too large).  poetry / pdm / black / mypy Python tooling deferred.  pyrefactor Python refactoring tool probe.  gunicorn (Python WSGI server using argparse) deferred.  waitress similar deferred.
  - Blacklist: added `uvicorn`.
  - Corpus 28,691 → 28,692.

- **R265 — moq source-direct (Go interface mock generator; stdlib flag StringVar/BoolVar variants)** (1 file new) — extends the R264 Go stdlib flag documentation series.  moq is the **Go interface mock generator** from matryer — generates Go mock implementations from interface definitions for use in testing.  Sister to mockgen (in corpus), testify (in corpus).  Pivots from container TUI (R264 ctop) into the **Go testing tooling cluster**.  Documents a DIFFERENT VARIANT within the same Go stdlib `flag` library — `StringVar`/`BoolVar` write-to-pointer variants vs R264 ctop's pointer-returning forms.
  - `_moq` (1-stem; matryer/moq main.go; **Go stdlib `flag` with StringVar/BoolVar write-to-pointer variants**; **7 grouped flags into userFlags struct + 1 mixed-pattern pointer-returning flag + custom flag.Usage override + positional args via flag.Args() + interface:alias colon syntax**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `main.go` lines 18-27 (userFlags struct with 7 fields + args slice)
    * `main.go` lines 29-63 (main fn: 8 flag declarations, custom flag.Usage, positional handling)
    * `main.go` lines 31-41 (flag.StringVar + flag.BoolVar 7 declarations + 1 pointer-returning flag.Bool — MIXED patterns)
    * `main.go` lines 43-48 (custom flag.Usage override)
    * `main.go` line 51 (positional args extracted via `flag.Args()` after flag.Parse())
    * `main.go` lines 66-67 (positional minimum check)
  - Critical extraction note: moq uses **`flag.StringVar` / `flag.BoolVar` write-to-pointer variants** at main.go:31, 32, 33, 34, 37, 39, 40 — distinct from R264 ctop's pointer-RETURNING `flag.String("name", "default", "help") *string`.  StringVar signature: `flag.StringVar(p *string, name, defaultValue, help string)`.  Writes to user-provided `&var` directly so the calling code accesses `flags.outFile` instead of `*outFileFlag`.  Documents the **2 stdlib-flag access patterns**: (a) pointer-returning form (R264 ctop), (b) write-to-pointer form via StringVar/BoolVar (R265 moq).  The Var-form is more idiomatic when collecting flags into a struct (like moq's `userFlags`); the pointer-returning form is more idiomatic when flags are local variables.  Documents the **stdlib-flag struct-collection pattern** for grouped flag handling.  Corpus's **first documented stdlib-flag `XxxVar` write-to-pointer pattern**.
  - Critical extraction note: moq uses **MIXED PATTERNS in the same CLI** at main.go:36 — `printVersion := flag.Bool("version", false, "show the version for moq")` is the pointer-returning form, while the OTHER 7 flags use the StringVar/BoolVar write-to-pointer form.  Documents the **mixed-stdlib-flag-patterns reality** — moq's authors used the pointer-returning form for `--version` (a local-only flag that doesn't need to flow into the `userFlags` struct) but the struct-write form for everything that needs to flow into the run-fn.  Practical hybrid usage.  Corpus's first documented **mixed-pattern stdlib flag usage within one CLI**.
  - Critical extraction note: moq uses **custom `flag.Usage` function** at main.go:43-48 — overrides the default help format with: (a) usage line printed first, (b) `flag.PrintDefaults()` for the flag list, (c) extra lines explaining the `interface:alias` syntax with an example.  Documents the **custom flag.Usage override pattern** — Go's stdlib flag package allows replacing the default help generator by assigning to `flag.Usage`.  Distinct from R264 ctop's separate `printHelp` fn (called manually from `*helpFlag` branch).  moq's approach is more idiomatic: `flag.Usage` is automatically called when parsing fails or `-h` is passed.  Corpus's **first documented `flag.Usage` override**.  Documents **2 stdlib-flag help-customization patterns**: (a) separate fn called from explicit `-h` branch (R264 ctop), (b) `flag.Usage` reassignment for automatic invocation (R265 moq).
  - Critical extraction note: moq uses **positional args via `flag.Args()`** at main.go:51 — after `flag.Parse()`, `flag.Args()` returns the remaining (non-flag) arguments as `[]string`.  moq expects at least 2 positionals: `source-dir` + at least 1 interface name (with optional `:alias` suffix).  The minimum check at line 66 (`if len(flags.args) < 2 { return errors.New("not enough arguments") }`) is manual.  Documents the **stdlib-flag positional handling** — distinct from R263 lazygit's flaggy `AddPositionalValue` with required-flag and R249 cairo-profiler's clap derive positional with `num_args = 1..`.  Stdlib flag has NO positional declaration syntax; positionals are just "everything after the parsed flags".  Manual minimum-count enforcement is the standard idiom.
  - Critical extraction note: moq supports **`interface:alias` syntax for naming the generated mock** — e.g. `moq -pkg different . MyInterface:MyMock` generates a mock for `MyInterface` named `MyMock` instead of the default `MyInterfaceMock`.  The alias-syntax parsing is in pkg/moq (not main.go).  Documents the **colon-separated positional alias pattern** in CLI design — a single positional with embedded structure (`name:alias`) parsed by the downstream library.  Distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's `value_parser = fn` (which validates a whole positional) — moq's colon-form is a STRUCTURAL parse (split + assign roles).  Common idiom in Go CLIs that need name+alias pairs (e.g. `docker run -v host:container`, kubernetes `key=value` flags).
  - Hunt-skip notes: buildkit (BuildKit Docker daemon; large) deferred.  zon / nx-cli / wgpu-cli probe.  isort / poetry-bin / pdm-bin Python tooling probe.
  - Blacklist: added `moq`.
  - Corpus 28,690 → 28,691.

- **R264 — ctop source-direct (top-like container monitor; Go stdlib flag)** (1 file new) — extends the R263 terminal UI tooling cluster into the **container-tooling TUI sub-cluster**.  ctop is the **top-like container monitor** — interactive process viewer for containers (Docker by default, optional runc on Linux).  Sister to lazydocker (in corpus), k9s (in corpus), dive (in corpus), lazygit (R263).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented Go stdlib `flag` package CLI** — completes the **4-way Go CLI library taxonomy** (cobra / pflag / flaggy / stdlib).
  - `_ctop` (1-stem; bcicen/ctop main.go; **Go stdlib `flag` package**; **8 flags + map-lookup validation + build-tag-gated connector + symmetric `-flag` / `--flag` syntax**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `main.go` lines 35-49 (main fn opens with 8 `flag.Bool/String` declarations + `flag.Parse()`)
    * `main.go` lines 127-132 (validSort fn: validates against container.Sorters map)
    * `main.go` lines 150-155 (printHelp fn: uses `flag.PrintDefaults()` for auto-help generation, appends "available connectors: ..." from `connector.Enabled()`)
    * `container/sort.go` lines 25+ (Sorters map: id / name / cpu / mem / io / net / pids / state / uptime — 9 valid sort fields)
    * `connector/docker.go` (`init()` registers "docker" — always available)
    * `connector/runc.go` (`init()` registers "runc" — gated by `//go:build linux` build tag)
  - Critical extraction note: ctop uses the **Go stdlib `flag` package** at `import "flag"` — corpus's **first documented stdlib-flag-package CLI**.  Documents the **4-way Go CLI library taxonomy** (NOW COMPLETE): (a) cobra (most popular; subcommand-first), (b) pflag (POSIX-like replacement for stdlib flag), (c) flaggy (simpler than cobra; R263 lazygit), (d) **stdlib `flag` package** (most primitive; Go's built-in flag-parsing library; R264 ctop).  Key characteristics of stdlib flag: (1) `flag.String("name", "default", "help")` returns `*string` (pointer); (2) `flag.Bool("name", false, "help")` returns `*bool`; (3) `flag.Parse()` parses `os.Args`; (4) **NO short/long distinction** — `flag.String("v", ...)` produces BOTH `-v` and `--v` (Go's idiosyncratic convention); (5) **NO subcommand support** built-in (each subcommand must call its own `flag.NewFlagSet(...)` manually); (6) `flag.PrintDefaults()` for auto-help generation; (7) **NO restricted-value validators** — validation done manually in switch or map lookup.  Documents the **stdlib-flag-package patterns** for corpus comprehensiveness — primitive but ubiquitous in Go single-binary tools.  4-way Go-CLI-library taxonomy NOW COMPLETE.
  - Critical extraction note: ctop uses **the stdlib flag package's `--flag` AND `-flag` symmetry** at the CLI — every flag declared as `flag.String("v", ...)` accepts both `ctop -v` and `ctop --v`.  Go's flag package treats short and long names identically (the leading `-` and `--` are both stripped).  Distinct from clap/cobra/pflag/flaggy where short and long are explicitly separate.  Documents the **Go flag-package convention** — short and long are aliases of the SAME flag declaration, not separate declarations.  Reflected in the completion file's `_arguments` lines as 4-way alias groups (e.g. `{-h,-help,--h,--help}`).
  - Critical extraction note: ctop uses **`validSort` fn with map-lookup validation** at main.go:127-132 — when `-s X` is given, ctop checks if `X` is a key in `container.Sorters` (a `map[string]sortMethod` from container/sort.go).  If not, prints `"invalid sort field: %s"` and exits with code 1.  Documents the **map-lookup-as-restricted-value pattern** — Go stdlib `flag` has no built-in restricted-value validator like clap's ValueEnum or flaggy's switch.  ctop's approach is to keep the valid set in a map (which is also used for dispatch) and check membership at flag-validation time.  Distinct from R263 lazygit's switch-based validation.  Documents **2 stdlib-flag restricted-value patterns**: (a) switch (R263 lazygit), (b) map lookup (R264 ctop).  The map approach is DRY (single source of truth for valid values + dispatch table); the switch approach is more readable but requires duplicating the value list.
  - Critical extraction note: ctop uses **runtime `connector.Enabled()` for help text** at main.go:154 — the printHelp fn appends "available connectors: " followed by the runtime-listed enabled connectors (joined by comma).  Connectors are registered via `func init() { enabled["<name>"] = NewXxx }` in each connector source file.  `docker.go` registers unconditionally; `runc.go` is gated by `//go:build linux` so the `runc` connector only exists when compiled on Linux.  Documents the **build-tag-gated CLI feature** pattern in Go — like Rust's `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` but applied to a whole FILE (init fn won't compile on non-Linux).  Plus the help text DYNAMICALLY reflects which connectors are enabled at compile time (so macOS users see only `docker` while Linux users see `docker, runc`).  Distinct from R260 dua's `#[cfg_attr(target_os = "linux", clap(default_values = &[...]))]` (Rust attribute-level cfg).  Corpus's first documented **Go build-tag-gated CLI feature**.
  - Critical extraction note: ctop uses **pointer-deref pattern** at main.go:51, 56, 71, 75, 79, 84, 89 — every flag value access is via `*flagPtr` since stdlib flag returns pointers.  E.g. `if *versionFlag { ... }`.  Distinct from clap derive (which destructures directly into typed fields) and flaggy (which writes to user-provided `&var` directly).  Documents the **pointer-to-T flag access pattern** specific to Go stdlib flag.  Idiomatic Go stdlib code but verbose compared to flaggy/clap.
  - Hunt-skip notes: nethogs (network top per process; C-based) deferred.  potato (?) probe.  emacs-bin / neovim-bin / chezmoi-bin probe candidates.  buildkit / moq Go-based candidates.
  - Blacklist: added `ctop`.
  - Corpus 28,689 → 28,690.

- **R263 — lazygit source-direct (terminal UI for git; Go flaggy library)** (1 file new) — pivots from theming (R262 matugen) into the **terminal UI tooling cluster**.  lazygit is the **terminal UI for git** from jesseduffield — terminal-based interactive git client with vim-style keybindings, panels for status/branches/log/stash, and extensive customization.  Sister to lazydocker / lazyjj / lazysql / atac / slumber / yazi (all in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented flaggy-based Go CLI** (every prior Go CLI in corpus used cobra/pflag).
  - `_lazygit` (1-stem; jesseduffield/lazygit pkg/app/entry_point.go; **Go with flaggy library**; **13 flags + 1 optional positional + multi-char short flags + env-var-as-default bridging**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `pkg/app/entry_point.go` lines 29-44 (cliArgs struct with 13 fields)
    * `pkg/app/entry_point.go` lines 180-247 (parseCliArgsAndEnvVars fn — 13 `flaggy.String/Bool` calls + 1 `flaggy.AddPositionalValue`)
    * `pkg/app/entry_point.go` lines 249-271 (parseGitArg fn: valid values are status/branch/log/stash)
    * `pkg/app/entry_point.go` lines 213 + 216 (env var defaults: `os.Getenv("GIT_WORK_TREE")`, `os.Getenv("GIT_DIR")`)
    * `pkg/app/entry_point.go` lines 84-90 (CONFIG_DIR / LG_CONFIG_FILE env-var bridging)
  - Critical extraction note: lazygit uses the **flaggy** library at `github.com/integrii/flaggy` — this is the **corpus's first documented flaggy CLI** (every prior Go CLI in the corpus used cobra/pflag; every prior Rust CLI used clap).  Documents the **3-way Go CLI library taxonomy**: (a) cobra (most popular; subcommand-first; kubectl/gh/gitsign/etc.), (b) pflag (POSIX-style flag parsing; primitive), (c) **flaggy** (simpler than cobra, more featureful than stdlib flag; auto-help generation; positional + named args).  flaggy API: `flaggy.String(&var, "short", "long", "help")` declares a flag; `flaggy.Bool(...)` for booleans; `flaggy.AddPositionalValue(&var, "name", index, required, "help")` for positionals; `flaggy.Parse()` to dispatch.  flaggy distinguishes from cobra by being **flat-only** (no subcommands by default; you add subcommands via Subcommand objects) which suits CLIs like lazygit that have no subcommand surface.
  - Critical extraction note: lazygit uses **MULTI-CHAR "SHORT" FLAGS** like `-cd`, `-ucd`, `-ucf`, `-sm` at entry_point.go:208, 211, 220, 223 — flaggy treats both short and long names symmetrically, so a "short" name can be multiple characters.  Standard Unix CLIs use single-character short flags (`-c`/`--config`); lazygit uses 2-3 char "shorts" (`-cd`/`--print-config-dir`, `-ucd`/`--use-config-dir`, `-ucf`/`--use-config-file`, `-sm`/`--screen-mode`).  Documents the **multi-char short-flag convention** — unusual but valid.  Corpus's **first documented multi-char short flag** (every prior clap-based CLI restricted shorts to single chars per clap's convention).
  - Critical extraction note: lazygit uses **env-var-as-default** at entry_point.go:213 + 216 — `flaggy.String(&workTree, "w", "work-tree", "...")` is preceded by `workTree := os.Getenv("GIT_WORK_TREE")` so the env var's value becomes the default for the flag.  Plus at entry_point.go:84-90 the parsed CLI value is REINJECTED into env vars via `os.Setenv("LG_CONFIG_FILE", cliArgs.CustomConfigFile)` so downstream code reading env vars sees the CLI override.  Documents the **bidirectional env-var-flag bridging** pattern in Go CLIs — env var initializes the flag default, then the parsed flag value is exported back as an env var for child processes / library code that reads env directly.  Distinct from R252 saya's `#[clap(long, env)]` (Rust clap auto-bridges in both directions) — flaggy requires manual bridging.
  - Critical extraction note: lazygit's `git-arg` positional uses `flaggy.AddPositionalValue(&gitArg, "git-arg", 1, false, "...")` at entry_point.go:190 — the 4th arg (`required`) is `false` so the positional is OPTIONAL.  Valid values restricted to `status / branch / log / stash` (decoded source-direct from parseGitArg switch at lines 254 + 258-263).  When invalid, lazygit exits with "Invalid git arg value: '...'. Must be one of: status, branch, log, stash".  Documents the **flaggy positional value with custom validation** pattern — flaggy doesn't have built-in restricted-value validators like clap's ValueEnum, so the validation is done manually in a switch.  Distinct from R249 cairo-profiler's clap-restricted value via ValueEnum.
  - Critical extraction note: lazygit uses **deferred env-var bridging** at entry_point.go:227-229 — `if os.Getenv("DEBUG") == "TRUE" { debug = true }` AFTER `flaggy.Parse()` to handle the legacy `DEBUG=TRUE` env var as an alternative to `--debug`.  Documents the **env-as-fallback pattern** — env var only applied if explicit flag wasn't set.  Practical for backward compatibility with older env-var-driven workflows.
  - Hunt-skip notes: goose-cli (Block AI agent CLI; ~2300 lines too large for single round) deferred.  wakatime-cli (Go/cobra; ~100+ flags) deferred.  yazi-fm (yazi file manager) probe — yazi already in corpus as `_yazi`.
  - Blacklist: added `lazygit`.
  - Corpus 28,688 → 28,689.

- **R262 — matugen source-direct (Material You color palette generator)** (1 file new) — pivots from database tooling (R261 replibyte) into the **theming / dotfile-management cluster**.  matugen is the **Material You color palette generator** — generates Material Design 3 color schemes from images (wallpapers, screenshots) or explicit colors, then applies the palette to dotfiles via templating.  Used by ricers / dotfile customizers to maintain consistent color schemes across the entire desktop.  Sister to pywal / wpgtk / oomox (not in corpus; matugen is the most active modern alternative).
  - `_matugen` (1-stem; InioX/matugen src/util/arguments.rs + src/color/color.rs; clap v4 derive; **27 global flags + 4 top-level subcommands + 3-deep color tree + 5 ValueEnums + raw identifier r#type + allow_negative_numbers + RangedI64ValueParser + closure value_parser + inline-struct variants throughout**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` lines 7-145 (Cli Parser struct with 27 global flags + Source subcommand; uses `r#type` raw identifier)
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` line 134 (built-in `clap::builder::RangedI64ValueParser::<i64>::new().range(0..4)`)
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` line 143 (CLOSURE value_parser `|s: &str| validate_float_range(s, 0.0..=1.0)`)
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` lines 35 + 118 + 126 (`allow_negative_numbers = true` on 3 fields)
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` lines 72-75 + 108-111 (cfg-gated field-level)
    * `src/util/arguments.rs` lines 162-223 (5 ValueEnums: FilterType / Format / SelectionPreference / SchemeTypes / SchemesEnum)
    * `src/color/color.rs` lines 30-34 (ColorFormat Parser enum: Hex/Rgb/Hsl inline-struct variants)
    * `src/color/color.rs` lines 46-60 (Source Subcommand enum: Image/WebImage cfg-gated/Color nested/Json)
  - Critical extraction note: matugen uses **`r#type`** at arguments.rs:23 — the Rust **raw identifier syntax** for using a reserved keyword as a field name.  `type` is a Rust keyword; `r#type` lets the field be named `type` while clap derive emits `--type` as the CLI flag (auto-derived from the field name by stripping `r#`).  Documents the **raw identifier (`r#X`) pattern** in clap derive — when the desired field/flag name is a Rust keyword.  Corpus's **first documented `r#X` raw identifier** in a clap derive struct.  Distinct from R253 fuelup's `Default_` trailing-underscore (which is for enum VARIANT names — the strip-trailing rule) and R253 fuelup's `#[clap(name = "self", subcommand)]` (which is for ENUM variant name override).  The `r#X` pattern is for FIELD names in structs.  Completes the corpus's **3-pattern Rust-keyword-avoidance taxonomy in clap derive**: (a) trailing underscore `Default_` for ENUM variants, (b) `#[clap(name = "self")]` rename for ENUM variants, (c) `r#X` raw identifier for STRUCT fields.
  - Critical extraction note: matugen uses **`allow_negative_numbers = true`** at arguments.rs:35 + 118 + 126 on `--contrast` (f64), `--lightness-dark` (f64), `--lightness-light` (f64) — these flags accept negative numeric values (e.g. `--contrast=-0.5`).  Without this attribute, clap interprets the leading `-` as a missing flag.  Distinct from R258 bevy's `allow_hyphen_values = true` (which allows ALL hyphen-prefixed values, useful for forwarding dash-prefixed args to downstream tools).  `allow_negative_numbers` is SPECIFICALLY for numeric flags — allows `-N.NN` to parse as a negative number but still rejects `--FOO`.  Documents the **`allow_negative_numbers` vs `allow_hyphen_values` distinction** — corpus's first documented `allow_negative_numbers`.
  - Critical extraction note: matugen uses **built-in `clap::builder::RangedI64ValueParser::<i64>::new().range(0..4)`** at arguments.rs:134 on `--source-color-index` — clap's built-in range-checking ValueParser via the builder API.  Documents the **built-in RangedI64ValueParser pattern** — clap v4 ships range parsers for i8/i16/i32/i64/u8/u16/u32/u64 + f32/f64.  Distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's custom `parse_trace_file` validators (custom fn-based) and R252 saya's `validate_non_empty` (simple String).  Corpus's first documented **built-in RangedXxxValueParser**.  When the value is a number with a range, prefer the built-in parser over a custom fn.
  - Critical extraction note: matugen uses **CLOSURE value_parser** at arguments.rs:143 on `--opacity`: `|s: &str| validate_float_range(s, 0.0..=1.0)` — clap accepts an inline closure as the value_parser (since the closure satisfies `Fn(&str) -> Result<T, E>`).  Documents the **inline-closure value_parser pattern** — distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's separate fn, R252 saya's separate fn, R253 fuelup's separate fn.  Corpus's **first documented inline-closure value_parser**.  Useful for one-off range checks where the validator doesn't need a name.  Completes the corpus's **value_parser source taxonomy**: (a) struct/fn-pointer (R248/R252/R253), (b) closure (R262), (c) built-in RangedXxx (R262), (d) ValueEnum-derived (most ValueEnums), (e) restricted-slice `value_parser = [...]` (deprecated in R261 clap v3 `possible_values`).
  - Critical extraction note: matugen has a **3-deep subcommand tree via inline-struct + nested-subcommand variants**: (1) `matugen <Source>` — image/web-image/color/json, (2) `matugen color <ColorFormat>` — hex/rgb/hsl, (3) (no further nesting; each ColorFormat variant takes a single `string: String` positional).  The `Source::Color(ColorFormat)` variant wraps another Subcommand enum via the tuple-struct variant pattern.  Combined with `#[clap(subcommand)]` on the wrapping enum, clap nests the parser.  Documents the **enum-wrapping-enum subcommand nesting** — for hierarchical subcommand trees.  Distinct from R254 subxt's 3-deep `Option<Subcommand>` progressive disclosure (which uses Option<> instead of tuple-wrap).
  - Critical extraction note: matugen uses **inline-struct variants for ALL Source variants**: `Image { path: String }`, `WebImage { url: String }`, `Json { path: String }`.  Plus `ColorFormat` variants `Hex { string: String }`, `Rgb { string: String }`, `Hsl { string: String }`.  Documents extensive use of **inline-struct variant** pattern (R258 bevy was first to document for Completions { shell }).  matugen uses it 6+ times, making it a common pattern when each variant has only a single positional.  Eliminates the need for separate `ImageArgs` / `WebImageArgs` / etc. structs.  Corpus's first documented **extensive inline-struct-variant usage** (>5 variants in one CLI).
  - Critical extraction note: matugen has **3 cfg-gated fields/variants**: arguments.rs:72-75 `#[cfg(feature = "dump-json")]` on `--json` field; arguments.rs:108-111 `#[cfg(feature = "filter-docs")]` on `--filter-docs-html` field; color/color.rs:53-54 `#[cfg(feature = "web-image")]` on `WebImage { url }` variant.  Documents the **mixed field+variant cfg-gating** within a single CLI — different features control different layers of the CLI surface.  Continues the corpus's feature-gating granularity taxonomy.
  - Hunt-skip notes: wakatime-cli is Go-based with cobra (~100+ flags) too large for single round.  taskwarrior / timewarrior C++ from upstream task.cc deferred.  matugen-cli was the rustdesk-style desktop integration tool from the same author.
  - Blacklist: added `matugen`.
  - Corpus 28,687 → 28,688.

- **R261 — replibyte source-direct (production-data anonymizer; clap v3.1)** (1 file new) — pivots from filesystem analysis (R260 dua) into the **database tooling cluster**.  replibyte is the **production-data-anonymization tool** from Qovery — dumps production databases (Postgres / MySQL / MongoDB) while transforming sensitive fields (PII, secrets) into safe synthetic data, then restores the anonymized dump into dev/staging environments.  Sister to litestream (in corpus), pgcli (in corpus).  **MAJOR MILESTONE**: corpus's **first documented clap v3 CLI** (every prior round R247-R260 used clap v4 — derive or builder).  Documents the **clap v3-to-v4 migration matrix**.
  - `_replibyte` (1-stem; Qovery/replibyte replibyte/src/cli.rs; **clap 3.1**; **3 top-level subcommands + 3-deep dump tree + 4-deep dump-restore tree + v3 syntax patterns: parse(from_os_str) + possible_values + builder-API ArgGroup inside derive + propagate_version**) decoded source-direct from:
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 9-18 (CLI Parser struct: --config/-c required PathBuf via v3 `parse(from_os_str)`, subcommand, --no-telemetry/-n; uses `propagate_version = true`)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 22-32 (SubCommand enum: Dump / Source / Transformer)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 36-46 (DumpCommand: List / Create / Restore / Delete)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 57-62 (RestoreCommand: Local / Remote)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 66-96 (RestoreArgs + RestoreLocalArgs)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 100-113 (DumpCreateArgs with MUTUAL requires + v3 `possible_values = &[...]`)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 116-127 (DumpDeleteArgs with v3 builder-API ArgGroup inside derive)
    * `src/cli.rs` lines 131-134 (SourceCommand: just Schema)
  - Critical extraction note: replibyte uses **clap 3.1** (NOT v4) at Cargo.toml.  This is the **corpus's first documented clap v3 CLI** — every prior round R247-R260 used clap v4 (derive or builder).  **MAJOR PATTERN MILESTONE**.  Key v3-vs-v4 syntax differences observed: (1) **`parse(from_os_str)`** at cli.rs:11 + 107 — v3 attribute for OsString-to-T conversion; v4 replaced with `value_parser` (which accepts any `Fn(&str) -> Result<T, E>` or built-in ValueParser).  v3: `#[clap(parse(from_os_str))]`; v4: `#[clap(value_parser)]` (auto-infers parser from field type).  (2) **`possible_values = &["a", "b"]`** at cli.rs:101 + 94 — v3 attribute for restricted-string ValueParser; v4 deprecated in favor of `value_parser = ["a", "b"]` (slice without `&`) or ValueEnum-derive.  (3) **`clap::ArgGroup::new("delete-mode").multiple(false)`** at cli.rs:116 — v3 BUILDER-API ArgGroup construction INSIDE a derive attribute; v4 added the `#[group(name = "delete-mode", multiple = false)]` derive attribute shorthand.  Documents the **mixed builder-API-inside-derive** pattern — v3 didn't have all derive shortcuts so it required builder constructions for ArgGroup, Command::group, etc.  Documents the **clap v3-to-v4 migration matrix** — corpus's first reference for understanding what changes when porting CLIs from clap v3 to v4.
  - Critical extraction note: replibyte uses **MUTUAL requires** at cli.rs:101 + 105 on `--source-type` requires `input` AND `--input` requires `source_type`.  This means BOTH flags must be specified together — you can't use one without the other.  Documents the **bidirectional-requires mutex** — distinct from R248 cairo-coverage's `requires = "unstable"` (unidirectional: 2 flags require 1 flag).  Plus the `--source-type` value `[postgresql | mysql | mongodb]` matches the `possible_values` restriction.  Useful for "either both present or both absent" semantics where neither flag makes sense without the other.  Corpus's **first documented bidirectional-requires mutex** pattern.
  - Critical extraction note: replibyte uses **`#[clap(group = clap::ArgGroup::new("delete-mode").multiple(false))]`** at cli.rs:116 + 3 fields tagged `#[clap(group = "delete-mode")]` at lines 119, 122, 125 — declarative mutex for 3 flags (`dump` positional / `--older-than` / `--keep-last`).  v3-style: the ArgGroup is constructed via builder syntax (`clap::ArgGroup::new(...).multiple(false)`) INSIDE the derive attribute.  v4 equivalent: `#[group(name = "delete-mode", multiple = false)]` shorthand.  Continues the corpus's mutex-pattern documentation (R250 sozo `#[group(multiple = false)]`, R258 bevy `conflicts_with_all = [..]`, R255 fuel-core single-pair `conflicts_with`).  Documents the **builder-inside-derive ArgGroup** pattern.
  - Critical extraction note: replibyte uses **`propagate_version = true`** at cli.rs:8 — every subcommand also gets `--version` automatically.
